Original_Search_Title: Habila Seeks Encouragement for Upcoming Writers
Headline: Nigeria: Habila Seeks Encouragement for Upcoming Writers
Body_Text:
Celebrated writer, Helon Habila, was recently a guest of the Advancement Office of the University of Jos to deliver a Distinguished Alumni Lecture as part of his tour of Nigeria. He spoke to Sunday Vanguard on the occasion.
HOW does it feel coming back to your former university after some years?
Well it's like a home coming I think, especially for University of Jos and this is my return after four years. So, I am happy to be here after all this time and to be doing this, especially here in Jos.
We understand you are on a tour of the country. What is it all about?
Yes! I've been to Abuja and I am going to Gombe from here. It was organized by our publishers, they are called Cassava Republic. They are my Nigerian publishers. We are issuing one of our books here in Nigeria. We aim to promote them and to also talk to people along the way and encourage young writers and do whatever we can, you know, to create the awareness on the need for reading and writing to young writers.
How can upcoming writers be encouraged in this age that reading culture has dropped drastically?
Yeah! What must be done is for our universities to invite writers, bring them to meet writers. People see books as if they kind of drop from the sky but there are people behind them. So, if you can demystify the books itself, then, human beings will read them and they also have their challenges they went through or the difficulties to do it. So that people can see that it is written by people or say that yes this person did it. If he has done it, I can also do it. So, that's what I think we should be doing, especially in universities. Also, the government should be organizing this kind of interactions.
What do you think has led to the state of things in our education sector?
It's a problem of leadership. You know we are just emerging from over 20 years of dictatorship, so there are different things. Most of the time, they ought to be expected because dictatorships don't cater to the needs of the individual. They tend to do what they want to do, pursue their own agenda so it's as if we are staring from zero, we are just starting from scratch. This is the first time, after a long time, that we have individuals being able to express themselves freely or to expect justice from the courts and all that. So, there was a decline because the leaders did not develop the country. They did not develop the people and did not give people freedom to develop themselves.
So, what can writers like you do to encourage younger writers and break some of the barriers standing in their way?
I think the best we can do is to give advice and to kind of impact our expertise on our people. I'm talking especially about working with younger writers who are really ambitious and talented and have no idea about how to go about realizing their dreams.
So, I think that's the best people like me can do is to rally upcoming writers, encourage them and give them any connection that they might need in order to publish their works. These are being done.

Original_Search_Title: Standing Up to Bullies
Headline: Uganda: Standing Up to Bullies
Body_Text:
Don't you just hate those times when you are stuck at the park and the number of people waiting at your stage could fill 10 kamunyes yet only one comes along every 10 minutes?
Here I am stranded and have been standing here since 6p.m.
It is now close to 8p.m. and there is no kamunye in sight. Each one that comes along is on such high demand that it frightens me to see people fight to enter it.
I do not know why these civilians do not sign up to fight in the army...They would not need any training, they are naturals! Then there is the case of the conductors and drivers hiking the price because they know that we do not have a choice.
Today though, the real problem is not so much facing those fighters; rather it is the fact that I just have the amount of the normal fare in my bag.
I do not have a single coin more. If this continues any longer, I am going to have a long night waiting at the park until these guys decide to go back to the normal fare.
After the crowd has slowly disappeared, the next kamunye comes along and is still insisting on charging us Shs1,000 instead of the usual Shs700.
Really! Can't these guys give a girl a break? I do not have that Shs300 extra and besides, we are not that many any more. I am glad that the rest of the passengers remaining think so too.
We all enter this kamunye and refuse to be bullied into seating four per row instead of three and paying above the normal fare.
The conductor announces that if we will not comply, we should all get out. Naturally, I do not argue. Knowing my pocket, I get out without any complaint. I have heard of people being manhandled by angry conductors.
The joy that fills my heart is so evident when I turn around and see all the other passengers get out as well. They think I am rebelling and they want to support me in my cause.
Little do they know that I have my own issues! That aside, two other kamunyes come by our stage just as the last person is getting out of this kamunye.
And they were charging the normal fare despite the fact that the mean conductor has whispered something in the line of us being bigheaded passengers who need to be taught a lesson.
Thank God these men have sense enough to know that the time for hiking the price is up. They are a godsend as far as I am concerned.
They even decide that instead of us paying Shs700 as usual, we can pay Shs600 since we have been waiting for a long time. Wonders will never cease! Maaso awo! I even have Shs100 to buy cassava for supper!

Original_Search_Title: Sanyu Babies Home Gets Donation
Headline: Uganda: Sanyu Babies Home Gets Donation
Body_Text:
Kampala — CHRISTMAS came early for the children of Sanyu Babies Home in Kampala when they received donations from the Indian community under the Shree Kutch Satsang Swaminarayan Temple.
Handing over the items on Tuesday, Nitin Vekariya, the community chairman, flanked by other members of the temple, said the donations worth over sh3 million were also made to five different homes for the disabled people and orphanages in Kampala and Jinja.
"As we celebrate the first anniversary of Shree Kutch Satsang Swaminarayan temple in Uganda, we decided to make better the lives of those in need among the community," Vekariya said.
The items included foodstuff such as rice, powder milk, sugar, posho, cooking oil, beans, salt, tomatoes, cabbages, Irish potatoes, sweet potatoes, bananas, cassava and biscuits. Others were clothing, nappies, soap and detergents among other items.
While receiving the donation, Christine Asege, a social worker at Sanyu Babies Home, thanked the community for the gesture.

Original_Search_Title: BoN Sees Potential in Manufacturing Sector
Headline: Namibia: BoN Sees Potential in Manufacturing Sector
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Namibia has the potential to manufacture goods such as pencils, fences, and swimming pool and bathing salts, large-scale production of leather goods, mattresses and fuel production from cassava, says a recent study by the Bank of Namibia (BoN).
Other new products the country could also venture into are metal products and auto components, unique wood, palm and clay products, leisure garments, felts (inner pads for jackets) made from wool as well as the large-scale production of hand-woven carpets.
The study that aimed at assessing the potential of the country's manufacturing sector as well as identify potential products within the manufacturing sector, which would be growth enhancing, found that some key products were not exploited optimally.
"A number of manufactures' products which could be produced more optimally are tiles and slabs, mineral water bottles, steel windows and door frames, polished diamonds, cheese, marula jam and cereals."
The report identifies the high input costs, particularly electricity, transport and harbour charges, the availability of quotas for the fishing industry and low "throughput" for the meat processing industry as well as unfair competition from well-established South African companies as maim constraints facing Namibia's manufacturing sector.
In addition, low levels of labour productivity, coupled with the negative impact of HIV/Aids pandemic on the workforce; the lack of shelf space for Namibian manufacturers in Namibian supermarkets; the low level of branding and marketing of Namibian products; as well as the unavailability of highly skilled professionals, were identified as other constraints that confront the sector.
"In this regard, the problem with importing much needed skills particulary pertaining to the process of acquiring work permits, was significant.
"Thus, plans to establish technology and entrepreneurship centres in the country should be concretised."

Original_Search_Title: Dancing African Night Away in New York
Headline: Uganda: Dancing African Night Away in New York
Body_Text:
Kampala — We might be poor, and some people even allege that we are intellectually challenged, but there is at least one thing no one can take away from us: We sure know how to party.
Still, when a friend at the Ugandan Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York tipped me off that the African Group at the United Nations was organising the first-ever African Night, I wasn't too sure that the event was worth my time. I mean, we are talking about diplomats here.
With the notable exception of the irrepressible former German ambassador to Uganda, Klaus Holderbaum, diplomats are not necessarily the kind of people you want showing up at your party. They take parties like just another diplomatic activity where every word and every step have to be weighed carefully.
But since I didn't have anything better to do that weekend, and the "donation" required to gain entry was only 20 bucks, I decided to give it a go.
I arrived at Libya House in my jeans and denim shirt after midnight on Saturday December 1, expecting to find the diplomats in their suits discussing the Darfur humanitarian situation and Iran's nuclear tricks at their tables. Instead, I was welcomed with the thumping feet and flying arms of thousands of people dancing to kwasa kwasa music. I didn't see anyone in a tie, let alone a suit. The crowd, half African and half white, were certainly loving every minute of it.
I quickly grabbed a green one and joined in. Since kwasa kwasa lost favour in Uganda a few years ago, I had kind of forgotten my ndombolo strokes, you know, the twisting of the waist and the drawing of circles in the air. But after a few missteps, I was up to speed, showing the enthusiastic white people how it's done. I only took a breather when they switched to Arabic music - belly dancing is not exactly my kind of thing.
Having come so late, I hadn't expected to find any food left; it may have been an African party, but we were not in Kampala or Bamako. To my pleasant surprise, I discovered a table in the corner with all tribes of African food - from sweet potatoes to cassava, chapatti to dodo and gonja to fufu. The only thing missing, curiously, was matooke. It was the first time, since I checked into this city, that I felt like "being home away from home".
The DJ switched to South African music, doing Lucky Dube, Yvonne Chaka Chaka and Brenda Fassie, and I was on my feet again.
"But how come they are not playing the Ugandan music you are always talking about?" my good friend from Benin couldn't resist taking a dig at me.
I kept hoping that the DJ would prove her wrong, what with all the talk one keeps hearing that Ugandan music is on the rise. It didn't look like the DJ was going to save me, so when he played something from Zimbabwe I quickly claimed that it was Uganda's Bebe Cool doing his thing. Ms. Benin bought it.
When a diplomat who had taken one too many "undiplomatically" revealed that the Ugandan mission had contributed just 100 dollars to the party, I sneaked away before my Benin friend jumped on that one too.
If it is any consolation, I discovered the following day that Uganda wasn't the meanest contributor. That honour went to a West African country that contributed 50 dollars.
The name of the country is Benin.

Original_Search_Title: 'None of Them Can Contest Me' Esther Pabai
Headline: Sierra Leone: 'None of Them Can Contest Me' Esther Pabai
Body_Text:
Freetown — First year female student in the faculty of Arts, Fourah Bay College contesting this year's Miss University beauty pageant, Esther Pabai yesterday said none of her colleagues could contest her in terms of beauty, physical fitness and other prerequisites to grab the crown.
Pabai, 20, said she believes in going for what she wants and for many times she had tried and has been very successful.
"I am not afraid of them because I have what to show the world that I am capable and fit to win the contest. I know what I am capable of doing and I know I will win," she said.
Pabai was crowned Miss Saint Joseph Secondary School (SJSS) 2004 and 2005 respectively. She also won Miss Inter-secondary School 2005 and contested the Miss Paddy's 2005.
Pabai's star is Aries and enjoys watching football and supports Manchester United. She said her family and boyfriend have given her the blessing to compete.
"I want to call on my fans and colleagues to continue supporting me by sending SMS to 434 Celtel network and vote for me," she said.
She said she listens to music at her leisure time. She also does some reading and dancing but dislikes lying.
She recalled that the only person who has lied to her is her boyfriend.
Pabai favourite African dish is cassava leave and rice while her favourite English food is couscous. Her best musician is Bartender by Tpain and her best movie is Ghanaian Beyonce. She said her best actress is Genevieve Nnaji.

Original_Search_Title: November Inflation Down 4.8 Percent
Headline: Uganda: November Inflation Down 4.8 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — INFLATION slipped to 4.8% in the year ending November compared to 5.5% for the year ended October due to lower food prices compared to a year ago, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics has said.
"The reduction was due to lower price levels for food which dropped by 0.19%," a statement from the bureau said.
Annual core inflation, which excludes food prices, fuel, electricity and metered water, behaved similarly sliding to 6.2% from 6.4% in October.
Meanwhile monthly headline inflation went up by 1.0% compared to the 1.3% rise in October. But food prices went up in most centers except in Kampala and Arua where there food price reductions.
High food prices pushed the October inflation rate to 5.5% from 5% in September registered in September.
Food prices went up in most centres during October with significant contributions coming from matooke, irish potatoes, cassava, fruits and vegetables like passion fruits, pineapples, cabbages, tomatoes and dried beans, the bureau said then.

Original_Search_Title: The Man Who Drove the Queen
Headline: Uganda: The Man Who Drove the Queen
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRADUATION, wedding day, the birth of a child .. Such are the experiences that will forever be special and memorable in many people's lives. But much as some of these experiences are shared, Salim Twalib Khalili's was exceptional - driving the queen of the United Kingdom and Ireland, during the just concluded Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM).
Khalili chauffeured Queen Elizabeth II, who was here to open the November 23-25 CHOGM, together with her husband Phillip, the Prince of Edinburgh.
The couple jetted into the country on November 22 and Khalili was their only driver until they left on November 24.
"I have driven some important people like Baroness Lynda Chalker. I have also been driving the British High Commissioners for 10 years. But driving the Queen and her husband was different. The feeling was overwhelming," Khalili adds.
"You lose the status of an ordinary driver. Your scale weighs heavier. It's like buying 1kg of meat and another quota of 3kgs; they weigh differently. For me, the queen was like 10kgs of meat - much, isn't it?" he adds.
Khalili did not have much conversation with the royals save for the curtesy greeting and the handshake, but that was enough for him.
"I had a handshake with the queen, then her husband, when I first met them at the airport and while bidding them goodbye. I felt very good just greeting her. It was a feeling of a social elevation.
Then I opened the door for her and my job was rolling," he says.
The aura with which Khalili speaks tells you that he is no ordinary achiever in his capacity.
Born on December 12, 1959 in Bombo to Salim Khalili Tanda and Margaret Nalugya (RIP), this former police officer had never thought of rubbing shoulders with the global cream like the queen of England.
Khalili who studied at the then Bombo Sudanese Primary School says he dropped out of school after Primary Seven and opted to join the Police force in 1979 because he did not love books much.
Call him a school dropout, but he speaks fluent English well enough to pass for a graduate. His accent is also clear, actually British inclined probably because of close interaction with his employers.
The light-skinned, medium-size father of seven is talkative and full of emotion. Khalili gives you the impression of an honest man who loves his job.
He speaks with a rich body language, often waving his hands in the air, shrugging and occasionally laughing. He loves to engage the people he is talking to and cares to know if you really understand what he is saying.
"I learnt to drive while in the police force and in 1985 joined the British High Commission as a driver," he reveals. One year later, the commission, like it does for all its drivers, sent him to the UK for a special driving course.
Even when the second and third training-which he undertook in July this year came, Khalili did not know he was destined to drive the queen until about one month to her coming.
"They first took me for a refresher course in the UK, telling me that I was going to drive a very important person, but I never imagined it would be the queen," he says.
When his supervisor called him on his return to announce the 'important guest', Khalili could not believe it. "I thought I was dreaming. I asked my supervisor over and over again, 'are you sure? who am I to drive the queen?"', he recalls happily.
Khalili says he emerged from the office with a very wide smile, talking to himself only to arouse the curiosity of all his colleagues who admired him for his luck.
His worry was how he was going to approach the queen.
I went and asked the commissioner and he tipped me telling me, 'almost the same way you have been with me. Only that you will have to be more careful and respectful', he recounts.
'I went and told my wife about my pending assignment and she was so proud of me. She assured me that I was fit enough for the job," he says.
But when he told his grandfather about it, his stake in the community's history went higher.
Apparently, in 1954, when the Queen first visited Uganda, it is still a man from his Nubian community, Idi Asuman, who was driving the then governor Sir Andrew Cohen in state house, who chauffeured her.
Asuman reportedly drove the queen from the airport, to Owen falls dam (Nalubale), which she commissioned and Queen Elizabeth National Park that is named after her.
This was historic for the minority Muslim group, which migrated from southern Sudan in the early 1900, predominantly settling in Bombo.
In 1962, another Nubian-Sebi Farusi Kamjara, lowered the British union Jack flag and hoisted the Ugandan flag when Uganda was getting her independence.
"I have been an addition to those achievers in my community.
In Bombo SS, my children are now popularly known as the queen's kids and everyone wants to shake my hand because I drove the queen," Khalili boasts.
And as an honour, the community gathered to have a special meal called Garusa; a mixture of maize flour and a lot of baking flour with a bit of sugar, kasera (mixture of maize flour, baking flour and cassava flour), crowning it with a big cock.
Nevertheless, every special occasion never misses a dark cloud.
"I feel sad that my mother, who passed away in 1971, could not be around to celebrate my life achievement. Even my dad is bedridden right now. I'm praying so hard he gets well and gets to know that I drove the queen," he says.
But what does he remember most about his 'royal stunt'? "Driving from Entebbe to Kampala felt like forever. The roads were so jammed. But the crowd was so cheerful, welcoming and well-behaved. I felt proud being Ugandan.
"I remember being so conscious and praying for no mishaps. If a tyre burst, or an accident. it would have been very shameful for me, but I thank God that it all went well. I feel so brave to have handled such a mighty person successfully," he says.
John Hamilton, the 2nd secretary at the British High Commission in Kampala, says that their decision to hire Khalili to drive the queen was entirely dependent on his experience.
"He is one of the most experienced drivers we have," Hamilton says, adding, "otherwise most of our drivers often undertake those special courses in basic driving skills, how to drive in convoys, invasive and defensive driving, and he did the job perfectly."
And while the rest of us scramble to buy the queen's picture on streets, Khalili boasts of an autographed photo of the couple, proudly hanging on his wall.
"They gave it to me in the car, after their dinner in Serena Hotel in Kampala," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Fed By the Bus
Headline: Uganda: Fed By the Bus
Body_Text:
Kampala — From drivers and conductors to cleaners and gatemen, dozens of Ugandans depend on the bus industry for their livelihood, Harriet Birungi discovered
RUNNING after a Gaso bus which is just arriving at the Baganda Bus Park in Kampala, Alex Twinamatsiko, a luggage carrier, tries to catch the attention of the alighting passengers. Banging his hand on the side of the bus he calls out:
suburb of Kampala, he is a married man with four children back in the village in Bushenyi District.
"I send them money twice a month," he reveals after a bit of probing.
How much he earns a day ferrying cargo to and from the bus park depends on how far he has to carry the luggage and the number of customers.
"From the bus park to the new taxi park, I charge sh500 while to the old park I charge sh1,000," he says. "If I have to go a bit further I charge sh2,000 or more," he says, adding that his fees also vary with the weight of the luggage and the amount of traffic on the roads.
"For instance, to carry a sack of matooke to Kisenyi, I charge sh4,000 as there is heavy traffic in that area," he says. "My reasoning is that I will spend longer on the trip and miss out on other customers at the bus park."
When setting his rates, Twinamatsiko also has to take into consideration the fact that he needs to pay the sh15,000 registration fee that allows him to operate from inside the bus park.
From his unstable returns, on top of the registration fee, he has to pay the park operators a sh1,500 retainer per week to make sure his job is secure.
Twinamatsiko's meals consist of energy-giving foods.
He is always careful not spend more than sh1000 on food. "I mainly eat posho, cassava, potatoes and beans," he says.
Twinamatsiko is not the only one earning a living off buses.
As a Kalita Transporters bus pulls into a washing bay on Rubaga road, a young man, with great excitement, assembles the tools of his trade - a yellow jerrycan which has been cut in half, water, detergent and a piece of foam tied up in an old T-shirt.
Then he quickly gets to work washing the bus. He will be paid sh10,000 for washing the exterior and sh20,000 if he does the interior as well.
Luckily, he does not have to pay any fees to make sure his job is secure. He simply has to show up the following day, or else someone new will be recruited.
"I only need to pay the boss a certain percentage of my earnings," he says.
"It's like some kind of rent for using his land," the cleaner, who does not want to be named, says, as colleagues warn him not to reveal the exact percentage they pay.
The cleaner adds that he is not married, but has many dependants who are AIDS orphans. Three of these dependants stay with him in Ndeeba, a suburb of Kampala while eight are in Rakai. "With the money I earn, I buy maize flour and send it to them by bus," he says as tears form in his eyes.
From hawkers, cargo handlers, brokers, security guards, receipt issuing officers, gate controllers, bus drivers and owners to turn boys, bus inspectors, park managers, garage service providers and the director of the bus terminal, a bus feeds many people.
Charles Namanya is the bus terminal manager. He says a bus officially employs four people - the turn boy, conductor, inspector and bus driver.
These get their pay from the business profits, on terms agreed upon with the bus owner.
A turn boy is in charge of loading and offloading passengers' cargo. "He must be registered with that particular bus so as to ensure trust amongst all parties that serve and are served by the bus," explains Namanya.
There are as many turn boys as buses. If a bus travelled without one, there would be chaos, with some people taking other people's property.
James Lubo, who has been a gateman for five years, compares a turn boy to a store- keeper or logistics officer. A turn boy's memory has to be very sharp, for him to remember which luggage belongs to whom.
However, like anyone else they make mistakes. "Many times passengers have come to us complaining that they have lost their luggage. The safety of cargo requires the combined effort of both passenger and turn boy," Lubo adds.
At the end of the day, a turn boy takes home sh3,000 per trip the bus makes. Depending on the season, he will on average take home sh6,000 a day.
Edward Ssabiti started out as a hawker dealing in electronics before he became a turn boy. He says to become a turn boy you have to prove yourself.
To become a turn boy, he says, you have to lobby the bus conductor, usually by paying him a commission. As a turn boy, one has to pay the operators of the bus terminal sh20,000 and they in turn issue you with an identification card. In addition, you pay a sh3,000 retainer monthly.
Namanya says the bus conductor, who issues receipts, also earns per trip. However, he is not paid until the bus is full and ready to set off. If he is a miser and unwilling to pay brokers to convince passengers to board the bus, he may never leave the terminal.
"You have to give the broker between sh500 and sh1,000 as an incentive. That way, the bus will fill quickly and the conductor will earn his sh5,000," explains the manager.
However, the payment is not immediate. The conductor has to wait until the sh25,000 park fee charged on each bus is paid. If there is not enough money to raise the park fee, the conductor has to forfeit his pay, says Namanya.
Conductors pay sh20,000 as registration fee to the terminal operators. The bus driver and inspector are meant to make sure that every passenger has a seat.
They are also in charge of servicing the bus and making sure it is roadworthy. They are paid sh20,000 per route and also have to pay a sh20,000 registration fee.
Unlike the others, however, they do not have to pay a monthly retainer. "Their daily contribution of transporting passengers safely and in keeping the bus in a good condition, is proof enough that they want their jobs," says Ssabiti.
To ensure that every bus pays the required fees and nothing is stolen in the bus park, the terminal operators employ security guards and receipt-issuing officers. Namanya says these are paid on a weekly basis. "Security officials employed at the terminal receive their sh15,000 pay every Saturday."
Before they leave the bus park, passengers often buy goods from the hawkers who sell anything from electronics and medicine to cosmetics, food and bread.
The hawkers are only allowed to operate after paying a registration fee to the terminal chairman, says Lubo. Their fees vary according to the products they sell. For instance, a hawker selling non-perishables pays an initial fee of sh20,000 and a weekly fee of sh3,000 to keep their positions, while food and bread vendors pay sh10,000 and a weekly fee of sh1,500.
Namanya says as the terminal manager, he takes home sh200,000 monthly while the bus owners pocket sh300,000 after all daily bus expenses have been met. Expenses include fuel, washing, servicing and repairing.
"To say that it's only the bus owners that reap from a bus would be wrong. More than six categories of people live off a bus," says Namanya.

Original_Search_Title: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Headline: Nigeria: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Body_Text:
Abuja — When Bibi Bakare-Yusuf holds her two reviewers' copies of Kemi's Journal—one printed in India and one in Nigeria—side by side, the difference is night and day.
Abidemi Sanusi's 2005 release looks vibrant with its red Indian-printed cover depicting Kemi's world—her thoughts, her person, her attitude. The same book, printed in Nigeria, looks a bit dull. The blue hues fade into the surroundings and the thinner paper inside smears slightly from ink that is not quite refined enough.
Cassava Republic, Nigeria's newest literary publishing company, prints in India. Bakare-Yusuf, its founder, says she wants its books to rival those published in London and the United States—not just in literary quality, but in appearance as well.
Cassava Republic is unique in Nigeria, not only for its quality printing but because it exists at all. When Bakare-Yusuf returned to Nigeria after a period of living in the United Kingdom, she was astonished. Literature and its publishers had all but disappeared, leaving religious works, self-help books, and textbooks to fill the shelves.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
The mood had changed, too. "I found it upsetting," she said. "Someone will spend 5,000 naira on a self-help or management book, but ask them to pay 850 naira for a novel and they will moan and groan."
Nigeria was once home to a flourishing market, but years of military rule pushed international publishers out; not a single one remains in the country today. Economic decline hit the pockets of average Nigerians hard and their budget for luxuries such as books even harder. Thousands from the chattering middle class left the country, including the intellectuals and consumers of Nigerian literature.
"It was the Sahara Desert period of Nigerian literature," says Ken Ike Okere, a co-founder of the Abuja Literary Society. "We didn't know if people were writing because we were not seeing it." Newspapers suffered too; while any of three large papers could sell 500,000 copies a day in the 1970s, today all the papers together are lucky to sell 350,000.
With no retail market and no capital, most publishers now survive by printing textbooks or religious publications, which have guaranteed sales to schools and churches. The market for literature is tougher, and most works that do appear are self-published. Distribution is limited and costly. Most publishers focus on urban centers because the cost of transport and the small market make sales elsewhere unprofitable.
With the exodus of intellectuals and the books they read, many aspiring writers in Nigeria have had no opportunity to learn from their peers. It is no surprise then, that Bakare-Yusuf has struggled to find good manuscripts from the 50 or so she receives every week.
"Think about Yoruba drum training," she says as an example. "It takes years to be a master drummer, years of listening to others, so why should writing be any different?"
Today, Bakare-Yusuf thinks she might just know how to reverse the decline.
"I'm interested in the bottom of the pyramid; the top can go to London to buy books. If I can get books to the masses, conversations about literature will start, a questioning spirit will develop," she explains.
So Cassava Republic is cutting prices and boosting quality. Books like Kemi's Journal that stand out on the shelf cost just 650 naira, compared to the average price of 1,000 naira.
That has meant taking a smaller profit—or none at all. But it's the future market in which Bakare-Yusuf and her company are interested. Once the masses start reading, demand will pick up, she says.
Elite book stores with proper shops are out. Instead, books are being bought in rackety shops outside city walls. In Abuja, where Cassava Republic is based, that means leaving the tidy streets of the capital and heading for the suburbs, where most people live in mud and metal houses. It is on the winding streets of Maraba, Kuje, and Nyanya villages that Nigerians are buying books.
Cassava Republic's raw material is different also. Its books are new, catchy, and accessible. Sanusi is just one of a handful of writers that Bakare-Yusuf has found herself. Another, Teju Cole, author of the forthcoming Every Day is for The Thief, had a blog that caught the company's attention.
And while some titles, including Cain Prize winner Helon Habila and Orange Prize winner Diana Evans, are reprinted from abroad, Cassava Republic has turned down offers from international publishers to reprint.
"I'm interested in authors who engage with the African experience—it's not enough just to have an African author," she explains. "They must be able to speak to the complexities of African realities." Soon, Cassava Republic hopes to re-publish The Hidden Star, by the young South African author, K. Sello Duiker.
Someday soon, more of those authors may come from Nigeria itself. As part of her work, Bakare-Yusuf invites authors whose manuscripts are turned down to take part in writing workshops and join a mailing list of tips and events. Elsewhere in Nigeria, others are doing the same. The Abuja Literary Society also edits manuscripts and helps authors with their work.
All this, says Bakare-Yusuf, is about getting deeper than literature: "When you ask someone to read literature, you are asking them not only to look into the past but to look into the future—to suspend their beliefs today." That just might help Nigeria think its way out of more than just the end of a novel.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Headline: Zambia: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Body_Text:
Ndola — BOY Bwalya does not know any of the young children in the neighbourhood because he is always indoors at his parents' home in Ndola.
When his parents receive visitors, Bwalya who is only five years old is locked up in the bedroom until the visitors leave.
Bwalya's predicament comes forth because he is mentally retarded as a result of the cerebral palsy he has been suffering from since he was born.
In many parts of the world, and Zambia included, there are many children who are denied the right to good living because of their conditions as cerebral palsy victims.
Cerebral palsy (sometimes known as little's disease) is a condition characterised by weaknesses of certain muscles commonly in the legs coupled with stiffness and awkward jerking movements.
There may be convulsions, impaired speech and mental deficiency but in many cases mentality remains quite normal.
The cause of cerebral palsy is not always clear. Damage to the brain accruing prior to birth or during the birth process may cause it.
Because of the stigma associated with children born with this affliction, a physiotherapist, Archie Hinchcliffe who worked in Zambia in the late 1980s formed a non-governmental organisation based in the United Kingdom (UK) known as Cerebral Palsy Africa (CPA) to improve the lives of the children with this ailment.
Cerebral Palsy Africa is active in Zambia and two weeks ago, a group of physiotherapists from Scotland came to Lusaka and Ndola to train physiotherapists advanced methods of care for the children with the ailment.
According to physiotherapists, manifestations of the disease may be noted first when the child is about six months old, the evidence being a delay in the ability to sit up, crawl and stand.
At Ndola Central Hospital where the training was conducted for two weeks, the hospital's executive director, Dande Malawo said the course which was on for the past two weeks entailed trainers going out to the communities to teach parents how to take good care of their sick children and there was also actual training for physiotherapists.
"This Cerebral Palsy Africa was started by Mrs Hinchcliffe in Scotland in 2005. Mrs Hinchcliffe who was the wife of the British High Commissioner in Zambia, Peter Hinchcliffe, was a physiotherapist who worked in Zambia in the late 1980s. She decided to form the Cerebral Palsy Africa for the children with emphasis on improving mobility so that they could engage themselves in activities of daily living. Cerebral Palsy Africa carries out activities in various parts of Africa which include training physiotherapists advanced methods of care for the children," said Dr Malawo.
The handling of the case also include speech training, muscle re-education, the wearing of braces, special tutoring in school and vocational guidance.
The main aim of Cerebral Palsy Africa is to encourage local people to support children with the disease and providing them with training, awareness raising and to help them with skills.
The training also involves the community making assistive devices using cardboards and cassava flour.
Zambia Society of Physiotherapy, northern region vice president, Mary Mumba said society was working with cerebral palsy children for the programme to progress well.
"Our society is working with cerebral palsy children to see to it that there is a link between the community and health facilities to ensure early intervention of help by making sure that physiotherapists are posted to local clinics and rural areas so that people there benefit," Ms Mumba said.
She appealed to the Government to assist in workshops like the one physiotherapists held in Lusaka and Ndola in order to fight the stigma associated with cerebral palsy.
Community Based Rehabilitation (CBR) members who attended the courses at NCH were pleased that there were people who were concerned and were willing to assist the children with cerebral palsy problems.
One of the members, Godfredah Bilali of Ndola's Chifubu Township said it was important that people who had children suffering from cerebral palsy took them to centres for attention.
"People should bring to centres for CBR, mentally retarded and cerebral palsy children. Many parents prefer keeping their children indoors for fear of the unkown. These children are not a curse. They are just like any other children and they need to be cared for," Ms Bilali said.
The Kitwe based community, Wukwashi wa nzambi, which helps in making assistive devices and wheelchairs for children with deformities said parents with cerebral palsy children willing to be assisted with these equipment could contact them at Chimwemwe Christian Brethren or Chritian Mission in Many Lands in Kitwe or call Henry Mutembu on 097-9233545.
Esther Mundia Muimo who has a cerebral palsy child said it was good for CPA to teach care-givers how to make simple assistive equipment for the cerebral palsy children.
"We parents with cerebral palsy children are grateful because they are teaching us how to be self-reliant and finding ways and means to help the children improve in their sitting positions. It is good that people are coming on board to help us.
It is forums like these where we can share our problems and try to break the stigma associated with cerebral palsy. We want the people in the community we live to get involved and together go forward and integrate the children in society," said Mrs Muimo.
A cerebral palsy child, Temba Mundia Muimo, a grade 11 pupil at Kabulonga Boys in Lusaka who spoke on behalf of other cerebral palsy children appealed to people to stop stigmatising them and instead see what they can do to help them.
Temba also appealed to the Government to create facilities that were user-friendly for the people that are wheelchair- confined and people who use clutches because in the end they must realise that they were also stakeholders in society.
"Our appeal to the Government is to see what they can do to help in the education sector for some of us who are in Government schools. They should introduce teachers who have done special education to help us because in most cases, we are slow learners and we need time to catch up in lessons," said Temba.
CPA's efforts to help children with cerebral palsy needs the support of all.

Original_Search_Title: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Headline: Nigeria: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The once colossal New Nigeria Development Company [NNDC] is set for a comeback as the Northern Governors Forum (NGF) has approved its recapitalization to the tune of N5 billion.
NNDC, founded in the 1950s by the old Northern Regional government, is owned by the 19 Northern states today.
Chairman of NNDC's board of directors Professor Halidu I. Abubakar, who disclosed this at the corporation's 39th Annual General Meeting at Unity Hall, Hamdala Hotel, Kaduna at the weekend, said there will be an additional injection of N250 million by each of the 19 owner states.
Professor Halidu said seventeen of the nineteen governors have already signed irrevocable standing payment orders (ISPO) for the money to be deducted at source from their share of the Federation Account. The ISPOs have been forwarded to the Federal Ministry of Finance, he said.
Commenting on the company's investment profile, Professor Halidu said NNDC has embarked on the pursuit of its telecommuni-cation project in earnest. He said a company, Gamji Telecom Limited, has been incorporated with Corporate Affairs Commission and an operating license obtained from Nigeria Communications Commission (NCC). He said as at March 2007, NNDC's total investment in telecommunication stood at N330 million.
The chairman also said NNDC has completed feasibility studies on the cassava project, which is expected to be set up in five owner states as soon as land is made available. A request for the provision for land will be sent to selected govern-ments and this is to boost the Federal Government's initia-tive on cassava production as well as to create employment opportunities for the teeming youths in the region, he said.
According to Halidu, the challenges of the economic reforms, which are a relatively harsh operating environment and increase in the cost of operations, affected NNDC's results for the period under review. He said gross earnings stood at N360 million. A loss of N1.4 billion on ordinary activities after taxation, exceptional and extra -ordinary items of was recorded during the year under review, as compared to last year's figure of N107.96 million.

Original_Search_Title: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Headline: Uganda: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA, a land gifted with breathtaking sights, is popularly referred to as the Pearl of Africa. It is described as a place gifted by nature, hospitable people with over 40 tribes with distinct cultural attributes, 10 national parks, warm climate and mind-blowing landscapes. Raphael Okello explores some of the attractions, arguably referred to as Uganda's wonders.
NATURAL WONDERS
The Rwenzori Mountains - snow in the tropics
The splendid world of snow and glaciers on the Rwenzori Mountains in Kasese, 270 kms from Kampala, has been a tourist attraction for hundreds of years. Since its re-opening in 2001, tourist figures have steadily risen.
In 2005, there were 800 visitors. Rwenzori is home to one of four remaining tropical ice fields outside the Andes (the highest mountain range in South America). In 1994, the United Nations Education Scientific Cultural Organisation gazetted it as a world heritage site.
Emerging from the Rift Valley floor to a wintry altitude of 5,109m, the Rwenzori's 120km-long ranges (the longest in Africa), support large tracts of evergreen vegetation and bamboo forests. The higher moorland zone is dressed with a unique cover of giant heathers, lobelias and groundsels. Its highest peak, the Margherita (5,109m), is the third highest in Africa and the mountain is considered the highest non-volcanic mountain in Africa.
Sempaya Hot springs - field of boiling water
The Sempaya Hot springs in Semiliki National Park in Bundibugyo, about 450km from Kampala, eject a cloud of steam seen as far as two kilometres. According to Uganda Wildlife Authority, Sempaya's water temperature -over 100ºC- is above the maximum temperature of most hot springs in the world (about 50ºC).
Tourists have been seen boiling eggs, cassava and green bananas in the two geothermal heated springs. The first one is a pool measuring 12 metres in diameter and the second is a field of geysers, both oozing steamy sulphur-scented waters reputed to have healing powers.
The River Nile - the world's longest river
In 1862, John Speke and Grant discovered the source of the Nile, the world's longest river (4,180 miles long) at Jinja, 87km east of Kampala. "It was a sight that attracted one to it for hours - the roar of the waters, the thousands of fish leaping at the falls with all their might ... hippos and crocodiles lying sleepily on the water ... as interesting a picture as one could wish to see," Speke described it.
More than 140 years later, the site has changed significantly. The source may have lost its sparkle, but the Nile has given birth to some of the world's most breathtaking and powerful rapids and waterfalls.
Bujagali and Itanda in Jinja are considered among the world's most spectacular white-water rafting locations and Murchison Falls, because of its deadly ferocity, is unchartered.
MAN-MADE WONDERS
Kasubi Tombs
This architectural masterpiece, built with wood, thatch, reed, wattle and daub, was used as a palace in 1881 by Kabaka Mutesa I of the Buganda Kingdom. After his death in 1884, the thatched dome at Kasubi Hill in Kampala, was converted into a royal burial ground for three other kings of Buganda - Daudi Chwa, Mwanga and Mutesa II.
With a diametre of 31 metres and a height of 12 metres, it has arguably been regarded as the largest mausoleum in Africa.
This colossal cultural monument, a prominent feature on the catalogue of travel agencies and tour operators, sits on 30 hectares. The site's main significance is in the spiritual value that the Baganda attach to it.
Nyero Rock Paintings
Widely recognised as some of the finest and most dramatic rock paintings in East Africa, the Nyero Rock paintings in Kumi district (eastern Uganda) are surrounded by intrigue and mystery. No one knows who drew the paintings or why they were drawn.
What is sure is that the geometric paintings in red, white and purple pigments, were drawn about 400 years ago. Some people have put claim to ancient yellow hairy bushmen whom they say once lived in the area.
They are also considered a thread of evidence proving that South African Bantu speakers migrated from Africa's inter-locustrine region as similar paintings have been sighted down south.
Either way, the paintings are a silent voice seeming to tell us about the life and traditions of our ancestors.
Kabaka's Lake - a 'mad' king's project
A king's unfeasible whims gave birth to a lake said to be the only excavated lake in East Africa and one of the biggest on the continent - the Kabaka's Lake at Ndeeba, Kampala. It covers two square kilometres and is about 200 feet deep.
Kabaka Mwanga II (1884-1888), the architect of this small lake wonder, had a penchant for swimming, canoeing and fishing. Whenever he desired to engage in any of these water sports, he was carried 11kms from his official palace in Mengo to his lakeside palace at Munyonyo.
A story is told that in 1887, during his return from Munyonyo, the king thought that if a lake were to be constructed linking the two palaces, the journey would be more direct and less cumbersome.
The lake would also serve as an escape route in the event of armed conflict with the British or other territorial enemies. It seemed like a wild conception, but with handheld hoes, everyone from chiefs to their subjects, were involved in the intensive labour.
Many died due to hunger, accidents, disease and fatigue. Eleven months after the start of construction, a rebellion by Christian converts in 1888 led to abrupt stoppage of work that had reached Najjanankumbi, two kilometres from Mengo. The lake has continued to exist in more or less the same state since 1888. It contains several species of fish like tilapia and lungfish as well as reptiles.
The Bahai Temple - Africa's mother temple
Built more than 40 years ago, the Bahai Temple is like a crown on the crest of Kikaya Hill, four miles from Kampala, off Gayaza Road. The soaring temple, located on 70 acres of tree-studded gardens, is one of a kind in Africa.
The splendid dome-shaped temple is among nine of the only Bahai temples in the world. Like other Bahai temples, the temple is nine-sided with nine corresponding doors to signify nine religions of the world. It rises 124 feet high and covers a diametre of 44 feet. Its green conical dome is made of tiny glazed mosaic tiles.
Inside, nine big pillars and 27 smaller ones help to support the temple. It took four years of building before it was opened to the public on January 15, 1962. It accommodates up to 800 people.
Others
The mountain gorillas of Bwindi Impenetrable Forest, Lake Bunyonyi (a crater lake) and Queen Elizabeth National Park, all in western Uganda.=

Original_Search_Title: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Headline: Ghana: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Body_Text:
Accra — One of the leading contenders in the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP) presidential race, Mr Alan Kojo Kyerematen, has stated that he would sweep over 60% of the votes at their December 22 Congress in Accra. "I say this with all humility that anybody who knows my track record, background, will confirm that when I talk confidently like this, I know why I am saying that."
Though Alan does not rule out a possible alliance with other candidates should there be a run-off, he strongly believes he has what it takes to win in the first round. To him, "there is ample evidence which assures me that I am going to win the race. Opinion polls have put me ahead of my opponents," he said. At a maiden media interaction in Takoradi, Mr Alan Kyerematen popularly referred to as 'Alan Cash' noted that his government would promote grass root capitalism where wealth would be created for the majority to enjoy. "The issue of poverty will be a thing of the past under Alan's presidency," he noted, adding that his leadership would transform Ghana's economy. He contended that he had what it takes to catapult Ghana to a middle-income status.
He promised to focus on key areas such as industrialization, Information Communication and Technology, Infrastructural development and tourism promotion. These four areas according to him were interrelated to his slogan 'Job for the people - Cash in your pocket' which would help his government achieve what he calls 'grass root capitalism, creating wealth for the majority of the people.' He noted, this meant that he would be moving from the social sector to that of productive where the country seemed to be performing poorly. "So it will be counter productive talking about educational transformation, health, macro economic development and good governance since that has already been achieved under the Presidency of Mr John Kufour".
Turning his attention on the Presidential Special Initiative, which was under his Ministry, Mr Alan Kyerematen noted that the achievement of the sitting government could not be counted without the mention of the PSI, in which he played a major role. He therefore denied that the initiative had failed saying, "If there is a significant achievement of this government that it will be proud of, it is the PSI."
According to him the primary objective of the PSI was to diversify the economy, which was concentrated on cocoa, gold and timber. "The PSI was meant to expand the economy through a diversification portfolio and to some extent that has been achieved." He mentioned the revival of the garment factories, the transformation of both the salt and the cassava industries, which has led to the creation of jobs for the majority of Ghanaians.
This is evident in the expansion of the production of cassava, which rose to 60,000 hectares and the mining of about 3.5 million tones of salt, annually. The continuous claim by his detractors to the effect that the PSI failed is never correct, he argued. Meanwhile reports reaching us at press time yesterday indicated that the publisher of the Chronicle group of newspapers, Nana Kofi Coomson met with Mr. Alan Kyerematen at the Goil filling station in Cape Coast on Tuesday, this week. Alan Cash was on his way from Takoradi with his campaign team. The publisher who had earlier apologized to Alan for failing to extend his sympathy to him after his recent accident in the Brong-Ahafo had fruitful discussions about the latter's candidature.
Dr. Osei, one of Alan's campaign team members did not however take kindly to the decision by his boss to meet with Kofi. Dr. Osei accused the publisher of writing dirty things about, Alan promising that he would never allow him to see (Kyerematen) when he assumes office as President of Ghana in January 2009.
Present at the meeting were Messrs. Partact, Efo Philipo of north Dayi constituency, Micheal Ampong among others. Mr. Coomson confirmed that he met with Alan and the above names when he was reached on his cell phone.

Original_Search_Title: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Headline: Nigeria: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Body_Text:
The Minister of State for Education (I), Dr. Anthony Jerry Agada on Tuesday 13th November inaugurated a 24-man National Action Committee on Read Project (NACORP).
In his opening remarks before the inauguration, the Minister commended the committee for their commitment and all the work they have done so far, from the conception of the project which is one of the Ministry's reform programme till date. He charged the committee not to rest on their oars but to deliberate extensively on strategies to employ to advance the course of the Read Project. Recounting his own experience as Permanent Secretary in Benue State where he had to give the needed support to all activities geared towards improving the reading culture, the "Book Minister as he is now fondly called reiterated the Ministry's desire to give the committee a 100% support to achieve their objectives. Dr. Mrs. Makoju, a Director in the Ministry in her address informed the committee that its activities will not be driven completely by the Ministry.
Hence, the need to work out ways of collaborating with the organized private sector to fund the read campaign activities. She however assured the committee that while no specific financial provision was made for the read project in the 2006/2007 budget, a moderate provision has been made for it in the 2008 budget to ensure that the project is not abandoned. Having been officially inaugurated, the committee re-assembled to deliberate on the work plan drawn up after the children and youth launch of the read campaign carried out by the administration of Dr. Oby Ezekwesili. After an extensive deliberation, the committee broke into five sub-committees namely: School Activities, Media/Advocacy, Professional Associations, Private Public Partnership and the Federal Ministry of Education.
Each group was charged with the responsibility of coming up with suggestions and strategies necessary to advance the course for wish they were assembled. The Nation Action Committee on Read Project (NACORP) comprises of all major stakeholders in the education and book industry among which are, the Association of Nigerian Authors, Nigerian Educational Research Development Council, Reading Association of Nigeria, Nigerian Publishers Association, Nigerian Booksellers Association, the National Library of Nigeria, Universal Basic Education Commission, the Nigerian book Project, Peniel Library, Cassava Republic Press, Pressfield Communications Limited, Education Trust Fund, Arojah Concepts and Culture Afrique among others.

Original_Search_Title: Taming the Garrison Commander
Headline: Nigeria: Taming the Garrison Commander
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yesterday, the self-styled strongman of Ibadan politics, Chief Lamidi Ariyibi Adedibu, was held by the Police and quizzed for about two hours.
He was subsequently charged to Abuja Magistrate Court in Abuja for alleged breach of public peace. He was granted bail and released.
The move by the Police to get at the controversial politician who for decades imposed his political agenda on Oyo State through intimidation and thuggery is a departure from the past. Adedibu had always been treated by officialdom as an outlaw who would get away with anything.
During the eight years of Obasanjo's administration, the notorious strongman reached the apogee of his political influence. He called the shots not only in his native Oyo State but also in some other states in the Southwest zone. In fact, the former President who is now the Chairman of Board of Trustees of PDP recently hailed Adedibu as 'the only father' of PDP. The party's national chairman, Dr. Ahmadu Ali similarly eulogised him as the 'Garrison Commander' of Ibadan politics. He declared that anyone who was not prepared to take orders from the revered kingmaker should pack his bag and baggage and leave town.
It was such statements that emboldened Adedibu in throwing caution to the wind to impose his warped political authority on the people. The fear of Adedibu was indeed the beginning of wisdom in Oyo State during the past nine years.
Alhaji Adedibu usually gave little or no regard for decorum while aiming for a victim. Former governor of Oyo State, Alhaji Rasheed Ladoja, got more than a fair dose of Adedibu's voodoo politics as he was literarily haunted and chased around until he was impeached. When the court later returned him, Adedibu and his henchmen could not give him any breathing space until Adedibu's anointed godson and Ladoja's deputy, Otunba Christopher Alao-Akala, was installed governor.
Feeding the Multitude
Adedibu was popular among the masses. Those who are hungry often go to his Molete residence and are fed. The regular meal served is amala, a popular Yoruba food made from cassava or yam flour. His generosity in feeding the multitude made him popular among the hungry masses. For this reason, there was always a crowd at his residence which was always at his beck and call. Many of them are miscreants and motor park touts known as "agbero" whom he easily mobilises. Thus Adedibu became known as an apostle of amala politics.
The politician has been variously extolled by his admirers. Praise singers are never in want around his residence and at any function he is attending. Some local musicians even composed songs in his praise. For instance, a prominent Ibadan musician described him as "an individual who is equal to half of Ibadan". The same musician described him as a man who would forcefully seize another man's chicken and as the owner of the chickens is crying and looking for his chicken, he (Adedibu) would be smiling and grinding pepper with which he would roast the chicken.
Adedibu's regular day begins like this. He wakes up at his Molete residence and takes majestic stroll to his expansive sitting room. There he holds court meeting his protégés, admirers, supporters and visitors. It was from that daily routine that he derived the title, 'Alaafin Molete'. At the Molete palace, the needy converged seeking one form of favour or the other from him.
He diligently attends to their requests one after the other. The usual requests range from introductory letters for contracts or employment, phone calls to people in government, financial assistance and the like. For those seeking elective or appointive positions in the state or even at the federal level, an anointing by Adedibu was vital.
Lawlessness
Adedibu typifies lawlessness, brigandage and terrorism. His thugs are dreaded throughout Oyo State and particularly in the state capital, Ibadan. He has an assemblage of broad-chested thugs who served as his private army. Some of them bore such nicknames as Tokyo, Sir K, Elewe-Omo, Destroyer and so on. They enforced his will as his word was their command. Some major lawless acts of his supporters include the following:
The Ladoja Experience
Adedibu made no pretence about the fact that he installed Rasheed ALadoja as governor. However, he fell out with Ladoja when he laid claim to a certain percentage of the state's monthly allocation of revenue. When Ladoja refused his demand and began to assert his independence of the Molete chief, he withdrew his support and declared war against him. He threatened to unseat him boasting that he had single-handedly installed and removed governors and speakers at whim in the state. He said he had limitless powers.
He was proved right as the group of State Assemblymen loyal to him mobilised sooner and impeached Ladoja. Before then, his hefty thugs had once sacked the Governor's office while the security personnel seemed helpless. Until yesterday, it might not be an overstatement to say that Adedibu was running a parallel government in Ibadan and was treated as somebody virtually above the law of the land.
NAFDAC
About two months ago, the Director-General of NAFDAC, Professor Dora Akunyili, lamented that Adedibu was frustrating the efforts of her agency in the fight against counterfeit drugs. According to Akunyili, who spoke in Ibadan, the octogenarian was using his thugs to shield importers of fake drugs in the state. She said each time NAFDAC officials sealed off a warehouse of fake drugs or other consumables, Adedibu would mobilise his men to reopen the warehouse by force. She said NAFDAC was simply helpless over the ugly trend and challenged the government and Police to do something.
Invasion of TV station
Adedibu's dreaded thugs once sacked a TV station in Ibadan while broadcast was in progress. The action was to forestall the announcement of the results of the local government election conducted by Ladoja. The election was not approved by Adedibu. It was later voided by his protege, Alao Akala, immediately he assumed power to the satisfaction of Adedibu.
Curiously, the Police never made any arrest following the TV station invasion. Over the years, particularly since the advent of the democratic dispensation, many people had been killed or seriously maimed in clashes with these thugs. Interestingly, there has been no reported arrest or prosecution of any of the culprits.
Abductions
Abductions and torture were among the methods employed by the thugs to beat his opponents into line. There was, for instance, the case of his men's abduction of Alhaji Layi Oladoja, a former director of O'odua Development Company. He was whisked away by the Adedibu men in his vehicle from the Eid praying ground in Ibadan. He was later rescued by the police. However, the police exonerated Adedibu saying he was not involved in the criminal act.
In all those lawless acts by Adedibu's me,n neither the Ibadan chief nor his men were arrested or interrogated by the Police. The only exception was the recent half-hearted arrest, interrogation and subsequent release of his chief helmsman, Tokyo (Lateef Akinsola), the chairman of NURTW, Oyo State chapter.
That Adedibu has all along been able to hold Ibadan and Oyo State to ransom is as a result of the support he enjoys from the Federal and State governments. Is his present ordeal a signal that the honeymoon is over or is it a mere window dressing to deceive Nigerians? With his immediate release after the charge, the likelihood is that he would return to a hero's welcome at Ibadan. He would likely continue to thumb-chest claiming that he cannot be dared by the Police.
For President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, this may be the acid test of his trumpeted rule of law policy.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Headline: Nigeria: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Ten persons believed to have violated environmental law in Kwara State were recently arrested and fined N1, 000 each .
The state government had recently banned street trading in unhygienic places in the state.
Eight of the offenders were arrested along Ahmadu Bello Way, while two were arrested at Ipata area.
The presiding judge of the mobile Grade I Area Court, Alhaji Ibrahim Abdullahi, released those who paid their fines while defaulters were ordered to be remanded at 'C' Division Police Station, Oja-Oba.
One Mrs Maria Peter was arrested for allegedly preparing local cassava food (fufu) in an unhygienic environment, and littering the main road with waste. She was also alleged to have rained abuses on health officers which made the judge to fine her N1, 500.00
Abdullahi said the fine became imperative to serve as a deterrent to those who do not keep healthy environment.
He therefore implored Kwarans to complement government's efforts by keeping their environment clean, and also dump refuse in roro bins provided by the waste management company.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Headline: Uganda: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Body_Text:
Kampala — UWA enforcement unit supervisor Steven Kimpwedde displays ivory pieces estimated at sh277m at their head offices. They were recovered by UWA in December 2006
ONLY elephants are supposed to wear ivory, so goes a popular slogan. But Wang Xiuli, a youthful Chinese, disrespected it. She was caught smuggling ivory recently. Wang was restless as customs officials checked her overweight bag in Busia.
Desparate, Wang gave a $20 (sh36,000) bribe to Nice Byarugaba, a customs official. Byarugaba declined it. Sensing danger, Wang ran and attempted to board a Kampala-bound taxi. But the officials caught up with her. They took her to Police and later to court, where she was charged for smuggling ivory.
"Court ordered Wang to pay a fine of sh10m," says Vincent Opyene in Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) law enforcement department. So far, Wang has paid sh2m. The court is holding her passport until she completes payment.
In the last six years, four large consignments of ivory were intercepted by UWA. However, "There is great suspicion that most traffickers have become smarter and ivory could be getting out of the country undetected," says Opyene.
About five years ago, a tonne of ivory was ground and mixed with cassava flour and smuggled to China. It was impounded in Shanghai.
Why is the trade continuing unabated?
According to a UWA official, in some cases, officials in charge of checking illegalities, turn a blind eye. "Such cases of illegal transactions in ivory have swallowed some of the security personnel who are supposed to be checking it," says a source that preferred anonymity. "There is no way such quantities can pass the check points without being detected."
Unclear circumstances
In the latest case, a consignment of ivory allegedly entered Uganda from the DR Congo through Burundi, but the trafficker and the ivory remained elusive until last week. According to a source, over 300 pieces of ivory were impounded three months ago and the trafficker arrested. They also arrested the trafficker suspected to be a Burundian, but he was released under unclear circumstances.
Poaching and corruption
Kampala has become the heart of illegal trade in ivory. The trade is masterminded by businessmen mainly from West Africa and China, who wield a lot of influence in the Uganda Police, the army and diplomatic circles.
Once, UWA used ivory and fake traders to penetrate the criminal gangs. "We thought we had got evidence and arrested the suspects on Friday and took them to Central Police Station, Kampala but after the weekend, the suspects were already out and mocking UWA," says a source. "There was no sufficient evidence to incriminate the suspects."
The hunters are hunted
All roads of illegal ivory from the DR Congo lead to Uganda because of the strong network of traders and security agencies.
According to sources in the wildlife trade, a former law enforcement officer in UWA, Sam Tugume, was on the verge of exposing the traffickers, but was arrested as a rebel of the People's Redemption Army.
In another incident last year, security personnel and the Uganda Revenue Authority impounded about 300kg of ivory at Kabusu in Kampala. But the culprits, believed to be part of rackets masterminded by West African businessmen, disappeared.
UWA and Government institutions building capacity
Over 30 officials recently convened at Kireka Sports View Hotel in Kampala at a meeting organised by UWA and Interpol on wildlife to share experiences with the Police, customs and immigration officials from Busia and Entebbe.
"We have to share experiences to break down the criminal gangs," said Karl Karugaba, working with the Lusaka Task Agreement. Karugaba, who worked with UWA for over a decade, remembers how frustrating it was to weed out criminals.
However, things are better and UWA is hot on the heels of the traders implicated in the illicit trade in ivory. "It is almost impossible to poach in the protected areas in Uganda," says Moses Mapesa, the executive director of UWA.
Mapesa attributes the illicit trade, which has left many elephants dead, to the political unrest in the DR Congo.
He says, this has created a safe haven for criminals disguised as investors to deal in ivory. As a result, endangered animals like elephants and rhinos have been butchered by gunmen in eastern DR Congo.
Fortunately, wildlife authorities in DR Congo are making most of the protected areas secure, according to Mapesa.
UWA's law enforcement department has increased its manpower, bringing on board people with skills in legal and intelligence gathering.
UWA now has an ivory detector that can be used to check the ivory in case it is mixed with other commodities like maize flour.
Byarugaba deserves a pat on the back because she did not accept Wang's $20 bribe. She let the law take its course and led to the recovery of ivory and Wang's arrest. Many people would be proud of being Ugandan if other Government officials acted like Byarugaba.

Original_Search_Title: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Headline: Uganda: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Body_Text:
The trial is like the field court martial of 1981--86 where the comrades compare contribution in a particular battle, ask why an individual is being punished for running away with some cassava tuber he uprooted yet the other who took a village girl and some chicken has been let free
When the Inspector General of Government (IGG), Justice Faith Mwondha, walked into the chief magistrates court on Buganda Road Monday last week, she was sure she had plugged the holes in her first major trial.
Having quietly brought on board senior prosecutor Michael Wamasebu, IGG Mwondha did not expect the walk-out of the lawyers for the suspects she is trying, former health minister Jim Muhwezi, his former junior colleagues Alex Kamugisha and Mike Mukula, and former State House youth-desk officer Alice Kaboyo.
The four are being jointly charged for various offences including abuse of office, causing financial loss, embezzlement, theft, obtaining money by false pretence, uttering false documents and other charges relating to the mismanagement of an immunisation fund worth $4m. Mwondha also did not anticipate that they would soon be running to the High Court to challenge the decision of the lower court's initial rulings on day one of the trial. But what does this mean for Ms Mwondha and the office she holds?
A former investigator in the IGG's office says the Muhwezi case is "a test case for the IGG." Mr Reagan Okumu, who has since joined active politics and is now a senior member of the opposition Forum for Democratic Change (FDC), believes Mwondha has put her office and personal credibility on the wire in this case. "The IGG is playing Museveni's game of whipping the people growing horns within the NRM," says Okumu.
His perception is shared by many but most importantly, the speed and commitment of the IGG to see it through is telling. She has for the moment ignored another file where all the three ministers are implicated relating to the Global Fund for HIV/Aids Malaria and Tuberculosis partly because, sources claim "it is a more complicated case."
Public expects conviction
"The public expects conviction and recovery; anything short of that will significantly undermine public confidence in the office of the IGG. If Mwondha succeeds in getting a conviction or a recovery then she will gain some credibility," Okumu said. Okumu, who is now MP for Aswa, worked in the IGG office before Mwondha came in.
Another official who worked at a senior level within the IGG's office during its formative years agrees with Okumu. The official, who preferred to remain anonymous for professional and ethical reasons, says he believes the conclusion of the case will be crucial as to whether Mwondha can retain whatever credibility she has. The public view of the IGG's office depends on the character of the person holding it, this official asserts.
The IGG office has walked a long road so far. Founded in 1986 shortly after the NRM came to power, Mr Augustine Ruzindana, was appointed first IGG via a radio announcement. Sources close to the appointing authority tell Inside Politics that there was never a formal appointment and public service worked backwards to formalise it at least two months after the initial public announcement. Ruzindana was deputised by Mr Waswa Lule, who was at the time still in exile in London and did not report to office until three years later. But even after he returned, he served only for a short time and resigned. Mr David Psomgen replaced him.
In 1996, Mr Ruzindana sought elective office and was elected MP for Ruhama County in Ntungamo District; he had resigned the office but the President was for a while reluctant to appoint a new office holder, which put Ruzindana in a dilemma to leave without handing over to anyone.
Later Mr Jotham Tumwesigye was appointed and Psomgen stayed as his deputy. Tumwesigye served two terms and quit in 2003. Again the President took his time before appointing a replacement until he finally announced the appointment of current office holder, Justice Mwondha, who had been Chief Magistrate in eastern Uganda.
Mr Ruzindana was for most of his 10 years in-charge pre-occupied with setting up the office, giving it traction both in the law and its public image. A source familiar with Mr Ruzindana's time as IGG tells Inside Politics that he worked more like the conscience of the young NRM government at a time when it was still filled with idealism, a hate for the way those before them had conducted the affairs of the country, a desire to do things differently and in disdain for many of the African leaders of the time.
He was therefore conscious not to wash all the dirty linen in public, preferring to avoid bare-knuckle battles. Ruzindana's time was also defined by a government still keen on building institutions and still frowned upon corruption which allowed him lee-way to formulate it out of the woodwork into an institution of international repute. Those who crossed Ruzindana's path testify that if a complaint was raised against you, he would summon you for a discussion and ask that you sort the matter out without much of a fuss. And they were willing to listen.
Tumwesigye largely carried on from where Ruzindana stopped but sought to assert the authority of that office a little more. He caused ripples when he allowed the media to publish declarations of leaders' assets and liabilities, opening a new chapter in the demand for leaders to explain their known and unknown wealth as the general public often raised debate over some leaders' known properties that were never made part of the official record.
He also cracked the whip especially among the lower rank civil servants, with reports that caused many district accounting officers to lose their jobs. But it was also during Tumwesigye's time that some of the people in government had realised that soft-talk never actually got them to lose anything and corruption had generally become part of normal life and leaders competed to have more knowing the repercussions were nil. The system was also still knit together.
Ms Mwondha came in at a different time demanding different approaches to deal with existing challenges. Her time coincided with the campaign to remove term limits and the visible discomfort of some the bush war heroes.
Unlike her predecessors, Ms Mwondha finds herself fighting more than just corruption, and the opposition she encounters is beyond guilty or not guilty but some sort of a vengeance by those who feel by going after the only space comrades in the struggle is also being invaded now.
Evoking the bush war every time the whip comes hard on those alleged to be corrupt is partly to say "why can't you keep the power and let us make some money" a doctrine that seemed to thrive over the years. Like Okumu says, action on sources of internal disharmony must be dealt with in a manner that will appeal to two distinct audiences; the internal audience of the ruling NRM and the external particularly the donor community.
Unfortunately for Mwondha, and what will make her case against the Muhwezi's a test-case and a game of ping-pong at the same time, is that those she is seeking to prosecute know they can use the political goals President Museveni is seeking to score to divert attention from the criminal aspects.
Justice Mwondha's person does not help matters either. Known to have wrecked the internal structures since the day she set foot on the sixth floor of IPS building; widened the rift with other players as well as alienating the media, her style of work makes her a character many in her firing line see as susceptible to being blown by the wind should the tide change and the political front is shifted.
Not spared of criticism
Like a former official at the IGG's office said, the holder of the office shapes its character and Mwondha will not be spared criticism as being hostile in her approach. She will not avoid accusation of being vindictive, especially given how she has pursued the likes of Ken Lukyamuzi and the Solicitor General (SG) Lucien Tibaruha and his immediate successor.
Does she attract admiration or disdain? The 'big fish' she has managed to drag out of office, through jail and now to court, will continue to try and show they are victims of an individual's desire to have his cake and eat it at the same time.
What Mwondha must be conscious of is that for many strugglists of the early 80s, the current trial is like the field court martial of 1981--86 where the comrades compare contribution in a particular battle, ask why an individual is being punished for running away with some cassava tuber he uprooted yet the other who took a village girl and some chicken has been let free. Unfortunately, the camaraderie that some people share obscures the bigger issues that concern the majority of Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
Headline: Kenya: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
