Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Festival of Creative Arts seems to have settled well into their new home, the Alliance Francaise auditorium in Nairobi, if the full houses they have been pulling are any yardsticks.
But more importantly, the organisers have not lost the art of selecting good comedies such as Robin Hawdon's Birthday Suite, which opened Friday and ends Saturday.
The farce has all the potential cocktail for a humorous melee. Put four impulsive people with very different reasons for being in two hotel rooms with an adjoining door that should be locked.
Add to it an excitable waiter and you have a recipe for explosive humour as the couples attempt to untangle themselves from their meshed relationships, tugging the waiter along with them.
But for the Festival, it will just be just another weekend since setting up shop two years ago. And the group has gradually made a name for itself as a formidable amateur theatre outfit about town since opening in 2005 with the comedy by Ray Galton and John Antrobus - When Did You Last See Your Trousers? directed by Sam Otieno and staged at the Kenya National Theatre (KNT).
"The Festival of Creative Arts was formed as a response to demands for quality play by local theatre-goers. We therefore identified plays that the public yearned for, and engaged experienced actors and qualified directors to sustain quality productions by investing in all aspects of production," says Eliud Abuto, the chairman.
To get a foothold in the precarious world of performing arts, Festival depended on the success of previous hit dramas like When Did You Last See Your Trousers?
"We began by adding professionalism to previously staged but popular plays as a way of pulling in crowds before staging plays by good, new playwrights," adds Abuto, who doubles up as the group's executive producer.
He has produced and adapted comedies and thrillers such as Eric Chapel's It Can Damage Your Health, a medical comedy directed by Sam Otieno at KNT in February last year, Jimmy Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava directed by Victor Ber at KNT and Giles Cole's Suspects, a thriller directed by Carole Odongo and Derek Benfield.
Another is A Fly in the Ointment that Arabron Osanya directed and staged at the Alliance Francaise where the group moved after two shows at KNT.
Since then, all their other plays including, Ray Cooney's Bedside Manners, Marc Carmoletti's Boeing Boeing and Derek Benfield's Look who's Talking, have been staged at the Alliance, also the home of Heartstrings Kenya, a trail-blazer in amateur theatre for 11 years, and still counting.
"Now we're staging new playwrights," says Abuto, referring to Dave Freeman's Kindly, Keep It Covered that the group staged in September, and Birthday Suite, their fifth production this year.
To keep the audience flocking for more, Festival casts a star-studded assembly from the pool of the best thespians and directors.
Examples are, in Birthday Suite, the indelible talents of Angel Waruinge, Nick Ndeda, Derrick Amunga, Mbeki Mwalimu (she first made her name in Where Did You Last See Your Trousers? and is the group's treasurer) and Francis Amos in the five hander farce.
"We hold quarterly auditions to pick actors that we may use over time. And those who have worked with us before stand a better chance, depending on the type of play," explains Abuto on cast selection.
Like most of their plays, its directed by veteran thespian Carole Odongo whose involvement in theatre dates back to 1995 in plays such as Meja Mwangi's The Plague, staged at the defunct Miujiza Players theatre, Nairobi.
"Directors are selected depending on the type of play and experience. We have set high standards and choosing the artistic director is carefully done. Currently though, Carole Odongo is the best artistic director in Kenya. She's very creative and is an excellent drama trainer," says Abuto, explaining why Carole has been their choice director in the bulk of the plays.
And if you enjoy watching plays, remember that they cost quite some money to produce. And although each play is unique and requires different types of stage sets, costumes, numbers of actors and props, on average, a quality show consumes up to Sh300,000, including publicity and other overheads.
Festival has, however, benefited from private companies who have recognised the quality of shows and the potential of theatre as a marketing tool. "Previous theatre groups took patrons for granted," says Abuto. "And companies wouldn't sponsor plays due to dismal turnouts, but currently, the patronage has increased, courtesy of professionalism as well as staging interesting, quality plays that theatre-goers want."
Kindly, Keep It Covered by Dave Freeman, was sponsored by IPAL who met the production cost, while the Nation Media Group took care of part of the advertising budget. "Festival was formed as a response to audience demands for quality and consistency," says Abuto; "and we've had a successful year. We see ourselves putting up plays every month from next year."
After Heartstrings and Unicorn Arts Productions, Festival is the third amateur theatre outfit offering quality shows and thus creating the much needed competition for good scripts, top talent and consistent audience.

Original_Search_Title: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Headline: Rwanda: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Body_Text:
Kigali — ABB, a Swedish firm and international leader in power and automation technologies is scouting Rwanda for investment opportunities in the energy sector.
Officials from the ABB Group of companies that operates in about 100 countries and employs 100,000 people were in Kigali last week together with a group of Swedish investors seeking opportunities in the energy sector.
During a trade meeting organized by the ministry of commerce and the Rwanda Investment and Export Promotion Agency (RIEPA), the area sales manager, southern Africa substations Mr. Stefan Kullander told East African Business Week that ABB is in position to help fill the gap in the energy deficit.
We are presently looking at the possibility of interconnecting countries so that the energy deficits in one country can be complemented by the surplus of another. We are looking at connecting Zambia, Tanzania and Kenya. Very soon I will be meeting the energy minister in Tanzania." said Kullander. The use of ethanol as a substitute for fossil fuel was discussed and one of the Swedish businessmen said his company can produce stoves and cooking utensils that run on ethanol.
Most African countries have the potential of producing ethanol not just from sugar cane waste but cassava by products and other waste.
According to RIEPA's director general, Mr. Francis Gatare, Lake Kivu has the potential of producing 700MW and its exploitation will benefit Rwanda and EAC members
The chairman of the private sector federation Mr. Robert Bayigamba who is a also a leading business man in the country called for the Swedes to consider investment in different sectors.
Rwanda, a country of 8.5million people seeks to turn itself into a middle income country by 2020.

Original_Search_Title: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Headline: Kenya: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Idd ul Fitr celebrations have provided the much needed respite from the packed political menu embellished by opinion polls and reeling from ODM Kenya presidential candidate Kalonzo Musyoka's "miracle" at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, last weekend.
The festivities broke to louder-than-normal calls by the Muadhin and as dawn broke on Saturday and as the bleating of goats being led to their slaughter filled the air in the Mombasa estates.
Huge pots of sumptuous biriani (a rice dish) served in huge platters and shared by groups, together with boiled goat meat and soup, was the order of the day as Muslims marked the breaking of the fast.
Idd is characterised by feting neighbours, irrespective of whether they are muslims or not. It is an occasion of one of the most remarkable outpourings of love I have ever witnessed.
This Idd was no different as my neighbours, the Alis, woke us up quite early in the morning with the Swahili sweet bread (mkate wa sinia) and home-baked doughnuts for our breakfast, and followed it up at lunch-time with biriani ya maini (rice with liver).
My eight-year-old son had his own version for the the joy: He spent the day at his friend Kipruto's birthday party next door alongside playmates.
His sister, on a half-term school holiday, was shooting the channels with remote controls in both hands, while their mother was busy with her own kitchen specials for the occasion.
On my part, I was catching up on my reading and nursing a king-sized one from a previous heavy night. Almost overnight, it was like a whistle had been blown to give the local residents some time off from the political hullabaloo to show some humanity.
Even at the Eid Baraza, which President Kibaki attended at Treasury Square, the Chief Kadhi was firm: No political campaigns - at least not overtly.
The only time we realised that the president was in town was when I and my "tribe" were diverted from State House Road as we drove to a popular family corner at Mama Ngina Drive as his motorcade left town. As we quaffed our madavu (coconut milk) and munched cassava crisps and roast maize, we were amazed at the serenity and hospitality of the day.
After days of rantings about political possibilities, Idd ul Fitr was like an oasis in the Kalahari.
My biggest surprise was when my new colleague, Mubarak, drove 15km to deliver to my family some sumptuous beef biriani cooked by his wife and mother.
Now the people are looking forward to the Diwali (a Hindu occasion) next week which, despite the extended fireworks, makes me realise how great and multicultural my immediate community is.
When my friends were fasting, I missed the "civilised" community who gather at the 100-year-old A.C. D'souza Bar at Kilindini, where nobody is interested in one's origin.
Kenyatta and Jamhuri Days as well as the voting day are on the way and I hope the people's Kenyanness will continue to flourish and show its superiority over the base sectarianism that threatens to consume us all.
Kenyans should not squander the goodwill that has made them what they are today.
On Wednesday, I met Mzee Pekeshe Ndeje, he of the "Similani!" chants at political rallies, and I gave him a lift to a rendezvous in a sudden rainstorm. Obviously enjoying his new-found popularity and accompanied by his two sons, he summed up the political scenario and how it should be approached:
"If you had eight children and five of them were complaining about the way you were treating them, it would be wise to listen to them since their disaffection might lead to the instability and failure of the whole homestead and even lead to death. Listen to all your children."

Original_Search_Title: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Headline: Uganda: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Body_Text:
Kampala — A person who imports counterfeit products is to pay three times the cost of the goods, the Uganda National Bureau of Standards' (UNBS) quality assurance manager, Deusdedit Mubangizi, has said.
"If you imported goods worth sh20m and they are found to be counterfeit, you will have to pay a penalty of sh60m," Mubangizi told reporters at a training session about the operations of the bureau at Nakawa, Kampala.
The training was part of activities to mark this year's World Standards Day under the theme "Standards and the Citizens: Contributing to Society."
He said archaic laws are responsible for the increase in the importation of counterfeit products.
"The law gives a penalty of between sh3,000 and sh30,000. We recently impounded 300 cartons of expired beers and the person paid only sh30,000," Mubangizi said.
He said a survey carried out by the bureau showed that problems in Uganda's market including sale of expired food products, tempering with expiry dates on food and food products labelled in foreign languages especially Arabic.
Others include products with high pathogenic organisms, sale of products that do not function and those that can injure consumers.
"We have "enterprising" Ugandans who pick empty bottles of Vim and fill them with cassava flour."
Meanwhile, 26 cartons (15,580 pieces) of sub-standard padlocks worth $9,862 (sh17m) are to be re-exported to Dubai, the bureau's head of import inspection has said.
Gyaviira Musoke said: "The consignment was inspected in July. It was found that a key could open several different padlocks, a threat to security, contrary to the requirement that padlocks shall be individually keyed."
According to the UNBS imports inspection and clearance regulations 2002, the 50mm padlocks specified as FF Italy are to be re-exported through the Malaba station.
The goods, which attract about sh8.6m in taxes, would be re-exported through the Uganda Revenue Authority's (URA) customs process and witnessed by the bureau's import officials.
The clearing agent, Novelty Agencies, will lodge a re-export entry with URA proving that the goods are re-exported in order to be refunded the taxes.
Sehil Kumar, the general manager of Max Imports and Exports, said: "In an effort to avoid total loss to an importer, we made an effort to take the samples to the country of origin for confirmation of the irregularities in the supplied products."
"We shall compel the supplier to meet the re-exportation costs and refund the money originally paid for the goods," Kumar said in a letter written to the UNBS executive director received on September 25.
The consignment was imported in container number FCIU 24105/3 by Max Imports and Exports whose offices are at Nabugabo Road in Kampala.
Vincent Ochwo, the UNBS head of engineering, said a genuine padlock should be resistant to rust when exposed to heat or cold.
Ochwo said the plug where the key enters should be firm (shouldn't come out of the padlock case when the key is pulled out).
Other products that were re-exported last month include 400 portable immersion water heaters and 100 cartons of Bint el Sudan Petroleum Jelly. The products had been imported by Robanne (U) Ltd and Makoha Peter respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Headline: Sierra Leone: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Body_Text:
Freetown — Massah Alpha, 23 was last week charged to court by the Family Support Unit (FSU), Kenema for allegedly burning the right hand of Regina Kallon, 11.
According to Police Constable 2531 Kabbia, the suspect, residing at Saama village, Dama chiefdom, Kenema district was reported to have forcefully burnt the right hand of Kallon.
Alpha who reportedly confessed committing the act said Kallon stole her cooked cassava.
Eyewitnesses account revealed that the relationship between Alpha and Kallon has not been cordial.
However, Kallon is responding to treatment at the Kenema Government Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: 200 Children Rescued from Pastors
Headline: Uganda: 200 Children Rescued From Gulu Pastors
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE POLICE on Friday stormed a new church for born-again Christians in Gulu district and rescued over 200 children who were under illegal custody.
The children have been reunited with their parents.
Three pastors of the church, Healing Outreach Ministries in Bungatira sub-county, Aswa county, were arrested in the raid that followed complaints by parents about their missing children.
"We mounted an operation and managed to rescue over 200 school-going age children who were under illegal custody," the Gulu Police community liaison officer, Johnson Kilama, said.
He identified the pastors as Lucy Anek Kigozi from Kampala, Alfred Kanyama, a teacher from Gulu, and Saverino Okello, a peasant in Gulu.
Kilama said they were shocked to find children from over 15 primary schools in Gulu and Amuru districts under the pastor's custody.
"The children claimed the Church invited them to register for scholarships," Kilama said.
Kanyama said the children started reporting to the Church on October 7. The children were being fed on boiled cassava, according to the resident district commissioner, Col. Walter Ochora.
"The church didn't have any means to give the children proper meals but decided to keep them there on boiled cassava," Ochora said. "The Police started searching for the children following a tip from Bungatira residents that a fake religious organisation was keeping children illegally."
Both Ochora and the Police said the Church duped the children that a Canadian donor would pay their school fees and provide them basic needs.
"The managers of the church claimed that they were looking after some children and had got a promise of funding from a Canadian religious organisation.
"So they rushed to the district and started inviting and keeping children whom they wanted to show to the Canadian when he comes to Uganda," Ochora said.
The actions of the church, Ochora said, were tantamount to abduction.
He noted the incident was one of the negative things coming up following the return of peace to the region. "Now that peace is returning to the district, all sorts of fake NGOs are coming here.
"We have passed a by-law requiring that all guests in the district should announce their presence to the office of the district internal security officer within 24 hours. "They have to state how long they will be in the district and the purpose of their visit," Ochora said. The Police spokesman, Asan Kasingye, yesterday said investigations into the incident were still going on and Pastor Anek Kigozi Anek had been released on bond.
Kigozi, the Church leader, claimed that Johan, a Canadian, had asked her to register orphans and widows who needed support.
"We are not a cult and we did not steal the children. The church invited the children who are orphans to come and be registered for the scholarships."
Last month, security agencies arrested 11 members of a cult, the Jerusalem Temple, which was preaching the end of the world. The Police warned the public against starting and joining cults and urged them to be vigilant and report such cases.

Original_Search_Title: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Headline: Uganda: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Body_Text:
Although the rains are still falling and the floods still bothering our people in different parts of Uganda, time to stop blaming anybody has come. What is needed is to assess our capability to deal with such disasters and move on to lay a foundation upon which the ministry responsible for disaster preparedness can base its future operations.
Today's disaster preparedness is nothing to write home about and there is no point crucifying the gentlemen who run the shambolic ministry. After all, how many helicopters are there in this country for instance? Two and a half or three.
That is what is shared between all the agencies and sectors that require the use of helicopters like health (for evacuation of patients and casualties), forestry (for monitoring encroachment and illegal felling activities), the wildlife authority (for chasing poachers and inspecting movement of herds), police (for chasing getaway criminals), media (for covering some events up close when roads are jammed with traffic) and of course the ministry of disaster preparedness, for getting close to disaster sites and helping the victims quickly.
I am told the health sector has half a helicopter because the one used by a private sector operator is reportedly often parked outside Uganda in a neighbouring country, and it may not always be readily available at a few minutes notice.
Aviation technology has in the recent couple of decades proved elusive and so let us forget the '70s when Uganda Air Force had dozens of supersonic MiG 21s and a host of other fighter planes, scores of helicopters and several Hercules transporter aircraft. That is the time when the Uganda Police Airwing had innumerable small fixed wing planes, choppers and airfields of its own which today are covered with cassava gardens and haphazard dwellings.
The time to focus on is today and tomorrow. Aviation aside, we need to accept that even vehicular road means of responding to disaster are not our strongest point. It is not clear if there is a single ambulance in the country that measures up to ambulances elsewhere which are in effect casualty wards in which an accident victim is treated by paramedics and made ready for further treatment including surgery en route to hospital.
In case of traffic jams and distance, someone does not have to die from bleeding, obstruction of air passages or wrong handling. Let us stop lamenting at our new public 'ambulances' that are really double cabin pick-up trucks that cannot conceivably accommodate a patient lying in horizontal position.
We should also not complain that many accident victims at night are taken to Mulago in police patrol pickups, thrown down there under bench with robbery suspects who are being carted to the cells.
What we need is to look at alternative solutions that are within our capabilities. Since our capacity to acquire, man and maintain machines for disaster action has proven inadequate, a time has come to try out alternative methods that do not require foreign exchange and large budgetary outlays to finance. We need to look at nature.
Let us call up all the veterinary graduates who are doing business in Kikubo or teaching Biology in secondary schools and immediately put them to work breeding animals that can be trained to operate in difficult terrain.
We need to develop mounted squads that can reach where wheeled vehicles will not dare and deliver relief and medical attention to our people. If we start breeding horses and donkeys now, by the end of next year we would be seeing the beginning of a well trained disaster and rescue unit that would have the capability to take medical personnel, foodstuffs, drugs and blankets to people in areas that cannot be accessed by cars.
It also time to remember that a fifth of Uganda's surface area is covered by water. This tends to increase when there are floods like these days, Therefore our capacity to move over water is highly inadequate when you consider that 20 percent of our territory cannot be covered using wheels or on foot, but by boat and swimming.
We therefore need to revive the boat building section of the fisheries department in Entebbe. Any other agencies that used to build boats need to be revisited. Our capacity to move on water must be boosted using available raw materials.
As for fire, it is rather hard to find local solutions to cover the gap created by our lack of fire engine trucks. It is tempting to suggest sprinklers for every building since we have no shortage of water. But our aversion to acquisition and maintenance of modern technology may not allow automated sprinklers.
Maybe we should require every building to keep several huge drums of water and some kanyamas (muscle men) around to start tendering the water when fire breaks out.
We are by no means advocating for sticking to ancient means of disaster handling. These are stop gap measures. By the time we buy enough choppers and fire engines several decades from today, these natural methods could have saved many lives in the interim.
Mr Buwembo is managing editor, Daily Monitor

Original_Search_Title: Independence in France with Juliana
Headline: Uganda: Independence in France with Juliana
Body_Text:
Like any other city, Paris is full of life. The witty conmen who sell fake merchandise, creepy characters that give suspicious glares and tricksters who do everything in their means to unwillingly relieve you of your possessions.
You could know it all, but unlike in other cities like Nairobi , you get tempted to walk on the lonely streets simply because of the allure of Paris.
On Friday October 5, many Ugandans from all over France and living as far as the Sarregue Mines (France - Germany border) and Geneva - Switzerland braved the not so friendly circumstances and biting cold in Paris as the Ugandan Community savoured an early celebration of their country's 45th Independence Anniversary.
Over 600 guests including diplomatic corps accredited to France, permanent delegates to Unesco, friends of Uganda in Paris and the business community crammed the 7th floor of the Unesco headquarters building located on Plot 7, Place de Fontenoy Street to be part of the celebrations.
The Charge d'Affaires of the Ugandan Embassy in France Ms Samantha Sherurah with two Ugandan girls studying in Paris - Agnes Kushemerwerwa and Sandra Nambooze ushered in the guests before unexpected songbird Juliana Kanyomozi got the evening started with the National Anthem.
Known to depart early, Juliana's contagious voice and slick dancing moves forced the diplomats to stay longer.
She had most of the crowd that comprised former Cranes custodian Livingstone Kyobe, Princess Ryan Keza Barigye (Prince John Barigye's daughter), Burundi's Princess Kamatari, Mr Pierre Lys (President of the Uganda-France Friendship Association), Professor Eriabu Lugujja (member of Unesco Executive Board) and Mr Omare Okurut (Secretary General of Uganda National Commission of UNESCO) singing along when she sang Kibaluma and Nkyanoonya.
During her performance Juliana made a killing when she sold over 50 CDs of her new Kanyimbe Album at 15 Euros (Shs37, 200) each.
It was a typical Ugandan night! There was a scramble for the preferred Uganda Waragi and home made cassava chips, samosas and local tunes dominated airplay during the night.
Hadn't it been for the stringent laws, the Independence celebrations would have ended later than midnight when the curtain was drawn on the memorable evening.
As the guests said their 'Au revoirs,' each was handed a Ugandan gift bag with a folder containing history, tourism, trade and investment information on Uganda.
The bag also had a desktop calculator courtesy of SN Brussels, a VCD of Uganda's flora and fauna, instant coffee pods and tots of Uganda Waragi. Real proof of Independence!
Betty Mpologoma goes solo
EDGAR R. BATTE
After years of musical collaborations, Betty Mpologoma feels she is ready to go it alone and give fans her 'exclusivity.' She has set out on a her first musical trip with her Mpaliriza track, off her Big Deal album. She has also shot a video for the song, which she expects to use as a precursor in promoting this album.
Big Deal has songs like Kyaterekera, Nandigumye, Todula and Teli Polisi and the title track Big Deal.
Mpologoma explained her change of career direction on draw backs. "At times, when you are in a band, you feel like you're putting in so much but working for general expenses of the group. You'll work for transport, costumes and generally survival. I guess I can now direct my efforts to my personal career," she said. The singer famed for her other songs like Akasiimu tugende nkakyajinge, Body Guard, Deejay nsomerayo and ekirango, attributes her musical background to her roots.
"Music runs through my family. My grandfather was an artiste, my sister too is one of the budding artistes. I grew up singing in the choir in an Adventist church as early as my Sunday school days," Mpologoma who is also a sister to artiste Phoebe Nassolo adds.
She got her first break through first by voicing ads under VCL, as well as miming with The Ebonies and also featured in their dramas like The Boss and Diamond Ring.
She was with The Ebonies for three years before Diamonds Production took her on in 2000. In 2003 she joined Kat Production and subsequently Jungle Production which she left in July this year.
She has worked with Dream Studio and Kasiwukira for her new album and expects to start off her new solo career with live weekly performances at a place she is yet to confirm.

Original_Search_Title: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Headline: Uganda: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Body_Text:
Kampala — A bomb exploded yesterday in Mbogwe village Buhanika sub-county in Hoima district, killing a 13-year-old boy. The second victim, a sister to the deceased, was rushed to Hoima Hospital in critical condition following the incident at Buhanika, about 10kms from Hoima town.
The officer in charge of crime at Hoima Police Station, John Bahemuka, identified the deceased as Bernard Atuhairwe, a Primary Five pupil at St. Petero Paul Primary School in Buhanika. His sister, Angella Nsungwa, 15, is a Primary Six pupil in the same school.
Their mother, Christine Kinura, 50, said: "My children told me that somebody had uprooted our cassava. I asked them to go and check, only to hear a loud sound. When I rushed there, I found my children in a pool of blood," she said, praying that at least her daughter survives.
This is the second bomb to explode in two days in Bunyoro region. A bomb exploded in Bulisa on Sunday, killing one man and injuring his two brothers, who are now admitted in Hoima Hospital. The three were sons to Leuben Kasumba, a herdsman.
The mid-western regional criminal investigations department chief, James Bangirana, said the bomb was a tortoise grenade. "We are investigating the incident because cases of bomb blasts are becoming common in the region. We shall use radio messages to sensitise the public about this problem."
The area MP, Kiiza Rwebembera, appealed to the Government to investigate the source of the grenades.

Original_Search_Title: Airtime Tops Spending List
Headline: Uganda: Airtime Tops Spending List
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS generally spent more on telephone airtime in September than they did on food items, a survey by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics has shown.
According to the Consumer Price Index (CPI) released by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) on Tuesday, the highest expenditure for most people was on telecommunications services.
The second highest expenditure was on the food sub groups, which have overall weight of 27.2 per cent of the total index.
Releasing the CPI figures at a news conference in Kampala, the Director of Macroeconomics at Ubos, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana said the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending September went up to 4.9 per cent from 4.3 per cent that was registered for the year ended August 2007.
"Ugandans are increasingly spending more money on telecommunication services especially on mobile phones," he said.
People in Kampala, Jinja and Mbarara spent most on telephone airtime, on matoke in Masaka, on charcoal in Gulu and on cassava in Arua.
Mr Ssewanyana said during September, food prices went up by 3.2 per cent in all centres, with a significant contribution coming from Irish potatoes, cassava, fresh fruits especially passion fruits and pineapples.
Likewise, prices of milk, some vegetable like cabbages, tomatoes, onions and dried beans also went due to low supplies.
Higher price levels in rent, fuel and utilities group in most centres were also registered.
Arua, Mbarara Kampala high and Kampala low centre registered the highest increase in food prices during the month.
Increase in the prices of paraffin, charcoal, petrol and transport fares were recorded in most centre.
In a new development, Ubos has come up with a new methodology of excluding items with unstable prices from the CPI basket.
Mr Ssenwanyana said the exclusion of food items, fuel, electricity and metered water from CPI basket will now help Uganda to have stable inflation rate.
Inflation figures
Following the exclusion of the listed items, Uganda's core inflation (originally known as the underlying inflation), which now excludes food items, fuel, electricity and metered water from CPI basket, was at 5.7 per cent in September.
The upward changes in the CPI comes at a time when Ubos has completed re-basing exercise of the CPI, which began in March 2006 and ended in September 2007.
It has seen Ubos computing 273 items and six urban centres chosen from the four regions to measure inflation in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Body_Text:
Kigali — Climatic change and external factors are to blame for the current increase in prices of foodstuffs in the country, a Central bank official has said.
A mini survey of markets and shops in the city indicates that prices of some foodstuffs and other essential commodities have continued to increase, some by as much as 50 percent, the highest in the country in past three months.
But the bank official who requested to remain anonymous refuted claims that the price increase was the result of a unstable economy. "If we import a commodity from a country that is experiencing inflation, the effect is transferred to our economy. But our monetary policy on local produce is still stable," the official revealed. The 2007 monetary policy report indicates that inflation slowed down in response to significant fall in prices of foodstuffs. Last month the Governor of the National Bank of Rwanda (BNR) François Kanimba revealed that the bank would contain inflation to less than 10 per cent. "Compared to the beginning of 2006, inflationary pressure significantly dropped during the first half of 2007", Kanimba said. Rose Mukarwego, a vendor at Muhima market in lower Kiyovu says she increased her prices due to high price from his suppliers. "Our suppliers have increased their prices on every commodity they supply us, so we have to do the same if we are to remain in the business", she said.
She said that the smallest cluster of yellow bananas costs Frw200-250 compared to previous months when it was selling at Frw150-200. The price Sugar has increased to Frw 700 from 600-650, a sack of charcoal now costs Frw4,700 an increase from Frw4,500 last month. Consumers of Irish potatoes, cassava, green bananas, sorghum, beans, rice, and groundnuts have to dig deeper into their pockets these days if they are to afford a meal.
"I know our clients are complaining about the price increment, but I have nothing to do. I also get them at high prices compared to previous months," Mukarwego explained.

Original_Search_Title: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Headline: Uganda: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO brothers in Atiak internally displaced persons' cam in Amuru district have been arrested for raping their 60-year old stepmother, the Police has said.
On September 15, Joseph Okumu, 21 and James Akena, 34, followed their stepmother to the field, where they raped her, the community liaison officer, Robinal Oceng, told journalists.
The two brothers were arrested on September 17.
"They followed their elderly stepmother who had gone to uproot cassava in the garden and raped her in turns, one after the other," he said.
Rape and defilement are rampant in the displaced persons' camps, with most of them never reaching the Police, he added.
"I want to ask people not to keep quiet about any case of rape.
"They should all be reported to the police so that the perpetrators are punished," he said.
Oceng added that the two brothers, who are being held at Gulu police Station, would appear in court as soon as their file was sanctioned by the resident state attorney.

Original_Search_Title: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Headline: Uganda: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Body_Text:
Kampala — ROSEMARY Nansambu, 9, would not be studying at Shalom Primary School, Mulago, if she was not selling maize on the streets.
She began selling maize last year after the death of her father, Charles Muyondo. Nansambu cannot recall the name of her mother, who died when she was younger but says: "Whenever I think of my mother, I cry."
Nansambu sells roasted and boiled maize in the evening near her home. Her three siblings do not go to school; they stay at home with their grandmother.
"I used to go with my grandmother to Kalerwe Market to buy the maize. When she started falling sick very often, I began the maize business. I had to do everything on my own," Nansambu says.
Nalongo, Nansambu's grandmother, says she is worried about the future of the orphans. "These children are not in school. I cannot afford to pay their fees. Nansambu is in school because she raises her school fees," she adds.
The grandmother of four says if it was not for lack of school fees and other basic needs, her granddaughter would be about to complete P.7. However, she says, there is a shortage of funds when there is a scarcity of maize.
During such times, Nansambu and her grandmother, sell roast cassava and sweet potatoes to earn a living.
However, many other children face Nansambu's plight. At Makerere, many children, Nansambu's age, sit in groups, selling food. Most of them, stay in the surrounding areas of Mulago, kikoni, Kawaala, Nakulabye and Katanga.
One Meddie Mubiru, 13, goes to Makerere University in the evening to sell maize. He studies at All Saints Primary School in Mulago.
He began selling maize at the age of eight, when his father abandoned him and his siblings.
"One day, my father came back home jobless. From that time, whenever we asked him for money to buy sugar and bread, he would say he was broke. He left us helpless. Since then, I have not seen him, although I'm told he lives in Kalerwe," Mubiru adds.
When his mother became ill, Mubiru started selling maize to raise school fees. "Whenever my mother was sick, I assisted her to run the business. I had to sell maize at Makerere University. At the university, I made friends with a cleaner, who advised me to sell maize in halls of residence and hostels around the campus," he says. Mubiru, who is now in P.6, wants to become a pilot.
Gladys Ayikoru, 12, studies at Makerere Church of Uganda Primary School. She also sells maize to raise money for her upkeep. However, Ayikoru stays with her parents. "My mother prepares the maize for me to sell. She stays home, while I go to Makerere university, where my father works as a security guard," Ayikoru says.
Florence Masia, who goes to school at Makerere Church of Uganda Primary School, says she was forced to start hawking maize at a tender age after her father died. "I get money for my school fees and other scholastic materials from selling maize on the streets in the evening.
Ten-year-old Olivia Gasi's mother died in April this year. On top of raising her school fees, Gasi has to take care of her three-month-old sibling. However, Gasi has managed to return to school because her aunt took the baby with her to Wakiso.
A report by the Save the Children's Fund in 2002, a national coverage survey shows that the majority of pupils aged six to 18 have dropped out of school at the primary level.
The report adds that the cost of uniforms, textbooks and exercise books was the main reason they dropped out of school.
According to the report released by Platform for labour Action in 2004, child labour continues to be a major problem of the developing world.
Platform for labour action is an NGO that strives to prevent child labour, by rehabilitating children. It aids children by giving them primary, secondary and vocational education.
In Uganda, it is estimated that children make up 62% of the poor; 2.7 billion of these children are working and more than half of them are aged between 10-14 years. The report reveals that with such statistics, it is inevitable that many of these children will end up doing hazardous, dangerous and exploitative work. According to the report, 227 children have been removed from work they were engaged in, 150 of these were in high-risk work environments. Seventy-six children have been placed in school and provided with basic scholastic materials like uniforms, exercise books and pens.

Original_Search_Title: Osunbor On Scale
Headline: Nigeria: Osunbor On Scale
Body_Text:
Lagos — SINCE the live appearance of Senator Oserhiemen Osunbor, governor of Edo State, during the September 5 celebration of the first hundred days on television, his image has soared widely. The event has afforded the former Senate Committee chairman on Judiciary, Law and Justice, a rare opportunity to display a great oratorical skill which pundits believe was strong enough to rubbish many town criers.
Osunbor from a dwindling image has risen to become the rave of the state. And no doubt many reactions so far has given the lawyer turned politician a nod of approval which is considered as a great punch on the otherwise strong camp of the opposition led by Adams Oshiomhole of Action Congress, AC.
Shortly before the ceremonial hundred days, the state government was embroiled in the decision to probe the sale and allocation of many government houses and lands by the immediate past administration under former governor, Chief Lucky Igbinedion. It had been alleged that some houses under the administration were given away as gifts to friends and relatives to the extent that a former governor in one of the South-south states got one for one thousand naira.
Forest reserves in the state were also said to have been allocated to party leaders who quickly fenced them for future development. As a result of this, Osunbor was said to be finding it difficult to win popular support to execute the probe. The commissioner of lands and survey, Mr. Anthony Iyalekhue, came under pressure from politicians who benefitted from the deals. The governor's confirmation of how the state industries were sold or privatized by the Igbinedion administration sent cold shivers across, that the past eight years were a hell.
Corroborating the governor, his special adviser on communication and public relations, Mr. Tony Ikpasaja, disclosed that the Ikpeshi company was established for over N500 million and became a big hope for the state which was just finding its feet in the mining sector. "The outright sale of Ikpeshi and Ehor Fruit Juice factories, under a very suspicious circumstance by the Igbinedion administration, has only taken the hand of clock backwards for the state," said Ikpasaja.
"For now, we are awaiting the faceless new owners of the factories, especially the Ehor Juice Factory, which have been overgrown by weeds, to take off. If these factories have been purchased by people and they are not coming out to operate them, that means there is either a problem somewhere or the transaction may have been fraught with filth and fraud." The cassava factory in Uromi, started five years ago, and which was also loudly sung by former Governor Igbinedion, also came shockingly to be a yet-to-be-completed project.
More worrisome is the fact the cement factory is no more operating at full capacity. And the people are feeling short-changed by the sudden change of ownership. The revelations by Osunbor about the sold properties of the state have whipped up so much sentiments that anger is welling up among the Edo people. Musa Igbadumhe, a native of Okpella, confessed that since the Igbinedion regime, the cement factory no longer attracts business to the town.
Other state companies which the governor did not speak much about but inviting curiosity from the public are the Edo Line, Edo City Transport Service, Bendel Brewery, Edo Broadcasting Service and Nigerian Observer. For what it seems, Osunbor is faced with a Herculean task of reviving many of these companies which in the past years were scandalized as existing for some selfish interests than those of the public. If laws are made for man and not man for law, the Osunbor government may be compelled to reverse some of the suspected deals especially the sale of government houses, privatization and change of ownership or management of the public utilities in the state.
Idugbor, a lawyer, lives at 134 Sapele road, Benin City.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — The Chairman of the Ogun State Cassava Revolution Programme, Prof. Dupe Olatunbosun has raised alarm over the efforts by flour millers in the country to frustrate cassava producers and processors by giving excuses to reject the cassava flour in producing flour.
The development came, even as some of the millers have been accused of stock piling their warehouses to deceive the Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON), the regulatory body checking the compliance of the millers on the use of the 5% cassava flour in their production.
He also hinted that to check this anomaly, a law that would make it mandatory for millers to include the cassava flour in their production would soon be table before the National Assembly.
Olatunbosun, who addressed a press conference at the Secretariat of the Ogun State Council of the Nigeria Union of Journalists (NUJ), in Abeokuta on Wednesday, said that with the new fraudulent practice of the millers in the country, the efforts of the Federal government at ensuring total compliance to the use of cassava flour or tubers could be a mirage.
The Chairman remarked that with increase in the number of processors to about 300 per cent since inception, the flour millers were fingered as having been responsible for not meeting the about $400m expected to be saved under the project.
According to Olatunbosun, "the number of processors rose by about 300 per cent since inception, but the flour millers are deliberately frustrating the producers and the processors. To recapitulate,if the policy had been faithfully implemented, about $400M was expected to be saved under this single project
"Under the scheme, 300, 000 metric tones of High Quality Cassava Flour (HQCF) or 10,000 trailer load of HQCF or 33 trailer loads per day or 1,000mt or 165 trailers of fresh cassava tubers per day was expected to be consumed."
Olatunbosun, who is also the Chairman of the Ekiti state Policy and Strategy Committee noted further that the flour millers devised ways to frustrate the federal government policy, among which was the price for the purchase of the cassava flour, which has not been honoured by them.
"As at March 2007, almost a year later, only 5,000Mt of HQCF was supplied to flour mills by processors. Obviously, the flour millers had devised ways to frustrate the policy. A March 30, 2006 agreement fixing cassava flour price at N75,000 per ton-ex factory was not honoured.
Others gave the ridiculous excuse that the wheat being imported into the country had low protein content-consequently mixing this with local cassavas flour would rob the finished product of adequate protein content. Some of the flour miller even stock plied to deceive Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON) and their fellow Nigerians."

Original_Search_Title: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Headline: Kenya: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Body_Text:
Who said theatre business is all fun without funds in return? Ask Eliud Abuto and he will tell you that theatre is as good business as any other.
Three years ago, the Festival of Creative Arts- a local theatre company he runs in the city-rose from the ashes of the now defunct Mbalamwezi Theatre Society.
Even with the renewed spirit with which it entered the market, the company could not manage to attract enough fans to watch plays it was staging.
Then staging most of their shows at the Kenya National Theatre, the company's best moment was an audience of 40 thespians.
But things have gradually changed especially on matters of business strategy and product development.
Today, anyone intending to watch a theatre performance by the group must book well in advance to watch a production.
Last weekend, the theatre company staged its version of Kindly Keep It Covered, a play written by Dave Freeman. Starring Derrick Amunga, Lydia Gitachi and Nice Githinji, the play staged at the Alliance Française attracted full houses through its run.
The auditorium has a sitting capacity of 210; there were six runs-after audiences requested for an extra show on Sunday evening.
"When we initially decided to venture into theatre, we knew that the theatre terrain in the country has been very rough. But armed with our professionalism, we were ready to trek on," says Abuto who is one of the group's director.
"Initially, we were just staging plays we thought were good; most of this time, we felt we knew what the audiences wanted. But we were wrong. When most of the productions failed to attract theatre fans, we decided to inject professionalism into the show."
Every time the group staged a play they sought to survey on consumer preferences. Using exit polls, the producer has a chance to get into the minds of those who frequent theatres, something that had eluded theatres for so long.
"We also make regular visits to different companies to understand what kind of plays Kenyans with some disposable income would be pay to watch on stage," he adds.
Going by the number of fans queuing for tickets to watch productions by this group lately, the strategy is already bearing fruits.
"Very soon, we might be looking for a more spacious theatre to accommodate all the fans," quips Abuto.
Crystal Mbuga and Godfrey Odhiambo in Kindly Keep It Covered that was staged by Festival of Creative Arts at the Alliance Française two weeks ago.
While still based at the Kenya National Theatre, Festival of Creative Arts suffered several cancellations thanks to orders from the Department of Culture.
There was also lack of continuity in terms of standards at the theatre that does not currently vet productions before they are staged.
"The problem in this case comes in when a group stages a poor production as this will cut off the fan base for other more serious groups," admits Abuto.
Lighting, acoustics and even seats at the theatre are a put- off. During the run of She Ate The Female Cassava, rains disrupted the show forcing the producer to refund money to the audience.
This is a hilarious Kenya play written by Jimmi Makotsi that revolves around a school girl entangled in a love web.
To escape from the National Theatre problems, the group sought a base elsewhere at the Alliance Française auditorium, which has become one of the favourite spots for the growing number of theatre goers.
"We have focused on changing perception that theatres are idle spaces for lay-abouts; make it a respectable profession that people can trust to offer quality entertainment," says Abuto.
While most theatre groups rarely engage the services of professionals in their act, Creative Arts has a team of marketers, managers, front office managers and even public relations officials who are professionally trained and with a passion for the arts. Most of these also double as cast to get a taste of the stage.
Mr Eliud Abuto, who is the glue holding this theatre dream, is a biochemist working at the Kenyatta National Hospital.
With an MBA degree from Moi University, the man is currently pushing the theatre company to a level where they can sustain a repertory theatre.
After experimenting with a number of genres, the group has now gone back to staging more farces that the audiences seem to follow keenly.
Some of these are When Did You Lastly See Your Trouser where a man finds himself locked in a space after losing his pants, Bedside Manners and even Boeing Boeing where a man has different women to serve him on different days depending on their schedules.
Even though farces are most popular with the audiences, says Abuto, the theatre company will be dropping an African play, satire along the way to spice up their act-when they must.

Original_Search_Title: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Headline: Uganda: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Body_Text:
Mr Wafula Oguttu's article, Save UPE Before It Turns a Chronic Disaster, (Daily Monitor on Monday) was extremely depressing reading. Of course, many people foresaw that the Universal Primary Education (UPE) would end up as a gulag of ill-educated children of the poor and working class, but no one would have imagined it would be as bad as Wafula described it.
The scary thing is that even the gloomy picture Wafula paints is not the worst, going by accounts one reads on the Internet about the state of primary school education in the more remote parts of the motherland. I remember almost seven years ago, we were driving on a weekend to a funeral in the western part of Tororo district and took a shortcut off Tirinyi Road.
Deep in the village we came upon a place that had a relatively new UPE school, iron roof and all shining on one side of the road. On the other was another school, a private one. It was built of grass-thatch and mud walls.
The shiny government school was dead. The grass-thatched one was full of life. We stopped under a mango tree nearby to buy roast maize and cassava, and asked the vendor why the government school was closed.
He told us the teachers were all across the road teaching in the private grass-thatched school. With its mud walls and all, it still had something the shining government school didn't. Parents paid, not much, the equivalent of about Shs50 a week.
With that, the enterprising headmaster paid the teachers a few thousand bob a month, bought chalk, put up blackboards, and so forth. In the government school, they went for months without pay.
For those who follow these matters, it's the same story, from Kenya's sprawling Kibera to the great slums of Mumbai. In these slums, the schools where parents pay one dollar a month, offer a better education than the government and, sometimes, donor-funded ones with their glass windows, which are "free".
It would be woefully wrong to conclude from this that "free" primary schooling cannot work. It can, of course. In the Nordic and Scandinavian countries, for example, they offer some of the best education in the world - and it is "free". We quote "free" because, really, there's nothing free. Someone pays for it - a taxpayer at home, or abroad if like UPE a large chunk of it is paid for from donor aid, or philanthropy if it's funded by some charity organisation. Someone always pays.
Anyhow, the bigger point is that a military dictatorship can offer free decent education; a theocratic state can; functioning democracies too can. However, no corrupt half-democracy where the gap between the poor and rich grows with every other day has been able to offer a UPE worth its name anywhere in the world.
This explains why UPE in Uganda is the mess it is. In the early stages, the government will push it as a populist programme to get legitimacy and win votes.
But UPE, even when it still produced mostly below average students as it did in the early stages in Uganda, eventually increases the number of slightly more enlightened people who understand things better than the totally illiterate peasant of old.
Before you know it, the first graduates become more aware voters. And soon, they are a problem for the government.
They begin seeing that it is corrupt, that only a few people are doing well, and instead of voting for it in gratitude, they vote against it. That forces the government to resort to more and more brazen vote stealing to win as we have seen in Uganda over the last two elections.
Eventually, the government becomes stuck. It can't scrap the programme, because there would be a huge backlash. But also it can't do the things Wafula is asking for to improve it, because it would be digging its political grave. Therefore, it does the next big thing - it turns UPE into a limbo; it's there, but its products are as good as people who didn't go to school.
Actually if you want to know what exactly is a government's intention, don't look at what it does at the primary school level. Look at what it does at university level.
Usually, it will stop funding the Arts and Social Sciences, and concentrate on Science and "practical" subjects as the Museveni government has done.
The reason for that is that historians, political scientists, and psychologists are the greatest danger to a decaying autocracy. First, they can profile the mental state of the strongman. They can read social unrest. They can make comparisons with other societies.
But the biggest nuisances of them all are historians and literature people. When the autocrat tries to rewrite the history of the country, and his own years in power, the historians keep the accurate record. And the writers and playwrights capture the true story for posterity.
So state scholarships for the Arts have to go. There are no prizes for what will be next - Law, of course.

Original_Search_Title: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Headline: Mozambique: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Eduardo Mondlane University (UEM), the largest higher education institution in Mozambique, has successfully conducted a study on using a mixture of wheat and cassava flours to produce bread.
The study was ordered by the government, seeking solutions to the repeated increases in the price of wheat flour by the milling companies, which subsequently increases the price of bread.
The results of the first study approved a mixture of 7.5 kilos of cassava flour for every 42.5 kilos of wheat flour. The researchers found that the resulting bread has the same characteristics of that made of wheat flour only, with the advantage that the new type offers better guarantees of conservation.
Happy with these results, the study group increased the proportion of cassava to 25 per cent - 12.5 kilos of cassava flour to 37.5 kilos of wheat flour. The idea is to advance to a type of bread that is at least 60 per cent cassava flour and only 40 per cent wheat flour.
The Minister of Science and Technology, Venancio Massingue, met on Monday with representatives of the milling companies, bakers, researchers and other food specialists to present publicly the project and ask for the necessary support from the bakers, who must be persuaded to adopt the new type of bread. The cassava- wheat mixture has already been approved by the health services.
Cited in Tuesday's issue of the Maputo daily "Noticias", Massingue explained that the idea of seeking alternative types of bread, in response to rising international wheat prices, was first advanced by President Armando Guebuza in May, during a meeting of the government.
"After that, we called a meeting of the Science and Technology Council, that quickly started work in the field", said Massingue.
UEM Engineering Faculty research director Carlos Lucas, who heads the working group, explained that the experiment was undertaken in three bakeries, selected on the basis of a questionnaire circulated to bakers in Maputo and Matola concerning the quantity of bread they produce and the complexity of their equipment.
After the proven success of the mixture of the wheat and cassava flours (the new type of bread was tested by 500 people), the idea is to advance to other experiments, with maize meal and soya.
Mozambique imports almost all the wheat it consumes. Only on the Angonia plateau, in the western province of Tete, is a small amount of wheat grown. But cassava is the most abundant staple in the country, and it should not be difficult to increase cassava production to meet demand from bakers.
Despite Massingue's insistence on knowing whether or not the bakers are prepared to produce this new type of bread, the response was lukewarm. Far from enthusiastically rising to the challenge of producing a healthy product using Mozambique's own raw materials, conservative bakers preferred to lament the lack of home-grown wheat.
Victor Manuel, of the Maputo Bakers Association, said the most important measures that could be taken would be for the University to increase the production of wheat in Mozambique, taking advantage of potential which he alleged exists in various parts of the country. (In fact, the climatic conditions in most of Mozambique are not propitious for wheat).
The millers also said that they need some time before they can give any informed answer on their real capacity to embark on this project.
The researchers believe that the country has enough cassava flour to produce the new bread. The only problem they saw was the lack of any tradition in the urban centres of eating bread made from anything other than wheat.

Original_Search_Title: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Headline: Uganda: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Body_Text:
Luweero — At times I ask myself whether this is the government I fought to bring into power. The people up there seem to have forgotten that we once shared a piece of cassava in the bush - former NRA fighter
For Isirayiri Bugembe, 52, a resident of Semuto, Luweero District when the National Resistance Army/Movement (NRA/M) shot its way to power in 1986, it was like reaching the promised land. To him, free education for his eight children, a good job, though he dropped out in primary two, a huge village house and respect from his peers is what lay ahead because of what he says was his role as one of the founding fighters of the NRA under Mr Yoweri Museveni.
Two decades down the road, Bugembe, looking weary and haggard after falling on hard times, is thinking twice. "At times I ask myself whether this is the government I fought to bring into power. The people up there seem to have forgotten that we once shared a piece of cassava in the bush," Bugembe, now a casual labourer and charcoal trader despite his failing health, told Sunday Monitor when this reporter met him early this month.
Bugembe's point of disillusionment came on May, 23, 2006. On that day Bugembe says a senior government official, who he says hid in his house for two weeks during the bush war, came to Luweero claimed he did not remember him.
"How can that person (official) claim he does not remember me when I used to hide him in my house. My wife and children looked after him, we ate from the same plate," he said. Bugembe, however, refused to divulge the official's name to avoid embarrassing him but blamed that attitude for the reason some former bush war fighters supported either Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) leader, Kizza Besigye, in the February 2006 elections or the Democratic Party's (DP) Ssebaana Kizito.
Bugembe, a known supporter of President Museveni according to his drinkmates in a bar selling local potent gin (waragi) on the fringes of Semuto Town represents thousands of NRA's unsung heroes who are living a life of deprivation today in what was then known as the Luweero Triangle. They live the hard life while some of their former colleagues today drive posh cars, own mansions, educate their children in affluent schools abroad and in Kampala.
It is people like him who attended the President's meet-the-people meeting at Mijinji, one of the former NRA guerilla bases, as he toured the former triangle in what was officially labelled a campaign to fight household poverty. Some observers, however, have seen the makings of presidential re-election campaign in the tour.
Even though the people he met still laughed at his jokes about the past, the President appeared ready to confront the issues of the poverty head on. He chose to divert from the general line that had been carried in one of his landmark speeches delivered in Jinja on February 9, 2002, about the "Evolution and Modernisation of Society". In that speech, he had blamed the failure of African leadership to improve the people's lot, but now he was castigating the people for their sad state affairs.
President Museveni addressing residents of Masulita in Luwero District, on August 30. Photos by Wandera W'Ouma & PPU
"If you take the case of Karamoja, for instance, is it fully employed or utilised? If you go to Karamoja you can see that everybody is asleep. The land, the forests, the human beings, and the cows are all asleep!" he mused at on rally while noting that this is characteristic of backward countries -- the under utilisation of the factors of production.
But former fighters like Bugembe would have thought that once they were in power it was an outright guarantee of the end of poverty. They thought they would receive hand-outs from the State, especially when the government established a ministry for Luweero affairs.
This was not to be, and this is the more reason they cannot reconcile President Museveni's theory about employing "full factors of production" and their difficult situation.
One felt that there is indeed a level of bitterness hanging in the air as we followed the President throughout his tour. He might have also been aware of this because unlike the 200km 'long march' from Luweero through Birembo in Mubende that marked the beginning of his re-election campaign in 2001, this time he is pressed deeper into the villages.
There were fewer rallies held at district headquarters this time. Mr Museveni also moved with a smaller entourage of ministers and hangers-on. In fact, it was interesting to see him cutting them off. At Kapeeka, the minister for Luwero, Nyombi Tembo, suffered this embarrassment just like Ruth Nankanbirwa (state for defence) at Ddwaniro, when they wanted to make long-winded speeches.
Instead, the President insisted on interacting directly with the people at a personal level.
He visited homes of the former fighters, some who like Bugembe might be feeling that they have been forgotten, received donations and pledges. For those who have died, the President promised to give assistance to their families.
However, those we spoke to still felt the President should go beyond the handshake he willingly extended. "I fought with him (Museveni). I lost a wife and my eldest son during the war. My goats and cows were taken by the NRA soldiers. I tried getting compensation from the President, his people blocked me," Joseph Lukabya of Lwamata, Kiboga said.
"Until I get what was taken from me, I think shaking of hands with the President makes no difference in my life," he added. Lukabya said he voted for DP's Ssebaana in the last presidential election.
It is people like this President Museveni might have been addressing when he said at the rally in Mijinji that: "All these ministers you see here are your servants. The other time I was a freedom fighter but you elected me into office, now I am your servant. If you don't vote me into office, I will not complain. I will retire to my home."
But he is not leaving everything to chance because cash donations of Shs10 million were doled out to at least every three villages per sub-county we visited from the presidential purse, ostensibly to help the people start up income generating projects.

Original_Search_Title: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Headline: Uganda: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Body_Text:
Kampala — SOME analysts have attributed the declining fortunes of the FDC to infiltration by state agents. This analysis is wrong.
FDC was founded on sentiments of a few people whose anger was not stemming from any fundamental disagreements but rather precipitated by factors like dissatisfaction with one's area of deployment, no deployment or dismissal from office.
The first writings of Dr. Besigye in 2000 complained of issues like katebe or failure by Museveni to listen to historical leaders, and narrated how some senior NRA commanders used to eat chicken in the bush while others ate cassava. These were not fundamental disagreements but rather personal concerns.
It was not a case of departure from the Movement ideology that hitherto fought for policy issues. For example, Besigye's first pronouncements were not denouncing the Movement but rather castigating President Museveni. In 2001, Besigye and his agents insisted that they were still part of the Movement but only wanted to reform its methods of work. That is why his 2001 posters were dominated by the yellow colour, to emphasis that he was not an odd man out in the system so as poach on the Movement support.
And surely he did. Besigye and group insisted on re-focusing the Movement to its original bush struggle methods of work. That was evidence to show that ideologically and structurally the Movement was clearly on course. The problem was with individuals and not the system. But this was a contradiction. If Individuals were bad, especially the head and founder, how then would the system be good. That is why none of those grumbling resigned from the Government to prove to the world that they were dissenting on serious principles. They all formed opposition groups after involuntarily leaving the Government. Such a party founded on petty selfish reasons cannot sustain a political cause that appeals to the entire country and therefore would not last.
It was not wrong to oppose President Museveni, but this was a different type of opposition because it was not based on principle. MPs who had joined PAFO, hoping that it was a genuine platform for parliamentary advocacy, abandoned it on discovering its sole hidden agenda of fighting President Museveni.
The Reform Agenda group first opposed the proposal to change the name to FDC. They also opposed the new alliance of Besigye and the PAFO members, on the grounds that the majority of PAFO MPs had opposed Besigye and harassed his supporters in the 2001 elections and were now selfishly turning to him to use his slightly better position to fight their new war with President Museveni.
Besigye accepted them not on principle but to increase his numbers and also on condition that he was to remain leader of any anti-Museveni group no matter what shape and form it took. Some of the reform agenda people were right to reject these people. The so-called harassment of Besigye supporters in 2001 was largely occasioned by these PAFO MPs especially those who were soldiers, but at the same time became the first to rush and embrace him a few months after.
FDC was formed under an adhoc arrangement that saw individuals temporarily abandon their differences to pursue a common agenda of opposing and removing President Museveni the man. They are now stranded with their own hatred for President Museveni and their agenda doesn't seem to pay quick dividends given the resounding rejection they have registered from Ugandans since 2001.
Consequently, some of their progressive members are asking whether it is sustainable to build a party based on the agenda of eliminating one individual. These and other challenges are for FDC to address in order to re-build the confidence of its members, especially those who may have joined it without knowing its clear vision and broader agenda. FDC must demonstrate to its members and to all Ugandans they seek to lead that their assignment goes beyond pulling President Museveni from power.
Therefore, I don't agree with the suggestion that FDC has been killed by state infiltration. Political infiltration is always the game with power contenders but it never hinders progress of any serious political activity. The problem is lack of an agenda that appeals to the masses.
Prophesies of doom such as the prediction of tsunami did not help the situation either; it was a wrong approach that could not convince Ugandans that in Besigye there was a better president than Museveni. FDC should be assessed looking at how it was formed and what aspirations it had during its inception. That is the only way we shall understand why it is growing weaker.
To my colleagues Hon. Otto and Hon. Okumu, the only option available for you is to trace these inherent weakness and discuss them openly with Besigye. Struggle to project the party beyond the single item agenda of being anti-Museveni. But if the going gets tough, don't get going. Come over to NRM.
The writer is the MP for Kibale County, Kamwenge District

Original_Search_Title: UNBS Suspends Sale of Omo in Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: UNBS Suspends Sale of Omo in Mbarara
Body_Text:
Mbarara — UGANDA National Bureau of Standards and police are investigating a case in which fake Omo was manufactured and distributed in Mbarara markets.
UNBS has suspended the sale of the suspected adulterated 20gms Omo sachet in Mbarara until investigations are complete.
This follows an incident in which Police, last week, impounded 20 bags of Omo detergent alleged to be counterfeit from Raheel Traders Ltd on Buremba Road in Mbarara town.
A trader Mr Milton Kusasira had complained to UNBS that the Omo he bought on August 14 from Raheel, which he stocked in his wholesale shop was fake.
Later the proprietor of Raheel, Mr Savani Riyakat, and Unilever Sales Representative, Paul Waswa were arrested on August 27 in connection with the fake detergent and later released on bond.
Mr Kusasaira said his customers complained that the Omo was like cassava flour and could not form lather. On returning it to Raheel, Mr Kusasira says Mr Riyakat rejected it and referred him to Unilever offices in Kampala. I decided to go to police because they did not help me yet I have been their customer for six years," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Cocainemania in Tema
Headline: Ghana: Cocainemania in Tema
Body_Text:
The substance that was hidden in18 slabs in the shape of a Bible and seized by the Tema police command recently, have now turned out to be what is known in the Ghanaian parlance as Kokonte (cassava flour) after it was sent to the Ghana Standard Board for analytical report.
The police are of the view that the substances were a scam, which was being executed by some criminals to dupe some people on the pretext of selling cocaine to them.
DSP Samuel Kwesi Ofori, acting Director of Police Public Relations told The Chronicle in an interview that "whatever way one sees it, but from both ends they are all criminal acts."
He explained that both the suspected criminals and the people who were suspected to have been victims of the fraudulent acts when identified by the police would be arrested since they were all illegal.
He continued that on August 28,the Tema Regional Command together with the Organized Crime Unit of the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) forwarded the stuff for analytical examination however; it proved to be corn flour and corn dough DSP Ofori said the Astra vehicle in which the suspected cocaine was concealed is not registered emphasizing that the registration number it bore was fake.
"The chassis number when checked with the Customs Excise and Preventive Service (CEPS) indicated that the car was not registered.
He intimated that all efforts to get the informant in the case to disclose his identity has proved futile while the police have not been able to arrest any of the suspects yet. He called on the members of the public who have information on the transaction to release it to the police
It would be recalled that on August 24 The Tema police located a vehicle around the Tema Port containing 18 slabs of substances contained in an Astra vehicle.
Speaking to members of the media, the Tema Regional Police Commander, Assistant Commissioner of Police (ACP) Kudalor said the Police had had a tip off that the said Opel Astra car was carrying some quantity of cocaine was heading towards the Tema Port and was expected to arrive to board one of the vessels.The Opel Astra car was subsequently found specifically at around the premises of the defunct Meridian Hotel, near the Harbour area.

Original_Search_Title: 2 Die After Eating Cassava
Headline: Sierra Leone: 2 Die After Eating Cassava
Body_Text:
Freetown — Foday Dabo, 4 and Moinamah Bawoh, 3 were Wednesday reportedly pronounced dead after they finished eating cassava prepared by their parents. The incident took place at Torpombu 2 Section in Kenema, eastern Sierra Leone.
According to Chernor Dabo, the father of one the deceased, he went out with his wife in search of food in the township but they were not successful.
"When we returned home we decided to cook cassava for the day's meal so that the family cannot go to bed hungry," he said.
He explained that few minutes after the kids had eaten the food they started complaining of stomach ache.
"Before we could rush them to the hospital, Foday had already dropped dead," he said, adding that Moinamah Bawoh died shortly after they arrived at the hospital.
The couple also complained that they experienced serious stomach ache after eating the cassava.
They are presently responding to treatment at the Kenema Government Hospital while Police are investigating the circumstances that led to the death of the two kids.

Original_Search_Title: Fragile Historic Truce Holds Out
Headline: Uganda: Fragile Historic Truce Holds Out
Body_Text:
Kampala — In a freezing air-conditioned conference room, delegates still wore sleepy faces - the result of having spent the night haggling. But though drowsy and tired, they still put pen to paper on the August 26, 2006 truce document. 365 days later, this fragile truce still holds, raising the hopes of hundreds of thousands.
The deal followed an intense month of negotiation, walk-outs and reneging by the parties to the talks. "We could not easily agree to a truce before getting assurances that they could not use it to regroup, re-arm and reorganise," said UPDF publicist, Maj. Felix Kulayigye. But information availed to Sunday Monitor indicates completely different reasons. At the time, the army's 5 Division was hot on the heels of two of the five LRA ICC indictees.
UPDF had intelligence that Raska Lukwiya was locked-down in Mucwini Sub-county, Kitgum, while Dominic Ongwen was trapped in Pader. These were high value leadership targets. Desperate to save them, LRA's second-in-command, Vincent Otti had declared a unilateral ceasefire on August 4, 2006 and asked the Ugandan government to reciprocate.
Dr Rugunda stalled, saying they were waiting to see the effect on the ground .This prompted the rebel delegation to walk out the next day, August 5, 2006.
Seven days later, the rebels were convinced to return by chief mediator and Southern Sudan's Vice President, Riek Machar.
By then troops from 5 Division were within reach of Lukwiya, who they engaged and killed in Obem Village, 7km southwest of Mucwini.
Since then there have been several alleged violations of the truce by either side. This is what the parties say: "We simply ignored all provocations by the LRA for the sake of peace," said Maj. Kulayigye.
But LRA's Chief negotiator Martin Ojur says: "We declared a unilateral truce first, they killed our people; they have been using the media to attack us. We chose to restrain our forces for the sake of peace," Ojur said in a telephone interview from Juba.
UPDF's alleged truce violations
August 2006 -UPDF forces kill LRA's Maj. Gen. Raska Lukwiya
September 2006 -UPDF attack rebels, killing three fighters in Amuru District
September 2006- UPDF surrounds LRA who were assembling at Owiny-Ki-Bul, southern Sudan
September 2006 -UPDF led a convoy of diplomats and journalists to Owiny-Ki-Bul against the rules
October 2006 -UPDF troops in southern Sudan arrested LRA negotiator, Rei Achama who was delivering food to LRA rebels.
October 2006 -UPDF surround LRA fighters who retaliate by engaging resulting in the death of army Captain Sam Muagrura of 91 Battalion deployed in southern Sudan, 115km North West of Owiny-Ki-Bul.
November 2006- UPDF restricts movement of LRA rebels while leaving northern Uganda to Owiny-Ki-Bul.
February 2007 -Hostile propaganda by President Museveni who allegedly called the LRA the Devil's Resistance Army at a Nairobi press conference
February 2007 -UPDF kills two LRA soldiers near the Sudan-Uganda border
May 2007- UPDF soldiers along the River Nile blocked LRA rebels from crossing from Eastern Equatoria to Western Equatoria
Source LRA
LRA's alleged truce violations
October 2006- LRA attempt to hijack a boat in Mangala, southern Sudan but are repulsed by southern Sudan forces
October 2006 -LRA ambush UPDF troops of 91 Battalion, killing Capt. Sam Mugarura near the banks of River Nile
