October 2006 -LRA stage multiple road ambushes on Juba-Nisitu, Juba-Gumbo and Nisitu-Jabuleni roads, killing 43 people and burning 12 vehicles. They also ambushed UPDF Intelligence Officer, Capt. Patrick Opira on Nisitu-Gumbo Road leaving one solder wounded.
November 2006 -LRA uproot cassava from civilians' gardens in Magwi, southern Sudan.
December 2006- LRA stage five ambushes on Torit-Korienglish Road, Jabul-Mugile Road leaving five civilians dead and four wounded. They also ambushed UPDF liaison officer, Richard Otto on Juba-Aru Road.
December 2006 -LRA rob business people near Boma and Tore and abduct an unspecified number of people.
January 2007-LRA stage an ambush in Kit and Manga, in southern Sudan wounding eight civilians and raided Luru Village for food and disarmed Southern Sudan soldier of his pistol and Submachine gun.
February 2007- UPDF 509 Brigade intercept a group of LRA rebels attempting to cross from southern Sudan into Uganda and kill two fighters.
April 2007- Seven civilian vehicles are ambushed at Kit, Aru Junction, Nimule-Mugule roads in southern Sudan killing 19 people.
May 2007- LRA commander Thomas Kwoyelo while crossing from Eastern Equatoria to Western Equatoria looted 100 goats and cows in Lariya and Adruba villages. Unspecified number of people killed. Rebels also raid the home of southern Sudan's Information Minister Samson Kwaje and disarm 13 militia men
Source UPDF

Original_Search_Title: Who Cares for the Aged?
Headline: Uganda: Who Cares for the Aged?
Body_Text:
Mukono — She is widowed and senile. At 95, Ms Bena Nakazi lives in complete uncertainty about what tomorrow may bring. Impoverished is what best describes the helpless old lady's life.
But regardless of the suffering that she has gone through, Ms Nakazi has had to persist. She doesn't live in total despair, as she has had to live each day the way it comes to avoid stressing herself every now and then.
And although some days might bring with them lots of gloom, the old lady says she is not about to give up. "I have always tried to find a way around my troubles," she says.
What constitutes the biggest part of the old lady's life is the dilemma of what to eat each day that sets in. And the moment she's able to find food, she considers her day a success. When Daily Monitor visited her, she was preparing a simple dish for lunch at her home down in the remote village of Kasanga, Ssi Sub-County in Mukono District.
Perched on a piece of Hessian square at the entrance of her back door, Ms Nakazi cheerfully peels the only cassava root that her small garden has luckily made available.
It is this single cassava root that ought to see her through this day and the day after, before she starts looking for food elsewhere. On her left are two cups, from which she sips what she would like to call tea.
But in actual sense, it's mere hot water with neither a beverage, nor sugar mixed in it.
"Hot water isn't good for humankind. But we have nothing to do to warm our bodies like on this cold morning," she says, "So we tend to fool our stomachs with hot water that what we are pouring in is tea."
Ms Nakazi says she has children but they do not help her. She says, instead they go to her home to get the food she grows.
Ms Nakazi is a reflection of the lives of hundreds of thousands of elderly people in Uganda. They are facing challenges at the time when they are powerless to make any meaningful change.
The 2002 National Census puts their number at 1.5 million. Although the figure is quite small in comparison to the other age sets, they are the most affected people in the country.
Recently the State Minister for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities, Mr Suliaman Madada, revealed that the government would start paying a monthly allowance to the elderly. The inactive poor old people would get Shs18,000, plus Shs10,000 if they are looking after disadvantaged children.
Donors will fund the pilot project, which will start this financial year in six districts.
Surprisingly, there is no policy to protect the elderly. Most of them die in the most inhumane way due to lack of health care and basic necessities.
Ms Deborah Kaijuka, the Executive Director of Uganda Reach the Aged Association, says the problem of the elderly suffering originates from the fading traditional social support system.
"People no longer live in homesteads or extended families where young adults supported their elderly parents. Instead many young adults prefer living in nucleated families," Ms Kaijuka says.
A homestead was like a chain of three segments where the active young adults were in the middle supporting both their parents and their own children.
Ms Kaijuka explains that the system has changed and now the young adults have moved to urban centres seeking better life and jobs, leaving the elderly people in the rural areas.
HIV/Aids
Sister Monica of the Good Samaritans, an NGO supporting and managing Bakateyamba Home for the Elderly at Nalukolongo in Kampala, says the new challenge facing the aged is HIV/Aids. "Many elderly people who would have retired from work are now shouldering the burdens of orphans of their children who died of Aids," Sister Monica says.
Ms Kaijuka concurs with Sister Monica. "Aids has eliminated the active middle age group thus breaking the link of support," she says. "Even some older people are infected with HIV/Aids since the time the disease broke out, many were still in the 40s and were sexually active." Ms Kaijuka says without a policy, it becomes very difficult to mobilise funds from donors yet the donors don't consider the elderly when giving grants.
She says the resources they get for the elderly are just 'a drop in the ocean'.
According to a draft of the National Policy on Older Persons, there will be an institutional framework in both the central and local government funding of national projects aimed at helping the elderly people. Unless a policy on the elderly is drawn, older people like Ms Nakazi will live and die in poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Hakuna Matata; a Favourite Pork Joint
Headline: Uganda: Hakuna Matata; a Favourite Pork Joint
Body_Text:
There are two joints adjacent to one another - Hakuna Matata 1 joint to the right and the newly opened Hakuna Matata 5 to the left on the road to Makindye Hill.
Main Attractions: The place is an attraction, first with a beautiful signpost spelling out what to expect on the menu - roast pork, chicken, fresh juice and snacks, cassava and potatoes and introduces available local dishes.
Being the speciality, the joint has been christened the 'pork republic'. There is a plus in terms of hygiene; what with a spotless white floor tiles.
The green flowers and towering trees in these tranquil environs create a good contrast to the white tiles. You won't wait forever to get served. There are a number of waiters and waitresses to attend to you. In the neat environs, are seats under green umbrella shades. This is your ideal place to catch up with friends after work, or over the weekend for a night out.
Ambience: It is both relaxing and energising depending on the crowd. Fridays and Saturdays are bubbly with good music.
Price range: Beers and tuskers go for Shs2,000 and Shs2,500 respectively. Roast pork is Shs2,000 per stick. For juice and fruits, you have to part with between Shs1,000 and Shs3,000. Pocket-friendly snacks are also available.
The crowd: Mixed crowd, a predominantly middle class who come around to catch up especially during the evening hours.
A.O.B: If you're a fan of football, there is 24-hour Dstv showing so you won't miss your big games. And for fans of Big Brother, it is screened live.
Place: Hakuna Matata 5
Location: Makindye
Accessibility: The joint is located by the roadside as you ascend Makindye hill from the city centre.

Original_Search_Title: Moxico - Dilolo Lake Commune to Have Tourism Complex This Year
Headline: Angola: Moxico - Dilolo Lake Commune to Have Tourism Complex This Year
Body_Text:
Luanda — An 11-branch tourism complex is being built at Lago Dilolo commune, Luacano district, eastern Moxico province, to encourage tourism exploration in the locality that has 12,734 inhabitants, mostly of whom are fishermen.
The complex will have, among other branches, restrooms, cafés, an area of assistance to tourists, restaurant, kiosks, game room and a recreational area around Dilolo Lake, which with 18 kilometres of length and seven wide is considered as the third biggest in Africa.
According to district's administrator, Pascoal Mucazu, the undertaking has been experiencing some delays due to difficulties encountered in transporting construction materials, mostly coming from neighbouring Republic of Zambia, but he secured that it will be concluded this year.
After speaking on the need to reap major profits from this tourist site and turn the country more attractive to the foreigners and encouraging them to invest, the municipal administrator appealed to the Hotel and Tourism Ministry, the provincial government of Moxico and the civil society to intervene in the exploration of the lake.
The official stressed the existence of beauty scenery, plenty of fish, rice, cassava production, factors contributing to the valuing of the area and commitment to the growth of Moxico.

Original_Search_Title: The Two Faces of Naguru
Headline: Uganda: The Two Faces of Naguru
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA'S residential areas like Wandegeya, Old Kampala and Nakasero are being swallowed by the spreading wings of the city. This has made places like Naguru Hill the option for the poor and the rich who wish to live near the city.
George Kibugujo, a commission agent, says getting a plot in Upper Naguru is as hard as the Biblical camel going through the eye of a needle. Prices range between sh20m to sh50m for a 50ft by 100ft plot, that is if you happen to get one.
"The advantage here is that the electricity poles are already in place, water is running, there is a low crime rate and the roads are good compared to Kololo," explains Kibugujo.
"The existence of Quality Supermarket in Ntinda, Ndere Troupe Centre in Kisaasi, Kadic health clinic and several schools give the place additional value."
However, unlike Kololo and Nakasero hills, which are strictly up market - Naguru has two faces. There is the striking Upper Naguru where having a tiled-roof on your house is a must. Homesteads have high wall fences to block the view of the shabby side of the suburb.
A walk in the neatly-tarmacked inter-connecting roads is a revelation of a serene atmosphere. The roads are littered with children learning how to ride bicycles and playing hide-and-seek behind 'whispering' palm trees. The children go to nearby plush schools like Kabira International and Kampala Parents schools. The little ones have a daycare centre in the neighbourhood. Here, trucks pick garbage daily. Water gushing out of huge pipes refilling swimming pools is audible to a pedestrian.
Most of the residents here drive four-wheel-drive vehicles. If you look carefully, it is not only their vehicles that are big, but their bodies too. The majority are trying to cut weight and this is where Kabira and BMK gyms in the neighbourhood come in handy.
Notices on gates scream: "This property is protected by Saracen Guards" making potential burglars flee like dogs with tails between their legs. Peeping into the compounds, is evidence that the shamba boys are doing their work. They are neatly trimmed like the beards of their bosses.
As you descend down the hill, you enter another world - that is Naguru Go-down and Naguru-Katale Zone. Here, children in tattered clothes compete for space with sheep, goats and rodents in the shanty homes.
Other than rent being affordable (between sh60,000 and sh100,000) for a bed-sitter, the value of property here is not any cheaper inspite of the area's ghastly appearance. The area is a small, closely-packed, dirty slum on either side of the road. There are small shops, butcheries, snack parlours, cassava roasters - all banded together. Cleanliness is unknown here. The area is carpeted with rubbish and broken glass. Semi-permanent houses are all over and face wherever the developers feel like.
Privacy is a word that does not exist here. A path may pass by one's door way.
Time check is 1:00pm and John Kachiga comes to the shop to buy two packets of Tyson (pineapple-flavoured) waragi (spirit), most probably for lunch.
What used to be posh Kampala City Council flats in the 1970s have become modern slums with algae matting the walls. They badly need of a coat of paint. The windows are gaping open with broken glass. The balconies are filled with broken chairs and rotten mattresses.
The tiled roofs of the houses facing Nakawa are in advanced stages of decay; timber doors and windows are falling apart and what used to be compounds are banana gardens.

Original_Search_Title: 7 Killed in Mpigi Crash
Headline: Uganda: 7 Killed in Mpigi Crash
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEVEN people were killed when a lorry and a bus collided head-on near Mpigi district headquarters on the Kampala-Masaka highway at 6:30pm yesterday.
Many of the victims died on the spot, while the injured were rushed to local medical centres after they were pulled out of the death trap by rescuers.
The Fuso lorry was heading to Kampala carrying cattle. The Mbarara-bound Isuzu coach belongs to Ibabu company.
Mangled bodies, carcasses of cattle and blood covered the accident scene. The vehicles were stuck together, and it took a breakdown to pull them apart. The villagers helped themselves to the carcasses.
Mpigi police commander Ivan Nuwamanya and the commissioner for traffic, Steven Kasiima, rushed to the accident scene. By 8:40pm, rescue work was still going on. Kasiima later said six of the dead were aboard the Fuso lorry which he said was from Ntugamo district.
The driver of the bus, Alex Kadogo, was the other victim, he said. Kasiima blamed the accident which occurred at Maziba Kalongo, on speeding and reckless driving.
He said Kadogo attempted to overtake a soloon car at a corner.
The bodies were laid by the road side and in a nearby cassava garden. Their identities could not be established by press time. The accident caused a massive traffic jam on the busy highway.

Original_Search_Title: Odm-Kenya Falling Apart As Kibaki Goes On Unperturbed
Headline: Kenya: Odm-Kenya Falling Apart As Kibaki Goes On Unperturbed
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I would have thought that I, Joseph, the dreamer of dreams, was most eminently qualified to become Kenya's special envoy to Sudan.
Apart from having been trained in diplomacy, I am able to dream in more than 10 languages, including two that Africa imported from Europe.
This is to say nothing of the fact that I was taught everything there is to know about the amoeba, the spirogyra and assorted flotsam and flotilla in Gikuyu.
But the Mzee who lives on the Hill thinks otherwise. You remember how in November 2005 we whitewashed the Mzee on the Hill in the referendum vote?
You remember how I told him that the time for change had come? Then I asked him to give me Francis Muthaura's job in Harambee House. He refused.
Now the Mzee on the Hill, otherwise known as President Mwai Kibaki, has refused to give me the job in Sudan. He has instead given it to the Mzee he described at Uhuru Park on December 30, 2002 as a man of "ineptitude and misrule".
He said that the man from Sacho, otherwise known as Mr Daniel arap Moi, was handing him a country in tatters and full of "miasma".
Now the "miasma man" is a Government ambassador and I, Joe, the dreamer of dreams, can only continue dreaming of better things to come.
I can only dream that maybe one day, Kiraitu Murungi and David Mwiraria will go back to Meru to eat cassava and look after goats while dreamers of dreams shows them how governments are run.
But that will be in post-ODM-Kenya and post-Narc-Kenya days. I was thinking that ODM-Kenya would take me to glory land.
But the thing seems to be dead, or dying. And the Man on the Hill is gleefully dancing on ODM-Kenya's grave.
That is why he can engage the old man of Kanu in true Mugumo tree style and ODM-Kenya can do nothing about it.
The ODM-Kenya family can only wallow in a web of political denial. They obtain in forlorn belief that this crowd puller will still rise up and walk again. When they form themselves in huge crowds they take this as a sign that the party is strong, vibrant and united.
They imagine that God is happy in heaven, looking at their party and saying "it is well". But a great person will remain a crowd puller, even in death. And ODM-Kenya was a great political outfit.
At the apogee of its glory, the thing straddled the country like the albatross. Its leaders gravitated from one corner of the country to the other, singing "Mapambano, mapambano, bado mapambano!"
They moved from here to there, stirring up wananchi with expectancy and sounding ultimatums to the President and the Government. They caused ministers to resign and forced the President to sack others.
Chris Murungaru went and George Saitoti went, too. Mwiraria and the powerful Kiraitu went. Kiraitu would lament to scribes, "What do you want and yet you made us to be sacked?" ODM-Kenya was not only barking, she was biting, too.
Then the big guns in ODM-Kenya, otherwise known as "luminaries", decided to become dreamers of dreams too. They dreamt of how they were going to make themselves big men, living on the big hill.
They started calling themselves horses and saying how they were the only horse that could defeat the horse called the big man in the big house on the Hill.
The big man was waiting for a good fight with them. But they were busy fighting each other, like foolish children. They even forgot about the Mzee on the Hill.
And so the Mzee began behaving as if they were dead and he was a Marabi dancer, dancing on their grave.
The Mzee on the Hill is now free to live his Mugumo dream. You remember how this Mzee once said that to remove Kanu; otherwise known as Baba Na Mama wa Taifa, was like trying to fell the Mugumo tree with a razor blade? And you remember how the Mzee from Sacho once told Raila Amolo Odinga, otherwise known as Tinga, alias Agwambo, that "Kanu iko na wenyewe"?
So what does it matter if "wenyewe" put on the colours of Narc-Kenya, Democratic Party, or whatever? What does it matter when a chameleon moves from a red surface to a green one? Does change of pigment make it a new chameleon?
Now Uhuru Kenyatta, who belongs to the Mugumo family tree, has decided to go back home. He is looking for excuses instead of real reasons to make a dignified return.
But has he not read where it is written: "I will set out and go back to my father and say to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me like your hired men?" For they say in the Good Book: "But the father said, 'Quick, bring the best robe and put it on him. Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet for this son of mine was dead and is alive. He was lost and is found."
Poor dreamer of dreams, the Mugumo tree and power alcohol in ODM-Kenya are shaking my dreams. ODM-Kenya has opened space for "ineptitude", "miasma" and big English words that we shall be saying in Uhuru Park in December 2011. But what a painfully long wait it is going to be!

Original_Search_Title: Weekend of Fine Repeat Comedies
Headline: Kenya: Weekend of Fine Repeat Comedies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Quick on the laughter lines of Marc Carmolleti's comedy Boeing, Boeing, Derek Benfield's Don't Lose The Place, which ended a fortnight ago, came yet another weekend of mirth last week, courtesy of comedies A Bridegroom for Sale and Tiga Miaki, the Kikuyu adaptation of Ray Cooney's How The Other Half Loves.
The common streak about them? They are all repetitions.
And while a group like Festival of Creative Arts have been setting a commendable standard in amateur theatre with its excellent casting, great scripts, superb sets, admirable marketing and good acting, the outfit has acquired an enviable following on the back of blasts from the past - When Did You Last See Your Trousers, She Ate the Female Cassava, A Fly in the Ointment and Look Who's Talking? which were all repetitions, albeit more professionally done.
Last week too, Heartstrings Kenya, a group that has been staging rib-cracking farces for the past 11 years, opened this month with Dennis Willis's comedy Bridegroom For Sale at the Alliance Francaise auditorium. The hilarious comedy was last staged nine years ago.
And next month, Festival will stage Benfield's riotous farce Bedside Manners "due to public demand" at the Alliance Francaise. The play was last performed by Mbalamwezi Players in 1998 and starred Suzanne Makena, Aleks Kamau and Tony Njuguna.
But why are theatre groups re-staging past hits?
"Because there are times when one wants a particular type of play or a specific theme and twist, but can't find it," says Sammy Mwangi, a director at Heartstrings.
"Then it could be that the play was not a hit when it was last performed and, as a director, you have seen it in a different light." Mwangi will next month direct Larry Shue's The Foreigner, which has not been performed before.
Eliud Abuto of Festival looks at redoing plays differently. "Local theatre has been suffering from lack of an audience for years. And re-staging hits is a way of bringing them back and thus creating audience loyalty after which one changes tact," he says. "Ray Cooney's Chase Me, Comrade and Francis Durbridge's Deadly Night Cap, which Festival will stage before the year ends, are not repetitions."
Titi Wainaina will direct fast rising theatre darling Francis Amos who will play Ferris, the manager of a hotel in Naivasha where Roger (Joshua Mwai) and Sally (Mbeki Mwalimu) arrive for a secret romantic escapade after leaving at home their spouses - Helen (Hellen Waithera), Roger's wife, and Sally's hubby Geoff (Derrick Amunga). But Helen and Geoff had also planned a steamy weekend together... in the same hotel!
Isn't repeating themed plays a way of short-changing theatre-goers while trying to remain relevant? "Kenyan theatre-goers are very discerning." says Mwangi. "And we gave them a raw deal when we staged A Fish Out of Water. We will never re-stage a play which is less than 10 years old."
Well, for sampling a fresh play perhaps one might consider watching Eugene Ionesco's classical The Bald Soprano, which ends today at the Phoenix Players.

Original_Search_Title: Wilson Road and Its Crooks
Headline: Uganda: Wilson Road and Its Crooks
Body_Text:
Kampala — Time check. 8.30a.m. In mid-town Kampala, Wilson Road is already a hive of activity. Forget that it is a rainy morning. No one seems to have been held up by the rain. Food vendors brave the drizzle, darting back and forth across the street.
Clutching flasks of tea and snack plates covered with polythene bags, they serve breakfast to their early regulars. Meanwhile, shop owners are opening up for the day. But there is this group of six youthful men that takes shelter on the veranda of the building that formerly housed a branch of Cineplex Cinema.
They are heavily bejewelled (in the flashy, cheap jewellery commonly known as bling bling), with several chains around their necks and others hanging off their trouser pockets and waists. They are clad in the latest trendy buggy jeans and tight casual shirts. Several punch keys on flashy mobile phones while others hold a heated argument.
On this rainy morning, I watch the group perched at the seat nearest to the window at Bon Apetit restaurant, where I am the first customer. As I take my breakfast, one of the young men leaves the group and struts across the street. He walks by the restaurant only to go by again, holding a car headlight.
He passes by a third time, this time without the headlight. When he eventually enters the restaurant, the fairly tall, dreadlocked, dark complexioned guy with a heavy-set face and bulging chest wears one of the heaviest scents of perfume I've been subjected to. He is the second customer in the restaurant and he takes the farthest seat opposite mine. Placing his order with a lot of impatience, he continues to punch keys on his handset, and taking brief breaks to throw me very inquisitive glances.
The sight of these young men is not a rare one around here. "We recognise most of them. We serve them tea sometimes. One day they go to those restaurants and take the most expensive breakfast on the menu, then the next day, that same person comes to me and orders dry tea and cassava. I think when they land a deal, they spend all the money in one day," says Annah Nanziri, a food vendor.
According to Nanziri, these men are always here. But unlike other people who are either hawkers or shop operators, these men don't have any business you can put a finger on. "Sometimes they are the first to arrive here in the morning. They don't operate shops and neither do they sell anything tangible. Some of them started coming here as young boys, but are now grown men. New members keep joining while others just disappear," she adds.
Wilson Road is one that owes its fame to this class of people. Generally referred to as Abafere (downtown slung for conmen), the sight of these young men is a nightmare to those who have had a close shave with them. To the passerby, these guys are the styled up, well-off youth, making their best attempt at looking busy. But to the neighbouring business owners, they are a crowd that would rather not be associated with.
James Akankwatsa, a newspaper vendor, narrates an ordeal that recently befell one of his colleagues at the hands of these 'refined' rogues. " Two of them came to my colleague and told him that someone in an office up in Kirumira Towers needed newspapers.
They guided him to the top-most floor. When they reached a certain corridor, they held him to a window and told him to choose between his life and all the money he had made, plus his mobile phone. They threatened to throw him out of the window if he didn't give the money up. Of course he chose his life. Now he goes without food because he is trying to recover all that he lost," he relates.
When the drizzle lets up, the remainder of the group, which looks to have hatched a plan from their argument, takes off in different directions. Betty Nabukenya, the pay phone attendant on the veranda next to where the group was convening, reveals that this is normally their place of rendezvous.
"They have been meeting here from the time the cinema was still open. Sometimes they would spend most of the day in the bar behind the building (DV 8), but it was also closed. Now this place is a venue for lunch hour prayers, so they can't go in. They just stand here or sit on the pavement and argue about football. Sometimes they talk about cars," she says.
The bafere have given Wilson Road such a reputation that it is one of those places where you can easily be conned of all your belongings in broad daylight. Since they don't look any bit like thieves and instead look like 'loaded' young men, people say you can never be suspicious of them at all. There is also a great paranoia that nothing genuine can ever be purchased from this side of town.
This perception however, in Eddie Nisha (a phone dealer)'s opinion, is simply not true. Nisha, who has been working from here since 1999, says that the bafere don't hang around here anymore. He adds that the Operation Wembley raids dismissed them though the street's reputation had already been tarnished.
"Those guys you saw there are genuine. They may look idle, but they work. Most of them are in the second-hand car business. One buys a car and they drive it around for a few days while they look for someone to buy it. When it is finally sold off, they buy another one. They earn their living," he says. But when you've listened to an experience such as the one that came upon the newspaper vendor, you are hesitant to share Nisha's opinion. In the eyes of many Kampalans, the bafere have become an experience people would sooner be ridden of.
Taking a quick look along Wilson road, all looks well; with most businesses picking up as the day progresses. But before most business owners and their customers can even imagine that all is indeed well, the street would have to be physically cleared of these lounging groups of youths.

Original_Search_Title: Woman to Serve One Year for Aiding Defiler
Headline: Uganda: Woman to Serve One Year for Aiding Defiler
Body_Text:
Jinja — Jinja chief magistrate Cissy Mudhasi last week sentenced a woman to one year's imprisonment in Kirinya Prison for aiding the defilement of a 14-year girl.
Aisha Nandego,33, a resident of Mafubira Trading Centre in Jinja district is said to have enticed the minor into the arms of a man only identified as Junior, by giving her cassava chips.
The girl told court that Ms Nandego told her about a man who was rich and loved her. Ms Nandego allegedly told her they would reap big if she accepted to date the man.
Although the girl declined, Ms Nandego could not hear of it.
She asked the girl to accompany her to the market only to lead her into the man's house.
It is said that after the two entered the house, Ms Nandego walked out leaving the girl inside. She then closed the two in the house at about 8pm. The girl said the man, whom she had not met beforethe incident, repeatedly and forcefully had unprotected sex with her.
Court heard that Ms Nandego unlocked the door at 10pm to let the girl out. She said Ms Nandigo took her to the man's room three other times. Court heard that Ms Nandego unlocked the door at 10pm to let the girl out.

Original_Search_Title: Private Sector to Export to Oman
Headline: Rwanda: Private Sector to Export to Oman
Body_Text:
Kigali — Information from Rwanda Investment and Export Promotion Agency (Riepa) indicates that the private sector is to start exporting food stuffs to Oman.
"Several (members of the private sector) have come inquiring about the Oman market. Actually one wants to start exporting pineapples," Jean Guy Africa said. He is Riepa's director for traditional exports.
The private sector's interests in the Middle East market come after Riepa shipped 35 tonnes of assorted food products to Oman.
The products included cow ghee, honey, Agashya passion fruit juice, sweet bananas, cassava flour, and chili.

Original_Search_Title: Justice Too Costly, Say Experts
Headline: Kenya: Justice Too Costly, Say Experts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Many Kenyans have no access to justice, and where it is guaranteed it is often delayed. Poor infrastructure, corruption and ignorance have escalated injustice.
This was said during a live debate on KTN's weekly interactive show, Newsline.
Four panellists engaged the audience in a debate on whether the justice system works. Eighty-five per cent responded negatively.
Dr Ekuru Aukot, executive director of Kituo cha Sheria, singled out inefficiency as the greatest impediment to judicial services.
"Most lawyers and the Judiciary have used Kituo cha Sheria as a dumping site where they refer cases they do not want to handle," he said.
As a result, he noted, people are forced to camp at the institution's offices from as early as 5am. He accused lawyers of abandoning cases they feel would not benefit them.
Mr James Maina, a Mathare resident, said many Kenyans cannot access justice due to prohibitive costs and ignorance. He said ignorance has exposed many slum dwellers to suffering.
On the recent security operations in Mathare's Kosovo area, Maina said many people in the slums would rather bribe police than be arrested and charged in a court of law. He said Mathare residents fear courts.
But Prof Gilbert Ogutu of the University of Nairobi traced Judiciary to a society's quest to resolve disputes.
"At the Luo Council of Elders, there are cases we handle at the family court. A committee of people sit and discuss. You cannot sentence an hungry person to seven years in prison for stealing cassava or a hen."
Ogutu is also the secretary general of the Luo Council of Elders.
But in the case of statutory law, such a person, on pleading guilty, would be jailed.
Ogutu urged Kenyans to use cultural laws to settle family, land and children disputes since Kituo cha Sheria can only handle 30 cases a week.
Mr Charles Kihara, a lawyer, said court filing fee bars many people from seeking justice.
A member of the audience accused lawyers and judges of perpetuating corruption. "Most lawyers take bribes from clients to ensure cases do not go through," he said.
The Government was urged to employ more lawyers to help Kenyans who cannot afford the cost of justice.

Original_Search_Title: Art Inspired By Personal Tragedy
Headline: Uganda: Art Inspired By Personal Tragedy
Body_Text:
Kampala — HE does not have enough money to put up an art exhibition but his work heralds a budding talent.
Edward Ojara is a 20-year-old student who completed S.6 last year. He is a self-taught artist whose art has been largely inspired by his misfortunes.
"I lost my parents. This forced me to work hard to support myself through school. Since S.4, I have been selling my art to teachers and villagers to raise pocket money and school fees," he says.
With his meagre income of about sh30,000, he is able to contribute to his family's domestic needs.
Ojara's work carries with it a rare sense of maturity, mainly portraying human forms and wild animals. He adds value to his work by turning some of the sculptures into water jars and flower vases.
This ability to make his work practical is commendable.
He employs rudimentary methods and improvised material to finish his work. His main medium is clay, iron bars and unrefined wood.
Sometimes, he accentuates his work with dyes, cassava flour, timber husks and cement bags. Thus, his work often lacks fine finish and detail. This does not, however, rob his work of creativity and purpose.
Ojara came close to recognition last year when he moulded a clay sculpture of President Museveni.
"I wanted to thank him for initiating free education, which I benefited from," he says.
When he reached Boma Ground where the President was addressing people, the security was tight. By the time he reached the presidential podium, Museveni had left.
Ojara is now eagerly waiting to join university, hoping this will further his career.

Original_Search_Title: Live-in in-laws: How Peaceful? (4)
Headline: Nigeria: Live-in in-laws: How Peaceful? (4)
Body_Text:
Hi, In-laws are generally perceived as "enemies" by couples. They are usually regarded as unnecessary interference in the scheme of things. Except in a few cases, many couples would rather keep their in-laws at arm's length. After all, too much familiarity breeds contempt. But as much as many couples would want to, the extended family practice in the African culture may not permit it. In fact, women (they happen to be more affected) who do not tolerate the infiltration of their partner's family members are often given social tags. The foundation of most relationships and homes have been terribly shaken due to the influence of in-laws. So, what happens when couples find it impossible to keep their extended family out of their homes? How do they survive, or maintain a balance? We spoke to some respondents who shared their experiences with us. Our fourth respondent, Tinu, (47), who says the first four years of her marriage was hell, continues her story below. Happy reading!
Ade's late night outings soon became a regular habit. He would stay out till the wee hours of the morning, returning when most decent people would have slept and woken up midnight to pray or do something useful. If I refuse to open the door, mama would be on hand to do it. She would insist that I am being wicked and wanted him to be attacked while waiting at the door. This is someone that was not attacked all the way from wherever he was coming from, but would now be attacked right at his doorstep. But, what did I expect from mama?
As far as mama was concerned, everything anyone does against me is alright. I must have been at fault, but everything I did was wrong because I am bad, and because one cannot expect anything better from Ijebu people.
As soon as my first child started speaking, the first few words she learnt were derogatory remarks about Ijebu people, courtesy of my mother-in-law. She would call anyone who annoys her Ijebu we mu gari, or Ijebu onigbe ndi. This is to tell you that Ijebu people are either backward or inferior to other people. That Ijebu people are so unsophisticated with their choice of food or taste buds so they can eat nothing else but garri (cassava). That Ijebu people are so unhygienic such that when they go to toilet, they do not bother to clean their buttocks. Other times, she would just say, Ijebu ologun, meaning that Ijebu people are synonymous with juju. You will be surprised what would come out of these children's mouth. And the brain behind it all was mama. It was as if I'd committed a crime by being an Ijebu woman. Why would she judge me by my race and not as an individual? Sometimes, I would just sit and imagine how many false stories she has been telling the children about me and my people.
The more I thought about my children, the more I had to also think about my husband who was now becoming more of a stranger to me than ever. We had become like cat and mouse in the same house, and I was beginning to avoid him like the plague, just to maintain the peace and not give mama further chance to run me down in front of my husband and my children. There was now a limit to how much complaints I could make, and who I could make the complaints to.
Then one day, my mother-in-law came home with two young ladies from her home town. She said they were her relatives and had just concluded their primary school education. Their parents were poor and could not sponsor their education any further, so she asked them to send the two girls to her in Lagos, promising to help them out one way or another. She, therefore, decided that the girls should be shared between her two sons. Since it was obvious that we both needed house helps, she decided that they should come and work for us, while we trained them in the craft of their choices in return. She then took one to my brother-in-law and left one with us. I resented the idea but there was little I could do since my husband had already agreed.
The young girls mama claimed had just finished primary school were already grown up and ready for marriage. The younger one who was left with us was 17 years. How could these sort of big girls just be leaving primary school? It was obviously not true. But who is to say otherwise when mama had announced? I could only stare at them in amusement. Little did I know that the whole plan will not be so amusing for long.
All that the girl did was to clean the house and follow my husband to the office. They said she was going to learn how to make soap, since my husband had a soap factory then. Soon, they said she was not learning again, but was going to replace the cashier who had just resigned. My husband and this girl now left home early each morning together and returned late in the night together.
The twins were already two years old by this time. Mama's troubles knew no bounds after this and never failed to ridicule me in front of this lady. Soon, mama started singing a new song. My reason for being antagonistic to her and her son was simply because of the young girl. I was plain jealous of her. She insisted that I should not worry too much as the girl would soon find her level and move out of my home. By this time, the girl who had been very respectful on arrival had become something else. She had stopped sweeping or even running any errands around the house. She only listened to mama and my husband. And since both of them were even out of the house for most part of the day, she was of no use to me. Even if she had been home, it was obvious she now considered me not important enough in the scheme of things in the house.
I didn't read much meaning to all their activities until one day this girl told me she was moving out as planned. One of her relatives had asked her to move in with them, so that she could get a proper job for her. I felt an uneasy relief because somehow, I was suspicious about the whole business.
About three months after her movement, the cat was let out of the bag. My sister-in-law visited me at the shop and made the revelation. She told me that Bola (my supposed housemaid) was pregnant for my husband and that he had rented an apartment for her in Surulere. She has, therefore, sent the other girl out of her home.
Luckily for her, her husband had not touched his own girl, or, at least, she was not pregnant yet. My brother-in-law's wife told me that she overheard the discussion of the development when she visited her husband's office. She had been surprised to see Mama and one of her friends from her hometown. In actual fact, the two girls were the woman's relatives. She was the brain behind their coming to Lagos. She opined that she suspected mama to have asked the woman to recruit those girls for her sons, since she had never hidden the fact that she never liked us, her daughters-in-law. She advised me not to allow them bring the girl home, nor kick me out as was their intention. She would support me against them, no matter what, she promised.
That night marked another turn for the worse in our relationship. I could not hold back my anger and tears as soon as he entered. I had avoided mama all day, just so she would not suspect that I had already been informed of their game plan. I wanted to thrash things out with my husband alone. But you can trust mama to intervene in the matter that she was not invited. She did all the bragging on behalf of my husband and the lady. She told me I could do nothing as they have been able to overcome all my witchcraft and juju that I have been using over her son. That her son is now finally free of me and my hold on him. That night, Ade became a little spoilt brat. He could not even stand up for himself, but allowed his mother to go on ranting like a bitch at me. I called him all sorts of names. But it did not change what had already happened.
As soon as he was out of my grip, Ade made for the door and did not show up for several days.
I could not say that I was actually shocked on hearing the news. It was more like my fears were being confirmed at last. Somehow, things continued to degenerate between Ade and I, such that I felt that he had been bewitched by these women. Someone who used to be so caring and loving, had now almost turned into a total stranger. He would not come home for days, except to give me our feeding allowance and check on the children. I was forced to live in mama's company more or less, as it was now just myself, the children who had practically become her soldiers, and the great mama.
But despite everything, we still managed to make love once a while. It was not so much out of love, but for the fact that I just had to find some relevance in the whole mess. The more I told my sad story to relatives and friends, the more I was advised on the need to stick to my guns and remain in my matrimonial home for whatever it was worth. They opined that since he was still coming home even if only once a week, it meant there was still something to be salvaged in the union. I must ignore mama and concentrate on myself and my husband. I was now neck deep in a competition for my husband's favours.
On such days as God would have it, I took in again. However, I lost it mysteriously in the third month to the delight of my mother-in law. Prior to this time she'd been telling me bluntly to leave the children and pack out of her son's house since he now has a new wife. My continued stay in the house was forcing him to pay rent to some landlord. If I move out, they would be able to move into the house and cut unnecessary costs. Can you imagine all these coming from a mother? Soon, her outbursts started attracting neighbours, but I kept my cool as advised by friends and relatives.
To my surprise, mama managed to win over my sister-in-law, the same one who promised to stick by me against them, no matter what. That one now visited her almost on a weekly basis with foodstuff and provisions. They would talk for hours, exchanging jokes and recalling past events. Mama would never say anything good about me despite all I did for her. Right until I was forced to pack out, I was still washing all her clothes, while I'd resumed my pounding sessions right after giving birth to the twins.
One Saturday afternoon, I came home from my shop to take something when I noticed that mama had a visitor in her room. As I didn't want to disturb her, I went about what I came for. As fate would have it, I was stepping out of my room just at the same time the woman was leaving and so we jammed at the corridor leading to the exit door. The visitor was mama's friend, Bola's aunt. I knelt down on both knees to greet her and on rising felt a sharp pain in my abdomen. Immediately, I was in a pool of blood. I fell down again, reeling in pain and I know my mother-in-law must have been genuinely shocked. She begged the woman to assist her carry me, while shouting for help. I was taken to the hospital by one of our neighbours and thus lost the pregnancy. It was while in hospital, that I decided I'd had enough of mama's troubles. If I wanted my life and good health, it was better for me to let go. If God still wanted me to be married, he would see to how Ade would come back to his senses and become the man he ought to be.
On my arrival from hospital, my mother-in-law became distant and began visiting her home more often. My relationship with my husband became ice-cold. All forms of intimacy came to a halt as he seldom visited home. Most times, his manager brought our feeding allowance.
But for the fact that the children remained with me, I would have been driven out right after my return from hospital. Bola's baby turned out a girl and a lavish party was thrown to mark its birth. She was now the apple of my husband's eye as he obeyed her beck and call!
Mama too was the dotting mother-in-law who cooked and washed her clothes, besides caring for the child. After a full year of living on my own more or less with the children, my relatives advised me to come back home. If my husband still wished to marry me, he could file a new application asking for my hands. When I made this known to him, he made no fuss about it until he'd gone to consult his mother. Mama came to inform me that no one was against my leaving since my husband didn't want me again. The only clause was that I could not leave with the children. I was horrified, but on consultations with my family, I was advised to leave them behind as she could never harm them. It was obvious that she loved the children. Her grouse for whatever reason only God knew, was with me.
That was how I left my home and children after four years of marriage. Even some of my husband's relatives greed that it was the best thing to do under the circumstance. As long as I remained, my home and even my husband will know no peace. Mama wanted her children to have children, but no wives. Before my arrival, she and my sister-in-law were not so friendly because she was fond of causing trouble between the couple. More so, her husband was a stubborn person right from childhood. So she couldn't penetrate too much into their affairs. But the reverse was the case with my husband.
Anyway, as I said, God is always on the side of the patient and the oppressed. It may only take a while. Mama took over my children, while my husband pitched his tent with his new wife. But the love soon went sour and turned to hatred. Exactly a year after I left, the two of them broke up and Bola returned to her aunty's house with her child. At the peak of their problems, he'd packed into another rented apartment, refusing to come home to mama. The fall out of the two love birds caused a strain in my mother-in-law's relationship with Bola's aunt. Rumour even had it that mama had accused her friend of backing out of their agreement. Whatever it meant, only God knows. Anyway, he came back to look for me a few months after Bola's departure.
Bola too had left without her child who was also placed in mama's care along with mine. He told me that he'd been informed by one of my friends that I was staying with my uncle and wanted to know how I was faring. He asked why I'd not bothered to come and check on my children. He wanted to know also if I was seeing any man. Naturally, one would want to bluff and express anger and regret. My husband is a very simple and caring man, but for his mother and the influence and power she wielded over him.
In short, that was how he started visiting me again, and expressed the desire to reunite. But my parents suggested that my mother-in-law must accompany her son to come and ask for my hand again. Mama was forced to come when her son told her he would have nothing to do with her again if she failed to allow him. Anyway, before the end of the second year, I came back to my house. Mama was forced to return to her house too and we have all been living in peace, at least what appears to be peace, ever since.

Original_Search_Title: The Plight of Congolese Women
Headline: Uganda: The Plight of Congolese Women
Body_Text:
These women are unfortunate because neither the local community nor the UPDF wants them. They don't fit anywhere since they are not citizens or refugees and cannot even speak the local language
Their eyes shone in anticipation as they gathered one by one on the dirty veranda in Kasubi, one of Gulu's settlements, obviously curious about the strangers present. In one corner of the veranda lay dirty saucepans of leftover food and cassava leaves that are a staple in the Congolese diet. Their dark skinned malnourished children are a marked contrast to their bleached faces and dark limbs popularly referred to as Mirinda-Pepsi.
Like their lifestyle that has been branded immoral, this is the theme of many spiteful and hilarious songs composed by local artistes in Northern Uganda. But that is the least of their worries.
"Survival is very hard without real jobs or reliable sources of income. When I came here in 2002 with a Ugandan soldier from DRC, I had anticipated better life.
Unfortunately, the man disappeared after a few months. Since then, I have been struggling to sustain myself and two children with the little I can get, and help from fellow Congolese," Sola Colleta says.
Colleta owned a grocery shop back home, which she relinquished to her relatives. Now, she makes a living plaiting hair for as little as Shs500.
Seven-months-pregnant Lucy Naji weeps as she narrates how another man denied fathering her baby. She turned to him for support after being abandoned by the UPDF soldier who brought her to Uganda. "To get money, I am forced to use any means. If you meet a man who can offer it, you end up going with him, only for him to also abandon you," she laments.
These are typical experiences of scores of Congolese women in Northern Uganda, who trekked from the DRC to Uganda with the Uganda People's Defence Forces in 2001.
They dreamed of love and life, but once in Uganda, fate turned on them as their lovers either died or abandoned them.
Destitute, is what the women settled in communities on the outskirts of barracks and some IDP camps where they live in abjection, trying to make ends meet, are. Amuru, Pajimu, Alero, Pajule, Pabo, Kasubi, Konypacho, Koro, Kerulen, Kanyagoga are some of the places they have settled.
Food and water shortages, inadequate medical care and accommodation, hostility of the natives and insecurity are challenges they battle daily. It is difficult to estimate how many Congolese women eke a living on the fringes of society, but locals claim there are hundreds.
Destitutes
"When you are sick, no one can treat you unless you have money or documentation of marriage to a soldier. But most of us are not in official marriages. When someone dies, we can't even bury so some people are just thrown in the bushes," says Betty Langu who shares stuffy, cramped quarters with four other women and five children in Pabo IDP camp.
The Congolese women in this camp live in such desperation they are willing to talk to anybody who might offer some hope. Most are either pregnant, carrying infants or trailed by thin, filthy toddlers.Francis Odora, the LC 1 of Small Gate Kasubi where Colleta and Naiji live, reveals that most of the Congolese women have resorted to prostitution to make ends meet.
Hostile conditions
This, alongside their poor sanitation and aggressiveness has prompted negative feelings from the community. Their situation is appalling but we haven't been able to do anything to help them.
However, in spite of these vices, a few have managed to remarry and integrate. The community is overtly hostile, evidenced by name-calling (Congole), spiteful comments, songs and negative attitude toward them. One resident accused the women of spreading HIV. Local leaders also revealed that the prevalence of HIV/Aids is high in these communities.
"They should have known that the soldiers had wives at home. I hate them because they are promiscuous. They hang out in bars and give sex for money," says Sam Otoo, adding that they are materialistic, and eat meat and chicken when they get money instead of investing in a better life. They are high maintenance, he says, so men use and dump them.
"These women are unfortunate because neither the local community nor the UPDF wants them. They don't fit anywhere since they are not citizens or refugees and cannot even speak the local language. For this reason, no civil society or government has come out to help them," reveals social worker Sandra Oder, founder of Children's Environmental Health Network, an NGO in Gulu.
Oder has visited a group of Congolese women who were languishing in the 4th division military hospital in Gulu barracks.
"Many of them were terminally ill and were lying on the floor under the care of children, without food or medication. I volunteered to provide food for some weeks. But this can never solve all their problems," Oder says. She recommends drafting a formal system to recognise and manage foreign women in conflict areas, brought by armed forces.
However, UPDF Spokesperson for Northern region Chris Magezi, dismisses such claims as ridiculous. He says the UPDF in Gulu has a record of only 45 soldiers married to Congolese wives who are well catered for in the barracks and medically in the army hospital.
"These women making claims can best be described as one-time girlfriends whom we never, as an institution, brought here. If they voluntarily followed our battalion that came on foot, what did you expect us to do? Chase them?"
Magezi argues that the army does not marry as an institution and cases of abandonment are the personal business of the soldiers. This casts a shadow on issues of child support that the women desperately need sorted out. To him, this is an immigration problem. "Our (immigrations) is weak. How can they allow people to come into the country without documentation? This is endangering our security," he says.
Going back home
At the end of the day, having been rejected by the institution that is responsible for their migration to Uganda, they can only hope that one day, they will find their way home.
"If only I could afford it, I would go back home. Just look at me. I am a grown woman hassling with life here in Uganda yet I had a good clothes business back at home," Akombe Mbugu says sadly.

Original_Search_Title: Simba Celebrates With Bonanza
Headline: Nigeria: Simba Celebrates With Bonanza
Body_Text:
Lagos — Simba Group of companies, Kary-Go tricycle assemblers in Nigeria, has kicked-off a bonanza tagged 'Simba scratch and win' to mark its 20 years operation in the country. Customers who purchase the company's products within the bonanza period will stand a chance of wining prizes ranging from motorcycles, generators, rechargeable lanterns, branded caps and pen.
Speaking to Business Trust on the bonanza in Lagos, Vinay Grover, Managing Director, Simba Group of Companies said they embarked on it to show appreciation to its numerous customers, who have been with them since inception.
"We are appreciative of our customers' patronage. They are loyal customers and have been with us through thick and thin. So the only way to show our gratitude is to institute something like this, where they can benefit freely from us," he said.
Apart from the scratch and win bonanza, the Simba chief executive said the company's loyalty scheme as well as its annual dealers conference were instituted to give back to its customers.
Simba Group, apart from being a pioneer assembler of motorcycles in Nigeria also popularised the conversion of cassava grinding diesel engines to power diesel engines in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Logari's Offers Quality Traditional Dishes
Headline: Uganda: Logari's Offers Quality Traditional Dishes
Body_Text:
My first encounter with Logari's resto was several years ago when they were ensconced in a tiny space the size of postage stamp, somewhere in the Diamond Trust building. At that time, it seemed very much of a Jua kali operation with even something as rudimentary as tablecloths being absent. Nevertheless, the food was always an outstanding feature and the prices amazingly pocket-friendly.
Many years later, and having acquired far bigger premises in a very prime and key location, there is simply no let up in the quality of the food with the prices still very accommodating in this era of food and other related inputs costing a pretty penny.
The secret has to be based on turn over since the place is capable of accommodating a good 70 or 80 souls at any one time. Luo would appear to be the lingua franca and this should come as no surprise since the food is in the main from the North, as in Acholi land. Well, this is not a bad idea since the places that serve local food from Buganda all tend to be very parochial, not to speak of Indian or Chinese for that matter.
Last Saturday, in the company of my young brother and being in the vicinity of Logari's, we ventured forth and decided to have a meal. For some strange reason, the waitress could not fathom the request that we would share the millet since a double dosage would be more than we could both readily consume and sure enough she brought two orders.
Somehow, she got to understand the need to return one of them and so we ended up with smoked chicken, smoked fish and matooke, rice, sweet potatoes and a teaser of greens.
The rice was an awful let down being slimy and woefully small in portion whereas it was quite the contrary with the millet. The matooke was expertly done while the dry fish and chicken were in every sense of the word ample.
The trademark of the sauces at this joint is the interplay with a groundnut paste that is combined with ground sesame paste and this forms the sauce. Thus any chicken, meat or fish that have been charcoal-grilled are then stewed in this concoction.
People from that part of the world are very fond of this type of cooking and they never seem to tire of it. I would suggest that one orders a side dish of that formidable and wonderful sauce that is known as boo/gobe in ground nut and sesame paste.
For those who may not be in the know, boo/gobe are the leaves of a legume (cow pea leaves) that are best gathered when they are young and traditionally they are never washed. Rather, they are sun dried to get rid of the insects and then they are beaten so as to remove the sand. However, in a modern day kitchen, I would recommend that they be washed, dried and cut into small pieces.
Prompt service
I am glad to see that Logari's are doing well and continue to turn out food that is not only tasty but promptly brought to one's table. The décor is unimpressive and the waiters could be better attired and given a better command of what they are serving.
It would be nice to be informed that the only part of the chicken available is the back rather than have it served without any forewarning. One might wish to be served another dish. Oh well, such is the world that we live in that such common courtesy is the rule than the exception.
Place: Logari's Restaurant
Location: Buganda Road, opposite Central Police Station
Rating: X
Smoke-Free Zone: Not sure
Type of food: So called Afro-Nile
Price range: Snacks: chips and chicken Shs3,500, chips and liver Shs3,000, chips and sausages Shs2,500, plain chips Shs1,000, Katogo Shs1,000, sausages Shs600, chapatti Shs50. Lunch: smoked chicken Shs3,000, smoked beef Shs2,500, smoked fish Shs2,500, Angara Shs2,500, beef stew Shs2,500, chicken stew Shs3,000, goat stew Shs2,500, fresh fish Shs2,500, molokony Shs2,000, boo gobe Shs2,000, Malakwang Shs2,000 - all served with a choice of matooke, rice, millet, yams, pumpkin, cassava, posho, sweet potatoes
Recommended Dishes: Smoked fish, boo/gobe, smoked chicken
Service: The food gets there within minutes but the hired help tend to be sourly and generally not very helpful and as for those uniforms, surely they have seen better days.
Ambience: A lot better than when they used to be at Diamond Trust, still room for improvement.
Sound level: Not the best
Opening hours: Lunch everyday but never on Sunday.
RATING
XXXX: UNRIVALLED
XXX: EXCELLENT
XX: VERY GOOD
X: WORTH A VISIT
NONE: SATISFACTORY to POOR
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The menu listing may change without notice.

Original_Search_Title: JCI Limbe Launches FCFA 3m Contest
Headline: Cameroon: JCI Limbe Launches FCFA 3m Contest
Body_Text:
The Junior Chambers International, JCI, Limbe Chapter, has launched an FCFA 3-million Best Business Plan, BBP, writing contest for young Cameroonians aged between 18 and 40.
The President of the Limbe Chapter, Takor Takor, broke the news of the competition during a press briefing recently.He said the competition was open to all interested young persons who have got some fresh business ideas that could be developed to the benefit the community around them.
As to the nature of the competition, Limbe JCI Public Relations Officer, PRO, Dona Forbin, said every competitor would be expected to explicitly put his or her idea(s) to paper, package and submit it either to the President at the DATA Enterprise Building downtown Limbe or to the PRO at the Limbe Council Chambers from June 1 to July 31.
"It could be a cassava transformation plan," she said. She further said that any competitor was free to submit more than one plan. "You must not be a JCI member," she said.Forbin also said that a jury shall sit after July 1, peruse the scripts and come out with a final verdict as to the best three projects.
The first prize would be FCFA 1,000,000, she said. In all, the PRO said some FCFA 3,000,000 would be shared to the winners in a grant awards ceremony programmed for September.
Takor Takor, 29, who is the Administrative Manager of the Data Enterprises in Limbe, said the aim of the JCI was to help the youths develop their leadership skills and be masters of themselves tomorrow.
"JCI is like a university. We encourage those between the ages of 18 and 40 to come up and test their entrepreneurial skills and develop them."We believe that any strong economy can only remain strong if it is matched by a strong private sector. That is why the economies of South East Asia are growing stronger and stronger by the day because of the strengths of their private sectors," said Takor Takor.
On a historical note, Takor Takor disclosed that the very first BBP competition took place in 2004 at the national level. This notwithstanding the Limbe Chapter had the pride of some of their members emerging as winners.
Past President of the Chapter, Carr Mafany, he said, earned FCFA 150,000 for a business plan on piggery, while one Rachel Angu picked the first prize of FCFA 200,000.As to how Mafany's piggery business has been faring, Henry Agah, 2006 President of the Limbe Chapter, said the project was flourishing.
"I know Mafany is a very rich man today thanks to that project," he said.Takor Takor said they would have to follow up the winners and ensure that they effectively use the money.
He also said since the competition requires only fresh business plans, there would be some experienced business persons or mentors to give guidance to the winners at any time they are in trouble of how to get

Original_Search_Title: Phone-Tapping, Mail Monitoring , Etc? Yeah, Right!
Headline: Uganda: Phone-Tapping, Mail Monitoring , Etc? Yeah, Right!
Body_Text:
Every once in a while, especially in the recent past, one hears tell of a security drag-net in some Western city in which terrorism suspects are a apprehended after lengthy periods of surveillance during which incontrovertible evidence in the form of intercepted telephone conversations, money trails and blood-curdling plots of mass murder are meticulously collected.
Confronted with such evidence in courts of law the suspects almost always own up and are put away for lengthy spells of imprisonment. During the last one year alone the London Metropolitan Police foiled several alleged plots to bomb significant landmarks in London.
Just last week four airport workers at the John F. Kennedy Airport in New York City were nabbed, allegedly for planning to blow up Airport fuel tanks and other strategic fuel pipes. If it had succeeded the plot would have caused an inferno of inestimable proportions.
In a world where all of us have become hostages of the blood feud between Israel and her backers on the one hand, and Palestine and her sympathizers on the other, there might be something to be said for the kind of universal and invasive surveillance first predicted sixty years ago by the novelist George Orwell, in the novel 1984, a drastic and terrifying portrait of the ultimate totalitarian world in which "Big Brother" - a euphemism for the State-watches and monitors every second of its citizens' lives via ubiquitous cameras and microphones, and where the penalties for the minutest transgression are draconian in the extreme.
George Orwell intended 1984 written in 1948 as a stern warning against the absolute and complete erosion of personal liberties and freedoms when the pursuit of political power becomes a consuming religion. As a result "Big Brother" today reigns supreme not only in the files and archives of the world's finest intelligence agencies but also in the intelligence networks of all cassava republics.
And so Uganda too is set to legalize clandestine surveillance by promulgating a law which will allow privileged security operatives to listen in on your telephone conversation, open and read your letters, and query any unusual money transfers on our bank accounts. If all that is going to prevent killing of innocent people anywhere in Uganda, then I am all for it.
Knowing the caliber of this country's intelligence operatives-from the General Service Unit of the 1960s, to the present Internal Security Organization and External Security Organisation of the last twenty-two years, nothing in Minister Amama Mbabazi's utterances reassures me that our cherished liberty will not be tampered with. I, for example, have always kept my E-mail a very tight secret, but this has not prevented conmen from sending me crazy schemes of instant wealth. And if one of them decides to send me an E-mail purportedly from Osama Bin Laden, what will happen to me?
Why not stick to the beaten path? Which is the cheaper option: to import obsolete gadgets at great expense for collecting information which is common knowledge in every public place, or to arrest suspects, clobber them into confession or commit them to some "safe house" to keep snakes and crocodiles company? What will happen to all that teargas? And all those water cannon vans? Who will the riot police beat?
In 1994 or thereabouts, Prince Charles was caught on audio-tape by an amateur phone-tapper telling Mrs Camilla Parker Bowles in a late night phone chat that he wished he was her tampon.
The eavesdropper sold this tape to a radio and television network regardless of the fact that the embarrassing contents had been made by the future king of the United Kingdom. That, of course, meant that the actual voice and transcripts of the conversation soon became available to all and sundry worldwide. Fellow citizens, brace yourselves for violations of a similar nature. "Big Brother" is watching you.

Original_Search_Title: From Humble Beginnings, Out of Poverty
Headline: Uganda: From Humble Beginnings, Out of Poverty
Body_Text:
Nabuule — FOR 12 years now, Amiina Nabuule has been financially independent. She shared her story with Anthony Bugembe
"My parents were very rich but refused to educate me. They considered boys' education more important. Girls were prepared for marriage.
I got married to a wealthy man in my home village, Kyaggwe in Mukono District. When I discovered that he was cheating on me, we separated. In 1995, I came to live with my sister in Makerere, Kavule. That was when I started frying snacks for a living.
Business success
I sell pancakes, samosas, cassava and potato pieces in addition to mangoes and bogoya (bananas). This business has been my only source of income.
I cannot borrow from any financial institution or women development group. Where would I get the security or money to pay back? On an average day, I make a profit of about sh4,000.
Work schedule
I wake up at 2:00am to peel the cassava and potatoes and mix the dough. Frying runs from 6:00am to 1:00pm. Then I attend to customers for the remaining part of the day up to 1:00am when I retire to bed.
Helping hands
My children are co-operative and understanding. Every morning before leaving for school, they walk to Kalerwe Market to buy cassava and the other ingredients I need. After school (4:00pm), they sell the snacks around Makerere University.
When I am sick, my children take over and run the business. I have trained them to handle the business.
The biggest portion of my earnings is used for feeding and paying school fees for my four children. My estranged husband refused to pay their school fees. I decided to do it on my own. The eldest is in S4, followed by one in P7 and another in P6. I also give each child sh100 daily to spend at school. Perhaps when the children are through with their education, I will build myself a house. I spend sh15,000 on rent every month.
Challenges
Sometimes not all the snacks are sold. At times some naughty university students eat them and refuse to pay.
Some guards also ask to be paid first before letting the children into the halls of residence or hostels.
I have gotten used to such disappointments.
If I hadn't, I would have wound up the business by now.
Working very hard, coupled with self-sacrifice, has made me successful. Despite my failing health and warnings from medics that I should not be exposed to too much heat, I continue to fry and sell snacks. It's our family's gateway to survival.
I also plan my time well. This involves waking up very early and retiring quite late. This has enabled me to remain financially independent for a long time.
Attracting and retaining customers is hard, but I have managed to attract them by establishing a good relationship with them. They address me as Jajja Muwogo (cassava), which to me is not an insult.

Original_Search_Title: From Clay to Cash
Headline: Uganda: From Clay to Cash
Body_Text:
Kampala — Joel Ogwang visited a community that earns an income from turning clay and metal into stoves.
HOLDING some wet clay, Mable Busingye's son, Isaac, imitates her. She is moulding pots and grills for charcoal stoves at her home in Kirinya village, Kito 'A' in Bweyogerere, opposite Mandela National Stadium, Namboole.
"He (Isaac) will be as hardworking as his father," Busingye says of the five-year-old.
She says she buys the clay she uses from vendors who transport it on trucks from Kakajjo, a nearby village.
For sh10,000, Busingye can get 20 bags of clay. Using stones, she crushes it into powder. And then she has to wet and soften it in preparation for moulding.
"I buy eight jerrycans of water to soften the clay," she says. Each jerrycan costs sh200.
Busingye, who goes about her work with a swiftness that is breathtaking, also parts with sh8,000 to hire men to trample on the clay mixed with water.
"I tried trampling on the clay to save the sh8,000, but didn't get a fine product," she says.
When the clay is mixed and ready for moulding, Busingye has to find a way to keep it properly since she can't use it all at a go. "This," she says, pointing to a kaveera (polythene bag) she is using to cover some clay, "helps keep the clay wet for up to a month."
Busingye makes at least 50 grills daily. When she is done moulding them, she has to dry them. This has to be done in a shade because "if you lay the grills under direct sunrays, they crack".
Whenever a grill develops a crack, it is destroyed.
About 90% of the households in this village make pots or stoves. Like any other venture, the stove business involves specialisation. "I make only the metallic exterior and buy the clay grills which I insert, then sell a complete stove," says Hassan Ntale.
A P5 dropout, Ntale sells each stove at sh4,500. His customers come from Kampala, Mukono, Wakiso and Bweyogerere. Ntale, who earns sh150,000 a month, says: "My customers buy in bulk and sell each stove at between sh5,000 and sh10,000."
Ntale and his wife live in a two-roomed mud-and-wattle house, for which he pays monthly rent of sh25,000.
"I will be living in my own house by the end of this year," he says. Ntale also pays fees for his four siblings, in addition to supporting his mother in Mubende.
Ntale's neighbour, Jane Takuwa, usually buys a tipper-full of clay at sh15,000.
Her 12 sons and daughters do the crushing, wetting and softening of the clay. "Why should I hire someone when I have men and women around?" she asks.
Takuwa's family make stoves which are entirely made of clay.
The family moulds 400 stoves a week. "I make 60 a day," says Julius Muwereza, Takuwa's eldest son.
The stoves are dried in a grass-thatched house. "It is very cool in there," Takuwa says.
"This keeps them from cracking."
The stoves take a week to dry, then Takuwa has to smoke them. She spends sh70,000 on firewood to do that. "It takes four hours to smoke all 400," says Rashid Musoke. "We normally do it at the weekend."
"They can easily break during the smoking," says Takuwa, who has spent 12 years in this business. She sells each stove at sh500.
"Much of the money goes to feeding and medication," she says. She also caters for her eight grandchildren.
"Your income is so small compared to the size of your family, can't your children find alternative jobs?" I ask.
She pauses for 30 seconds, before she retorts: "Do you have one for them?"
Her sons, she says, tried to do petty jobs in Bweyogerere, but quit because of exploitation.
Yusuf Mukiibi says he tried to work at a construction site, but got disgusted and left. "I was mixing sand and cement, but one time my boss said he couldn't pay me because he was, after all, giving me free learning experience," he says.
Middlemen buy the stoves at sh500. They re-sell them to retailers at sh700. Muganga, a trader in Mukono town, who buys the stoves at sh7,00, says: "I sell each at sh1,000. But during the Christmas season, I sell them at sh1,500 each."
In spite of the free primary and secondary education, many children in this community don't attend school.
"We only survive on making stoves. If my children were away at school, who would make the stoves, and how would we survive?" Takuwa asks.
Kato, a father of six, says by the time he makes 45 stoves, his back is aching. "If I had something else to do, I would have quit (this business)," he says.
Middlemen also make the business a challenging one. "Sometimes they take the stoves on credit and don't pay for a whole month," says Musa.
Ntale says his business is risky. "I almost lost my finger while cutting the metallic frames," he says.
In spite of their hard work, most people's living conditions have not improved.
The majority live in mud-and-wattle houses, roofed with papyrus mats and polythene bags. Only the so-called tycoons have houses roofed with iron sheets.
Sadly, a lot of hard-earned cash is spent on smoking and consumption of alcohol.
To boost their earnings, some residents grow cassava, maize and potatoes.
It is tough going, but they know success doesn't come cheap, so they are not giving up.
A driver himself, Bosco Mabonga's wife makes sigiiris. "Her earning is too little but the good thing here is that she's kept busy," he says.
John Sebugwawo, who makes pots, says the costs of clay rise by every day that passes.
He earns sh150, 000 from selling pots who prices range between sh1, 000 and sh18,000 depending on the size.
"We have a group of five people. We pool funds and buy the ebbumba," says the father of eight. "It's very expensive buying it alone."
Smoking and alcohol consumption of, especially Malwa, is high. This drains their hard earned cash.
Each family here has an average of four children. "Every child is an additional burden. I don't know what I was thinking by producing eight children," says Sebugwawo.
They may not look successful, but there is clear commitment to work by residents of Kirinya 'A' zone. They say success doesn't come cheaply, so they aren't giving up.

Original_Search_Title: Inharrime District to Process Coconut Oil
Headline: Mozambique: Inharrime District to Process Coconut Oil
Body_Text:
Inharrime — The district of Inharrime, in the southern Mozambican province of Inhambane, is to start refining coconut oil within a month.
So far, the district has been exporting raw coconut oil to various places within the country and to the southern African region.
"We are now setting up a refinery, which must be completed within a month", said the factory's owner, Naimito Mussa.
Mussa was speaking on Thursday to reporters accompanying President Armando Guebuza on his tour through one of the major copra producers in the country.
Mussa did not give any figures for the costs of this investment, but he said that, when the refinery starts functioning, the state will gain a lot in terms of taxes.
He said his company has the capacity to process one tonne of copra per hour, which will give half a tonne of coconut oil.
The factory will employ 60 workers from Inharrime, and guarantees to purchase its copra from local producers. It is buying copra at prices varying between 4 and 4.4 meticais a kilo (there are about 25.9 meticais to the US dollar at current exchange rates).
One of the main constraints that used to face industries in Inharrime was the lack of a reliable power source. This has now been solved with the extension of the Mozambican national electricity grid, based on the Cahora Bassa dam on the Zambezi.
A second industrial unit in Inharrime produces cassava flour, using technology imported from Brazil and Germany. About half a tonne of cassava flour a day is produced, which is mostly sold to local health units.
The importance of these two factories was stressed by Inharrime traditional leaders when they briefed Guebuza shortly after his arrival.
The fact that the government has distributed cattle to many families in Inharrime also deserved a positive remark on the part of these leaders, as well as from participants at the rally addressed by Guebuza on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: Zim, Indonesia Sign Empowerment Pact
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zim, Indonesia Sign Empowerment Pact
