Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE and Indonesia on Monday signed an agreement on women empowerment in Jakarta to strengthen co-operation on women's economic capacity, exchange of information, knowledge and technology.
Indonesian Embassy spokesperson Mrs Ita Mappajarungi said the signing of the Memorandum of Understanding between the two countries was a follow-up to the letter of intent on the same issue which was signed in August last year when Indonesian State Minister of Women Empowerment Professor Meutia Hatta Swasono visited Zimbabwe.
Prof Meutia Hatta and her Zimbabwean counterpart, the Minister of Women, Gender and Community Development, Cde Oppah Muchinguri, signed the agreement.
Prof Swasono said women in Africa and Asia comprise more than half the total number of women in the world and the welfare of the community depended on their achievement.
"This is one of the reasons why women should be given bigger opportunities to improve their lives," she said.
"The empowerment of women is an effort to eradicate discrimination against women and to strengthen women's capacity so that they can play a bigger role in the development of the community."
Cde Muchinguri said the agreement offered Zimbabwean women a chance to learn from Indonesian women's skills and the technology they use to add value to raw materials.
Women in Zimbabwe, she said, could, for example, learn how cassava is processed into different kinds of ready-to-eat food in Indonesia, thereby enhancing their income.
Cde Muchinguri said women in Zimbabwe had scored many successes over the past 27 years.
This progress, she said, included the increasing number of educated women (90 percent of literacy among women), the passing of the Domestic Violence Act and women's rights over land.
She also hoped that the agreement could be a foundation for the enhancement of communication and strategic co-operation in the field of development and women empowerment.
Prof Meutia Hatta and Cde Muchinguri plan to establish a joint working group to formulate priority programmes which will cover empowering women economically, promoting women's role in politics and public office, gender mainstreaming and protection of women.
Minister Meutia Hatta added that this co-operation will become an important part of the New Asia Africa Strategic Partnership.
She also hoped that the meeting of the NAASP will be held simultaneously with the Asia Africa Conference Meeting.
Cde Muchinguri is leading the Zimbabwean delegation which arrived in Jakarta, Indonesia on May 24.
The delegation consists of several senior members of her ministry and the Minister of Lands, Land Reform and Resettlement Program, Cde Flora Buka.
During their stay in Indonesia, the delegation will visit Mother and Child Health Centres in Jakarta, handicraft centres in Bali and hold meetings with the board of the Association of Indonesian Women Entrepreneurs in Jakarta and business women in Bali.

Original_Search_Title: Professors And Bishops Have Become Soldiers of Fortune
Headline: Kenya: Professors And Bishops Have Become Soldiers of Fortune
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Is it possible that one can hope to find a corrigible institution in a generation that seems to love to bury itself in avarice, debilitating hypocrisy and dishonest doublespeak?
We read in the Good Book about Saint Peter saying to a cripple, whose habit and practice it was to sit and beg outside the Beautiful Gate of the Temple in Jerusalem: "Silver and gold have I none, but this I say to you - in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk."
But, today, are our holy shrines and academies equally enjoined in factional pursuit for silver and gold, with the tribal leader as the head hunter and a later-day untouchable tin god? Do silver and gold seem to be as precious to the churchman in every corner of the republic and to the university academic on every campus as they are to the ordinary unschooled hedonist?
It would seem that the Church of Christ in Kenya today would mind less that someone was committing sin than it would mind about who was committing the sin. If it is 'one of our own' then it is all right. That seems to be the philosophy. Pious greed and learned avarice, therefore, travel on hand in hand as spiritual and intellectual hunter-gatherers of material fortune turn a blind eye where they should be keeping the nation informed on what ails it.
For my part, I have this week been lumbered with a plethora of irate intellectual email. My sin is that I dared wonder on this page last week why our scholars should display the same primitive appetites as the ordinary hunter-gatherer - the lot who collects and hoards even what he has no use for. If the scholar should be a hunter-gatherer, one would think that he would hunt in the jungle of knowledge and store in the granary of scholarship?
But not so any more. Plato of ancient Greece once drew clear distinction among three segments of society. There were those whose lot it was to gloat and eat. They drew their life force from raw instinct and raw appetite. They hunted, gathered and ate. Sometimes they even looted. Plato saw them as the limbs of society. They were innocent and rarely concerned with anything beyond what sheer instinct could address.
Next to these hunter-gatherers was a lot driven by emotion and raw passion. These are Plato's second category of the human soul and of society. Their life force comes from hot passion. Very simply put, they act and react purely on the basis of sensation. Reason and logic have no place in their scheme of things.
Finally, we have the philosopher-kings, the thinkers and the voice of reason in society. These would be the men and women of the academy. Their lot is to exercise the mind - to think for society and to rule, for, as the poet Okot p'Bitek would say, the intellectual is the ruler. He rules people's minds and hearts.
But how will our literati claim to be our philosopher-kings when they succumb to the same appetitive drive and to the raw passion of the ordinary man and woman? How can they be kings when they resort to such mundane 'reasoning' that because everyone else is greedy and tribal they are also entitled to descending to that level?
Kenyans have missed the train of transformative leadership and reform at virtually every boarding point. What do our scholars and spiritual superiors have to say for themselves? In 1992 some university professors and churchmen were peddling themselves as Youth for Kanu. Others called themselves scholars for Kanu while others offered prayers for Kanu. But they were basically hunters of fortune salivating after Kanu's election moneybags. And where they were not following Kanu's moneybags, they were worshipping outside empty tribal shrines opposed to Kanu. Without any serious persuasion beyond wanting to see their tribesman in the Big House on the Hill, they accused Kanu and Daniel arap Moi of all manner of ills, both real and imaginary.
The problem was not that what Moi and Kanu were doing was bad. The problem was that it was not their tribal surrogate in charge. Today Moi is a retired old man, eating yams and cassava in Sacho. What do we say about the continued bloodletting in our country? In the 1990s we blamed Moi for everything that went wrong. While defeating his Uhuru Project we sang everywhere that all was possible without Moi. With hindsight, we now know just how so wrong we were. The men and women of letters and our spiritual superiors who should tell it to us, as it is, have become conspirators. They have taken their place on the tribal queue, waiting for the droppings from the tribal high table. The latter day professor and the bishop are largely soldiers of fortune, just like your regular dogs of war.
Like the ordinary yodel from the tribe, the professor and the bishop are money and hero worshippers. They alternate between an empty tribal cave and a decadent tribal shrine. They are as protective of the tribal chief priest as is the ordinary tribal yodel is. Like the later, they believe that the tribal chief priest will bring bounty to them and to the tribe, at the expense of the rest of the citizenry. The quest for truth and righteousness wherever they may be found has been replaced by the quest for hedonism wherever it may be found. We are left to say with Alan Paton of South Africa, Cry the Beloved Country.

Original_Search_Title: Open Letter to Umaru Yar'Adua (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Open Letter to Umaru Yar'Adua (2)
Body_Text:
Enugu — THESE days, Nigerians spell words and punctuate very badly, and these Nigerians include holders of PhD in English. Even government literature contain words like "gotten", "offense", "program", "organization" and "labor". And Ministry of Transport has suddenly become Ministry of "Transportation"!
This is almost unfortunate. Can we not have standards, which we and our visitors must abide by in our own country? When people went to study or live in America, Europe and other places in the world, was the idea for them to come back with junks and disorganise Nigeria?
If we are not careful in this country, we will not be able to understand one another any more as everyone will be speaking and writing whatever and however he likes and claim that he was taught so by some alien. And before you know it, one of those junkies will come up with the "smart" idea to do away with examinations and tests altogether. Without rules and standards, you couldn't have examinations and tests, anyway. And law and order would be meaningless. We must banish the "anything goes" culture from Nigeria.
It is erosion or lack of standards that has made it impossible for us to be law abiding, to produce things, and for things to work here. Without removal of cultural junks, intellectual junks, material junks, ideological junks, theoretical junks, junk attitude and behaviour, and other junks from Nigeria, nothing will work and we will continue to consume only that produced by others, however, unsuited their products might be to our environment and aspirations.
Your Excellency, Nigeria has no foundation; the country still needs to be designed and anything existing but out of place in this design would either disappear by itself or must be pulled down. For example, as we have British English standard in Nigeria, no one (not even computers) should be allowed to come here or influence us from outside with non-British English spellings, construction or pronunciation. And for as long as we produce oil and gas in Nigeria, we must never have shortage of these energy commodities. And that no one will export cassava when the Nigerian market is short of cassava products like garri and fufu. And that electricity supply must never be interrupted.
The foundation I talked about, therefore, can be seen as a set of guiding principles which, if followed faithfully, would produce a great country. Without such guiding principles and faithful adherence, we would continue to grope in the dark.
I urge you not to see the next four years as any other four years in the history of Nigeria - years of contractors and public servants colluding to waste our resources on white elephant projects and meaningless jamborees. I urge you to seek to remake Nigeria, starting with a real constitution based on sound principles and written for Nigerians (not for a man or a group of men) and for eternity. And structures of all tiers and arms of government that will work, i.e, encourage production, stimulate demand and consumption, and promote love, and happiness among Nigerians. With simple but principled steps, life for Nigerians can easily become meaningful and enjoyable.
I am sure none of what I have said here would accord with the wish list of your coordinators and the groups of solidarity visitors or courtesy callers you have been receiving since you were declared winner of the presidential contest. But, I hope your eyes will be on history and not necessarily on the demands of individual Nigerians. I wish you a history-making tenure.
Dr. Ogbuagu, a Development Philosopher, writes from Iheuwa Centre, Enugu.

Original_Search_Title: Exploiting the Innocent Children
Headline: Uganda: Exploiting the Innocent Children
Body_Text:
Reports about child abuse and neglect are numerous in our media. The government has done little citing inadequate legislation yet a look at certain protocols Uganda is a state party to reveals that there are mechanisms in place under which these problems can be addressed. Ms Asiimwe examines some of them:-
Until I interacted with Uganda Child Rights NGO Network (UCRNN) recently, I did not know that Uganda is signatory to the Optional Protocol to the Convention on the Rights of the Child on the Sale of Children, Child Prostitution and Child Pornography.
Uganda signed this Protocol in August of 2002. The Protocol enjoins states to protect children against sale, child prostitution, and child pornography. Part of the responsibility of States that sign up to these UN instruments, is to report on progress of implementation. Uganda, through Ministry of Gender, Labour and Social Development, recently prepared its first periodic report. By and large, our government concedes, that it has not done enough.
The Optional Protocol has not been domesticated yet, meaning that it has not yet become part of our law. There are a few provisions in our Penal Code that touch on child prostitution, but they are not adequate. The government points out that sale of children, child prostitution and child pornography are prohibited under the Sexual Offences Bill. However, this Bill has been sitting on the shelf since 1999.
The government also concedes that it has not done any sensitisation about the Protocol and says that it does not have enough resources to address the problem. As a result, child protection is rarely a priority issue .
Compounding the problem is that there is inadequate information about the magnitude of sale of children, child prostitution and child pornography. Information obtained from Police Headquarters by UCRNN is that they do not have adequate information.
The challenge is that as these crimes go on unchecked, our children continue to suffer the consequences of sexual violence and exploitation. UCRNN highlights a few case studies from a report done by Save the Children (Uganda) in 2006: "I saw a woman from Matanyi who had a baby and a young girl of about six. They all looked very hungry. She was selling her older daughter. One woman bought her at Shs30,000.
However, this woman pleaded in tears with the mother who had sold her daughter to her have the baby as well for Shs15,000. But the mother said 'I cannot sell you my baby. I have sold you her sister in order to feed her (my baby). Since I now have some money, let me feed her until she grows. Then I may give you this child as well."
An LCI secretary narrates this story, "One day I went to Ochorimongin. I disguised myself as an Iteso and joined the others to take ajono - a local brew - on the eve of the Friday market. In the home where we were drinking from, there were two women - SL and LJ and a number of girls aged 14 and above...what I saw broke my heart.
These women had gathered the Karimojong girls and they were using them to cater for their survival. One of the Itesot men came and asked the women to let him have one of the girls to work for him. The woman demanded a fee and the man promised to pay 4 - 5pieces of cassava tubers for 4-5 hours of work. Another cyclist came in and asked to have a girl, for a 'wife'. This cyclist mobilised other men to come and take 'wives'"
"This morning (18/07/06) I saw many young children leaving in a bus going away. Very young children between the ages of 7-15 are leaving to search for food. There is a generational extinction coming to our area. During holidays because there is no food provided, children go out to look for food. They only come back when school resumes. When children come home and find their parents have gone to look for food, they also go away.
I was also informed about EG from Teso. EG collects children from Matanyi and parades them under a tree in Ochorimongin nicknamed 'supermarket'. People come all the way from Mbale, Kapchorwa,, Iganga and Jinja to this 'supermarket' to buy girls.
When asked why he is selling children he says, 'The Karimojong stole our cattle and that means we have already paid bride price for these children so they are ours.' EG demands Shs3,000 as a 'transport refund' for each girl taken" - Chairperson LC2.
The vulnerable children of Uganda will never know our government cares for them until it puts the right measures in place to protect them.
The writer is a member of the Daily Monitor Panel of Experts, a lawyer and a member of Fida Uganda with extensive experience in policy research and advocacy on human rights and governance.

Original_Search_Title: A Fortune From Pork
Headline: Uganda: A Fortune From Pork
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEEING a Land Cruiser with a customised number plate, you would expect to see a managing director. But the man had a humble beginning, with only sh12,000 as capital.
Hakuna Matata, which literally means 'there is no problem', is the name for a chain of popular pork joints in Kampala. They belong to 33-year-old Moses Kasajja.
"Give me pork for sh6,000 and 'escorts," one man shouts out at one of the famed pork joints. The pork is served on wide platters. The 'escorts' include vegetables and cassava. The topics discussed are football, politics and business.
Hakuna Matata pork joints are the brain child of Moses Kasajja. His story is one of patience and perseverance. He started in Luweero, moved to Maddu in Mpigi in the late 1980s and finally to Kampala. In Kampala, he sold clothes at St Balikudembe Market, before he found his niche in pork.
"I started with around sh12,000 in 1990," Kasajja says. At the time, he had four poles and papyrus that created a shade. It was the typical village pork joint: Dirty men without shoes, chopping a small piece of pork. The joint had no name. Kasajja says: "It was very difficult to see people of class eating pork in the open.
Many people bought the pork, roasted it and ate it at their homes. I worked harder and saved a lot," he says. "One of the biggest problems in Uganda is that people do not want to save. They think that one must first have millions before he or she starts saving."
"I expanded the pork shade in Makindye, added a few chairs and another shade. From my savings, I was able to establish the chain of pork joints."
As the business expanded Kasajja's patrons found problems directing their friends to the joint. The issue of the name then came in.
"Hakuna Matata' appealed to me. It is soothing when one says, there is no problem," he explains. He operates five pork joints. Two in Makindye and the other three in Kabalagala, Nakulabye and Kiwatule.
Kasajja dresses colourfully. When The New Vision visited him at one of his joints, he was dressed in a basketball outfit of the Los Angeles Lakers. He looked like a basketball player.
The pork joints are painted white with a little brown and red. "White signifies cleanliness," he explains.
Like the rest of the walls and floors of all the joints, the toilet walls are covered with white tiles. There are cleaners in the toilets all the time.
Animal paintings also feature at Kasajja's joints. At one of them in Makindye, there are big statues of cattle, crocodiles and lions. Around them is a fountain. Because Kasajja loves perfection, he says it takes long to construct the joints. "I grew up seeing cattle. My grandfather had so many and I loved them," he says.
By any standard, Kasajja is a successful man. "One of the reasons I have succeeded, is because I supervise my businesses," he says. Kasajja and his wife wake up at 6:00am everyday.
"We buy pork from Wambizzi near Nalukolongo," he says. On a holiday or a weekend, each of the five joints slaughters about 10 pigs. A pig weighs about 25kgs. Therefore, an average of 250kgs is sold at every joint. Once the pork is delivered at the joints, the fat is removed.
Years ago, there was a belief that roasted pork was the reserve of the poor. "Today, I think that pork is the reserve of everybody, including the rich and famous," Kasajja explains. He says unlike chicken, goat and beef that are roasted everywhere, pork is exceptional.
His customers include professionals and non-professionals. At Kasajja's pork joints you will find an assortment of drinks, pool tables and luxurious unisex hair salons. He never had formal education. Unlike most business people, Kasajja attributes his success to savings.
"I have a dream of owning a building in the centre of the city. You can only do it if you have support from the bank," he says. Adjusting his LA Lakers unscripted cap, he says failure to go to school was not an obstacle, but a challenge. He has been able to view many things with a much wider lens.
"All of us have unique factors that can help us succeed whether we get an education or not. I discovered these traits and adopted them," he adds. Kasajja employs at least 150 people. He loves cars. Among his fleet of vehicles is a Land-cruiser with a personalised number plate. "I like watching motor sport. I also do martial arts," he says.
Kasajja was born to Michael Kyeyune, a resident of Wakiso district. Kasajja married in the early 1990s. He has six children, the oldest of whom is in S2. Kasajja is a happy man, thanks to pork.

Original_Search_Title: Who Succeeds JAK?
Headline: Ghana: Who Succeeds JAK?
Body_Text:
Accra — Asked about Poland, where two brothers are ruling the country as President and Prime Minister, the anti -Addo Kufuor agitators say Ghana is not Poland and that Addo Kufuor should give way to other candidates to succeed President Kufuor.
In Tamale, they call him 'Mr. President'; in Kumasi Kejetia, they call him 'Chief' at Kpando, they call him 'Fia' and at Sunyani they call him 'Adwumawura'. The popularity of Alan Kyeremanteng, soft spoken, cool and collected, has surged in recent times, especially amongst ladies and young men in the party and even beyond in all the Regions, thanks to his looks, which have assumed magnetic characteristics these days.
Alan's idea of Empretec and his conviction that the private sector has the capacity to transform Ghana into a prosperous nation is held in high esteem by the youth, hence their hope in his candidacy. The failure of the Presidential Initiative on Cassava has been used as a yardstick against him but the success in other initiatives -oil palm, tomato and others have forced his opponents into sudden retreat. His recent rise in popularity polls has, not unexpectedly, woken up slumbering foxes, vultures and jackals in the party to hunt him down, using all kinds of tricks to 'block' his candidature but they seem to have hit a buffer.
Born in Kumasi with Fanti/Ashanti parentage, the 49 -year old Alan is 'supremely well qualified to build on the huge achievements of President Kufuor and to keep the NPP solidly united", Edmund Larbi told me at Cape Coast, only last week.
Like Nana Addo, Alan was born into the Busia - Danquah tradition. He is also a lawyer and strong believer in liberal capitalism but like Alexander Hamilton of the United States{US first Secretary of State}, he strongly believes that those who through no fault of their making are poor and downtrodden, should be helped by the State to climb the social ladder.
His personal record of philanthropy, which has saved thousands of waywards from the streets and poverty-ridden alleys, attest to his warmth.
Untainted by alliances and splinter 'fiefdoms within the NPP', his opponents say he is media shy and has cocooned himself from the grassroots. They claim it is "not easy to see him" and "he does not pick his telephones" when he is needed. Others also see the temporary slow down of the Presidential Initiative on cassava/starch as his main weakness.
Yaw Osafo Maafo, the former Finance and Economic Planning Minister, is certainly a power in his own right who cannot be ignored easily unless he decides to cut short his ambition to move to the Osu Castle.
Dubbed the 'Sarkozy' of Ghanaian politics, his splendid performance as Minister of Finance and later Education is still as solid as ever. He is a real leader, intelligent, matured and knowledgeable, whom many say, could put the NDC in political coma easily, but simmering 'grudge' within the party could land him on a bed of thorns for the rest of his life if he decides to push his ambition. He has explanation to do on why he was removed as Minister of Education and Sports last year.
The hullabaloo surrounding the stadia contract to the Chinese and the Ghana Publishers/ Macmillan contract case, which the State said it is "going to appeal" are just but two issues, which could easily be albatrosses around his neck.
Hackman Owusu Agyeman is a nice man who can easily fit into Kufuor's shoes. Very loyal and a major linchpin of the party who commands respect, both locally and internationally, he is politically matured, having served as Minister of Foreign Affairs and Interior. His weakness is that he is relatively old, about 64 years. As a Minister responsible for Water Resources, his inability to ensure regular supply of water to majority of Ghanaians, could be a problem when he is nominated as NPP flagbearer.
Several political analysts say Professor Frimpong Boateng could win the 2008 elections if NPP nominates him as their flagbearer on grounds that he is nationalistic and a hard working nice man, who has sacrificed his comfort abroad to help his country. He is also squeaky clean and has not tainted his hands in the rough side of politics.
A cardinal fact in politics is that it is a profession, which is not good for nice people. Many politicians know him more as a CPP sympathiser than being a member of the NPP. He seems to have no kingmakers and foot soldiers in the party to 'evangelise' on his behalf, hence his candidature is more likely to be scuppered by extremists within the party, and leave him frustrated before he realises what he is actually doing. Good luck.
Prof. Mike Ocquaye has all the tools and experience to call on Ghanaians to vote for him as President of Ghana in 2008 but in all the constituencies, which matter most to NPP, he is not popular. Perhaps he has not been using the media or has cocooned himself from the people. He has a lot of travelling to do at the constituencies and media houses, if he really means to become the flagbearer.
I really doubt if his age and health will allow him.
Until Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku's recent promise that Ghanaians will not see darkness again when he becomes the next President of Ghana, many NPP members/ supporters thought he had given up his ambition. His strengths are that he is a founding member of the party, mature and a strong campaigner.
He is yet to recover fully from his removal from the Ministry of Trade, in the formative years of the NPP administration, to his dumping at the scrap-yard of NEPAD/Regional Cooperation. His chances seem to have evaporated; he has no chance this time.
Papa Owusu Ankomah is a real gentleman, who rose through the Ministries of Justice, Interior and now Education and Sports unscathed. Relatively young, he has the world at his feet. On the side of the coin, his popularity does not go beyond Sekondi/Takoradi.
Dan Botchwey is a young politician who can cause an upset. As a successful party organiser, he takes credit for NPP successes in the last two general elections, which brought the party to power. His asset is that he knows very well almost all the relevant delegates who will select the new flagbearer, but his downside is that he is yet to fully recover from his dismissal from the Information Ministry. The rancour and bitterness, which followed in the trail of his removal is still in his head.
Fourteen- day fame seekers - Nobody knows these two returnees from America - Agyako Kyeremanteng and Dr Arthur Kennedy. They might have been successful as a banker and a medical officer respectively, but in the villages, districts, and constituencies, much is not known about them.
It is doubtful whether they actually know the objective problems facing the people in these areas.
Kyeremanteng Agyako has so far proved himself as a knowledgeable presenter of issues to only few people in the party, while Arthur Kennedy seems to be feebly using the media to publicise his manifesto.
Nkrabea Effah Dartey and Kwabena Agyepong seem to be advertising themselves for political appointments after 2008. The NPP will be doing good service to themselves if they forget them now and concentrate on the right candidates. It is their democratic right to vote and be voted for but they should not waste our precious time and resources.

Original_Search_Title: NAFDAC Under Dora Akunyili
Headline: Nigeria: NAFDAC Under Dora Akunyili
Body_Text:
Lagos — JUST recently, Nigeria was honoured with the Universal Salt Iodization (USI) compliant certification for recording 100 per cent salt iodization at factory and 98 per cent at both household and retail levels in the country. The USI certification award makes Nigeria the first African country to reach such an enviable target in the fight to eradicate Iodine Deficiency Disorder (IDD).
In giving Nigeria the award which took place in Istanbul Turkey, the Global Forum on Micronutrient said the country has made unprecedented progress in the campaign against the endemic problem of Iodine Deficiency Disorder (IDD).
The feat was made possible through pragmatic regulatory and enforcement action of the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC); and such has been the destiny of the Agency since April 2001 when Prof. Dora Nkem Akunyili was appointed its Director General.
The six years of her stay have seen unprecedented achievements so much so that NAFDAC has become a household name in Nigeria and a well- respected organization among members of the international community.
NAFDAC was established by Decree 15 of 1993 (as amended in 1999). The Decree was passed as an Act of the National Assembly CAP No 1, Laws of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 2004. The laws establishing the Agency gave it powers to control and regulate the manufacture, importation, exportation, distribution, advertisement, sale and use of food, drugs, cosmetics, chemicals, detergents, all drinks, including packaged water and medical devices.
The Agency is therefore saddled with the responsibility of safeguarding public health in the country. Thus, NAFDAC is expected to achieve by ensuring best quality, safety and efficacy of all medical, food product and all other products used in the country.
Prior to her appointment as head of NAFDAC, the Agency was poor in performance, which led to high incidence of fake drugs, unwholesome food and other substandard products which put the lives of the people at risk and actually resulted to preventable deaths of millions of people. The development naturally attracted biting criticism from the public who accused the Agency of non-performance.
Some of the problems that NAFDAC was bogged down with before Prof. Akunyili took over the mantle of leadership included expired products and those without expiry/best before dates, products labeled with the intention of extending their shelf-life; non- iodized or insufficiently iodized salt; improperly processed and unregistered packaged (pure) water; beer without alcoholic content indication and low quality soft drinks.
Others included deceptively labeled juice drinks, bakers using potassium bromide as bread enhancer. (The chemical is known to cause cancer, kidney failure, loss of hearing and break down of vitamins cases), palm oil mixed with Annatto dye to enhance its colour and increase in volume and substitution of edible salt with industrial salt.
In the same way, tins of baby milk filled with cassava flour and sweetened with sugar filled the Nigerian market, green fruits like mangoes, bananas and pineapples were forced to ripen through the application of heat generated from sacks of carbide, a product that causes diarrhea, nausea, gastro-enteritis and stomach ulcer. Foods were spread with insecticides to protect them during storage.
Consequently the insecticides have their residues retained by the foods, a situation that is dangerous to health. There were substandard and gelatinous cosmetics products in Nigerian markets, including body creams containing dangerous bleaching agents like mercury and hydroquinone, packets and empty bottles of designer perfumes were imported into Nigeria and filled with locally diluted concentrates while toothpastes without or with insufficient fluoride were sold to the people.
Because of inefficient regulation by the then NAFDAC, consequences of fake/counterfeit drugs increased in dimension leading to the following negative situations: Fake drugs embarrassed Nigeria's health care providers and eroded the confidence of the public in the nation's health care system, local drug manufacturers closed shops due to undue competition (even some multi-national companies left Nigeria).
Within the same period, Nigeria was rated as one of the countries with the highest incidences of fake/counterfeit drugs. This led to the rejection of drugs produced in Nigeria by West African countries. Estimates of the extent of counterfeit drugs in circulation in Nigeria ranged from 48 per cent to 80 per cent in 2001. The surveys were conducted by Pooles (1989), Lambo (1990), Ochekpe et al (1993) and Tailor et al (2001).
The Amazon enters
When Prof. Dora Akunyili took over the leadership of NAFDAC in April 2001, with the collaboration of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Department for International Development (DFID) the Agency was able to make an overall assessment of the problems confronting the drug sector in the country and came up with the following findings:
Mr. Melah, a social critc, writes from Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: FDB Denies Giving Permit to Two Drug Barons to Import Cocaine
Headline: Ghana: FDB Denies Giving Permit to Two Drug Barons to Import Cocaine
Body_Text:
THE HEAD of Quality Control Laboratory at the Ghana Food and Drugs Board (FDB), Rev. Jonathan Yaw Martey yesterday told an Accra Fast Track Court trying two alleged drug barons, Kwabena Amaning alias Tagor and Alhaji Issah Abbas, involved in the much-talked- about missing 77 parcels of cocaine that he did not know the accused persons.
In his evidence to the Court presided over by Justice Jones Dotsey, Witness said that FDB does not issue licenses to individuals to import narcotics drugs into the country but rather corporate entities which required the consent of the International Narcotics and Drugs Board.
He held also that he did not issue any permit to ship cocaine into the country, adding that the FDB since its establishment in 1992, had not issued permit to any of the accused persons to import cocaine into the county.
Responding to questions during cross-examination, Witness admitted working on cocaine samples for analysis at the Ghana Standards Board.Describing the nature of cocaine, Witness informed the Court that it appears in a white crystallized powder depending on its quality.
Mr. Martey had also denied reading or hearing in the media that some cocaine sent for testing had turned into Kokonte powder (cassava flour).
Tagor and Alhaji Issah are facing four and three counts respectively of dealing in narcotic drugs, conspiracy to deal in prohibited business, namely narcotic drugs, supplying, buying, and crediting, contrary to the laws of the Narcotic Drugs Control Board and have both pleaded not guilty to the charges.
Earlier, the two had been charged together with Victor Kisseh, alias Yaw Billah, Alhaji Moro Mohammed, and Kwabena Acheampong who is now a prosecution witness in the same case.
The case has been adjourned to May 15 2007 for continuation of trial.

Original_Search_Title: Kasajja Makes a Fortune From Pork
Headline: Uganda: Kasajja Makes a Fortune From Pork
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEEING a Land Cruiser with a customised number plate, you would expect to see a managing director. But the man had a humble beginning, with only sh12,000 as capital. Hakuna Matata, which literally means 'there is no problem', is the name for a chain of popular pork joints in Kampala. They belong to 33-year-old Moses Kasajja.
"Give me pork for sh6,000 and 'escorts," one man shouts out at one of the famed pork joints. The pork is served on wide platters. The 'escorts' include vegetables and cassava. The topics discussed are football, politics and business.
Hakuna Matata pork joints are the brain child of Moses Kasajja,. His story is one of patience and perseverance. He started in Luweero, moved to Maddu in Mpigi in the late 1980s and finally to Kampala. In Kampala, he sold clothes at St Balikudembe Market, before he found his niche in pork.
"I started with around sh12,000 in 1990," Kasajja says. At the time, he had four poles and papyrus that created a shade.
It was the typical village pork joint: Dirty men without shoes, chopping a small piece of pork. The joint had no name. Kasajja says: "It was very difficult to see people of class eating pork in the open.
Many people bought the pork, roasted it and ate it at their homes. I worked harder and saved a lot," he says. "One of the biggest problems in Uganda is that people do not want to save. They think that one must first have millions before he or she starts saving."
"I expanded the pork shade in Makindye, added a few chairs and another shade. From my savings, I was able to establish the chain of pork joints." As the business expanded Kasajja's patrons found problems directing their friends to the joint. The issue of the name then came in.
"Hakuna Matata' appealed to me. It is soothing when one says, there is no problem," he explains. He operates five pork joints. Two in Makindye and the other three in Kabalagala, Nakulabye and Kiwatule.
Kasajja dresses colourfully. When The New Vision visited him at one of his joints, he was dressed in a basketball outfit of the Los Angeles Lakers. He looked like a basketball player.
The pork joints are painted white with a little brown and red. "White signifies cleanliness," he explains.
Like the rest of the walls and floors of all the joints, the toilet walls are covered with white tiles. There are cleaners in the toilets all the time.
Animal paintings also feature at Kasajja's joints. At one of them in Makindye, there are big statues of cattle, crocodiles and lions. Around them is a fountain. Because Kasajja loves perfection, he says it takes long to construct the joints. "I grew up seeing cattle. My grandfather had so many and I loved them," he says.
By any standard, Kasajja is a successful man. "One of the reasons I have succeeded, is because I supervise my businesses," he says. Kasajja and his wife wake up at 6:00am everyday.
"We buy pork from Wambizzi near Nalukolongo," he says. On a holiday or a weekend, each of the five joints slaughters about 10 pigs. A pig weighs about 25kgs. Therefore, an average of 250kgs is sold at every joint. Once the pork is delivered at the joints, the fat is removed.
Years ago, there was a belief that roasted pork was the reserve of the poor. "Today, I think that pork is the reserve of everybody, including the rich and famous," Kasajja explains. He says unlike chicken, goat and beef that are roasted everywhere, pork is exceptional.
His customers include professionals and non-professionals.
At Kasajja's pork joints you will find an assortment of drinks, pool tables and luxurious unisex hair salons.
He never had formal education. Unlike most business people, Kasajja attributes his success to savings. "I have a dream of owning a building in the centre of the city. You can only do it if you have support from the bank," he says.
Adjusting his LA Lakers unscripted cap, he says failure to go to school was not an obstacle, but a challenge. He has been able to view many things with a much wider lens.
"All of us have unique factors that can help us succeed whether we get an education or not. I discovered these traits and adopted them," he adds. Kasajja employs at least 150 people. He loves cars. Among his fleet of vehicles is a Land-cruiser with a personalised number plate. "I like watching motor sport. I also do martial arts," he says.
Kasajja was born to Michael Kyeyune, a resident of Wakiso district. Kasajja married in the early 1990s. He has six children, the oldest of whom is in S2. Kasajja is a happy man, thanks to pork.

Original_Search_Title: Sampling Gulu's Hangouts
Headline: Uganda: Sampling Gulu's Hangouts
Body_Text:
For a weekend get away, Gulu mostly serves up low-grade accommodation, hearty but costly meals and a vibrant nightlife that extols area musicians. The wearisome four-hour journey from Kampala by road has few delights - skewer served goat at Kafu, the roaring Karuma falls and cute monkeys on the homestretch.
That will ordinarily make you crave arriving at a hotel where you will be given a welcome drink and hot towel. Acholi Inn, for a long time one of the shining pieces of the Uganda Hotels consortium will offer you neither.
But then again, no one cares about the finicky traveller in this tranquil town emerging from a civil war. Still, Acholi Inn is the choice venue for high-profile events like the recent Miss Uganda regional final.
But the open sky has its drawbacks where the promise of a starry night can quickly turn into one where rain clouds hang low and the chilly weather leaves contestants wearing gooseflesh. Punctuality is not a virtue here. A 7p.m. starting time on a poster will be read as 9.30p.m., the actual time revellers will arrive in droves and pay Shs5,000 uncomplainingly.
Apart from that and a restaurant that serves meals a la carte, there is nothing about this almost rundown place that could use a facelift.
The tragic thing is that you will most probably not find room at the inn especially if the inter-university games officials are in town. The pretty young things that stroll along the town's shop-lined streets must be due to the existence of a university in the district.
Gulu Sunset Hotel on Lagara Road is the next alternative for budget accommodation. But it's only after one checks into the three-storey facility that one comes face to face with the odds upcountry facilities face.
A three-day power outage meant National Water and Sewerage Corporation could not pump water, an occurrence that renders the "self-contained" useless if guests cannot use flush toilets or have a shower.
The hotel got around this mishap by ferrying water in 20-litre plastic jerry cans and rationing one for each guest. Heaven knows how they carry them up the narrow stairway. There is no better lesson in using resources sparingly than that. I did not have an arresting view from my hotel room window and the overnight blare from a nearby nightclub meant I could hardly get any shuteye.
Most of the Gulu streets are a cluster of shops with no surprising food finds but lunch can be had at the adjacent Diana Gardens, a hangout that could pass for a sports bar.
Its strategically placed TV sets screening DStv Supersport soccer matches positions it as the ideal place to catch up on the Premiership or La Liga action. The soccer lovers here are a surprisingly very disciplined lot.
At least they did not bang or step on the tables while celebrating a goal. Diana Gardens serves up a bland buffet at Shs4,000, one where the local malakwang (a slimy vegetable soup) delicacy usually eaten with layata (sweet potatoes) is absent.
Be sure to try out the grilled pork served with either French fries or crispy cassava chips. It is an evening snack to delight the palate. It must be an ideal venue for private parties as the expatriate community that was holding an outdoor birthday party there can attest.
The shiny metallic beverage flasks at Peyero Guest House (next to Diana Gardens) will lure anyone craving a tea or coffee but the waitresses' quirky behaviour here is in its own league. When we pointed out that the milk in our African tea tasted curdled, we were shrugged off with a "that's all the milk we've got" response. Trust the "call the manager" demand to always work. A lady apologised profusely about her employee's unbecoming behaviour and offered us "black tea" on the house.
Going dancing
If you carried your dancing shoes, be sure to head to Havana (entry is free) and although you will not be met with salsa dancing and Cuban cigars, it is a place that "happens" and also one where you will at least find Smirnoff Red Ice (there was a scarcity of Smirnoff Black Ice that weekend). Havana that Saturday seemed to be living in a time warp.
The DJ played 80s South African bubblegum music; Yvonne Chaka Chaka, Brenda Fassie, Pat Shange Sipho "Hotstix" Mabhuse - the works. Like I said, local artistes rule and for almost two hours, the DJ played synthetic larakaraka/ding-ding and bwola beats that almost brought the house down as locals replicated the captivating dance routines on the discotheque floor.
Colonel Walter Ochora, the flamboyant former district chairman who never ceases to tell all and sundry that it was he that announced the July 1985 coup that exited Obote, was absent. Everyone knows him as a fixture on the town's revelling scene.
It's a relief though that Gulu is not quite like Kuito, the bullet-ridden Angolan city that was for a long time the poster child for civil war devastation.
And the best advice for any Kampala dweller heading up north would be to pack your own Smirnoff Black Ice if you feel you will miss its crispy taste while you are there.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rises Again to 8.9 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Rises Again to 8.9 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — INCREASES in fuel prices leading to higher transport cost and the impact of high utility charges have sharply increased the inflation rate.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) released by Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) for April indicates that Uganda's underlying inflation - the price of items excluding food items - has risen from 8.5 per cent in March to 8.9 per cent in April.
On the other hand, the CPI figures reveal that the annual headline inflation for the year ending April 2007 rose to 6 per cent from 4.5 per cent for the year ended March 2007. The annual headline inflation measures the changes in prices of all items including food items.
Speaking at a news conference at Statistics House on April 30, the Director of Macroeconomics, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana said since the beginning of the fiscal year 2006/07 there has been persistent increase in Uganda's inflation rate both underlying and annual indicating that the economy is having internal shocks.
"With just two months remaining to the end of the financial year, we do not expect the inflation rate to go down in this fiscal year. We note that the economy has been experiencing high inflationary pressure, and we do not expect that the inflation rate will settle at 5 per cent or below," he said.
"Unless everything registers a zero or negative rate, then we cannot expect a low inflation rate for the year that is about to end," he said.
Though the government wants the inflation rate to be below the 6 per cent mark, it remained stable at 8.9 per cent in January and February 8.9 and 8.5 in March.
He said that the increase in electricity and water tariffs twice during the year had a lasting impact on high costs of utility services.
Mr Ssewanyana said the reasons for the rise in headline inflation included the rise in food items such as fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry, fish milk and sugar, fuel and utilities compared to the same period last year.
On the monthly changes, he said that the monthly inflation rate for April rose further by 2.6 per cent compared to an increase of 0.4 per cent in March, which he said was the highest monthly increase since March 2006.
"Prices of vegetables, especially beans, onions, cabbages and tomatoes as well as fruits (passion fruits and oranges also increased in a number of centres," he said.
Other items, which registered higher prices, include fish, milk, rice bread and sugar. Mbarara registered the highest increase of 14.6 per cent followed by Masaka and Kampala at 7.1 per cent and 6.1 per cent respectively.
Other price movements during the month of April were registered petrol, and paraffin which went in various centers.
In addition, prices of washing soap, new materials and rental charges also rose in some centres officials attributed the in crease rental as result of increase in electricity and water.
Under the period under review, food prices increased by 5.7 per cent.
All centers except Mbale registered increases in prices of staple foods, especially matooke, Irish potatoes and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Entrenching Positive Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Entrenching Positive Leadership
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA today is a veritable 'failing' state, if not actually a failed state. With at least 70 per cent of its population living below the poverty line, an average life span of just a little over 43 years and infant mortality and maternal mobility being among the worst in world, hardly can anyone be in doubt that we are in dire straits. Not only have we displayed our inability to perform a task as ordinary as counting ourselves, we are exhibiting to the entire world our gross lack of capacity to effectively manage the transfer of power from one administration to another through the ballot box.
In almost every department of human endeavour, Nigeria has been found wanting. Despite being the world's eighth largest oil exporter, we have been unable to be assured of absence of queues at the filling stations. Nor can we guarantee 24-hour supply of electric power at any point in the country while criminals seem to have taken over in our cities and villages with police response to the cries and anguish of citizens muffled, weak-kneed and despicably few and far between. Not only is corruption rife in the land, people in leadership positions across the country seem to have lost every sense of shame. The immunity of our uniformed personnel in their inter-relationship with their hapless compatriots is unrelenting. The near total collapse of social infrastructure throughout the country has reduced our daily existence to a most primitive level. The sense of disillusionment and hopelessness that has gripped the land is reflected in the long queues of our youth (and some elderly ones, too) at foreign consulates in quest of visas in a bid to escape the nightmare that Nigeria has since become. Even football, which used to be possibly the only unifying factor in the country has fallen on bad times, so much so, that the Super Eagles are now anything but super. In sum, everything seems to have gone awry and there does not seem to be any light at the end of our miserable tunnel.
Undoubtedly, Nigeria is incredibly blessed. Aside from having the largest population on the African continent, Nigerians possesses an abundance of talents. There is hardly any area of human endeavour that Nigerians have not produced experts in. With 80 universities and numerous other institutions of higher learning, we have nearly two million students pursuing different courses that can clearly make a difference in Nigeria's underdevelopment if carefully deployed.
As far as natural resources go, Nigeria is Africa's leading oil producer and the world's leading producer of cassava and niobium, a strategic mineral. In fact, there is hardly any mineral on the Mendeleey table that Nigeria is not richly blessed in. Our rich tropical forest contains flora and fauna that are the envy of others. With gas reserves enough to last over 300 years and a vibrant and intelligent population, Nigeria is clearly destined for greater heights. It is on account of this, that the eyes of foreign investors are riveted to the country despite its harsh economic and political environment.
Claude Ake never tired of observing the deleterious consequences of Nigeria's "disarticulate" economy, by which he meant an economy which produces what it does not consume and consumes what it does not produce. He argued that except, unless and only to the extent that Nigeria is able to solve this riddle would the country's future be assured. I agree with him absolutely. However, the problem with Nigeria, as Chinua Achebe argued, was that of leadership. According to him, Nigeria might have all the human and natural endowments necessary for an effective take off, but without the requisite leadership, all is liable to come to naught. I also agree with him.
In view of the foregoing, the task of identifying and empowering a leadership possessing the requisite leadership values for transcending the privations and distortions that haunt Nigeria constitutes, perhaps, the most important issue confronting the country today. In the midst of false prophets and all manner of pretenders, it is getting increasingly difficult to identify elements possessing the wherewithal to move the country forward. Nevertheless, the truth of the matter is, for as long as we fail and, or neglect to enthrone the right kind of leadership, for that long would Nigeria continue to wobble round and round like a barber's chair, with all motion, no movement. But then, what is the right kind of leadership that we are talking about?
Leadership is a combination of both vision and mission for the betterment of the existential conditions of the masses of the people. In other words, the ability to transcend immediate privations and articulate a blueprint for rapid socio-economic transformation, undoubtedly, constitutes a major ingredient of leadership in contemporary Nigeria. More important, positive leadership entails not only the ability to 'talk the talk" but also being able to "walk the talk."
Accordingly, a leadership which make loud noises on patriotism and self-sacrifice but see nothing wrong in taking the first flight out to consult foreign doctors at the first signs of a headache or muscle pull is not one deserving of the appreciation of the generality of Nigerians. A leadership which deems it fit and proper to salt away huge sums of unearned income in foreign locations or finds it necessary to dispatch its children and wards to far-flung corners of the world for their upbringing and education is definitely not one that can lift Nigeria out of its current morass. A leadership that sees nothing wrong in appropriating national heirlooms and the collective patrimony in the face of mass poverty, privation and alienation is totally unbefitting of being entrusted with the destiny of the nation.
The point being made is that leadership should be by example and not just by precept. If a leadership fails to share the experience of the people, then it should be considered as having been bereft of any claim to presiding over the affairs of the people. In fact, the days of such a leadership would surely be numbered and when the inevitable occurs, there would be no tears shed. On the contrary, the people would actually rejoice in expectation that its replacement would ameliorate their niggardly existence.
As our people say, fish starts getting rotten from the head. So, a leadership manifesting clear symptoms of leadership failure simply cannot endure. Such a leadership is apt to instigate restlessness among the people such that before too long, it would be confined to the ash heap of history. Failure by a leadership to properly articulate the way ahead and put round pegs in round holes could, more often than not, be fatal to its tenure. Furthermore, hardly is anything more deadly to any leadership today than lack of probity and transparency. Corruption and non-accountability constitute the bane of leadership in many parts of the so-called Third World. Therefore, any leadership that craves positivism must be upright, honest and accountable.
Regrettably, for a variety of reasons, ballot-box democracy has not always ensured that the best candidates occupy leadership positions in our society. Quite often, the most gifted in intellect or perception are not always the most endowed, materially speaking. Nor are the best and brightest enthused by the rough and tumble of what goes in a country such as ours for politicking. Thus, more often than not, we had ended up with mediocre people in public office. Admittedly, meritocracy, by and large, operates in the private sector but that is not to discount the impact of influence-peddling and the old boy network in leadership selection even in that sector.
What it all boils down to is that we can be oblivious to the emergence of leadership positions in society only to our peril. If our votes count, (and there is no reason why they should not count), our people must be fully ready to exercise their franchise at the polling booths in order to ensure that the political leadership that emerges at the end of the day reflect our value preferences. It is only by so doing that we would be able to hold government accountable to us, the people. If the notion of a social contract is to mean anything at all, the political leaders must be constantly reminded of their manifestos or stand accused of political swindle. We need to make sure that the days are gone when the electorate merely cast their votes and then go to sleep. A good done of skepticism of the people vis-a-vis their leader is quite healthy for democracy and good government. As long as the people remain docile, accepting without complaining whatever their leaders dish out to them, for that long would their interests be discounted in the scheme of things.
Leadership is all about service to the people and not self-aggrandizement as many of our leaders think. In our peculiar circumstances, we should cease and desist from perceiving our leaders as bosses instead of servants. This carry-over from colonial times needs to be jettisoned in favour of an attitude that is 21st century-compliant such that we become ready, willing and able to stand up to our leaders whenever they falter or depart from the pursuit of the greatest happiness of the greatest number of our people.
It is generally agreed that ours is a great country with tremendous potential to actualise its manifest destiny if only we are able to get the leadership question right. It is true that past history has not been particularly cheering news to many Nigerians but that should be no reason for despair since we need constantly to keep hope alive and believe in our ability to effect a turnaround in our affairs.
The problems of poverty and underdevelopment are such that only a gifted and imaginative leadership can be in a position to confront and transcend them. Whether or not we can identify and install such a leadership is a matter lying in the womb of time. Meanwhile, the next elections would afford us another opportunity to have a rendezvous with history and we can only hope the people would make the right choices.
-Prof. Oyebode, a former Vice Chancellor of University of Ado Ekiti, is currently head of Jurisprudence and International Law, University of Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Cleaning And Beautifying Kampala is for Us All
Headline: Uganda: Cleaning And Beautifying Kampala is for Us All
Body_Text:
Kampala — I WANT to begin by thanking the residents of Kampala for the numerous and very encouraging responses I received on my first article on the subject of how we can work together to improve Kampala city's standards of hygiene.
It showed me that this city can be clean because the people who live in it care and can change their attitudes and personal habits to ensure that it does.
This time, I would like to, one more time, dwell on the matter of personal responsibility with regard to the cleanliness of our homes and our neighbourhoods, because "charity begins at home" as the saying goes. We will, next time, tackle the issue of the city authorities charged with garbage management, as well as other matters in the public domain.
As I said before, we must begin by sorting out, and in our homes, the type of waste we generate, and decide how it should be disposed of. There is the obvious and most sensitive waste which is common to all of us - the waste generated by our bodies: urine, human excreta and the water we use whenever we wash our bodies.
A good number of Kampala city residents have indoor plumbing and it is supposed to almost automatically and cleanly dispose of this type of waste. Unfortunately, systems break down, especially if they are not maintained properly. It is very important for the householder, or the landlord, to keep their sewage systems well maintained so that this human waste can be transported to a safe place where it can decompose safely and even enrich our environment.
Left to float above the surface of our roads, as is often the case, it becomes a hazard to our health and an ugly, smelly testimony of our inability to manage our waste. Here, I would particularly like to appeal to landlords of some of the old buildings in town to ensure that their sewage systems are repaired or up-graded, to avoid the spillages we commonly see on our city streets. I would also like to appeal to the users of such toilet facilities not to flush material that does not dissolve in water down their toilets.
Then there are large areas of our city where residents use the pit latrine. Constructed properly, and in the right location, the latrine is an efficient way of human waste disposal, if the householder cares to maintain it and clean it and its environs properly. As a matter of fact, the people in the countryside, who have no modem sewage systems and have to rely on pit latrines, do manage them very well and they are cleaner than the city dwellers. Here, we must task the landlords to ensure that their properties have adequate pit latrines and bathrooms, and that when the pits are full, they are promptly emptied, for the sake of the people they charge money to live in their houses.
I have said that there must be laws and by-laws to ensure that communities and individual householders observe strict standards of hygiene. And I have been informed by Local Government that there are laws and by-laws on our books. If such by-laws exist within the city of Kampala, then they must begin to be strictly enforced. Simply because you have built a home and enclosed it within a wall does not give you the right to make the entire neighbourhood suffer with your broken-down sewage system! You are free only so long as your freedom does not encroach on the freedom and well-being of your neighbours. We can only be called a "community" if we learn to respect and uphold the rights and freedoms of all those in our neighbourhood.
The second type of household garbage mainly comes from food-processing and food-preparation. The types of foodstuffs we eat tend to produce a good deal of waste - for example, banana, cassava and potato peelings are quite bulky. Fresh vegetables and fruits also generate waste product. This is not necessarily a bad thing, depending on how we handle these waste products. The wise householder will see value in these bio-degradable products and make manure out of them for his or her flower bed or kitchen garden. The objectionable thing you must not do is to let the house-help throw them outside your hedge, in that "no man's land" which quickly becomes the dumping ground of all the neighbours. Rather, teach your house worker to sort out garbage, burn that which does not rot, and make compost with those which degrade. Then, your problem will be the stuff that is harmful to the environment, such as the bottles and the plastic bags.
I am aware, of course, that wastes such as plastic bags and plastic bottles are a menace. While the authorities are still struggling to find long-term solutions to these problems, what should the ordinary resident of Kampala do with them? The first thing you can do is stop littering the city or your residential area with these items! Everyone can do this. Secondly, get back into the habit of carrying a natural-fibre basket to market, even to the supermarket, for your shopping.
You do not have to carry 10 plastic bags home each time you buy food! This way, you will also support the women who weave these baskets. Happily, I have learned that there is now an industry in Kampala which buys used plastic bottles and "buveera", if they are clean, for re-cycling. This is excellent news, and we may soon see the boys around town actually collecting such items and earning some money from them.
I would appreciate your responses and input to this discussion, using the contact below. I am convinced that if we think together, we will defeat the menace garbage represents in our city.
The writer is the First Lady of Uganda

Original_Search_Title: Witnessing the Ame University Funeral
Headline: Liberia: Witnessing the Ame University Funeral
Body_Text:
These are excerpts from a cleric and a school pupil's conversation somewhere around the administrative building:
School Pupil: "Are you serious Bishop, by the end of the tenure you will be a millionaire?"
Cleric: "That's exactly what I mean; I'm not discussing illusion my young man, I soon as am out as Bishop, I am in as a Liberian millionaire.
School pupil: "Oh, Great idea! Great idea! So, by what means?"
Cleric: "Little inquisitive chap, you do not have to be a UL student to decipher how much power I've to turn the wheels of fortune. Oh, you think I spent all my money to become Bishop for nothing. The country running ooh, my pekin (small brother)!
School Pupil: "You are leaving vague expressions in my mind. Who you care to comment?
Cleric: "To be frank, everything is now 'spoils of war' and the 'documents pass through my office before going to the oversee partners.
I waited in vain anticipating the early morning crow of the few hens and roosters left in the community. I rolled in bed, or rather changed position from one end of the mattress to the other, keeping this motion a pattern whenever a particular place soaked-wet with perspiration. I kept at this tactics until every spot on the pallet soaked deep with sweat as if immersed in a bucket of water. As a matter-of-course, in the ensuing discomfiture, the only alternative to the thinking mind would be, slipping off the mattress onto the cold flood for temporary comfort. Dishearteningly however, the congestion of the room's square, and the fact that the four-inch mattress filled every space in the tiny room negated this option. The only additional space in the US$50 a month downtown rented room, was occupied by a table that had a lamp, a pile of books and a single bowl used to mix my gari (cassava dough). And so, my fate would be left to be decided by renegade mosquitoes operating within my premises without permit, singing their strange songs as they feasted on my most vulnerable parts.
I rolled again to the next end of the pallet and jammed the wall, taking advantage of a little dry spot. My ears remained open, eager to hear the fowl's announcement of coo-coo-lea-coooo! But instead of hearing the crowing of a cock, the merciless stinking of the lethal insects brought to my mind a fresh reminder of my being a product of the AMEU, an Institution once associated with all the positives; now with a chief mastermind of negations and from all the many diabolic unearthing, it would appear as though it is a part of our culture to import all the unwanted religious toxics there, and as far as our happy history of this great revolutionary learning laboratory is concerned, the unraveling contretemps are the perfect doings of a cleric who has now come to undo what great bishops of the 14th Episcopal District of the AME Church had collectively done in the running of the fastest growing degree granting institution , my dear African Methodist Episcopal University.
These are the woes that baffle even the least literate, my eighteen-month old boy being amongst them. He once questioned, "It is true that all the good people in the AME Church are dead. What has happened to all the white bishops from America? Why did the Church leave all the good people there and tote all the bad things to come bring trouble to us?" These were not mere questions thrown to test the brightness of a father, rather, my boy sought cogent and satisfactory answers in the midst of torturing agony; his tears filled his eyes and pour over like a pit less carrier. I was still in deep sleep as my boy became infuriated. He cried restlessly without comfort and then he started chewing his book bag leaving the threads fully exposed. Without any available corridor for solace, he did the unbelievable: started the masticating the threads left over. His shouts compelled gathered birds to be strewn to the four winds of the heaven. But that was many years back though memories are still afresh as though it was just yesterday. These are the compelling factors motivating my fight against eco-social ills. Almost my entire generation got sacrificed on the desecrated altar of merciless dogmatism. And I must fight to prevent others from being sacrificed; this is how I honor their memory, not vice versa.
I still remained in bed however, hugged to the wall awaiting the announcement of a particular rooster that refused to crow. There were similarly other colleague roosters and hens, too in this neighborhood. You could find them everywhere a year ago until just now. Someone has said that the prevalence of unparalleled corruption at the AME University under the current regime has led to a conspiracy theory against chicken kingdom! Even chickens can no longer be allowed to be domesticated in the midst of the absence of everything. Dogs, too, are in harms way as scores of animal thieves roamed all over the place, carrying hammers and sticks carefully fixed to hustle a 'whuu' (Vai name for dog) for a hot market! I still waited, half awaked, half asleep and it was beginning to look like the chickens were on a go-slow. But no, the ones in my community had simply been quieted due to the fear of some "lynchpin" the era and we were left without a crier to wake us up daily. But all was not lost, a nearby mosque took up the responsibility and the early morning prayers of their religious leaders console us to forget the fowl's disappearances.
I was now up, I anxiously put my phone on, being opportune to be a faithful subscriber of Lonestar Cell, my phone has a clip art coding system, I received over 50 messages signaling people wanting to call me. The first caller was an unknown student, only identified as Fred. "The bombshell has been dropped". He echoed. Alieu, the Student leader at the University and his co were suspended for two semesters" There were other callers, one after the other, all sharing the already acquired information. There were voices filled with intermixture of facts, some bit of fantasy, a little more of sentimentalism, and a lashing at friends and foes alike. Almost all of the callers, except two took a clear position on the unfolding institutional burlesque. On the other hand, as the maelstrom whirkpooled across the land, the conversation became entrenched in the minds, mouths and midst of the ordinary students. And day, by day, it seemed to be gearing up to unimaginable proportions to the disadvantage of the conspirators.
First, the factor of timing heavily weighed against the masterminds of the subterranean intrigue. In their underestimation of the influence and inveigles of Alieu Nyei, they unleashed the game plan in manners and forms totally detached from the most rudimentary political wisdom. Had they not been overly emotional, unnecessarily overcharged and, having worked out too much with fist punches bigger than their boxing gloves, they may have at least succeeded to a level we can not determine. The cleric should have known that the premature hatching of a conspiracy to kick Alieu Nyei and others out with polo boots would have created a countermeasure. To think that they expected Nyei to play freeze like his name and form a cheering squad to go into applaud for his own demise, is a monumental flabbergast. This is their dilemma; two semesters or more will be left as an open space where anything could happen before the set date to present the resolution. In his calculation, once you get the board to reach a decision, it becomes the "word of God that goes out and cannot return void." But I am sure they have now learned in the strictest way that the legitimacy of any decision is determined at conscious, not in the cabal chambers of an existing parallel throne! More so, when you gave two semesters to an Alieu, it will simply be melted by descending heat waves and in the process, every space is occupied through expansion. So, the current countermeasures are just a part of the expansions of the melted Alieu, Bility, Charles and others that has just now transformed into tormenting heat waves causing a lot of discomfort for the disciples of lawlessness.
From another crotch, the concourses of the unethical warriors proved to be astonishingly misguided and unequivocally consigned to mediocrity. This fact became evident when the cabalists refused to change strategy even though their mode of operation and the very axis, upon which the conspiracy spanned, had become public knowledge. When "The School's Funeral; beware the ides of March" was envisaged and written, it was not an attempt to build an embankment for the shielding of an individual, it was an objective drive to prevent the paralysis of a shrieking cleric who receives over Thirteen million Liberian Dollars annually that amounts to 90% of the AMEU's running cost that it is diverted to someone's personal coffers. The schemes of an unsuspecting religious mischief maker who sub contracted a spinster, Christiana, who is on records for calling the Student Leader an Islamic Muslim fundamentalist thereby fueling religious tensions and sanctioning the campaign for suspending Alieu on the basis that he is Muslim leading students at a church run university. More so, when the church' annual conference is about to be staged, the cleric is embarrassed that his shady deeds will soon come to light. This is the key motivator behind the purported scam to dislodge student advocates who are bent to seeing the growth of the institution.
Hence, the objective mind will easily see the nature of the plot revealed. As early as 2005, the Bishop's first casualty was Professor Forh, now Congressman, who he managed to muscle out to resign as Vice President to set the agenda to empty the coffers, it is baffling how 'distinguished members of the school's board who allow their thoughts to be subpoenaed to please the cleric. This is absolutely unfortunate! Outside of that terrain, the devil-may-care approach of these clerical cronies, oops! I mean, Board members who appear to have placed themselves on oath to satisfy the bishop and save their sitting per diems, which leaves us wondering as to whether they still deserve the honorable appellation.
As I view this matter from a vantage pedestal, my hands and mouth overflows with shocks, surprises and utmost suspense. That a cleric will stoop so low and remains an eminent danger in tearing to pieces what other bishops have helped to construct disappoints us all. Unlike the satire of pigs which portraits the dirt-ridden animal as having no understandable value for the gold ring pierced in its nose, this is an assumed "man of God" who must realize who he is! Pluralistic Liberians in an ever changing multicultural environment have good reasons to raise concerns in the unfolding institutional embarrassment. Lord, save us from evil! Sadly, the mawkish approach of this honorable cleric in the unfolding hodgepodge deserves the condemnation of every well-meaning son of the soil. Whilst it is their right to discipline students involve in riots, this was just a wakeup call, a mere genuine protestation to bring to the attention of administration that the COUNTRY WAS RUNNING and parents could not afford the 80% down- payment. Whatever the outcome of the mediation presently going on between the Education Ministry and the Bishop is, I will do a formal communication to the A.M.E. Church in South Carolina, USA requesting the replacement of the bishop, and the delay in reinstating the students will only be paralyzing the Bishop's faded objective in becoming a millionaire.
Thank goodness, some members the US-based Church Council are here to witness the conference!

Original_Search_Title: Coke Turns to 'Kokonte'
Headline: Ghana: Coke Turns to 'Kokonte'
Body_Text:
The cocaine racket that has hit the Police administration has taken a ludicrous twist following the outcome of forensic tests carried out on recently seized drugs.
The Chronicle has gathered that tests conducted on the substance, which was believed to be cocaine that was captured in the operation conducted in Tema by a group of officers from the Monitors Unit at the Accra Regional Police command, have proved negative.
Sources at the Police Headquarters hinted the paper that the parcel of the substance, when sent to the Crime Laboratory for a forensic test had turned out to be what is known in Ghanaian parlance as Kokonte (cassava flour).
It would be recalled that the paper had reported that some officers at the Monitors Unit had gone for an operation in Tema upon a tip-off and returned to their command base with a parcel of what was supposed to be the narcotic substance that had been captured in the operation.
It later emerged that the actual quantity that was captured by the peace officers was more than one parcel and so questions were raised about the whereabouts of the remaining parcels.
Subsequently, a civilian, Richard Ackon Mensah, was arrested by officials of the National Criminal Investigations Department, attempting to sell cocaine at Nyamekye, a suburb of Accra, which he later confessed was given to him by a member of the Monitors Unit, Lance Corporal Russell Ekow, who was part of the team, which went for the operation in Tema.
Meanwhile, at the same time Ekow had been given a 10-day period to produce a suspect named Sebastian, who had been arrested in the Tema operation, for negligently allowing him to escape while he was escorting him from the Labadi Police cells to the regional Headquarters in Accra.
At the moment, officials of the Accra Regional Police Headquarters have decided to keep mum on the issue on the grounds that the whole issue about the cocaine racket had been referred to the national Criminal Investigations Department (CID) Headquarters for investigations.
Some of the top hierarchy in the Accra Regional office had expressed ignorance on the drug racket with the explanation that they were not investigating the cocaine issue but rather were investigating how Ekow who went for the operations had negligently left Sebastian loose.
About two weeks ago, the Accra Regional Commander, DCOP Douglas Akrofi Asiedu a.k.a. Charge, told reporters of the paper at his office that he was not aware of issues related to the cocaine but said what he was aware of was that Ekow had been given a ten-day ultimatum to produce the suspect. "So what I know is about the jail break for which he was to face service enquiry," he added.
DCOP Akrofi said he had heard the CID headquarters had arrested Ekow on a drug-related matter but that was not under his domain.
The Accra Regional Crime Officer, Mr. Boi Bi Boi, when contacted last Sunday, was emphatic that he was not aware of the details of the cocaine case and that it was a matter in the hands of the National CID.
