The Accra Regional Police Public Relations Officer, Clemence Dompreh told The Chronicle in an interview that he was not aware of the cocaine testing negative but what he was aware of was that the case was under investigations by the CID.
"I am not aware about the cocaine testing negative. What I am telling you is that I am not aware of anything you are asking me of. The fact I know about is that the case is being handled by the CID headquarters" he told The Chronicle.
Meanwhile, Chronicle sources have expressed shock at the attitude of the police insisting that there had been a directive, which instructed that all forensic reports should be forwarded to their various Regional headquarters, where such requests were made from.
Suspicions are high that the cocaine turned into Kokonte in the hands of the police, since part of the cocaine that was captured by the CID, when it arrested Richard at Nyamekye proved positive at the spot testing.
It is believed that either the parcel that was submitted by the officers on the Monitors Unit were substituted with the Kokonte and presented as part of the exhibits found in the operation or the cocaine turned into Kokonte after submission to their bosses in the office.
The Chronicle has received a secret signal from the Police headquarters that the Inspector General of Police (IGP), Mr. Patrick Kwarteng Acheampong is fuming at the cocaine racket in the police service and has therefore instructed the CID to investigate the issue meticulously without fear or favour.
The Sources said the Police Chief was ready to deal with any officer who is involved in the case.
Chronicle further efforts to contact the Operations Director of the CID, ACP Charles Tokor proved futile as he was said to be engaged when this reporter got through to his cell phone on two occasions. One Alhassan, who responded to the phone call on one occasion, assured getting back to the paper but as at press time, no one had called.
Meanwhile, seven officers from the Monitors Unit at the Accra Regional command were yesterday, again escorted by their colleagues to the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) headquarters for interrogation on their alleged involvement in the racket.

Original_Search_Title: Labour Seeks Two Years Grace for Floor Mills Over Cassava Fortification
Headline: Nigeria: Labour Seeks Two Years Grace for Floor Mills Over Cassava Fortification
Body_Text:
ORGANISED labour in the food and beverages industry has described the two months grace given to Flour Mills Industries in the country to comply with the 5 per cent fortification of Cassava Flour as unrealistic saying most bakers are complaining of less yield and short life span for Flour fortified with cassava leading to either closure or skeletal operations of majority of flour mills companies.
Under the umbrella of the National Union of Food, Beverages and Tobacco Employees (NUFBTE), labour lamented that in addition to negative effect on operations, the jobs of members of the union are under threat.
NUFBTE in a communique at the end of its National Executive Council (NEC) meeting held in Enugu recently, appealed t.o the government to grant the flour mills industries a grace of two years to comply with this directive.
In a communique signed by Comrades John Oyenemere and Idowu Yussuff, President and General Secretary, respectively, the union said: "The NEC in-session viewed that the two months (2) grace given to Flour Mills Industries to comply with the 5% fortification of Cassava Flour as unrealistic as bakers are complaining of less yield and short life span for Flour fortified with cassava, this has made majority of the Flour Mills Industries to either close down or go on skeletal operations thereby putting the jobs of our members on the line. The NEC in-session therefore appealed to the Federal Government to grant the Flour Mills Industries a grace of two years to comply with this directive." On the epileptic power supply across the country, communique said members ofNEC expressed sadness that since the past years, the government. could not provide stable power to the citizenry despite billions of Naira spent on power.
According to t.he communique: "The NEC in-session is dissatisfied over the epileptic power supply which went down from 3000 mega watts m 1999 to 1000 mega watts in year 2007 in spite of 600 Billion expanded on power generation. The NEC in-session therefore call on the Power Holding
Company of Nigeria (PHCN) to take stringent measures to ensure adequate electricity supply to ensure the smooth operations of our industries so as to restore the hope of our employers in building this ,nation. Since Electricity\Energy supply for production has been so costly in our industry, the NEC in-session is therefore appealing to the Federal Government to approve a tax-relief for companies running their operation, on Diesel so as to ease their financial burden. The NEC in-session having considered all areas, calls on the general overhauling 'and total review of all tax laws in the country which are outdated and begging for review. It is on this note that workers believe that whatever increment is made on salaries and other Fringe Benefits may not be meaningful."
Commenting on the political situation in the country, the communique added: "The NEC in-session icondemned -in totality the shoddy, unconstitutional insensitivity, double standard, political meddling and very outrageous decisions INEC had taken to satisfy the over bloated ego of its paymasters and their nefarious intentions and therefore advised INEC to thread softly and avert the eventual disaster that is I waiting to happen which will be a great cost to our democracy."

Original_Search_Title: You Can't Uproot a Ugandan Pumpkin
Headline: Uganda: You Can't Uproot a Ugandan Pumpkin
Body_Text:
Kampala — YOU can take someone out of Uganda. But true to the Song of Lawino proverb, you can never uproot a pumpkin from its homestead. So is the case with UK Senga Betty Mandazi's home which is a magnet for Baganda living in London. "Some how I have to adjust to some things like the English law which prohibits child beating.
But the protection makes the kids so unruly. It makes them indulge in extremes like drugs, crime and early sex," she observes.
"I wish my two girls could sift out the good in English and the good in Buganda to make one nice mixed grill of cultures," she says with a grin. Contrary to expectations, after living in UK for decades luganda was the first language for her children and she sticks to the spare the rod and spoil the child belief.
"I see people coming to UK and in thirty days they speak English which is harder to understand than what the Queen of England or PM Tony Blair speaks.
The more reason even if my children were born here they must communicate with their grannies in Luganda when they go back home." In her modest home located in East London you stumble into people from all walks of life.
The doors to Senga' s home are like that of a Police Station-never shut. At one time it is the first son Kato Lule(former president YusufLule's son.). In the long queue are members of the royal family, entertainers, computer wizards, accountants and priests.
After a heated banter of catching up with Ugandan politics, entertainment, sports and business, aunt Mandazi whets her guest's appetites with a bottle of wine.
Then she invites all to the dining table. Come summer and there is BBQ carnivore party. It is an occasion to welcome summer. Inspite of being in the heart of London, like a home woman it is "Karibu" (welcome) tradition in this cosmopolitan side of the world also.
In this Ugandan Londoners home there are no banana leaves to steam the bananas, cassava or potatoes. But on sneaking into the kitchen it is evident she took an extra mile to use polythene papers to improvise.
At table dig a fat chunck of steaming banana bread. They roll it into a ball. Thumb a hole in the boulder. Then fill it with thick groundnut stew before posting it down the wine oiled throat. The stuff slides down so smoothly.
It is so soft one need not to chew it. Come to think about it. The food tastes better when you chop it with the African fork (hands.) Oiled by enva endirwa (greens.) There all you need is a huge appetite. Nakati or egobe is cooked, dried and pounded for a longer shelf life.
Meat is salted and smoked in the firewood kitchen in Uganda before being flown to UK. bananas from Jamaica. With this concerted conservatism in a foreign land Senga does not spare the boys and the girls a word of caution. "Like the old times you have to seek partners. Marriage is still a respected institution in Uganda.
And if you get a lady -treasure her like gold. Those little gifts like a ring, necklace or handkerchief make the petals of a lovers heart flutter with joy.
Take her out when Cameleon, Ragga Dee and are having shows in Club 9.. ." Senga does not spare the gorgeous girls any barbs. "Divorce is not fashionable. Mubigumire (toughen up to cushion marital hard times. )
Always respect your husband," sighs Senga under her breath. "It does not matter -even if you have a better job or more money.
There is a limit to equal rights." She shrieks with laughter. "Nature put it that men dominate. That is why they were hunters and protectors while we stayed home to take care of the home."
As if agreeing with Senga, the British law announced recently that husbands can beat their wives provided they can say sorry. But she does not spare the boys the rod. She advises them to find suitors and marry. "The family is the pillar of our survival," Senga tells the boys in the habit of sowing their wild oats abroad.
"You should be proud of the good side in our society and blow it out of proportion. Muwase, muzale-baana bange (marry and bring forth children)" she counsels youths in the Diaspora. "A bakajja mumpe details zamwe njabafunira ababeezi (New comers to UK just give me your contacts and I will put you in touch with good people.)
Going generic Senga Mandazi finally calls on her guests trapped in the foreign lands to stick to their customs. "The name Baganda comes from the word ganda (stem) it was because the English and Swahili adulterated our language and we got to be called Baganda," she gives a mini history lesson.
These messages punctuate enormous cartons of Stella Artua, Genius and Uganda Waragi being uncorked. And as she takes more beer the lessons get more precise and less articulate.
"Each of you belong to one of the 50 clans. Don't think because you are in Europe you can inter marry or eat your totem. The message will get home by phone or intemet- it's taboo."
All in all it was such a jolly good party.

Original_Search_Title: Hoima Eagerly Awaits Oil Boom
Headline: Uganda: Hoima Eagerly Awaits Oil Boom
Body_Text:
Nairobi — HE WALKS WITH CONFIDENCE along the murram road to his hotel near the shores of Lake Albert. Occasionally, people stop him for a chat. The women wave and the teenagers bow. A prominent man in his community, he could become a crucial player in the oil boom everyone is expecting.
Ofungi Kasangaki is the Local Council (LC 1) chairman of Kyeihoro village, in Buseruka sub-county of Hoima district. The setting of Kyeihoro is similar to Kaiso, Tonya, Kiamboga and many others on the shores of the Albert.
This is the area in western Uganda where foreign prospecting firms have announced discoveries of oil deposits. Figures are hard to come by, but by one estimate, there could be as many as 250 million barrels of the black gold in the ground here.
Like Kaiso and Tonya, Kyeihoro is a fishing community. It is 80 km from Hoima, the nearest big town, which is 203 kilometres from the capital Kampala.
Most of the people living in Kyeihoro depend on the lake for survival. Fishing is the main activity and they exchange their catch for food from the neighbouring villages, mainly cassava, sweet potatoes, and maize.
Ironically, the good murram road that connects to the lake has meant that fish stocks are getting depleted. At least three fish collecting trucks come to the landing sites every day to pick up the night's catch.
Most of the people who live here are members of the Alur community from Nebbi district in West Nile region. With a population of about 1,500 people in an area of about 12 square kilometres, only 15 per cent of the residents are Banyoro. The rest are Congolese and Bakinga.
Prospecting for oil is still underway, with commercial production not expected for several years.
The Congolese who cross the lake occasionally while fishing and those who have fled the war in nearby Ituri region have settled here too. Most of them are members of the Lendu ethnic group from the Democratic Republic of Congo and have intermarried with Ugandans. Residents say they are living in peace so far.
Marine officials, however, have a problem with the Congolese fishermen.
"They do not enforce strict fishing gear requirements on their side of the lake," says Mandy Robert, a maritime officer at Kyeihoro. "This is depleting the fish stocks."
"The Congolese population has increased. We cannot estimate the percentage increase but I think that the endless war in Congo sparked off the exodus to Uganda," says Harriet Musinguzi, Hoima District Information officer.
The struggle between the Congolese, Alur and the Banyoro could mark the beginning of the oil curse in this region. All of them feel they have a right to belong. Already, however, the Alur and Congolese are taking up jobs with the oil companies.
Some of the people here have mixed ethnicity. Ofungi, whose second name is Kasangaki, says he was born in Bunyoro. Kasangaki is a name from Bunyoro while Ofungi is from the Alur. He says this is his home but talks fondly of Nebbi as the land of his forefathers.
Ofungi, like the entire local community here, has big dreams of an "Oil City," Many people here believe the fishing villages will be turned into an oil city with magnificent hotels, high rise buildings, shopping malls, white sand beaches, leisure parks and so on.
THE OIL CITY WOULD OF course be inclusive of the neighbouring Kabwoya Game reserve whose animal population has doubled. Many animals can be seen roaming in the reserve in the evenings.
Joselyne Nyangoma, the environment officer for Hoima district says the buffalo, warthogs, Uganda Kob, antelopes and other animal populations have increased in Kabwoya. She attributes it to the heavy deployment of soldiers who are guarding the oil wells and who have also checked the previously rampant poaching.
The reserve has a safari lodge and a runway where jets carrying tourists land.
The government dismisses talk of an oil city and says there is nothing like that on paper. All that is being done, the government says, is planning for the area that is undergoing development.
"We are making a development plan for the Albertine Graben region. There is physical planning for the townships, infrastructure upgrading and development for the place," said Ernest Rubondo, assistant commissioner of the Petroleum Exploration Department in Entebbe.
BUT IF THE DEVELOPMENT plan comes to pass, will people like Ofungi who have set up small businesses survive? Will the development plan take them into account? Won't the displacement be the first oil curse in Bunyoro?
Government officials say some people may indeed be displaced and relocated elsewhere and that the oil policy in the offing will take care of all these issues.
So far, people like Ofungi have managed to put up simple hotels, which are big investments by the standards of the region. They are respected people in their community and can function as a link to the oil exploration companies.
Ofungi has helped several community members get odd jobs around the Tullow oil camp in Kyeihoro.
"When there is work to be done, the companies ask us, the local leaders, to recommend workers. The educational background is not considered, it is your sweat," said Ofungi. The pay is usually Ush5,000 ($2.8) per day, sometimes more."
This, by local standards, is good money, and it may improve with time when oil activities increase in the area. For now, Tullow Oil is trying to improve infrastructure for the community; roads, a wooden classroom block, health centre and borehole as well as a Lake Albert rescue project. John Moley, Tullow Oil Uganda country manager, says his company, which has operated in the area for three years, is building infrastructure to benefit the community.
The few children who have not abandoned school for fishing have benefited from the school facilities developed by the company.
Tullow has also decided to put some teachers on its payroll to improve the quality of education.
Many locals are afraid of relocation. While there is a current mini-boom, many are wary about the future, especially if they have to be relocated to make way for the oil exploration and exploitation.
But officials in Hoima district are upbeat. District officials are planning to develop the district's infrastructure as soon as their percentage of the revenue comes through.
The percentage the district is entitled to is not yet known as the oil policy has not been finalised. A committee has, however, been set up to take care of the area's interests.
"Since the colonial days, we have not had a tarmac road in the district," says Yunus Mugabe, speaker of the district council. "When we get the money from the Fund as stipulated in the draft policy, we shall carry out our development plans for the three year duration that we usually develop."
The town has big plans for the oil money, but interestingly, many people are ignorant about the location of the wells. They cite impassable roads - that have since been greatly improved - for not making the journey there. They also cite the stringent process one has to go through to visit the wells.
The traditional King of Bunyoro, Solomon Gafabusa Iguru I, has twice been denied entry to the oil wells area during his travels around his kingdom.
In Hoima, where most of the Banyoro live, many frown at the idea of "foreigners" taking their jobs down at the camps. But some are content with the booming business. Mugabe says those in the hotel business are doing well. "The hotel rates in Hoima are twice what they were some two years ago. Most of them are usually fully booked by evening."
BUT SO FAR, MICROFINANCE is the most lucrative business. Microfinance institutions have multiplied, giving a chance to whoever needs credit to start up money-making businesses.
In less than six months, the number of microfinance institutions has gone up by 60 per cent and their interest rates are as high as 20-25 per cent, says Mugabe.
The microfinance firms are probably attracted by the belief that the oil will trickle into the pockets of the local people and that they shall pay off their loans. For them, and many of the residents in this part of western Uganda, the countdown to the oil boom has already started.
.

Original_Search_Title: Research Council, Ghanaian Experts Aim High Quality Cassava Flour
Headline: Nigeria: Research Council, Ghanaian Experts Aim High Quality Cassava Flour
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Raw Materials Research and Development Council (RMRDC) is partnernering with Ghanaian experts on the production of high quality cassava flour in Nigeria.
The idea is achieve the 10 percent requirement for high quality cassava flour for producing bread and confectionaries.
RMRDC in partnership with several stakeholders Thursday started the training programme with the assistance of technical experts from Ghana for Nigerian industrialists involved in the sector.
Already, the target of government is to popularize cottage level production of high quality cassava flour and glucose syrup from cassava. Among other things, participating industrialists are being introduced to a simple production process recently developed in Ghana by the resource persons.
The training is designed to train the trainers on the production for high quality glucose syrup. It is expected that participants that would be trained at the programme would in turn train others at zonal and state levels.
The Ministry of Science and Technology recently constituted a committee of stakeholders to produce a blue print on cassava development. Part of the activities of the committee included a study tour of selected cassava producing countries to find out what they are doing with cassava and explore areas of possible collaboration with other countries.
In a message to the participants, Director General of the Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Prof. Peter Onwualu stressed that the Council believes in the establishment of small scale, and cottage level processing centers to produce the needed high quality cassava flour. According to him, "This is because cassava processing is a traditional business of the local populace in this country. Processing centers for the high quality cassava flour production should therefore be such as to fit into the cultural practices if the people. "In this regards, the Council has been making a lot of efforts to identify the technology that will easily adapt to this situation. This technology has now been identified.

Original_Search_Title: IT World - Nigcomsat Ready May - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: IT World - Nigcomsat Ready May - Minister
Body_Text:
The Nigerian communication satellite will be launched on May 14, 2007, Minister of Science and Technology, Professor Turner T. Isoun has said.
Isoun said the satellite had already been completed and moved to the launching site.
Professor Isoun who addressed journalists at a workshop organised for science correspondents in Abuja said the purpose of the workshop was to bridge the existing gap between the scientists and journalists especially in the space technology.
He said it was important to get journalists involved in the activities of the science and technology if the sector has to grow.
He said going to space is the fundamental decision by the federal government in its desire to uplift the wellbeing of Nigerians through using space technology, saying in the fast eight years, Nigeria has recorded success in the area of space.
He said their administration had raised the level of space technology from zero to number one in the sub-saharan Africa.
In his remark, the director general of the National Space Research and Development Agency (NASRDA), Professor Robert Boroffice said part of the mandate of the NARSDA is to promote space education and manpower development to actualise the gains of satellite technology through appropriate skills acquisition.
He said more Nigerians are being trained in all aspects of satellite technology in order to create the critical mass of experts needed to actualize 'our own goal of building future satellite in Nigerian by Nigerians'.
He said the earth observation satellite- NigeriaSat-1 launched in 2003 is doing well in the orbit and had met over 250 requests for data by researchers and students in universities.
According to him, satellite data derived from NigeriaSat-1 has been used in addressing the following national problems such as monitoring health and yield determination of cassava, Fadama land information system for rice growing, gully erosion, desertification, deforestation and other environmental impact assessment of oil producing in Niger Delta.
"In addition, a telemedicine network that will link university teaching hospitals and other primary health centres is on progress," he added.

Original_Search_Title: ADF Remnants Hiding in Bundibugyo - RDC
Headline: Uganda: ADF Remnants Hiding in Bundibugyo - RDC
Body_Text:
Bundibugyo — REMNANTS of the Allied Democratic Front rebels are still hiding in Bundibugyo district, the RDC has said.
Samuel Kazinga told Daily Monitor on March 20 that Intelligence reports show that there were a number of remnants of the ADF rebels hiding in the district.
Mr Kazinga said he had received reports that suspected rebels on March 16 uprooted cassava stems from the garden of Misairi Kule, a resident of Nyambega village, Itojo parish in Karugutu sub-county.
He said he later pitched camp in the area for over a week to monitor the situation and sensitise residents on security matters.
"I warned them [residents] against collaborating with rebels but advised that they should give information on the suspects to the authorities in time," he said.
Mr Kazinga said many traders in Karugutu had been tormented by the increasing number of thugs breaking into their shops.
He observed that residents had relaxed on monitoring their security.
The RDC said on the night of March 5, thugs broke into more than 15 shops at Karugutu trading centre and stole property worth millions of shillings.
He instructed LCs and Special Police Constables to be on the lookout for wrong elements in society.
The ADF mainly operated in Kasese area.

Original_Search_Title: Primary Teachers - Worth of Glory And Honour
Headline: Rwanda: Primary Teachers - Worth of Glory And Honour
Body_Text:
Kigali — If there are professions that deserve constant praise in our societies, then they are teachers. Primary school teachers in particular, play a pivotal role in shaping the young into reasonable citizens of tomorrow. I, being a former village primary teacher, have got experiences to share in addition some village primary school teachers in Rwanda that I talked to. Their stories are both interesting and awesome.
I started teaching in a primary school on a voluntary basis. I asked the headmaster (in a Umutara-based primary school) to let me volunteer to which he gladly nodded. We used to wake up in a chilly morning, take a cold shower in a makeshift bathroom made of banana fibres, pick our books and off we leave for the school.
As a new entrant in the teaching arena, there are a few things that took me time to get accustomed to; keeping on an empty stomach to 1.00pm, rushing back to our small house made of grass and mud only to eat cassava mixed with some crushed ground nuts and take a big mug of water and identifying students so congested in a class. People always used to give those stares of surprise I was the only physically endowed man in terms of seize.
The place, however, was not a total bore because there are so many humourous and awesome stories which teachers, students and the villagers could relate.
Here is a story that would make you feel like visiting such areas under any circumstances. I was having a chat with one teacher Eric (not the real name), and when I asked him what it feels like being a village Primary teacher, he had this to say:
"You know am actually a young man of 25 years of age and at first I took myself as an important man in the village. But the misconception is slowly dying down. When I started teaching, I could be invited to represent my school in government meetings and felt real big for that. The authority of ordering pupils to do what I commanded made me feel equally great. You know when you are young and you are given the whole week to command the school as a duty master, you feel so big. Another interesting thing for a young teacher is to see himself determining promotions, repeating, suspension and expulsion of pupils in a school. As you mark the papers you actually think that all is in your hands and this gives a kind of superiority complex that most primary teachers enjoy. And I used to get one or two free bottles of beer at least three times in a week from parents. All these coupled with the fact that when one stays for a given job for long tends to love it, makes me what I am today.
"But, for sure, I am getting to terms with life facts. I wasted a lot of time teaching in a primary school. It's high time I thought of continuing with my studies or resigning for a petty business. I did not know that some people earn millions every month. I thought that they are all like me! Your environment can enslave you for ever. I am now 25 and I do not know if I will ever get money, marry, build a house, drive a car and all those that makes life meaningful."
He concludes as he sweeps his last drop in the glass in a rather thirsty manner. I had to order one more to keep him in the mood.
If you are to make an analysis of the tale, do it in any way you want.
Having got a young male part of the story that was overwhelmingly interesting, I decided to go to the female young teacher for the other part of the story. It was not so easy to get a female teacher for a rendezvous (audience). There were few obvious cultural reasons; one, young female primary teachers either stay in their parents' homes or share one house. They not visit bars. When they drink, they do it indoors for it is against the Rwandan culture for a young female to drink beer in an open place. Two, if you proposed to meet a girl when she is alone, then you may stand to be misunderstood. So it took me more energy than anticipated but finally I convinced one of them to come and visit me and my fellow teachers in our house. She came one Saturday afternoon.
Mary however, gave me her part of the story concerning how she saw herself as a primary teacher in a village:
"I completed 'O' and 'A' levels. So now I teach in a Primary school. As to how I feel about the job, it is fine with me. I wake up early in the morning go teaching. At the end of the month we are paid our salaries that are of course not enough to make our ends meet. But we keep going. You see when you are a teacher, you do not expect much in terms of salary. The job, to many of us, is actually a transition to another profession.
"To be sincere most of us, girls, you see here teach, are waiting to get married to some men who earn a reasonable income for a home. And we feel we can get better jobs in future. Teaching cannot be the end of life because it will not allow you enjoy life. When we go to Kigali, we see how our age-mates dress and we envy them. It tells us much about what we miss in life. Otherwise, our parents and relatives keep on supplementing on what we earn as we search for better means."
She concludes shyly as she mentions that parents and relatives have a hand in getting them potential husbands (suits).
What implications, then, does Mary's story reflect? The implications may be far reaching depending on the interpreters or the analysts. Nevertheless, female teachers seem to be more comfortable than male teachers in village Primary schools. This is evidenced from the latitude of hope from the two parties. Male teachers seem to be more desperate than female teachers. Female teachers can be supported by their parents when working and have hopes of getting married to well to-do families. This partly explains the difference.

Original_Search_Title: Catholics Flock to Shrine for Virgin Mary Vision
Headline: Uganda: Catholics Flock to Shrine for Virgin Mary Vision
Body_Text:
"The Virgin Mary is there, that one in a sparkling white cloak holding her hands together," 9-year-old Bella Priscilla Pirwoth called out as she pointed to the top of a pine tree near Nyapea Catholic Chapel.
A flock of children joined her chorus as bewildered adults perched on the apron of the church building craned their necks to catch a glimpse of the apparition. Such scenes have been common since early January at the chapel known locally as Awedoke and sandwiched between the residence of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate and the quarters of the five diocesan priests.
The late Bishop Negri and his fellow Italian Verona Fathers founded the chapel in 1933, and Catholics in the region will celebrate its diamond jubilee next January.
But a year before the celebrations, the phenomenon has become a major attraction, pulling thousands of people from across Uganda as well as neighbouring southern Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Father Emile Onegwa, an elderly priest, says the vision of the Virgin first appeared to him inside the chapel on July 21, 2005, then again on February 4 last year. This year, he said, the vision has appeared around January 9 on a pine tree a short distance from the old building where the maiden batch of aspirants, or candidates to be nuns at Mary Immaculate, professed decades ago.
A nun joins other faithful in prayers at the chapel. Photo by Tabu Butagira
The rattle of arriving vehicles, the blare of prayer calls and the sounds of joyous worship fill the air in this previously hushed and forested neighbourhood about 10 kilometres northwest of sprawling Paidha town.
The chapel itself is only a few kilometres from the DRC Justine Ringtho, chairman of the six-member local Apparition Committee, said only about 2,000 of an estimated 900,000 believers who had flocked to Nyapea by February 22 had actually seen the apparition. He did not explain how he knew this but said he was among those who had seen it.
"She appeared in white and blue gown with a crown on her head surrounded by bright shining stars," he said. During an outdoor mass Thursday in front of the chapel, Fr. Onegwa told the crowd of hundreds that now was the right time for them to look deeply into themselves.
"The Virgin Mary has come to grant us salvation. Let us pray hard so that she helps us to overcome all difficulties and we start our journey to heaven peacefully," he said. The crowd remained quiet, then burst into deafening ululation and clapping.
Lent, the Christian period of introspection and repentance that precedes Easter began last Wednesday. Bella Pirwoth riveted the crowd with her testimony, then drew laughter when she gently teased her tearful mother Rose Mary Pakech for failing to see the apparition for the second day after travelling from Arua and offering constant prayers and reciting the rosary.
"Since Mum, you have failed to see the apparition, don't blame yourself. Just keep heart and thank the good Lord for keeping you alive," Ms Pirwoth, a pupil at Awindiri Primary School, said.
Paul Bedijo, a teacher of French at St. Aloysius College Nyapea, refuses to give up the struggle after three unsuccessful attempts to see the apparition. "I am a staunch Catholic and believe that Mary the Mother of Jesus has a role to play in our lives," he said as he jostled with children to catch a glimpse.
'"If She has appeared somewhere, I want to see Her for it will be a clear confirmation and evidence of what I believe in." The selective manifestation of the apparition is already causing emotional and social ripples in the local community amid wide-ranging inferences that it only appears to the faithful. There is a growing belief that since children are largely faultless, they are able to see it.
Desperate believers, mainly women, have turned to praying in front of statues of Virgin Mary installed inside the chapel, and others are keeping vigil on the chapel grounds.
At least 10 of the 300 some people gathered at the Nyapea Chapel Thursday professed to having seen the apparition.
But the bishop of Nebbi Diocese, Rt. Rev. Martin Luluga, has repeatedly declined to comment on the perceived miraculous happenings, explaining that such matters are "very complicated, and I have also not seen it yet".
Stephen Ocopcan, who is compiling a list of the progressive events of the apparition, said some of those individuals who have failed to see it are so distressed they are claiming to have seen the divine sight for self-fulfilment.
"I know it (apparition) is a miracle that happens to different people at different time and for different purposes. People are not seeing it because they are unhappy, ever longing for material possession without much time to renew their faith," said Ocopcan, who claimed to have seen it. "Apparition requires and brings change in people's lives. It is not a fashion."
The influx of those hoping to see the apparition has imposed additional demands on church administrators who have increased the number of weekday masses from one to three. As a result, offertory collections at the chapel have shot up from Shs15,000 to Shs 50,000 a day.
And some local businesses are booming. The demand for rosaries has grown tremendously. Stanley Gobson Omung, the chief supplier, said since last month he has sold 680 sparkling rosaries at Shs500 each, earning Shs340, 000.
Makeshift markets have sprouted up around the chapel where vendors sell all kinds of food including anyoya mix (boiled maize/beans), cassava, bananas, avocados and sugar cane.

Original_Search_Title: Pork in the Sudan
Headline: Uganda: Pork in the Sudan
Body_Text:
Kampala — This is something different. You actually travelled all the way to Sudan in search of pork?
No, we did not get into the Sudan itself, but we were pretty close. I would venture to say that we were less than an hour's drive from the border.
Getting to Classic Cuts is a bit of a gamble. It is one of those places that only the locals appear to know about because getting there from the central business district of Arua requires a person to have a degree in Geography. It is all about turning left, then right, then over a small hill and the farther you drive out, the more you ask yourself whether there is indeed a pork joint ahead.
And once you get there?
The first impression you get is that you are pulling up at a homestead. The second impression you get as you clamber out of the car is that the folk of the homestead are taking an afternoon nap.
And the third impression you get when life begins to stir is that this is not a pork place, but most certainly a homestead and that the man of the homestead is hosting a barbecue for his friends.
Talk us through the place.
Just picture a homestead. That is exactly what Classic Cuts looks like. There is a main house that is the focal point of the place and right in front of it are a number of trees under which the patrons sit.
Behind the main house are the kitchens - one for preparing the pork and the other a modern kitchen where they manufacture their own sausages and to oue surprise, beef burgers.
I presume you placed orders for kilos upon kilos of pork?
That we did considering the size of the group and I am sure that there were well over 10 kilos of special roasted pork. Way before the pork was served, by the smell emanating from the kitchen, it was obvious we were in for a treat. They did not disappoint for when put before us the pork looked very succulent. Once we started eating, all was well. The pork was very tender, in fact they would give Zanzi in Ntinda a run for their money. We then washed down the pork with a couple of beers and were set for the rest of the day.
THE GOOD POINTS: The pork without a doubt!
THE BAD POINTS: Remembering how to get there, as it is quite a drive out of Arua town.
THE PRICES: Beers cost sh1,500. Special roasted pork is at sh4,500 per kilo while ribs and roasted rib chops go for sh3,000 per unit. Extras such as cassava, avocado, tomatoes and salads will set you back sh1,500. Whole chicken costs sh10,000 while drumsticks, the breast and the wings (500gms) go for sh6,000. Beef burgers cost sh5,000.
THE KABOOZI: "How close are we to Sudan?"
THE STAFF: Not exactly the best, as they seamed to be made up of family members.
THE TOILETS:Pit latrines, so I guess I need not go any further on them.
THE CROWD: On the day we were there, it was full of MTN staff: PRO Tina Byaruhanga, Chris Ireland, Robert Afema and Guspa Mbowa, to name a few.
Classic Cuts is located in Sudan Zone, Mvara. Open all week from noon till customer demands. For reservations: 0752612573

Original_Search_Title: Butare Market Vendors Cry Foul Over Poor Sanitary Conditions
Headline: Rwanda: Butare Market Vendors Cry Foul Over Poor Sanitary Conditions
Body_Text:
Huge — Vendors and residents in Butare Town, Huye District are complaining of poor sanitary conditions in Butare Market, currently under rehabilitation. Several vendors who talked to The New Times complained that since the district authorities started rehabilitating the market last month, sanitary conditions inside the market have drastically deteriorated. According to the vendors, many of them have since either been forced out to trading in the street on the outskirts of the market or squeezed to one side of the market, making it very difficult to effectively supervise cleaning.
"The situation in the market is very bad and may result into an outbreak of diseases. Trading in the market is uncontrolled and everything is scattered with no one supervising the cleaning of the market," Vedaste Muvara, a vendor, told The New Times.
He added: "It's difficult for cleaners to sweep in such an overcrowded place."
Many other traders say the market's poor sanitary condition is due to overpopulation in areas that are not under reconstruction.
"They used to splash water in the market before sweeping, but today they don't due to the overpopulation," said Jean Hategeka, who sells clothes in the market.
He said there is a dusty smell and it is too hot in the market as vendors have little space for their activities.
But traders say the most challenging sanitary problem for them is lack of toilets.
"Imagine a whole market has no toilets, and this complicates the whole situation.
Those that were used before were too old and are being rehabilitated," a tomato vendor, Mariya Mukaneza, said.
Vendors said they are very much concerned, calling on the district authorities to wind-up the rehabilitation works to save their businesses and lives.
Another problem, they say, is the new stands in Rwabayanga Avenue near the market created by some vendors who failed to get space in the main market.
At Rwabayanga traders' stands which are near the market's main dustbin, women are selling maize and cassava floor in very bad conditions.
Some stands are very dirty, but foodstuffs and other products for sale are still placed on them.
"You can see, we are selling our goods near the dustbin and this affects our clients. Only poor people are buying our food," said Denise Uwitonze.
She said the district allowed them to work from Rwabayanga as they await completion of the reconstruction works at the market.
Uwitoze further said that the authorities are even demanding taxes for her previous stand in the main market so that she could occupy it when the reconstruction is complete.
Market cleaners hired by Huye District authorities concurred that the major cause of the hygiene problems is overpopulation in the market. According to one cleaner only identified as Mukama, the market was clean before the rehabilitation works started.
"But for us we do our best. Only that people continue dirtifying the market," she said.
Eric Uwimana, another cleaner, said good hygiene cannot be expected in the market unless the rehabilitation is completed.
When contacted, the district official in charge of Infrastructure, Jean Marie Vianney Munyaneza, said the first phase of the market's rehabilitation will be completed by next month. He however called on vendors to ensure proper sanitation in the market.
He said the completion of the first phase will help the market have toilets again, bring about 250 traders back to their previous stands and solve the sanitation problems.

Original_Search_Title: Manufacturing Falls Behind
Headline: Namibia: Manufacturing Falls Behind
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The manufacturing sector needs to increase its share of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) to 30 percent for Namibia to achieve its targeted economic growth of 7 percent.
The concern remains that the current 10.7 percent that the sector contributes is lower than what is needed for the country to achieve its developmental goals as stated in the country's road map - Vision 2030.
The Vision 2030 document expects the manufacturing and services sectors together to contribute 80 percent of Namibia's GDP, and the export of largely processed goods to account for not less than 70 percent of Namibia's total exports.
But since 16 years ago, Namibia has had very little industrial growth, so much so that it continues to import most manufactured products, mainly from neighbouring South Africa, because the sector is characterized by structural weaknesses and operational constraints that include high input costs relating to electricity, transport and port charges.
"The manufacturing sector has an unusually low share of Namibia's national output, employment and exports compared to countries such as Singapore, South Africa and Ireland," says a summary paper, "Assessing the Potential of the Manufacturing Sector in Namibia".
The report, compiled by the Research Department of the Bank of Namibia, was to identify and assess potential products within the sector that could increase growth and also to suggest possible policy options to increase manufacturing output to achieve the goals of Vision 2030.
The bank conducted research and field surveys with selected government ministries and agencies, the private sector and 26 manufacturing companies in Windhoek, Arandis, Swakopmund and Walvis Bay.
The study identified high input costs, availability of quotas for the fishing industry, low throughput for the meat processing industry and unfair competition from well-established South African companies as some of the constraints facing the local manufacturing sector.
Other problems facing the sector include a small domestic market, which leads to the lack of economies of scale, low levels of productivity, the negative impact of HIV/AIDS, lack of advertising opportunities for Namibian manufacturers, unavailability of highly skilled personnel and the processing of and acquiring of work permits.
The BoN paper identified new products which Namibia could consider producing, including plates, pencils, desk chairs, large-scale tiles, marble, granite, karakul pelts, mineral water bottles, processing Devil's Claw, cassava for fuel production, metal products, auto components, unique wood, palm and clay, ready-made fish meal and leisure garments and many others.
Yesterday, the Namibian Manufacturers' Association held a workshop to develop a Marketing Strategy for Namibia. The terms of reference for the strategy were developed by the NMA and approved by the Office of the Prime Minster and the Trade and Industry minister last year.
Prime Minister Nahas Angula yesterday expressed concern over Namibia's inability to diversify its economy by creating new industries in the secondary sector. He said Namibia faced high unemployment, which could be attributed to capital-intensive production and lack of a value chain.
"A value chain is created through value addition. Value addition creates a chain of activities, which create more employment. At the same time, the value chain enables a country to derive benefits from its products," said Angula when he opened the workshop.
He added that more people would derive benefits from production processes if more people earn an income and also lift themselves out of poverty.
He said problems were rife, yet Namibia exports both raw materials and capital and although mining contributes significantly to the GDP and government, minerals are exported in the form of concentrates, yellow or in barely processed form.
Some of the constraints that the PM said should be investigated, as Namibia forges ahead in creating a vibrant secondary industry, are the availability of a framework or infrastructure for technology leveraging, the provision of necessary support for market research and development, a competition regime to protect infant industries and the availability of managerial and administrative capacities to develop a competitive manufacturing sector.
According to the bank's study, products that Namibia could use optimally that are already being exported elsewhere are tiles, slabs, monuments, grave stones, tables, beds, cupboards, school and office furniture, beer, carbonated soft drinks, meat-packing marking machines, dairy products, mineral water, fruit juices, steel windows, door frames and aluminium windows.
Other products are maize-meal and hoodia, automotive and washing-machine parts, field shoes, tin cans, packaging material and salt.

Original_Search_Title: Beans On High Demand
Headline: Uganda: Beans On High Demand
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for beans and maize flour has escalated, leading to an increase in prices, a weekly survey has shown.
Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market, said: "We are receiving some food but it cannot satisfy the growing demand."
"Schools are scrambling for beans and maize flour. Demand in southern Sudan is up. What do you expect?" he asked.
A kilogramme of maize flour was sold at sh800 from sh700, beans went for sh1,000 from sh700 while maize was at sh350 from sh300.
There is plenty of Irish potatoes, cassava and sweet potatoes.

Original_Search_Title: FG Extends Deadline On Cassava Content
Headline: Nigeria: FG Extends Deadline On Cassava Content
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has extended the deadline for flour millers in the country to comply with the five per cent cassava content in flour production.
Commerce and industries minister Aliyu Modibbo granted the extension to April 1, at a meeting in Abuja, with the management of all the flour mills in the country.
The minister said there would be no further extension at the expiration of the deadline.
"We had given you a deadline of Jan. 1, 2007 but some of you have not even made efforts to comply. But the Federal Government in its magnanimity decided to extend the date," he said. Modibbo said after this extension, all the millers who did not comply would have their mills shut down.
He told them that the intention of government was to shut down 14 mills in the country for non-compliance by the end of January, but decided that the date be extended.
The minister tasked the millers to take "solemn undertaking" that they would comply by the new date.
He urged the Standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON), conveners of the meeting, to ensure that they monitor the operations of the millers in order to ensure strict compliance.
The meeting was attended by representatives of 14 of the 15 millers in the country.

Original_Search_Title: SON Explains Closure of Flour COYs
Headline: Nigeria: SON Explains Closure of Flour COYs
Body_Text:
The Standards Organisation Of Nigeria (SON) says its decision to shut down some flour milling companies is in line with the agreement reached at a stakeholders meeting held with President olusegun Obasanjo in 2005.
The organisation's head of public relations, Mr Adebola Fashina, who explained the closure in a statement in Lagos said that the companies were shut for contravening the policy of ensuring 5 per cent cassava content in flour products as agreed upon at the meeting. Fashina explained that July, 2006 was given as the deadline to comply with the policy, adding that there was no justification whatsoever for any company not to meet the deadline.
He said that the 12 companies were shut down after due inspection and analyses which indicated non-compliant with the policy.
"We are Invoking the organisation's powers under the enabling Act 56 of 1971, Standards of Organisation Nigeria," he said Fashina listed the companies to include Crown Flour Mills, Interstate Flour Mills, Standard flour Mills and Dangote Flour Mills all in Lagos.
The others are Ideal Flour Mills, Kaduna, Life Flour Mills, Sapele and Mix and Bake Flour Mills, Warri and Niger Flour Mills, Onitsha. The organisation said that the exercise was still ongoing and and insisted that shut down companies would only be re-opened after due process and a public undertaken to comply with the policy.
However, a source at SON says that the affected companies would soon be reopened.
The reopening of the companies may not be unconnected with an extension of the deadline granted by the minister to the companies to comply with 5 per cent cassava content in flour products.
The Minister of Commerce and Industry, Aliyu Modibbo, said on Tuesday in Abuja that the deadline for flour millers in the country to comply with the five per cent cassava content in flour production had been extended to April 1, 2007.
The minister said there would be no further extension after the expiration of the deadline. "We had given you a deadline of Jan. 1, 2007 but some of you have not even made efforts to comply. But the federal government in its magnanimity decided to extend the date," the minister told flour millers during the meeting in Abuja . (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Guzzlers
Headline: Uganda: Guzzlers
Body_Text:
There is something interesting about Kampala people. Rather than spend time out in cool and posh places, many prefer to spend their outings in bufundas.
As a result, this has put places like Joy's corner in Wandegeya in business where you would catch a big crowd anytime of the weekend. Another place that has sprouted with yet another set of hangouts is Kamwokya Town Centre. It's an enclosed space with many joints and among these is Guzzlers.
In every sense of the word, Guzzlers hosts people who love their drink and that is exactly what it boasts of.
Unlike the other nooks in the centre, which mainly prepare muchomo, Guzzlers has brought something new to attract customers. The place has started preparing Kigere (cow hooves) nebigenderako like boiled cassava and Irish potatoes.
You won't have to worry about parking space because the enclosure accommodates a good number of vehicles. From the Kamwokya Town Centre entrance, you would have to count five joints to your right to get to Guzzlers. Though quite small, Guzzlers also hosts football fans to its 30-seater interior with games on Dstv.
Just at the entrance, is a muchomo roasting point for different types of meat, and this supplies to all the hangouts in the centre. Sanitation standards are commendable save for the noise at night, which culminates from different kinds of music being played from the different joints.
Whereas there is security in the confines of the centre, you might need to take care of the security of your car if you park outside.

Original_Search_Title: Census And the Igbo - Arguing the Wrong Points
Headline: Nigeria: Census And the Igbo - Arguing the Wrong Points
Body_Text:
Lagos — I would not be making this intervention were it not for what I have observed in the debate so far since the census figures were released. The reason is that we knew ab initio that the census was destined to be another fraud foisted on the nation to sustain a lie. The enumeration itself was a fiasco and we said so here. I have always wondered why we have a slide movie attitude to a larger national problem. Oh, today it is a disastrous national identity card, and we raise hell that it failed. Next, it is voters' registration, and we cry to high heavens that it is a fiasco. Ah ha!
The census comes with its historic lies and fraud and we split hairs about it. What of the election? When last did we have an undisputed election? Even the near perfect one in 1993 was annulled and the nation was dragged through needless agony for that. Why did Nigerians expect that the census would reflect correct population figures? We were all witnesses to the disaster that the headcount was. In any case, Nigeria itself is one huge fraud run by racketeers. Why then would the census meant to sustain the fraud be different, even if conducted by an Obasanjo? Would you sow yam and reap cassava?
I have been a bit concerned by the drift of the protest from the Igbo of the South-east. If care is not taken, the Igbo will play into the hands of their enemies. Many people have argued against the figure awarded to the South-east. They are right to say that the figure was absurd, like the figures awarded to other geo-political zones. These figures have no basis in geography or rational observation.
The enumeration, in the first place, was a fiasco, not withstanding that the Chairman of the National Population Commission, Mr. Samu'ila Makama, admitted that they captured only about 90 percent of the population. Let us even take his word for it that only 10 percent of the population was not counted. In matters like this, 10 percent error margin is very significant. We may ask to which geo-political zone was the uncounted 10 percent awarded? There is no arguing that the census figures were inaccurate. That is an established fact.
But what I feel concerned about is that the Igbo are not looking at the story the figures are telling us. In the first place we must all remember that the census did not set out to establish the population of each ethnic group in Nigeria nor did it set out to establish how many Christians, Animists or Muslims there are in Nigeria.
It was a very stridently agued issue before the exercise that ethnic and religious parameters be included in the headcount. But the government refused. The Ohaneze Ndigbo even threatened that the Igbo would boycott the census, if these two information were not included in the census. But suddenly, Ohaneze relented and became more interested in Obasanjo's third term project.
The point therefore is that the census figures are not ethnic mapping figures. You can reject it an inaccurate figure but do not imply that even the inaccurate figure ever intended to refer to the population of the Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Christian or Muslim.
The figure of 16 million awarded to the South-east, therefore, is not saying that there are 16 million Igbo in Nigeria. The figure, to my understanding is simply saying that only 16 million Nigerians are living in the South-east.
That figure also includes Yoruba, Hausas and other Nigerians living in the South-east and who were counted there. My family and I were counted in Lagos. So were Nigerians of other ethnic origin who harkened to Governor Bola Tinubu's appeal and stayed back in Lagos to be counted.
Therefore the figure that Gov Tinubu is contesting vis-à-vis the Kano figure, includes all of us who were counted in Lagos. The Kano figure, right or wrong, includes other Nigerians who were counted there. Therefore, it is wrong for the Igbo to infer that the 16 million refers to Igbo population in Nigeria. To so imply is dangerous in that Nigeria can run away with the impression that the Igbo have accepted that they are only 16 million and begin to use it. Whenever the Nigerian nation is bold enough to find out how many Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Christian or Muslim are in Nigeria it will have a headcount that would reflect so.
The more important question to ask is: why is it that Nigerians, including the Igbo, have found the South-east unattractive to live in? The answer is simple, especially if you notice that nearby Rivers has witnessed population increase due to the oil industry while the South-east has manifested a denudation (at least that is what the census figure is telling us). The South-east has no economy to sustain people. It is still regarded by the Nigerian state as a war zone and those who remain there are no more than war prisoners.
The policy of the Federal Government since the end of the civil war has ensured that that geo-political zone remained economically depressed and its citizens flee into exile in their own country. It began with the territorial amputation of Igbo land and sequestering of the oil-bearing lands and sea access in the care of the "minorities".
Only God knows how safe the oil wells taken from Igbo are today, but what that act ensured was that the economic activity that would have sustained the Igbo was now in a territory where they are regarded as strangers and their real estate regarded as "abandoned property". People go where there is an economy. How can anybody live in the South-east where there is no electricity, no road, no water, no industries, no jobs and no federal presence?
What Igbo leaders, if any group in Igboland can truly be so described, must do is to actively challenge the federal and state governments to make the South-east part of Nigeria a zone of economic activity. They can do this if the Igbo can make their votes a bargaining power, not what is thrown away on sentiment or to please middlemen and jobbers.
They must re-examine if the strategy of a big national party can make Igbo votes count in a skewed federation like Nigeria or whether such votes can serve better purpose if warehoused in a small party. In other words, the Igbo must take their destiny in their own hands and desist forthwith from continuing to be purchasable merchandise. As for challenging the figures of true Igbo population, that is something that can be done in less than three weeks at minimum cost.
There is no part of the world where the Igbo reside that they are not organized into unions. Through these unions and the Eze Igbos, the census of the Igbo outside Igboland can be obtained. In Igboland itself the aladinmas and awuruawus or amaramas can in two days collate the figures kindred by kindred. This is very important so that if Nigeria ever gets to counting the ethnic groups, there will be empirical basis to challenge the figures if they are suspected to be fictitious. For now, they should not ascribe to the census what it was not intended to be.

Original_Search_Title: Crash Kills Four Traders
Headline: Uganda: Crash Kills Four Traders
Body_Text:
Kampala — AN over-loaded truck over-turned and killed five people near Kahangye bridge on Thursday evening.
Thirteen others were seriously injured at the boarder of Rukungiri and Ntungamo districts.
Rukungiri CID chief Henry Mbabazi told journalists that the Fuso lorry which was carrying 26 passengers and sacks of rice, beans, cassava, peas, ground nuts, lost control as it was negotiating a corner before the bridge.
He said the lorry was coming from Bikurungu Market in Bwambara and was heading to Kabale.
The dead were three men from Kabale as well as a woman, Dainah Mwesigwa and her three-year-old son from Kazindiro, Bugangari in Rukungiri. The bodies were taken to Nyakibale Hospital mortuary.
The injured were rushed to Nyakibale and Mbarara regional hospitals in critical conditions.
Five people got minor injuries and three, including the driver, Dege Mishaga, escaped unhurt.
They were identified as Patrick Tukamuhabwa, 26, Gerevasio Kiteta, 33, Owen Tumuhimbise, 21, Ian Asiimwe, 16, Delison Musinguzi, 28 , Drake Twinamatsiko, 38, musinguzi Tumwine, 22 and Muhamood Bamuranga, 26. This is the third accident on the same spot in less than three weeks, due to a sharp bend at the spot.

Original_Search_Title: Save Our Souls, Mr. President
Headline: Nigeria: Save Our Souls, Mr. President
Body_Text:
Lagos — I hate to disturb your peace, Mr. President, but what else can we do when Nigerians are not living in peace? That is why I have decided, in my own humble cubicle, to write you these few lines today, hoping that someone close to you will read and summarise to you before sunset. The situation in the country today is "dire". I know you hate that word, because in your own books, Nigerians have never had it so good. Things are getting better, as your supporters usually sing. We have debt relief, we have cassava explosion, we have 10,000 megawatts, we have gas and oil or oil and gas, we have minimum wage, we have foreign reserves, we have everything, according to your supporters. I have no intention of saying anything contrary. But I intend to draw your attention to the fact that the situation in Nigeria is dire. Heed this SOS and save our souls.
No, Your Excellency, I am not talking about the regular supply of darkness by NEPA/PHCN in the last two weeks. I feel very sorry for Nigerians who don't have generators and those who have generators but don't have fuel. As for me, I have been generating, consistently, 2.1 KVA per day in the last three years and I am able to watch my English Premiership matches, work on the computer and have some light around the house for a few hours in a day. I pity some 139 million Nigerians who don't have generators. And, no, Your Excellency, I am not talking about the fuel situation. I understand we now import every litre of fuel we consume in Nigeria because of circumstances beyond NNPC's control. Accept my sympathy, Mr. President, but that is not the SOS I am talking about.
Have you been listening to news lately? Just recently, a young woman was killed at Apongbon, Lagos. According to reports, Mrs Clementina Saduwa, the Product Manager of Ericsson Nigeria and a loveable mother of two, was on her way home from work at 8 pm when her car broke down on the Apongbon Bridge. The boys swooped on her. In panic, she wound up her side glass. Angered, the boys attacked her with a sharp object and she later died in the hospital. Her husband became a widower instantly, and he must be having a hell of time explaining to their kids that "mummy" would never hug, cuddle and kiss them again, because "mummy" would never come home again. That is just one of the thousands of cases we read about all the time. Hardly does any day pass without this "dire" situation confronting us.
What's your business with that, Your Excellency may ask. A lot, Mr. President. I don't know if you glance through the papers, but there is insecurity in the land. The spate of robberies should worry you. I can list a thousand and one reasons why the population of armed and violent robbers is on the increase, but that is not my aim today. What you are doing about the dire situation is what I and many other Nigerians are worried about. I am not a crime buster or a crime expert, but something tells me that there is something we are getting wrong. We can increase the strength of police force from 50,000 to one million and still achieve no results. We can import ammunition from Russia and Ukraine and still achieve little or no results. You know, as well as I do, Mr. President, that there is certainly something we are not getting right. Given the human and material resources we have lost as a nation to armed robbery in recent times, I would have thought that by now, some drastic and far-reaching measures should have been taken if indeed we value human life in our country.
Take Apongbon, for instance. That is at the heart of the financial capital of Nigeria, full of young professionals who are daily contributing their quota to the development of this country. They close from work at 5 pm and are sacred to death to leave Lagos Island because of the robbers-cum-murderers who lie in wait for them at strategic corners. I am not even talking about the bad patch of road at Apongbon, which I am sure would not cost us the entire foreign reserves to repair, especially as we are talking about the financial capital of Nigeria. Your Excellency, everybody knows that Apongbon is a dangerous spot. Everybody, including the Inspector General of Police. Yet, for whatever reason, there is no extra effort to provide security for the place. Instead, you have hundreds of policemen and women stationed at Allen Roundabout, Ikeja, and Maryland, also in Ikeja, waiting for any motorist to breach the traffic light so that they can pounce of him and strip him to the last kobo in his pocket. The dark spots all over Lagos, and all over Nigeria, that need security are abandoned to the robbers, while the lucrative places are over-policed. What this means, Mr. President, is that we have no respect for human life in this country.
Like I almost said, we can attribute the spate of violent crimes in recent times to harsh economic conditions which I know you would not agree with since you, as the Father of Modern Nigeria have solved all our problems. However, in as much as I agree that there are crimes all over the world even in the best of economies, what I cannot stand is the fact that we have folded our arms and accepted it as part of God's design for our lives. We have got to a stage where the Inspector-General of Police will tell us, as he did during Christmas, that these things are "normal" and should be expected. No, this is unacceptable. Remember, Your Excellency, when your daughter was attacked by armed robbers some years ago. Didn't the police get to the root of the matter? Didn't you order the Nigeria-Benin Republic border shut for some days? Didn't the police arrest the culprits and the ring leader? You know the right thing, Mr. President, when it affects you personally, and so I completely disagree with those who think you don't.
Violent robberies are not limited to Lagos. They are everywhere, including Abuja. I would have expected a state of emergency to be declared right now in the area of security of lives and property. I would have expected that proper measures would have been taken by now to forestall as well as combat robbery operations. To start with, what has happened to police intelligence? There were times in the past when the police took information gathering very serious. There were serious efforts geared at locating the abodes of robbers and questioning landlords who house people of dubious character without raising questions on their means of livelihood. Nigerians were given hotlines to call and information boxes were put in place for Nigerians to report persons they suspect to belong to the underworld.
Also, why is it that in the year 2007, our police force does not have a fingerprint system in place in order to be able to solve crimes? Isn't it ridiculous that police arrest suspects, charge them to court, get them jailed and yet there is no record of finger prints? People go to jail and come out without their fingerprints being taken. We held a national census in which Nigerians, including criminals, were supposed to have been counted. No attempt was made to seize the opportunity to gather fingerprints.
We are currently doing voter registration for people aged 18 and above. The process includes taking the fingerprint and photograph of every eligible Nigerian. I would expect this to be of interest to the security agencies, but I'm sure another contract would need to be awarded to gather fingerprints afresh instead of utilising what we already have. Even if the fingerprints are gathered, where are the laboratories to analyse them? I'm aware that the federal government has been talking about building a forensic lab. I won't be surprised if this is just a PR gimmick.
As I confessed earlier, Your Excellency, I am not a security expert, neither do I know much about crime control. I think, Sir, that it would be in the interest of Nigeria and Nigerians if we can gather the best of security experts, urgently, to tackle this problem. It certainly wouldn't take a genius to figure out that dark spots should be well policed.
As for what to do with sophisticated robbers with sophisticated weapons, I'm sure those who become millionaires and billionaires by taking up strategic positions in the police force should spare a few thoughts on how to tackle them efficiently-since that is what they were employed to do. I hate to see those pot-bellied police chiefs eating pepper soup and drinking beer while robbers take over the running of affairs on the roads and in our homes. I hate to think that Clementina Saduwa, and hundreds of other innocent Nigerians, would have been alive today if only their country had offered them simple protection. It hurts, Your Excellency. Save our souls, Mr President.
Impeachment as Tea Party: The merchants of impeachment are back to work. We have been told that the governor of Adamawa State, Mr. Boni Haruna, is on his way to the "gallows". Adamawa lawmakers, we are told, have decided to impeach him from Abuja in order to secure the release of their Speaker and four colleagues from EFCC cells. In Ibadan, combatants loyal to Ali Must Go's Garrison Commander, Lamidi Adedibu, are also sharpening their knives in readiness to behead Governor Rashidi Ladoja. Even the House of Representatives briefly toyed with the idea of impeaching President Olusegun Obasanjo-a move that was called off obviously because the movers might have realised that they were about to play into Obasanjo's hands as the general elections draw near.
Senator Ken Nnamani, Senate President, issued a note of warning at a dinner with media executives in Lagos recently. Impeachment, he said, is like the death sentence: you don't pass it except there is no other reasonable option available. At the slightest provocation in Nigeria, the impeachment axe is dangled by the lawmakers as if it is a toy. The immediate past Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Alfa Belgore, noted as much when he said recently that the lawmakers should stop abusing the impeachment clause in the constitution. He said politicians have not been following the laid-down rules and procedures in trying to carry out impeachments. "You can just wake up in the morning and say yesterday, we saw the governor laughing, we must impeach him," he said, while cautioning lawmakers that in the 250 years of American democracy, impeachment has taken place just once.
Belgore knows what he is saying. He was the CJN when the most bizarre cases of impeachment took place, notably that of Governor Peter Obi of Anambra. It was a clear abuse of the constitution, right from the way the impeachment notice was peddled around, the timing of the legislative hours and the venue of the sitting. The Appeal Court is now expected to rule that the process that led to the impeachment is arrant nonsense. "The judiciary as an independent body must live up to expectation to check the excesses of desperate politicians," Belgore said, challenging the judiciary to be truthful at all times.
This impeachment nonsense must stop. And the courts must protect our constitution no matter whose ox is gored.

Original_Search_Title: Choir Leader Defiles Nine Children
Headline: Rwanda: Choir Leader Defiles Nine Children
Body_Text:
Kigali — The choir leader of Bethsaida Kids' Choir Jean Baptist Tuyiramye Safari, 26, of ADPR Kabagali in Kibaza Cell of Kacyiru Sector in Gasabo District has been alleged to have defiled nine members of the choir he leads, impregnating four of them. The news of the alleged acts of defilement came in the limelight after one of the defiled girls, only identified as Anike, bled profusely after an botched abortion. She is admitted at the Kigali Central Hospital/Muhima Maternity, where she is in a coma.
Anike, a primary six leaver that was set to join secondary, was, on 11th January, sent to Gitarama to look for a school but, having realized that she was three months pregnant, instead went to Tuyiramye. The latter advised her to carry out an abortion. The rudimentary methods that were used at first failed, until a one Sarah tried to use a cassava stem to extract the foetus, leading to severe bleeding.
It is alleged that Tuyiramye used to tell the female choir members that he had to find out whether those that were serving God had their virginity intact, defiling them in the process.
"We used to practice from the church. But as time went on, he [choir leader] told us that we had to change the venue and, instead, practice from his home where he stays alone with his young sister," one of the defiled choir members who preferred anonymity told this reporter.
Church leaders found at the temple told The Sunday Times that they were shocked.
"We trusted him so much. Most of us have always looked at him as a model Christian. There is a time when I found him punishing one of his choir members, saying that he saw her standing with boys. So I thought that the guy is serious and I started respecting him, only to be iced by his defiling acts," said Berchimas Habiyambere, whose 13-year-old daughter is a victim.
Tuyiramye is now said to be in hiding either in Southern Province or Burundi. Police are investigating.

Original_Search_Title: Prisoners Form HIV-Positive Association Behind Bars
Headline: Burundi: Prisoners Form HIV-Positive Association Behind Bars
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — HIV-positive inmates in Mpimba Prison in Burundi's capital, Bujumbura, have found that the best way to survive the hardship of being incarcerated in the overcrowded facility is by banding together to support each other.
Originally named 'Mboshwe Kabiri', Kirundi for 'detained twice', the association for prisoners living with the virus later settled on a more uplifting name, 'Turemeshanye', meaning 'support one another'.
Georges Ndabihawe, chairman of the association, told PlusNews the group helped each other access food, drugs and psychological support. The organisation was especially useful because many HIV-positive prisoners' families gave up on them, particularly when they were not expected to survive a long sentence.
The jail was built to accommodate 800 prisoners but currently holds more than 3,000, and food is very hard to come by. The diet of 350g of beans and 350g of cassava flour per day was "not even enough for a child", said Martin Yabu, who is HIV positive.
"Doctors tell me to eat well, to cover myself up correctly; sometimes I feel like laughing at them," said Vincent Tugirimana, who is also HIV positive. He has been incarcerated since 2000 on a charge of murder but is still awaiting trial.
Tugirimana has always managed to get something to eat when taking his morning dose of antiretroviral (ARV) drugs, but for the evening one, he said, "it is only God's will".
According to Baselisse Ndayisaba, coordinator of the Burundi chapter of the Society for Women against AIDS in Africa (SWAA), the problem does not lie in the quantity, but the poor quality of the prison diet.
To help positive inmates supplement their diet, SWAA Burundi helped Turemeshanye start a small restaurant in 2006 as an income-generating activity. The inmates take turns to work in the restaurant and are thus able to supplement their diet.
The women's organisation also provides ARVs, and twice a week brings a doctor to Mpimba to treat opportunistic infections. The government's treatment programme bypasses prisoners, and the group is now lobbying for inclusion in the national rollout.
Despite the existence of the 60-member Turemeshanye, Ndabihawe noted that stigma remained high in the jail and many inmates were reluctant to join the group, as this would confirm their HIV status to the rest of the prisoners. "Those who accept to come in the open have no choice," he said. "Rather than dying unattended, they accept their status."
SWAA Burundi is tackling ignorance about HIV in the facility and regularly shows documentaries, which are as much to educate inmates about the pandemic as they are to prevent those who are negative from contracting the virus.
Most of Turemeshanye's members have been trained as peer counsellors, and chairman Ndabihawe said the group also aimed to make sure negative prisoners stayed negative.
"Some inmates may develop high-risk behaviour such as homosexuality," Ndabihawe said, adding that poor living conditions forced some female inmates to sell sex for money. Condoms are not distributed by prison officials, but SWAA offers them to prisoners who request them, who are mostly female inmates.
A New Year's Day proclamation by Burundi's President Pierre Nkurunziza that the elderly, children and people suffering from incurable diseases would be released early from prison has given the HIV-positive inmates some hope, but until then, Turemeshanye will keep lobbying the national AIDS control council for better food and access to drugs.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: FEC Rejects Relocation of Barracks
Headline: Nigeria: FEC Rejects Relocation of Barracks
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Executive Council (FEC) has rejected the relocation of all barracks away from civilian populated areas as a means of stopping the constant clash between the military, police and civilians.
