It however, after going through a white report on the clash between the police and soldiers between May 22 and 25, 2005 during which lives and property were destroyed, decided to discontinue the use of military stickers by civilians, encourage more interaction between all the groups and make available vehicles that would carry troops instead of using civilian vehicles.
The Council also approved the setting up of a Committee to advise the government on how to ensure discontinuance of such incidence.
The Council meeting which was presided over by President Olusegun Obasanjo also approved a N27.1 billion contract (N35 million per unit) under a Private-Public sector Partnership (PPP) for the construction of comprehensive healthcare centres in each of the 774 Local Governments in the country as a means of bringing government's involvement closer to the people.
The Minister of Information and Communications, Frank Nweke disclosed these while briefing State House Correspondents alongside the Minister of Commerce and Industry, Dr. Aliyu Modibbo.
He added that President Olusegun Obasanjo has directed the immediate enforcement of an order he gave earlier on the use of five per cent cassava content in all the nation's flour mills.
While emphasising on the need for all Nigerians, whether in the military or civilian to respect each other, Nweke added that the Council also resolved that civilian membership of military and paramilitary Mess be encouraged; a peace committee be put in place immediately by all the nation's local and state governments in order to nip in the bud, any outbreak or escalation of such incidents; joint training courses encouraged for the military and police at all ranks in order to help lower the feeling of superiority by member of the forces; and the government takes pro-active steps to enhance the roles of military and police agencies.

Original_Search_Title: Huíla to Rebuild Over Sixty Bridges This Year
Headline: Angola: HuÃ­la to Rebuild Over Sixty Bridges This Year
Body_Text:
Lubango — Sixty four bridges in the southern HuÃ­la province will, this year, undergo repairing works foreseeing the improvement of road circulation, announced Friday the local director of Public Works, RosÃ¡rio Ima Panzo.
The official said that these bridges were identified in the whole province as needing a serious and urgent intervention, with highlight to those over the Cassongue, Catapi, Cuvunge (Caluquembe), Cutenda and Cassava Rivers.
RosÃ¡rio Panzo also revealed that this year 1,000 kilometres of roads will be rehabilitated and asphalted in the region, with prominence to those that link the cities of Lubango and Huambo and Matala/Menongue.
At the moment work is underway on the Lubango/Quipungo/Matala, Quilengues/Benguela and Lubango/Cacula roads.
After consigning the rehabilitation works of the Lubango/Huambo road whose works might be concluded during this quarter, HuÃ­la province will have all its roads rehabilitated, since those which connect to Benguela and Cunene provinces have already been handed over to the respective contractors.

Original_Search_Title: Guests in Accra
Headline: Ghana: Guests in Accra
Body_Text:
Kampala — A traveller's ability to communicate with the locals at his destination is important, as I learnt recently on a short stay in Accra, Ghana. Boiling Ghana, I should say, but that will come later.
We arrived at Kotoka International Airport at about 3:00pm. The airport is named after a military general who shot dead Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana's first president and father of the nation. Kotoka himself was later assassinated in what you can call a palace coup.
We were driven to the out-of-town Earlbeam Hotel. We checked in and washed off the sweat gathered between disembarking from the bus and entering the hotel (gosh, it is hot and humid in Ghana!) and then, a Kenyan man I had met on the plane, and I decided to go see the town.
We did the best to make some sense of the Twi-influenced English of the hotel staff, asking them about the features to look out for in the neighbourhood.
"Look out for the Achimota Overhead and the Kwame Nkrumah Sseko," a manager told us. We wanted to ask what the overhead and the sseko were, but he had turned to other matters.
Outside the hotel, we asked another man what the overhead was. He said that's what it was, an overhead. He said if we boarded a taxi, we would not miss it. And the sseko?
He didn't have another word, but with an exaggerated swing of the hand, he made it clear what it was: a roundabout. They are not called roundabouts there; they're called circles.
To board the taxis, you need the Ghanaian cedi, and we had American dollars on us, so went to a nearby forex bureau.
When the lady at the till gave the man ahead of us his money, he fished out a kaveera (polythene bag) and packed the cedis into it. I thought: this guy must be going to buy a car or a house.
You think the Ugandan shilling is weak? For a dollar, Ghanaians pay 9,200 cedis! So when I asked the lady to change $20 for me, she gave me more money than could fit in all my pockets.
Ghanaians keep right on the road, and for someone who has grown up in East Africa where everybody keeps left, you could be knocked down as you cross the road. Instinct tells you to look left, right, and then left again.
Reality, however, has many huge taxis and other vehicles coming at you at top speed. But cross the dual carriageway we managed to, and boarded a taxi to the overhead. What is this overhead, we kept wondering.
We should not have bothered our heads, because when we saw it, we didn't need to ask: it was a flyover. Not the flyovers like the ones being built over the Northern Bypass, my dear.
These are giant works in concrete, multi-lane roads above roads, casting a deep shadow under them enough to require lighting, day and night.
Business never stops at the Achimota Overhead, and one of the items on sale there are used transporter saloon taxis, the type commonly called special hire in Kampala.
The majority of these taxis are Opels, sprayed in maroon, yellow and black.
The conventional commuter taxis, however, are big estate Benzes, whose staff are mostly young men the majority of whom are no older than.
The conductors travel with their trunks sticking out of the car, and immediately they see a catch, they shout a Twi phrase to the driver, who steps on the brakes, swerves off the road and pulls up the handbrake, all at a go. It is a hair-raising experience travelling in those taxis.
The average Ghanaian owns a better and bigger car than the rich Ugandan. Everywhere you go, you see giant Benzes, Fords, Cherokees, Chevrolets, BMWs, Volkswagens, Hummers and a sprinkling of Pajeros and Prados.
After taking a few pictures at the overhead, we boarded another taxi, to the Kwame Nkrumah Sseko. This is where the real life was.
Life at the circle is a beehive of activity.
Taxi touts literally try to drag potential travellers into the cars. Young girls balancing trays of fish deep-fried in okra oil, buns, banku (balls of mingled cassava flour) and oranges dash here and there.
Hawkers vending mobile phones, clothes, watches and other wares traverse the place. Idlers while the time away, doing what they do best: nothing. Travellers passing through the town cast a hasty glance here and there, before shooting across the street at the speed of light. You need to be alert, to make it anywhere.
I was famished by that time, and told Joe, my Kenyan friend, as much. He suggested we look for a not-so-posh place to taste what the ordinary Ghanaian eats as s/he struggles to make it through the day.
We found one such place and placed our orders: two balls of banku each, with fish in okra oil soup. When the food was brought, I realised I should have been less adventurous.
The sauce was pure pepper in pure oil, with a chunk of fish sitting in there.
But hunger has its own power, so we struggled with the piripiri and oil, and when we were done, each of us was dying for a drink. I asked for soda.
"Soda? We don't have it," the waiter said. But in his fridge were all brands of Coca-Cola and Pepsi products. I said: "But you do, it's there!" That's when Joe said: "In West Africa they don't know soda.
That's an American term! They either call each brand by its name, or you ask for a soft drink."
So I asked for a Fanta, to fight the burning sensation of the piripiri.
Eating done, we went out to take pictures.
And take pictures we did. We took pictures of the phone (mainly Nokia, Samsung and Motorola) kiosks by the roadside; of posters of the Black Stars, the national soccer team on sale by the roadside; of double-decker buses snaking their way through the hold-up; of long lines of the Benz taxis, parked and waiting for passengers; of women selling bangles, ropes, wire gauze and other such wares, as well as foodstuffs like deep-fried fish, yams and roast meat; of people walking, lounging and wooing each other on the overhead overhanging the circle... We took pictures.
Zooming in and out; peering and bending; standing on tiptoe and moving backwards. I was thus engaged when a hand tapped my shoulder. I turned, to see a young man of about 26 or so, in a blue uniform not unlike the kind worn by most private security organs here in Kampala, only his was rather threadbare.
"May I see your permit?" the guy asked. My heart sank like it was tied to a 50kg-weight. In an instant I was surrounded by four of these guys, and they were towing and shoving me, telling me I either show them the permit to take pictures of Ghanaians, or they take me to The Station.
Joe was also surrounded, and the two of us were led down the overhead, to a waiting Police car near the sseko. There, we halted, trying to plead our case. We said where we come from, people may take pictures of open places like roundabouts (what are roundabouts?
Circles... sorry, ssekos) and other such places. Only gazetted places are illegal, but these are clearly marked as such. Where do you come from? East Africa. Oh-oh! So you take our pictures, to go and show the world there that Ghanaian police is lousy?
That was their problem, that we had taken pictures of the Police without their permission. But we had not. I had taken pictures of the sseko, the fountain therein, the Ghana Commercial Bank towers behind it, and, there in the background of one of the pictures (as I later discovered), was the police car, looking like a dot.
So close to an hour later, after we had argued back and forth, us quoting the Ugandan and Kenyan law, and them insisting Ghana is a free country, where the legal system would release us if they found us innocent, the leader asked us to step aside.
By this time, the cameras had been grabbed and hidden in the little deep-blue Opel saloon.
"The problem, my brothers," he said calmly for the first time, "is that the Police here are serious. You can't take a picture of me without my permission. If you ask, I say yes.
If you don't, trouble." He then asked me to delete all the pictures, and Joe to tear the film and give him the offending pieces. Then we were released.
Day One over.
Another day Joe and I took a stroll to the beach. As we walked, we saw men and women stop, step aside and pee. Yes, pee on the streets, without a single symptom of shame or fear. And the women don't even bend or squat!
They hold the panty aside and let out the gush! Other pedestrians step aside and go on their way.
But that was nothing. At the beach, we found a line of men squatting in the sand, apparently watching the waves come and go. We were wrong.
As more men came and passed the squatting squad, they went a few metres to the left and, letting down their pants, they too squatted and - oh God, how do I say this without feeling what should have been their shame? - and they did potty!
There, on the beach. So when the waves come, they sweep away what should have been proof of really nasty manners.
And they know it's wrong, too. For while Joe was still taking pictures, a teenage urchin came upon us, asking who had allowed us to take pictures "of our dirty beach". He actually asked Joe to cut the film and hand it over to him, or else he would call the Police. We knew the story, so we did not wait.
But an urchin? Man!
Then there is the city, Accra. Accra is divided into two amazing parts. On the western side is the business area, very like our Nabugabo, Kisenyi or Market Street areas.
Tall buildings interspersed with two-to-three-storeyed structures, sandwiching narrow, filthy, crowded streets, where all manner of wares are sold.
Taxis, private cars, trucks and buses jostle for the space, creating jams the size of infinity. The noise is prohibitive, as are the prices of the simplest of the wares. Even with the inflation in the country, a simple shirt going for 150,000 cedis is the upper limit! We refused to ask about the decent ones.
Then on the eastern side of the city, you find the other twin, who looks like the rich neighbour's son. This is where you find financial institutions, the High Court, the Kwame Nkrumah Museum, foreign embassies and government ministries and departments.
Boy, is this side of town beautiful. Gorgeous buildings, neat grounds, no crowds, a serene environment. But the grandmother of them all in beauty is the Independence Square, still under construction.
It is a sprawling place, sitting on maybe four square acres, with stadium-like stands and pavilions scattered here and there.
The podium, on which the president will be standing to address Ghana, is something that confounds attempts to describe it. I wanted to reach for my camera to take pictures, but saw the seemingly inattentive sentries and remembered the Sseko incident, so I just let the fingers caress the camera.
Joe did not even reach for his bag where his giant Pentax camera was. He is much older and senior in these things, you know.
Ghanaians are very patriotic people. The fact that they just celebrated 50 years of independence makes it even more pronounced.
Ordinary Ghanaians have flags on or in their cars; they fly flags in their compounds; they speak of John Kufuor, the president, the way Balokole speak of Jesus, and they think the sun rises in Ghana and then shines on the rest of the world.
Talking about Balokole, every street corner has a church structure, and the structures are as flamboyantly built as the people behave.
The most dominant church in Accra seems to be the Winners' Chapel, which has branches almost everywhere. And the pastors - Ghanaian pastors are small gods and goddesses.
They wield plenty of power and respect, and people talk about them with awe.
Finally (what better place to talk about this than at the very end of the story) are the coffins.
Ghanaians have style, and this shows even in their coffin designs. When a man who loved his bottle passes on, they buy him a coffin in the shape of what kept him busy all his life - the bottle.
They also have coffins shaped like aircraft for frequent 'fliers', shoe shapes, heart-shaped coffins and the conventional shape for people who lived plain lives. Ghana, a place you can't bury without a sounding eulogy.

Original_Search_Title: A Trip Into Toro Culture
Headline: Uganda: A Trip Into Toro Culture
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT was my first time to travel aboard a Kalita Transporters bus from Fort Portal to Kampala. Considering that I am accustomed to the Mbarara-Kampala buses that stop only at designated places I found the many stopovers on this route unnecessary and time wasting.
It was already 6:00pm and the bus still needed more passengers! Why didn't these people converge at one point so that they could all board at once, I exclaimed loudly!
Mid-way through my whining, I turned to the passenger seated next me. His expression said it all. He was angrier than I was. Eyes red, he was ready to burst with anger. He opened his mouth to yawn and I was enveloped in a cloud of bad breath. Looks like he had been on the bus for a week.
Turning my head to the window for fresh air, I saw him. Clad in a checked shirt, black tailcoat, khaki trousers, banana fibre hat and wearing a mini basket around the neck, and holding another containing coffee beans, several pieces of mulondo (a cream-coloured root) and herbs, John Kamwanyi aka Magezi gaa Toro (Wisdom of Toro) go onto the bus.
At first I thought he had a mental problem. However, I soon realised how wrong I was. Like a roadside preacher, the strange-looking man suddenly began stressing to the passengers the importance of the culture of Toro and why the pasengers should all strive to be proud of their origin, Toro - he was assuming that everyone on board was a Mutoro. Before he could go any further, the bus stopped for, I don't know how long. Just as the passengers began complaining of how late it was getting, Kamwanyi, taking on the role of a tout said in Rutoro: "Mutembe, mutembe abagenda Kampala. Emotoka enu, nimugira bagiretiro oha?" meaning, "Could all Kampala-bound passengers get into this bus. You are the reason it's here." The conductor just looked on.
As the journey got underway, Kamwanyi pulled some mulondo roots from his basket and began talking about their medicinal value. "This root creates an appetite for food when chewed before a meal. It increases sexual prowess, just like viagra and helps to treat bad breath," he explained as he handed out free samples and offered to sell at a giveaway price of sh100 per root. At the next stop, Kamwanyi bought some pineapples and roasted cassava.
Generosity is one of the virtues of the culture of Toro, he said adding: "Since I couldn't afford to buy for all of you, I will share my food with the children and their mothers only."
His next topic was why people should desist from eating fried foods and go back to the Toro way of cooking where a little ghee is used to spice up food. "Fried foods are the source of many health problems arising to day. They are responsible for high blood pressure, cholesterol and obesity. Personally, I eat steamed foods and that is why I have not fallen sick in many years. But if you must continue eating fried foods, then you must take some of Toro's herbs, which I have properly dried and ground so that after a meal, you take them to clean your body system," he explained.
Continuing with food, he showed us Toro's traditional plate he called olwabya. This kind of plate does not get worn out as many others do. Actually it doesn't need washing with soap, as it doesn't retain any grease. However, to make everything easy for myself, I chose to have three of these plates fixed on a piece of wood. I did this to make sure that I have one for food, main sauce and additional sauce at all times and yet easy to carry with me everywhere I go. "I never use any other plate. It's always my lwabya or no food," he asserted.
As part of our culture, this wood, bowl like plate is our traditional plate that even at cultural functions is used to serve and eat food. It makes food tasty!

Original_Search_Title: Living With Rape, Harassment in the Northwest
Headline: Central African Republic: Living With Rape, Harassment in the Northwest
Body_Text:
Betoko — The insurgency against the government of Gen François Bozize has brought untold suffering to civilians in the northwestern Ouham-Pende province of Central African Republic (CAR).
"I was raped by a Chadian soldier in Betoko when our village was raided," 21-year-old Celine Nadima said. "Four other girls were raped on the same day. I became a mother a few months later."
Betoko lies 21km from the Chadian border and 45km north of Paoua town where Bozize's presidential guards killed 104 civilians on 29 January 2006 in a clash with "armed bands".
Initially, the CAR government dismissed the insurgency in the northwest as the work of bandits. The CAR army said it was a low-intensity war launched by civilians with no formal military training. But with no let-up in the attacks, Prime Minister Elie Doté acknowledged the existence of a rebellion in the region in March 2006.
The acknowledgement brought a response. Upon realising that the insurgency was expanding, the government deployed the elite presidential guards to the area, with backing from Chadian forces.
A high-ranking CAR army officer, who requested anonymity, defended the involvement of the Chadians, telling IRIN: "The CAR and Chad have signed an agreement authorising the armies of the two countries to hunt rebels across the border."
However, another CAR military officer, in an interview on 16 February 2007, in the town of Bossangoa, said: "The crossing of the border by Chadian soldiers is unfair."
Over the past year, the hitherto insignificant skirmishes have blossomed into a full-scale insurgency and spurred a humanitarian crisis in a region where Bozize, in October 2002, began the revolt that eventually toppled President Ange-Félix Patassé.
"On 10 July 2006, a group of Chadian soldiers came to our village. They fired randomly at the villagers, wounding some. Other villagers ran and hid in the bush. Many young people were captured and beaten by the raiders," Nadima explained.
"I am lucky I did not have any problems during childbirth as I was beaten and raped when I was pregnant," she added.
Little is known about the attackers, but residents said they were Bozize's opponents who wanted to undermine his authority because he overthrew a legitimate government in March 2003, mismanaged public funds and divided the nation.
Local civilians say the guards burnt down many villages on allegations that local residents hid rebels who are their relatives.
"I was tied up like a goat for two days without food and water. I was later freed as one of the Chadian soldiers who knew me pleaded for my release," said 32-year-old Florent Dolomgoto.
I am lucky I did not have any problems during childbirth as I was beaten and raped when pregnant
"They carried bags of millet, groundnuts and cassava and drove a herd of cattle away after the attack," he added.
Border crossings
Angry that Chadian soldiers cross the border into the CAR and harass them, local villagers say it is an unfair strategy. But the Chadians tell the villagers they do so to flush out rebels.
Aid organisations in the area have been advocating for the restoration of security in the region, saying security was needed to improve living conditions for civilians who seek safety in the bush.
A recent report by CAR's National Red Cross, CARE, World Vision and Human Rights Watch on the humanitarian situation in the region noted: "Civilians in the area are living in great fear as presidential guards often move into the zone to kill civilians and burn villages in reprisal for rebel activities there."
Some residents seem resigned to their fate. Others cannot bear the shame of atrocities and have opted to leave.
"Some of my fellow [rape victims] have left the village; they were ashamed of what happened to us," Nadima said.
Lack of assistance
The villagers claimed the CAR government had done nothing about the burning of houses and looting of their villages. They also said they had not received adequate assistance from either government or humanitarian organisations in the area.
The United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) estimates there are 150,000 internally displaced persons across the CAR.
In the northwest, one international agency is trying to provide medical help - Médecins Sans Frontières France. But local residents claim it mainly cares for emergency cases and children.
"The people in the area do not know much about the activities of this organisation," MSF doctor, Sylvie Baillat, said. "Caring for children is our priority but it is not true to say that MSF is caring for children only.
"We were focused on children before; this may be perceived as a strategic error but we will correct it. I am going to meet villagers next week to tell them that we're treating adults as well so as to change their perception of our activities," she added.
For Nadima, the assurance that the adults can receive any medical treatment has come too late. "I have not received any medical treatment since I was raped," she said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Two Masaka Wetlands Polluted
Headline: Uganda: Two Masaka Wetlands Polluted
Body_Text:
Kampala — A UN Habitat report has shown that Nakayiba and Nabajuzi wetlands in Masaka are being polluted by wastes from car garages.
Nabajuzi wetland serves as the main water supply for Masaka municipality and yet car wastes are dumped there without purification, the report noted.
George Kasumba, a consultant, told an urban development meeting in Masaka recently that people around Nakayiba wetlands grow yams, sugarcanes and sweet potatoes in the polluted swamp and sell them to town dwellers, posing a health threat.
He also pointed out that the garbage dumped at Bwala and Ssenyange hills is forced back to the town due to the strong winds and the flowing rain water.
The Masaka Municipality MP, John Kawanga, thanked the UN Habitat for providing over sh800m to improve Nyendo from a slum to a properly planned town.
He urged the municipality residents to embrace the changes initiated by the UN Habitat and local technical team.
"None of you would be happy if the neighbour constructs a toilet besides your sitting room. We need a properly planned town."
Jossy Materu, an urban settlement officer, said the municipality needed to fight environment abuse and poverty, if it was to be elevated to a city.
Masaka district chairman, Vincent Ssempijja, said homes and shops without toilets would be razed beginning next month.
Participants resolved that the municipality procures more land to broaden space for commercial and industrial programmes.
But the council speaker, Denis Majwala, said powers to allocate plots and land within the municipality belong to the district land board.

Original_Search_Title: A Propaganda Gift from the Gods
Headline: Ghana: A Propaganda Gift from the Gods
Body_Text:
Accra — In one of the most awe invoking 'accidents' of the electioneering campaign, the most troublesome legislator in the country, Hon. Albin Sumanu Babgbin, member of the vetting committee, and representative of the people of Nadoli West, stumbled upon Mr. Alan Kwadwo Kyerematen with bountiful wads of money at the Gariba Lodge, Tamale, right in the open.
Whether in jest, or to signal to Alan that he had spied him in the act of desecrating democracy - Alan is not in the business of buying yams to flog at the Kokomba market - (but for giving 'T&T' to selected delegates at the extraordinary Congress), the sight startled the impecunious legislator.
'Cash,' yelled the legislator to the former Minister of Trade, who wants to ascend the high office of President without going through the mill. 'Cash!' Alan yelled back, without batting an eyelid, suggesting that he felt he was rather being hailed or serenaded.
Then he continued with what he was doing - without a twinge of conscience - his business of consulting with delegates without addressing them.
The public relations nightmare that an Alan victory will create is unimaginable, with Bagbin being handed this propaganda gift - Alan Cash, buying votes democracy.
"I am winning whether you like it or not," he confidently asserted, as he dished out money to chairmen and delegates from the Northern Region, who had made it through the gruelling process of emerging as delegates, at constituency congresses throughout the nation.
More than 2,500 delegates will converge for the extraordinary congress of the New Patriotic Party (NPP) this Saturday.
It underlines Kyerematen's reputation as a cash-stained gentleman, and his increasing reputation as 'Alan Cash,' who can purchase delegates with no time spent on speeches.
The Chronicle confirmed the sighting, in an interview with Hon. Innusah Fuseini, Member of Parliament (MP) for Tamale Central, who was in Tamale with Bagbin.
He said he was indoors himself, but it was Bagbin who entered the hall, bumped into the great cash feast, and returned to tell the tale.
It was echoed in an innocuous sounding remark by Hon. Fuseini, the combative MP for Tamale Central on Metro Television Monday morning, when he let the news of the sighting of Alan Kyerematen and the cash tale out.
AKUFO ADDO IN FIGHT BACK, ALAN IN FREEFALL
With Nana Addo's researchers on the field, featuring Mr. Steve Mallory, publisher of the African Observer, the handwriting signified an Alan freefall. Nana Akufo Addo, the popular presidential candidate, anxious to put himself out as the next in line for the presidency, stepped up his campaign in the Western Region, with a dash to the region last Tuesday, specifically, the mining town of Tarkwa.
Accompanied by Mr. Abu Jinapor, the former youthful NDC man now turned NPP commentator, Nana Addo told delegates that Kofi Coomson had written that he was now falling behind in the region, and was imploring the delegates to prove him (Coomson) wrong, by voting for him.
He gave less than what the delegates expected - ¢1 million - as they had heard that Nana was paying executives ¢3 million and ¢10 milliion per constituency chairman in the Ashanti Region.
Whether Nana Addo's cash-for-cash duel with Alan had left the Abuakwa legislator out of pocket was difficult to tell, but the sums he gave to the 22 delegates - now confirmed delegates in the Western Region, was disappointing to them.
EJISU ROYAL
Alan, the Ejisu royal who has earned the nickname 'Alan Cash,' unbelievably run short of money early Sunday morning when he returned to Takoradi and began consultations with delegates in the regional capital.
This was in full glare of some of his lieutenants, Hon. Joe Ghartey, the Attorney General and Member of Parliament for Essikado-Ketan constituency, who had succeeded in getting about four of his constituency executives into the camp of Alan.
The Minister for Trade and Industry and Member of Parliament for Effia-Kwesimintsim, Hon. Joe Baidoo Ansah, is also in the Alan camp with his classmate at Ahantaman Secondary School, Mr. Phillip Kwesi Nkrumah, the Metropolitan Chief Executive for the Shama Ahanta East Metropolitan Assembly (SAEMA), who is also a delegate for Alan. Then there was the DCE for Nzema East, Kwejah Ellimah, all present when Alan run short of money.
Alan, after paying out to 140 delegates, who make up 14 constituencies out of the twenty-two constituencies in the Western Region, an amount of ¢700 million, run short of money and openly told the rest of the delegates, who make up the other eight constituencies that he had run short of money.
He further told them that he would dispatch his brother, Steve Kyerematen, the top insurance executive who is part of his team, to send their part of the booty (money) through money transfer.
YAA ASANTEWA
Though Alan, at the meeting with the delegates, identified two of the delegates, Jerry Hanson, an Organizer and Naana Amoah, Women's Organizer, both from the Effia-Kwesimintsim constituency, as not being in his camp, he was not perturbed and still went ahead and paid them, avoiding the discriminatory practice of Nana Addo in Kumasi recently when he paid ¢10 million per chairman, when he separated the pro-Alan chairmen from his give-athon.
Nana Amoah, pointedly, told Alan in the face that she was not going to vote for him, to the hearing of the assembled at Raybow Hotel, who were surprised that she would be so bold.
In the case of the organizer, Alan humiliated him in the presence of the delegates to the effect that the former was behaving as if he was in his (Alan) camp, yet he was not.
Alan boasted before her, that he was going to win and would still work with her after his victory. He subsequently named her as the 'Yaa Asantewa' of her constituency, a sobriquet that was accorded her by Hon. Joe Ghartey.
Though Jerry Hanson confirmed this, in a telephone interview with this reporter, the Women's Organizer did not want to talk much on what transpired between her and Alan.
The eight other constituencies, on the other hand, which are to receive their share of Alan's 'booty' would also receive a total amount of ¢400 million. That would bring the total amount of money Alan had blown here on Sunday morning to ¢1.1 billion on the twenty-two constituencies.
Alan is going round each constituency in the country splashing ¢50 million on the ten elected delegates in each constituency. This has been confirmed by the Western Regional Coordinator of Alan, Mr. James Obeng.
DISCRIMINATORY
He told this reporter that Alan was not discriminatory in the sharing of what he called 'T & T' to each constituency. The eight constituencies, which did not receive their share of Alan's booty and expecting to receive it, were the Ahanta West, Effia-Kwesimintsim, Elembelle, Evalue Gwira, Sekondi, Essikado-Ketan and Jomoro constituencies.
Mr. James Obeng, Regional Coordinator of Alan Kyerematen, and Joe Ghartey's constituency secretary, declined to comment on the money his boss paid out to the delegates, after this reporter had contacted him, but by the next day he was espied at Barclays Bank, Takoradi, taking delivery of the cash for the unpaid delegates.
Asked how his boss run short of money, Obeng explained that Alan thought of the importance of meeting the delegates, after he was coming back from his tour of other parts of the country.
MESSAGE
It is not about money, but the message Alan was coming to give to the delegates that is why he came and not to just give out money. The message in brief was the threat that he would win whether they liked it or not.
Some constituency executives, this paper spoke to confirmed that indeed Alan doled out fifty million to each constituency as their transport fare. Meanwhile, four other presidential aspirants, who also made stops in the region, to interact with the delegates also doled out a whooping sum of ¢1,170 billion on the 220 delegates, who would vote at the party's congress to elect the next flagbearer of the party.
Nana Addo Dankwa Akuffo Addo, Hon. Yaw Osafo Maafo and Dr. Kwame Addo Kufour doled out one million cedis to each of the 220 delegates bringing the total amount paid by the three to ¢660 million.
The Vice President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama and Emmanuel Boakye Kyerematen Agyarko, who are also in the race also doled out an amount of ¢88 million to the 220 delegates from the west.
The Vice President paid each delegate ¢500,000 whilst Boakye Agyarko paid ¢400,000 per delegate.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Items Soar in Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Items Soar in Lagos
Body_Text:
Lagos — BARELY three weeks to this year's Sallah and Christmas celebrations by Muslims and Christians, prices of foodstuffs have suddenly gone up within the Lagos metropolis by over 20 per cent.
The affected items include rice, beans, yam, fish, meat, fruits and vegetables among several others.
Daily Champion investigations revealed that a bag of rice which earlier cost about N5,500, now sells for more than N7,000.
Also, prices of a carton of chicken and turkey have increased by more than 25 per cent, forcing consumers to go for fish.
A carton of turkey early last month sold for N4,200, while chicken sold for N4,000 but presently, both products sell for N5,000 and N4,500 respectively.
A cold-room operator at the popular Daleko Market, Iyana Isolo, Lagos, Ms Nonye Okafor, who spoke with Daily Champion attributed the sudden price increase to rumoured appreciation in the value of the CFA, currency of French speaking West African countries as well as sudden hike in fares by transporters.
According to her, "a carton of Turkey in Cotonou is N4,200 while that of chicken is N3,500. The transport fare added to the cost paid for the good's crossing the border and then transporting it to the warehouse add up to the price we have now.
"Also there are many risks involved. Even there are indication that it might even increase as Christmas draws nearer," Ms Okafor said.
A survey at the Ijora Market, Apapa revealed the same situation as a carton of fish (Titus brand) which used to sell for N4,300 now sells for N4,700.
At the Mile 12 Market, Ketu, a tuber of yam earlier sold for N200, now sells for between N200 and N250.
Traders at the various markets who spoke with our reporters, however lamented low patronage, saying that price hike is affecting their daily sales.
According to one of them who simply identified herself as Iya Sadiq, the Stallion brand of rice now costs between N6,800 and N7,000 while Golden Caprice rose up to N9,500. In her words, a bag of rice before costs N5,500 but now an average bag cost N6,500 excluding some foreign and local brands which are more expensive.
"During the Obasanjo regime, he banned importation of some of these foodstuffs so that the local brands will sell. But you find out that some of the local products are even more expensive than some foreign brands. Is it good? Make government do something. A bag of beans is now N10,000 though before we can sell it for N9,000 or more but not N10,000," she said.
Another trader who pleaded anonymity asserted that the price of the Mama Africa brand of rice is N7,000 while the price of Host, another brand is N6,000. Speaking further, the trader stressed that Royal Umbrella and Mana-Gold brands of rice have also shot up.
Reacting to the hike in price of bags of beans, the trader hinted that, it is only when a new variety of beans arrives that the price increases even as she emphasised that this period is not the season for a new variety of beans.
She further attributed the hike in price to the very many risks encountered while smuggling some of goods.
A civil servant and mother of two, Mrs. Josephine Eke, lamented the hopelessness of the situation even as she enjoined the federal government to lift the ban on some of the products so that their prices could fall.
Furthermore, she asserted that the lifting of the ban will encourage healthy competition while prices of foods would be determined by market forces.

Original_Search_Title: Akiolu Tasks Tiv Leaders On Citizens Rights
Headline: Nigeria: Akiolu Tasks Tiv Leaders On Citizens Rights
Body_Text:
Lagos — Oba of Lagos, Oba Rilwanu Babatunde Akiolu (1) has advised Tiv political leaders in Benue State to be honest and sincere in their dealings with the citizens.
He noted that the Tiv people and indeed, the people of Benue State are hardworking and honest which certain few individuals should not rubbish the attributes.
The monarch, who spoke in his palace, when the President of Mdzough U- Tiv, Lagos State chapter, Mr. Simon
Avar Udu Gbaji led other officials of the organisation on a courtesy visit, hailed the President Umaru Yar'Adua for the giant strides he has made in his short time in office, saying the president was God-fearing.
Akiolu said there were some Nigerians out to derail the present adminitration through bad advice but that God would not allow them to succeed.
According to him, all corrupt leaders in the country would have their judgement on earth before they die, and charged Nigerians to be steadfast and support government programmes.
He regretted that the leadership of the Tiv in Lagos State was not alaways present at functions of the palace and called for a change.
The Oba spoke against the back drop of a request by the visitors that they be given a seat on the council of Obas.
Earlier, Gbaji had said the visit was the first time the Tiv in Lagos would pay homage and solidarity visit to the royal palace.
He prayed God to continue to shower His grace and mercy upon the Oba and to endow him with the wisdom of King Solomon.
The president said MUT propagates the true values, culture, tradition and language of the Tiv people and subscribes to the unity and indivisibility of Nigeria.
Gbaji recalled the cordial relationship that has existed between the Tiv and the people of Lagos in particular and the South West in general.
"We take pride, however, in the fact that Tiv people have played significant roles in the socio- political and economic life of Lagos State. For instance, one of the architects of what has today become a mega city was a Tiv man in the person of Hon. Tashaku Orodi, One - time minister of Lagos Affairs in the first republic", he stressed.
The president said the Tiv . People have played and continued to play very significant roles in the Economic development of Lagos as much of the foodstuffs, particulary yams sold in Lagos markets come from Benue.

Original_Search_Title: Letter From Buea
Headline: Cameroon: Letter From Buea
Body_Text:
Dear Ngwa,
My brother, scandals in this country have kept me mute. I cannot find words enough to describe the sudden events that have assailed this country recently.
These days I sleep with one eye closed and the other open since Her Majesty's visit was announced. Even when I go to the rest room, I am careful because a security officer could be there. Her Majesty's coming is something else.
Those palace guys make us believe that we are some Talibans seeking to take away life from Her Majesty. If she made a surprise visit to see the school she had ordered to be built, the miscreants called contractors would have been in jail by now. That would have been the scandal of the year.
I hear the man who got the contract lives in the palace and dines with the presidential couple. Someone said the structure that looked like a building that survived a flood disaster costs FCFA 250 million. It is scandalous.
I saw the big man in Buea sweating endlessly to make sure the embarrassment could at least be averted. What you will find on that day, if you happen to travel with her, is a new structure altogether. When I visited the place, I wondered whether a foreigner had done the job in a hurry and left.
My brother, how could you imagine that our men were killed by friendly fire? What would one not hear about the peninsula saga? Tongues have been wagging that there was illegal trade between the khaki boys and one of the group tried to play smart. I even hear some high placed khaki boys were implicated in the deal and are now cooling their feet somewhere.
I say, someone from your area whispered to me that the coup plotters, who attempted a little something recently, were the same ones involved in the treacherous deed. Meanwhile, enemies of the secessionist movement have pointed accusing fingers at them for masterminding the incident.
To compound matters even more, our Awala brothers want to make nonsense of the Green Tree arrangement. Some cynics have concluded that the Green Tree might soon become Brown Tree and the Lion Man's efforts would have been wasted.
I was told that the former Asso Rock tenant acted against the wish of the lawmakers in order to please the white overseers. The matter, I hear, has divided the C party, as there are accusations of black legs in the house.
Ngwa, why do your men behave as if they need our consent to amend the constitution? They have the yam and the knife; let them cut it the way it best suits them. The sheep-like attitude of the C militants does not surprise me. On the contrary, I would have been surprised if they stood up against it.
The way they go about it gives the impression that it is a foregone conclusion; we are just wasting time and energy trying to resist it.Do you know that some parties that have paraded themselves all this while as opposition, recently joined their voices for the change? I am not surprised; everything is possible in Cameroon.
Instead of amending the constitution so that their man stays on, I suppose they should go to the Almighty to amend the constitution of life that limits man's stay on earth. In that way, they can be sure that their man would become immortal. Since your people claim that the Lion Man has now become our oxygen and our Saviour, let him stay on.
I only hope the morsel of food they have put in their mouths will go down their throats. When calamity comes, it shall not only visit us; it shall visit them too.Bad tidings are beginning to visit our S party. Our Ntarinkon Lion is yet to recover from the attacks he received after he blundered in his declaration in the capital city. I even fear he may soon be running the party alone since everyone seems disgruntled and wants to leave.
Recently, some S militants who failed in the recent elections led their disciples to where the bread is better buttered. They blamed our man for high-handedness and favouritism. When actors in a theatre show fail to leave the stage when their performance is over, the audience may leave them.
Ah, I almost forgot to tell you that our Royal Highnesses are now looking at each other with mutual suspicion. The virus of constitutional amendment has so affected them that I hear their meeting on Friday will bring down lava from the mountain. For now, I cannot tell you where it will take place because the house is really divided.
The Highnesses from the Pamol ended are fuming that they are being deprived of what is rightly theirs. They say they cannot allow the reigning Highness to effect any amendment till they have also enjoyed their turn. But someone whispered to me that some Pamol
Highnesses' lips have been oiled and they would simply join the reigning Highness to clap the amendment. So the December 1 meeting would just be a formality and the nonsense would be voted.
Stay well.Mbella.

Original_Search_Title: Residents Drag Dog to Police Station
Headline: Nigeria: Residents Drag Dog to Police Station
Body_Text:
Kano — Disturbed by incessant attacks on the people of the area and their animals, residents of Dakata, a suburb of Kano dragged "Bush" to the police station for what they described as "terror attack" on them. Weekly Trust reports.
But for the timely intervention of the police who issued the arrest warrant on 'Bush', angry pestle-wielding housewives could have lynched the dog that has attempted to kill the sheep, goats and chicken of Dakata area in Kano. Until they were cautioned that Bush might cost a lot of money, their initial decision was to crush his skull instantly without informing the police.
Bush's crime is "terror attack" against his fellow animals daring to cross his path. Since it was brought to the area, the residents of the area complain that their animals are not freely roaming the area to graze and scavenge. "My goat escaped by whiskers from Bush's mouth, and even when it was rescued, I had to slaughter it because it was in a terrible state," a resident groaned. They also complained that the dog does not allow their young kids to move about freely without fear.
A formal complaint was made to the police who immediately issued the warrant of its arrest. But the baffling part of the saga is how and who was served the warrant. Of course, the master of the dog, Alhaji Isyaku Adda'u was summoned along with his pet to the police for interrogation.
The police first asked whether Alhaji Isyaku has the license to domesticate this vicious animal, or whether he is keeping it illegally. Alhaji Isyaku hastily brought the Pet Green Book (PGB) of Bush, which indicated its name, date of birth, sex, heath status, etc.
The owner told Weekly Trust that Bush's doctors, Dr M.D. Yahaya and one Dr. Innocent are the best around. "Sometimes Bush will spend nearly a week on admission, eating fish, milk and liver," he said Alhaji Addau told him the dog is a crossbreed of Rottweiler and German shepherd and knowing fully well that it can be vicious to strangers, he always chains and restrains it before taking him out with a muzzle. He regretted what happened.
Bush was briefly detained and later released on bail, but on the condition that it will not be allowed outside without a muzzle and that the master should ensure that it is domesticated securely to prevent future recurrence.
A resident of the area, Malam Bunu Ibrahim said the dog is disturbing their animals, and their children cannot freely play in the neighbourhood. He added that such kinds of animals should not be brought to slums but highbrow areas where children and pets are rarely seen outside. Bunu also said it is a powerful dog that could assault an adult let alone a child. "We have every right to complain because when it barks even adults are frightened," he argued.
Weekly Trust observed that the dog is fierce, and the children of the area never play near the compound. The residents lamented that such kind of dog is not supposed to be in their area because "the dog is not the type of dog which is friendly and benign, and our children play around and our animals also roam around."
But Bush's master said he has never sent Bush to attack anybody and never allowed him to roam around freely, "so there is no cause for alarm."
Adda'u said he is keeping the dog, not only as a pet but as a source of income. He said during the heat period of dogs, bitches of different breeds are brought into his house and the owners pay N10, 000 to N15, 000 for three days mating. He explained that Bush is a rare breed that is why one Lebanese priced it N250, 000, "but I declined because Bush is my companion, and cannot be sold" he said.
He told Weekly Trust that the dog regularly undergoes medical checkups and immunization against rabies. "Bush regularly observes distemper vaccine, which prevents it from regular fever and coughing. He also eats food like pounded yam and other assorted meals from expensive restaurants," he said. He also boasted that he spends more than N40, 000 on his pet monthly.
The owner of the dog said he is domesticating the dog because it is said to be highly intelligent and agile with a strong work drive. "This type of dog is often deployed in various crime detecting roles, hence the name Police Dog," the master, Alhaji Isyaku said.

Original_Search_Title: Hausa Community in Jos Protests LG Hqtrs's Relocation
Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Community in Jos Protests LG Hqtrs's Relocation
Body_Text:
Jos — School and commercial activities were disrupted in Jos, Plateau State yesterday as the Hausa community in Jos North local government observed a one day sit-at-home action to protest the state government's relocation of the Jos north local government secretariat.
The state government had about two months ago directed that the secretariat at the heart of Hausa area be relocated to the Jos metroplitan Develop-ment Board (JMDB) office along Ibarhim Dasuki street.
Our correspondent observed yesterday that the protest took place at places like Yan kaji, Yan doya, Yan alubo, Angwan Rogo, Masallacin Juma'a Street, Bauchi road and others where people chose to stay within their homes instead of going out for business.
The protest, our correspon-dent observed, was peaceful as the people preferred to stay indoors in full compliance with the sit at home order which paralyzed Hausa dominated business activities like sale of second hand cars, commercial motor cycles, retail in food items like chicken, yams and other ornaments.
Sources however said there were attempts by some youths along Bauchi Road to block the roads leading to other parts of the town early in the morning, but that such actions did not last as the youths were advised to remove the barricades.
The secretary general of the peace initiative committee of the Hausa community in Jos north, Alhaji Abdulrahman Bikos, confirmed to newsmen that the protest is to challenge the order relocating the secre-tariat of the Jos North local go-vernment.
He said the sit at home protest is one of the activities lined up by the community to alert the state government that they are not happy with its attempt to relocate the secretariat.
The state's commissioner for information, Nuhu Gagara, expressed surprise at the report of the protest, saying there was no correspondence received from any community to indicate there was going to be such.
The Police Public Relations Officer of the Plateau State Police Command, Kassim Bala, con-firmed that the protest had been peaceful as there were no incidences of break down of law and order.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Want Pay Boost - Why ?
Headline: Cameroon: Â«BellyÂ» Politics
Body_Text:
Ensuring that our members of parliament live a decent life is an honourable option. They are the people's representatives and deserve a minimum of comfort! That is why in Cameroon today, MPs are among the best paid.
How many people can boast of a monthly salary of FCFA 899,000? Few! In a country where some civil servants earn FCFA 40,000, with average public service salaries revolving around FCFA 100,000, what the MPs bag home is considered as a fortune by many. Yes, a fortune in our context. Even if the pay check cannot match the millions which parliamentarians in some neighbouring countries receive.
Charity, they say, begins at home. But to imagine that the first collective action of our newly elected gentlemen and ladies is to ensure a salary increase for themselves is simply incredible! Fuelled by an internal wrangling between bureau members who have lofty salaries and allowances and ordinary MPs with "little" salaries, parliamentarians have seen themselves torn between their personal wants and serving the people who voted for them. A tract containing proposals to beef up the allowances of MPs have been circulating in the corridors of the National Assembly since the start of the present session. Great is the astonishment of Cameroonians who are learning that their MPs are lobbying for a swell of about 50 per cent of their pay package (from FCFA 899,915 to FCFA 1,312,000), and the allocation of a non-refundable allowance of FCFA 10 million for equipment to each MP. They are equally asking for an increase in micro projects grants from FCFA 8 million to 12 million. And talking about micro-projects grant, what guarantee does the population have as to the proper management of the money when most MPs have, in the past, been accused of embezzling the funds?
What people expect from their elected representatives are proposals and decisions that reflect the aspiration of the majority. If the time is ripe to talk about the salary situation in Cameroon, then it should be systematic with the interest of everybody taken into consideration. The pay problem is general. The salary scale in Cameroon today is not the best. Two salary cuts of State employees, coupled with a steady increase in the standard of living - increase in the cost of basic commodities, taxes, building materials, fuel, etc, have made it difficult for many Cameroonians (both those in the public and private sectors) to live comfortably. The majority of people live from hand to mouth. A category "A2" civil servant today can hardly build a two bedroom flat or buy a used car from his savings; if at all he can save! Gone are the days when the State used to offer car loans to its employees. Reinstating the salaries to what they were yesteryear is therefore a major preoccupation to Cameroonians.
But some people have been quick to argue that increasing the pay package of our law makers will make them less susceptible to corruption as they can better resist pressure from lobby groups. This equally holds for other Cameroonian workers! However, the hypothesis remains arguable for both cases! Experience has proven that one doesn't need to be poor to embezzle. Proof of this is the fact that most of those who are now languishing in jail for squandering the tax payer's money were not that poor! They had good jobs and comfortable salaries.
In effect, there is nothing wrong with MPs asking for wage increment. But thinking only about themselves has been considered by many as a great betrayal of the people's trust from whom they have just got their mandate. Anyway, they have the yam and the knife; especially during the present legislature when the National Assembly might be expected to take certain daring decisions. Our MPs can decide to be the true representatives of their people and by implication their aspirations, or limit their stay in parliament to swelling their pockets. History alone will judge them!

Original_Search_Title: Studio 53 Visits Country, Three Other Countries
Headline: Nigeria: Studio 53 Visits Country, Three Other Countries
Body_Text:
Abuja — DStv audiences in Nigeria, Ethiopia , Benin and South Africa will this week have an exciting time as M-Net's STUDIO 53 makes stopovers at these destinations during the latest episode of the lifestyle program!
STUDIO 53's first stop will be Nigeria where the Onitsha people marked the new yam festival (Ofala) in October with great revelry. IK joined the celebrations where the colourful ceremony saw lots of dignitaries in attendance and like every year locals gathered to pay homage to their King.
STUDIO 53 will then head to Ethiopia where Fareed savours the enchanting décor at the St George Art Gallery in Addis Abba. While there, he will meet with celebrated hand carved furniture designer, Saba Alene who in recognition of her innovative design and promotion of Ethiopian culture received the prestigious World Intellectual Property Organisation's award for inventive design in 2005.
Then, it will be the turn of Benin where STUDIO 53 highlights some of the attractions this beautiful country has to offer.
Situated on the Gulf of Guinea, between Togo on the west and Nigeria on the east, this West African country has a rich cultural heritage and varied scenery making it an attractive but relatively unknown tourist destination before leading to South Africa where Rosi attends the Albert Luthuli Musical in Durban.
The life and achievements of Chief Albert Luthuli, a forefather of South Africa's liberation struggle was celebrated with a vibrant musical production even as the DSTv audience have been urged to join STUDIO 53 as they bring this inspired performance to them.

Original_Search_Title: Struggle - No Retreat, No Surrender - Uwazurike
Headline: Nigeria: Struggle - No Retreat, No Surrender - Uwazurike
Body_Text:
Lagos — Leader of the Movement for the Actualization of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB), Chief Ralph Uwazuruike, has declared that he will not be intimidated to abandon the struggle.
The MASSOB leader made the declaration in Okota, Lagos State yesterday during the celebration of the new yam festival organized by Eze Ndigbo, Lagos State, Eze Christian Nwachukwu.
He recalled the genesis of the struggle to emancipate the Igbo, saying MASSOB was formed to correct the injustice against Igbos in Nigeria and vowed that the struggle will continue until justice and equity were addressed in Nigeria.
He said it was because of marginalization that an Igbo man cannot contest the governorship in states like Lagos, Kano and other states where they have a great number of residents.
He thanked those who made it possible for his release and maintained that he would not retreat or surrender in the struggle to ensure justice and equity in the country.
Uwazuruike also recalled how Nwachukwu emerged as the Eze Ndigbo, Lagos State. He explained that the position for the head of Igbo became relevant in 1990 due to the emergence of numerous groups and individuals claiming to represent the interest of the Igbos.
According to the MAS-SOB leader, there was no title known as Eze Ndigbo at that time, adding that Nwachukwu was the chairman of council of Igbo chiefs in Lagos State at that time, and that it was the unanimous decision of chiefs that an election was conducted to elect Eze Ndigbo for in Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Tribunal - Fear Grips Bayelsa
Headline: Nigeria: Tribunal - Fear Grips Bayelsa
Body_Text:
Tension continue to grip aides of governor Timpire Sylvia of Bayelsa State ahead of today's judgement on the petition by AC candidate, Prince Ebitimi Angbare at the Election Petition Tribunal.
The tribunal chairman, Justice Haliru Mohammed had last week, listened to statements from PDP, AC and INEC counsels. A total of 29 witnesses appeared for all parties, with INEC having 12 while AC presented 8 witnesses.
A check among PDP supporters and aides revealed that tension is very high. While many of the aides believe it could go either way, some youths in the state have been mobilising to storm the tribunal venue in support of AC. Attempt by LEADERSHIP to speak with the SSG and the state police boss on the issue failed as they declined comment.
A close source told LEADERSHIP that the aides have decided to hold vigil at the Government House. At the Angbare camp, some members of the AC national leadership are believed to be mobilising the supporters of the party. It would be recalled that the AC candidate had approached the election tribunal in May, asking for the election that purportedly brought in Governor Timpre Sylva to be annulled and another election ordered.
Meanwhile, five-year-old Divine Emeruwa, daughter of a production superintendent of Shell Petroleum Development Company in Port Harcourt kidnapped three weeks ago has been released by her abductors.
The joint task force and SSS operatives secured the release of the young girl last Saturday. She was handed over to the security operatives by emissaries of the gunmen who in turn handed her over to her parents.
The gunmen had first demanded N30million ransom but later scaled it down to N2million before the girl could be released. However the JTF said that no ransom was paid before the release of the girl was secured.
Little Divine Emeruwa who was in fairly good health after her release said she was fed on a meal of rice and yams by her abductors throughout the period of her captivity.

Original_Search_Title: FCT Sports Fiesta Ends Without N20.7m Budget
Headline: Nigeria: FCT Sports Fiesta Ends Without N20.7m Budget
Body_Text:
Abuja — The curtain was drawn Friday on the fifth edition of the Federal Capital Territory Sports Festival without approval of the over 20m naira needed to run the event by the FCT administration leading organizers and participants to groan under the yoke of poor logistics.
The four-day sports gathering, was characterized by poor planning and organisation as a result of lack of funds to enhance smooth execution of some of the programmes for the annual event.
Sunday Trust gathered at the FCT Sports Council secretariat in Garki that the sports council was handicapped, as it could not get the approval and subsequent release of the N20.7 million budget proposal sent to the office of the FCT Minister of State, as at Thursday evening.
According to our source who does not want his identity disclosed, the budget proposal file was returned back to the Director Sports Council Wednesday upon reaching the honourable minister's table, on account of improper channelling.
"The delay in the release of the funds for the hosting of the annual sports show was caused by the mix-up in the budget proposal journey, it was earlier sent to the FCT Minister, Aliyu Modibo Umar; instead of the FCT Minister of State, John James Akpanudoedehe wherein the proposal was sent back to the Sports Council, informing the Director last week Wednesday of the appropriate quarters of request for the festival funds" he said.
Our source also said the authorities responsible for the festival know what to have done to ensure a hitch-free fiesta but the FCT sports director's move was thwarted by the honourable minister who is of the opinion that going out to promising sponsors for seeming minor funds would affect the National Sports Festival coming up in Kaduna next year.
Premised upon this, the Mambilla Barracks camp of the male athletes was saddled with hardship involving accommodation, feeding and general upkeep.
A participant who simply identified himself as Steve said feeding was not consistent even as it was untimely.
"Feeding has not been okay at all, we had boiled yams and tea as breakfast the other day and on Wednesday, we missed lunch so during dinner, beans, Amala and Eba were served together but I tell you, the beans was without salt. A plate of food is N250 naira per head after we protested against an earlier N300 meal per person.
"It will interest you to note that the watery tea we have here is accompanied with only three slices of bread. I am sure of a greater quantity and quality at a 'Mai Shai' place than what they give us here". He said.
Coach Habakkuk of Kwali area council was however of the opinion that the event was fair considering the fact that the FCT Sports Council was able to secure accommodation for the athletes in an environment devoid of social vices and thoroughfares from non participants and outsiders.
"The festival is going on fine, we are also meeting up with the medals and if you look on the score board, we are currently on top, it is so because the organisers have fed us well and we sleep where there is security and you don't go out and get divided that your property is at risk of theft you know this is a military barracks, so all you do is remain focused and face the games.
"Most people do not understand what sports is all about; I tell you sports is discipline so the issue of where we are sleeping is no issue as it only prepares you to tackle challenges in life as they come". Habakkuk said.
FCT Director of Sports, Dr. Geoffrey Ogu, who had a chat with our reporter in his office posited that the required success was registered as far as the sporting event is concerned even as he said hiccups were ordinary and typical of any fiesta involving large crowds.
"The sports festival has been fair considering the fact that the safety and comfort of athletes which is paramount has been achieved, the contingent arrangement excellent and I can tell you that in terms of officiating we are on top of it, this is evidenced from the fact that we have not registered any profound protest with regards to irregularities.
"Budgeting seems to be fluctuating because of the various council participants who are increasing in number by the day and so a fluctuation in one aspect affects most others. I am sure of a wonderful closing ceremony which among other things involves the launching of the FCT Sports Development Initiative," he added.
The FCT sports festival started last Monday with 500.000 registration fee per local council. The participants contested for honours in 31 events during the four days it lasted.

Original_Search_Title: Why I Stand Surety for Uwazuruike -Ezendigbo
Headline: Nigeria: Why I Stand Surety for Uwazuruike -Ezendigbo
Body_Text:
The Ezendigbo of Lagos State, Eze Christian Nwachukwu has said that his standing surety for bail of MASSOB leader Chief Ralph Uwazuruike was borne out of his concern for the plight of the Igbo activist.
Speaking with Vanguard in his Palace at Okota, Lagos, Eze Nwachukwu said he was touched by the long incarceration of Uwazuruike even after his counterparts from other ethnic groups had been released.
Uwazuruike, leader of the Movement for the Actualization of the Sovereign State f Biafara (MASSOB) was granted three-month bail by an Abuja high court last Friday to enable him bury his late mother.
His bail bond was signed by Senators Uche Chukwumerije and Ikechukwu Obiora, while Ezendigbo of Abuja, Eze Nwosu Ibe and that of Lagos Eze Nwachukwu also stood for him.
Eze Nwachukwu said: " As an illustrious son of Ndigbo, Chief Uwazuruike is a committed Igbo leader, who believes in fighting against social, political and whatever injustice in the land."
According to Nwachukwu, "having released the leaders of the Oodua Peoples Congress (OPC) Dr. Frederick Fasehun; Chief Gani Adams and leader of the Niger Delta People's Volunteer Force, Alhaji Asari Dokubo, we thought that the MASSOB leader would also be released.
Moreover, I believe leaders of the already released ethnic rights activists stood surety for them. So I felt Uwazuruike deserves the support of Igbo leaders in his legal attempt to get bail."
The Ezendigbo of Lagos thanked the Senators who signed the bail bond for the release of the MASSOB leader and also Senator Ike Ekweremadu and Mr. Festus Keyamo whom he said played vital roles to secure temporary bail for Uwazuruike.
He expressed hope that after the three-month bail, God will intervene to ensure complete freedom for the Igbo leader.
Meanwile, all is set for the New yam (Iri Ji) Festival of Eze Nwachukwu on Saturday, November 3, 2007 at his palace in Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: What Will Prof. Maurice Iwu Be Remembered For?
Headline: Mauritius: What Will Prof. Maurice Iwu Be Remembered For?
Body_Text:
I am quite sure that, I'm not the only happy Nigerian democrat celebrating the judicial demolition of the fraudulent electoral umpire erected by Prof. Maurice Iwu across the country.
This is because, for the first time in the history of the nation, the wrongs are being righted and the people, including the masses are excited. So far, three gubernatorial elections have been cancelled and two of the affected candidates, who were "duly cleared" by Iwu, have been disqualified by a credible authority from contesting further polls. With all these, the loquacious Iwu is still pretending to be standing. He pretends as if he is not bothered, but all of us know, that by now, the man is sleepless, restless, full of shame and enveloped in guilt, except if he is not born of a woman. I wonder what people like Iwu took Nigeria for,a banana republic? Where any thing goes? To him, the country has reached a climax in vices related to governance, so a leader can do anything and go away with it. Hypocrites like Iwu do not believe in the country, but always try to show a fake and false sense of patriotism. I have never seen an elder behaving like him, contributing negatively and yet showing no remorse. But it is time for Iwu to know that times have change, and the country has no place for him anymore. He has knowingly or unknowingly, discredited himself to a point where only his beneficiaries have respect for him.He has entered the bad books of our electoral memory, and each time we think of him,we remember; arrogance, lawlessness, brutality, nastiness, inefficiency, a man full of inferiority complex who seeks to measure it with stubbornness,a compromised fellow, a mediocre, a stuffless politician and a godless creature. He has shown to the whole world the example of a bad umpire,whose services should not be sought. Only people like OBJ can reward such a man with either OFR, MFR or the likes, for doing what only a "conscienceless" man can do.
Imagine the situation where elections are being annulled, and some of the contestants disqualified.We are lucky that third term did not succeed, if not, he would have been promoted, and a state banquate organised to thank him for creating electoral confusion in the land. But thank God we have a credible judiciary that kept its cool,watching the electoral stupidity being unleashed on the nation by a man who was prepared to sell the nation's democracy for a plate of Obasanjo's yam.Let him pay a visit to any aggrieved state in the country and see what will happen to him.He will taste a bitter pill of what OBJ went through in the hands of Niger Delta youths at the Transcorp Hotel last Thursday, while celebrating the removal of Celestine Omehia. Obasanjo was trying to enter the lift to go to the executive floor 10, when he was buoyed unstopped,and forced to go back by a number of youths who were ready to ridicule him.
Iwu is a disgrace to the academic world,and the entire Nigerian community.He is highly qualified for the prisons for taking the country backwards. He has caused a lot of damage to our collective integrity.Now government have to look for extra funds again to conduct fresh polls in many places. He should not be allowed to conduct any other election in the country again, or else there should be massive boycott of the polls,because he will repeat what he did.He told the world that he will do the same thing again if given another chance.So,why give him another chance.He has no respect for even the president, if not why the reckless outburst, when the president himself has rated the polls low,and set up a review and reforms panel?
Both the presidency and the senate should commence the legal process for the removal of the most corrupt and fraudulent INEC boss the nation has ever had. He should then be prosecuted.The outcome of the tribunal verdicts alone,is enough to put the man behind bars.He unlawfully and passionately disqualified those on the black list of his master, OBJ, and made it look as if he was being patriotic. He was a full fledged PDP member without an open identity, but his actions confirmed it. That was why he fought members of the opposition parties openly with his God given but manipulated intelligience, abusing them without courtesy, just because he had the protective priviledge. He should be made to provide the funds needed for the conduct of fresh elections as the first punishment, before being escorted to the Adamawa or Jos prisons,where Obasanjo failed as an inmate, to be rehabilitated.
Please before any one is appointed to such sensitive positions,the presidency should allow the security agencies to do a thorough job of assessing, not only the moral qualifications of such persons, but more importantly, their mental alertness, psychological frame of minds, social focus/order, and political spiritual objectivity and direction,respectively. We can no longer afford intellectual lunatics, who parade and masquarades leaders, to destroy our developing democracy. For the judiciary, I hail you. Baba Kutigi,and your men, May Allah bless and reward your love and patriotism for our country.We need men like you to remind and stop the Iwus, from burying our democracy.

Original_Search_Title: Travel Tips for Itinerant
Headline: Nigeria: Travel Tips for Itinerant
Body_Text:
Lagos — "There is no lack of good advice, only those who listen are very few." - Swahili proverb
It has become imperative for those of us who roam the thick forests of Africa, and fly around the skies of the world to advise our country men and women on the dangers lurking around some of the most cosmopolitan cities of the world.
The recent killing of South Africa's superstar singer, Lucky Dube has compelled me to write this piece. While our hearts go out to his family, friends and his country, South Africa, which had succeeded in attracting the world's greatest celebrities in the past, we must draw useful lessons from the great tragedy.
Once upon a time, the conspiracy was that Nigeria is the most dangerous place to visit. There were big billboards around some airports warning visitors to Nigeria that they did so at a great personal risk. All the American artistes flew over Nigeria to South Africa to perform at mega jams, until our man, Prince Nduka Obaigbena, and a few corporate organisations fought a gallant battle, for the restoration of our pride as a nation, by doing the impossible, in inviting the best of the best in the world of entertainment to Africa's most populous nation, Nigeria.
In an unprecedented display of superlative strength, the THISDAY festival assembled a rare parade of Beyonce, Jay Z, Snoop Dogg, Ciara, Busta Rhymes, Missy Elliot, En Vogue, alongside the best of Nigeria. Not done yet, we were soon mesmerized with superlative jams by the ultimate showman, P Diddy, Kelly Rowland, Shakira, John Legend, Neyo and others. The superstition against Nigeria was thus debunked. We must salute the uncommon courage and dare devilry of the corporate organisations that made it possible for our dear country to shine again.
I seriously sympathise with South Africa for the way her beautiful and indeed breathtaking landscape is being ravaged by violence. In his twilight years, the Mandiba, Nelson Mandela, may have to sideline his AIDS campaign to join hands with his people to root out the scourge of serial killings. Visitors to South Africa are now warned to be extra-vigilant. Nigerians who are well-known to carry raw cash like mobile vaults are advised to watch out as they arrive the airport in Jo'burg. In fact, driving out of the airport in posh cars may not be too wise as they become instant targets of armed robbers. There have been recorded cases of visitors being trailed from the airport to the many exotic hotels in highbrow Sandton. Also, changing money at the airport forex bureau is an easy way to attract unwanted attention.
Those who love to argue at public places are warned to desist from such dangerous habits. If possible, do not walk any dark corners alone, and check very well before you open your hotel doors to strangers. Always peep through the door holes to ascertain the identity of room attendants and if possible, keep your doors tightly locked even while house keeping is going on. Do not treat this as false alarm, so as not to become a veritable source of statistics.
After South Africa, we move to the famous London Heathrow, where some evil geniuses are waiting for Nigerians who tend to lower their guards as soon as they land at the world's busiest airports. Never leave your luggage unattended, because you may lose your valuables within a twinkle of an eye. The airport rats often target rich and famous Nigerians who they know are fond of loading their pockets and bags with wads of crispy notes in pounds and dollars. Sometimes they pounce on their victims as they look the other way while engaging in gossips and get distracted. At other times, someone tells you, as you push your trolleys toward the exit that something just dropped from your bag and within the minute you look or walk back to check, your handbag, usually is gone forever. I know of several high profile victims. These guys always make you look stupid.
A special warning goes to those who drive around London and other cities. You must keep your doors tightly locked, and do not wind down your windows, in order to escape carjackers. A friend was stopped by some youths who, acting like good Samaritans, told him there was smoke coming out of his bonnet, and as he came down to check, got a rude shock when they quickly took over the driver's seat, and drove off with his car, his mouth wide ajar! He never saw his car again.
Please don't ever send your kids on useless errands, as many cases of kid-snatching have been reported. Black-on-Black violence is now very rampant. Avoid engaging in unnecessary argument with dangerous gangsters who would not think twice before putting a trigger on you, and take the life he did not create.
I had a not so pleasant experience at the lounge of Hilton Metropole, on Edgware Road, London, a favourite home of many Nigerians. We were having our drinks when these two young boys, who later turned out to Romanians walked in. Pretending to be beggars from a "poor nation", they approached our table, and held out what looked like handbills, and pleaded; "Please give us money", and dropped the papers on our table. Foolish us, little did we realize that the evil geniuses merely dropped the papers to cover our mobile phones while they cleverly smuggled the phones from under. A flash of sorts from my friend's glittering phone caught my attention, and by the time I shouted "your phone, your phone," the robbers took to their heels.
As we rose up to chase after them, I realized my brand new phone had also vanished. We ran after them like Ben Johnson, but I couldn't run fast enough like those "antelopes". My friend was fitter so he chased them like a mad horse towards Paddington, and actually caught up with one of them. We searched his body for my phone, since he had been on my side of the table, but he had apparently passed on the phone to another member of the gang who must have been waiting at the entrance of the hotel.
We dragged the boy back to the hotel and called the police in from next door. We got a rude shock when the police told us, after punching his name into their system, that the boy was their regular customer, already on a "wanted list" from age ten.
