I insisted on a trial, and we went to court. It turned out to be a waste of time, as he was under-aged. He was in court with his parents who tried to intimidate everyone with menacing their looks. The boy was discharged.
One last warning in London. Mind your bank account details, and credit or debit cards. The bad boys can collapse your empire before your very eyes. They have perfected the amazing science of penetrating the most secure of devices. If possible, don't keep too much cash in your regular accounts. Also, you can relate directly with your account managers and encode your cards to request for due authorization each time it is swiped. Yes, it is not very convenient, but you are better safe than sorry.
Next, we move to the United States, once described by John Pepper Clark as "America, their America". I believe that American Immigration or Homeland Security and US Customs are trained to intimidate any visitor, and even their own citizens. All the protocol you enjoy in London is totally absent in America. Fast tracks are not even available for business and first class passengers. For example, accredited members of the Nigerian High Commission in London can welcome a Governor near the aircraft, it is virtually impossible for our embassy staff to walk through American Immigration as escort of a VIP.
On a trip with a Nigerian Governor a few years back, I was happy to see the wonderful reception we got in London but stunned to see how the youthful American security guys removed everyone's baban riga, including that of the governor, removed our shoes, while the then Consul General stood helplessly at the distance, and later apologized that "this is what we go through here," to which someone replied that "is it possible for us to ask a visiting American Governor to raise up his hands for a body search in Nigeria?" I leave you to answer that question.
On another occasion, I had traveled to Washington with a friend who's also a publisher. The gentleman had his genuine British passport. I had crossed the Immigration and waited for him to join me by the conveyor belt. I waited for over one hour, and was confused by his disappearance. The security people told me I had to go out and wait for him. Again, I waited for over an hour, prancing up and down. I knew the man very well as being upright and not someone to partake in stupid ventures. The problem was that there was no way to ask what had happened to him, unlike in London where you can seek information at the airport, and you would be told if he was detained by them or not.
In frustration, I had to leave the airport, thinking that the worst scenario was for him to be deported back to our Great Britain. I was checking into my hotel when he rang my roaming number that he had just been released. The Immigration seeing that the man was born in one unknown village in Ondo State of Nigeria had wondered how he got his British passport. All explanations fell on deaf ears, and he was detained by this muscle-flexing lady until another officer took over from her, and allowed him to go immediately. Just imagine!
There are many sad tales from around the world. Since September 11, the world has changed for ever. To travel is like going to war. You must search your bag thoroughly for anything that can cause you to spend long hours at airports. Most people now dress scantily, and it is not out of place to wear your flat pair of slippers. Never wear laced shoes, unless your tummy is flat enough and can bend down with ease. Removing your waist belt would save you some minutes. Carrying elubo (yam flour) and garri in your bag can land you in trouble. Don't even attempt to carry meat and fish, dairy products and vegetables into America. Of course, some of our stubborn women will never listen. It is not uncommon for them to invade foreign airports with their odoriferous spices, which no air-freshener can cure easily. A search of some bags would reveal all manner of strange food items, bush meat, stockfish, snails (crawling, fried, frozen, peppered, etc), dry fish, roasted guinea fowl, as well as some efficacious juju to hypnotize any poke-nosing customs officer. It is indeed a comedy of errors.

Original_Search_Title: Ohazulike Advises Igbo to Shun Vices
Headline: Nigeria: Ohazulike Advises Igbo to Shun Vices
Body_Text:
Lagos — EE Ndigbo in Lagos, Eze Nwabueze Ohazulike has advised Igbo in the state to shun actions capable of demeaning the Igbo nation.
Eze Ohuzulike, who led Ndigbo in Lagos on Saturday to celebrate new yam festival called on Igbo sons and daughters to avoid any act capable of staining the good image of the Igbo nation.
Ohazulike who also is the national president of the Traditional Council of Ndi Eze Ndi Igbo in diaspora described cultism as a great distraction that must be avoided by all Igbo youths, adding that those involved in it should have a rethink.
"Cultism is a very big distraction and should be shunned by our youths and elders."
The royal father maintained that there are no short cut to success other than hard work and purposeful planning, which are the hallmark of every Igbo man, wherever he finds himself.
He added that Ndigbo are known for hardwork and advised that they should keep up with such virtue even when the results are not positive, stating that one day God will answer their prayers.
"Ndigbo should work hard, they are known as hardworking people. They should continue in their hardwork even when there are no positive results, one day God will answer them," he said.
Eze Ohazulike also implored them, not to look for trouble in whatever they do because there is no alternative to peace anywhere in the world.
"Without peace we can do nothing, we have toiled and continued to toil tirelessly for almost a decade to spread the gospel of peace and inter-ethnic harmony in Lagos and beyond. We have done these sacrificially, not minding the cost in terms of energy and resources, our efforts, nevertheless, have not been in vain," he said.
He added that since the present Igbo leadership assumed office in Lagos, no major conflict involving Ndigbo and other b

Original_Search_Title: Battle of the Souls Impresses At  Film Festival
Headline: Kenya: Battle of the Souls Impresses At  Film Festival
Body_Text:
On April 29, a film made in Uganda, by a Ugandan and of a storyline that is a spin-off from a Ugandan society personality's former lifestyle premiered at the Didi's World auditorium in Kansanga. By the end of the 120 minute film, most of the audience had been blown by the cinematography of Matt Bish's movie - Battle Of The Souls.
Fast-forward, the 2nd Kenya International Film Festival (KIFF) is held in Nairobi and Battle Of The Souls is a part of the competition that includes films from all over the world; Congo, South Africa, Italy, Rwanda, Ethiopia, Netherlands, Sudan, Israel, Tanzania, Burkina Faso, Mali, France, Portugal, USA, German, Morocco, DRC, Cote De Voire, Morocco, Niger, India, Cameroon, Sweden, Mexico, Cuba, Iran, Spain, Kenya and Uganda-although, only movies from Africa are eligible for the competition.
In over 100 films that were to be screened during the festival, Matt Bish, the creator of Battle Of The Souls and one of the many Ugandan filmmakers who were a part of this festival, had secured his place among the Top 20 (African) films at the festival.
"...Because I was among the top 20, I was able to travel with most of the cast for the festival. Apart from the meals (which we had to buy ourselves), we were otherwise well catered for," says Matt Bish.
He says that when he joined the competition, he was sure that his film would win the best East African Film award hands down.
"Most of the other (East African) films were not even half as good as an average Nigerian movie. So I was certain that Battle Of The Souls would take the trophy."
Yet although the Ugandan feature film was returned home with an attached credit "Best East African Film", there was a twist - it wasn't exactly the best East African Film as per the judges. A fact Matt Bish had to clarify (on phone) with the director of KIFF - Mr Charles Peter Asiba - when we met at his Media Pro Studios in Muyenga this week.
" I don't understand what this certificate signifies exactly," an un amused Matt Bish said bringing out the pink certificate that declares; Special Mention for the Best East African Film Award - Battle Of The Souls By Matt Bish (Uganda).
The director of KIFF explained that the certificate was to recognise Battle Of The Souls as a great East African Film, even if it didn't take the award. " The Award for Best East African Film went to Fimbo Ya Baba by Vera Pieroth (Tanzania). Battle Of The Souls would have won if Fimbo Ya Baba were not in the competition. It was good, but it didn't take the trophy," Asiba patiently explained.
The KIFF website details that Battle Of The Souls got a Special Mention because of its impressive camera work, composition, photography and sense of suspense.
Still, Matt is not impressed; "I'm a very competitive man, and I can't pretend to be happy to be named second best, to win number two," he says.
However Matt Bish will confess that the festival was a foot placed forward for him and the movie; "This festival exposed me as a filmmaker, and Uganda as a whole. People from around the world were able to see that something of quality and professionalism can come out of Uganda. Actually the night the film premiered (October 3) was the best night of my life. We had the biggest audience and everybody stayed until the end of the movie (the movie is now 108 minutes). And when it concluded, everyone stood up and gave the most deafening applause.
Actually one Kenyan guy, a young man from Busia later told us that we had told his story. He said that he had always wanted people to know that the underworld is real, and while watching this movie, he says he saw his life being played out on screen."
And it isn't only Matt Bish who was in high spirits after October 3. One of the members from his cast, Joel Okuyo, who plays the role of Wycliff- the devil/underworld Lord, was the toast of the night.
"There was this film maker who walked up to me and told me that I am the Brad Pitt of Africa. It was really exciting. And I learnt a lot too, from the entire festival. I attended almost all the workshops that had been organised," Okuyo tells.
The workshops at the festival included the Maisha Lab, the Critic Workshop which involved journalists and news editors, the Script Writing Workshop that targeted youth and budding script writers, the Pitching Workshop that included film makers and producers, script writers and directors and the Radio and TV in promotional African cinema.
The big winner of the festival was Gaston Jean-Marié Kaboré (Burkina Faso) whose film Buud-Yam took the over all award of Best Feature Film. Gaston J-M Kabore, is one of the most influential filmmakers working in Africa today, and the film Buud-Yam has won the prestigious Etalon De Yenenga at the Pan-African Film Festival in Ouagadougou and has been screened at the Cannes International Film Festival.

Original_Search_Title: Chukwumerije Advocates Generous Budget for Tourism
Headline: Nigeria: Chukwumerije Advocates Generous Budget for Tourism
Body_Text:
Senator Uche Chukwumerije has advocated generous budgetary allocations for Nigeria's emerging tourism sector.
He made the call Thursday in Abuja during a courtesy visit to the management of the Nigeria Tourism Development Corporation, to draw the attention of the tourism development corporation to some tourist sites in his constituency which he said needed urgent government attention for development.
Stating his mission to the NTDC management, the senator representing Abia North Senatorial District said; "My mission is to explore ways in which, through the NTDC, I can tap from the tourism potential in Abia North.
"We feel that NTDC would be interested in the long juju of Arochukwu, which is more popular than that of Senegal, but less developed."
Senator Chukwumerije also said that similar attention need to be paid to the popular slave route and the slave market in Bende local government area, where slaves were sold years ago, and the Imo River which has the potential to attract tourists from far and near if developed.
Speaking on measures for sustenance of the gains the sector has recorded in recent times, Chukwumerije recommended generous budgetary allocation to build infrastructure, to keep pace with the growing level of awareness which he feared might outgrow infrastructure, if urgent steps are not taken. "My fear is that awareness might outgrow infrastructural facilities, but with the level of commitment so far shown by Otunba Olusegun Runsewe the corporation's director general, coupled with his infectious enthusiasm, my fears are eliminated," the senator said.
In his response, the NTDC boss told his guest that the vision of his management team was to make tourism a national discourse. For everyone to make his contribution as tourism cuts across all sectors, trade and vocation, hence it employs the greatest number of people globally.
"We are building a yam house in Igbo-Ukwu in Anambra State, just as we built a fish house in Argungu to celebrate the new yam festival in the East and Argungu fishing festival in the North respectively.
"Some of our products, like the modern motorpark will raise the living standard of our youths through employment generation, as well as our 150 buses which we are importing. And we want Abia State to buy into the project.
Otunba Runsewe pledged the corporation's commitment to tourism development in all parts of the country, and said the NTDC will put in place a machinery to document all the tourist sites in Abia State to enhance his corporation's task of developing and promoting them.
The NTDC management later took the senator round to see some facilities at the NTDC headquarters; its ultra-modern gallery, the tourism village and the hall being built with locally sourced building materials.

Original_Search_Title: Plateau Residents Seek Alternative to Bread
Headline: Nigeria: Plateau Residents Seek Alternative to Bread
Body_Text:
Jos — Residents of Plateau State have begun seeking alternatives to bread the major breakfast menu in many homes following the scarcity occasioned by the strike embarked upon by bakers.
Bread became scarce in the state since last weekend as bakers ceased production in protest against the high cost of inputs and to signal their desire to again increase the price of bread, the second in five months.
As a result, many lovers of bread have been going without it as the usual bread stands have virtually become empty while the few available are below the quality they are used to.
Faced with this reality, many residents have now turned to other alternatives because according to them, life must go on with or without bread.
According to those interviewed by Vanguard, they have temporarily replaced bread with biscuit, yam, fried potato, puff-puff and akamu pending the return of bread to the streets.
However, some of them said the planned increase in price of bread might soon take it out of the reach of many and urged government to do something about the situation.
Some others while appealing that the price of bread should not become prohibitive said they cannot do without it and appealed to the bakers to call off their strike.

Original_Search_Title: Cameroonians Called to Boycott Bread
Headline: Cameroonians Called to Boycott Bread
Body_Text:
The National Workers' Trade Union has urged Cameroonians to massively boycott bread following recent moves by bakers to increase the price of 210 gram loaf of bread from FCFA 150 to FCFA 200.
The call was made during a meeting presided over by the Minister of Commerce, Luc Malgloire Mbarga Atangana, at the conference hall of the Ministry on September 13, aimed at looking into the possibility of maintaining present bread prices.
The Trade Union's spokesman, Isaac Bishala, advised Cameroonians not to consume bread any longer, till further notice.He said this move came against the background of the unilateral increase of the price of bread without prior negotiations with stakeholders.
Making allusions to what obtains presently in Italy with the boycott of spaghetti, (Pasta) in connection with the skyrocketing prices of flour in the world market, Bishala said Cameroonians could do same.
Similarly, during a meeting between the Minister of Finance, Essimi Menye, and stakeholders of the bread sector, equally insinuated that instead of buying bread Cameroonians could comfortably eat grated coco yam.
The Minister is said to have suspended the customs duties on flour for two months - from September to November.Meanwhile, bakers enumerated several problems they faced related to expenses incurred.
The Centre Provincial President of Bakers' Union, Joseph Innocent Djolta, said the most pressing problem is the distribution chain through which bread reaches consumers.
He said if the distribution chain could be scrapped and bonuses instituted, this could solve the problem.
He advised that Cameroonians should buy their bread at bakeries.Djolta disclosed that the Greeks involved in bread production also pose a serious problem for local bakers as with credit from their country they easily produce large quantities and sell at a lower price.
While the Greek-produced bread is given to distributors at FCFA 110, the cost of production for local bakers is FCFA 143, which puts them at a disadvantage.
Besides the Greeks represented by ACROPOL, clandestine bakers, equally kill the market for established bakers as they supply almost all the shops in the quarters making it impossible for people to buy straight from bakeries.
A commission set up to look into the issues of bread production by the Minister of Commerce would meet today, Monday.Though the Commerce Minister has called for the respect of the price of FCFA 150, there are still reports that some distributors continue to sell for more.

Original_Search_Title: Ruler Boosts Owerri-Orlu Road Project With N3.6m
Headline: Nigeria: Ruler Boosts Owerri-Orlu Road Project With N3.6m
Body_Text:
Lagos — TRADITIONAL ruler of Awo-Mbieri autonomous community, Imo State, Eze Dr Emma Umez-Eronini has announced a donation of N3.6 million towards the Ugbele road project, a paved stone road, leading into the community from Nwaorieubi on the Owerri -Orlu Road.
This he said, was his commitment, in the next 12 months, to the road project which he is building for his people, which had already gulped N12 million.
He made the announcement recently during the new yam festival "Iriji Awo" in his palace. He disclosed that N300,000 would be spent on Labour, N440,000 on sand and gravel while N1,800,000 would be spent on cement .
Eze Eronini said he had made the donation to give more impetus to the road project, assuring that this would not stop him from making donations to development projects in the community .
The traditional ruler further announced a special birthday present, a donation of exercise books to Awo-Mbieri students and youths which would be made available through the Awo Development Fund. He appealed to friends and well wishers to support the fund.
Eze Umez-Eronini, who is the founder of Keep Imo Beautiful Society, once reputed to have been responsible for the status of Imo as the most beautiful state in Nigeria, hailed the government of Governor Ikedi Ohakim for initiating the " Clean and Green Initiatives for sanitation and cleanliness.
He further commended the administration for the proposed dualization of all roads leading into Owerri, the state capital and called for the repair of the road from Nkwesi to Aba, which he described as the most important road in the state.
Earlier in their address , read by the president - general of Awo-Mbieri Autonomous Community Progressive Union, Mr. J.N.J Ewurum, the people described Awo as "Development-Conscious Community" recalling that way back in 1984, Awo won a certificate as the most development conscious community in Mbaitoli LGA, a status which had been given a bigger boost by the enthronement of the traditional ruler since 2003.
He appealed to some citizens of the community aggrieved by the loss of the Ezeship stool to give peace a chance and come back to the fold. "The issue of Ezeship in Awo Mbieri is settled and there is no need hanging on it forever," he stressed.

Original_Search_Title: Rep Charges Yar'Adua On Power Sector
Headline: Nigeria: Rep Charges Yar'Adua On Power Sector
Body_Text:
Deputy chairman of the House of Representatives Committee On House Services, Hon. Aliyu Dikko, has advised President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua not to back down on his promise to declare a state of emergency in the power sector, saying unless the energy industry is revived, the nation's economy will remain in the doldrums.
Hon. Dikko who disclosed this in a press statement in Abuja at the weekend said only a reliable power supply can stimulate the real sector of the economy thereby creating jobs for the teaming unemployed youths.
"The economy of this nation cannot be revived unless the power sector is re-engineered. Efficient power sector is a critical catalyzing agent because without it, there will be no meaningful economic growth and development. It is only a reliable power supply that can stimulate the real sector of the economy."
The member representing Binji/Silame federal constituency of Sokoto State also charged the president to pay adequate attention to the issue of security as according to him "security is very critical to meaningful development in any organised society."
He called on the people of the Niger Delta region to embrace peace and cooperate with the administration of President Yar'Adua as he tries to broker peace in the troubled area. He expressed optimism that with the Niger Delta plan, the quality of life of the people would soon improve.
On the first 100 days of president Yar'Adua, Hon. Dikko commended the president for his strict adherence and observance of the principles of rule of law, adding that constitutionalism was the pillar of any successful democratic governance.
He also commended Governor Magartaka Wamakko of Sokoto for bringing positive changes in the state in his first 100 days in office.
Meanwhile, Rep. Dikko has commenced his economic empowerment programme through the implementation of micro-credit scheme. He has already distributed over 60 motorcycles and donated sums of money to the people of his constituency to help alleviate poverty.
Hon. Dikko has also concluded arrangements to distribute food items such as grains and yams, to his constituency even as he will soon launch his economic empowerment programme, Dikko Economic Empowerment Programme (DEEP), which is aimed at human capacity development.

Original_Search_Title: Perspectives From America
Headline: Nigeria: Perspectives From America
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE State of Maryland, from the testimony of most of the people in attendance, had become somehow notorious for hosting Nigerian public officials.
And with several journalists around, it was quite natural that we would all make fun of the occasion. We obviously imagined how some of the headlines would read: 'Abuja Big Boy Paints Washington Red'; 'Another Nigerian Public Official in US Owambe' etc. It was a reunion of sort for many Nigerians when my friend, Sunday Dare, head of the Hausa Service of the Voice of America (VOA), hosted me to a reception penultimate Saturday night. With several Nigerian professionals in the house, the occasion provided me the opportunity to render an account of the first 100 Days of President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua as well state my case as to what I was doing in the United States .
I arrived Washington Monday last week and almost instantly, my trip became the object of controversy. A news article captioned, 'I am in the US to Understudy the Americans', drew a lot of flak from some Nigerians who took turns to attack me on the popular website, Nigerianvillagesquare.com. There were many incendiary comments with some stating that I must have come to launder my 'loot' since, as a commentator stated, he had it on good authority that a Nigerian official usually took $5000 as daily estacode for foreign trips. He even cited the 'example'of an official who allegedly took $70,000 for a 14-day trip. I responded by stating that my estacode was $350 per night. I doubt if anybody believed me. It was only natural that this strange tale would sound more credible than mine, coming at a time when the whole world was being treated to salacious details of some mega 'renovation'.
However, I took all the comments in my stride. As the Yoruba adage goes, if you tie corn to your apron, you have automatically presented yourself as a playmate for chicken. By my appointment, I have become a sitting target for all manner of critics. My response to those comments then as it remains now is that my mission to America was far from any aesthetic sightseeing. Building capacity for my job is a conscious decision and this particular trip to a country I had visited several times before was at my instance.
Prior to now, I had been a journalist all my working life and I am very much aware of my challenges in the new terrain in which I find myself hence the need to learn from the experiences of others. I realise that being a presidential spokesman is a whole lot different from editing a newspaper. It is a political assignment that requires different skills than those employed by a reporter. I had no doubt that I would benefit from the opportunity to meet in the United States people holding similar portfolios, people whose experience and exposure while would offer me a glimpse into the world of the representative voice of the presidential system, a job that comes with its own contradictions.
While I had a rudimentary idea about what being a presidential spokesman entailed, I was certain I could learn useful lessons that would make me do the job better. All factors considered, I think I made a good decision going to America and I thank the US embassy in Abuja and the American State Department for facilitating the trip.
The last ten days have been worthwhile and my encounter last Wednesday with Mr. Tony Snow, the outgoing Spokesman for President George Bush at the White House, was rather instructive and memorable. It was Snow's last press briefing before he stepped down the next day after serving under a tumultuous era. In what was his valedictory briefing, there was no let-up; it was all about Iraq, one area where his principal appears to be failing in the eyes of majority of Americans. Gordon Johndroe, special assistant to the president and national security council, who also doubles as Snow's deputy put me through the nexus between the White House press corps and the media and contrary to what I had read, the relationship is not necessarily adversarial.
At the State Department, where there seems to be more cooperation between the reporters and the spokesperson in terms of news sourcing and clarifications, I was taken through the entire process, right from the early-morning collation of vital information from different regions and departments to the 'gaggle', and then the formal briefing by Spokesman Gonzalo Gallegos before the subsequent off-the-record session. Tom Casey, the deputy spokesperson who had spent two years in Nigeria in the early 90s filled in the gaps for me in the interactions that showed his deep knowledge of our country. I also met Ms Claudio Anyaso who left Nigeria to become the director, Office of Public Diplomacy and Public Affairs at the State Department.
I had other meetings at the Government Accountability Office, the Congress, the Foreign Press Centre and Transparency International of US. At Washington Times, I had a lunch briefing with members of the Editorial team, many of whom appeared to know little about Africa . But what I took with me came from a chance meeting, at the lobby of the newspaper building, with Editorial Page Editor, Tony Blankley, who was spokesman for Newt Gringrich, arguably, the most visible, if not the most powerful, Speaker in American history.
Blankley was coming from the funeral of a friend so he was in an introspective mood. Obviously speaking from experience, he was kind enough to offer fresh perspectives on the relationship between the spokesperson and the media and gave some cherished personal advice. Washington Post was not on my schedule but I was there nonetheless. I met top editors but there was one small office that interested me most even though the occupant was not there: the office of Bob Woodward. He was said to be working on his next book, another bestseller with rich insight no doubt. Back to the reception in Sunday's house. With Prof. Bolaji Aluko leading the charge, I was taken to task on many issues as I rendered account of what has transpired in Nigeria in the first 100 days in office of President Yar'Adua. Virtually everyone had an opinion on the April elections and understandably they were not complimentary.
I encouraged my audience to look at the positive side: For all of Nigeria's long history of electoral irregularities, the nation, for the first time, finally has a sitting president honest enough to admit that the process that brought him to office was flawed even while we all know that in Africa, credible elections do not always approximate true democracy. To break the cycle of disputed elections in Nigeria, President Yar'Adua, has established both an Electoral Reform Committee, headed by a respected former chief justice, and publicly preached the importance of due process, the rule of law, and upholding the Constitution.
I equally made it clear that while it is important that Nigeria reforms its electoral process, the challenge for the man on the street in our nation remains that of poverty. The conventional critique, especially among Nigerians in the Diaspora, is that our oil-rich nation has enough for the basic need of all citizens - if not for the greed of a tiny elite. While inequality has indeed held back Nigeria so has a lack of financial and physical infrastructure.
Distributive justice
I informed my audience that to reposition the economy, President Yar'Adua has begun implementing a seven-point agenda with emphasis on observing the rule of law, promoting job-led growth, and ensuring distributive justice; while his first piece of legislation will advance land reforms, and would empower land owners to have property rights, enabling the poor and disenfranchised to link to the financial and capital markets. Other issues on his agenda include providing the requisite environment for foreign and domestic investment in critical infrastructure like transport; massive intervention in education and a plan to tap the considerable wealth and experience of Nigerians in the Diaspora.
It is no secret that poverty and asset development cannot be tackled without revamping the nation's energy sector. I cited the example of the absence of integrated planning between gas and power which has led to a situation in which even the on-going multi-billion dollar power projects may fail to yield expected national dividends - since 90 percent of the country's gas infrastructure is for export-oriented projects. I reiterated President Yar'Adua's resolve to tackle the problem head-on by declaring an emergency in the power and energy sector even as he has premised future reforms on the notion that domestic needs for gas have to be met first before the nation can export. This fact informed the appointment of a minister whose principal duties are to oversee exploration of non-associated gas and reduce barriers to local investment.
The challenges of restructuring the oil sector are indeed enormous. It is one sector where rent seeking has unfortunately become a pervasive social behaviour in both the private and public sectors. A new energy council, with a six-month mandate, I explained, is now working to unbundle the Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) into commercial entities so that our oil sector can move from an extractive one riddled with corruption, to an industry that serves the common good by increasing national production and propelling the journey to a modern economy.
In all, I think my message was well received and the exchanges were enriching. With Mrs Dare's delicious pounded yam and other delicacies, the session ended in the wee hour of Sunday morning on a convivial note for everyone. Shortly after, we all watched on the Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) as the Golden Eaglets supplied the icing on the cake by winning the under-17 World Cup tournament. It was a good time to exchange congratulatory compliments, a good time to be Nigerians. The question remains: should I have gone to Washington to seek broader knowledge for my job? The argument of some people is that the capacity-building efforts required to turn Nigeria around is not in the area of communication. While I agree to this point, communications, however, happens to be my turf.
As Tony Snow noted last Wednesday at the White House, the job of spokesperson to the President is an important one and he should know having been on both sides of the divide. Yet a good spokesman, according to John Hughes, former State Department spokesman and Ronald Reagan aide, is one who not only have the requisite access to be in on the making of policy but someone with the strength of character to speak up and warn of possible adverse consequences of unpopular policies. Because, as he argued, 'if you are going to be there for crash landing, you have to be in on the take-off.'
This means that my job as spokesman for the President goes beyond being a megaphone for this government, I must be able to understand, and properly articulate, the policy thrust of the government. It also means that to be effective I must be a part of this policy formulation process - as the person to put it in its proper context and ensure understanding across a broader spectrum of Nigerians. Fortunately, President Yar'Adua has granted me that access. Such responsibility therefore requires a measure of preparation and exposure, for the simple reason that I take my work very seriously and even more so because I take the Nigerian people exceptionally seriously.
Olusegun Adeniyi, Special Adviser to the President on Communications just returned from Washington where he attended an International Visitor Leadership Programme organized by the United States Department of State.

Original_Search_Title: NDC, NPP Clash Over Projects in Techiman District
Headline: Ghana: NDC, NPP Clash Over Projects in Techiman District
Body_Text:
A section of Members of Parliament (MPs) and Party Executives of the largest opposition National Democratic Congress, (NDC) led by the former Regional Minister, I.K. Agyei Mensah suffered humiliation at the hands of the NPP led by the current Brong Ahafo Regional Minister, Ignatius Baffour Awuah, during the celebration of the Sliver jubilee of Oyeadeyie Asa Akompanin, chief of Krobo in the Techiman Traditional Area.
The 25th Anniversary of the enstoolment of Oyeadeeyie Asa Akompanin was marked alongside the annual Yam Festival of the people of Krobo.
It all happened when the former B/A Minister under the NDC Government, I.K. Agyei Mensah tried to intimate that the NDC had received $4 million dollars from the EU for development projects for which the Party should be given credit.
Right after his speech which was to be the last on the programme, the Regional Minister, Hon Baffour Awuah was given the platform to deliver a short address.
His introduction by the Master of Ceremonies (MC) was met with wild cheers with some people describing him as the best Minister the Region has ever had.
When he took the microphone, he told the gathering that his senior brother was talking of development projects of an annual funding of $4 million but the construction of the Bui Dam alone cost $622 million.
This comparison drew a thunderous applause from the chiefs and the people present.
Some youth argued that Mr. Agyei Mensah should not have made those weak remarks since they, the youth, gave the current Regional Minister the win over the NDC.
However Mr. Agyei Mensah during his speech advocated for mature politicking in the country, which he advised should be devoid of insult, personal attacks and blame games.
He stressed that the peace of the country should be paramount, aside one's political affiliation because it is the aim of every political party to help in the development of the nation but with different policies.
The veteran politician, who was flanked by both MPs for Techiman North and South, Mr. Alex Kyeremeh and Addai Simmons respectively as well as Constituency Executives of Techiman North and South, stated that it was time for the NDC to come back and accomplish the mission of taking Ghanaians to the wonderland of peace and development.
The current Regional Minister, who was also flanked by Constituency Executives, started his address with a peaceful message after reacting to that of his senior colleague's.
After countering the remarks of Mr. Agyei Mensah, the current Regional Minister urged the chiefs to use Ghanaian culture to spearhead the fight against the spread of negative foreign culture, particularly indecent dressing among the youth.
Earlier, the Chief of Krobo, who is also the Kyidomhene of the Techiman Traditional Area urged colleague chiefs and other members of the chieftaincy institution to discard all unprogressive and powerless traditional gods and accept the one and only Almighty God.
He stated that gods and deities had over the years failed to bring progress to traditional communities stressing that the Krobo area was progressing because they had adopted the Almighty God in 1940 when their god 'Anikoko' proved powerless when it was stolen and has since not been found.
Oyeadeeyie Asa Akompanin during his 25 year old reign has been able to embark on development projects like provision of potable water, support for education, improved roads and construction of a modern palace among others.
The occasion was used to lay the foundation stone for two projects, a ¢200 million library for Krobo and a ¢400 million Museum to promote culture, history and tourism in the Techiman Traditional Area.
The MP for Techiman North, Alex Kyeremeh pledged his continued support for the library project with the provision of 120 plywood boards and packets of roofing sheets as well as 100 bags of cement.

Original_Search_Title: Encroachment On Wetlands - Who is to Blame?
Headline: Uganda: Encroachment On Wetlands - Who is to Blame?
Body_Text:
Kampala — A pile of red bricks outside a leaky shelter belonging to Moses Kasedde at the edge of Nakivubo swamp shows his intentions to construct a better house. He is one of the thousands of the urban poor who have over the years settled in crammed houses at the edge of Nakivubo swamp in Kitintale.
But like many of his neighbours, Kasedde is worried that he may never realise the rewards of his sweat.
"I hope nothing goes wrong because I have gone through many hardships, trying to put a decent roof over my children," says Kasedde, a father of six, who works as a food vendor in Kitintale Market.
"Only death will get me off this land."
However, his aspiration is under threat. About five years ago, the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA) declared that three of Kampala's wetlands would be turned into reserves.
The top environmental watchdog made a statement of intent when they demolished a house belonging to Godfrey Nyakana, the LC3 chairman of Kampala central division, which he had built in the marshy area separating Kitintale and Bugolobi.
The act shocked the encroachers as NEMA warned that it would leave no stone unturned to secure this wetland, which is critical for filtering water before releasing the clean water into Murchison Bay.
The water treatment costs at Gaba Water Works located a stone throw away from Murchison Bay has increased by three-fold because the ability of Nakivubo swamp to clean out the waste is getting compromised.
But two years after the demolition of Nyakana's house, encroachers have moved deeper into the swamp and have set up 100 new houses.
In other parts of the wetland near Silver Springs Hotel, tonnes of marrum are being poured into the swamp to create land, while pushing water further.
Not far from Silver Springs, there is a dumping site that has kept on eating into the wetland disregarding the Environmental Act that outlaws dumping in wetlands.
According to a report released by Dr. Aryamanya Mugisha, NEMA's head recently, the original area of Nakivubo has reduced from 4.4 square kilometres to 2.8 square kilometres in 1991, then to 1.9 square kilometres in 1995 and 1.3 square kilometres in 2000. Current estimates indicate the size to be less than one square kilometre.
"The population of illegal settlers in the wetlands is estimated to be over 10,000, with the main concentration being Kitintale zone in Nakawa division," reads the report.
This kind of destruction is repeated in a nearby swamp known as Kinawataka located between Kyambogo, Banda, Mbuya and Naguru hill.
"We have written to the LCs to stop the destruction of the swamp, but they have not given us any feedback," says Paul Mafabi, the commissioner in charge of the Wetland Department.
"It is the responsibility of the communities and KCC to protect the wetlands."
Mugisha says the role of environmental bodies is to advise on how the wetlands should be managed, but at the moment, the priority is to remove the encroachers and stop fresh encroachment.
He adds: "Time and again, people living in wetland areas where marrum and waste dumping has taken place, have indicated that the dumping is done by unknown truck drivers at awkward hours."
In addition, the developers move in during holidays when the enforcement staff are not on duty.
So far, NEMA and the Wetlands Inspection Division organised meetings with the communities around Kinawataka and Nakivubo, warning wetland encroachers to vacate so that wetlands can be left to do their role.
While the environmental bodies are planning to restore the degraded parts of the wetlands, KCC is busy creating turmoil by offering more leases in the wetlands.
In parts of Kireka and Bugolobi, stories are abound that the swathes of Nakivubo and Kinawataka are being leased to developers. Most of the developers that have failed to get land outside the swamp are now competing with the yam cultivators in the swamp.
Sources revealed that a rich Somali business woman has "compensated" yam growers and brick-makers near Silver Springs Hotel to vacate the swamp.
"That part of the swamp may be no more by the end of the year," a source told The New Vision during a survey recently.
In Kampala district, most of the wetlands have been taken up for industrial and residential developments and this trend has not been halted.
The absence of housing schemes for the urban poor has complicated work for the environment agencies because the desperate poor people move into the wetlands.
The political support for the protection of the environment is fading. The country has many laws on the environment, but most of them have been shelved as politicians seek cheap popularity as they protect "their voters."
Over the years, there appears to be increasing activities being undertaken in wetlands in the name of fighting poverty.
While some of these activities are incompatible with wetland conservation, their promoters have vigorously defended them as intended to assist the fight against poverty.
For instance, brick-making in wetlands, which is done for economic gains, has tended to give no regard at all to wetland conservation or restoration.
"It is probable that this attitude stems from the old perception that wetlands are wasted land," Mugisha says.
He says preventing encroachment is better than evicting encroachers. But now the environmental bodies are in a maze and hampered by lack of funding to proceed with establishment of the reserves.
"We intend to evict all encroachers on wetlands in Kampala starting next month," says Mugisha.
"This is important because when the city wetlands are gone, the burden of meeting the swelling cost of cleaning water and treating diseases will rest on the shoulders of Kampala's residents."

Original_Search_Title: ANPP States May Hold Polls in January
Headline: Nigeria: ANPP States Hold LG Polls in January, Says Kumo
Body_Text:
Lagos — All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP), has said it is considering holding local government elections in states it controls in January.National Secretary of the party, Senator Sa'idu Kumo, said yesterday in Abuja that the proposal was to accommodate various interests. Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), had directed the 29 states under its comtrol to conduct the council polls in December.
Kumo said, "we (ANPP leadership) are considering holding the local government elections early January 2008, or on December 29, to give adequate time to states and aspirants to prepare."Our state governments, aspirants and candidates require sufficient time and logistics for the exercise. However, we will not just do it because others are holding theirs in December."According to him, annual religious and cultural festivities usually take place at the end of the year, there is, therefore, the need to consider the sensibilities of stakeholders.Kumo identified such festivals as fasting, Christmas, new yam festivals and pilgrimages as well as end-of-year family andcultural meetings. He said the proposal and exact date would be ratified by the party's NEC at a yet-to- be announced date.However, he advised state governors and contestants to prepare for the election ahead of its formal announcement.

Original_Search_Title: "We Want Citizens to Appreciate the Importance of Tourism"
Headline: Nigeria: "We Want Citizens to Appreciate the Importance of Tourism"
Body_Text:
Many might not like his style and some of the issues he had canvassed for in the last one year, but one thing that cannot be taken away from Otunba Olusegun Runsewe as the Director-General of the Nigerian Tourism Development Corporation (NTDC) is that he has capacity to thread where others would not.
The former Executive Director with the National Orientation Agency (NOA), came with a new slogan and promise, which was 'Practical Tourism.' Though many questioned the rationale behind the slogan, but today Runsewe admits that some strides have been made, stressing that the tourism scene has been busy with the parastatal notching up successes in a lot of unique areas.
Speaking during the week with Vanguard, Runsewe said "We have been able to do some unique things that have probably not happened before. We have been able to do a lot of things in anticipation of giving Nigeria a new tourism endorsement," he says, stressing on the need for the environment to be properly sanitised before one can talk of selling tourism.
"You cannot say you have a product to sell when you do not have the image to sell it. We are making sure that everything is in order and works well," contends the DG. In this regard, one of the very first things that NTDC did was to create a care centre at both the domestic and international wings of Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Abuja.
The centre is a care and information based one where every possible need of travelers is attended to even before they stepped out of the airport. Attempt was also made at cleaning up operation of the airport car hire service providers with avenue to purchase new cars opened up, office equipment provided for them and the airport premises given a new lease with the provision of mobile toilets and refuse bins.
This gesture was also extended to other areas of Abuja in line with the cleaning up by the FCT authorities. A new signage now dots the city courtesy of NTDC. The gospel of tourism was also taken to Nigerian schools with youth tourism and clubs introduced to many schools.
This, Runsewe says, was informed by the need to help the student population redirect their energies to gainful and meaningful venture. "We have been able to work on the student population for them to leave cultism and embrace tourism. That is why we have the Nigeria Youth Tourism Clubs and we have been able to put a tourism village in the schools to create a sense of belonging for the students."
At the states level, NTDC has built a fish house in Kebbi as a means of enhancing Argungun International Fishing Festival while in Anambra State, a Yam House was built to promote the celebration of the New Yam Festival in the eastern part of the country.
The corporation also engaged state governors in Anambra, Enugu, Ebonyi and Imo strategically to enlist their support in tourism development.
The chapter on hotel registration, grading and classification, which has been a problem for every Chief Executive Officer of NTDC, was re-opened too with a new level of commitment given by the Director General.
To ensure that it achieved the desired success, NTDC entered into partnership with a London based group, AA. Runsewe regards the deal as a major breakthrough for the country insisting that there is nothing wrong in working with a foreign group to effectively carry out the exercise.
In dousing the fears of the people, he informs that NTDC has not appointed the group to help grade hotels and other tourism related facilities in the country but rather to give international endorsement to it. "We need an endorsement. We are not grading the hotels for Nigerians alone but for the international audience.
"Our target is beyond Nigeria, so what we are saying is that what they (AA) should come and do is simply come and partner with us and that is all. They are not going to stay here and do the classification for us. It is just to partner so that the foreigners coming in would know that we are not far away from what they are doing in their countries."
Otunba Runsewe said that the registration exercise has commenced in Abuja as the corporation has deployed its field officers for the exercise in conjunction with the hotel owners Forum in Abuja.
He promised that the system would be fully proofed as the corporation has provided a 14-seater bus and computer set for the exercise even as he informed that e-registration of hotels is now possible. Some other issues canvassed by him include the provision of first aid boxes for hotels, taxi parks and other offices across the country; Nigeria and Abuja tourist maps; a guide on Nigeria hotels donation of toilets to markets and public places; and the production of Nigeria premier payment solution card in partnership with the United Bank for Africa (UBA).
Alongside these issues, that of training of the staff of the parastatal and their orientation has been taken to a new height with over 60 per cent of the work force so far retrained. The office environment has been improved with the Abuja office and the zonal offices transformed - made pleasant and captivating enough for visitors and the workers to operate in.
With the level of sanitisation already achieved, Runsewe believes that the ground has been tilled for people, particularly the private investors to do business with the body.
"The issue of private sector partnership is not that easy because you cannot go and bring somebody to come and invest such a huge amount of money without first preparing the ground. "Because the private investor wants some mileage from it. So what I have been able to do now is to stabilize the corporation and make it creditable for anybody to deal with. With what we have on ground now, I can assure you that there will be no problem. I cannot blame anybody for not coming to deal with us in the past but they have started coming, now. Everyone now wants to do business with NTDC because they can now see some - level of seriousness on our part."
The next agenda now, he says, is to see how domestic tourism can be re-invigorated. A number of programmes, he contends, are in the works. One of such is the 'Park and Ride for Abuja, a tour package that would take people to all the attact- ons in the capital city. At the States level, NTDC in partnership with the states governors, plan to build a motor park in each of the state and provide tour buses too.
Once these two have been done, we shall now get at least one destination in each of the states and compel the state develop it. If a state develops a destination, it reduces unemployment," says the DG even as he stressed that this would help in the creation of jobs at the states level.
If there is one thing that has troubled the tourism boss, it is the issue of operators in sector, who he says are not pulling their weights in tandem with the new move in the sector. The reason why NTDC has not been able to strike a deal with the operators, he says is because most of them are not legally registered as dictated by the laws.
"A lot of the operators are operating illegally and they are the ones giving Nigeria a bad image. Why on earth would they not follow the laid down guidelines of operation? Why would someone who is not known to NTDC call himself or herself an operator and then defraud people?" he queries.
On the thorny issue of promoting Nigeria and taking private operators to foreign fairs, Runsewe says, it is not the duty of the government to sponsor operators as it has been canvassed by the operators. "For international tours, they want us to pay. How can government pay to take you on international fairs? We can't, we shall be setting a bad precedent," he says.
Furthermore, he believes that the operators are not promoting Nigeria but their businesses and that what the government can do is only to assist them when there is need for it.
"They are not promoting Nigeria, they are promoting their pockets. What they need to do is to come to us for assistance. We are open to everyone, we want to help," says Runsewe.
He also decried their attitude and level of performance which he describes as very poor and discouraging. "They are not putting in enough. They cannot make money by sitting at home. They can only make money by exposing themselves." Runsewe admonishes the operators to embrace the changes going in the sector and partner with the corporation in developing tourism.
On the working relationship between government agencies and the corporation, the DG says he has enjoyed cordial relationship with them. However, he says things could be better, especially in the aviation sector. He advocates for a partnership with the Federal Airport Authority of Nigeria and other agencies in the sector to ensure that the airports work properly as they are the first point of contact for tourists coming to the country.
Looking ahead, Runsewe's appeal is for Nigerians to give the corporation a chance and to understand what tourism is about. "I just hope Nigerians will come to appreciate the importance of tourism as the only vehicle to alleviate poverty, to eradicate crime and reduce idleness among our youths."
On his expectation, he intones, "my expectation is that Nigeria becomes the most preferred tourist destination in Africa. With the infrastructure and with all the strategies we have put on ground, I believe that we shall achieve it." Perhaps what has given him happiness, he says, is because he has succeeded in selling tourism to his fellow country men. "I can tell you that we have succeeded in selling tourism to Nigerians and it is being accepted. That is one of my greatest joys."
The greatest challenge that he has faced in the last one year, he reveals is in trying to change the perception and orientation of the people. "It's a challenge working with people who have a fixed perception, people who are already used to a system. The challenge was in trying to convince them that what they were used to in the past was not good enough for us that we need to have a new focus. It has been very challenging but right now, it is being accepted. The challenge was worth its while?

Original_Search_Title: New Life for Displaced People
Headline: Nigeria: New Life for Displaced People
Body_Text:
Lagos — CHUKWUDI OBI toured the displaced person's camp in Ikosi-Ishieri area of Lagos state and came back with the story of a people who are enjoying a far higher standard of living than what they were used to before.
The signpost written in black colour against a white background reads:
Lagos State government (LASEMA) welcomes you all to the Temporary camp-site for the displaced people of Agiliti-Owode, Ajegunle, Mile 12 and Maidan-Orile communities of Ikosi-Ishieri Local Council Development Authority of the state.
At the gate, one witnesses the strong presence of armed policemen. Each person going in and out of the place is made to sign a register .
A walk around the school premises that serves as the temporary camp would not suggest anything near its usage. Children were seen playing around, women, in clusters 'gisting', some plaiting other women's hair and the men, also in clusters talking in whispers and thinking most of the time.
There were also spots where the children were immunized and vaccinated.
At the spot that served as medical unit of the camp, most of the people there were women backing their babies, single ladies and couple of teenage girls. The medical team comprised of qualified doctors and nurses seem very enthsiastic about their job and worked diligently.One of the medical doctors who spoke to Daily Champion, Dr. Afolabi Grillo disclosed that hypertension, malaria and gastro-enderitis were the predominant ailments the patients suffered from.
He further stated that about 40 to 50 patients come in daily adding that the supply of drugs in the unit is far below what the patients require.
Dr. Grillo while stating that government cannot cater for the displaced people alone, called for a private sector partnership.
"Private companies should wake up to their responsibility and help government do this. It is part of their corporate social responsibility. They should wake up and share resources with these displaced people", he said.
A Community Health Officer at Ikosi -Ishieri LCDA and a World Health Organisation (WHO) focal person Ms. Oluwatoyin Enigbokan posited that since the people came into the camp, there has not been any complicated case even as she stressed that they have been on ground at the camp since they were relocated to the school.
According to her, the Lagos State government provided majority of the drugs which they are dispensing to the people free of charge.
Her words:
"Most of these drugs here are very effective and expensive. They were provided by the Lagos State governor and we are giving it to the people who need it free of charge. We have been here since they came and there has not been any major medical case here. We are taking care of the pregnant women very well. UNICEF has immunized the children against killer diseases . We will continue working here till they leave".
She also added that the mosquito-treated nets helped to reduce the very many cases of malaria at the initial stage.
Within the over 10 days that these people relocated to the primary school, a female petty trader, among the displaced persons, Jamila Lateef, gave birth to a bouncing baby boy who they named Usman, at the naming ceremony .
According to Jamila whose husband is a vulcanizer, her fears knew no bounds as they experienced the flood. She expressed the convinction that God was on her side throughout the whole period till they were relocated to the primary school.
The petty trader who was observing siesta when Daily Champion called stressed that she had no complications during delivery.
Confirming this, Assistant Chief Nursing Officer of Ikosi-Ishieri Maternity clinic, Mrs. Helen Modupe Adeniyi noted that Jamila did not experience any for delivery complications saying that it was their expertise coupled with the grace of God that saw her through.
She disclosed that, the baby boy has been nicknamed 'Omisore' ( which means water has done a good thing to me), among other pet names.
The Catering manager, Jamil Kazeem Rebete took this reporter round the cooking section. Bags of rice, tubers of yam and other edibles where seen arranged in an area of the room while the deep freezer was stocked with condiments.
Speaking on the eating pattern in the camp, Rebete averred that the people, who are accommodated in ten rooms of between 50 and 60 in each room, ate, one room at a time.
"We issue tickets to one room and when that room finishes, the next room starts. We have 10 rooms here and we make sure that all of them eat well. Their lunch normally starts by 2 pm.", he said.
Among the people sitting under the tree, suporting his jaws with his hands, his thought far away is a lanky man who simply identified himself as Hakeem. He opined that the services obtainable at the camp was satisfactory but was quick to point out that being in one's home was the best alternative.
Hakeem, who has been in the camp for one week, stated that the camp management provided them with all the necessary things they needed, saying that they even have light in the camp more than they do when they where in Ajegunle.
Hakeem is not in this alone. Just in front of one of the rooms meant for the men, a cluster of men sat in small chairs none, talking to each other. The appearance of this reporter broke the silence and the men were very reluctant to open up. With a lot of smooth talk and sweet words, some were willing to speak.
One of such men was engineer turned man of God, Seun Adegbuyi.
Pastor Adegbuyi who is the General Overseer of Soldiers of Christ Evangelism Ministry International said that they are well taken care of in the camp but called on government to speed up actions to relocate them.
Hear him:
" We thank both the State government and the Local council development for taking good care of us. But you know that no place is better than home. That is why we are using this opportunity to appeal to Fashola to quickly do something before the children resume school."
The pastor noted that morning services, and night vigils are part of the activities that take his time in the camp.
Another man, a technician and father of five, Engr. Wahabi Ajibola Owoade's case is even worse.
According to him:
"In my mind, I am not really ok because I do not feel comfortable staying here for long. I am not free here. When your mind is not settled about your family and what tomorrow will be you are not comfortable."
"What I am saying is that while at home, some people will try to reach me but here nobody can reach me .I wanted to go out and do things for my customers but I cannot because some of the officers might come and pay us a visit. They want to see us in person. If they don't see us, they will not budget for us."
Continuing, Engr Wahabi highlighted that he had sent two of his children to stay with some of his relations while the remaining three and his wife were in the camp with him.
A trader and pregnant woman, Mrs. Tosin Adenike enjoined government to help them find accommodation even as she added that most of them were in dilemma owing to the state government's bid to sanitize that area.
Earlier in a chat, Camp Commandant, Adebayo Toafiq noted that the camp houses over 400 displaced people in the flood of August 3 and that the camp has all it takes to take adequate care of all of them.
He further noted that UNICEF, Vivian Fowler Memorial School and some women organisations had paid visits to the camp.
Daily Champion gathered that the state government and Ikosi-Isheri local council development area were concluding moves to equip the displaced people with vocational skills, Taofiq noted that the camp also had a 10 KVA generator and an increased water supply that will help run the place efficiently.
According to him, the quick intervention of the state and local council governments saved the situation a lot.
The camp commandant who stated that everything has been provided for the displaced people noted that even malaria treated nets, food items and other toiletries have also been provided for their upkeep.

Original_Search_Title: The Untold Story of My Life - Julie Coker
Headline: Nigeria: The Untold Story of My Life - Julie Coker
Body_Text:
Lagos — Some people were born great.
Others achieved greatness. But every great man or woman has a history of greatness. And for Julie Coker, her story of greatness did not start the day she became a TV star.
It started at the age of fourteen when she set out from Lagos to Delta State in search of her mother who had taken ill over a year and had relocated to the village for recovery. Julie was unfortunate to be married off to an old man without her sick mother's knowledge. But as she sat shivering in her opulent home of the man they married her off to, 'an angel' came in the person of her mother's younger sister who stole her out of the marriage and sent her straight back to Lagos in a ship. And at Lagos, the nuns in the convent came to her rescue, helped her get a scholarship to complete her education and paved way for her greatness in her career.
The Beginning
I was brought to Lagos by my mother when I was eighteen months old. I had a balanced childhood from the time I was six and started school at St. Mary's Convent School. I was six years old and that was in 1946, Broad Street Lagos. The school is still standing there. I think I was more like a child prodigy because I learnt my poetry very quickly. I was very good in English and very poor in Maths. I always scored zero in arithmetic and it was discovered by the nuns. If we had a theatre then, I probably would have acted in some plays because whenever they had a play, I was there. If it was an opera, I was always the first in the list. When the school participated in competitions at the festival of arts level, I was always representing the school. This was a primary school. It was alright for me to go in at age 7, 8 or 9. By the time I was eleven, I went in for the Festival of Arts competition and I came second, behind an English boy.
The nuns were very mad at me because they thought I did very well. One of the judges, lady Kofoworola Abayomi said I would have won the Best Performer prize but when I finished reciting my poem, I was so excited with my good performance that I left the stage without courtesying. I was able to deliver the lines exactly like an English man. The judges were so excited that a black girl was reciting poetry so eloquently and she said, oh, Juliana Coker, pupil from St. Mary's Convent School scored so many points but she failed to score some points because she forgot to courtesy at the end. The English boy who came from Corona School scored some points because he gave a very deep bow. The nuns were English and they knew I made a grievous error. Back in school, I was flogged. And that taught me to be very conscious in whatever I did. Instead of trophy, I came with just a certificate. I learnt to always tidy up the rough edges and do things properly.
So, when it was time for me to go to Holy College, I went in with the same confidence. There was another nun there. Mother Mary Magdalene. The nun was very curious that I scored zero in Maths for the Common Entrance. I scored the highest mark in English. So, she sent for me to retake the Maths. She was thinking that maybe, I didn't have the correct materials to use.
But when I got there, I told her that I forgot my pencil and paper at home. The way I spoke, with confidence, 'I left my paper' and she was fascinated and she said, wow! I'll take you and I will see how well you do. The first year in school, I came first in the class. Despite my poor performance in Maths, I scored very well in other subjects and had the best average that placed me topmost in the class. That was my first year at Holy Child.
Search for my mother
It was during that period at Holy Child that my mother took ill. She had been ill for sometime, since I was eight years old. I was her only child. I grew up in Lagos and I knew only the Lagos Island. I didn't even know Apapa. I went to church in Elegbata. My school was beyond the Supreme Court, this big imposing building which was pulled down and we now have the fountain which never worked. There used to be beautiful structures there but later on they were pulled down for no just cause. But it didn't affect my growing up because we took everything in our stride. All we needed to do was go to school but we knew that something was happening in the country.
We sang God save our gracious queen. But we knew people like Azikwe were already agitating for our independence. We didn't sing Nigeria we hail thee. I never knew why we changed to Arise O compatriots. It was as if we were down and need to arise. We would have held our heads high if we still sing Nigeria we hail thee. I always have this feeling that the present anthem has an effect on us, as if we are dancing backward or something. But that's by the way. I was talking about my mother.
Before her illness, my mother got a job in the school where I was but she became too ill to continue and she went to stay in the village. I'd never been to that village and didn't know where it was, she left me in the care of an aunt. Before then, we lived in a big compound run by the pastor of my mother's church. My stepfather was is brother-in-law. There were many relations living there. It was like a community. I was the only child and my mother didn't even had a child for my stepfather. I was really spoilt. My stepfather was an organist in the church. I sang well in the church choir.
His was an Anglican church. I also sang in the Catholic choir. I was attending two different churches. That added to the talent that was already innate in me. I was always striving to be on top of everything and my stepfather always put me in front to show other people that I could sing better. And I knew a lot of Catholic and Anglican songs and that was like a springboard into the life that I was being prepared for.
But when my mother wasn't working and had gone back to the village, there was no money to pay my school fee. It was then that I decided to go and search for my mother. I didn't run away but my great aunt with whom I was staying when my mother took ill wanted to adopt me but I told her I was going to search for my mother. I didn't reject her love but I missed my mother. So, I left her. It was at Igbosere street then until my friends started picking me in their Jaguar to school. They were Darocha Afodo family and my friends were their grandchildren. It was my first time of living a sophisticated life, you know, going to school in a Jaguar. My friends were twins. The boy was at St. Gregory and the girl was my school mother. They made live in a luxury I could not afford.
Going to look for my mother was like an adventure. I didn't know her village but I knew it was not far from Sapele. I got on the boat and it took us four days and three nights to get there. I passed my mother's village without realising it. I was like looking for a needle in a haystack or going on a wild-goose chase. I didn't know where to locate my mother but I knew I could search and fund her. The only fear I had then was that she might have died because I didn't hear from her for over a year. No communication. No letters and that left me high and dry I told myself I must find her even if she was dead.
When I got to Sapele, nobody knew her but somebody said, if you are talking about Ureju, there might be a boat. I had to wait till the following day to be put in a boat going back to Lagos but would stop over at the village. When we got there it was night and the boat stopped mid-stream for the villagers to come and fetch their passengers. So, they shouted from the middle of ocean for the villagers to bring a canoe to pick their passengers. The canoe came but nobody knew me. I had no money and no food but was fed by public spirited people. I was fourteen and alone. But I must have been good to other people because I could read their letters and spoke Yoruba to them. But I had a goal, I had a purpose.
But as the villagers came, they said they didn't know any Julie from Lagos. I had no native name. So, I said Julie Coker. People thought I adopted the name Coker but my father came partly Sierra-Leonean, partly the Coker family in Abeokuta. But by the grace of God, I could speak Yoruba to communicate with my father's family but I was not living with them.
Eventually, at my mother's village, somebody raised a lantern to my face and said I had the face of my grandmother. So, I got in the canoe and was paddled to the village. It was the first time I was going to stay in a village in my entire life. It was a different world. There was no light but I had learnt to take a lot of shocks in life. But I didn't see my mother when I got there. Even though I didn't know the people, I stayed with them thinking my mother had died and they didn't want to tell me. Nobody was talking about her. So, I was expecting that later they would take me to her grace and I would cry a little bit or somebody would come and sympathise with me over her death but they said nothing.
My grandmother was still alive and I knew then I was in the right place. She was very old and very kind. I also saw my mother's younger brother who came to Lagos before and saw me and called my name. I calmed down and they scolded me for taking a long journey alone to the village in the ship. I didn't have money that could pay back for a trip back to Lagos but I knew before I left Lagos, I asked God to take me to my mother wherever she might be and I think God just did that because I didn't know where to find her but I found her. It was God that did it and took control. It was not funny being amongst my people and they not knowing me. I kept looking for one familiar face until I saw my grandmother and knew I looked like her and then, there was my uncle.
In the morning, my cousins took me to fishing because it was from the fish that they would make breakfast of pepper-soup and yam. So, I went fishing with the family. I almost forgot my sorrows. I was to sheepish and afraid to ask about my mother. But later on, I mustered courage and asked after her.
Meeting my mother again
I asked after my mother in Itsekiri language. That was after we had had breakfast and gone further in the sea to fetch drinking water. They said they would take me to see her and they did. My mother was very, very weak because she wasn't getting the proper medication she needed. They wanted to take her to a native doctor but she would not allow them because her faith forbade her. She had accepted Christ when I was six and was always reading her bible. She was doing same in the village and they were making fun of her and the bible. They were not Christians and, even till date, a lot of them are not Christians and that is why I love to go to that village to evangelise. I do that from time to time whenever I'm in Warri or Sapele. I usually went with other evangelists because I could not do the job alone. I'm very proud of sticking out my neck for my mother and that search experience made me fearless.
Eventually, I came into the warm embrace of my mother, even though she fell down a couple of times. She was not strong. I refused to return to the house with my cousins. I stayed with my mother, prayed with her and sang with her. She was going through a lot of trauma because of the rejection she suffered from her people. She refused to be part of the morning divination to the local deity. It's part of their routine and they expected her to sit down with them and plead with the deity to heal her. My mother refused that and held unto her bible and they thought she was mad and ostracised her.
But when I came back and joined her in reading the bible and singing hymns, they said, okay, she was not mad afterall. She told them she had discovered the one true God and would serve Him alone. Even though my mother had TB and did not take any medication, she got healed by reading the bible and singing hymns. I was doing that with her, singing Praise my soul, the King of Heaven, Give thanks to the Lord for He is good and His love endureth forever. So, the people were surprised. They couldn't read and they thought since my mother could read, it was pride that kept her from joining the divinations to find out what was in the offing for the day. They wanted to find out what was wrong with her through divination but she refused to cooperate with them and held onto her bible. But my mother did not die.
My childhood marriage
I started in my mother's village for sometime and then it was time to go back to school and I asked for school fees. I knew my mother had no money because what she saved had been expended. She was still ill but stronger and I stayed on and showed her love. I can tell you, love can heal but the sickness really affected her income. Here was a woman doing so many things. She could sow, weave, bake, cook and do many things but all that had gone.
My stepfather had remarried and had other children. I'm still close to his children. When I asked my uncle for school fee, he didn't have enough. He was a councillor, duly elected but didn't have to pay my fee at Holy Child College and take care of my academic needs. At the end of the day, he went to my grandmother to augment whatever I had raised. It still wasn't enough. At the end of the day, my mother's aunty, that was my great-aunty, came to the rescue and she asked me to follow her to Sapele from where I could go back to Lagos by road.
At the end of the day, it turned out that aunty had made plans for me to be married off to a rich man in Sapele. I had no knowledge. But, instead, I saw myself being taken to a storey building, very beautiful one and there, this huge man with very imposing figure. He had other wives and wanted another one and I was given to him at age 14: I learnt he paid a huge bride price for me because I had standard six and was in secondary school. I was too frightened and I sat there shivering, he walked into the room and I was alone. My grand aunt had disappeared.
But an 'angel' came in form of my mother's younger sister who grabbed my hand and ran. She told me that they heard what happened and sent out a search party and she was lucky she headed to that direction. So, my mother's younger sister took me out of the place and went into the town to look for more money from members of the family who knew the importance of girls education at that time. She was not educated but she wanted me to get educated. She hid me somewhere in the village and the next morning, she took me to the garage, paid my fare, gave me some money and sent me back to Lagos. Back to Lagos, I went back to school.
My scholarship
The nuns knew that something was wrong when I got back to school. The principal of the school, Rev. Mother Mary Magdalene, now took me to the scholarship board at the old secretariat in Lagos, I went before the board and was interviewed after which I got a scholarship to complete my education. I will always thank God for all He did for me. Even though I passed through the valley of shadow of death, no evil befell me. I was able to complete my education and had a certificate that helped me get a job in the Convent School in Warri.
When I was in class four, a photographer from Daily Times took my photo and published it. That prepared me for the Miss Western Nigeria competition. I won the competition, came to Lagos and that became my ticket to Spain. That was also how I got my ticked to television.
