I couldn't become a Rev Sister because I was in entertainment and couldn't live a secluded or holy life. I was singing, acting and dancing. I was exposed even by the nuns themselves. So, all the razzmatazz of stage craft actually pointed me to the right direction. The nuns knew that if you have a good voice, you could become a singer or broadcaster. Princess Grace Kelly of Monaco went to a Convent School and passed through the training that took her to Hollywood and she kept going back to the Convent School for training, not to become a nun but because the Convent School was like a finishing school. They train you for all kind of things: domestic science, catering, becoming a housewife or anything you want to be. That was how I started off and become somebody in life.

Original_Search_Title: 'Nekan Ademola - a Great Man From a Great Family
Headline: Nigeria: 'Nekan Ademola - a Great Man From a Great Family
Body_Text:
Lagos — RETIRED JUSTICE JIDE OLATAWURA remembers Justice Adenekan Ademola
Chief Adenekan Adekunle Ademola was called to the Bar in the Middle Temple, London. He enrolled in the Supreme Court of Nigeria on Saturday, August 15, 1953. It was after his enrolment that he started his practice in the Chambers of Chief H.O. Davies. After his enrolment, he started appearing in Ibadan in the Supreme Court, Ibadan (now High Court of Justice Ibadan). I was the Clerk of Court in Ibadan.
He later moved to Ibadan. He entered into partnership with Chief Ayodele Okusaga. The Chambers - Okusaga and Ademola, Solicitors, popularly known as Gbagi Chambers - was located at 23 Lebanon Street, Ibadan. The two partners were hardworking and they appeared regularly in court. When Hon. Justice Myles John Abbot was transferred from Ibadan, Ibadan had the good fortune of having Sir Adetokunbo Ademola as the Chief Justice of Western Region. I was His Lordship's Clerk of Court. Mr. O.O. Sobodu was my Senior in the Court. He was the Registrar of Session.
Chief Adenekan Ademola appeared regularly in our Court. A handsome young man popularly referred to by his colleagues as "Prince Charming". I resigned from the Judiciary and I went abroad for my Law Studies. It was in 1959 I wrote him to find out if I could join their Chambers. He replied that I should come after my qualification. After my enrolment, I went straight to his Chambers. As at that time, Chief Okusaga was very busy with politics, consequently, Chief Adenekan Ademola was the only lawyer in the Chambers. In 1962, their partnership was dissolved, and he moved into 38, New Court Road. It was when we moved there that he invited me to form a partnership and that we should have a law firm in the name of ADEMOLA AND OLATAWURA. I accepted the offer. That was the beginning of the relationship. Our firm had so many retainers: Mobil Oil, Total Oil, University of Ibadan and University College Hospital, N.K. Zard and many others.
He had complete confidence in me and by the time we opened our Accounts with the Banks we were both signatories. I was completely surprised. I thank God I did not abuse the trust.
Justice Adenekan Ademola was a visionary leader. He made sure that, relying on my experience in the court as a Clerk of Court, I went to court often. Our partnership was dissolved as a result of his appointment as a High Court Judge on 16th June, 1970.
He was a man with a large heart. He was generous, dependable and hardworking. Despite his family background - a man from the Royal family of Abeokuta and the position of his father as the Chief Justice of Nigeria, he displayed that uncommon trait of simplicity, humility and love not easily found in people with the same background.
Quite apart from his brilliance at the bar, he conducted himself as if he had no edge over those from poor family background.
His friends fondly called him 'the Great Neks' and as said earlier, "Prince Charming".
Those of us who passed through his Chambers respected him for his brilliance and unparallelled respect for his juniors. He never lost his temper. His deep interest in his juniors was their fortune. I was completely surprised when he telephoned me from his house and I punctuated our conversation with 'Alright sir, OK sir"; he said suddenly, "Jide, I cannot remember when I was knighted".
His generosity knew no bounds, moreso at the end of the year. "Jide I have drawn some amount for Christmas, please feel free to sign the cheque for the Christmas'. I signed the cheque and two days later he phoned to ask, "Will that amount be enough for your pounded yam and bush meat at Ikole?". He was a wonderful man, caring and friendly.
A client from Denmark phoned him to come to Copenhagen in respect of an urgent matter and that all expenses would be paid. He immediately told him that he would send his partner in the office. I went and spent five days there.
The Judges respected our Chambers for industry. He made sure we placed order for the complete set of All England Law Reports and contributed towards the daily supply of "LONDON TIMES" because of the Law Reports therein.
He was appointed a Justice of the Federal Court of Appeal (later called Court of Appeal) on June 15, 1977 and was posted to Kaduna/Enugu. As at the time I was appointed to the Court of Appeal in October 1980, and posted to Enugu, he was then in Kaduna. He came back from Kaduna, packed his belongings and asked me to move into his official quarters. I was lodged in Hotel Presidential, Enugu. It is unbelievable, but it happened. He transferred his official driver and police orderly to me. He was really a brother. He was the first person to telephone me on my appointment to the Supreme Court. I regard myself as a member of the family ever before we entered into partnership because Sir Adetokunbo was keenly interested in my progress. He was my mentor.
Now, he is no more, but he has left behind an imperishable legacy of judges who passed through his chambers: Late Justice C.A. Abimbola, late Justice Owolabi Kolawole, Justice Ademola Ajakaiye who was once the Chief Judge of Ekiti State and now His Royal Highness, the Oluyin of Iyin-Ekiti, Hon. Justice Olukayode Somolu and my humble self. True, he is no more but he will live for ever not only in the hearts of those who worked with him but as well as those who knew him. His activities at the Ibadan Bar bore testimony to his youthful activities in King's College, Lagos.
He is blessed with a loving and caring wife and children who should be the pride of any father. May his ever cheerful soul rest in peace. Adieu, the great lover of all that any honest man works for. A gentleman from a great family.
- Justice Olatawura is a retired Justice of the Supreme Court

Original_Search_Title: Hello, GSM And NCC!
Headline: Nigeria: Hello, GSM And NCC!
Body_Text:
The Global System Mobil telephone, GSM better known as mobile or cell phone, has taken Nigeria by storm. From the fledgling beginnings of nought-nine-nought when the system was only affordable by the rich, it now has Nigerians from all walks of life as subscribers.
The mobile phone has indeed ceased to be a status symbol and has become a handy appliance making communication in a digital world more available, if not necessarily affordable.
Businesses and transactions which previously took weeks or days can now be carried out instantly. The carriers love the instant hit, the dependence on their networks for calls and the uniquely Nigerian habit of long telephone calls. These factors keep the naira rolling into their bank accounts at digital speed.
That GSM has caught on like a harmattan inferno may be seen in the ubiquity of the advertisements: watch the bright colours, the tempting offers and sleazy faces on the billboards. Then there are also recharge card vendors at virtually every street corner and community. Before now, a popular soft drink was the symbol of modernity; this enviable status now has to be shared with the GSM and its accessories. This is perhaps why some younger folks once called it General Street Madness, a status complete with its language. Now there are expressions like "migrate" even when one remains in one location. Subscribers are also able to "flash" each other as if they were in a contest with the Sango, the famed god of thunder.
The huge number of subscribers within just six years must count among the seven wonders of the business world. According to Engineer Ernest Ndukwe, Executive Vice Chairman of the regulatory body, the National Communications Commission, NCC, a whopping 97 percent of telephones in Nigeria are now GSM. I know of no other country where the GSM is apparently so popular or is so much relied upon for telephony. But how exactly did this roaring success come about?
The alternative before GSM was NITEL, now called land line. By the time GSM made a debut, Nigerians had had it with NITEL. It was near impossible to get a new line because the process entailed enough paper work to intimate even a permanent secretary. Then there were also itemised costs of various cables and sockets which amounted to hefty sums depending on the distance from the nearest serving point. There were also noisy lines that usually went dead and resurrected without notice.
Dealing with NITEL maintenance crew was even a completely different matter for which one needed large reservoir of patience. To follow subscribers to clear a fault, palms often had to be greased and their ladder transported to and from the site. The billing system was perhaps a sneak preview of ENRON type accounting system which conceals more than it reveals. A lump sum is indicated but without stating numbers called, when and how long the conversation lasted. Appeals for review characteristically fell on deaf ears and before you can say hello, the line gets tossed.
It was that bad but its monopoly as the only provider of telephone ensured it stayed in business. It did not seem that NITEL appreciated it was in a market, much like the sellers of spare parts, yams or any commodity. The difference is one sold tangible commodities and the other sold service. NITEL has now probably learned a hard lesson, if only a bit too late.
GSM was therefore a welcome relief. In place of the long drawn process of getting a line, SIM cards could be picked up and pronto, the line is in service. Rather than haggling over bills, the pay-as-you-go (which as we shall see has its problems) seemed more transparent. In a word, NITEL's big gaps were filled by the GSM operators. This is probably why it is celebrated as part of the success stories of the much-touted reforms. The argument is that breaking NITEL's monopoly facilitated the emergence of GSM miracle. The GSM carriers seem to have capitalised on their positions as the only reliable sources of telephony to give Nigerians a raw deal.
First, it was the question of billing which was in minutes rather than seconds. At that time, a call of say 30 or 40 seconds was rounded to one minute at the subscriber's expense. Now the system is so (over)crowded that it is difficult to make a call. At the recent hearing in the Senate and House of Representatives, there was a common complaint that the rate of drop calls (better yet, failed calls) has increased to the chagrin and embarrassment of subscribers. The major networks routinely send out several messages to explain away their poor performance.
Often, such messages raise more questions than answers. When you dial and receive the message "Network busy", were you supposed to know when it is not? They often suggest one should try later, but when later exactly? Or when they indicate "Error in Connection", the subscriber knows that since the call was made like others which went through, the error could only have come from the providers. To worsen the situation, subscribers are told the "number is not available" when it actually is. I have crosschecked this several times either with the other phone or a friend's and either way, the message erroneously indicates that available numbers are not available. Many times, the entire network disappears only to reappear several hours-in some locations days-later in a telling irony of how far away- and near- Nigeria's telephony is from NITEL. Many times, it is impossible to load credit or check the balance.
The itemization of poor services is a long list. Sometimes, you connect but the other party is completely inaudible. At times, the phone sounds like the horn of the locomotive train with the characteristic fon-fon sound. Many times, rather than transmit, your own voice bounces back in a loud echo. While the glitches go on, the bills are reading. Why do the carriers make one pay for services not rendered such as voices bouncing back or completely inaudible? These are some of the complaints about which the National Assembly wanted a stop. The two Houses directed the regulatory agencies, including the NCC, to be more alert in protecting Nigeria and subscribers.
So far, little has changed since the directive went out over a month ago. Matters are not helped by some of the statements of Ernest Ndukwe who now comes across more like a staff of the Public Relations Department of the carriers than a member of a regulatory agency for the government and people of Nigeria. In responding to the complaints, he alleged that the problem was due to poor infrastructure in Nigeria. He was quoted as saying, "... in our peculiar case, generators have become the main source of power supply with the power utility on standby and in some cases never available" (Sunday Vanguard, June 8, page 7).
Few would vouch for the reliability of the public power supply but the limitations of infrastructure are apparently on both sides. How, for example, does public power supply translate to voices that bounce back or are inaudible? Is the unreliable power supply why the network is frequently said to be busy? To complete the seeming PR briefing for the carriers, Engineer Ndukwe provided figures of the major carriers' infrastructural development plan. He said Celtel has budgeted (as opposed to released or invested) $1.4b within the next 12 months; MTN's budget is $1 billion and Globacom's is $500m, "all in a bid to ensure that the quality of service improves" (Thisday, July 26, 2007, page 54).
It all sounds impressive except that the NCC executive vice chairman was silent on protecting subscribers. What should subscribers do when they are charged for calls that connect but the party cannot be heard? What about the "error" in connection? In many instances, sections of an SMS text would be missing in transmission. Who really will speak up for fleecing of subscribers being perpetrated by the GSM carriers?
The point is that we have listened to Engineer Ndukwe ad nuseam about the plan -and the apparent good intentions-of the GSM carriers. He is, so far, either silent on protecting subscribers or has not deployed the same zeal in speaking about this. The engineer who has the investment plans of the major carriers at his fingertips should also tell Nigerians who he is paid to protect about the carrying capacity of each of the networks.
At the present level of their equipment, (part of infrastructure?) what is the carrying capacity? Pointedly, how many lines can they effectively carry and how many are they carrying? They even offer all kinds of specials such as free calls on weekends or at odd hours but often, the networks are overwhelmed most of the special periods. Is this something that Nigerians should be protected from or should we keep our fingers crossed and, in the words of the Catholic liturgy, wait in the joyful hope for the announced infrastructural investments to mature?
There is no doubt about the benefits of GSM to subscribers. I have heard of neighbourhood watches in these times of insecurity where people use the mobile to contact each other about any intrusions. Many stranded motorists, and passengers too, are able to reach family and friends back home and/or call technicians for help. Some of the banks now inform customers almost instantly when there are any transactions in their accounts. One could even send and receive money via the mobile as part of the emerging banking on the move. Through special facilities, it is possible to send and receive email as well as news from the major radio, TV and wire services. Someone enthused that GSM is literally providing the digital world at one's fingertips. But without effective regulatory mechanism, these advantages could also open up the country to exploitation.
The fear is made more real by the dominance of foreign interests in GSM; all but one of the major carriers is foreign. Indeed, at inception, virtually everything from the equipment down to the recharge cards was imported to drain foreign exchange. It took some determination to compel them to produce recharge cards in Nigeria. So far, transfer of GSM technology has hardly gone beyond the local production of recharge cards as the major pieces of equipment and their components continue to be imported. Engineer Ndukwe did not say how much local content there is to any of the infrastructural budgets. At the moment, little has been heard from NCC about transfer of technology or what subscribers should do when they are fleeced.
When next one makes a call and cannot hear the other party but is billed, what should s/he do? Given the heavy reliance on the GSM, the amounts involved in billing for services not received could only be in the millions. Does this not bother the NCC enough to warrant some statement? There is an Idoma proverb that God made the nose to guard the mouth from odours. In this case, why does foul odour continue to assault the mouth in spite of a functioning nose? Perhaps, there would be some difference if more attention were paid to protecting subscribers rather than the current semblance of talking on behalf of the major GSM carriers.
Alubo is Professor of Sociology in the University of Jos, Plateau State.

Original_Search_Title: Blocking Roads, Blocking Peace
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Blocking Roads, Blocking Peace
Body_Text:
Bouake — Harassment, extortion and physical assault are rampant at Côte d'Ivoire's ubiquitous roadblocks and unless stopped the scourge will make true peace and stability impossible, Ivorians say.
"The economic damage [of these roadblocks] is bad enough," Ivorian Chamber of Commerce President Jean-Louis Billon told IRIN from the commercial capital, Abidjan. "But the most revolting part of this is the human rights violations. There are invasive body searches, including of women. We heard of cases of rape." He added: "And this, from those the citizens should be able to count on for security."
Roadblocks have long been a fact of life in Côte d'Ivoire, where comedians and musicians have made fun of the gendarme who draws a few extra bribes from citizens as the weekend approaches. But the phenomenon has exploded in the five years of unrest gripping the country since a botched coup in 2002, with both government forces and rebels seeing the transport circuit as a lucrative enterprise.
Billon says he has travelled some 10,000km throughout the country by car in the past few months. After his most recent tour in late July he wrote a letter to several government ministers, including defence, interior, justice and human rights, calling for an end to abuse and extortion by security forces on the roads. "This racket contributes to the destruction of social cohesion and the impoverishment of already destitute populations," the 31 July letter says. "These barriers to the free movement of people and goods... slow down our economy and stifle trade within the country."
Billon and others contacted by IRIN said the problem existed in both the rebel-held north and government-controlled south. Billon said: "On the government side, there is such dysfunction and lawlessness - it's truly revolting."
Ivorian political leaders on 30 July proclaimed at an arms-burning ceremony, "the war is over". But Billon said hauliers, merchants and anyone else who confronts extortion and harassment on the roads has not yet known peace. "We say, 'peace has arrived' - OK, but people's everyday living conditions must change. We will truly have peace in Côte d'Ivoire when we can move about freely." He said the problem hits war-displaced families particularly hard, complicating their already tough task of returning home.
In his speech at the 30 July ceremony in the former-rebel headquarters, Bouake, rebel leader turned Prime Minister Guillaume Soro said: "Peace has arrived. Wherever you are in Côte d'Ivoire from now on you can circulate freely."
"The height of humiliation"
Samuel Koffi, 45, told IRIN his most fervent wish was to be able to do just that. "They can hold whatever ceremony they want. All I care about is being able to travel throughout my own country without facing this unbelievable abuse on the roads."
Koffi, who lives in the northwestern town of Odienne, recently travelled to his home town in central Côte d'Ivoire for his father's funeral. It was his first time making the trip from rebel-held to government territory since before the conflict. "It's the height of humiliation," he said. "The way these officers shake us down for money and abuse people - and it's all done as if it's completely normal. Nothing is hidden."
He spent 7,000 CFA francs (about US$15) just on bribes at roadblocks - at least several days' work driving his taxi in Odienne. "These days, I could even go a week without making that." At one point he was running out of money and he pleaded with a gendarme. "I told him, 'Please - I'm travelling to my home village for my Dad's burial.' The gendarme shrugged and said, 'I don't give a damn.' All he cared about was getting money from me."
...The political leaders mock our suffering...
Koffi said he heard government forces at roadblocks harassing young women on the southbound bus, saying, "You're up there sleeping around with rebels - now you come down here to bother us."
"Utter mockery" is how Koffi saw the 30 July Bouake peace ceremony. "The political leaders mock our suffering," he said. "They all know full well what's going on." Asked what it would take to put an end to abuses by security forces on the roads, he said, "Political will - period. No miracles needed here."
Ghosts from the past
Many say the hateful and pitiless behaviour on the part of those manning roadblocks mirrors the very kinds of social division and abuse that were integral to the causes of Côte d'Ivoire's rebellion. One factor that gave rise to the rebellion was northerners' exasperation over what they called blatant discrimination and abuse on the part of government security forces. At roadblocks people with names from northern ethnic groups would be singled out and made to pay bribes.
"Now," Koffi said, "Absolutely no matter what papers you have, no matter what your name or ethnicity is, you're going to suffer this humiliation and extortion."
Chamber of Commerce President Billon said he worried this problem would sow trouble at exactly the time Côte d'Ivoire needs stability most. "This problem weakens the social fabric; this must be fixed if we really want peace."
Economic burden
Social cohesion aside, the racket on Côte d'Ivoire's roads continues to batter families economically.
The extra tariffs hauliers must pay on the roads force up the prices of goods. "Five years ago, three yams [a staple in Côte d'Ivoire] cost between 300 and 500 CFA francs ($US0.62 to 1.04). Today, they cost about double that," said Aly Cisse, who sells yams in a Bouake market. The price of most items in the market has gone up by 30 to 50 percent, he said.
A commercial truck driver told IRIN despite talk of peace, people in his profession are increasingly facing a burden on the roads. "We were told that when things started to normalise in the country, we would no longer be victims of this extortion," Dramane Dante told IRIN. "But in reality, things are only getting worse."
Officials with the government and former rebel forces say things are getting better. "This racketeering is certainly a reality," military prosecutor Ange Kessi Kouame told IRIN. "But we're committed to fighting this." He said things are starting to improve, and added: "Especially since it ruins the economy, we must fight this to the end."
Commander Losseni Fofana of the former rebel New Forces said his forces provided passes for vehicles to travel throughout the north. At the road barriers, he said, "there are formalities to take care of". He added: "If this racket still exists, these are isolated acts that we will fight."
"Isolated acts" is not how Billon sees it. "There is too much dysfunction that persists in Côte d'Ivoire. These roadblocks are just one example of the impunity and all that a citizen experiences in a lawless state."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Pilgrims From the Diaspora Welcomed At Elmina
Headline: Ghana: Pilgrims From the Diaspora Welcomed At Elmina
Body_Text:
Several Africans from the Diaspora, who undertook a pilgrimage to the Northern sector of the country to trace the former slave routes as part of PANAFEST/Emancipation day celebration, were on Friday welcomed at a solemn ceremony at the forecourt of the Elmina castle.
At the ceremony dubbed the 'Akwaaba' ceremony, the visitors, many of them African-Americans, were given the traditional local dish of mashed yam as well as palm wine to drink.
This took place after libation had been offered to the gods for their safe arrival, amidst traditional drumming and dancing and acrobatic display.
The 'pilgrims' in turn, hugged Nana Kodwo Conduah VI, Omanhene of the Edina Traditional Area and other traditional rulers and the District Chief Executive of Komenda-Edina-Eguafo-Abirim.
Dr Leonard Jeffries, a leading member of the Africans in the Diaspora, described the ceremony, as a replicate of a re-union, when family members come together, during occasions, amidst drumming and dancing, offering of libation and performance of other rituals.
He said the theme for the festival, "re-uniting the African family," was very significant and that it dawned on everybody to re-unite Africans in the Diaspora and their brothers and sisters at home.
Dr Jefferies said there was no doubt that Africans in the Diaspora and those on the continent were one family and it was therefore, necessary that these two groups pooled their resources towards the development of Africa.
He stressed the need for those in the Diaspora to come back to Africa and invest in areas like industry and health care delivery, for accelerate the development.
Mr Asmah briefed the visitors on "the Elmina 2015 Strategy." a development programme aimed at enhancing socio-economic conditions in the area.
He urged them to return to their roots and invest towards the socio-economic development of the country.
Mr. Asmah said the 2015 strategy aimed at developing tourism and education in the district, saying, the strategy opened, "the flood gates to our brothers and sisters" in to come to the country.
He said the presences of Africans in the Diaspora reminded Africans "with pain, how their ancestors were stolen from their birthplace."
Nana Conduah expressed satisfaction that the Diasporans were returning to their roots and noted that as people with one destiny, it was imperative that they came together to champion a new course for Africa.
He urged the Africans in the Diaspora to avail themselves of the dual citizenship introduced by government to settle in the country to help in its development.
Nana Conduah gave the assurance that traditional rulers were in the area were ready to offer land for investment to any African in the Diaspora.

Original_Search_Title: What 'Nigerwives' Love About Nigerian Men
Headline: Nigeria: What 'Nigerwives' Love About Nigerian Men
Body_Text:
Lagos — Everywoman dreams of a full, rewarding and a happy life with the man of her choice. But just how far are they willing to go for love?
It's been said time and again that love makes the world go round; and women, particularly, follow the dictates of their heart. Whereever the drum beats of love sounds, there, they will go; even to the ends of the earth.
Nseobong Okon-Ekong enters into the world of some women from other nations who are married to Nigerians. It is an exciting discovery that shows a mini united nations in Nigeria.
Kudos to our Nigerian men who have shown that when they travel, it is not just for the proverbial golden fleece or greener pastures, they are not at all averse to matters of the heart.
Do the women find our men interesting? Alright, we are talking about marriage here; and it is for better, for worse; till death....So these women, originally from China, India, United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Phillipines, Ghana, Jamaica and South Africa to mention a few have formed a union called, Niger Wives Association. There are chapters in every state, with the national secretariat at the premises of King's College, Adeyemo Alakija Street, Victoria Island, Lagos. Every first Saturday of the month, they meet at the Corona School in Ikoyi to discuss common interests.
These women have been a mighty blessing, not only to the families that they are married into, but also the community and entire country. The Niger Wives are involved in some fundamental charity projects that touch on the fabrics of society. Their national project, for instance, is a continuous to improve the welfare of the blind and partially sighted people in Nigeria.
They are from any other country of the world, but Nigeria, while many starry-eyed Nigerian youths live and die for the American dream, their fires of passion are fanned by personalities who embody all that they crave after in men; and the authentic Nigerian dream.
The usual foreplay of boy-meets-girl and the perpetual session of love that follows were properly established in every case. In each example, the two lovebirds hardly could keep out of each other's arms. They become so inseparable that parting ways become unthinkable. When it is time for these men to return to Nigeria, the women do what many of their friends and family predict. They follow their beaus back to Africa, to Nigeria!
At first, many of these women did it for curiosity, even though they were no doubt madly in love, they still wanted to know if, like the famous African Prince, in the movie, 'Coming to America', these Nigerian men were princes returning home to a royal court where a host of servants would attend to their every need.
In deed, the live many of them have lived since returning to Nigeria has not been far from that of the fairy tale Prince Charming.
Nigerian men love the good live; even those of them from the most humble background assume flamboyant lifestyles once they return home with their wives from other nations.
Having been consumed by the charm of these Nigerian knights in shining armour, the finality with which these women dump their country of birth reminds one of the Biblical Ruth who demonstrated oneness with her husband by declaring the famous lines; "my people shall be your people and your God, my God".
The same sentiments were displayed when Winnie Mandela, whose dedication to her former husband and President of South Africa, Nelson pushed her to reach into her soul and come up with this famous statement, "I did not only marry a man, I married a struggle".
Today, these women from other nations who are married to Nigerian men have become more Nigerian than many Nigerians, largely because they emptied themselves for their husbands without worrying if the men were giving back as much.
While most members of the Nigerwives, as they are loosely called, had travelled for the summer, those around freely sheared their experiences and challenges of living in Nigeria.
Diane Oputa, originally from the United States, but now married to popular entertainer, Mr. Charles Oputa, better known as, Charley Boy said, "I was madly in love with Charles and agreed to follow him home to Nigeria after his studies. I came then, spent some months and went back". Eventually, when she was prepared to go the whole hog with Charles, while Charley had not yet proposed marriage, nothing could dissuade her.
Ever so supportive of her husband's career, Diane readily agreed to partner the new (at that time) Charley who was trying to launch himself into the mainstream of the entertainment industry in Nigeria.
Quickly snapping out of the culture shock, Diane joined her husband in shocking timid minded Nigerians with their bold, bohemian life style. Many dismissed them as unserious, but the tenacity with which their "royal punkness" carried themselves and their art was targeted at opening doors. Now that the Charley Boy phenomenon has become an identifiable brand, Diane can look back at those early years with satisfaction and say in unmistakable terms that the chemistry between herself and "this Nigerian guy is just right. We are more than soul mates. We were made for one another".
"We met in a hair salon where I was working then. He was always coming in a Limousine to do his hair. We got married in 1984, here in Nigeria. It is not easy being Charley Boy's wife, especially with his weird lifestyle. I cope with it day by day. It is with God's grace and blessings in a relationship that keeps us together. We talk a lot, we communicate, he doesn't leave me behind in things that he is doing. We also agree to disagree. Most times, people walk up to me and ask how I cope".
Diane's essential Nigerian trip has been a mixture of the good, the bad and the ugly. All these shades of circumstances have not only played themselves out in her relationship with Charley Boy only. "It's been the same with my new family in Nigeria and the environment I find myself in today. I mean some of the things that pass for normal are unthinkable in the United States, but that is the way it is here; so I have to learn to deal with it".
In the beginning, things were even more challenging and Diane confesses that there were times she felt like packing her stuff and getting out of Nigeria, but there is something about Charley Boy that restrained her; "again my parents in-law are so wonderful. They gave me hope and encouraged me to look beyond Charley's naughtiness in those early years".
One of the earliest sources of frustration for Diane was the fact that she realized she could not totally have her husband to herself. "In Africa, it is so different. The family system is extended and large. Everybody is important and they feel hurt if they as much as perceive that you are not paying as much attention to them as you should. They can easily accuse you of being a snub, because you didn't return a greeting".
With a husband who lived a racy live, there was no leisure of learning slowly. "I had to wake up and run first before I asked to understand why I was on the run. Things happened very fast. Overall, I love Nigeria. This country is going to explode in a positive sense of the word and I am happy to say that my husband and I were able to see ahead of our time. We are on the threshold of a new and wonderful Nigeria that the rest of the world would be forced to reckon with. The silent revolution going on now in the polity is good. It is just that we operate in a sector of the economy that demands high visibility, but I am not essentially a loud person. When I am not working, you find me in my house. I always find things to do. You need to see the things I have created with my own hands. I derive a lot of joy in creativity. I feel like I have given life to something".
"I'm working on two other shows that would hopefully be on air by September. One of them will be called TAAKAIA. It means, Teaching Africans Arts and Krafts. It is going to be a kind of self-empowerment programme where I will be able to enlighten a few people on what they could do with African arts and crafts and how to use them to earn income.
It's been 23 years of marriage and a lot of people don't understand that the most essential ingredient that has kept the relationship going is "God's grace".
Need to know more? Diane is happy in her marriage. "We are always happy as a couple with lovely kids. Like it is expected in every marriage, once in a while, we do have our ups and downs. The grass is not always green. We don't let things interfere with our marriage foundation. That is the rock that holds everything together".
While some Nigerians are finding it difficult to speak their spouse's language, Diane has overcome that obstacle. "I can speak a little bit of Ibo if I'm in the village, I know how to greet. I know the titles. But my Yoruba staff are trying to teach me Yoruba. She understands the power of language and how it creates a warmer relationship. She tries her best to exploit all the cultural elements in the environment and put them to work for her
"You should know that the way to a man's heart is food. My husband likes all kinds of foods and that has helped me in knowing how to prepare a variety of dishes. He likes Edikan-Ikong and Afang soups much and I prepare them for him very, very well. I also like local vegetable foods very much. I like pounded yam a lot but it made me put on much weight, so I had to quit. I also love pepper soup".
Another Niger wife is Mrs. Megan Olusanya who on 22nd June celebrated the 44th year of marriage to her husband, Ambassador (Professor) Gabriel Olusanya former director general of the Nigeria Institute of International Affairs (NIIA) and Nigeria's Ambassador to France. Originally from Jamaica in the West Indies, Megan met and married her husband in Toronto Canada. They returned to Nigeria in 1965. Using her husband as an example, she thinks Nigerian men deserve a pass mark for their gentlemanly conduct. "They are supportive and make excellent role models. I am not saying that they are 100 percent good. There are ups and downs".
Coming from a country that boasts of a good African heritage was a mighty help. But a few things still came as a surprise. For instance, all the strict formalities and respect demanded by the Yoruba culture was by far different from the standoffish attitudes in her own country. "This is a good thing, because it is sad that children in the West don't respect their parents".
Mrs. Olusanya hasn't gone back home to Jamaica in the last seven years. She misses home for two reasons: The food and not being able to talk with members of her family. Eighteen hours is a mighty long time to travel; and that is all it takes to go from Nigeria to Jamaica. Living in retirement now, she engages herself as the administrator of Niger Wives Association. She is often busy with the various projects conceptualized by this group of foreign women who are married to Nigerians. Everything she has now is thrown into this charity as a way of exercising her brain, helping younger women who are married to Nigerians to settle in and also to satisfy a yearning to serve humanity in a selfless capacity.
Some of the better known Niger Wives include the Iya Oge of Lagos, Chief (Mrs.) Opral Benson who is originally from Liberia but is now married to Nigeria's former Information Minister, Chief T.O.S. Benson, Mrs. Alice Da Silva, owner of Alice in Wonderland at Joel Ogunaike Street, Ikeja, Mrs. Doris Fafunwa, wife of the former Education Minister, Professor Babatunde Fafunwa and the wife of the CEO of the Silverbird Media Group, Ben Murray-Bruce.
Mrs. Betty Okuboyejo came to Nigeria 44 years ago with her husband. It's been 11 years since she lost him, but she still remembers like yesterday when they first met in Oxford and got married. They returned to Nigeria in 1963 and lived at first in Abeokuta; then they moved to Ijebu Ode before coming down to Lagos. Living in the Yoruba hinterland gave her the opportunity of getting closer to Yoruba society to look at the way they dress and other aspects of their lifestyle that would have been difficult to do in Lagos. At that time there were not many Europeans in Abeokuta and she had to mix freely with Nigerians.
Interestingly, she spent her working life making 'adire' and other ready-to-wear clothings. One of her children, Nike has developed this trait from her and taken it to higher heights.
Mrs. Roseline Afolabi has travelled to all parts of Nigeria, except the farthest ends of the northern Nigeria like Sokoto and Maiduguri. Originally from the island country of Haiti in the West Indies, Mrs. Afolabi finds Nigeria very much like her country in terms of the climate and food. "For me, it is like moving from one town to another. But I love living in Lagos and other coastal environment because it reminds me of my own country. Nigeria is a country full of promises and blessed. Nigeria is a great country. I like the spirit of the Nigerian people-very enterprising and generally intelligent. Nigerians stand out from other Africans".
A trained Pharmacist who worked as a teacher and administrator in the Nigerian educational system, Afolabi said the only challenge she has faced so far in over three decades sojourn in Nigeria are the Nigerian languages. In my country, we speak French and Creole, but it is different here. I do not know of other Haitian who is married to a Nigerian".
Mrs. Michelle Ukoh has only been in Nigeria for three years with her husband, Mazi Chukwu Francis Ukoh who is from Arondiziogu in Imo state. They met and got married in the United States 20 years ago. Immediately she got into the country, she hooked up with the Niger Wives Association. Her interaction with the group is one of the most rewarding experiences she has enjoyed in Nigeria so far.
Mrs. Ukoh owes her proper integration into the Nigerian society to senior Niger Wives like Mrs. Okuboyejo and Mrs. Da Silva who emphasized the importance of cooking Nigerian meals for her husband and dressing up in Nigerian fashion. From them she learnt one of the secrets of making success out of marriage to a Nigerian man: Don't try to curb his cultural instincts; the children belong to him; do whatever he tells you and you must be ready to accommodate his extended family.
Ukoh, who is a licensed principal in the United States, last year, opened First New Generation Study Centre for children between the ages of eight and fifteen. One of the values she inculcates in her students is to live a purposeful live and to have faith in Nigeria. "I am happier here. The resources are here if you know how to harness them. I like the food and the value that is placed on family. This is a place you can still raise children.
No matter where we may have come from originally, we are here to stay. We aim to contribute our best to make our home Nigeria, a better home. When you do not plan to move, you make your home more conducive, that is what we have been doing variously in our various endeavours.

Original_Search_Title: Immediate Democracy and Development Initiative
Headline: Africa: Immediate Democracy and Development Initiative
Body_Text:
Lagos — IT is ironic that recent national debates in Africa are dominated by ethnic agendas, whereas Africans abroad tend to be more aware of a shared destiny.
I attended a discussion recently by students from West Africa (although, at least, one identified himself as an American whose parents were born in Nigeria) who were studying in America. Each student presented highlights of his or her country and, then, there was a general discussion. The first comment came from a female student who said that, after listening to all the presentations, she still thought that her country (not her ethnic group) was the best.
That comment started a brief banter on whose country was really the best and it reminded me of a 'joke' made by a top diplomat during a meeting of some Africans from a particular country in Washington D.C.
The comment was that despite all the problems facing that great African countries, none of its citizens wished to exchange his or her nationality for any other nationality. Before you could counter that with examples of Africans who have given up their African citizenship in return for dual citizenship in European and North American countries, the speaker clarified that he was referring to the nationality of some of those small countries in Africa that were so small that they did not have an address. Ha-ha-ha, many people laughed.
I asked the students, after their banters, if there were any lessons that Africans could learn from the United States of America, Russia, China or India, given the way that their unity makes them strong economically, culturally, diplomatically and militarily. Is it possible for our people to unite and form one federal republic instead of maintaining the absurd boundaries that serve to weaken us? This question started a heated debate, during which the students pointed out the obstacles to unity in the form of language barriers and national pride, while others supported the unity project that many of our founding fathers bequeathed to us in their political wills.
Some of the students pointed out that countries like the US, Russia, China, India and the European Community do not have a single language or culture. Moreover, if the existing African countries already contain multiple languages and cultures, then multiculturalism cannot be used as an excuse to resist greater African unity. Some pointed out that Americans are proud to be from specific states, but, that such pride in their home states do not detract from their national pride and unity. In the end, the students concluded that the potentials for unity are great but they feared that industrialised countries would block any attempts to unite Africa because they are the ones benefiting from the status quo.
That raised another question: in what ways do industrialised countries benefit from the current weakness of the mushroomed African states? The answer came in the form of the cheap supply of raw materials and the suggestion that if Africa is united, it will be able to fix the prices of raw materials, like crude oil, coffee, gold, diamonds and cocoa. Therefore, according to some of the students, the industrialised economies would suffer from having to pay higher prices for raw materials. If this is the major reason why any industrialised country would oppose African unity, then, it should be clarified to them that a united Africa would not pose a threat to their economy or otherwise. Rather, such a strong united republic would be more prosperous and, consequently, would be better able to buy more from and sell more to the rest of the world. Given the economy of scale, the bigger volume of trade would benefit everyone in the world. Africans are not going to drink oil, eat gold and smoke diamonds, we will still sell them at going rates!
Besides, the prices of raw materials are largely determined by market forces of demand and supply, and not dictated by any one country. Thus, if a united Africa wishes to continue producing nothing but raw materials, the prices will continue to be cheaper than manufactured goods for obvious reasons. If, on the other hand, the People's Republic of Africa develops industries and manufactures goods for sale in competition with the industrialised countries, the example of China shows that such will not be a threat to the industrialised countries whose citizens would benefit from better quality and cheaper products contrary to the monopolistic situation where powerful corporations fix prices to the disadvantage of consumers worldwide.
Moreover, Africans should not be motivated by fear in responding to the long overdue project of genuine unity among our people. We should be motivated by the gains that unity would herald for our people. For instance, the masses of our people are voting with their feet across the continent and beyond in search of their livelihood. They do not recognise the artificial boundaries drawn by Europeans in the Berlin conference of 1850. They are only frustrated by bureaucrats who insist that Africans who travel to other African countries are illegal aliens to be deported en-masse, while North Americans, Europeans and Asians are given almost unrestricted access to all parts of Africa visa-free.
What the ordinary people want is the opportunity to sell their groundnuts or textile, their labour power or skills, their cultural artifacts and industrial goods in any part of Africa the way Americans do in America and the way Europeans do in Europe. If my sister who sells yam is allowed to sell yam all over Africa, she would become a billionaire instantly. If my younger brother who is an undergraduate is allowed to apply to any university in Africa without being made to jump through visa hoops, young scholars would be more mobile on the continent and such competition would obviously help to improve the quality of higher education.
If my cousin, who was unemployed with a PhD, was allowed to seek employment in any part of Africa, he would not be unemployed much longer and the employers, no matter in which 'State' they were located in the People's Republic of Africa, would reap the benefits of his neglected skills while giving him the opportunity to actualise his full potentials. The pressure to emigrate to Europe and America would be drastically decreased while some of the conscious African Diaspora would even emigrate back to Africa to help with the reconstruction process..
Furthermore, a strong and united Africa would benefit immediately from the elimination of duplications in bureaucratic services. For instance, instead of having 54 armies, 54 air forces, 54 navies that specialise in killing our people in Africa, there will be only one People's Army, etc., that will be more professional and much less expensive, but much more effective in defending our people. Instead of having 54 embassies in more than 200 countries around the world, each one of them paying exorbitant rents, Africa will only have one embassy in each country and such a single embassy will be better funded and, therefore, more effective than the current situation where some of the so-called embassies cannot even afford to pay electricity and telephone bills.

Original_Search_Title: Does the President Know Where Some of the Bulls Are Coming From?
Headline: Gambia: Does the President Know Where Some of the Bulls Are Coming From?
Body_Text:
Women who are engaged in the operation of small scale enterprises are growing. However, they are finding it very difficult to guarantee regular supply of commodities. Let us take the meat industry as an example. As the President made claims that most people engaged in the selling of meat are foreigners, some Gambians especially women have decided to become involved in the business.
Foroyaa is making special effort to study what we can call a "Bana bana trade."
The "Bana bana" is a middle person who stands between the producer and consumer. Many Gambian women have been building their entrepreneurial capacities to move across borders as "Bana bana" traders. They have been involved in bringing vegetables and spices from Senegal, banana, palm oil, yams, coconut from Guinea, Guinea Bissau and Cote d'Ivoire, respectively.
Recently, the women have decided to take the real bull by the horn. Many are shifting to the meat industry.
What a number of them are complaining is the sporadic nature of the sale of bulls in the Gambian markets by Gambian herdsmen. It becomes all the more difficult to find bulls when festivals are taking place which often require countless numbers of bulls to be slaughtered to feed the participants.
It is now becoming clear to such women that the butchers used to depend on "Bana bana" traders who go all the way to Mali to bring bulls for consumption in The Gambia.
The Gambian women have also taken the trail. They are now going up to Mali to purchase bulls.
The claim that the Gambia is the supermarket of the sub region is becoming less credible as the Gambian "Bana bana" traders go to the sub region to purchase firewood, palm oil, bananas, yams, coconuts, vegetables and so on and so forth.
The government needs to study the nature of the informal sector to know the trend of production and the origin of commodities consumed in The Gambia.
This will determine the level of dependency of the Gambia on sub regional markets for the consumption needs of the people and for re-export. This should inform planning and policy making on the economy.
Festivals do bring people together to drink and dine but the real achievement is how to bring the people together to produce, trade and consume the products of their sweat so as to promote the general welfare.
Hungry people cannot enjoy watching the few who have the opportunity to eat, drink and be merry just for a period and then move into the vicious cycle of poverty again.

Original_Search_Title: Electoral Grumbling From Dublin
Headline: Nigeria: Electoral Grumbling From Dublin
Body_Text:
PROMPTED by the vitriol both nationally and internationally that followed the April 2007 Elections, President Umar Yar'Adua, on his swearing-in ceremony at the Eagle Square in Abuja on May 29, had promised to carry out a comprehensive electoral reform during his tenure. However, before the proposed reforms are kick started, it is important for the government, especially the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to pay attention to the grumblings of some Nigerians abroad on the nation's electoral process in the collective march at deepening participatory democracy in the country.
Tired of eating oysters and other fried foods of the Irish despite their warm hospitality during a short visit to Dublin, Republic of Ireland recently, I had gone, along with some colleagues, to Parnell Street, which is known as 'Little Africa', to try get a good Nigerian dish. It turned out to be a good decision as I settled down for pounded yam with egusi soup and a plate of assorted meats, all going for just 10 euro. It was a sort of meeting point for many Nigerian professionals there. I soon engaged them on the situation back home.
While those of us at home have faulted the processes leading to, as well as the outcomes of the April elections, lampooning the Maurice Iwu-led Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) for giving the country what is Nigeria's 'worst elections in history as an independent nation', Nigerians abroad, especially those in Dublin-and there is a good number of them-having a totally different angst against the elections. It's a simple one, but very fundamental.
Their grouse is simply their exclusion from the electoral process. According to them, that they decide to travel out, like other Nigerians abroad, whether for studies, or for business, or in search of greener pastures, or even leisure, should be no justification for being disenfranchised from exercising their constitutional rights in the general elections held every four years. They believe that in this age of digital technology, such tasks are not too Herculean. Irrespective of their location, they say they have much stakes in Nigeria, and are worried about happenings in the country and their loyalty to the nation remains unchanged.
For instance, in spite of his belief that Nigeria is blessed with everything needed to make it work, Emeka Okereke from Orlu in Imo State, could not fathom why INEC did not put in place logistics to make Nigerians abroad cast their votes like Nigerians at home during the general elections. For him, no excuse from INEC is tenable.
"I'm sure if you ask the head of the electoral body why Nigerians abroad couldn't vote, he'll find a lot of excuses to exonerate himself from what is simply a negligence of duty. But it's better we accept responsibility and say hey, we fail this time, next time, we'll do our best to get Nigerians abroad to vote." , he said.
Despite my explanation that many Nigerians back home, including the former Senate President, Ken Nnamani couldn't cast their votes during the elections and the fact that voting did not hold in many cities and towns across the country, Okereke, and some other Nigerians like Reginald Kanu from Ajah in Abia State, and Adesola Adegoroye, a lawyer, were not swayed. "The right to vote as a citizen is a constitutional one. Other countries put the necessary logistics in place at their foreign embassies to ensure that their nationals there are able to vote, so why can't we?" they asked.
They also wondered why the chairman of the electoral commission should be a member of the party in power. Such kinds of stipulation, they argued, only promotes manipulation, bias and favoritism like it was experienced during the events leading to the last elections. Instead, they suggested that the electoral commission should be peopled by representatives of the major professional bodies and labour groups in the country like the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA), Nigeria Medical Association (NMA), the Nigerian Union of Journalists (NUJ), the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) including leading members of religious unions and should be chaired by a retired justice of the Supreme Court. Such members of the commission, they suggested, must also be people of impeccable character and unquestionable integrity.
Personally, much as these viewpoints and the feasibility of the arrangement could pose serious technical challenges, I believe their grumblings, their opinions, whichever way one looks at it, still make much sense.

Original_Search_Title: The Good Side of the April Polls
Headline: Nigeria: The Good Side of the April Polls
Body_Text:
Lagos — In Yoruba philosophical thinking, the good and the bad always walk together. In other words, there is nothing in this world that is so good that will not have some bad aspects, no matter how minute; and the same thing holds true for the worst situations. Which was why I started searching for anything, just about anything, that can be termed good in our electoral misfortune in the last two months, in order to assuage my own pains over it.
And presto! I remembered the political campaigns across the country. I got so amused that I began to laugh - what I have not done in such a long time. During the campaign, I thought most of the politicians were behaving like clowns, sidestepping issues of concern to us. But now it has occurred to me that some aspects of the campaign outings could actually assist the country in wealth creation amidst the ravaging poverty in the land! And this, through the much maligned cultural phenomenon started by the Yoruba and now gaining acceptance throughout the country - the Aso-ebi syndrome. At every campaign stop, the politicians made new clothing to suit the particular area of the country, and the whole crowd - from the former President to the political thugs - appeared in the same outfit. Think of the boost in economic activities that we had and we did not realize it. The textile industry for once recovered quickly from the doldrums, the fashion designers had a field day. Even the ordinary tailors hanging around in street corners had good business and made more income. All the lace-wearing big wigs changed their attire to the common man ankara and adire. Think of the morale boost for the roadside beggar who would now be able to boast that he wore the same aso-ebi that the former and the new President had in their wardrobe.
Thinking of the sheer spectacle that these aso-ebi campaigns provided, some individuals were just a delight to behold. The gentle and unassuming Umar Yar-Adua beamed and frolicked away in the Ijaw very fashionable attire. In fact he could have been mistaken for a Niger Delta militant on holidays! When he appeared in adire, I was beginning to think that he might have had some ancestors in the ancient Owu kingdom. And Muhammadu Buhari. He was resplendent in complete aso-ofi outfit with the abeti-aja traditional cap. In this kind of clothing it was impossible to look anything but regal and fun-loving and also more impossible to look draconian even if he tried. He struck me as a resurrected "Palm Wine Drinkard" of the late Amos Tutuola's tale. What is more, on that occasion he revealed a gap-tooth smile that rivaled that of Ibrahim Babangida. This surely was another human side of the hitherto unsmiling draconian general.
But in all these, the former President Olusegun Obasanjo took the cake. He came out so debonair in every conceivable attire, from the South-South to the far North. What struck me most was that he was able to portray without himself realizing it, the little differences in the traditional out-fits of the various parts of the far North, as we Southerners always thought that all Northerners dressed alike. For a man of seventy plus, the popular saying that there is no old person in Ghana holds true for this gruff general turned politician. Apart from his clothing, the provocative way he donned his caps-from the variety that exists in Yoruba land to the broad-brimmed hats in the South-South, to the peculiar Ibo variety and the embroidered or plain ones in the North - would make any young man green with envy. No wonder he is considered an in-law in many parts of the country as he himself confessed in one of the political rallies in the East.
The job creation that these campaigns provided can really not be fully quantified just yet. Let us wait until we carry this phenomenon to the global level. Think of what a regular fashion parade modeled after the campaigns can do to boost the tourism industry. Donald Duke has done his best in this regard in his Tinapa project. But how about encouraging foreign tourists to troop into such resorts, as other "Tinapa" are bound to emerge in other parts of the country? I would suggest that we have a national troupe made up of former heads of State to put up fashion parade shows at national tourist points like the Argungu Fishing Festival, Osun Oshogbo festival, new yam festival, etc. We should encourage former Heads of State like Yakubu Gowon, Shehu Shagari, Ibrahim Babangida and Ernest Shonekan even though they ruled before the aso-ebi thing became a powerful social weapon, to quickly learn the ropes and join the troupe. In other lands former Presidents and Prime Ministers are not allowed to waste away in retirement, but are given specific assignments to help their countries. And while we are at it, how about asking Maryam Babangida to represent women's interest in this national troupe? She was a First Lady who took local fashion seriously and is still a show-stopper any day. And she may have a thing or two in this fashionable enterprise to pass on to Turai Yar-Adua in her new Women Empowerment Programme. The advice is sure to fire women's creative energy for a better life.
I am convinced that such a National Troupe will be in future a major foreign exchange earner. At a time that some European countries and the U.S.A are preparing for their elections they could hire our troupe to teach them new tricks in political campaign. The troupe can put up side shows to give their electioneering some pep. Better still, they can be taught how to wear aso-ebi made of some of their own peculiar outfits. Think of Tony Blair and his Labour Party members all appearing in Scottish kilts with bag-pipes at campaign rallies, or Hillary Clinton and the U.S Democratic Party in Red Indian feathery outfits. And Barack Obama. In fact being an African-American, he will need our National Troupe more than any other candidate for election abroad.
Apart from the National Troupe, we could ask our own W.S to use some of the energy he is expending on this election wahala to turn the campaign displays into a stage play, or better still a musical show, for our enjoyment within the country. Remember he's "King Babu"? This can also be exported abroad, as his creative works are always accepted with rapture on the international scene.
The result of all these? A swelling foreign reserve account that will make Charles Soludo grin from ear to ear and back off from some of the economic measures that are squeezing the generality of the people to the bones, so the country can continue to accumulate more and more reserve for our foreign creditors to suck dry eventually.
And so, while the country is trying to right the wrongs of the past few months and embark on electoral reforms, which the new President has vowed to spearhead, let us keep searching for other good aspects of our life as Nigerians during these past few months. Who knows, we may come up with some other things that would affirm our reputation as the happiest people on earth. Again as the Yoruba say, the fact that one is weeping should not blur one's vision or prevent one from seeing - "bi aba nsukun a ma riran".
Aboyade, a professor, wrote from Ibadan

Original_Search_Title: Inside Utako's Green Area Bars
Headline: Nigeria: Inside Utako's Green Area Bars
Body_Text:
For Mr. Chris Abbey, tenanting the lush green-grassed plain down the valley beside Chida Hotel at Utako District of Abuja for at least four hours from the late afternoon is a daily routine, except, perhaps, on a few Sundays or when he is out of town for whatever business.
He is accustomed to a spot not far from the tarred road where he always parks his Honda Xala car. Any moment from 5.30 pm he arrives there. He sits on one of the four white plastic seats rounding the white plastic table at a position that guarantees him the opportunity to view the larger portion of the plain, and most of the a-table-and-four-chairs patches at the quite expansive open-air bar, keep regular vigil on his car and notice the arrival of his drinker-friends early enough to begin cracking the regular jokes even before they take seat. Most days, he arrives the bar earlier than them.
The vantage position affords him the opportunity to call the bartender shuttling between the liquor store and Peppersoup kitchen down the valley and the clusters of drinkers up the plain, the Maisuya or the roast fish seller just beside the road pavement. For the next four to five hours, it is Suya and roast fish eating and drinking business roundtable. They will trickle away home by 11 pm. Other 'business friends' will take over the table.
The two plains down the valley at Augustus Aikhomu Way and O.P Fingesi Street of Utako District are, perhaps, some of the most bubbling open-air bars in the federal capital city. They bubble for about twenty hours of the day at different levels, depending on the times of the day when most residents frequenting there, retreat home or elsewhere for leisure. Peak bubbling hours are between early evenings and midnight. A substantial number of drinkers continue their 'business' here to the early morning couple of hours. Depending on when one feels like going out for his or her drinking leisure, drinkers are often sparse on the plains most days, especially weekends, from as early as 9 am.
Several liquor stores with, mainly, Peppersoup kitchens that also sell some African staples like Pounded Yam, Eba and Boiled Rice with Vegetable, Egusi and Obono soups and stew, sparse down the plains, do business here all that long.
Each operator has a collection of plastic tables and seats. The tables will be laid for the arriving drinkers at their choice locations by the bartenders sometimes right in the thatched-roofed scantily organized parlour, but most times out on the plain within the operator's space of operation any time of the day. It seems each operator has his or her space of operation. There are no physical demarcations among the sparsely flowered spaces. Such demarcations seem mainly determined by the different sets of the drinkers who seem accustomed to sitting on choice locations due, perhaps, mainly to respective idiosyncrasies.
From early evening, the bartenders of each operator will arrange the tables, rounding each with chairs, mostly four, at intervals of metres from each on their operational space on the plains. Most drinkers, perhaps, because they are a working population, begin to trickle in from as early as 6 pm and by 9 pm this entire location of the valley is flooded with drinkers. The service lanes and pavements of the two roads around the location are flooded with cars in the fashion of a big motor rally.
Drinkers will cluster round the tables according to friendship or any other association groups. Of course, there will be the usual parade of women of easy virtue roaming the spaces, although many gentlemen do come with their ladies. For at least the next seven hours, it is no rest time at all for the bartenders. They are engaged in seemingly uncountable shuttles between the liquor store/kitchen and the tables, serving different brands of beer, wine and soft drinks according to order. The open-air drinking 'business roundtables' will be in session in an atmosphere lightly clouded with cigarette smoke to midnight, when it will begin to weaken down to the early morning couple of hours.
Traditional Hausa Kalangu musicians will be performing from one table to another, praise-singing for the drinkers for some cash. Fresh hot roast fish and Suya (roast beef) sellers are ever in the wait beside their roasting basins. The fish and Suya are heavily peppered to the taste of the drinkers.
Drinkers tenant tables for hours and depart for others to take over throughout the entire business hours. It would seem a bad day for the open-air bars when it rains, but not entirely so. So long as the rain is just a light drizzle, some compulsive frequenters of the bars will be found even out in the open spaces, drinking. So long as it is not about midnight, many drinkers, either out of habitual compulsiveness or mere accustomedness to the outing, will still trickle in to the open-air bars after the heavy rainfall to form a sizeable crowd of 'roundtablers.'
It is quite an interesting scene.
"I have grown used to coming here to drink mostly every evening since I came to Abuja ten years ago," Chris Abbey, a building engineer, offered. He would not mention the construction company he works for. "I feel wild and free, like one Benson and Hedges advert said, to enjoy my drink in the midst of my friends as a gentleman, but don't go and write that I get wild when I drink. I don't," he explained as a bartender opened his bottle of Gulder beer by 6 pm this Thursday.
"For me, I don't see any reason why one should confine himself to an enclosed space, however roomy it is, to drink in a situation of leisure. Leisure should come with the type of freedom provided by such an open space. Again, if you consider hygiene, you will agree with me that it is safer to sit in the open space to drink. When the puffs (of cigarette smoke) get heavier and the breaths mix with the smoke, and it gets hotter with a big crowd, many drinkers, including me, are not comfortable in an enclosed space. It makes me unnecessarily weak, I mean the kind of weakness that is not associated with the drinking itself," Abbey maintained.
He said: "It is a matter of freedom. At your workplace you are not free. Whether you are working in the office or outside, you are not free, because you are engaged in an activity that has time, schedule, format and method, which you must offer and adjust your whole self to. After the working hours, it is time for leisure. Leisure must offer you the freedom denied you by work. So I see no reason why I should not be free at leisure." He turned to his glass for several gulps.
"What is your business with my name?" another, who barely identified himself as a federal civil servant, queried with some anger. "Freedom, yes! But apart from the free and open atmosphere, you will find that it is easy for you to make your choice of where you want to sit for your enjoyment in the open space. It is also easy for you to move around for whatever reason. Sufficient space for easy movement is another advantage. The bar operators themselves have sufficient space for business," he observed.
He believed that there are no sufficiently roomy enclosed-space bars in the city to contain its after-work drinking population, and most bar operators themselves are unable to scale the difficulties at securing suitable locations where large population of drinkers could go to for more lucrative business.
"I feel more in a natural setting, sitting in the open-spaced bar under these trees to enjoy my drink," said A.D Abraham, continuing, "I always feel I am way back in the village where we sit outside or around palm trees to enjoy our drink like Kainkain and Palmy (Palm wine). No one disturbs the other. You get the natural freshness of things. I have been to places where Burkutu is taken up North here. When you go to villages in the North where people take Burkutu you will see that they sit outside, drinking it and eating pork or dog meat in some places. Whether you mess around and misbehave after drinking or not, you feel more natural doing it outside. That is why I prefer coming here to drink. Come to think of it, I don't even owe anybody any explanation. Leisure time is my time."
Musa (he would not mention his surname) is another frequenter of the Augustus Aikhomu Way open-air bar. "The green areas in the city are for leisure. They are for the relaxation of all at whatever time of the day. You can always go there for party, social meetings with your friends and rest and repose when you want to be alone. For those who drink, drinking is an important part of all these. So, I see no reason why bars can't operate in them to do business. They have enough space for business and we have enough space for the leisure offered by their business. How do you see it?" jested.
According to him, since most buildings in the city can not offer facilities for drinking bar business, the Federal Capital Development Authority (FCDA) should consider the comfort of most residents by improving green areas with leisure facilities such drinking bars, but at a controlled number to consider the comfort of the non-drinking population and guarantee safety for all and the prevalence of law and order in the environs.
Musa sees nothing wrong in turning portions of the Abuja green areas to bars, arguing, "Most of them are down the valleys, these valleys are still not developed for anything else and I don't see how they can be developed for anything else soon. If we don't have enough bars, parks and gardens around the city for the drinking and leisure seeking population I think all such natural places that are still not developed for anything else should be allowed to serve that purpose for the residents."
A lady would not mention her name. "Why do you people always think any lady you see is a prostitute?" she queried with anger. "This is a meeting point. We arrange to meet with my men and women friends here to enjoy our time in the open. I am waiting for someone." She would not say more. Shortly after, a Mercedes Benz 190 car stopped on the pavement. She stepped forth towards it. The occupant came out. They both strutted to a table.
The open-air bars in the green area at Augustus Aikhomu Way and O.P Fingesi Street of Abuja's Utako District liquor drinkers who, it is said, include top civil servants, military and paramilitary officers and businessmen, seem feeling freer than at any other place to do their thing.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians to FG, Labour: Think of the People, Go for Dialogue
Headline: Nigerians to FG, Labour: Think of the People, Go for Dialogue
Body_Text:
By Chioma Gabriel, Assistant Editor, Dayo Johnson, Akure, Emeka Mamah, Kaduna, George Onah, Port Harcourt, Wole Mosadomi, Minna, Ola Ajayi, Ibadan, Simon Ebegbulem, Benin City, & Gbenga Ariyibi, Ado-Ekiti
As Nigeria faces economic paralysis as a result of the on-going nation-wide strike, Nigerians from different walks of life in these encounters with Saturday Vanguard say the on-going strike could have been avoided if government has the interest of Nigerians at heart. As it is now, Nigerians are groaning under the impact of the strike which commenced Wednesday and has crippled activities throughout the federation.
Chief Rasheed Ghadamosi, the chairman of Petroleum Products Pricing and Regulatory Agency, PPPRA, said the strike has little to do with the pricing of petroleum products and expressed dismay that NLC did not consider the hardship the strike would create for the ordinary people.
"Asking me to comment on the strike is wrong because I don't think I have anything to do with the strike. I'm surprised that those who spear-headed the strike did not consider the interest of Nigerians before calling the strike because of its consequences. I hope they realise what they have done to Nigeria and the people.'
Ibrahim Yakassai of the National Universities Commission, NUC, said his organisation was not part of the strike.
"As you and I are talking, I'm not on strike. I'm in the office working and you can come and see for yourself. But I think this strike could be avoided. I think the NLC should call off the strike and settle for dialogue especially since government has conceded some of its grounds. The strike is not doing anybody any good and I believe we can do without strikes in this country. Organized labour and civil society groups should opt for dialogue as a way of resolving some of these things because of the severe effects of strikes."
Reacting also to the strike action, Dr. Lateef Adegbite, Secretary-General of the Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs told Saturday Vanguard that he would prefer the government listens to the yearnings of the people and always put the people first before taking some of its decisions, especially those that would badly affect the people.
"Government should always have the interest of the people at heart before promulgating some of its economic policies. Democracy is about the people and if the people now say they don't want N70 for a litre of fuel but N65, the government should concede. Democracy is about what the people want. There are also other areas of grievances apart from fuel. I'm advising that government adjust these other economic areas to fall within the peoples' demand. That is democracy."
In his own reaction, NUPENG secretary general, Elijah Okougbo said that his organisation is a loyal affiliate of NLC and has to ensure the NLC strike is successful.
"Government should parley with the people and organized labour to ensure dialogue. We have given the NLC our mandate and we are supporting the strike.
So, while NLC is in Abuja negotiating, we are here making sure that the strike succeeds. The purpose has to be achieved and the only way to ensure its success is through negotiation with government."
Chief Mike Ozekhome, lawyer and human rights activist said that NLC has made its point by calling for a strike.
"But I think it's okay. Continuing the strike is unnecessary now because the Federal Government has made concessions on many of the issues raised by NLC. VAT has been reverted to 5%. Workers salary has been increased by 15% and even back dated to January. Fuel has been reduced from N75 to N70 although I believe it should be put to N65, I think this can be done by negotiation.
"On sale of Port Harcourt and Warri refineries, I think the issue should not be tackled with fiat because it could lead to a legal battle. What I think should be done is for NLC to negotiate with the Federal Government, or Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE). I'm saying this because we practice constitutional democracy in this country and some of the people who bought the refineries took loans to buy them.
So, President Yar'Adua cannot just revert on that or the buyers can go to court. It's madness to have sold the main source of our national income. If we had kept quiet, they would have sold the Mint.
