I won't be surprised if we hear one day that they have sold CBN and Aso Rock. We should have untouchables in this country and refineries should be part of them.
"But like I said, former President Obasanjo sold the refineries by military fiat and made announcements for the sales 48 hours after. That issue, as it is, can only be reversed at this point through negotiation because we are in a democracy and I know, Otedola and others would go to court if the sale is reversed.
Obasanjo did not have any right to have sold the refineries in the first instance. But I'm appealing for patience. Nigerians should have patience with Yar'Adua and give him a chance. He should not be the lamb of God that takes away the sins of Obasanjo. We are losing N3 billion everyday by this strike and I'm pleading that the strike be called off."
But the national chairman of Alliance For Democracy, Senator Mojisoluwa Akinfenwa said that since the strike was not politically motivated, it was okay.
"The NLC wants the price of fuel to go down. It wants VAT to be reverted to the status-quo. This is because these increases have created suffering and hardship for Nigerians. The other issues raised by the NLC are also in the interest of the common man. So, I think the strike is in order but since government has made some concessions, NLC should use dialogue and negotiation to resolve it so as to have amicable settlement."
While eminent Nigerians and leaders of public opinion bare their minds on the appropriateness or otherwise of the stands taken by both the Federal Government on the one hand and labour on the other, ordinary Nigerians are already feeling the pinch, with anxiety mounting over possible reactions if something is not done soon. Nationwide, Nigerians are paying through their noses as cost of consumables, especially food items have hit the roof. The strike action which led to non-availability of petrol in turn made transporters increase fares, in many cases, by as much as 300 per cent, while it has become almost impossible for many to move from one point to the other since vehicular traffic has slowed down to a trickle.
In AKURE, the Ondo State capital, business and social activities have been completely grounded as the week closed, just as the strike action bit harder. Commercial vehicle drivers and Okada operators which did not join the strike on the first day joined by Thursday, forcing many people to trek long distances. The abnormal situation ignited unsavoury developments of its own; there was a breakdown of law and order as the wind screen of some commercial vehicles (taxis) were smashed following a report that they flouted a directive by there unions that they should join the strike action.
It took the intervention of the leaders of the union as the major Oyemekun Road in Akure was barricaded by some protesting drivers who preferred not to join the action.
Also an attempt by some taxi drivers to increase fares from N30 per drop to N40 caused skirmishes amongst the drivers. Some of the drivers felt there was no justification for the increase in the transport fares.
Expectedly, black market operations boomed in the state as filling stations that still have fuel preferred to sell late at night for between N130 and N150 per litre.
Only independent marketers sold fuel to motorists in the state on the second day of the strike action.
To further ensure total compliance the labour leaders in the state went round and shut the few independent filling stations that were dispensing fuel on Thursday to motorist at cut throat prices. Government establishments, banks, schools remain shut as school children turned the major roads into football pitches.
In KADUNA, bus fares have risen by 40 percent. The Kaduna metropolis was a little bit more lively on the first day of the strike as only few commercial vehicles plied the roads while armed policemen patrolled the city, blaring sirens. Most of the petrol stations were closed although the commodity was sold in the black market at an average of between N155 and N160 instead of the N110 sold in some filling stations on Wednesday.
Most of the beer palours were also deserted as traders who normally patronized them after the day's sales kept off the places. Transport fares which normally was between N30 to N40 went up to between N50 and N70.
The story of hardship continues, with not much difference. In PORT HARCOURT, workers in the private sector, traders and essential workers in the employ of the Rivers State government have been compelled to trek long distances to their places of work due to the non-availability of commercial vehicles and petrol in filling stations.
The situation has caused parents of children in private schools to order their wards to sit at home. Not even available commercial motorcycles were enough to ferry the commuters to their destination because of the total absence of petrol in Port Harcourt.
As a result, commercial vehicles owners have withdrawn their buses and taxies, causing the available ones to jerk up transport fares by as much as 60 per cent. For instance a one-way trip from Lagos Bus Stop to Mile 1, which costs N30 before the scarcity, has shot up to N70. For okadas, the change in price has gone up to 200 per cent.
Following the lack, many commodities in the markets have acquired new prices. Even prices of local snacks such as maize (corn), ube and sugar cane have been topped by between N20 and N40 per item. Five sizeable pieces of ube now cost N200 as against N100 before the strike. Garri, tomatoes, yam, and fish have become food items with appreciable price increases.
The strike has equally caused government hospitals to reject patients both at the casualty department and those requiring fresh admission. Saturday Vanguard gathered that the rejection was occasioned by the absence of junior staff of the hospitals.
But medical doctors and chief nursing officers were on duty when Saturday Vanguard called at Braithwaite Memorial Hospital, University of Port Harcourt Teaching Hospital as well as other government health institutions in the state. The juniour nurses joined the strike.
The on-going nationwide strike took a new dimension in MINNA, the Niger State Capital Tuesday when a handful of civil servants led by officials of the state branch of the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) staged a peaceful demonstration at the state secretariat to further express their dislike to the unpopular programmes inherited by the present Federal Government from the Obasanjo regime.
Led by the state chairman of the congress, Alhaji Musa Bako, workers marched from the Labour office through the major street of Minna, (Tunga, Paiko Road) and terminated at the state secretariat.
The workers who were carrying placards with different inscriptions "Federal Government, revert to N65 per litre now; masses, defend your rights and interest; No to mass sack in public sector; No to sale of the refineries and No to fuel increase were joined by other members of the public who share the same view with the workers as they marched through the street.
At the state secretariat, armed policemen had already been placed at strategic positions to prevent any brake down of law and order.
State governor, Dr. Muazu Babangida Aliyu who received the protesters along with the Head of Service and other top government officials assured the workers that their grievances will be delivered to the appropriate authorities in Abuja for immediate attention.
According to him: "We will be asking the Federal Government to negotiate properly because this is not the time to allow the issue to escalate as we don't want any ulterior or exterior motive to take over the process".
"We will urge those responsible to make sure that we arrive at a solution to this problem soonest so that we all go back to work promptly because if the government and the NLC continue to fight, it is the common man that will continue to suffer", he observed.
But as the strike took effect, it has taken a toll on people of the state as all petrol stations are without fuel, thereby escalating transport fares.
The black marketers are now experiencing boom in their trade as a gallon of fuel now goes for between N600 - N800 depending on your bargaining power while transport fares have consequently risen by 100 per cent to 120 per cent depending on distance.
Desperate travelers to other parts of the state now spend hours in the various parks due to low patronage by commuters who are forced back home due to the high transport fares.
And in various markets including Minna Central Market, Gwari and Bosso Markets, prices of foodstuffs and essential commodities have gone up considerably as a result of the fuel scarcity and 10% VAT increase. Most civil servants and other car owners have resorted to parking their vehicles while commuters especially traders now trek several kilometers to their places of work.
The story is however a bit different in IBADAN, Oyo State.
Except for civil servants, bankers and teachers in the state who strictly obeyed the stay-at-home order, artisans, market women, commercial drivers and other private companies treated the strike with kid gloves.
Also, tanker drivers were seen lifting fuel from the depot while thousands of motorists were scrambling to buy fuel in most filling stations in the city.
The popular Bodija, Sango and Dugbe markets were filled to the brim with buyers and sellers transacting business. Prices of goods were not affected by the strike.
A buyer, Mrs. Kemi Ajayi said except tubers of yams which were sold at relatively high prices, prices of food and other commodities remained unchanged. She said the market women did not have many customers to buy their wares, which was why the prices did not change.
The strike action also had very little effect on transportation fare. Transportation fare from Mokola to University of Ibadan was raised from N30 to N40, while drivers on Bodija, Challenge, Ojoo, Iwo Road charged the same fares they were collecting before the strike commenced.
But some drivers have refused collecting N10 per drop in some places. They now charge between N20 and N30 depending on the distance.
Drivers plying Lagos-Ibadan charge different fares. Passengers traveling from Ibadan to Lagos pay N400 while they pay triple of that amount -- N1,200 -- when coming back.
A worker in the state, Mr. Tunde Sanni said the strike had affected him in a serious way, adding that the strike did not allow him access to his bank for withdrawal.
At the filing stations, though the official pump price appeared on their fuel dispensers, most of them now sell between N100 and N120 per litre.
The long queue of vehicles at the filling stations scared some people away and resorted to buying from thriving black markets at N1,200 for a can of four litres. Places where black markets thrive are Mokola Roundabout and some places close to Apata Depot.
If people in Oyo State are experiencing mild hardship on occasion of the strike, not so in BENIN CITY, Edo State.
The strike has crippled economic activities in Edo state leaving citizens of the state in hardship since the directive from the leadership of the NLC was heeded to the letter.
The state secretariat known as the Palm House has been under lock and key; while the Government House looked like a graveyard as workers completely stayed away. Apart from the strike action, the effect of fuel scarcity had already taken a toll on the citizens of the state and unfortunately, the strike action came and worsened the already bad situation.
A litre of fuel in the state is sold at N150, forcing commercial bus drivers in Benin to park their vehicles leaving residents of the state to walk long distances to their homes. To worsen the situation, the NNPC mega station situated along Sapele road which is the only place that members of the public could buy fuel at the stipulated price stopped sales immediately the strike commenced.
Transport fare from Benin to Lagos which in a normal situation was supposed to be N1,300 is now N1,800. From Benin to Port Harcourt which used to be N1,800 is now N3,000. Also fares for transportation in the cities in the state has skyrocketed. Commercial buses operating within Benin City now charge N30 while taxis charge N50 against twenty naira and thirty naira previously charged respectively.
The same situation could also be said about food stuffs because vehicles who convey the products from the villages now found it difficult to purchase petroleum product unless they go to the black markets which is very expensive because ten liters of petroleum product at the black market is sold for N2,500.
Consequently, the situation made the cost of food stuffs and even other products very expensive and many homes who were unable to afford the prices of these products have been left gnashing their teeth, hoping that the situation would get better.
The story is not different from what people are experiencing in ADO-EKITI, the Ekiti State capital. Prices of all commodities have gone up considerably, while prices of foodstuffs have doubled in the market, as retailers and wholesalers have increased prices of their food items
Motorists have withdrawn their vehicles, while the only few ones that are plying the roads have increased their fares beyond normal prices that people are used to before the strike
For instance, the Akure- Ado Ekiti , a distance of about 300 kilometres that motorists charged about N100 now attracts N300, Ado Ekiti - Abuja that previously attracts N1,200 before now is N3,000. The taxi cabs that charged about N30 before now charge between N50/N60 per drop.
As a matter of fact the popular commercial motorcyclists otherwise called okada now exploited the advantage of the strike and fuel scarcity to charge exorbitant prices on the roads.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Activities Grounded in Asaba
Headline: Nigeria: Economic Activities Grounded in Asaba
Body_Text:
Asaba — Economic and social activities were at low ebb Wednesday in Asaba, Delta State capital and adjourning towns as workers joined their colleagues in the nationwide strike by organized labour.
Already the price of a litre of petrol as at Wednesday morning sold as high as N150 at black market rate in few filling stattions that opened for business in the early hpurs of the day.
Checks by our correspondent in Asaba showed that all government offices financial institutions, schools and majority of the petrol stations in the city were under lock and key while residents remained in their houses.
The absence of petrol and commencement of the strike forced most motorists to keep off their vehicles. Most motorists had following the apparent scarcity of petrol at the various stations deserted the major routes in the city, a situation that enabled commercial vehicles and okada riders to charge excess fares for passengers embarking on long distant journeys within and outside the town.
Further investigations revealed that charges to short distances within the city cost N40 as against the usual N20 charged before by taxi drivers plying the roads while those on long distant travels paid as high as N150 additional fare for a journey that initially cost N80.
Also the prices of food stuff sky rocketed as news of the strike filtered the ears of market women and traders at the busy Ogbeogologo market in Asaba.A tuber of yam that sold for N100 three days ago now costs N150 while the prices of fish and meat had increased tremendously.
In addition, most private business outfits which opened, complained of patronage.
However, Asaba metropolis was calm yesterday as residents went about their normal activities without any molestation by any group.
The personnel of the state police command had taken measures to check any breach of peace as men of the anti- crime squad patrolled the streets to ensure safety of citizens in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Rights Activists Fault Plea-Bargain for Ex-Governors
Headline: Nigeria: Rights Activists Fault Plea-Bargain for Ex-Governors
Body_Text:
Enugu — Human rights activists have said it will be a tragedy for the Nigerian society, if the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), allows former governors accused of massive corruption to go free, on the basis of the agreement reached from their plea-bargain with government.
Executive Directors, Tropical Watch, a human rights body, Barrister Sam Mbah and Mr Nana Ogbodo, told THISDAY in Enugu, that allowing the governors to go scot-free after collecting some or half of their loot would be a bad precedence and could rubbish the corruption war.
Mbah and Ogbodo said such arrangement would instead of reducing corruption, increase it, since those involved would feel that all it takes is to return what has been stolen if caught.
According to Ogbodo, that somebody returns what he has stolen is not enough, the punishment is to serve as deterrent to others. If what everybody knows is that if he is caught, he will give some and hide some, is tragic.
Ogbodo also said the import of plea-bargain was that the suspect, having acknowledged that he is guilty, seeks a lighter sentence, but this does not mean that he has to go free.
"If you go free, then it portrays our laws as being made for some and others are free from it. Don't forget that in some states up North, some people who have stolen yams, have their arms amputated.
Don't forget that here in the South, some people who stole N1000 or N2000 are wallowing in jails, yet somebody who stole billions of Naira is negotiating a light sentence or nothing at all," Ogbodo said.

Original_Search_Title: What is to Be Our Fate?
Headline: Zimbabwe: What is to Be Our Fate?
Body_Text:
Harare — THE first sentence in my letter last week, Vanhu vatambudzika -- the people are suffering -- was sent to me by a reader. Ever since that article I have had no rest from Zimbabweans.
Ms Siboniso wrote to say that I should be careful not to go home soon. The CIO may be looking for me. Another wrote to ask when the new dispensation will come.
Before the death of our beloved Masipula Sithole, I had asked him to critique a section of my History of Zimbabwe. The issue here is very simple. When will we know that the end time for the regime has come? Sithole said that certain things must happen, before the end comes.
First, the opposition forces must pose a real threat to the good life of the establishment. In order to do this, they must rise to the same level of force that the regime has. Such force must be able to stop the regime from functioning, and make life unsafe for their investments.
In that respect, the international community can do a lot more than it is doing. Investments abroad can be frozen until a settlement of internal forces is reached. The US has done that many times to enforce its will on weaker powers. In 1979, it was the Nigerian military government, which threatened to seize British oil interests over the Zimbabwe issue, which brought Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher to the table. Ian Smith was just a crony of the imperialist forces and found his ship in a dry dock.
But the key to any negotiations always lies with the balance of forces internally. The guerilla leader, Mukuru, issued a fatwa against 50 members of the Muzorewa government, including three high-ranking officials. The Smith-Muzorewa officials could no longer travel anywhere in Zimbabwe without military escort. One thousand whites per month, fearing for their physical safety and having lost their investments were taking the chicken run to South Africa.
There was also a kind of suicidal atmosphere among the Zimbabwean populace. The Smith-Muzorewa government maintained a vicious murderous special force called Dzakudzakus. Despite their viciousness, Zimbabweans threw all caution to the wind and were able to talk freely in buses, despite the consequences. We have not reached that stage yet.
This boldness originated with ZANU youths, nicknamed the chain gangs. At first the chain gangs trained themselves as a defence force against ZAPU thugs in Highfields. Though very few, usually not more than 12 in any one gang, they were brave and suicidal. They never ran away from fights, whether these were provoked by ZAPU or the Smith forces. With bicycle chains attached to sticks, their weapons were more blood-letting than life threatening. The dreaded secret police could only go into Highfields in troop strengths.
We have not yet reached that stage of parity between opposition forces and government forces. The opposition forces, by chosing the non-violent approach, make themselves sitting ducks for state-sponsored bashing. It is a long term approach. The state may in fact enjoy bashing exercises, but it loses moral ground in the long run. Governments do not function entirely by force, but by moral authority.
The idea of an economic collapse is crucial to whites who have a comparison basis with the western world. The regime members themselves have only Southern African Development Community (SADC) countries as their comparative basis. Having lived on yams and mpupu in Malawi, Mozambique and Tanzania during the liberation struggle, Zimbabwe is still a paradise to them. Besides, with the power of the state, they control two sources of foreign exchange, the platinum and diamond mines coming on stream as well as the diaspora parallel exchange rate. The Catholic Bishops, in their April 5 statement wondered aloud: "The people of Zimbabwe are suffering. (Yet) A tiny minority of the people have become very rich overnight, while the majority is languishing in poverty, creating a huge gap between rich and poor".
The bishops plead with the regime for a change of heart and mind, seeking repentance. No, my beloved bishops in Christ, the prodigal son did not repent because of his good heart. He lost everything before he came to his senses. But take heart. Sithole did say that miracles can happen. Sithole said an internal awakening can happen when regime insiders foresee that a prolonged attrition could wipe out all gains of the past, and an internal revolution (read coup) may try to forestall this by negotiating some sort of arrangement with opposition forces. In this scenario, the church can play a pivotal role because of its ability to nurture forgiveness. Whoever tries to fight the church fights a losing battle, or is a fool or a knave, because churches thrive under persecution. The churches have access to foreign radios, their own newspapers; have a weekly sermon and freedom to preach. No government can take away these dangerous instruments against oppression.
Until the balance of power shifts in favour of opposition groups, there will be no negotiation. Politicians do not negotaite with people they can bash at will, anytime, anywhere. They will negotiate with the opposition, however, when there is a real chance of being bashed themselves, and when their internal supporters face the possibility of losing their status, wealth and prosperity.
All men are self-interested. There is no such thing as repentance for its own sake. People repent when there is a real threat of going to hell. The regime members are doing well, thank you. They can fly to Singapore any day of the week. Their children are studying abroad. They drive Mercedez Benzes. They have real US dollars in their safe boxes under their beds. Life has never been so good. Why should they bother with Tsvangison?

Original_Search_Title: Ehindero And the Police Image
Headline: Nigeria: Ehindero And the Police Image
Body_Text:
Lagos — How time flies. It is pleasant to know that nothing on earth lasts for ever. At last! The querulous Inspector General of the Nigerian Police Force, Mr. Sunday Gabriel Ehindero, has been ordered by the President Umaru Yar'Adua, to proceed on retirement and hand over to the most senior Deputy Inspector General of Police (DIG).
"Pompa! Pompa! Ehindero, like the old Roman bride, the Nigerian Police High Command cried!" The time had come to escort the bride to his new home in Ondo State. While a band of police flute-players and torchbearers assembled at the door of the mansion in Lious Edet House in Central Area Abuja, Ehindero embraced his former colleagues and started sobbing. "Goodbye! At least I made my mark!" he may have said.
At home in Ondo State, a lavish banquet would commence, with festivities lasting throughout the day or days. Mr. Ehindero would there after, be remembered for setting a superb table for even the commonest meals, and this feast would as expected be every ones delight.
Of the seven courses that would follow, roast boar, pounded yam and egusi soup, would be the principal dish, but there would also be more exotic viands, including Phrygian grouse. He is going to be very moderate about his celebration, after his meritorious service of 36 years and six months to his father land. This moderate display of wealth would come at a time, when Nigeria's vulgar rich are inflicting fifteen and twenty course dinners on their friends. Ehindero the guests would agree would have shown remarkable restraint in his menu.
As Ehindero celebrates, one question that must be asked, is, was he really a successful I.G.P.? Before any body draws a conclusion, one would want to find out whether Ehindero did anything meaningful, during his tenure of twenty seven months as I.G.P., to improve on the image or the situation that existed in the police force fifty-one years before his appointment?
On January 2nd, 1954, in the then Daily Service, the highly respected journalist, publisher, politician, federal parliamentarian, governor, and statesman, late Chief Victor Olabisi Onabanjo asked this question about the Nigerian Police in an article entitled "Tell us!
He wrote "Police Department needs a thorough overhauling. You only need to listen to nasty remarks about members of the force being made by magistrates. Police constables and even officers go to court to tell deliberate lies in order to obtain convictions. A recent example was at the hearing of the charge against Councillor Fasola for giving the police false information. He was discharged; and my guess is that it was the police that were acting falsely. But can't the public be told whether promotions in the force depend upon the number of convictions a constable or a prosecutor obtain? If this is so, then it means that promotions depended on the volume of lies that can be told against innocent citizens. The government must act; the situation has become intolerable".
In the case of the late Chief Bola Ige's murder, which was the last television drama staged by Ehindero before he was asked to disengage from the police by the president, another master of journalism practice, Dr. Olatunji Dare, comments on the performance of the Nigeria Police, exactly fifty three years and four months after Chief Onabanjo's question about the Nigerian Police Force was left unanswered. He writes "The case grew bizarre last week when, in one of his farewell interviews, President Olusegun Obasanjo said in response to a question about the epileptic power system that Ige had tried unsuccessfully to fix as Minister for Power and Steel; that Ige had been murdered on the orders of a drug baron, whom Ige had netted as Federal Attorney-General. Ehindero commented about it in the next day, claiming that the drug baron, aforementioned, had organized Ige's murder from prison, having tried to bribe Ige to drop charges against him and failed. The police, Ehindero said triumphantly, had infiltrated the drug baron's network to crack the case, a tribute to their professionalism. The products of this diligent manhunt, or "monumental breakthrough" as Ehindero described it - would be paraded in public the very next day.
That day came and went, but no parade. No drug baron, no hired guns. The usual problems of logistics, apparently. But the next day, Ehindero made good his promise, producing for the benefit of curious reporters the alleged drug baron, one Alhaji Moshood Enafemi, described as a convicted drug baron, and three others, presumably the contract killers. All four were nothing if not bemused, as was the audience.
The star of the parade was a masked figure, who claimed that while serving a jail term, he had overhead Enafemi openly soliciting prisoners who might be willing to kill Ige for a fee. And when Ige was murdered, the masked man claimed, Enafemi had slaughtered a ram for the prison community by way of celebration.
It was not immediately clear why the masked fellow had kept this secret to himself for six years.
But it was clear that his last "breakthrough" had not resulted from superior detective work, contrary to Ehindero's earlier claim. In his re-telling, the break-through had "divine providence" stamped all over it. The masked man had simply walked into the police office one day and spilled the beans.
How credible is the figure in the mask? What is his motive? What forensic tests have been carried out, where, when, and by whom? Does the forensic evidence link the alleged killers indissolubly to the crime? Do other kinds of evidence corroborate the claims of the masked figure? In whatever case, why rush to parade the alleged mastermind and conspirator and paint potentially prejudicial pre-trial information all over them instead of arraigning them in court in keeping with due process?"
Hum!! What else can one say? You can now judge whether or not Ehindero did anything to improve on the image of the Nigeria Police. I wish him, happy retirement life all the same.
-Omonhinmin, staff of Voice of Nigeria, wrote from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Scarcity Halts Activities in Abia
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Scarcity Halts Activities in Abia
Body_Text:
Umuahia — ECONOMIC and social activities were almost grinding to a halt in Abia State following the scarcity of petroleum products in the state.
Since the recent increase in prices of petroleum products by the federal government, all the major marketers have not been selling, while the Independent marketers that have the products sell between N130 and N150 per litre of petrol and N120 per litre of kerosene and this increase appears to be having a ripple effect on goods and services.
Many commercial vehicle operators have parked their vehicles due to their inability to fuel their vehicles, leading to the hiking of fares from one point to the other in the state. For instance, journey from Umuahia to Aba which used to attract N150 fare is presently attracting N 250, while a trip from Umuahia to Owerri has increased from N150 to N200.
Also, prices of food items have increased with seven cups of garri being sold for N100 and a medium --sized tuber of yam going for N250 from the former price of N150. Similarly, sellers of commodities that are brought from the northern part of the country have doubled their prices which they attributed to high cost of transportation.
Some of the attendants at the multinational petroleum companies said they did not know why their stations have not been getting supplies, adding that they come to work everyday without having anything to do.
Vanguard however found out that most of the major marketers have been diverting their supplies to filing stations outside the cities where they sell at exorbitant prizes, just as they sell their kerosene at night to roadside dealers.

Original_Search_Title: Mbale Selects Miss Uganda Representative
Headline: Uganda: Mbale Selects Miss Uganda Representative
Body_Text:
Saturday's Miss Uganda pre-selection in Mbale served up better hopefuls compared to the thin pickings in Gulu and Fort Portal.
The beauties: Regional winner, Mary Kancho Tawaali (C) with 1st runner-up, Deborah Nakumiza (R) and 2nd runner-up, Tina Magambo. The three beat 11 other contestants. Photo by Eddie Chicco
The 14 girls at Mount Elgon Hotel had better gait on the ramp, better creative dress designs and mostly breezed through Ben Mwine's question and answer session. The two judges Miss Uganda franchise holder Solaya Zalwango and event coordinator Jessica Kyeyune admitted to difficulty in choosing the eventual winner.
"The girls were very prepared and some had participated in previous pageants which made choosing a winner so hard," Zalwango told a post event media conference. The "creative wear" dresses aimed at showing off the contestants' ingenuity.
That counted for much at the uganda telecom-sponsored event since the girls' aptitude would be mostly pegged to how well they explained their designs. There was sisal rope embroidered onto black linen, a pandering to the sponsor by a contestant who adorned her dress with air time cards, yam leaves of white paper and a rare exhibition of our national colours by way of red and yellow beads dangling from the hem of a black dress.
There was collective shock when Mary Kancho Towaali beat crowd favourites to wearing the regional winner's sash.
The 23-year-old Uganda College of Commerce [Tororo] student may have been inconspicuous among the glow of her prettier competitors but the shouting red anti-HIV/Aids ribbon emblazoned on her white dress struck a chord. The acting awards the soft-spoken Business Studies Marketing major had won in high school also helped in making her elocution faultless.
Towering Deborah Nakumira, a Kampala International University student and Tina Magambo, an A-level school leaver were announced first and second runners-up respectively.
The duo gets another shot at landing a place among the 24 Miss Uganda finalists during the Kampala pre-selection on June 9. Seven other contestants; Rebecca Obinya, Justine Nambuya, Maria Bella Owino, Gloria Kyasimire, Rachel Atuhairwe, Justine Magooba and Mona Lisa Mudeenya will also have another go at th e Kampala pre-selection.
The next regional pre-selection takes place at Jinja's Crested Crane Hotel on June 2 while the final takes place in Kampala on July 28.

Original_Search_Title: Now We're On Our Own - Mamman Vatsa's Son
Headline: Nigeria: Now We're On Our Own - Mamman Vatsa's Son
Body_Text:
Kaduna — HER death came quite unexpectedly. There was no news about her illness. Even her passage was not known within the city of Kaduna until many days after. But soon after the news filtered in, some sympathisers, especially the masses, began to flood to the home of the Vatsas to commiserate with them over the death of Hajia Safiya, wife of Major-Gen. Mamman Vatsa who was killed for his alleged role in the coup to topple the regime of his bosom friend, Gen. Ibrahim Babangida.
For the immediate and extended families, the pains of bereavement are grave and deep. Today, they mourn their departed mother who sacrificed a lot and suffered sundry deprivations since the death of Gen. Vatsa.
According to Jonathan Vatsa, the youngest brother of the late general and former minister of the Federal Capital territory, "it's not every woman that can endure what our late mother, Safiya went through. Despite the storm and hardship, she stayed and died in her husband's land. What brought her over to this part of the world or over the shores of the River Niger was marriage.
"In times of difficulty, she stood like a rock. Her advice to us were words of wisdom. A vacuum has been created in this family by her death. However, God is the source of everything. He will surely bring a replacement.
"I was about to abandon my schooling but she insisted that I must complete my education in spite of the difficulties. She practically ordered me to go back and continue my education. We are where we are today because of her. It is however one of God's promises that people will be born and would die one day. As for us, the biggest legacy we can give in her honour is to take up the challenge from where she stopped."
Mallam Umaru Garkuwan Malali lived with the Vatsas for over three decades. "I knew the Vatsa family during the civil war when Maj.Gen. Mamman Vatsa married his wife newly.
He brought her to Kaduna... It was here (in Kaduna) she was converted to Islam and changed her name to Safiya. Her death will remain a huge blow. She was a rare gem and a dedicated housewife. May the Almighty Allah grant her paradise."
Haruna Vatsa, eldest son of the deceased also spoke to Saturday Vanguard on his mother and the uncertain future that the children now face as orphans.
How did your mother die? Was she sick?
No. She was not really sick. She had slight cough a week ago and I asked one of my brothers to take her to the hospital. He came here from Abuja last weekend. She said she was even feeling okay when my brother came to take her to the hospital. We talked last with her on Sunday when she called me to say that some people wanted admission into the Nigerian Military School and asked whether I could help. I tried to find out from her whether they obtained admission forms and she said yes.
We talked for about an hour on the telephone. She called my younger brother, Jubril asking that we come home for the weekend. We promised that we'd be here next Friday (yesterday) because we thought it was better to travel after the handover of power on May 29. So, that morning, I was at home when somebody called me to say that my mother's cough was worsening and I said they should take her to Giwa hospital in Kaduna . But my mother insisted that they should call one man for her. The next thing we heard was that she had died.
And suddenly you become orphans...
Yea, that's life for you. You are talking about losing a mum, someone who had been taking care of you all your life. Somebody that had been living with you all your life and suddenly, you hear that she is no more and she was not sick as such.
Yes, we are complete orphans now. The second light in the family is gone. But we are proud of our parents. We have always had God to take care of us. We know that the Almighty Allah who we worship will see us through because He has always been with us throughout. We don't rely on any man. All you need is to have faith in God and He will see you through.
We learnt that your mother was damn devoted to your father...
She was really devoted to him, she was a devoted wife. We, young men of these days, do not have such devoted wives. My mother had so much love for our father and her husband. Why her devotion and love for our father continued to grow even after he died, we couldn't understand but I think it was just loyalty to the wedlock. You marry somebody for better, for worse. I know that if it was my father that found himself in the position my mother found herself, he would have equally done the same for her.
Your mother wished that your late father, Major-Gen. Mamman Vatsa should be reburied and his benefits in the military paid. What is going to happen now that she did not realize her dreams before death struck?
I don't want to go into such issues now. I think that since the light is still glowing, we are there. We are there for everybody. But let us mourn her first.
How many of your father's friends have visited or called to sympathise with you people since she died?
My father's friends had long abandoned us. For those who have not experienced this type of betrayal, I will keep advising that people don't rely so much on fellow human beings. Some people (father's friends) have not seen us in the last 20 to 22 years. We are not expecting anybody to come and mourn with us. It is a wicked world, a world full of sycophants.
Even President Olusegun Obasanjo that is going on May 29, some people he was seeing every 20 minutes, won't see him again. When his time is up, only a few will go and sympathise with his family. That's life for you. Most of the people you see here are those close to me or my mum or my brothers and sisters. We are not really waiting for anybody to come or not come. There is no genuine relationship.
When my father was around, the sort of people who were around him were many. But when I look around now, I begin to imagine whether those people were actually spirits because none of them comes around again. They could equally come tomorrow; but they are not here today.
How did she wish to be buried?
The place she was buried is where she had always told us that her husband had wished that both of them should be buried (pointing to labourers who were preparing a tomb).
But generally, what have been the experiences for the Vatsa family since the death of your father?
I can open a school and start lecturing people on our experiences. It is more than what one can learn in schools. People are hypocrites. Only the former Head of State, Major-Gen. Muhammadu Buhari has called to sympathise with us. He was not in the country when our mother died. But he not only called us on telephone, he sent somebody to represent him during the burial. Also, Gen. Garba Wushishi is like a father to us. He also was not in town, but sent people from his office to represent him. Another person who has called is Umaru Nasko, son of General Gado Nasko. We are not sure that his father, General Nasko has heard about the death.
Why did your mum not travel to her home town for 12 years?
It was because she was devoted to our father. In this place, our mum could stay for two months without electricity. If you came around to stay with her, she would ask you to go and look for what to eat and stop being her guard. Since my mother lost her mum in 1992, she has not travelled to Calabar.
So, what does the future hold for you and your other siblings without your parents?
It is the time to take the bull by the horn. God will see us through definitely. If it is man, he will not give his neigbour water to drink. But with God, everything will be alright. We've seen it all. She had been with us for the past 25 years and she was never scared of any problem. Everything will be okay because nobody stays forever. People should just be prayerful.
The most important thing is to try and be good. If you live a very good life, there will be no problems. Some people have left whatever they are doing to stay with us since her death and I pray that the day I die, my friends will continue to relate with my own children the way they relate with me, just like they did when my father died. Friendship should be like a chain. It should transcend from parents to children and continue like that. We used to hear that some friends can even be better than brothers, but we don't know where our father's friends are now.
What lesson have your learnt from all these experiences?
The lesson is that this world is vanity. If you are a governor today, the same way you went up is the same way you'll come down. Some governors today will cease to be governors on May 29; they will come down and we shall continue to buy fuel from the same filling station and they must queue to buy fuel. We must start to educate ourselves about life.
What other things do you think were of more interest to your late mother other than keeping the dreams of your father alive?
Nothing interested her more than relating with the ordinary people. All the people you see outside were her friends. People make mistakes for others to learn from it. This is a new chapter in our lives. My mum was fond of relating with people who were not of the same class. She did not like classification of people.
When she was alive, sometimes, you would see her go out to buy roasted yam and put oil on it and eat. She would go out to buy roasted corn or fried groundnut and she would just start eating it. She could also dress up and if you asked her to go and change because the dress was not suitable, she would refuse, telling you that such vanities were not her concern because she had passed the age where she would be so attracted to fashion. But you know your true friends when you are no longer in a position to give contracts. However, my mum lived a fulfilled life.
As the eldest son in the family, what will you do to keep the family united?
This is one thing that comes with good upbringing. What brings disunity in a family is if you have people who don't know their problems. I know my problems and my brother knows his problems just as my sister knows hers. We must be united because there is no daddy or mummy anywhere anymore. That's the bitter truth we must forever live with.

Original_Search_Title: Who Bells the Cat ?(13)
Headline: Nigeria: Who Bells the Cat ?(13)
Body_Text:
The lure of the flesh has always been a stumbling block between man and his God. This, indeed, was stressed by the Almighty when He decreed that a man should take a wife unto himself if he finds it uncomfortable to stay pure. The provision, over the ages, has, however, not been satisfactory enough for most of our men who find it irresistible at one time or another to stray into the warm embrace of other women, religion, law, vows and promises, etc not withstanding.
For many of these men, the idea often starts out as adventures, for the fun of doing it, or to join the bandwagon. But, oftentimes, child's play may become life size. Adventure becomes misadventure and, without planning for it, a second wife is born. So, how does he break the bad news to his wife?
Typical of your favourite column, some respondents will be sharing their experiences with you in this and subsequent editions. I hope you will find them interesting. First, let's take the story of Steve (57), engineer/businessman, narrates what happened after his young girlfriend got pregnant. What did Steve's wife do? Find out as story continues below:
I definitely cannot afford to let that happen to me. I have seen such happen to people I know. Children tend to have sympathy for their mothers when these sort of things happen. Before you know it, they will see you as their mother's enemy and, therefore, their enemy too. And from such children, it is impossible to get the best, no matter how much you spend on them.
They will never be grateful. They will see whatever you do as their right and not something they should show appreciation and gratitude for. Woe betide you if anything goes wrong with you in any way. You will suddenly find yourself on your own. They will not really show you the care you need or deserve.
It happened to my own father, and I didn't want such to happen to me. It was, therefore, important that I get the support of the children, whenever the cat was let out of the bag.
However, I had to be sure that Rita will have the baby and that her parents will support her too. I knew that with the pregnancy, they will have very little choice anyway. No parent will want her child to have a bastard child, they will do all that is possible to force her to bring a man that will accept it.
Not to talk of when someone was willing and ready to take the child and mother. And though Rita was not sure of how they would eventually react, she too became optimistic that all will be well. That was how we decided that she should not terminate the pregnancy and that I would take care of her as much as God would allow.
Initially, she was a bit confused, and so, for about a week, she refused to see me. But, I kept calling her at home and the office, warning that if she gets rid of the baby, I will never see her again. It worked and after asking a few friends, she agreed to marry me. She, however, insisted that her parents would want something official to be performed.
They would not like the idea of her just moving in with a man. They will insist that she is living a lose life and is not officially recognised as a wife in my family. I was to meet her mother first and a meeting was arranged. Initially, Rita's mother did not like me at all and she did not hide it. She told me she had always had high hopes for her daughter and had dreamt often of the day she would give her hand out in marriage to a young man of her own age group, not someone of their own age group.
That it was a disgraceful thing, considering their circle of friends. People would think that she did not raise her daughter well. Short of being rude to me, she began to ask questions about my family, finances and what I intended to do for her daughter. She told me point blank that she would not stand for any nonsense from my wife, children or family towards her daughter.
That her daughter cannot live with my wife and kids in the same house and that I had to cough out whatever I wanted to give to her child and grandchildren before I grew too old to know what to do. She was a no nonsense woman and I knew I had to win her support first if I wanted to get anywhere with Rita or her father. I promised her all her wishes will be taken care of and she had nothing to worry about.
Still, Rita said her mother gave her a real tough time before she finally consented to speak to her father on our behalf. But, she insisted that Rita had to break the news by herself first. Eventually, I was invited and I promised to perform all the traditional wedding rites and take good care of their daughter.
The following day, I sent a thank you card, accompanied with several tubers of yam, a bag of rice, carton of milk, beverages, sugar and toiletries to the family. Though Rita's parents were not poor by any standard, and would not need them, I felt at a loss for what to do and just wanted to get their approval.
That settled, the next step was to tidy up my own end. I bought a new jeep for my wife and gave her N2m with a promise of an additional sum very soon. It came to her as a surprise. I had never given her such a lavish gift before. She was very grateful. She prayed fervently for me, promising to rededicate herself to making our marriage more meaningful.
I felt very guilty and scared of what will happen when she discovers that everything I was doing was an attempt at bribing her or forcing her to submission. I told her I just hit it big on a deal and wanted to spoil her a little for all her love, care and support. She was happy. It lasted until she discovered my real reason for playing Father Christmas.
My decision to do those things for my wife was my uncle's idea. After I had settle things with Rita's parents, I went to see him on the need to assist me to get people to talk to my wife. He advised me that the mistake most men make when they decide or are forced to take second wives is to go public with the idea even before giving the woman at home a chance.
That I should not jump into conclusion that my wife will be uncooperative about the whole thing. That I should call her into confidence and tell her what I'd done by myself. Even if I have to put in one or two lies to make her see reason with me, it was better than calling friends and family members together to force it down her throat.
To most women, he said, it was an act of dismissal of sort. It was as if they were being told that they had failed as wives and, therefore, they needed to be replace with a better woman to do the job. He told me he could read my wife properly and that if I handled things well, she will even give me her total support.

Original_Search_Title: Goje's Wife Gives Recipe for Development
Headline: Nigeria: Goje's Wife Gives Recipe for Development
Body_Text:
Gombe — Wife of the Gombe State Governor, Hajiya Fatima Yelwa Goje has called on the people of the state to ensure the prevalence of socio-political peace and stability in order for government to fulfill its plans for the citizenry and the state in general.
Making the call, while receiving the delegation of the state branch of the National Association of Yam Sellers (NAYS) at the Government House, Hajiya Goje explained that the time has come for politicians to imbibe the culture of tolerance for peace to prevail, stressing that it is in an atmosphere of peace and tranquilly that government could address pressing issues that bother on the development of the state.
She noted that re-election of her husband was another landmark in the history of the state, adding that "as more development projects and social amenities would be delivered by the Governor in his second term in office".
The Governor's wife, who commended members of the association for their support for the administration assured them that government would impact the dividends of democracy.
"As the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) has put in place projects and programmes that are relevant to the people's need," she added.
Speaking earlier, the leader of the delegation, who is also the chairman of the association, Alhaji Yusuf Na'Baili represented by his Assistant Secretary, Malam Mohammed Garba, said they were at the Government House to register their support to the government.
While commending Goje for the success recorded so far and security agents for their roles during the April general elections which saw a violent free polls, he called on the people of the state to shun all forms of differences and contribute their quota towards the development of the state, and promotion of democracy in the state.
The chairman appealed to the government to consider the inclusion of the Association in its activities to enable it have a sense of belonging expressing their commitment and locality to the administration in its quest to achieve growth in all the sectors of the state economy.

Original_Search_Title: Three Robbery Victims Hospitalized for Gunshot Wounds
Headline: Nigeria: Three Robbery Victims Hospitalized for Gunshot Wounds
Body_Text:
Three robbery victims are presently receiving treatment at an undisclosed hospital for bullet wounds they sustained during a robbery operation along a road leading to a yam market at Kubwa in Abuja.
The robbery operation was said to have been carried out by a five-man robbery gang that placed barricades on the road and robbed motorists and residents of their belongings.
According to the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) police commissioner, Mr Lawrence Alobi, the three victims are responding to treatment at a government hospital.
The police commissioner revealed that one of the robbers was shot by the police during the operation and he died before reaching the hospital.
City News reporter learnt that one of the victims of the robbery was reported to have used his cell phone to inform the local police in the area about the robbery operation, which prompted the divisional police officer (DPO) of the area, Mr. Yahaya Danjuma a Superintendent of police to lead a team of policemen to foil the robbery.
The police commissioner said when the police later searched the scene of the crime one double barrel gun cut to size was recovered.
He said other members of the gang fled the scene and are being trailed by detectives from the command.
He urged members of the public to always give information to the police on the movements and activities of criminals to enable the police arrest them.
"These criminals are not living in a world different from ours,' Alobi said.
He appealed to the public to give the police information about any suspicious character, assuring that such report would be properly investigate and action taken. 'Go to the nearest police station in the FCT and confer with any senior police officer or in the alternative, call us on 0803 288 8006, 09-234-1470 or 09-234-1199,' the police commissioner said.
Three robbery victims hospitalized for gunshot wounds

Original_Search_Title: AEPB Officials Demolish Illegal Structures On Roadside
Headline: Nigeria: AEPB Officials Demolish Illegal Structures On Roadside
Body_Text:
Traders who sell along the sides of major highways along airport -Gwagwalada road were on Saturday raided by officials of the Abuja Environmental and Protection Board (AEPB), who burnt their stalls.
Those who were lucky saved their goods from being carted away while the unlucky ones had their goods confiscated and arrested.
City News reporter observed some of the stalls burning while traders had to enter into small bushes to retrieve their goods that were hidden from the AEPB officials.
Isah, an onions seller, lamented that he lost his goods in the raid. "I sighted them raiding my co-traders' stalls and decided to run away to avoid arrest," he said.
He said the AEPB officials descended on his stall and burnt everything inside.
Mama Sheri described the action of the AEPB officials as unfair. "We were not given any prior warning and this is a trade that I have been doing for some years to feed my family," she said.
She appealed to the AEPB authorities to allow traders to continue with their sales in the area due to the high patronage.
Travellers who normally park by Giri junction to buy yams, tomatoes and bread were nowhere to be seen as the whole area was deserted due to the raid.
Officials of the Federal Capital Territory administration had recently vowed that all illegal structures in the FCT would be removed before May 29.

Original_Search_Title: Globacom Premier League: Fresh Battle for Super Four
Headline: Nigeria: Globacom Premier League: Fresh Battle for Super Four
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dolphin FC of Port Harcourt and Nasarawa United of Lafia arrive from continental assignments abroad to go straight into different battles in the domestic scene. The "Solid Miners" from Lafia head to Port Harcourt for a postponed league game against the "Fishes" tomorrow knowing victory could help their push for a place in the season-ending Super Four tournament.
But the wards of Daniel Amokachi must first push to the back of their minds the infamous antics of their Sudanese opponents Al Hilal that saw the Nigerian side eliminated from this years Caf Champions League in Khartoum on Friday.
However, the hosts need the game more because anything short of victory will confirm the reigning Coca-Cola FA Cup champions for relegation to the less glamorous national division one. Dolphin will push their case hard at the University of Port Harcourt ground with Coach Ifeanyi Onyedika urging on Akeem Yakubu, Peter Ebibor, Junior Osagie, Suleiman Samaila, Gbenga Ajala, Oluwole Johnson, Seyi Ogunsanya, Jacob Aikhiobare, Nasiru Hussein and the hero of their Confederation Cup penalty shoot-out victory over Hassania Agadir of Morocco, goalkeeper Chijioke Ejiogu.
Nasarawa, now dropped to the Confederation Cup where Dolphin are, and billed to play compatriots Kwara United for a place in the group phase next month, will rely on Chinedu Ezimora, Oluwaseun Falodun, Kingsley Ezebili, Andrew Nwodu, Foster Amadi, Nasiru Salisu and the man who got a red card in Khartoum, Stephen Saviour. The two teams will however use this as a dress rehearsal for their Week 17 games coming up on Wednesday.
With only 15 points from 15 matches and firmly stuck at the bottom of group B, Dolphin must win this one and then go ahead to beat Lobi Stars in Makurdi on Wednesday to consider themselves as standing some chance of escaping the drop.
On their part, Nasarawa, with 21 points from 15 matches, can go up to second position if they win today and consolidate with the home game to Gombe United on Wednesday.
Enyimba FC, whose chairman Felix Anyansi-Agwu recently watched his beautiful wife give birth to twins (a boy and a girl) and still in celebration mood, head group B with 31 points from 16 games, and are the only team to have picked a spot in the Super Four billed for Abuja from May 16-20. Gombe United, Insurance and Lobi Stars each have 23 points, with Bayelsa United on 22 and Zamfara, Nasarawa and Akwa all on 21. Group A is headed by Enugu Rangers, who have 27 points, same as second-plced Wikki Tourists.
Heartland of Owerri, who pounded Nasarawa FC 7-0 in the FA Cup Round of 64 on Wednesday, are third with 26, Kano Pillars have 25. Prime FC have 23, Kwara United have 22 and defending champions Ocean Boys have 21. Just as Lobi Stars are hosting Dolphin and Nasarawa entertain Gombe United on Wednesday, El-Kanemi FC, who have had a season to forget, take on Bayelsa United at the Maiduguri Sports Center .
El-Kanemi have 16 points from 16 matches and are set to go down to the lower cadre of the domestic game if they fail to win any of their remaining matches, except Zamfara, Nasarawa and Akwa fumble exceptionally.
Super Four hopeful Insurance FC of Benin light up the ancient city when they entertain top guns Enyimba at the Samuel Ogbemudia Stadium. The other game is between new boys Akwa United of Uyo and Zamfara United, which takes place far away from the former"s home turf due to the ban clamped on the place by the Nigeria Football League, following mayhem that attended their Week 15 game against Enyimba.
In group A, leaders Enugu Rangers seek to consolidate when they visit Kwara United at the Ilorin Township Stadium. The Afonja Warriors are just returning from defeat of famous Union Douala of Cameroon in the third round of the Caf Confederation Cup and know they now stand a real chance of survival after two wins lifted them to 22 points. It will be a game to savour in Ilorin .
The tail of the campaign has seen the "Flying Antelopes" moving back to their home-ground from the Beverly Hills Stadium in Nnewi, and resurgence by a couple of strikers who had been out injured.
A win takes Rangers to 30 points and close call on Super Four, while defeat means Kwara are toying with their luck. Kano Pillars, in 4th place, are away to poor-playing defending champions Ocean Boys in the Niger Delta. Pillars will confront this game with the mentality of the yam-pounder and it is safe to say the homers will fight even for a single point. Defeat means Ocean Boys are romancing relegation. Victory will take the northern side to 28 points and whistling distance of the Super Four.
On their part, Adamawa are preparing to return to the national division one after only one season, following a poor campaign that has given them only 13 points from 16 matches. Despite hiring a Dutch coach, the newcomers were abject throughout and it can be said that having to play their first five matches away from home-ground did not help their cause.
On Wednesday, they host Niger Tornadoes, a team that flattered to deceive. Niger Tornadoes, at a time reigning at the top of group A, but now down to 8th with only 20 points and relegation noose not far away, know this is one match they must not take lightly. Wikki Tourists clash with Heartland of Owerri in a must-watch game at the Tafawa Balewa Stadium in Bauchi. Wikki are buoyed by the return of Flying Eagles" assistant coach Ladan Bosso, who has been released to help their cause in the last two games of the campaign.
Bosso was with the Flying Eagles at the African Youth Championship in Congo and had been holding forth since the sack of Godwin Uwua, and before the arrival of Musa Abdullahi. Prime FC of Oshogbo have a great chance to lay claim to Super four when they host abject Kaduna United at the Oshogbo Township Stadium.
The Osun State team has shown tremendous will and commitment despite poor welfare and the deaf ears that the government of Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola has turned to the pleas of players and officials for relief.

Original_Search_Title: Market Women At a Loss
Headline: Ghana: Market Women At a Loss
Body_Text:
THE ONGOING re-denomination exercise with various media and other public education campaigns seems to have created much awareness in terms of the fact that there would be a new currency from July but little with regard to the conversions.
This came to light when some Officials of the Statistical Service, led by the Acting Government Statistician, Professor N. Nsowah-Nuamah toured parts of the Makola market in Accra to ascertain how far traders understood the exercise and their readiness for the new currency.
Interaction with most of the traders indicated that they were aware there was going to be a new currency but most of them found it difficult to understand the conversions, especially now that the money was not in circulation.
Professor Nsowah-Nuamah told Business Chronicle there have been concerns that the introduction of the new currency was likely to affect inflation and the Service wanted to find out how far that could be possible hence their interaction with the traders.
"We want to see if the traders understand the concept and how that would impact on prices", he noted, adding that prices would only soar if the traders did not understand the exercise properly.
He said there could be money illusion with the introduction of the new Ghana cedi and pesewa because buyers would be tempted to buy more because they might think they have more money due to the smaller denominations that would be in circulation.
According to him, traders might also be tempted to increase their prices because they could have the perception that selling in the new currency with the lower denominations was too cheap.
Yaa Asantewa, a yam seller told the team that presently she did not know much about the re-denomination but her children have promised to teach her, as there had not yet been any form of education in the market.
Also, Awenye, a tomato seller said though she was aware there was going to be a new currency in July, she has no idea as to how to do the conversion from the present currency into the soon to be used Ghana cedi and pesewa.
According to her, it would be much easier for her to understand the conversion when the currency is in circulation than presently without it, saying, "when it is out and I see it, I will understand and nobody can cheat me but they have to educate us before the time."
The Queenmother of the Kwasia market in Makola, Madam Lydia Abbey and her vice, Mrs. Diana Amon who were able to do a few conversions, indicated that there had not yet been any form of education in the market on the re-denomination exercise.
They said they would soon carry out a sensitization programme for the market women in groups, particularly item leaders who would in turn educate their colleagues.
Some traders noted that they were not very conversant with the radio and television adverts because they often got home late and so tired that they did not pay attention to such adverts.
In response to the level of awareness of particularly market women, Prof. Nsowah-Nuamah pointed out the need for the Bank of Ghana (BoG) and the National Commission for Civic Education (NCCE) to step up the education to reach all categories of people to ensure the success of the exercise.
According to him, "apparently, they have not done much. There should be vigorous education and it should come from all quarters."
He said it would be important for the BoG and other stakeholders to consider other ways of reaching the market women in view of the fact that most of them did not pay attention to adverts in the media.
He mentioned the need for all, such as District Assemblies, community leaders, religious bodies and schools to get involved in the education and urged the market women to endeavour to educate themselves in order that they would not be cheated when the new money comes out.
He said the literate should enlighten the illiterate and discuss with their siblings, particularly school children, saying, "when they are well versed, there will be a spill over effect."
Also, he pointed out that though the Statistical Service did not have the responsibility to educate the people on the exercise, they were planning to hold a workshop for market women from whom they collate prices for their work to help them understand the exercise.

Original_Search_Title: Man, Student Flogged for Stealing
Headline: Nigeria: Man, Student Flogged for Stealing
Body_Text:
A labourer was yesterday given eight strokes of the cane on the orders of a magistrate. The man was also sentenced to thirty days in prison for stealing twenty-nine tubers of yam belonging to a market woman.
Habila Ishaya, 27, of Jabi village took the tubers when he was cleaning the premises of Utako modern market with his team. He told the court that he was tempted to steal the tubers.
He was caught when a security man attached to the market nabbed him.
Police investigations said Ishaya confessed to the offence, adding that six tubers of yam were recovered from him.
The seller Dorah Samuel said she reported the matter to the Utako police station when she discovered that the tubers, valued at N3, 000, were missing.
The accused pleaded guilty to the offence after the contents of the first information report were read to him.
Prosecutor Amikor Obed informed the court that he was a first-time offender and prayed the court to try him summarily.
Magistrate Idayat Akaani sentenced the accused to thirty days in prison or a fine option of N1, 000.
Meanwhile, a junior secondary school student was on April 17 publicly whipped for stealing.
Abdul-Rashid Ibrahim was caned on the orders of a magistrate for stealing seven tiles from the premises of Sageto Construction Company, where he was doing a vacation job.
He was caught by the company's guard and taken to the police.
The teenager pleaded guilty to the offence during his plea last Thursday and was sentenced.
Magistrate Bashir A. Alkali said: "You have committed an offence of stealing, contrary to section 287 of the Penal Code. I will not sentence the accused person to jail because of his age. I will therefore follow the Sharia law and order that he be given twelve strokes of the cane according to section 7 of the Penal Code and the tiles taken from him."
The teenager was taken outside where a policeman administered the punishment on him.
The victim told City News that he stole the tiles to get money to pay his school fees.

Original_Search_Title: Of MPs' Self-Defeating Tactics
Headline: Uganda: Of MPs' Self-Defeating Tactics
Body_Text:
IT was simply not hard to believe: Ken Lukyamuzi goes missing? As soon as news trickled in that the talented melodramatic politician was absent without leave, I only suspected that he had gone underground. But his daughter and heir to the Lubaga South throne Susan Nampijja, pleaded otherwise.
She told Parliament that she believed her father had been abducted from his Wankulukuku home by plain-clothed security personnel on Sunday, April 15. His mobile phone, she said, had been switched off since. Given the mayhem Kampala has been engulfed in lately, it was only necessary to offer the Lukyamuzis the benefit of the doubt.
"Government should tell us where The Man is. Where is our Party President? The home was invaded by unidentified men who were armed and wearing civilian clothes," she pleaded.
However, the tone in her voice betrayed a deep sense of guilt. It seemed she was certain she wasn't speaking the truth but was under strict instruction from papa to stir controversy in the House--like he often did during his stint as MP--to keep the Lukyamuzi family tradition alive.
"I would like to assure my colleague that your father is not lost. He is under the mayuni (yams)," Okot Ogong (Dokolo) quipped, sending the House into a frenzy of laughter. Mr Lukyamuzi's Natete "yam experience" in 2003 has turned out to be the highlight of his jokey political career.
FLASH BACK
The Conservative Party leader organised a rally at Natete and invited prominent opposition MPs from northern Uganda to discuss the raging war between government and the LRA. Police sent him notice barring him from discussing the war but okayed the rally and said he should discuss the environment, road construction and HIV/AIDS.
People assembled in droves. Then, a mobile police patrol pickup on routine guard in the area, drove past the assembling grounds totally oblivious of the activities going on and as soon as Mr Lukyamuzi spotted the 999 blue patrol vehicle, he spontaneously dashed to a nearby yam garden, burying himself in the tall leaves. Bystanders looked on in total amusement.
FAST FORWARD
On Thursday, as I prepared for an early morning meeting, I was interrupted by the buzzing sound of a newsroom telephone. This particular intercom is in the general newsroom and cannot be used to make calls outside the company. I assumed it was an in-house call. "Hello, Daily Monitor, this is John Ken Lukyamuzi The Man."
"Ohh Honorable, how are you doing?" I asked. "I am doing great," he said. "I am voluntarily presenting myself to the Police this morning at 9:30 at CPS and I am inviting you my noble friends to witness this glorious event."
I cut in: "Ohh, we heard that security men besieged your house and whisked you away as an exhibit in investigations over the Mabira demo or is it that you mysteriously disappeared?"
"My friend," he replied," it was necessary I go into hiding. These men plain clothed and gun wielding had come for me. I had to protect myself."
"Where have you been hiding?" I asked. "Haa, that's a matter of national security," he cheekily replied as we exchanged pleasantries and hung up. Yes, it was him, vintage Ken Lukyamuzi.
True to his word, he presented himself before police, was arraigned in court and granted bail. But then there was a technical problem. The court's cash office was closed. He couldn't pay bail. Not bent on spending the night behind bars, Lukyamuzi had paid his bail even before he was arraigned in court, it turned out. He had it all figured out.
But the same cannot be said for the youthful MPs Odonga Otto (Aruu) and Erias Lukwago (Kampala). Although the duo's planned demo against the infamous "Kiboko squad" was noble and rightly so to defy calls not to assemble at the constitution square, they sheepishly booked a one way ticket --and a weekend--to Luzira prison after they presented themselves to police on a Friday afternoon without any summons.
It is a given that if you are arrested late Friday and charged in court, any bail application is thwarted because the court's cash office is guaranteed to be closed. It would seem it was a calculative move by the MPs, albeit desperate.
It was a sight to behold as the two firebrand opposition MPs tried to resist arrest and were manhandled by ten policemen. (See picture). Enraged by the treatment, Mr Otto reportedly slapped Deputy Police Commander Emmanuel Muheirwe and disgraced himself in the process. Although I pitied the duo, I couldn't help but marvel at their determination and eagerness to spend the weekend in prison. For all your antics, welcome back Honorable MPs!

Original_Search_Title: A Booming Slave Market in Our Time
Headline: Nigeria: A Booming Slave Market in Our Time
Body_Text:
Birmingham — I've always taken the submission that there is something devilish about power and wealth as one of those moral aphorisms employed to temper the impetuous and the too ambitious until a wayfarer whom I gave a lift called my attention to the fact that Jesus did not refute the claim by the devil that all earthly power and wealth belong to him (the devil) during that famous temptation of Jesus. After failing to convince the very hungry Jesus (he just finished fasting for forty days) to turn stones into bread, the devil took him up to a height from where he showed Jesus a panoramic view of all the kingdoms, sovereignties and wealth of the world and told Jesus that all these belong to him (the devil) and that Jesus will easily have them if he prostrates and worships him (the devil). Indeed, it is very instructive to observe as my friend did that Jesus did not put up a disclaimer to the devil's very serious claims of ownership of these principal forces (power and wealth) that rule the world today. Another instructive revelation is that these 'demonic' patrimonies can easily be inherited by simply mortgaging one's soul to the forces of darkness.
These revelations are very instructive, if not, how else can one make a sense out of the roles of people like Babangida, Abacha, Bokassa, Mobutu, Mugabe, and indeed most of our (s)elected leaders today who through power and wealth have destroyed their people and themselves. Of course for him to be the devil, those that co-operated with him must end up in shame.
Indeed, there is something evil about power and wealth and one of the greatest expressions of their dehumanising regimes of all times was the experience of the once legitimate slave trade. The month of March, 2007 marked the bi-centenary of its 'abolition'. It was an ignominy that dented history for over four hundred years. While it lasted, over 12 million Africans where marketed. Out of this number, over three million died on way bill. Slaves were made to endure long lasting journeys in shackles. British ships alone made about 11,000 slave voyages. In 1781, one hundred and thirty-three African slaves where thrown overboard the ship 'Zong' into the freezing sea. Then, in 1807, with interventions of nobles like William Wilberforce, the Slave Trade Abolition Bill was passed in the British Parliament. The 'lower' House of Commons were more disposed to the passage of the Bill than the 'upper' House of Lords that took additional two years of persuasion before they reluctantly acceded.
Very telling is the fact that the macabre dance was supplied with rhythm from both sides of the divide as our African forebears were active participants in the deals. Were they not the vendors while the white man was the consumer? Slaves were bought after due market processes like negotiation and bargaining. They were not abducted. This complicity of our forefathers in the evil merchandise is most unfortunate. Shall we subscribe to the notion that they were exploited or taken advantage of because of their relatively 'low' level of awareness when history is replete with instances where many of them fought gallantly even against the white man with his superior arsenal when it came to safeguarding their own territory or property? Hence, it will be unfair to them to say that they didn't know what they were doing.
This is why some people can ever contemplate sharing the feeling that perhaps such selfishness that leads to man's inhumanity to man or being willing arrows in the bow of the devil is in the gene of the black man. God forbid! But then, what compunction, positive lesson, influence or change has the ugly experiences of the past had in the affairs of the present generation of blacks to prove otherwise. If slavery equally means being perpetually hooked to the greed of power and wealth, are we better off today? What has changed? If there is change in form, is there any change in content? Beyond neo-colonialism, it is as though history is repeating itself today. As the world marks the two hundred years of the official end of the slavery experience, many disillusioned Africans today would wish that their forbears were among those that were sold off into slavery. By now, they would be bona-fide citizens of America and Europe and wouldn't be going through the trauma of unsuccessfully seeking visas to check out for good from this 'God forsaken African continent'!
My country Nigeria will soon celebrate her 50 years of independence and self-rule. It will be 50 years of the reign of darkness where the fittest (the powerful and the wealthy) survived through brazen forms of tyranny, brigandage and rascality while the masses slaved in perennial penury. It was as though we got independence to put ourselves in bondage with over 90 per cent of the resources in the hands of less than 10 per cent of the population, a cabal of heartless hypocrites who callously insult our collective intelligence and even impudently mouth the name of God as they embark on the inquisition of their people in the name of government. Social institutions are either non-existent or have deteriorated irredeemably.
On the health sector for instance, my people die in droves that could have lived. Yes, avoidable deaths. And one does not have to be God or medical personnel to be sure. Only two months stay here in the United Kingdom has revealed too many things. Almost every second house over here has a healthy old man and/or woman of over eighty. Many are in their nineties, some in their hundreds. Statistics from the tombstones at the cemetery shows that the average age of the deceased is over eighty, an indication that it is possible for death to come naturally at the end of life, after a happy old age.
