The mortality rate is too low not just because mosquito and malaria have been eradicated but more so because there is a functional health sector. The National Health Insurance Scheme is working. In my country it has remained a political gimmick for over twenty years.
Over here, every community has what I will, for lack of a better description, call a five-star hospital where UNTH or UCH can only possibly serve as a subterranean sewage pit. Indeed, the aesthetics of ordinary grass lawns alone in these hospitals is enough to revive a patient admitted for depression. Should the sick look out from the dilapidated windows of UNTH today, the harassing view of Ogbete market, where human beings have a lot in common with the diarrhoea-ridden goats they have brought to market, the patient will possibly need the unavailable oxygen machine to breath again. Among my people, the mortality ratio is the exact opposite. Majority of deaths occur at infancy, followed by "untimely deaths" which are announced "with deep sense of lose but with gratitude to God!"
Here in the UK, there is a strong patronage of fruits and vegetables as recipe for longevity. Should one then ever dream of longevity for my people when the average family in my village don't know what is appetiser or dessert not to talk of conceiving fruits (economic trees) as necessary appendages to food. Woe betides that child who goes to eat mango or pawpaw after any of the major meals of yam on bare palm oil or garri with fishless soup. He is not only undisciplined for eating in-between meals which is a sign of gluttony, he is above all inconsiderate for doing what is capable of truncating tomorrow's feeding because that pawpaw could have been sold to augment tomorrow's meal. For this, he will receive a thorough beating. These are the different strokes of poverty among my people. Of course those in Aso Rock eat apple and popular legend has it that a specie of it subversively cut short their life and life time ambitions. The obsequies for our dead health sector were recently held with our Vice President's trip to London aboard a presidential jet to mend a torn ligament incurred during a light morning exercise. The final interment was done by our president elect in a German hospital where he went to have a bed rest from exhaustion since according to him, he was not sick.
The ruin of the education sector is best described by the alleged response of one of the Northern Governors who said that he is not bothered by all the negative press reports about him since 95 per cent of his subjects are illiterates who can neither write nor read what the 'stupid' journalists were writing about him.
On security, it is not an overstatement to state that every Nigerian police officer on the road is an armed robber since he is raising his gun with his right arm while collecting your money with his left. A friend once contended that in the event that the men 'on duty' fail to stop him if he comes across a police check-point, he will respect himself, gently park his car and go to them with the twenty naira and tell them, 'please collect your money to avoid accidental discharge!' But the sub-human living condition of the Nigerian police is one of the saddest commentaries on our irresponsibility as a nation. Are we not being too pretentious handing a gun to a hungry man and expecting him 'to serve and protect with integrity?
On general welfare, it was once credited to a Second Republic Minister that we Nigerians are not poor because we are yet to start eating from the dustbin. How true and prophetic he was then because compared to what is obtainable today, we were indeed very rich then. Today, eating from the dustbin is not just a metaphor for poverty but the conventional way of life for many. A visit to the nearest dustbin will relay that poverty has turned our men and women into scavengers that struggle over 'wastes' with vultures, the natural lords of the manor. One of the national dailies once interviewed these human scavengers and they generally confessed that they live on high dosages of antibiotics to contend with the infection which they normally experience while ransacking the putrefying bins in search of what they can lay hold on to make ends meet.
Rev Fr. Ikwuka, CSSP, wrore in from Birmingham, United Kingdom

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo'll Occupy Aso Villa Till Oct. - Apostle
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo'll Occupy Aso Villa Till Oct. - Apostle
Body_Text:
Lagos — Apostle B.C. Esiri is the Host Minister of the Soldiers of Christ International Ministries with headquarters in Benin, Edo state. He spoke to correspondent ETAGHENE EDIRIN on revelations which he has concerning the Nigerian polity. Excerpts!
What did God tell you about Nigeria?
God loves this country so much and wants to use Nigeria for a revival which will go through the church to the society, the country, Africa and the world.
For sometime now, these revelations have been withheld. Satan tried to disturb, causing a lot of problems. But this time, God has decided to put a final full stop to it. That is why I've come to explain certain things.
Nigeria's problem is a spiritual one. It is a problem many people cannot understand. There's a kind of fight in the spiritual realm. We can see that from the scriptures in the lifestyle of Isaac and Ishmael. When you read Genesis 17:18 - 21 and Genesis 21:7-14.
In Galatians, Paul also referred to this fight when he said "the children of the bound woman cannot inherit, or drag anything with the children of the free woman".
And this is a spiritual matter; you cannot understand why Atiku is fighting Obasanjo.
If anything does not take place in the spiritual realm, it cannot manifest physically.
Although you see if physically in terms of money and misunderstanding, but the actual truth is that there is a spiritual tussle between the Muslims and the Christians.
You can see that when somebody in West Germany sometime ago drew a cartoon of Prophet Mohammed and suddenly Muslims in the North started burning churches.
How do you understand such a thing? You cannot because you cannot relate with what is happening. To us who are spiritual we know what is happening.
Something happened in Germany, they did not burn churches, but in Nigeria they started burning churches. That's because the Muslims there don't have power, but here they tend to overshadow everything.
As Christians, we must look at the problems of Nigeria from the spiritual aspect of life.
I read in a newspaper where Pastor Tunde Bakare was trying to give his support to General Buhari. I'm bringing this matter out because many ministers do not look at the scriptures to see what it says.
Buhari could be a good person morally. The issue is not morality but what the Bible says.
I want to let my friend Tunde Bakare know that even though Buhari is kind, Buhari is not fit to rule this country, in the sense that at a time he made it clear that he would Islamise Nigeria.
Even Sani Yerima of Zamfara state said the same thing. I wrote him and told him that he cannot do it. I opposed it and prayed against it.
So, you'll discover that all these people you're seeing now, they are not the right people, including Atiku. I've been saying it. Atiku cannot rule this country, Buhari cannot. Even Yar'Adua cannot rule this country.
That's why so many things are now happening. Things are not going the normal way. Physically, it looks as if it is Yar'Adua, I can assure you, when that time comes, he will not enter. Why?
Why?
Those of us who are looking at the spiritual aspect of Nigeria are conscious of it.
Nevertheless, Obasanjo cannot go beyond October this year. Politically, he cannot go beyond because God is bringing somebody to rule this country.
Whether we like it or not, Obasanjo will not go away easily like that, at that time. The poll may not give the results the way we want.
We must understand one thing. The one-eyed man is better than the blind. So among the blind people, Obasanjo is still better for now. That's the truth. So I want Nigerians to take it easy with him and give him a little time. Because these people we're seeing on board now, none of them will come in. they are all having one problem or the other in the spiritual realm.
Obasanjo has messed up the whole system, nevertheless, like I've said, God is giving us leaders of his choice who will be better than Obasanjo.
The truth of the matter is that God wants to do something in this country Himself.
On power supply
Another thing we need to address is the Power Holding Company of Nigeria. This has been a problem in this country and the Federal government just kept quiet as if nothing is happening.
I want Nigerians to know that God is annoyed with that body. So they have two options; either they lower their tariff or they will allow God to dry up the dams. Because there is no essence of collecting money from people every month and you will not give them the light. And you keep collecting the money without shame?
God is not happy about it because these are pharaohs that we now find in Nigeria. Anything that fights against progress is another type of pharaoh, and these kinds of people must get out.
So PHCN, I'm giving you this warning, if you keep collecting this money without giving the people light, I give you between now and the next month, you'll see what will happen to your dams.
They will dry up, and your existence will stop.
On political god - fathers
The political godfathers we have all over the country who have become a kind of curse to the politics of the country, I want you to take note as well. That money, they'll not pay you back and you'll not enjoy it.
Because the role you're playing in politics is destructive. You get one hooligan, get money and begin to campaign to put him there.
And that's how we get agbero leaders nearly all over the place.
So god-fathers in politics, take note of what I'm saying. I man what I'm saying, because when a prophet speaks, if he's a true prophet, it will come to pass.
That evil money you're using for politics, you'll not get it, and you'll not live to see that money. Take note of it. Because these things must stop, you cannot be sponsoring demonic people into positions of authority.
God is waiting for any person or party who wants to constitute a problem in the society. God is in control, we are praying.
Anyone who is carrying guns, be you party member or senator, know that there are people like us praying.
We'll pray and you'll die with your people, down to your fourth generation, because enough is enough.
So all those thugs and young boys who follow these people to cause problem, let me tell you, it is foolishness because these people you're fighting for, their children are in London and America.
And you come out and push your chest forward that you want to fight for them.
I want to let you know today that if you continue in it, you'll die with the politicians.
There are decrees that God is now putting up in this country. No man can understand it. We understand it because it's a serious matter.
The people have been tricking us and tricking the poor people.
So please forget about making war and go and vote. Stay quietly and let there be peace in this country.
You may ask what must be done to Obasanjo now; he cannot stay there throughout, yes. Leave him alone, give him a chance. His time will come and he'll go away.
I can assure you, this year whether he likes it or not, he must go away.
On Federal schools
Federal government must continue to take responsibility for the federal schools or else the minister and those who connive to sell out schools will fall into the hand of God.
We cannot see why some states in the western part are paying examination fees for their students, and here is the federal government closing up the schools they met. Is it not a disgrace?
The federal government should repent.
The minister has been a very good woman but I want her to change and reconsider the situation. Because God is going to take over the matter against her if she continues with it.
You cannot close federal schools and deprive poor people the opportunity of going to schools. Yet you say free education. What are you talking about?
This year God is fighting all kinds of pharaoh who are standing against progress. Bad things will be dealt with this year. The wrath of God is going to fall on them, because they are the custodians of idols.
There cannot be progress when you have idol worship. It has come to the stage when Nigerians can even serve yam, and fish, and even sand.
We are complaining about the government going bad, but those worshiping idols are contributing towards the backwardness. It is not only Obasanjo, it's the entire system, because it's a spiritual matter.
The king should take not that if you're a leader and you promote idols, take note that God is coming for you very soon. They will give way this year because God is highly annoyed.
On a final note, the people should pray that every bad policy of government which is meant to wrap up the poor people will not continue.
God is not interested in negotiation.
Go to Abuja and see how the poor people are suffering.
Abuja is supposed to be the capital city for everybody, but it is now meant for the rich. And everybody is hailing Mallam El'Rufai.
The truth of the matter is that if you make your streets beautiful and your people cannot eat three square meals, of what use is the beauty of the roads.
Okada people are there, you chased them away, now supply them what you want you cannot do it properly.
These are issues that need to be addressed. I want those vying for presidency and governorship to know that God is going to bring down any one or sector that stands as a pharaoh against the people.
The politicians are going about kidnapping and so on. I want to let them know that if you kill to enter, you will not enter, you'll die as well. The Bible says whatsoever a man soweth that shall he reap.
So take note you thugs, you assassins, you terrorist, you armed robbers, take note that God is coming for you. Everybody will begin to get their reward.

Original_Search_Title: DCE Chased Out of Office
Headline: Ghana: DCE Chased Out of Office
Body_Text:
THE DISTRICT Chief Executive (DCE) for Sene District Assembly (SDA) in the Brong Ahafo Region, Madam Cynthia Titiriku Danso has been banished from entering the capital of the District, Kwame Danso, to transact business as the political head of the area.
The official vehicle of the DCE has also been impounded by the youth, who have handed over its ignition key to the Police at Kwame Danso, on the grounds that she was using the official vehicle for her personal duties without attending to office work.
The people are accusing her of not respecting the traditional rulers and even her New Patriotic Party (NPP) executives. She has also been accused of not being punctual to duty and reporting to work at her leisure time.
Speaking to The Chronicle in separate interviews, the youth from the district, covering the three traditional areas and the constituency executives of the NPP, have all called for the removal of the DCE. It is only those in the Kajaji Traditional Area, the home town of the DCE that did not accuse her or call for her head.
Madam Titiriku has been accused of being corrupt, distrustful and disrespectful to traditional authorities and engaging in mass acquisition of properties.
The spokesperson for the three traditional areas, which include Bassa, Dwan and Wiase, Nana Otchor Adu II, also the Acting President of the Wiase Traditional Area, called on the government to investigate an alleged embezzlement of ¢ 1.3 billion belonging to the assembly by the DCE.
The chiefs, the youth and the NPP constituency executives have also urged the anti corruption institutions, such as the Serious Fraud Office (SFO), the BNI and other relevant institutions to investigate to ascertain where the DCE, Madam Titiriku had money from, to put up four houses at her hometown, Kajeji within a three-month period.
These groups disclosed to The Chronicle that they could not understand how the DCE, within three months of recovering from sickness, which took her away from the office after her appointment as DCE, could put up such magnificent buildings.
The Chronicle's highly-placed source at the District Assembly also shared the same sentiment of the people, adding, "some of us were shocked when we saw the buildings during our visit to the town when the DCE's mother passed away."
The source endorsed the decision of the three groups namely, NPP, the youth association and the traditional authorities, to investigate the operations of the assembly, as they were ready to co-orperate.
Nana Adu II, also the Akyeamehene of Wiase Traditional Area buttressing his point on the DCE's disregard for tradition, disclosed that on one occasion, a three-member delegation from Bassa, Dwan and Wiase traditional areas was sent to Madam Titiriku to plead with her for the release of a charcoal vehicle impounded by the assembly.
According to the chief, when the delegation went to the DCE, instead of her to personally come to meet them, she rather sent her driver to tell the chiefs that she would not have time for them, since she was traveling.
Nana Adu told the paper that, one of the delegates, Nana Krontihene of Bassa Traditional area, then suggested that they should sit down and wait for her.
The spokesperson told The Chronicle that later, Madam Titiriku had second thoughts and invited them in.
Nana Otchor revealed that when they had access to her, a lot of suggestions on how to resolve certain issues were raised, including the lifting of the ban on burning of charcoal in the area.
Another issue that the traditional authorities accused the DCE of, bordered on discrimination.
According to Nana Adu, during the celebration of this year's yam festival by four traditional areas in the district, Madam Titiriku donated only two crates of minerals, one crate of Guinness, 15 plastic chairs and ¢ 1million to three traditional areas namely, Bassa, Dwan and Wiase out of the four, but donated ¢25.5 million to Kajeji Traditional Area, because she hailed from there.
Since the incident occurred, the DCE had switched off her mobile phone and efforts to get her to comment on the issues have proved futile.

Original_Search_Title: Life At the Mercy of Political Nangaism
Headline: Kenya: Life At the Mercy of Political Nangaism
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Recently I witnessed a bizarre scene in Rift Valley Province that left me wondering whether there is any difference between our political situation and what is happening in Mugabe's country.
A group of hired armed thugs christened the 'Baghdad Boys', violently stormed a local bar, descended on innocent patrons, beat them up and left them for the dead.
The victims, it was alleged, had openly contradicted the MP in one of his recent political rallies.
This is what is referred to, in other terms, as the thuggish censorship of the freedom of speech. Hardly a week passes without similar cases being reported from various parts of our country. In the General Election year, the frequency of such devilish acts is bound to escalate.
Just like in Chinua Achebe's A Man of the People, very many hooligans are now grouping and re-grouping. They are waiting to exploit the carefree attitude of the electioneering period to terrorise, beat and kill innocent wananchi perceived to hold contradicting views against those of their ideal Nangas and Odilis.
As Achebe demonstrates in his novel, it is yet another season for the flourishing of Nangaism and Odiliism.
I think the essence and value of electioneering periods is often misunderstood both by the politicians and the electorate in general. Most of us take it to mean the flexing of our political muscles and grabbing the most from our long lost politicians. This is partly why we end up sending people with questionable integrity and who have little regard for human life, to the highest echelons of authority.
This is should be a period that is supposed to be celebrated by observing significant freedom rituals of speech that go with the election and ascension to power of the chosen leaders.
These rituals ought to begin immediately Parliament is dissolved for them to achieve the desired results, of positive and constructive criticism. Here, I am thinking of the power of free speech that our politicians so much abuse or approach with dread.
It should be remembered that in most of our African traditional societies, critique of authority was not only an annual event, but ordinarily, it was also the province of certain functionaries such as the queen mother, the divine drummer, court minstrels and so-called praise poets. These people occasionally took the opportunity to incorporate, with impunity, critical comments in the high-sounding appellations addressed to the kings, queens, priests, and chiefs while they sat in State.
Cultural mores encouraged stylised communication that avoided open confrontation with authority. Largely to the public, though, was backstage clandestine discourse ostensibly spoken at the back of the dominant people. This was often veiled rhetoric in the form of indirection, metaphor, proverb, allegory, circumlocution, innuendo, and related literary devices.
Indeed to institutionalise public and open evaluation of wielders of power, communities in Africa sometimes set aside days on which social norms were frozen and verbal and nonverbal behaviour otherwise considered unacceptable were licensed and even encouraged.
During such ritualistic occasions, ill feelings harboured over the past year were ritually expunged and critique of social and political deviants openly verbalised. Children were openly allowed to criticise or even abuse elders. The chiefs and misdeed, misconduct, and scandals were subjects of open reference and public ridicule.
In such events, akin to some of our present communities' rites of passage for example the Bukusu, Tiriki and Tachoni, the deeds of our rulers and elders are brought into the open forum of songs, dances and poetry. They are critically assessed in order to check the extent to which they promote the people's collective aspirations over the past years, principles of leadership, the use and abuse of power. The relationship between the ruler and the ruled is often evoked in applying the criteria of good governance.
In our oral literature lessons, we are often told that our traditional society recognised that it was unlawfully to restrict the speech of any human being, since lives can be saved by the very act of speech. It is often quoted, 'One does not push another's speaking instinct back into the entrails'. In other words, one does not suppress another's instinct to speak.
The instinct to speak is depicted here as an enclosed animal; as it attempts to exit society is forbidden to push it back.
Therefore, as the incumbent members of the August House and others interested prepare return to us for votes this year, let them be well informed that we, the electorate, have a lot in store for healthy dialogues with them. This will especially be on their performance in the last five years as wielders of both the yam and the knife. Let them be ready to participate and accommodate views from the open public court of justice. Let them not shun and suppress the voice of the majority by the use of 'Baghdad Boys'. For it is the majority who speak on the way they desire to be led.
If we have to forge ahead, then a cleansing of our suppressed subconscious is inevitable. Instead of a National Prayer Day, I suggest we have an Open Social Forum Day on criticism before this Parliament winds up its business.

Original_Search_Title: Going Back to Nigeria? Not in a Million Years
Headline: Uganda/Nigeria: Going Back to Nigeria? Not in a Million Years
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA was not conned by Nigeria at the MKO Abiola Stadium in the recently-concluded Africa Cup of Nations qualifier. It was robbed, raped! I saw it unravel.
Shorlty before the match, the Senegalese referee, Diatta Badara,who was appointed to oversee this fixture, walked to the touchline and hugged Bolaji Ojo-Oba, the Nigerian Football Association general secretary. For the uninitiated, it was an innocuous gesture of friendliness.
To those in the know, it was ominous. Allegations that Badara had been bribed to ensure that Nigeria won at all costs were a testimony of what defines the West African country: Corruption.
That reminded me of 1998, when Transparency International released a list of the most corrupt nations in the world; Nigeria came first followed by Pakistan.
The joke is that Nigeria was originally ranked second, but bribed Pakistan so that it (Nigeria) could take first place.
Lagos
I was there recently to cover the Nigeria-Uganda match and the warm reception I was accorded on arrival at Murtala Muhammed International Airport, belied the narrations I was privy to about Nigerians.
But, paradoxically, the need to be cautious still managed to permeate through.
A rather unusual warning, 'Valuable items are kept at owners' risk', is a regular feature in the city. This notice was put up in the two major hotels I lodged in during my nine-day stay.And like they signified, in Lagos you do not know who to trust.
A high crime rate, corruption and other fascinating misdemeanours define a city they say is best toured on foot as a result of the dreadful bumper-to-bumper traffic; it takes about two to three hours to travel 10-20 kilometers, and the outrageous hooting can stretch even the Bibilical Job's patience.
In 2006, Lagos was understandably projected to be one of the world's five largest cities. It is the commercial and industrial hub of Nigeria, with a GNP that triples that of any other West African country.
It has benefited from natural resources like oil and coal. And just like London or Paris, you could tour it for months and still see everything.
Nigerians
But by far, the most distinct feature of Nigeria remains its people. And this is where the legendary stereotypes come to the fore; several locals welcome visitors to disguise their sinister intentions.
Robbers have a field day and nobody seems to care. I realised that you just have to mind your business. I had been advised, while receiving my visa at the Nigerian Embassy, not to move after 7:00pm, when the crime rate starts to peak.
It is easy to fall prey, especially if one is a foreigner, and nothing betrays this crucial fact more than one's accent. I met many 'Good Samaritans' who volunteered to help, but like any other good student, I was often well-armed with the appropriate response.
However, I fell victim to a cab driver in his late 50s, who extorted - there is really no other word - in the excess of 6,000 naira (sh83,000) from me after learning that I had no clue about my the location of my destination.
I was compelled to consult three bodaboda riders over which routes would lead me to my final destination, parting with tips of no less than 500 naira (sh6,800) for each in the process. Driving in Lagos calls for a rare steadiness, and the public service industry scares in its disregard for passenger safety; I sighted some passenger service vehicles without doors.
The typical Nigerian is also an utter stickler for cars, with Hondas, BMWs, Range Rovers and the Mercedes latest models.
There is a particular shortage of Toyota Coronas. And one local wag revealed to me that a Nigerian would easily spend nights on a tree, but own a top-of-the-range Honda.
Abeokuta
My journey from the Muhammed Airport in Lagos through Victoria Island to Karuma close and later Abeokuta, was one that will fester long in my memory.
The temperatures soared to 340C. Abeokuta, situated 72km south of Lagos, is a sleepy town that only rose gradually to life as a result of the first-ever-high profile football game to be played at its MKO Abiola Stadium built in 1974.
It was an amazing choice that was at odds with the past when several of the Super Eagles' matches were customarily played either in Abuja or Lagos stadiums.
The food
Nigerian food represented everything I had ever despised about meals prepared in foreign lands.
Apart from the grilled chicken and boiled rice that I often had for lunch and supper, everything left of Nigeria's most cherished dishes in most of the celebrated restaurants and hotels in Abeokuta was terrible.
The amount of pepper and cooking oil spread into the dishes would have left even the most intoxicated Ugandan upset. Pasted yam was the most prominent of dishes served with red beef in a host of restaurants.
The Super Eagles
The main attraction, the Super Eagles, were exclusively booked in Continental Suites Hotel, and such was the adoration of the everyday Nigerian, that, in an effort to touch the cloaks of their heroes, would receive battering from the security personnel at the hotel.
Classes at a primary school next to the Super Eagles' Hotel were interrupted by fans who were struggling at the wall fences to have a glimpse of the stars they only watch on television.
TV programmes were interrupted by updates of the team's sessions. Victory parades were in full swing, even days before the game, with debates on how many goals the Cranes would carry to Kampala.
Mood in the Cranes' camp
At Gateway Hotel, where the Uganda Cranes were accommodated, the Ugandan community in Nigeria arrived in two buses to render full support.
But such was the imposing presence of the Nigerian fans that the Ugandan army could not be traced.
Within the team, Coach Laszlo Csaba handled the match's groundwork with humour and tact and there was a general feeling of goodwill among the players. "We are determined to play for one another.
We do not have stars in this team, everybody here is a star," Kiazer Chiefs midfielder David Obua stressed the night before the game.
That night, Csaba sat his players down for a presentation that made his charges believe that they were good enough to match the world's best. Famous players like Everton's Joseph Yobo and Victor Anichebe are Nigerians.
Chaos
Press conferences here are an absolute nightmare. Nigeria's German head coach, Berti Vogts, while making his first appearance in front of the local media, was bewildered by the chaotic press conferences in Nigeria.
Even on the day of the match, there was chaos and human traffic. Even a mysterious armoured tank, deployed in true Nigerian flamboyance, was helpless. Tickets were bought off the market and later resold by shrewd con-artists at inflated prices.
Those who would never contemplate spending a cent on a football match resorted to the suicidal, hard-core tactics of climbing walls.
Over 40,000 fans were believed to have entered the stadium that is meant for 15,000 people.
Life in a stadium
Nigeria, when compared to the lukewarm Uganda, football has a long and proud tradition that is of immense importance to the culture of the country.
At Abeokuta, everytime I overheard a couple of Nigerians talking animatedly, there was a fair chance the discussion largely was about football.
Football is a passion, a dream, and, for many, a way of life. From what I noticed, following a team does not start just before kick-off, with a beer; instead it floods a fan's life.
Fully-grown adults with responsible jobs lay awake in a couple of hotels in Abeokuta debating or betting over the expected number of goals. Football is taken so seriously here because it gives fans a place and voice.
Most machinations of Nigerian society go on behind closed doors, whereas football takes place on an open stage. It brings meaning to many young disenfranchised people.
Yet contrary to Uganda, where club sentiments at times override national solidarity, I experienced one of the most attractive aspects of football in Nigeria. There is the sense of togetherness in a stadium.
There is a lot of prejudice and stereotyping. But in the stadium, everyone is equal.
The night before the game, many fans were arrested for disorder. Nigerians displayed a different image from the one their reputation carries, for instance when the refree camcelled Uganda's equaliser.
"The expectations were high. We won, but it was not the way we expected to win. There was nothing to rejoice about. We just had to go home," one Nigerian journalist lamented.
Kanu hosts
The Cranes' loss did not deter my efforts to fulfill a lifetime chance of wining and dining with the Super Eagles' stars at the launch of Nigeria captain Nwankwo Kanu's multi-million photo studio.
Newcastle striker Obafemi Martins and Chelsea midfielder John Obi Mikel attended.
It is not easy to break into celebrity social gatherings in Nigeria, but with the help of a celebrated magazine editor, I did.

Original_Search_Title: Who Bells the Cat (4)?
Headline: Nigeria: Who Bells the Cat (4)?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Hi, The lure of the flesh has always been a stumbling block between man and his God. This indeed was stressed by the Almighty when he decreed that a man should take a wife unto himself if he finds it uncomfortable to stay pure. The provision over the ages has however, not been satisfactory enough for most of our men who find it irresistible at one time or another to stray into the warm embrace of other women, religion, law, vows and promises, etc not withstanding.
For many of these men, the idea often starts out as adventures, for the fun of doing it, or to join the bandwagon. But oftentimes, child's play may become life size.
Adventure becomes misadventure and without planning for it, a second wife is born. So how does he break the bad news to his wife?
Typical of your favourite column, some respondents will be sharing their experiences with you in this and subsequent editions. I hope you will find them interesting. First, let's take the story of Shade (42), a Pharmacist, as she narrates how her husband married her colleague at the same office. Her story:
He said the outing was very important to him, in fact, a matter of life and death. If I failed to accompany him to the place, it meant that I had no respect for him and was ready to leave his house. I was shocked. What was going on here so early in the morning. I quickly told him this was surely the devil's handiwork and that he should get hold of himself and not allow the devil have his way with us. He just turned around and told me that I am the devil at work who wants to disrupt his plans. We started arguing back and forth and finally, he confessed that he was the host of the ceremony we were attending.
Naturally, the first thing that rushed through my mind was a naming ceremony, my husband had impregnated someone and she'd had his child. But I was too scared to ask him just in case it turned out to be true. So, I sat down calmly and asked him when he had been planning such an important event without hinting me about it. He told me he didn't need to plan anything for a long time. So, what was the big event all about? My God! You would be shocked at the way we were talking about this thing as if it were a normal everyday occurrence. He was behaving as if it was the usual thing and that he did not owe me an explanation. Or he did not owe it to anyone to be decent about his actions. He just blurted out that a girl got pregnant for him and he wanted the baby, so he had no choice but to marry her.
Though I could not have expected anything short of a pregnant second wife, I was still shocked nonetheless. I asked how old the pregnancy was and he told me six months, retorting that he didn't know what that had to do with me. Then, I asked for her identity and he rebuffed me saying that since I would meet her later in the day, there was no need for the question. I had never been one for much words too, but I managed to ask if what he'd done was good. He should think of all the long years of suffering together, and after 26 years of matrimony. Just when things seemed to be turning out fine and we should be thinking of settling back to enjoy the fruits of our labour, he'd decided to take on more problems. He replied that the financial aspect of it had nothing to do with me as he would not be spending my money.
I was quiet again, and the next minute, tears were rolling down my eyes. I started abusing and cursing him. If he had dealt fairly with me, God will be the judge between us. Little did I know that I was in for more surprises. He quietly told me not to curse him on his wedding day, a day of immense importance in his life. I stood staring at him for a while, not knowing what to say. I called him a fool who ought to be ashamed of himself. How was he planning to explain his action to the children? He replied that it wasn't my headache, but his. I started weeping aloud, but he warned me about embarrassing myself, stressing again what could happen if I failed to follow him. That was how I attended my husband's wedding ceremony, though lots of people later told me that I was stupid to have followed him. I should have stuck to my guns too. In fact, he was sure that I would fall for the trick and it must have been planned in collaboration with the lady. However, that is not necessary now as the deed was already done.
All arrangements had been made by my sister-in-law, Steven's older sister. Sister Bea and I had never really got on well, especially in the early days of our marriage. We'd had cause to clash a couple of times. But it was always settled for us by other relatives, while I would be asked to apologise to her being the wife in the family. However, over the years, we had both been able to mark out our territories, identifying each other's limitations. A false display of peace and affection naturally settled in over time since we hardly ever saw each other unlike how we used to in the early days. I never visit her, so it was just natural that she too never visits me.
We only meet on neutral grounds at family occasions. And so it was no surprise that Sister Bea was the Chief Hostess and Master of Ceremony and Chief Bridesmaid all rolled into one. She was all over the place, determined to see that all went well, and I would see how she was capable of arranging her younger brother's life. Sister Bea's friends were ladies in waiting, or what could I call them being the organisers, running back and forth. Sister even had the guts to kiss and greet me "Eku inawo eni o, a ri ayo nibe o," meaning, "Congratulations, may the union bear good fruits." I was cold to my bone marrow. Imagine being kissed by a cobra. I managed to mumble a reply of sort, but I was really hurt. This time, Sister Bea had hit me below the belt.
But that was not the only shocker waiting for me. It turned out that the wedding was no little party at all. It was neatly packaged with all the trimmings. But for my bias and bitterness, I would have given Sister Bea and her team a pass mark and even recommend her to wedding organisers. She made sure she brought all the necessary items used for such ceremonies, nothing was left out or negotiated. It was as if she was organising a wedding for a young couple. All the yams and fish and goats were accurately numbered. To crown it all, when the bride was led out to meet her husband and guests, she was wearing the same fabric with me and Steven. No thanks to Sister Bea of course.
The girl and her family turned out to be a nice piece of work. She must have lost her bearing somewhere along the way perhaps due to the influence of bad company.

Original_Search_Title: Please, Nnamani Needs a Job After May 29
Headline: Nigeria: Please, Nnamani Needs a Job After May 29
Body_Text:
EVEN though they are of different generations, I cannot help thinking that Chiefs Ken Nnamani and Pius Anyim today look more like identical twins, politically speaking. Yes, both of them have had the opportunity of serving the nation at the highest level as Senate president. That's not special, you may say, considering the nation's geo-politics which ensures that that office is zoned to the South-East. Far more striking, I think, is the sorry manner their reigns as Senate president ended.
Having failed to secure a return ticket, Anyim "retired" prematurely from politics. With the primaries of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) now concluded, it is also now certain that come May 29, there would be no more job for Nnamani in Abuja. The case of Anyim was quite ironic, though. Here was a young man in his early 40s telling the nation in 2002 that he was "retiring" from politics when many thought it was only morning yet. Politics, they say, is the art of the possible.
Unfortunately, many of our politicians of humble beginnings, once they get thrust onto the pedestal of glory, forget their past. In 2003, this was the bitter lesson Anyim learnt. Earlier in the day, he had chosen to engage the governor of his state, Dr. Sam Egwu, in supremacy battle, given his new status as "number three citizen", forgetting that as a Senate president, he was only "first among equals" in the Senate chamber. But in real politik, the governors hold the yam and the knife as far as local politics is concerned.
It was not surprising then that Anyim could not secure second term ticket, having "bitten the fingers that fed him". To be fair to him, Anyim tried to make the best of a bad situation by adopting an adversarial position against the executive arm of government in the last days, having realized that he stood to gain nothing again. It is precisely in this context also that I choose to situate what some observers are now calling the "eleventh hour" activism of Nnamani as the outgoing senate president.
Given this melodramatic turn of events, the question people are bound to ask is this: Has it got anything to do with geography? My answer simply is no. I honestly don't think the relatively higher turn-over of Senate presidents in the last eight years is a function of the nature of the politics of South-East.
Even then, it is on record that the decision to zone the office to the South-East in 1999 was based on consensus forced by President Olusegun Obasanjo. Nowhere was it written in PDP's book that the head of the upper legislative chamber must be from the South-East. But once it emerged that the president came from the South-West and the vice president came from the North-East, Obasanjo pushed through the argument at the party caucus that, to give the next most visible geo-political zone a sense of belonging, the office must be zoned to the South-East.
Rather, I strongly believe we should blame the actors and not the Igbo system. Perhaps, the best (I mean those with clout and distinguished pedigrees) never really had the chance to rise. For the time he headed that hallowed chamber, Chuba Okadigbo (of blessed memory) brought stature and class to the office. Perhaps, if the pioneer Evan Enwerem (a.k.a Mature) was also allowed to stay longer, given his pedigree, he would also have made some mark. Others who came (including Nnamani) were former political nobodies, as it were.
By next month, the Senate would have wound up but with the shelf still full of bills which would have helped deepen democracy but which the Senate, for reasons far from being altruistic, has ignored. Surely, the issue of the constitution amendment bill, popularly called the Third Term bill, is bound to come up with all kinds of pretenders scrambling to claim the glory for its "demise". In this regard, Nnamani has been more visible both by way of utterances or spins by his publicists to make it look as though credit for the "killing" of the bill belongs entirely to him. Nothing could be further from the truth.
But, the purpose of my penning this article is not to do a postmortem on the ill-fated constitution amendment bill. I would rather save that for another day. However, in the interim, suffice it to say that, regardless of the antics of revisionists, the nation knows those who played double game. If credits are to be claimed at all, I expect people like Speaker Masari to occupy the frontline.
I recall that when the report of the National Dialogue was being presented in 2005 by Obasanjo, it was Masari who first stuck out his neck to tell the president pointedly that "We shall continue to support the executive till the expiration of our tenure on May 29, 2007". Masari had mustered the courage to say that long before it became fashionable and indeed profitable for Nnamani and other latter-day "pro-democracy" activists to don their garish togas.
Today, somehow, Masari has also lost out in the local politics in his native Katsina State where he had wanted to become governor but failed to secure the anointing of Governor Umar Yar'Adua. But, unlike Nnamani, Masari has continued to comport himself without betraying the slightest trace of bitterness. To me, that is the hallmark of a true politician: Magnanimous in victory and gallant in defeat.
One would perhaps still have excused the senatorial haste in passing the EFCC bill had it not become so manifest that what motivated it primarily was political malice and vendetta. It is all too clear that the almighty senate president is bitter that his arch enemy, Governor Nnamani, has a senatorial ticket despite EFCC's "indictment" and he, "Mr. Clean" at the National Assembly, will be jobless after May 29. So, he must hurt the interests of all those who made Enugu governor's ticket possible.
To be sure, I don't condone corruption. But the fact remains that the EFCC claims remain accusations which are yet to be proved in the court of law. If the governor is eventually tried and found guilty, then the full weight of the law should be brought down on him. But, what is deplorable is this lynch mob mentality of admitting charges as guilt.
Balarabe wrote from Kaduna State

Original_Search_Title: Violence - Anti-Riot Policemen Patrolibadan
Headline: Nigeria: Violence - Anti-Riot Policemen Patrolibadan
Body_Text:
Ibadan — An Armoured Personnel Carrier (APC) and a detachment of anti-riot policemen were yesterday sighted at Challenge area, scene of the violent clashes between supporters of the Action Congress (AC) and the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) on Monday.
No fewer than seven people, including a woman who was said to be negotiating the price of some tubers of yam, were believed to have been killed in the violent crisis that broke. The lifeless body of an inter-city commercial driver killed during the attack simply identified as Dapo, was left at a popular filling station until yesterday morning when it was evacuated by soldiers.
Also yesterday, soldiers were deployed to the scene of the clash even as smoke of bonfire set during the crisis by some hoodlums were still raging until the bonfires were dismantled by combined forces of soldiers and mobile policemen.
As tension raised by the violence died down around 10am, the soldiers were withdrawn to their barracks while mobile policemen were still stationed at the scene. They gathered in front of a popular eatery in the area.
The Monday attack, according to eyewitness report was a reprisal to the ambush on the AC gubernatorial campaign train led by a former governor of the state, Alhaji Lam Adesina by suspected PDP supporters around Efusetan area of Challenge in the state capital. In the convoy with Adesina were the party standard bearer in the poll, Prof. Taoheed Adedoja, his running mate, Chief Caleb Oyaniyi and other party chieftains.
During the attack, many supporters of AC in the convoy, including an aide of the gubernatorial runningmate, Ola Osioyemi received several matchet cuts on different parts of their body, following which they were rushed to some undisclosed private hospitals in the state capital for treatment.
However, as suspected thugs of PDP were said to be celebrating the defeat of their opponent during the attack, suspected loyalists of the AC reportedly re-grouped and launched a counter attack, killing three people from the PDP side. The counter attack later snowballed into a major attack where guns, matchets and sticks were freely used.
Bonfire were later set on Challenge highway, making vehicular and human traffic impossible. Though there was no report of vandalisation of property, several ladies were reportedly assaulted by hoodlums while unsuspected private vehicles which passed through the road were held hostage by hoodlums who demanded ransome from the drivers before they could be left alone.
When newsmen visited the Felele home of the former governor yesterday, he was said to be sleeping and demanded for some time to enable him react to the development. The official vehicle of the Commander of MOPOL in the state was sighted in front of his house, with the occupants disembarking to survey the environment.
Meanwhile, the Speaker of Oyo State House of Assembly, Hon. Abraham Adeolu Adeleke has clarified that the joint sitting of the two factions of the state Assembly was in the interest of the masses and not for any political confrontation with the state Governor, Senator Rashidi Ladoja.
It could recalled that since crisis broke in the Assembly more than two years ago, it had been difficult for the two faction to sit under the same roof.
However at the 5th remembrance service of his father, Chief David Gbadagesin Adeleke at Alaafia Oluwa Baptist Church, Fiditi, Adeleke, speaking with newsmen contended that the joint sitting of the two factions in the Parliament Building held last Friday was not an indication that he had shifted to the side of pro-Adedibu lawmakers. Adeleke re-affirmed his allegiance to the 1999 constitution and his loyalty to the Ladoja-led government, saying that the joint sitting was in the interest of the masses and development of the state.
Reacting, to the orgy of violence in the state capital, the Director of Akala campaign organisation, Prince Dotun Oyelade, said "while we don't know the details of what happened at Challenge, the PDP governorship candidate, Otunba Adebayo Alao-Akala, has been appealing to people of the state, most especially his followers, to maintain peace and order and he could not have supported any form of violence."
He added that Akala and leadership of the party were on their way to Oke-Ogun area of the state for campaigns and he had not been in Ibadan for five days.
The AC governorship candidate in his comment, said "I've undergone character assassination from the Governor Rashidi Ladoja-led government all because of this noble cause of transforming the state for the better. Now, with attack on my life and eventual escape from the hands of those assassins, I'm more committed to the pursuit of this cause which I tagged God's own project and I remain undaunted. And come May 29, when I assume office as the governor of Oyo State, the masses will heave a sigh of relief."
The police spokesman, Ola Akinmoladun who confirmed the incident added that mobile policemen had been deployed to keep vigil at Challenge while some people had been arrested in connection with the fracas.

Original_Search_Title: Where PHCN Blames Residents for Non-Performance
Headline: Nigeria: Where PHCN Blames Residents for Non-Performance
Body_Text:
Benin — Edo State is surely a state to be anytime any day because among other things, it is probably the only state in the country where you are constantly put on your toes. It is a popular saying that if you can work and live successfully in Edo State, you could as well not only live in any other part of the country but also in the world over.
The reason for this is very simple, Edo is known for its varieties of crimes which could be seen as a constant lesson for residents. If you are visiting the state for the first time and you are not the determined type, you could be defeated by the enormous of crimes in the state and, thereby lose control of your determination.
Investigations have shown that irrespective of the vocation and assignment in your hands, there is a particular crime that has been designed to be your major headache and this will constantly be worrying you on daily basis.
In a clear term, aside from the robbery activities and other related crimes that have become more or less one of the features of the state with its attendant tension on men of the Edo State Police Command, there are some crimes that are peculiar to other disciplines.
In the health sector, the issues of fake and adulterated drugs are a prevalence thing in the state and this has continued to frustrate adequate health delivery in the state. The NAFDAC officials in the state have been rendered incapacitated by the activities of the fake drugs producers.
In the area of water, there are organized criminals that ensure that water pipes within the city are constantly vandalized so as to frustrate the state water board. This is the same situation the Power Holding Company of Nigeria, (PHCN) finds itself.
Among all the federal and state parastatals in Edo State, PHCN is presently the most troubled service company because of the level of crime being committed against it by some of the electricity consumers in the state.
Apart from the incessant vadalisation of some of the facilities of the company which results in the failure of the system, another major problem confronting the company is the issue of "Hanging ".
According to the Principal Manager (Public Affairs) of the Benin Electricity Distribution Company, Mrs. Grace Nwobodo, hanging in a local parlance "refers to an illegal act of reconnecting electricity supply after the customer has been officially disconnected by the Power Holding Company for non payment of electricity bills.
"This act perpetrated by means of using a bamboo stick as the instrument for hanging back the disconnected service wire in a loose manner onto the main power supply line".
The development is not only giving the authority of the Power Holding a sleepless night because of the attendant effect on the installation particularly the transformers but also making the whole system worrisome.
It is a true saying that since the assumption of duty by the present management of the company in the Benin zone, which controls Edo, Delta, Ekiti and Ondo states, under the leadership of Engr. (Mrs.) Joyce Nwaoko as the Chief Executive Officer, there has been considerably improvement in the service delivery of the Company.
Apart from treating the numerous customers paramount important, she has equally introduced a dimension to the services of the company in the sense that reported faults are being attended to promptly.
Again, in the past, for any community to response to request of new or change of transformers, it was like dropping water at the back of stone and such development compounded the electricity problems confronting the zone.
Interestingly, the testimonies are all over the zone that this resourceful woman has brought electricity service closer to the people. Save for the national epileptic power supply in the country, the situation would have improved more. Workers in the zone and even in all the districts are now alive to their responsibilities.
In the last few months, the zone has distributed and installed over 50 new transformers to boost power supply to the zone and more have been pencilled for the same.
Sadly, the disturbing trend of hanging is making mess of the tireless efforts of the management of the Benin Electricity Distribution Company.
This phenomenon is fast gaining more grounds in the state and particularly in the Benin Metropolis. It is a common sight to see bamboo tress of different sizes and length beside residential houses within the city precisely in the low profiled but heavily populated areas of the city. They are not meant to stake yam, rather those bamboo tress are weapons of power Installation in the area and fearfully, some of the men that engage in this illegal acts have started teaching their male children the act of hanging wire.
Their mode of operation is very simple but deadly on the power holding installation. They usually wait for darkness to prevail where they know that Power Holding officials could not see them, before carrying out this act.
As soon as it is getting dark, they will go to the back or side of their house and bring the long baboon tress that is very dried and use it to hang the peeled service wire that connect power to their house, and hang it on one face of the main service line outside. This enables them to get supply instantly.
The following day, as early as 6.00am, they are already awake and use the same bamboo tree to remove the hung wire and fold it inside their houses.
Investigation revealed that many of the houses that had been disconnected for three years or thereabout are enjoying cosntant light through the bail act, and the residents do these with impunity.
The effect of this is very enormous on the system and even on the person perpetrating the act. In the first place, just as the person is trying to give PHCN a sleepless night, he himself does not have enough time to sleep because he has to wake up earlier than expected to remove the hung wire and many of them perpetually live in fear of being caught by the roving task force set up by the PHCN authority for this purpose.
According to the image maker of the PHCN in Benin, "For the avoidance of doubt, those who engage in this form of illegality need to appreciate the danger inherent in this practices as it thus becomes the proverbial case of cutting off one's nose to spite one's face.
"For those who have suffered adversities as a result of hanging service wires would definitely have a lot of bitter stories to narrate. The list of associated danger is inexhaustible as it includes electrocution leading to outright death or permanent incapacitation or deformity, damage to appliances; loss of dignity and possible arrest and prosecution".
Secondly, this act has been proven to be responsible for the breakdown of the installations of the company particularly the transformers due to the friction that occurs at the point of hanging the wire which does not contact well on daily basis.
On the general operations of the PHCN, in the zone the consequence of this illegal unwholesome act has continued to put the company at a very tight corner.
Mrs. Nwobodo, who is visibly aggrieved over the incident as shown on her face while speaking with Daily Champion in the matter said, " In variably, the danger involved in hanging wires far more outweigh the perceived monetary gain people think they derived from it.
"Prominent among loss being incurred from the practice are, heat loss, which builds up at the point of the loose contact. This loss is unaccounted for and it forms part of the non-technical losses that the company is battling to reduce. The fluctuation caused by this partial connection can also be detrimental to equipment such as transformers, feeder pillars and others.
"Another loss from this menace is the uncollected revenues traceable to stolen energy which is not recorded by meters, as these are usually by-passed in most of the cases.
"The enormity of these losses can never be overemphasized and their effects on revenue collection are catastrophic which impinge negatively on the Power Company's ability to provide system expansion and reinforcement projects" she stated.
One of the dimensions to this crime is that presently some of the people engage in it have become wild and could do anything to protect themselves and this is one of the reasons while the Power company is finding it not too easy in combating this organized crime. The perpetrators are often armed with dangerous objects like sharp machete with which they have inflicted injuries on men of the PHCN taskforce in the past.
One cannot but express worry over the nature of the illegal act and the level of personalities involved. In one of the thickly populated areas of the city, few weeks ago, Daily Champion roving round the area discovered an elderly man of about 70 years supervising his three male children on the hanging process right in front of the house.
The question is what moral lesson or legacy this old man is leaving behind for these children? The children carrying out the act looked very fierce and could devour anybody trying todisturbed them. It is obvious that this aged man grave does not know that he is hanging the future of the children and the children on the other hands does not know that their future and destiny is being hanged too by their actions.
The analysis is very simple, some of the customers say they were over billed and disconnected. Well, if that is the case, hanging of wire is not the right
solution. One would have expected such aggrieved customers to go to the nearest service centre and complain rather than taking action that is inimical to the lives of the entire family and smooth service delivery in the area. Those children who often stay glue to their fathers when the act of illegal hanging of wire is taking place will definitely graduated from that low profiled criminal act and start vandalizing other bigger installations before ending up as seasoned robbers.
Business and other Commercial activities are being affected in the City currently due to lack of power supply because even the little power supply can not be accessed by the consumers because hanging of wire has damaged some of the installations.
Ironically, this same untrained children being taught by their parents on how to hang wire are the same that will lead riotous to the nearest PHCN office calling for the head of any of the staff over power outage.
This is ridiculous.
The basic truth is that the authority of PHCN is doing wonderfully well to ensure adequate power supply and distribution in the zone and precisely in Benin City. It is now the responsibility of every residents of the city to rise up and fight this illegal act.
Street leaders and Community leaders should raise up men and women that will be vigilant enough to apprehend those engage in illegal hanging of wires.
Those neck deepen in this act should be exposed and made to face the shame rather than being glorified bearing in mind that if the system in the area failed, the whole residents will be affected.
The traditional institutions, religious bodies and other interested non-governmental organization should take up the campaign and sensitize the people on the danger of hanging of electricity wire. The efforts of
the in-house task force set up by the PHCN to combat this crime should be complimented.
One of the concerned residents of the City, Mr, Julius Ihonde who condenmed the act said, "Let us know that vadalisation of oil pipelines in the Niger Delta started this way and now it has become a national problem. Hanging of wire and other illegal activities that undermine adequate supply of power is not a crime against the officials of the PHCN; rather, it is a
crime against ourselves and the future of the incoming generations.
"Let us stop hanging the future of our children. That is the reality of the problem on ground", he concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Neither Fear Nor Favour
Headline: Cameroon: Neither Fear Nor Favour
Body_Text:
Last Thursday, the Head of State, Paul Biya, appointed members of the National Anti Corruption Commission, NACC; exactly a year after the public and independent structure saw the light of day. The team is headed by Paul Tessa. Their principal missions are to follow-up and assess the effective application of the government's plan to combat corruption. Members of the commission have been rendered strong as they have all the independence that is required of their task. The law has given them all the prerogatives needed for the challenging function. NACC members cannot be pursued, arrested or detained with regards to their opinions, actions during the exercise of their duty. In addition, the State would ensure their protection in matters relating to threats, insults, violence, assault, abuse against their person and defamation.
The fight against corruption in the country is therefore expected to move to a higher level. Paul Tessa and his men would collect information, investigate, study cases and publish texts on corruption. They are also supposed to seek ways to pre-empt the disgraceful act, by identifying the causes of corruption and proposing solutions to competent authorities. What more, NACC members can initiate procedures on all cases of fraud and corruption that are brought to their knowledge through what ever means. It is therefore important for the members to keep their eyes and ears open. Informal sources of information should be encouraged. After all, their sources are protected and can be exposed only in the court.
This will be a rare occasion for Cameroon to bid farewell to the wrangling, manipulation, favouritism and partiality in the public contracts domain; one of the sectors where "oiling of lips" has often been considered as normal. Members of the commission are obliged to evaluate the conditions of attributing contracts and go out to the field to assure the physical control of the execution of projects. The ball is therefore totally in the court of the group of 12 in whom President Biya has bestowed his confidence. The task is enormous, judging from the present situation in the country where corruption has become the norm in most public services. Cameroonians and above all donor organisations are expecting a new lease in the fight against corruption. The pioneer officials of NACC have six years (two terms of office) or less (three years for one term) to write their names in golden letters in the annals of Cameroon, go unnoticed or enter the records for unorthodox practices. They have the yam and the knife.
It is therefore with neither fear nor favour that NACC members should embrace their task. The fact that they are answerable to the Head of State is expected to place them beyond certain forms of pressure. And judging from the calibre of persons that make up the commission, most of whom have proven their integrity and morality in other spheres of national life, there is no doubt that a real war has been launched against corruption. Their actions would certainly leave no place for embezzlers of State funds to hide. But Paul Tessa and co have to above all stick to the oath of office which they will take at the Supreme Court which calls for strict professional secrecy. In a domain as delicate as fighting corruption, where money changes hands freely, huge personal interests rife and welfare of lobby groups at stake, the importance of professionalism in handling issues cannot be overemphasized. In effect, the NACC members will be judged, not only by the quality of the yearly reports which will be sent to the Head of State and later published, but also by how far they succeed in effectively fighting corruption.

Original_Search_Title: Fufu-Making Machine Invented in U.S.
Headline: Nigeria: Citizen Invents Food Processing Machine
Body_Text:
A US-based Nigerian, Dr Gregson Oghafua has invented a food processor, "Fufumagic", to enable lovers of pounded yam and other staple African food eliminate the rigours of pounding.
Oghafua demonstrated the invention at the Heart of Africa project launch in Houston.
Speaking to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) at the weekend, Oghafua said that "Fufumagic" was a revolutionary food processor designed to allow Nigerians, other Africans and fufu lovers to continue to enjoy their favourite food away from their countries".
He said that he got the motivation for the design from his love for fufu.
"Having acquired a lifestyle of convenience as a result of the nature of my work, I worked hard to develop the Fufumagic, as soon as God blessed me with the original invention in December 2004," he said.
He said that the machine reduced the time it would take in preparing variety of fufu.
"With Fufumagic you just add water to a bowl, flour to a dispenser and pushing two buttons and a steaming fufu is out ready for eating.
"You may even programme your Fufumagic to cook on a delayed basis to fit your schedule".
The inventor disclosed that the food processor was also programmed to cook rice and has a "Manual Mode that allows you to cook just about anything, including stews, soups, gumbo, and more".

Original_Search_Title: 1 Killed in Mbale Amid Land Strife
Headline: Uganda: 1 Killed in Mbale Amid Land Strife
Body_Text:
Kampala — ONE man was hacked to death in a suspected ethnic conflict over a wetland used for growing paddy rice and yams in Mbale district.
The Thursday incident at Nyanza village in Namatala swamp along the Mbale-Tirinyi road, left Mohammed Kinyowe, 30, dead, according to Taire Idwege, the eastern regional Police chief
Idwege said the deceased was decapitated with a machete. The Police said there is an ongoing row between the Bagisu and Bagwere in the area over the use of the wetland.
Idwege said that eight suspects had been arrested, but declined to disclose their identities. "A mob armed with pangas and clubs descended on Kinyowe as he was digging. The mob hacked him to pieces," Idwege said, adding that "his head, hands and legs were severed off and carried away by the assailants."
It took the army and the Police four hours to calm the situation as the enraged relatives of the deceased blocked Mbale-Tirinyi highway, seeking revenge.

Original_Search_Title: April Polls Winners Will Need Huge Dosage of Prayers - Adeboye
Headline: Nigeria: April Polls Winners Will Need Huge Dosage of Prayers - Adeboye
Body_Text:
Lagos — SIXTY-FIVE years ago, a male child was born but nobody in the sleepy village of Ifewara imagined that the strappy young lad was going to grow up to place the then unknown village on the world map. Hence, this boy passed through life, the hard way, without shoes for the first 18 years of his sojourn on earth and had to borrow sometimes oversized sandals from an uncle when it was compulsory for him to appear in public.
The lad in reference is the quintessential general overseer of the Redeemed Christian Church of God, RCCG, Pastor Enoch Adejare Adeboye. He had not only brought glory to Ifewara, his birth place, the man who dreamt of becoming the first youngest vice chancellor in Africa had also brought panache to Christendom worldwide. All those who are critical of the ostentatious lifestyle of Pentecostal pastors are often careful to single out Pastor Adeboye and a negligible few as exceptions. On Friday, this revered cleric turned 65 and in this interview he took a look at his fast voyage to the top despite his humble beginning. Ever cautious of his image and name, Pastor Adeboye who was accompanied by boardroom mogul, Elder Felix Ohiwerei, had very unpleasant words for journalists who, he reasons, had consistently tried to smear his reputation.
BACKGROUND: I was born on March 2, 1942 in a city, I call it a city, Ifewara where I attended Stephens Primary School between 1948 and 1955. I then went to Ilesha Grammar School between 1956 and 1960, after which I taught as a pupil teacher at a Modern School and later Ondo Boys High School for nine months in 1963. I went to the University of Nigeria, Nsukka from 1963 to 1966 but as a result of the civil war, I came over to what used to be known as the University of Ife, now Obafemi Awolowo University. I finished my first degree between 1966 and 1967.
After teaching for two years, I came to the University of Lagos for my Masters degree from 1969 to 1974. Then I went back to Okeigbo/Ifetedo Grammar School to teach because I had the bond with them to serve them for five years. I think they found it a bit uncomfortable having a M.Sc Mathematics degree holder teaching in a grammar school; so after one year, they forgave the rest of my bond. I was then able to come back to the University of Lagos in 1973 to continue with my studies for Ph.D in Mathematics. I was lecturing in the university as well as pursuing my Ph.D since I already had a masters. I finished my doctorate degree in 1974 and I taught there till 1979 when I moved over to the University of Ilorin for a session and then from there, the call to the ministry came. I became the general overseer of the Redeemed Christian Church of God (RCCG) in 1981.
What was the problem that took you to the Redeemed Christian Church of God which was at the time dominated by illiterate pastors?
Well, I've shared in my testimonies that I had a problem that all my Mathematics couldn't solve and that problem had to do with one of my children, the third one, who had a sickness that the doctors couldn't handle and somebody referred me to the RCCG that if I get there, and they prayed for the child, the child will be made whole. I must confess, I didn't like their mode of worship.
It was too noisy for me, coming from a Cathedral type of worship. The first thing that attracted me to the RCCG was sincerity, their zeal. Whatever they were doing, they were doing it with all their heart, they were not pretending. And I also saw on the faces of the leaders, who were illiterates, absolute peace. It was as if they had no problems at all in the world. And I wanted that peace more than anything else. Fortunately for me, they didn't solve the problem of my child immediately and they kept on saying that I must surrender my life to Jesus, I must confess my sins to Him which irritated me, to start with because I felt that 'what do these illiterates know about sin.' What they are calling sin, by my own interpretation, it was just a way of life, social behaviour. We did everything everybody was doing.
You know, you have a wife in the house, one or two girlfriends by the side. That was what everybody was doing. But after about one month, suddenly the message penetrated, and then suddenly I realised that they were not asking me to surrender to any man but to my maker. I grew up in a home where you can't call it holiness, but at least, they taught us to fear God. It was after I grew, particularly around the time I was about to enter the university that all these pranks became the order of the day. I remember those days where, I call it piety, I can't call it holiness, was the order of the day and I felt that that was a good time and if what these people are saying is that I should return to those days, why not. I went forward, gave my life to Jesus Christ and everything changed. And I've changed since then.
Many people have described you as a Daniel of this generation, others have said that you are a Prophet Elisha who patiently waited for Elijah to go away so that he could get a double portion of his anointing. How do you describe yourself? Secondly, when you became the general overseer of the RCCG, it wasn't this big. As we were told, a lot of people had to leave the church because you were just coming into the church and all of a sudden, you became the GO. What was the greatest challenge you faced at that time?
Well, if somebody call me a Daniel, that's quite interesting. I'm hearing it from you for the first time. It's interesting because I remember the first play I acted in the primary school way back in 1951 or so, was the role of Daniel. That's quite interesting. Maybe, there's something about me, I don't know. And if somebody called me Elisha, interesting too because, I love Elisha passionately and I spent more than three years studying him. After I studied Elijah, and I like Elijah. I like his power, I like his ability to pray and get results in three seconds. But then, I found that Elisha was a better fellow. He's cooler, like I used to say, I regard Elijah as a caterpillar but I see Elisha as a tractor.
I like Elisha a lot. What is also interesting is that my mother said to me, when she was alive, when she was taking me for baptism the Anglican way, you have to choose a Christian name and she wanted to choose Elisha but when she got there, she forgot the name and so she chose Enoch. I would rather say that I am Enoch because that's what my mother finally chose. And there's something I like about Enoch and, that is, he worked with God in holiness, purity.
The Bible says that Enoch worked with God and God took him. He did not die, God just took him. He kept working with God till he walked away, which is the way I want to go (laughter). If the Lord tarries, I want to just walk away to heaven. Go to church at night time, return home, eat pounded yam and go. No sickness, no ache, no problem. That's the way I want to go, but I also like the anointing of Elisha, of course. So, maybe, just combine all of that, maybe Elisha, Elijah but make sure you give prominence to Enoch.
Elderly pastors
Secondly, the challenges I faced. It's not many people who left the RCCG when I became the GO. It was some of the older pastors and believe me honestly, I think I can understand why. No one, some of them had been with the church since inception in 1962. I came in 1973. But God told the founder then, that I was to succeed him. I didn't like the idea one little bit. All I was interested in was my career in the university but he heard from God. And as a matter of fact, I heard from God before he heard from God but of course, I was the most educated at that time and I felt that those elderly pastors were a bit afraid. I'm using the word very carefully; afraid of what could happen if a man with a Ph.D becomes the GO, will there be room for those who are not highly educated?
I think that was their concern. Everybody wants to be secured. There was nothing I could do to convince them that as soon as I take over, I won't want to turn the church around. So, that's why they left, but not too many of them. They were about four in all and they were all elderly pastors. The youths, of course, were extremely happy and many of the old men and women who were spirit-filled, said that they would go with whatever the old man said. If I had any challenge at all, it was really merging the old with the new. The old were set in their ways, the new were a bit more progressive, a bit more aggressive, maintaining a balance so that the young would not despise the old because I value the old. Someone said, "make new friends but keep the old because one is silver and the other is gold."
I value the old because of their voice of experience and experience is the best teacher. The elders will say that you can have as many clothes as an elder, but you can never have as many rags. So, I value the old. But, of course, I know that if the church is to become what God wants it to become, it's going to take the youths to take the church to where it's going to be. So, I know I had to go with the youths and in any case, I became the G.O as a youth. Going with the youths without jettisoning the old is probably the biggest challenge, but thank God, we did very well. The Holy Spirit helped us.
Administration is a very vital aspect of controlling any organisation, including the Church. Considering the establishment of RCCG across the globe, how has it been, sir?
We had tried very strictly to follow principles of the Bible. In the Bible, you have leaders of 50, leaders of 100, leaders of thousands and so on and so forth. And there was a time Moses was having a problem coping with the children of Israel, and his father-in-law, the voice of experience, (he was an old man) came to him and said you will wear yourself out if you try to do these things by yourself. Get some elders to work along with you so, in the RCCG, we did exactly that - followed the principles of the Bible. We have deacons, deaconesses, we have assistant pastors, pastors, area pastors, zonal pastors, provincial pastors in that heirarchy, so that at the end of the day, they do the job and just go on for the ride.
Doesn't the size pose any problems for the Church? Is it not the fight for positions that has brought all the problems?
No, no. problems will always be there. The initial church, if we're to use the word church, that Jesus pastored contained only 12 members, but He had a lot of problems with that church.
