What's your comment about Rev. King's case?
I have always known one thing, you can deceive some of the people some of the time, but you can't deceive all the people all the time. Sooner or later, the truth will prevail. I just believe that God is showing the world again that these people playing pranks might succeed for a while and time will catch up with them and deal with them at the appropriate time.
Sir, let's look at Nigeria, there's so much uncertainty in the air as we approach the elections. At the moment, there's a kind of confusion, people don't really know what 2007 holds. As a man of God, what would you have to say about this?
That God is working His purpose out. When God wants to achieve a particular purpose, occasionally, He has to confuse men, so that out of the confusion, He will come out with His own purpose. The Bible says where is the wisdom of men, has God not made foolish the wise men of the world? If everything is smooth sailing, the wise ones will be able to use their wisdom. But when He confuses everybody so that you don't know exactly what is going on, by the time He shows up, you know God always has the joker card. One thing I know is it's going to be well with Nigeria. I can say that one for sure.
In your prophecy for this year, you revealed that the April general elections will be far more peaceful than expected, but serious prayers will be needed afterwards. Do you envisage any turbulence after the election?
I'm not a political prophet (laughs). Fortunately or unfortunately, I don't know how people suddenly began to consider me a prophet. I'm a pastor. Unfortunately, some people seem to take my words rather too seriously in which case I have to be very careful what I say. All I can tell you is this, we are praying. We started praying from the 1st of September, 24 hours a day and we'll keep praying for a whole year for Nigeria. When I asked my church to begin that prayer, some people asked me that 'does that mean you foresee trouble?' I said no. We are praying so that there will be no trouble. I have to be careful what I say, so that I don't cause unnecessary panic. When I said that the election will be far more peaceful, I would have loved to say that the election would be absolutely peaceful.
What God told me is that it will be more peaceful than expected. In other words, there are some people who are expecting absolute violence all over the place, blood flowing like a river, it's not going to be like that. We're still praying that there will not be hiccups at any time at all. However, there's no doubt about it that whoever wins is going to need a huge dosage of prayers. You know Nigeria very well. Nigeria is not easy to rule. Anybody who is going to make a success of rulership in Nigeria needs divine assistance seriously, particularly in the next regime. Whether at the governorship level or presidency, you're going to need a big dosage of prayers. There are people who will say simply because we lost, we will not let the one who won succeed. Am sure you can relate to that one but with God all things are possible. But in any case, God's purpose will be fulfilled. That's for sure.
The elections are very near, that is if it is not postponed; what advice for the politicians in Nigeria?
Well, let me say one thing for sure. I don't believe that the elections will be postponed. I'm not a politician but my Daddy didn't tell me that the election will be later than May. He doesn't have to tell me everything, I mean the Almighty God. So, let us focus on April. My advice is that if only Nigerians will return to God. If only we will let go our selfish ambitions and aspirations and just let God be God.
This nation will not only become the greatest in Africa, it will become the greatest in the world. I am convinced about that. That Nigeria is going to become the greatest country in the world, that one is a foregone conclusion because my Daddy told me that years ago.
How soon? That is left to Him. If we all turn to Him now, within a decade, the whole world will be saying that there's no country like Nigeria. But if we continue to follow our own ways and continue to go contrary to His will, then He will still achieve what He wants to achieve but it might take a little longer. Like the children of Israel, they could have reached the promised land in 40 days but it took them 40 years because they won't allow God to do what He wanted to do. So, my advice to Nigerians is to come back to the Almighty God. Let's submit to Him, let Him be the one who will choose for us, then all will be well.
As a Nigerian, are you worried about the build-up towards the elections? We have the president pitched against his vice, we have the Niger Delta crisis, a whole lot of problems even associated with the primaries. Are you bothered about that?
No! I'm not a single bit. I'm not bothered because I know that the Almighty is in control. And if God is in control, why should you worry. He doesn't sleep. So, He is keeping watch. I need sleep. The one who's watching over me is watching. The one who is watching over Adeboye is there so he is not worried about any build-up. Like I told you, all these confusions, God has a had in it because He wants to, out of the confusion, bring out something. Because he said let there be light, the Bible said the earth was without form, everywhere was dark, confusion was reigning and He said, let there be light and in an instant, there was light. That's what I expect to see in Nigeria. He will speak again and say let there be light and there will be light.
With all what God has done with the church, you said you have not even started, what then is our vision for the church, if God has not started with all there is now? When you're about to walk away like the biblical Enoch, what will you want people to remember you for?
Well, I just want to be remembered like Enoch is remembered that he is a friend of God. That's all. That there was this young man from an unknown family in an obscure village who became a friend of God and walked with God all the days of his life. That's what I want to be remembered by. As to how could you say you haven't started with all that God has done; the vision of the church is simple. We want to make heaven, we want to take as many people with us to heaven as possible. To achieve this, we want to establish a branch of the church within five-minute-walk-distance from one another. We want to have at least a member of the RCCG in every family in the whole world. When you consider that, you'll know we haven't started. We don't even have a church within five-minute-talk-distance in the whole of Nigeria here, not to talk of the whole world. So you know we are a long way. Thank God we are in about a 100 nations in the world now.

Original_Search_Title: Pounding On the Wall of Sheraton Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Pounding On the Wall of Sheraton Lagos
Body_Text:
Lagos — Normally, the Sheraton Lagos and Towers makes a loud boast of its African heritage. It is everywhere. Stamped boldly in copious works of arts that convey in, sometimes, surreal and at other times plain, straightforward messages and situations about life and living in Africa.
These works adorn the walls of many of the rooms in the hotel including offices, service areas and guest lodgings. But no where is this intricate representation and celebration of the local environment made manifest as the startling packaging of mortars and pestles on a wall at the Crocpot Restaurant of the hotel.
There is a little chance that a hotel of the size and reputation of the Sheraton Lagos and Towers may harbour one or two mortars and pestles. The bigger possibility is that much of the operations in its kitchen have been automated; so the place of the good old mortar and pestle which demands a whole lot of physical exertion in preparing your favourite meal is now consigned to artistic expressions on the walls of the Sheraton Lagos.
Mortars and pestles are hewn out of wood and shaped into utensils used to pound food items. These exhibits can be found delicately mounted on the walls of the hotel's Crocpot Restaurant. It's presence here is significant. This is where the hotel offers African (Nigerian) and continental foods. With its Nigerian guests, pounded yam (that should normally employ use of mortar and pestle in the traditional setting) is a hot item, very much in demand.
The hotel's Public Relations Manager, Bose Oni-Okpaku described her establishment's proclivity towards African arts as a deliberate attempt to project the cultures of the host community to "peoples from all over the world who have short, medium and long term contact with the hotel. We don't want them to come and go away with a feeling that it just a nice hotel. The art works is one way of leaving a lasting good impression, especially on those of them who are artistically inclined".
These works are acquired at art exhibitions and through direct marketing by the artistes. At few instances, it is the creative output of the "Sheraton family". The pestle and mortar exhibit is said to have been inspired by the wife of the General Manager, Alexander Gassauer.
She it was who mounted this artistic reminder of how foods like pounded yam used to be prepared in Africa before the advent of automation.

Original_Search_Title: Love for Sale On Valentine
Headline: Nigeria: Love for Sale On Valentine
Body_Text:
Lagos — I have always admired Nigerians for their ingenuity and sense of creativity. And they never fail to display that in almost every department of human effort including and especially in the most esoteric subject of love.
The last celebration of St. Valentine's Day here in Nigeria is a veritable case study. I found out that more than ever before, love was on sale in this part of the world. The events of February 14 clearly showed out fast love is fading from our land. I was amused to find out that most men and women in this part of the world did not see Valentine's Day as anything more than an opportunity to express their sexual desires. It was simply seen as a day set aside for sex.
And in a country where multiple sex partners is a fashion, where the 'happening girls' on campuses are those who can boast of a large rucksack of sugar-daddies otherwise known as 'aristos', it became a major problem especially among the women, how to satisfy the long list. Some of them I can say, on good authority, have either lost or having major problems with their relationships either with their young boyfriends or the "aristos" who have refused to accept the idea of not having a piece of the action on a day like that. I know a number of women who on that day had to shuttle among three different men in three shifts-morning, afternoon and night. The more creative and efficient ones were able to do more. It was that serious. If you think I'm talking about the known, professional prostitutes, living in hotels, you are dead wrong. Those under focus are the regular home babes you are dying to take home to mum; the undergrads. And for the men, the moment you don't see her that day, you already know where you stand. The situation became so choking that many of the babes had to permanently switch off their phones and probably remove the SIM cards even! All you have to do the next day is blame it on the network. And you wouldn't feel any guilt doing that anyway as network services that day were highly chaotic.
And for the sex workers and club girls, it was busy day! Apart from their regular clients, those whose babes had deliberately made themselves unreachable needed somewhere to fall back on, having either made up their minds to have fun that day or had left home to catch fun only to be ditched by their dates. Don't forget that the idea of catching fun, to an ordinary Nigerian especially the youth nowadays is nothing but sex. Even if you are compelled to first observe the "preambles"- dinner, cinema, beach, name it; the ultimate fun is the bed act. Anything short of that is unacceptable. And if you think that love or sex is free, even from coming from your girl-fiend, then you are mistaken.
I heard that the pick-up prices(by the professionals) were jerked up slightly that day in line with the situation on ground. That therefore brought a complete negation of the actual meaning of Val's day and love or the expression of it by most Nigerians.
From my own reading of the Scriptures, love is a purposeful and conscious commitment to sacrificial action for another. In the Bible it is a fact that loving God is equated with obeying His Word. The two are believed to be inseparable.
According to the Dictionary, Love is a strong liking for someone. It's a strong passionate affection for another person.
The word "love" is from Old English lufu, connected with Sanskrit lubh, 'to desire' and Latin lubere, 'to please'.
But from all indications, love seems to have been re-branded and redefined especially by Naija babes. For many of them, love is now love only if and when it is wrapped in expensive gifts and more love when it is accompanied by money.
And since the whole thing is now monetized, the more men you have on your list, the fatter your bank account or fuller your wardrobes.
One of the love books I had read during my growing up days defines love as a feeling to be learned. It explains what it means when we "fall in love," and when two people meet and it is "love at first sight."
But I dare say, not in Nigeria anymore. Or so it seems. I used to hear of love being blind but that I can say is an old and obnoxious aphorism. If love is still blind in other parts of the world, definitely not here. Love here sees clearly and even wears bi-focal lenses everywhere it goes. In this country our own love here has therefore turned to a demonstration of selfishness. If you are attractive to me, be nice to me, meet my needs and love me I in return will "love" you. Our own love is based on getting something in return for love shown. Apart from black Americans who seem to share similar values as Naija babes on the question of money for love, you can still find true love in most parts of the world. But here, if you do not please me then I have no love for you. As such, love must be earned by making someone else feel good.
Every February, in most parts of the world, candy, flowers, and gifts are exchanged between loved ones, all in the name of St. Valentine. But here, what we exchange is sex in the name of money..
Interestingly, most of those engaged in that orgy of sex on Valentine's day had no idea whatsoever the actual significance of that day. All the story about St. Valentine and how he died are irrelevancies. What they can tell you is that it is the day lovers express their love. And how else can 'lovers' here express love if not in bed? But remember, no money, no love here. And our women are unpretentious and unapologetic about it. Those who find it a bit belittling to name a price have a clandestine way of collecting their 'pay' either in form of 'I want to fix weave-on' or telling you one major problem she has in school for which she needs an urgent financial assistance or 'car hire fare'.
Since that Wednesday, I couldn't help remembering with deep nostalgia, how love was freely given and voraciously consumed in those days at no cost at all. In fact, it was the woman that saw it as a mark of duty to take care of her boyfriend, cook food for him, give him provisions and so on. Of course, the man also performed his own duty of taking care of her but out of his own freewill. There was no price tag to love then. We didn't have to price sex then. But with each passing day, we began to redefine love gradually until we finally gave love our own meaning.
Surprise birthday bash
I honestly thought the era of surprises were over until two days ago. I was summoned to the house and on getting home, what I saw was a fairly large crowd of my friends and new faces in my living room. Beautiful, classy and truly nice chicks, a total departure from the money-for-hand, back-for-ground kind of girls you see almost everywhere in Lagos, all beaming with smiles.
They all sprang into celebration song as I stepped in and followed that up with hugs and kisses. There was so much to eat, pounded yam and egusi, fried rice, jollof rice, various species of salad and assorted drinks of exotic wines, spirits and all you can think of.
And the mastermind behind all this was Moria, my sister (not by birth) who I suspect has the password to my heart. It was a pleasant surprise which will linger in my memory for long.
It was my birthday.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja's Mobile Social Scene
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja's Mobile Social Scene
Body_Text:
How the Federal Capital has developed a social character refreshingly different from other places
It is 5.30 pm on a Saturday.
A sizeable length of two canopies tied to each other, providing the required shade for the party, stood about two hundred metres inside the Millennium Park, filled with the partying guests, most of them women and children, all seated.
A sizeable crowd of uninvited guests, most of them leisure-seeking frequenters of the park every late afternoon, had built up from one side of the shade to the other, quite in a crescent shape, providing a fairly sufficient enclosed frontage for the party. Most of them were standing.
The MC was dressed in a tight garment of assorted colours, covering the body from the neck down to his feet, and a cap of rags, also of assorted colours, apparently for the amusement of the children.
At intervals, a handful of the guests, most of them mothers and children, would take to the lush green-grassed turf, doing their thing, some of them on the invitation of the MC, according to the programme, and others just moved by the assorted music played by the live band, much of it mimicked. The Johnsons were throwing a birthday party for their three-year old daughter, Christie, here.
Most parties for child naming, birthdays, wedding and the celebration of festivals and other cherished moments in the Federal Capital City are staged in the Millennium and other amusement parks, rented halls in hotels, and such other similar locations, and, only on very rare occasions, at open spaces at rancour-free locations and times.
This method of socio-cultural practices portrays confinement and restriction imposed, perhaps, by the socio-economic setup and necessities prevailing in the city. It contrasts with the original traditions, norms and cultural practices of the various ethnic and religious communities who trooped from their different cultural and sociological backgrounds and locations all over the country to populate the city over the last 30 years.
A section of the Muslim and many northern ethnic communities contracting marriages or performing child naming ceremonies would, for example, always invite a gathering of the people to the frontage of homes or such other locations, distribute kola-nut, date-palm and confectionaries, many of them even with praise singers which, however, sometimes depend on the social status of the celebrants and their parentage. Many other ethnic communities in most locations in the country share even assorted drinks as vital stages in the process of contracting marriages or naming their new born. However, the section of the Muslim community confining such practices to the Mosque (naming the new born after the dawn prayer and contracting the marriage after the afternoon prayer, all in the Mosque) has enlarged over the recent decades. After which ever instance, elsewhere in Nigeria, festivities and parties follow freely without any restriction on where and how they should hold.
The practice in Abuja fairly contrasts with what obtains in the satellite towns within the Federal Capital Territory where the many resident communities say they have been conducting such practices according to their various cultures and traditions, albeit substantially polluted by the evolving socio-cultural setting of Abuja. How fast their original cultures and traditions fade away, get polluted by or gradually melt into the socio-cultural evolution in Abuja in their conduct of such practices seems determined by their proximity to the capital city. Many a resident of some of the satellite towns closer to the city says he has never witnessed, say, the Muslim style open space gatherings for child naming or wedding Fatiha even among the Muslim community during his period of residence there.
Abuja seems brewing its own culture of conducting wedding, naming and birthday ceremonies and such other festivities, in sharp contrast to the original cultures and traditions of its various resident communities.
How do the communities see it? What are its implications on their original religious and ethnic cultures and traditions?
"Many places abound here that are more suitable and offer much more comfort and even luxury for organizing such ceremonies than the open spaces and frontage of homes, streets and other locations converged on in all our other villages, towns and cities," Muhammad Bello, who frequents the Abuja Millennium Park, observed in support of holding such ceremonies and parties in Abuja in parks and gardens, saying, "there are such beautiful and comfortable places like the Millennium Park here, Park and Zoo, Abuja Garden Park, all these places have been provided for such ceremonies and parties and leisure."
He said "I am not aware that staging such ceremonies and parties at frontages of homes and such other locations, as is the cultural practice for ages in our various ethnic and religious settings, has been banned here, but residents of Abuja have evolved this culture of staging their own in the halls, parks and gardens because it is more convenient and seems to all the most civilized thing to do."
According to him, Abuja is evolving its own practice different from the cultural practices of the various ethnic communities because "the city is a convergence of all - this man hails from the north, this from the west, that from the south while his neighbour hails from the east. It is a convergence of divergent cultures, norms and attitudes. So it is difficult for one culture to stand alone, distinct."
He, however, admitted that the evolving practice in the Federal Capital City would have grave long term implications on the original cultures and traditions of the various ethnic communities populating the city.
"To be candid, I will observe that our original cultures and traditions will gradually fade away in this situation. If someone arrives at a new location from his socio-cultural background and imbibes new practices, he gradually forgets his own, and it gradually fades away from his way of life because he is not practicing it," Muhammad maintained.
For Peter Egwan, the evolving practice for ceremonies and parties in Abuja cannot impact negatively on the cultures and traditions of the various religious and ethnic communities resident in the city.
"It can never affect their cultures because culture has to do with inheritance. You inherit it. It is not easy to abandon inheritance all your life. You may only suspend practicing it when you are in a new location where you will not find it easy to practice it, and you resume practicing it whenever you go back home. A different culture has evolved here in Abuja. Everybody here behaves like a rich person. For however long you stay here in Abuja, whenever you go back home you can still fit into your cultural background," Peter believes.
Evelyn is a Law student at the University of Abuja. She is also in support of staging such ceremonies and parties in parks and gardens.
"Holding ceremonies and parties along the streets and at open spaces, I believe, will have a negative impact on the youth in the sense that it is staged at their own discretion. They go the way they want. Nobody controls their conduct. They do whatever they want and get away with it," she observed, believing, "but in the FCT where such ceremonies and parties are held in secluded places like the parks and gardens, the situation is under control because there are times set for them, and the security aspect also matters."
According to her, "When it comes to the conduct of the celebrants also it is under control, unlike in our various ethnic home locations where you can hold such ceremonies and parties anywhere, and armed robbers or other miscreants can come in and disturb the peace because there is no security. Here security is provided and there will be a time limit."
She observed that the various religious and ethnic communities could be allowed to hold ceremonies and parties according to their respective norms and traditions so long as such practices are under the control of security agents. People should not be allowed to take such practices to any length to the detriment of security.
"People can be allowed to go by their own cultures and traditions if, and only if they won't breach the peace of others, because this is the Federal Capital City of Nigeria," Evelyn observed.
Aminu Saminu hails from Danmusa local government area of Katsina state. "This is the nature of a city like Abuja. Various peoples have converged from all directions. It is, therefore, natural to observe that in the new sociological setting they have built, they imbibe the cultures of each other. That is why you can observe that the Hausa from the far north, for example, cannot uphold his original cultural practices here. That is, therefore, why the various resident peoples in the city cannot stage ceremonies and parties strictly according to their original ethnic cultural practices," he offered.
According to him, staging parties and ceremonies in parks and gardens has its advantage and disadvantage.
"Its advantage is that the celebrants and invited guests have sufficient space in the parks and gardens for dancing and other movements. They are provided more sufficient space than on the streets, at home frontages or in the courtyard of their households. Its disadvantage is its impact on religion. For the Muslim, the evolving practice here, in my view, can weaken or vitiate his religious deeds. Islam has prohibited the mixing of married women with other men. We have seen in this park married Muslim women coming in dressed in the religiously prescribed dress of long gowns and veils under which, unknown to other people, they also have tee shirts and pair of trousers on them. But as soon as they come in they pull their gown off, remaining with the tee shirts and pair of trousers and they immediately take to the turf, dancing. The ceremony or party over, they will then wear their gowns and veils and go back home."
He opined: "This practice also weakens our original cultures and traditions. If it continues, we face the risk of losing our cultures and traditions which form our identity. Our posterity will not have a cultural heritage to hold on to as their identity. Our children and grandchildren born and bred in Abuja may only read in books about the cultures of their parents and grandparents." According to him, "So long as a resident is allowed to hold ceremonies and parties according to his religious or ethnic culture, he will observe all the practices according to the culture, and his children will not imbibe the culture of other people. But for all peoples from divergent cultures and traditions to hold such occasions at the same place, like the parks, will teach children to emulate others and imbibe the cultures of others instead of practicing and safeguarding their own," Aminu believed.
In Kubwa, Benjamin Uzor, who hails from Cross River state, also spoke.
"I have noticed that Abuja has not really maintained the values and customs of the peoples residing in it. The Gbagyi, who are supposed to be the traditional owners of this place, are not really represented, which actually killed other values, because what we are trying to practice in Abuja is a form of western culture. That is why you see people taking their ceremonies to the Millennium Park and the hotels after their weddings and traditional marriages. They go to such places for their receptions, which is actually killing the values and customs of Nigerians," he offered.
"Not only in the main city, Abuja, even in Kubwa here it is the same practice. On Saturdays, if you go to our popular hotel here, you see people going there for their receptions instead of having canopies around them, calling all the traditional musicians to come around as happens in other places like Lagos. I am from Cross River. What happens there doesn't happen here. So I believe that they are just trying to westernize everything here, may be not wanting to disturb the peace of others. I believe that may be the reason," he explained.
Benjamin observed that this practice has negative effect on the original ethnic cultures and traditions of the various peoples.
"By the time you organize an open reception like what we have in Lagos, you will actually invite traditional musicians and dancers, which helps to promote our cultures. But going to the hotels or parks where you will have all these musicians with guitars, has negative effect on our ethnic norms. So our cultural values are killed."
He did not see any hope for such cultures in Abuja for now. "It is going to be difficult because there was a time the FCT minister was even trying to ban public microphones even in the Mosques and Churches, that they should confine the use of such facilities to their environments. If that will happen in a religious gathering, what about any other public gathering? So it is going to be a problem."
To ensure his children do not lose a substantial part of the culture and values of their parents, "I take my children home around Christmas and during the new yam festival around June when they are on holiday, for them to have a feel of our culture and traditions."
Benjamin, however, saw reason in the government restricting such ceremonies to parks, gardens and hotels.
"People coming into parks, gardens and hotels for such occasions are controlled by security officials. But in the open space you will not know when miscreants will hijack the occasion because Nigerians are not used to inviting the police to control their gatherings. So this practice in the Federal Capital is okay for now ,because we are not mature," he believed.
"Here in Kubwa some people (Muslims) perform the wedding Fatiha in the Mosque, while some invite a gathering and do so in front of their houses," Abu Sufuyan, who has resided in the satellite town for about 20 years now said, adding, "this is also the case with the child naming Fatiha. Some people perform it in the Mosque and no other ceremonies or parties follow afterwards, while others perform the naming Fatiha in the Mosque and stage all other cultural ceremonies following it at their houses. Yet others hold both the naming Fatiha and the ceremonies at their houses. Each of these takes place here. It is a matter of choice. Nothing has been prohibited by any law or abandoned by the people. You can even invite praise singers and musicians if you are rich enough to do so."
He observed: "No ethnic community can lay whole claim to cultural practices here in Abuja. For example, in a Hausa child naming gathering, you will observe some people dressed in the traditional attire of flowing gown and cap, while others will be wearing jeans with tee shirts tucked in. The man dressed in such smaller clothes may not even be Hausa. He may be Muslim. He may, yet, be a Christian, but he is a friend of the father of the child whose naming ceremony he has come to attend. He will even partake in the kola nut distributed. He will also invite you to his own, unless you refuse to honour the invitation."
According to Yunusa Haruna. "Here we conduct all our cultural practices for weddings and naming ceremonies and other festivities wherever we want to. I am not aware of any law or anybody prohibiting or restricting or discouraging us from conducting ceremonies according to our cultures and traditions. Musicians and dancers perform freely. We have been doing our things here freely."
In Mpape, Abdullahi said he had resided there for three years now, but had never witnessed any gathering for child naming or any other similar ceremony there among the resident communities, especially the Hausa. He believed most people go to their respective states and conduct such practices in their original ethnic socio-cultural backgrounds.
Abuja seems evolving its own socio-cultural practices for its residents, ceremonies and parties included.

Original_Search_Title: 2007 - Before the Landslide
Headline: Nigeria: 2007 - Before the Landslide
Body_Text:
"I even made him minister without the knowledge of Agagu, yet he said he wanted to contest. I even begged him to support Agagu for second term, but he was adamant. But unfortunately, when he was the minister, he misbehaved and the EFCC would soon investigate him"- President Obasanjo.
That, by the way, was our all-knowing President Olusegun Obasanjo. He was speaking at the flag-off of the campaign swing of the PDP candidate Umar Yar'Adua in the southwest region. At the same event, he also vowed that the PDP must capture Lagos "by all means." The PDP chairman, Amadu Ali, backed that up when he added "We must capture Lagos state come 2007 at all costs; we would make sure that every state in the southwest would be won by the PDP." He told his bemused audience that in 2003, the election in Lagos was manipulated in favour of the Alliance for Democracy [AD]. The PDP garrison commander could even afford to finish his speech with a bit of humour when he concluded - please hold your laughter - that the PDP was assured of victory in Lagos because rigging would be avoided this time around.
So what do we glean from all these? The first is that the EFCC has always been, and will always be a mere tool in the hands of the president. The president may be his gregarious self, but I doubt if he would have made the statement quoted above if Dr Olusegun Mimiko had heeded his advice not to challenge Agagu's tenacious hold on Ondo State. The second is that the president is not a respecter of the rule of law. He has 'convicted' Mimiko even before a formal indictment or appearance of any competent court of law. The damning outcome of the president's indiscretion quickly manifested in the fact that soldiers had to be drafted to the streets of Akure to quell a major breakdown in law and order that resulted from the fracas between supporters of the PDP and the Labour Party, on whose platform, Mimiko intends to actualize his dream.
But the third quite inevitably is about the patent arrogance of the PDP top guns and the cold-blooded ability of its 'leader' Olusegun Obasanjo, and Chairman Amadu Ali to predict the outcome of the elections even before they hold, and that should be of serious concern to all genuine democrats in the country and indeed the international community. The flipside of course is that if the intention of the PDP was to intimidate its rivals, then it is doing a very poor job of it. The party is only succeeding in serving notice that it intends to cling onto power at all costs using the incumbency factor and the coercive instruments of state at its exclusive disposal.
And yet it is not as if the incumbency factor in any democracy or election is such a bad thing altogether. It is just that in Nigeria, like in some parts of Africa and the rest of the third world, the factor is more renowned for its notoriety or abuse. In civilized democracies, the incumbency factor becomes useful to the extent that the incumbent candidates are able to flaunt their achievements in office before an often appreciative electorate and ride on the popularity so garnered to victory at the polls. The reverse, of course, is that non-performance could also undermine the chances of the same candidates. But in Nigeria, the reverse has always been the case.
Here, candidates utilize the incumbency factor for all the negative reasons. They see it in the primitive control of the exchequer, the police and the SSS. It is defined in the contradictions and the unfortunate utterances of Maurice Iwu, the INEC Chairman of late; it was obvious in the manner Lamidi Adedibu was able to relocate the headquarters of INEC to his private residence and so easily manifested in the Gestapo that has been made of the EFCC. But above all, the quest for the powers of incumbency was why Funso Williams had to die, and why supporters of Danladi Baido, the disqualified gubernatorial candidate of the PDP from Taraba State, rained stones on the president and other party top-guns when they kicked off the presidential campaign of Umar Yar'Adua in the North-East the other day. But like I hinted earlier, whether for the right or wrong reasons, the incumbency factor is never a thing to be treated with levity. At least one example will suffice here because of its close similarity to the trap a fine gentleman like Umar Yar'Adua now unwittingly finds himself.
The year 2000 presidential elections in America like we all know was decided between Clinton's incumbent deputy Al Gore, a Democrat, and the brash, unscholarly upstart from Texas, George W. Bush, the Republican candidate. Gore had a strong base as the incumbent Vice President. The election was held on November 7, 2000. Heading into the elections, it was obvious that Gore had the slight edge forcing Bush at straws such as his condemnation of Clinton's Somalia policy in statements such as "I don't think our troops should be used for what's called nation-building." Only he can possibly explain the presence of American troops in Iraq at the moment, of course, but that is not even the issue here.
As it were, Al Gore's campaign team had another problem which had to be carefully juxtaposed against whatever gains they hoped to reap from Clinton's otherwise flawless presidency. Clinton also carried a baggage over the little matter of the Monica Lewinsky affair which was capable of damaging Gore's candidacy particularly because morality was mentioned most frequently by Republicans as the single most important president facing the nation heading into the elections. So what did Al Gore do about the problem? He studiously did everything he could to avoid the Clinton scandals. He completely avoided public appearances with Clinton, who was shunted to low visibility appearances in minority areas.
That brings me squarely to the subject of the campaign strategy adopted by the PDP candidate, Umar Musa Yar'Adua ahead of the much anticipated April presidential polls in Nigeria. In the last two months, I have spoken to a few influential people who know the candidate very well. All attested to his uncommon intellect and qualities as a fine gentleman. But then, elections are not won on individual qualities alone. Yar'Adua will be the first to agree that even without the derogatory reference to him as the president's anointed candidate, he can surely do without his tagging as "Daddy's boy" like the Action Congress repeatedly did at formal take-off of the campaign of its own candidate Atiku Abubakar over the weekend.
Except the Yar'Adua campaign team is also intoxicated with its power of incumbency, they should be ruffled by comments such as "A vote for the PDP is another vote for Obasanjo, a vote for another round of pauperization of the masses and the enrichment of the few. Atiku stands for change and that is why we are presenting him to Nigerians today." So what change were they talking about? Anyone who has lived in Nigeria over the past seven and a half years should know that the problems of the Obasanjo government have been more than the issue of morality. And, as there seems little use for sophistry in describing the hell that has become the lot of ordinary Nigerians, let me break things into these simple figures.
When he was elected president in 1999, the pump price for petrol fuel was twenty-one naira per litre. The same product now sells for sixty-five naira only in Abuja and Lagos and over one hundred naira everywhere else. Three medium-sized tubers of yam sold for eighty naira; they are now priced at over three hundred. A bag of rice sold for three thousand, two hundred naira; it now costs over six thousand naira depending on the brand. The cost of a bag of cement was five hundred and fifty naira in 1999. It now goes for over one thousand naira. And to put our misery into proper perspective, crude oil, the mainstay of our foreign earnings, which was priced below twenty-five naira at the beginning of his presidency has been selling for over fifty naira for much of the past several years.
Since 1999, this government has not repaired or built one single refinery despite colossal sums wastefully expended. It prefers to import fuel, mostly by those with close links to the presidency. The same fuel queues we experienced during Abacha's time are back to haunt us. This government promised to make power cut a thing of the past but more and more Nigerians are in darkness even after billions have gone down the drain. Worse still, the vast majority of Nigerians have no access to healthcare, decent meals, education or affordable housing. 300 billion naira was reportedly voted for roads in Obasanjo's first term, except that the roads 'constructed' were so sophisticated that they were also invisible. Crime and assassinations also escalated, and with the release of no fewer than twelve thousand skilled gunmen from the police force last week, the situation is certainly expected to get even worse.
These, sadly, are the legacies of the Obasanjo much-vaunted economic reforms, and if I were Umar Musa Yar'Adua, I will ensure that the president never ventures anywhere near my campaign rostrum for his negative value. What the president's increasing visibility on the Yar'Adua portends is that for most Nigerians, the elections mean a vote on the continuation or otherwise of the same legacies. At the end of the day, what will readily spring to the mind of most Nigerians is not the debt relief obtained from the London or Paris clubs, but the reality of their abject poverty and destitution in the midst of plenty. They remain unconvinced by any high-sounding economic slogans imported from abroad that cannot put food on their tables, wipe the tears from their eyes or even nurse their wounds. But above all, it is why, except the PDP intends to deploy the incumbency factor to dubious effect, the president remains a liability and not an asset to the candidacy of the likeable Umar Musa Yar'Adua.
I also hasten to add that the more he continues to lead the aggressive campaign of the PDP, the more the campaign assumes the character of a referendum on his calamitous tenure. The more he also gives the impression of a desperate man intent on saving himself, and quite tragically for Nigerians, the more the April polls are likely to be a landslide for the establishment candidate. The only difference is that if the degree of national degradation and mass frustration are anything to go by, there would be many victims and few survivors in this particular landslide. In the meantime, let us pray...

Original_Search_Title: 'Like Akala, Etiaba Will Be Thrown Out'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Like Akala, Etiaba Will Be Thrown Out'
Body_Text:
Abuja — The intrigue, treachery and horse-trading associated with Nigerian politics make it oftentimes, unattractive. Of recent, a section of the Nigerian Judiciary has received considerable bashing from the public for its role in the questionable impeachments of some state Governors. Chief Niyi Akintola, SAN, who has been involved several litigations of political colouration, in a chance encounter with Funsi Muraina in Abuja, castigated the political class for turning the Judiciary in some states to pawns in their complex maneuvers for power.
There is no doubt that as one of the lawyers who handled Ladoja's case, you are very happy the way it turned out?
Yes, I am very happy like many Nigerians who believe in justice and fair play.
Impeachment seems to be a very cheap tool in the hands of politicians. Where have we as a country got it wrong? Taking Ladoja's case as an example, do you think politicians have learnt a lesson?
I wouldn't say so. One thing that is common with Nigerian politicians is that they never learn. An average Nigerian politician is a peculiar person. He does not believe in any principle and he lives only for today and not for tomorrow. He does not have the word 'Legacy' in his or her dictionary. You see; Nigerians' memories are very short. Nigerians are easily forgiving.
They forget easily and that is why it is possible for the child of Abacha to want to stand for election. That is the predicament we find ourselves in. If they have learnt anything from Ladoja's case, the kind of thing happening in Anambra state would not have arisen. It is highly despicable because I don't know what Governor (Virgy) Etiaba stands to gain.
Are you saying that the matter has assumed a ridiculous dimension?
That is what I am saying. What I am saying is that what is going on in Anambra State is despicable. The Governor stands to lose more. Going down the political history of the country, she is the first woman to become a Governor, albeit illegally. She will also be the first female Governor to defy NJC's advice or order. She will also be the first woman Governor to be thrown out using Ladoja as a test case.
Opinions are being mobilised against NJC, that it has acted beyond its power to write a letter to the Governor to remove a Chief Judge. What is your reaction to this?
Whoever canvasses such an opinion must be out of tune with the provision of the Constitution. We have been having these despicable acts all over the place. Kwara State Chief Judge ought to have gone too; and that is why people were telling the immediate past Chief Justice of Nigeria, Justice Modibbo Salihu Belgore to remove the log in his eyes before he removed the speck in other people's eyes. They argued that it is happening in his own state.
The Constitution is so clear about the duties of NJC. The NJC has the power for the removal or dismissal of any Judge. NJC's role is to recommend, and once that is done, it leads to either retirement or dismissal. I wouldn't expect Etiaba to stop at nothing than to swear in the Acting Chief Judge as advised by the NJC. She too, was a beneficiary of an illegality.
Why is it that Judges are falling into the trap? As law officers, they are supposed to know more about the rules governing their conduct.
There are two examples of it. It appears you don't appreciate the evil ingenuity of Nigerian politicians. If you know the background to a number of cases, look at Judges that fell in the case of Akwa Ibom. In fact one of them is dead now. Those who led them into the gallows are now back in the same camp. Those who initiated the cases in Akwa Ibom that led to the dismissal of these Judges who were then at each others throats are now back in the same camp, eating from the same pot!
The evil ingenuity of an average Nigerian politician is unrivalled. They can lie to you that they are just coming from the President and this is what he wants. One of them was narrating his experience that a politician phoned him and said that he just spoke with the President who indicated what government wanted. When you look at S.292 of the Constitution, any Governor who slept and woke on the wrong side of his bed would remove the Chief Judge as he likes. There is no security of tenure of office for the Chief Judges. The Constitution says that the Governor could remove the Judge on 2/3 majority of the House. All the Governor would do is to call the Speaker and say, 'Please assemble your men, I want to remove the Chief Judge.'
How does the NJC come in, in view of this constitutional posture?
Under S.292 of the Constitution the NJC has no role as the Chief Executive of the State could remove a Judge with the support of 23 majority of the House of Assembly.
Does that justify the allegation that NJC is Overstepping its bounds?
There are other sections of the Constitution that give powers to do what they did and the NJC was very right. If we want sanity and rule of law in this country we must support the NJC. As a matter of fact without the NJC, the Judges would have become useless.
They are now a pawn in the hands of the executives. Look at what happened under this Constitution. In one of the states, a Judge had his water and electricity disconnected because the Judge gave judgment that is unfavourable to the incumbent Governor. Justices of the Court of Appeal were ejected from their houses in Ibadan because they gave judgment against the government of the day under this dispensation.
Will it be right to say that the Judiciary is after all not independent?
The retired CJN said everything in his valedictory remarks. What form of the independence when the Constitution says that salaries of judges should be paid from Consolidated Account and the law is not followed? Even the stipends that are supposed to be paid by the state governments are being denied them.
Even the Imprest due to judges is not forthcoming. How many judicial officers do we have per state? Just very small compared with political appointees. Some of the Governors have as many as 43 Special Advisers!
But the truth of the matter is that the Judiciary is still in chains even under this dispensation because the Judiciary is not truly independent when it cannot control its own purse. The Constitution provides that their salaries and other allowances are charged under the Consolidated Account. But when you look at the way it is being disbursed, the Executive uses the knife and cuts the yam anyhow because it is in possession of the knife and the yam. So, the Nigerian Constitution gives so much power to the Executive.
Like S.292 of the Constitution I was talking about, it says if the President wakes up on the wrong side of the bed, he can remove the CJN, the Chief Judge of the High Court and the Chief Judge of the High Court or Judges of the Federal High Court. All he needs is the support of the National Assembly. Once he gets 2/3 majority of members, he can remove the Judge anyhow.
In the case of such an unfortunate event happening, like the President or the Governor waking up on the wrong side of the bed as in Plateau State where a Judge of the High Court, the number six in the hierarchy was brought and made the Acting Judge of the State by Governor Joshua Dariye. Is it not honourable for these Judges to decline the appointment?
That is a question of morality. But when you look at the evil ingenuity of Nigerian politicians one would not blame the Judges falling easy prey to their intrigue.
And where do we go from here, because it's an unfolding scenario; we have just two months to the general elections when Nigerians would witness more litigation?
Thank God for the Judiciary. Against all odds it has done well, of all the three arms of government it is the best. It has this in-built mechanism of checking the excesses of its members. Look at the number of Judges that have been shown the way out via dismissal, suspension and retirement. The Judiciary has not been sleeping. We should not stretch the federalism principle too far. We should be practising the federalism that suits our own situation. There is no other body like NJC that can sanitise the system. If you place payment of salaries of Judges in the hands of Governors, you can imagine what would happen. The Governors wield a lot of powers on their Chief Judges and that is why many Judges are not interested in taking appointment as Chief Judges.
You've just extolled the virtues of Judges, but we have situations in the recent past when contradictory decisions and injunctions were given in similar cases.
This worries the people especially laymen. What people don't understand is that our legal system is aquisitorial in nature, that is, justice according to law. When you talk about frivolous injunctions, it is only the court that can determine that. For example, if you're to be thrown out of your house tomorrow and you go to court to seek court order to stop forceful ejection, the court would not close its eyes against your prayer. An Ex-parte order could be granted, even in the bedroom, where it is necessary. In a situation where a Speaker of the House swears to an affidavit and says that he and all other members are to be arrested tomorrow by the police and it should stop the move it would be an act of irresponsibility of the court not to grant the order.
The Legislature is part and parcel of government. People have been making the mistake that it is only the Executive that constitutes the government. The Executive alone doesn't constitute the government.
So if the Legislature now says an agent of government like the EFCC or police says that we should close down tomorrow, then it would be the height of irresponsibility for a Judge not to grant the injunction.
There is also a misconception that when a matter involves an individual and a federal agency, the matter should automatically go to the Federal High Court, it is not true. It depends on the claim before the court. Claims on damages, breach of human rights, breach of contract could go to the State High Court. The Supreme Court has said that in various cases. We need to erase the ignorance, because it is not in all cases that cases between individuals and federal government go to the Federal High Court. It depends on the way the claim is couched. Against the EFCC in Ekiti State, I deliberately went to the State High Court because the case was on the breach of the fundamental rights of my client. When Chief Olanipekun, SAN went to the State High Court on behalf of Fasawe he based his claim on Fundamental Human Rights which both the State and Federal High Court share concurrent jurisdiction. For example, if the Federal Ministry of Finance is owing an individual for the contract executed, you can go to the State High court; it all depends on the ingenuity of counsel handling the case for the client. It is the claim that gives jurisdiction to court. If the claim is drafted in a way that gives it Fundamental Human Rights flavour, the State High Court would assume jurisdiction. You sleep on the bed the way you lay it.
One of the reasons the common man has almost lost confidence in our judicial system is the issue of continuous adjournment. Now that the Judiciary is craving total reform, do you support the move?
The Supreme Court has shown the light in Ladoja's Case. It is left for other courts to follow suit. If you look at the way the Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court handled that case, it ended within a year, and that is commendable.

Original_Search_Title: I Can't Do Without My Champaign-- Princess Latifat Lamoliatte
Headline: Nigeria: I Can't Do Without My Champaign-- Princess Latifat Lamoliatte
Body_Text:
When Princess Latifat Momodu-Lamoliatte left Nigeria for educational pursuits in France, little did she know that in less than ten years, she would see her designs on the runway. Influenced by French culture, her label Ravissant mode, which means unique fashion, comprise of sophisticated, ultrafeminine pieces, with a unique line for men and children, all made with a blend of African fabrics.
The Auchi born designer always had a flair for fashion, since her adolescent days, when she used to sketch fashion illustration for friends and tailors to sew for her. Though she studied Foreign Languages at the University of Lagos, her passion for fashion came to the fore at the University Standard Grenoble, France where she studied French Literature and did Masters in International Law.
After six and half year sojourn in France, where she got married to a French man, Princess Latifat came back to the country early last year to live her dream as a designer, when she unveiled her lebel; Ravissant mode to the fashion world.
As a fashion designer, do you have a speciality? An area you think you could have an edge over other designers?
I don't know about others, but I am very good with evening gowns. Making boubous too is another area because, I am an embroidery specialist, I could get very creative with boubous.
What fabric do you love working with mostly?
Adire. I love the Adire Eleko from Abeokuta, they are really lovely. I mix them with chiffon sometimes and they come out fantastic. I love them.
What is fashion like for you?
I love exclusive designs, I make sure I look very nice all the time. Whenever I have to go out I wear clothes that are peculiar to me alone.
Do you always dress African?
I mix them, sometimes African and western. Most of the time African. I wear evening gowns too if I have to go out with my husband. I love boubous, I attend parties in my boubous and I stand out.
What distinctive thing do you like about the French fashion?
I do a lot of window shopping in Paris and I am fascinated with the way they put their designs together. It's just different, you won't find it in London or America.
Your jewelry taste?
I love jewelry a lot especially unique pieces. I wear gold, silver, beads, anyone depending on my outfit.
One beauty product you can't go without?
My cream. I make sure I apply my cream everyday, else I won't be comfortable.
How do you pamper yourself?
I go shopping and get a total make-over.
What is your signature fragrance?
Escada and Magic Noire.
As a designer what inspires you?
The environment, the people around me, I look at someone and I can tell what will suit that person. Ideas just come, especially late at night. I sketch out all I have noticed during the day. I tend to focus on inexplicable designs that people don't really understand.
How do you spend your 'me' time
I sleep, I shut out everything and sleep. Sometimes I watch movies or go out with the family. I love reading too.
Who are your favourite shoes and bag designers?
I love Channel and Cartier for bags. But I really don't mind, I could wear anything so far it's nice.
Favourite food?
I love the French Cuisine, I love the French wine, I love the French fashion. I love everything about the French. You know the French people have class. I like pounded yam and efo riro, but I don't eat it all the time, maybe once a month. I eat jollof rice more.
Fitness tips?
I eat three times a day, but I try not to eat after 8 p.m. I go for a walk sometimes, it helps me stay fit and trim. But one thing I can't do without is drinking champaign.
What part of the world do you love most?
Paris -- 'See Paris and die'. I think this phrase is very true. Paris is beautiful. I am never bored of going there.
What challenges do you encounter as a designer?
It's difficult to please people. Some customers are very difficult, you have to be very patient with them. Though I have tailors I make sure I supervise them all the time and it's not that easy.
What is in your make-up bag?
Powder, lipstick, eye shadow, liner that's all.
What is your take on the fashion industry in Nigeria?
Nigerian designers are not doing badly at all. I've seen what they do and I believe they are doing very well. But, someone else has to come out with something different, that is what Ravissantt Mode is all about.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Support Sciences, Loan Scheme On
Headline: Uganda: Govt to Support Sciences, Loan Scheme On
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has announced plans to start a loan scheme for students pursuing science courses in both government and private institutions.
"The government is ready to start a loan scheme for Ugandan students pursuing science courses, not courses like Latin," Museveni said on Friday during the commissioning of Kampala International University's western campus teaching hospital based at Ishaka town in Bushenyi district.
The construction of the structure cost sh80b. The project managing director, Haji Hassan Basajjabalaba, said they planned to offer free medical services to the community if the Government supports the institution.
Basajjabalaba explained that they are negotiating with the Government to provide them with medicine and medical equipment.
Earlier, the university's vice-chancellor, Prof. Ndawula, said the medical students' dropout rate was alarming due to the costs attached to the courses.
"We suggest that a loan board be put in place so that Ugandan students can afford the courses," Ndawula said.
Basajjabalaba thanked the governments of Kenya and Tanzania for paying fees for their students, who are currently the majority at the university.
Museveni praised Basajjabalaba for supporting the Government by creating employment opportunities and providing services to the public.
He said since the government could not provide employment for all the people, it would support the private sector to employ them. The university employs 1,026 people, including professors and consultants.
Museveni criticised some leaders for doing nothing to help unemployed people.
"We have a problem of unemployment, but people are just asleep, especially leaders," he said, and hailed Basajjabalaba for being imaginative.
"This is a brainchild of Basajjabalaba, who, through imagination, was able to convert 12 acres of land into this great project," Museveni noted, adding that if Basajjabalaba had grown sweet potatoes on that piece of land, "he would not have achieved more than what we can see today".
The construction started in 2004. The campus is supported by the governments of Cuba, Nigeria, Korea, Philippines, Egypt, Kenya, Tanzania and the United States.

Original_Search_Title: New Dawn Awaits Northern Region
Headline: Uganda: New Dawn Awaits Northern Region
Body_Text:
Kampala — Sugar canes as tall as elephant grass, sweet potatoes as big as a cow's head and the sound of singing, drums and harps (adungu) spread like sweet smelling dry millet next to smoky homesteads is what the rural North of Uganda once was.
Around this time last year-driving across Atiak to Moyo in West Nile, the sudden appearance of hundreds of bright colored water jerricans in the middle of IDP settlements crowded like mourners reminded one of the decay of this society.
The receding of the war drums to such far sounding areas like Garamba National Park in Congo as a result of the one and a half year Juba peace talks is once again breathing life into long forgotten fireplaces around which old men told stories to attentive young minds as is the tradition of the Luo and neighboring tribes, while women mingled kwon (millet bread) mixed with dry meats and vegetables.
It's possible the old days will not return, at least not in the form that a new generation of northern Ugandans remember. Having grown under the clutches of a nightmare not of their own making, the future of these Ugandans depends on how local and international peacemakers sustain the peace that has so far been largely a promise that Joseph Kony and his remaining rebels will make a deal with the Ugandan government.
With the New Year comes the hope that Kony will negotiate in person his abandonment of armed struggle at Juba. It is a faint hope but the rebel leader has few choices. The last 12 months has seen his forces split and the people he claimed to serve alarmed by his treatment of his second in command- Vincent Otti. Alienated from the Acholi, Kony has little hope of survival except as a mercenary, wanted by an international court for crimes against humanity.
As the clock ticks to the trickle of people back to their homes, 2008 will be the year of the greatest challenge to making peace in Uganda. Firstly, Ugandan and regional actors must allow for Kony to be relocated further away from the torched geography of this 21-year conflict. One way under the Juba process is for him to surrender to his former benefactors, the government of Omar El Bashir in Khartoum and be in a supervised in-country exile within Sudan.
Whatever deal is done, the bigger task rests with the Ugandan government. If Kony was committed to resisting President Yoweri Museveni's National Resistance Movement throughout this period, the military backed politics of Kampala bears the bigger responsibility of letting him succeed in laying to waste the Northern countryside.
The government of Uganda mustered just enough political will to contain the scourge of LRA within the North and looked on as military leaders looted their own resources for the war, as seen from the testimony of Gen James Kazini in the Ghost Soldier inquiry.
As the North imploded into mass human suffering, its own politicians were a minority in the government unable to rally the rest of the country or the rest of the world to bring an end to the conflict. If Juba succeeds, the political career of Northern leaders as well as that of Mr. Museveni who has faced a consistent resistance from both armed insurgents like Kony and unarmed political opposition from local and Diaspora Northerners- will be shaped by how well they respond to the task of reconstructing the country beyond the bridge at Karuma.
One opportunity that the Juba process has offered national leaders is to use the reconstruction process to rehabilitate the historically brittle relationship between Northerners and Southerners.
As the New Year dawns, attention must turn both to the conclusion of the formal peace process in Juba and the steeper challenges of putting children back in schools and mothers in their own kitchens. The US$600million Peace Recovery and Development Plan (PRDP) that the government announced six days after Independence Day this year should not be turned into a granary for government fat cats, but a test case for the commitment of leaders from the North and South to right the wrongs of the past two decades.
If anything, peace in Northern Uganda may impose even bigger challenges than replanting sugar canes along former rebel paths. It will test the ability of Northern leaders to remain steadfast and committed to delivering to their people and the NRM government of building a legitimacy based on the delivery of public goods.
So far- judging by the vicious fallout between MP Okumu Reagan and Hon Morris Ogenga Latigo, the political unity of the North may become the first casualty of the peace process. This is bad news for those who have had to pay the true cost of war- the returning IDP's.
NRM too, a party that has faced its own internal wars, has reason to move beyond its comfort zones and speeches to take the task of rebuilding seriously. One huge task will be the creation of incentives, including tax breaks, and other sweeteners for exiled Northerners to return and participate in the rebuilding process. Another will be a robust veteran's assistance problem for ex-combatants that take livelihood planning for former fighters beyond occasional handouts.
An optimistic view is to see the reconstruction of Northern Uganda itself as a turning point in the new political relations within Uganda's multiparty politics.
The pessimistic view is much worse- and fears taller than elephant grass loom ahead if peace fails.

Original_Search_Title: Defence Force Kills Congo Soldier
Headline: Uganda: UPDF Kills Congo Soldier
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE UPDF have shot dead a Congolese soldier and injured another after they allegedly raped two teenage Ugandan girls in Kanungu district. Lt. Kiconco Tabaro, 2nd Division spokesman, said the incident occurred at Kasenyi Nyondo hills in Kayonza sub-county.
Tabaro, who was addressing reporeters on Friday in his office in Mbarara town, said the Congolese soldiers last week crossed from Buramba unit and violated the girls in a garden where they were uprooting sweet potatoes.
He said soldiers from the 69th battalion, who were patrolling nearby heard an alarm from the girls and swung into action, killing the soldier and recovered his gun with 59 bullets.
Tabaro said the accomplice fled back into DR Congo with chest injuries.
"Maj. Jozzy Kanku, the commanding officer of the 63rd battalion of FARDC (Congolese army) confirmed that one of his soldiers is critically ill and is admitted in Nyamirima Hospital in DRC," he said.
Tabaro said the girls had been taken to Bwindi Health Centre for examination.
He said the army was on high alert and asked residents living near the border to maintain the highest level of watchfulness to avoid such incidents in future.
Lawless elements from DR Congo have caused mayhem the border, bringing misery to residents.
They routinely sneek into the Ugandan side and loot property.

Original_Search_Title: Otafiire Warns Immigration Officials On Foreign Investors
Headline: Uganda: Otafiire Warns Immigration Officials On Foreign Investors
Body_Text:
Kampala — LOCAL Government minister Maj. Gen. Kahinda Otafiire has warned immigration officials to stop frustrating foreign investors by delaying or refusing to issue them with work permits.
"Foreign investors who come here to do business and qualify should get their work permits so that they do their business without interference from anybody in the country," he said during the launch of the Pakistan Society Uganda Association on Sunday in Kampala.
"A work permit is not a privilege. It is a right. If I have decided to invest my money in Uganda, nobody should stop me." Otafiire made the remarks after the association's members complained of harassment by immigration officials whenever they go for work permits.
"You will get your work permits if you qualify. Those officials cannot disturb you because we are inviting investors to come and do business here in order to create employment for our people."
He said he would consult with the internal affairs ministers to address their complaints.
The minister noted that foreign investors had contributed greatly to the country's development. He appealed to the Pakistanis and other foreign investors to forget the past atrocities during the regime of Idi Amin, who expelled the Asians from Uganda.
"If you labour to build the country, that country owes you protection," he added.
The state minister for foreign affairs, Henry Okello Oryem, urged the business people to get dual citizenship once the law is passed in Parliament.
"Lobby for business in Uganda. You can invite business partners to invest in small cottages."
He also urged the Pakistani community to diversify their food by eating matooke and sweet potatoes.

Original_Search_Title: Lazing Languidly At Louis Trichardt
Headline: Zimbabwe: Lazing Languidly At Louis Trichardt
Body_Text:
THE previous time I was in Louis Trichardt -- in the Soutpansberg Mountains, 100km south of the Zimbabwe border -- it was named Makhado.
That was almost six months to the day before a recent trip. Two days later, the courts ruled the pretty town should again be named after the Boer Voortrekker; one it had carried for 170 years; 'Makhado' (a Venda king) must go.
Now both names are colloquially used; there was some geo-political muddle whether we were in Limpopo or Northern Province! When I first trekked through this breathtakingly beautiful area a third of a century ago I was unequivocally in Northern Transvaal, of which locals were immensely proud.
Until recently Zimbabweans needing a short "sanity break" to RSA, away from shortages, power cuts, economic and political turmoil, or for a shopping trip, travelled to Polokwane (née Pietersburg). Now all major chains are in Louis Trichardt: 208km and two R23 toll gates (return) closer.
Inn on Louis Trichardt is a favourite with Zimbabweans. An associate member of Inns of Zimbabwe, their Innsider card gives holders 20% off rooms and meals. And when that 20% is in rands and the only way most folk can acquire forex is on the black market at around ZW$50 000:R1, a worthwhile saving.
As usual the gleaming inn was full, mainly with Zim-registered cars late on Sunday, after a harrowing journey from Masvingo. Thankfully cool, we missed expected blistering October heat, but roads were awful, with pot-holes, gaping chasms, road kill, ranging from civet cats to donkeys, forming unwanted ghastly, bloody scenery changes.
Cows, calves, donkeys and skeletal curs wandered across the road, causing vehicles, already swerving to miss the worst damaged surfaces, to slew erratically, adding to the danger.
Beitbridge's welcome includes the heart-rending sight of a 1 000 year-old, maybe two millennia, old once stately baobab, bulldozed for a soccer-tourism orientated pre-2010 road improvement plan, one suspects will not be finished. Three dead donkeys, one in a state of bloated malodorous putrefaction, lay in CBD streets.
There is a slack (compared to Botswana's strict frontier crossings) foot-and-mouth dip, almost dry; probably weeks since disinfectant was renewed. But car tyres aren't dipped and as baboons scampering unchecked from one state to the other, back-and-forth across the bridge over Kipling's great, grey-green, greasy Limpopo, certainly don't bob paws and claws in the mixture, it seems an exercise in futility. As hundreds of border jumpers in search of greener veldt, brave the croc-and hippo-infested river, snakes and armed South African patrols weekly, it's hard to see why it's there at all.
Crossing south involved almost three hours of bumbling bad-tempered bureaucracy, but we were surrounded by hundreds of Zimbabweans, clutching pathetic packages, trying for papers from unsympathetic RSA functionaries. Returning on Wednesday took 20 pleasant minutes.
Suites at Inn on Louis Trichardt are luxurious. Everything works: including power and water: lots of it, piping hot, in basins, deep baths and showers. A magnificent welcoming basket of fruit and chocolate greets guests. IoZ outlets don't have TV or radio, but travellers down south demand mod-cons and an hour's zizz on an Emperor-sized comfortable bed, after a soothing soak, munching fruit, sipping tea, watching World Cup rugby, was great.
In a cozy bar, icily cold Amstel put an edge on an appetite already honed for starters of succulent smoked salmon with cottage cheese, then nourishing brown onion soup.
Mains were the poached kingklip with asparagus and hollandaise sauce I had, or roast leg of lamb with rosemary and mint sauce or a vegetable stir-fry. I declined sweets of coup Denmark or rib-sticking Malva pudding and custard, but attacked a groaning cheese board with fresh fruit.
On Monday, Zimbos oohed and aahed over the sort of grilled rump steak and pepper sauce we once took for granted; or roast pork with sherry sauce; or vegetable pizza; after asparagus roulade with herb mayonnaise and grand home-made vegetable soup. Again I left sweets but tucked into cheese and biscuits
Kippers, served with full-Monty breakfast, were a dream, as were creamy garlic mussels with brown bread fingers opening Tuesday's supper; soup was celery and apple. No prizes for guessing which main course I chose from roast beef with port sauce, vegetarian lasagna or grilled sole topped with shrimps and mushrooms, Lyonnaise potatoes, and seasonal veg.
After cheese board -- with more varieties than previously-- and port, I was convinced by folk at the next table that a melktert was not to be missed. So I didn't miss it.
With Innsider card discount, dinner, bed and breakfast at Inn on Louis Trichardt costs R401,20 single and R696,85 for double occupancy of a suite in the Garden Wing or hotel.

Original_Search_Title: Promoting Domestic Tourism Through Cultural Cuisine
Headline: Namibia: Promoting Domestic Tourism Through Cultural Cuisine
Body_Text:
Windhoek — There is an old saying that the meaning of food is an exploration of culture through food. What we consume, how we acquire it, who prepares it, who is at the table, and who eats first is a form of communication that is rich with meaning.
A female sociologist, Josephine Beoku-Betts, once said that this area of work - food preparation and dietary practices - has traditionally been viewed as a "natural role" for women for a long time.
And it is indeed women who swamped the recent City of Windhoek's arts and cultural festival, /Ae//Gams, with their offerings of traditional cuisine.
The /Ae//Gams is an annual event, which aims to foster positive images and revive arts and cultural activities in the city.
The festival ran under the theme "Ipele ka setso sa gago", which comes from Setswana, meaning: "Have pride in your culture".
Dressed in traditional apparel, women arranged tables to form a huge circle, each table filled with fares, which they showed off proudly at the United Nations (UN) Plaza in Katutura.
Among them were entrepreneurs Hileni Tanyaanda and Magano Theophilus.
Two members of the Ndilimani Cultural Dance Troupe after mesmerising spectators with their breath-taking dances - clapping, stomping, dipping, and leaping - headed straight for Tanyaanda's cuisine table, where she had prepared Oshiwambo traditional food.
One of the dancers opened a silver bowl to expose mouth-watering golden brown half chicken pieces coated in marula oil. She opened the next bowl of steamed dried spinach, seasoned with marula oil and salt. She moved to yet another bowl with Omahangu porridge.
"Wow, you have porridge too," the dancer remarked as she unleashed an intricate ululating shriek, "Hi-li-li-liiii", a shout common at traditional weddings.
Tanyaanda's eyes were filled with joy upon receiving such a yummy response from the dancers and saw the opportunity right to disclose her winning recipes.
"My chicken is best prepared without onion," she said, her dark brown eyes glittering in the shadow of a tent. It was 3 pm.
"Nowadays, people use onion, which is not the traditional way of doing it. And also it does not need a lot of water. All you need is low heat to cook it tender. Once tender, you add a bit of salt and a little Ondjove (Oshiwambo term for marula oil)," she said.
With regard to the spinach she remarked: "First you soak the dried spinach in water. Once soft, you steam it and it also does not need a lot of water. After steaming for a while, you throw out the water, and put new water.
"People like to put tomatoes and onions, but this is not really necessary. All that is needed is a pinch of salt and Ondjove, or a little butter,"she said further.
This was the third time for Tanyaanda to take part in the cultural festival which she said reminded people of their culture as well as providing entrepreneurs, like her, with an opportunity to market their businesses.
"Since I have started, this show is becoming better and better each year.
"I am sure next year will be best. I also like the idea that the events are held here in Katutura where most of the citizens live instead of town. Not everyone can afford transport to town."
She said the City of Windhoek gave them financial assistance to attend a course at the Namibian Chefs Association, where they received training in food hygiene.
"The hygiene part was very important for me. We were trained not to poke our nose or scratch our hair when handling food, or put our cell-phones in our bras and that we should try and keep ourselves clean all the time.
"Sometimes we, as sellers, do all these funny things before our customers and then wonder when people walk away without buying anything even though we can clearly see that the person is hungry. We are the ones who chase our customers away because of the way we present ourselves."
Next to Tanyaanda was Theophilus, who has a small stall at Oshetu Market at the Single Quarters. Like Tanyaanda, she is not a newcomer to the show - it is her second time.
Apart from the crisp chips, sweets and soft drinks she was selling, she also had with her fried potato chips, Russian sausages and boerewors as well as hotdog rolls.
Theophilus said on Friday she had prepared Oshiwambo traditional food, but decided to make 'hotdogs' on the Sunday to also cater for other cultural groups.
