"This show is about different cultures and we need to provide a variety of food for every one. We also have tourists coming to this festival each year and sausage and roll is the traditional food for some of them, especially Europeans," she said.
"This event is aimed at celebrating and raising awareness of multiculturalism in Windhoek. It is a great event that should never be missed," she added. According to the festival coordinator, Wilfried Gaeb, the cuisine exhibition is free of charge and everyone is welcome to sell their food provided that they meet the hygiene standards. One such requirement is that food should not stand for more than four hours in the sun, otherwise it will not be fit for human consumption. "The number of participants has increased over the years and we particularly challenge men to bring in some change," urged Gaeb. He said many showgoers have realised that the country has different cultures, and learnt to be tolerant of other people's cultures as well as support each other.
"For example, if a Nama-speaking person sees that it is only Oshiwambo food on display, he would be keen to try out the food and also vice versa." He said the aim of the festival was not only to create cultural awareness, but also domestic tourism.
"People visiting friends and relatives make use of the traditional food and services that the City of Windhoek provide. This is also a great opportunity for SMEs (Small and Medium Enterprises). The money goes straight to them, the people at the grassroots."

Original_Search_Title: How Does Country Rate in the Aids War?
Headline: Kenya: How Does Country Rate in the Aids War?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — This week, Prof STEPHEN LEWIS, former UN envoy on Aids in Africa, aswers your questions.
Why do you seem to have a lot of interest in Africa, especially over HIV/Aids?
- George Morara, Nairobi.
My interest in Africa is nearly lifelong, beginning when I first visited the continent, Ghana to be precise, in 1960. My commitment to Aids, like so many others, was the inevitable reaction to a crisis unfolding before our eyes, and it grew stronger as I travelled for my role as UN envoy on Aids in Africa in 2001-2006.
I understand you are likely to tour Kenya soon; what's the purpose of the visit?
- Ngugi Peter, Thika.
There are two purposes. The first is to address the conference of the Commonwealth Lawyers Association. The second is to visit community projects funded by my foundation in Canada.
Kenya boasts over 80 per cent HIV/Aids awareness. If the awareness programmes have achieved such success, from your global knowledge of the scourge, why is it that it has not translated into an equally dramatic fall in the prevalence rate?
- Brian Maiyo, Brisbane (Australia).
This is a good question pondered by many of the experts. It would seem that awareness of HIV/Aids does not quickly or easily translate into behaviour change. This seems odd, but it is borne out by long experience.
Which world organisations give funds or small grants to community-based organisations or self-help groups involved in helping Aids orphans?
- David Omare, Masaba.
Mine - the Stephen Lewis Foundation - funds a great many excellent orphan groups at the grassroots in some 14 countries. The Firelight Foundation, American Jewish World Service and Keep A Child Alive are all terrific US NGOs that do the same.
On the other hand, much more extensive funding is made available through major international NGOs such as World Vision, Save the Children, Care and Amref. Within the United Nations, Unicef from time to time funds individual projects. Many churches do the same.
Recently, Kenya's National Aids Control Council released figures showing that the HIV/Aids infection rate was on a decline; does such information help in the fight against the disease, or does it make people careless and vulnerable?
- Miriam Gichana, Kisii.
A decline in the prevalence rate brings the hope of defeating the pandemic, and the reality of hope far outstrips any possibility of a tendency to carelessness.
Why does HIV/Aids seem to be a disease of the poor countries? And why do the developed countries seem not bothered or unaffected by the scourge?
- Amos Kinyua, Embu.
All of the major communicable diseases - Aids, TB and malaria- afflict poor countries in a far more devastating manner than is the case in rich countries. Wretched levels of health, limited access to health services, widespread hunger, desperate living conditions ? all of these aggravate, indeed invite, disease. But it is mythology to say that Aids doesn't affect the Western world.
Canada, the US, the United Kingdom and others have significant numbers of people infected.
But what those countries can purchase with their wealth are highly developed health systems, immediate access to drugs, health professionals and universal educational systems for messages of prevention, so that it is possible to keep the pandemic under control and confined to most high-risk groups.
It has been reported that uncircumcised men are at a higher risk of contracting HIV/Aids; what is the correct position?
- Paul Mwangale, Nairobi.
It has now been shown conclusively that male circumcision reduces risk. Studies in Uganda, Kenya and South Africa demonstrate a reduction in transmission among circumcised men of over 60 per cent.
This is quite remarkable. As a result, UNAids and the World Health Organisation have issued guidelines for the safe provision of circumcision in medically approved facilities.
Every country should be on the circumcision bandwagon for it will save countless lives. It should be noted, however, that 60 per cent is not 100 per cent, and it's not yet known whether the female partners of circumcised men are at any reduced risk at all. So whether circumcised or uncircumcised, condoms remain a key to safer sex.
Don't you think that NGOs are spending more money and resources tabulating facts and figures instead of taking care of the actual HIV/Aids victims?
-Fredrick Correa, Nairobi.
I'm not sure that the endless tabulation of data is a neurosis confined solely to NGOs. Some UN agencies also suffer from this pattern.
In fact, there is a growing tendency to believe that the repetition of statistics in report after report, coupled with endless meetings, seminars and conferences going over the same material again and again somehow constitutes progress.
It doesn't. And it is an odious substitute for serious work on the ground.
It's said that women and children bear the brunt of the HIV/Aids pandemic; what do you think should be done to arrest the situation?
- Wanjiko Karago, Ruiru.
There is no question that African women and children bear the brunt of the pandemic. The prevalence rates among women are much higher than among men, especially in the 15-to-24 age group. The pandemic is driven in large part by gender inequality and the sexual, political and economic empowerment of women which is crucial if we want to turn the tide.
One of the most important potential developments is the creation of a new international agency for women, at present being discussed by the United Nations.
If it were to happen, it would give women activists and women living with HIV/Aids the resources and the voice to fight the pandemic at the country level.
The situation around children is calamitous. Only 10 per cent to 15 per cent of those who need treatment are getting it ... a much lower ratio than is the case for adults.
Less than 15 per cent of HIV-positive pregnant women have access to the drugs that would prevent transmission of the virus during birth and breast-feeding. That's frankly intolerable and inexcusable. And the situation of orphans, as everyone knows, is a true and growing crisis. We have let the children of Africa down.
How has the Catholic Church's stand on the use of condoms affected the fight against Aids globally?
- Ng'ang'a Gatibui, Ruiru.
The stand is very damaging. But it appears - despite some hopes - that it will never change its position. Fortunately, a lot of the Catholic leadership in Africa turns a blind eye to the Vatican, and condoms are regularly used by parishioners. But it must also be acknowledged that the church does tremendously valuable work in the area of care and help for people struggling with the virus.
I sometimes feel that the soul of the church is at war with itself: disparaging condoms on the one hand and on the other easing the inevitable damage once HIV/Aids has taken hold.
It is said that poverty, ignorance and politics play a big role in the spread of HIV/Aids in Africa; did you experience this during your tenure in office?
- Francis Njuguna, Nairobi.
Yes, I encountered poverty, ignorance and politics galore during my tenure as envoy. I've made some comments on poverty. The levels of ignorance about the virus in some countries, especially among the youth, require a tremendous educational campaign.
And politics? Well, the best example of twisted and perverse political practice lies with the government of South Africa. At the outset it denied the connection between HIV and Aids - a position which left the rest of the world gasping.
Then it made absurd claims for a bunch of preposterous interventions, eg sweet potatoes, garlic, beetroots and lemon juice, that made South Africa a laughing-stock.
And even today, the government of South Africa is rolling out treatment so slowly that hundreds of thousands of people will die before they receive the drugs. There is now, however, some hope: a new plan is in place, although many activists are not confident that it will be fully implemented.
What in your opinion should be done to make ARVs affordable and more widely available in Africa?
-Muwonge Dan, Kampala.
There are generic drugs that may be bought by governments at a very low cost, mostly from India, as a first-line response. However, a growing number of people will need the next level of treatment as they develop resistance to the current drugs. The so-called second-line drugs must have their prices dramatically lowered. Hardly anyone can afford them as things now stand.
Where are we likely to be in 10 years as far as vaccination and other initiatives against HIV/Aids are concerned?
- Githuku Mungai, Nairobi.
You ask a vexing and frustrating question. There is a tremendous international effort to discover a vaccine, but many experts suggest that it's at least 10 years off.
That's a clear tragedy because ultimately a vaccine is the only definitive answer to ending the pandemic. But alas, up to this point, the virus has outwitted the science. There is a similar effort being made to discover a microbicide for women to prevent transmission during sex.
That may be a little closer, perhaps five or six years. It would make a tremendous difference, but it is not itself a cure. As a cause for optimism, however, it should be noted that there are many ongoing trials for both vaccines and microbicides, a reasonable number in Africa. No-one is giving up.
Africa is still underfunded by the UN in the fight against HIV/Aids; what efforts are being made to give more?
- Ombasa David Mangabo, Nairobi.
Although the UN agencies don't have that kind of money themselves, the UN could certainly apply greater pressure than it has done thus far in prodding the G8 to provide resources.
We're several billions of dollars short of what will be needed over the next few years. The Global Fund to Fight Aids, Tuberculosis and Malaria is always reduced to begging for money.
Kenya, through the Association of Kenya Medical Laboratory Scientific Officers (AKMLSO), an affiliate of the International Federation of Biomedical Laboratory Science which is based in Canada, won the bid to host the 29th IFBLS congress in Nairobi in 2010; would you mind gracing the occasion as an invited speaker on HIV/Aids in Africa?
- James R. W. Sakwa, National chairman, AKMLSO.
I'm not sure that "gracing" is the word I'd use, but I'm more than happy (and privileged) to accept the invitation.
How does Kenya rate globally in the fight against Aids? And can the effort be described as a success story?
- Ibrahim Kipchumba, Nairobi.
Kenya is doing better and better against the pandemic. Certainly, the prevalence rate has come down dramatically. Health minister Charity Ngilu has done a first-rate job in the rolling out of ARV treatment.
When you get leadership of that kind, you make progress. My sense is that Kenya desperately needs a greatly intensified response to orphans, and it also needs a fully sophisticated welfare/social security scheme that will put sufficient funds into the pockets of impoverished and affected families, often grandmothers.
The abolishment of primary school fees is an important step, and I hope President Kibaki is thinking about similar legislation for secondary schools.

Original_Search_Title: Diabetes - One Illness, Two Approaches
Headline: Namibia: Diabetes - One Illness, Two Approaches
Body_Text:
Windhoek — CHENEY Viljoen (12) and Andre Kopp (13) are at first glance average teenagers, one living in Tsumeb and the other in Windhoek, but once you get to know them you notice that they are diabetics who have to inject themselves with insulin on a daily basis.
Andre was diagnosed in 2004 when he was ten and Cheney was nine when she was first diagnosed with diabetes.
Both children experienced the same typical symptoms.
They were extremely thirsty and drank constantly, they went to the loo a lot and had no energy.
When Andre went to the doctor, his blood-sugar level was so high that the ordinary measuring device could not measure it.
He was immediately admitted to hospital and had to stay there for five days so that the doctors could get his sugar level down.
He received intravenous insulin and since then he has been injecting himself four times a day with two types of insulin.
He injects himself before each meal and once at night before going to bed.
"It is not sore at all, it is like a mosquito bite, as the needle is very thin and small," he says.
When Cheney was diagnosed, her blood-sugar level was 48.1 - a healthy person's levels are between 4 and 8 - and she too was immediately admitted to the ICU where she stayed for a week.
Andre is lucky, as his mother is a teacher at his school and she called all his classmates together to inform them and answer questions about his condition.
His classmates are so supportive, they don't even eat anything sweet during the school breaks.
Andre's sugar levels are a bit up and down at the moment, but this can be attributed to puberty and hormones.
Being diabetic has not changed his life drastically, according to Andre.
"I am still very active in sport and this also helps to keep my sugar levels more stable.
I do karate, play hockey and squash, participate in all cycling events and swim a lot."
His mother, Sabine, says the whole family is eating healthier now but other than a change in their diet there have been no big changes since Andre was diagnosed.
Cheney is not so fortunate, as she lives in Tsumeb, where many people don't know about diabetes.
Her classmates think it's a contagious disease that they might catch from her.
Her sugar levels are also not under control and she has had to be flown to Windhoek for emergency treatment twice.
Lisa, Cheney's mother, cried when she first head her daughter's diagnosis, as she had no knowledge or understanding of the illness.
Cheney also has to inject herself four times a day.
"There is no support group for parents, children or other diabetics [at Tsumeb] and most of the doctors are also not very knowledgeable and familiar with the disease.
It took us a long time to find a doctor who could help us but unfortunately she is in Windhoek and I thus drive regularly with Cheney to the capital," says Lisa.
Although the two children are the same age and both have to inject themselves with insulin, they handle their illness very differently.
Andre takes diabetes in his stride, excels in sport and talks openly about his life as diabetic.
Cheney, on the other hand, is angry and depressed about her situation; she feels alone and refuses to talk about it.
Diabetes mellitus occurs when there is too much glucose in the blood.
Insulin is a hormone that lowers glucose levels in the blood.
Glucose, a simple form of sugar, is the main source of fuel for our bodies.
Diabetes mellitus develops when the pancreas, which is the organ responsible for producing insulin, is either unable to make insulin, or the insulin is unable to work effectively, or both.
Without insulin doing its job, glucose builds up in the blood, leading to high blood glucose levels which cause a variety of health problems.
The body converts the glucose from food into energy.
Glucose comes ready made in sweet foods such as sweets and cakes, or from starchy foods such as potatoes, pasta or bread once they're digested.
Under normal circumstances, the hormone insulin carefully regulates how much glucose is in the blood.
Insulin stimulates cells to absorb enough glucose from the blood for the energy, or fuel, that they need.
Insulin also stimulates the liver to absorb and store any glucose that's left over.
After a meal, the amount of glucose in the blood rises, and this triggers the release of insulin.
When blood glucose levels fall, during exercise for example, insulin levels fall too.
A second hormone manufactured by the pancreas is called glucagon.
It stimulates the liver to release glucose when it's needed, and this raises the level of glucose in the blood.
Insulin is manufactured and stored in the pancreas, which is a thin gland about 15 cm long that lies crosswise behind the stomach.
It's often described as being two glands in one, since in addition to making insulin it also produces enzymes that are vital for digestion of food.
These include lipase, which helps to digest fat, and amylase that helps to digest starchy foods.
It also releases 'bicarbonate of soda' to neutralise any stomach acid that may otherwise damage the lining of the gut.
There are two types of diabetes.
Type 1 diabetes is caused by an autoimmune disorder, which is a problem with the body's immune system.
In a healthy body, specialised cells in the pancreas make insulin.
Therefore people with type 1 diabetes require insulin injections to control their blood glucose levels.
It usually occurs in people under 30 years, but can occur at any age.
Type 1 diabetes affects approximately 15 per cent of people with diabetes.
In type 2 diabetes, the beta cells still produce insulin.
However, either the cells do not respond properly to the insulin or the insulin produced naturally is not enough to meet the needs of the body.
So insulin is usually still present in a person with type 2 diabetes, but it does not work as well as it should.
Some people with type 2 diabetes can keep it under control by losing weight, changing their diet, and increasing their exercise.
Others take one or more medications, including insulin.
It usually occurs in people who are over the age of 50 years and have a family history of diabetes.
Type 2 diabetes affects approximately 85 per cent of people with diabetes.
Being overweight makes insulin less efficient at controlling blood glucose levels.
This is often referred to as insulin resistance.
Type 2 diabetes often responds to a healthy eating plan, appropriate exercise and weight reduction; but sometimes tablets and then later, insulin may be required.
Diabetics need to monitor and control their blood sugar carefully as too high blood sugar - or hyperglycaemia - strains the body and can cause problems later on.
These problems are called complications and include:
It can cause problems in legs as well as other systems in the body and often leads to the toes, feet and legs having to be amputated.
The kidneys filter waste out of the blood when they don't work well, poisons can build up leading to kidney failure and patients having to undergo a kidney transplant.
The signs and symptoms of diabetes include increased thirst, slow healing cuts and wounds, blurred vision, unexpected weight loss, frequent urination and feeling tiered and lethargic.
Once a person is diagnosed as diabetic, healthy eating, regular exercise, medication, regular visits to the various medical professionals like the doctor, dentist and dietician, the monitoring of the blood glucose levels and a positive mental attitude are vital in keeping the blood-sugar levels under control and thus being able to have a long and healthy life.
Blood sugar levels can easily be tested at any pharmacy or doctor.
Some famous diabetics include tennis player Billy-Jean King, Blues legend B.B. King, South African politician Winnie Mandela, rock pioneer Elvis Presley, R&B star Luther Vandross, composer Andrew Lloyd Webber, actress Halle Berry, singers Johnny Cash and Aretha Franklin and writer Ernest Hemmingway.

Original_Search_Title: Dine Ugandan Style At SAB Restaurant
Headline: Uganda: Dine Ugandan Style At SAB Restaurant
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is midday, I am right in the middle of Kampala, the central business district. Everyone is rushing. I am hungry and the traffic jam is irritating, adding to my hunger.
I move towards the Constitutional Square with my nostrils up, looking for a good place to have lunch. Opposite Kampala Central Police Station, I see Sab Restaurant. I walk in, only to find another jam at the counter. Everyone is paying for food, while pointing at the large colourful menu on the wall behind the cashier.
In front of me are three white ladies, two Asian men to my right and the rest fellow Africans, demanding service. Sab seems to be a popular restaurant in the centre of town.
The menu - ohh how splendid! Every dish is cooked Ugandan style. Studying the menu, I get in a dilemma of choosing what to eat. There is variety. Close to 20 items to choose from, vegetarian and non-vegetarian - the amount of money in your pocket would be the limit. Prices at Sab are affordable to an average Ugandan. A fat man who is said to be the owner and manager of the restaurant, visits every table, talking to guests. He left the European ladies smiling - I am sure they will return to Sab.
I paid sh3,000 to the cashier and got a receipt that I used to order for beef stew, rice, posho, kalo (millet bread) and irish potatoes. Thereafter, I walked to the sink to wash my hands. Surprisingly, there was Dettol anti-bacterial soap.
After taking my seat, the waitress came in a 'flash' with a steaming tray. On it was my beef stew - of course, my appetite rose at the aroma rising from the thick beef. If you enjoy white rice, try to remind the waitresses here, otherwise, they will bring something else. I did not do so and was given brown fried rice, a slice of posho and sweet potatoes instead of Irish potatoes. The waitress told me they did not have Irish potatoes, only to see them being served to another person, a few minutes later.
Anyway, my enhanced appetite could not wait for any explanations. I immediately started digging into the plate with the rage of a hungry lion.
Even when the manager came to my table, I had no time to waste, I gave him thumbs-up and invested the remaining time mining the spicy fresh meal.
As I left Sab, I wondered why they served so much food for the money that is a fraction of the charges at some fancy restaurants in town.
Sab is a people restaurant. In the week when Uganda hosts CHOGM, I will return to the restaurant to celebrate.
RATING/OUT OF FIVE
Staff friendliness 2
Service speed 4
Cleanliness 3
Food quality 4
Value for money 4
Staff attentiveness 1
Ambience 3
Toilet cleanliness 2
Drinks 4
Overall rating 55%

Original_Search_Title: Local is Lekker?
Headline: Namibia: Local is Lekker?
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Let us set the record straight right from the beginning - I have never been a great fan of local music. When funds permit, I do buy a local artiste's CD or even two for Ubuntu's sake.
Don't get me wrong - I am not from the school of thought that regards everything local as being of inferior quality. In fact, on the contrary, we do have some good music producers that expect nothing but the best from their artistes. We also have a handful of promoters who do their best to take their artistes to greater heights. OK, enough praise singing, let's get to the nitty-gritty.
The question at hand is - how does one identify real Namibian sounds. I mean do we go for the version of Pulakena? Or should rather award it to the Unam choir? We could likewise regard the Mshasho boss of representing true Namibian sounds - that is of cause provided we award one or the other price to his rival - GMP.
Please let us not dwell any further on Mshasho and GMP - I shudder to think what will happen to me. What about Peter Josef Augab or maybe Jackson Kaujeua? I don't know do we have more choices? Of course, we have more choices! We have more self-proclaimed hip-hop artistes emerging daily that it is almost hard to keep track. Roger-D, AKA The Notorious, Slum Party Maestro PG, Fennie Fabulous - we all have them! I wonder if they went to the same school of names like our Shebeen Owners from the North.
But seriously, which is the real Namibian sound? I mean, Stella could have won us a Kora. Had she won, then everyone would be asking where she is from. That would undoubtfully set the stage for the rebirth of Namibian music. No need to dwell on couldas and wouldas. Let's face the real issue at hand. We do not have musical chords originating out of our motherland - period. Or any that would enjoy a consensus as being a true representative of Namibian music.
And I thought our diversity and democracy was something to be proud of! Bubblegum music is no wonder the most influential genre in Namibia, However, just like a bubblegum that has exhausted its sweetness - we have to spit it out some time, and chew on our own unique genre!
Being an ardent fan of Golden Oldies myself, I have resigned myself to doom and gloom. It will rain potatoes the day you see the Chez Ntemba DJ inviting everyone to the dance floor to the tunes of Credence Clear Water Revival's Bad Moon Rising. You will also never enter Club Thriller, and find yourself swinging hips to Billy Joel's River of Dreams. Now you know why I stay at home on weekends! But then again - that's just me.
Meanwhile, I will keep trying to find genuine Namibian music - I just might tend to like it. If you agree, say Eewa!

Original_Search_Title: Two Toddlers Die From Suspected Food Poisoning
Headline: Zimbabwe: Two Toddlers Die From Suspected Food Poisoning
Body_Text:
Harare — TWO children from Rusape died last week while a third one was admitted in hospital after they had eaten suspected poisoned sweet potatoes.
The children -- Rutendo Munyarari (4) and Lameck Chipomho (one-year and 11 months) -- died on admission to Chiundu Clinic while the other one, Takudzwa Severa (2) whose condition was reported as stable is still admitted at Rusape District Hospital.
The incident that occurred at Bwititi Village under Chief Tandi was confirmed by the Manicaland provincial police spokesperson Inspector Brian Makomeke.
He said Takudzwa's mother, Ms Charity Bwititi (23), served the children with the sweet potatoes and left them alone before she went to a nearby garden.
When she returned 30 minutes later, she found them lying on the ground froth coming out of their mouths. She took them to a clinic where the two were later taken to Rusape Hospital and died.
In a another incident, a woman from Chisumbanje committed suicide after an altercation with her husband who was accusing her of having an extra-marital affair.
The incident occurred at Madenji Village under Chief Garawa on Saturday last week at around 7pm.
Police said Edna Mutende (29) was sleeping in their bedroom with her husband, Samuel Kambuya (78), when an unknown person knocked on the door.
Kambuya attended the knock but he did not find anyone and then accused her wife resulting in the altercation after which Mutende went outside and drank some cotton pesticide.

Original_Search_Title: Café Viva's New Look And Service
Headline: Uganda: CafÃ© Viva's New Look And Service
Body_Text:
Recently, I met Dr Vali Jamal and he chided me for not having been to his restaurant, Café Viva, in recent memory. He intoned that it was "different" and that by all means I should schedule a visit and see for myself.
Well, I must confess that it came as a surprise when fellow trencherman and I finally did go to visit Café Viva last Sunday. It's not just "different" - it's altogether "new." The walls have been repainted, off-white, and the murals have been extended to cover all the walls. One mural runs in a strip all round the room, while on one wall is a depiction of a rain forest. Upstairs the motif is trees, in daytime and at night! There's also a red-and-black zebra pattern. It reminded me of a Paris sidewalk cafe.
There are other things different about Café Viva. It has been Ugandanised, meaning all the staff are now local. The menu has been revamped. There are lots of upmarket continental dishes - fish fillet in white sauce, three different kinds of penne, lots of fresh salads, sandwiches and succulent burgers. I had his penne in coriander dish when I first reviewed him; I'd still recommend it.
He is also putting some of his eggs into the traditional-food kikapu, to mix up a cliché. The matooke is cooked kama jumbani (as it should be - i.e. steamed in leaves and stalk. Vali Jamal is Kenyan-born.) As stew, there is a choice of fish or beef. The fish portions are large and at Shs5,000, this is a steal. The beef version is only Shs4,000. The service comes to your table and you get as much as at those "help-yourself" places that abound in downtown. Why would you not choose Café Viva, which has five toilets! Apart from that, he wants to make "the best Rolex" in Kampala any executive can sit down and partake of for breakfast or bag it.
Jamal assures me that when it comes to omelettes, one can choose from a French Omelette, a fluffy or soufflleed, a firm or sweet omelette or even a Spanish omelette with potatoes, garlic, cilantro etc. "Of course, you can have it as part of an English breakfast, too."
Jamal himself made me a soup. Like the fine wine we were having, it had hidden aromas you only discovered as you went along: citronella (lemon grass), coriander, rosemary - and something else. I just couldn't make it out. "Well," he said, stirring up mischief, "I started the soup with excess water from the rice that was boiling for the biriyani." Innovative! He then brought out a whole tilapia in coriander paste and massala. I mean, if you get such a lovely fish in the middle of Kampala why would you want to go all the way to Munyonyo? Okay, you would - for the outing and the fresh lake air.
The biriyani I ate was the most delicious I've had in Kampala - not even in the upmarket Kololo palaces. It was an "integrated" biriyani, where the rice is layered with the curried chicken and Irish, infusing all the ingredients with the multi flavours. "You don't get this anymore even in Asian homes!" says Dr Jamal, "what you get is coloured rice and a very thick curry at the side." Dr Jamal had even "smoked" the biriyani by dropping ghee on a piece of charcoal. No ghee in the biriyani, though, "that's out these days." Even his onion kachumbali for biriyani ("a must") had been smoked.
We chatted about the India Vs England test match in progress. India were doing well. He commiserated about this with a group of English tourists as they stepped in, saying, "Don't worry, rain will come to the rescue. Fish 'n'chips?" No, they wanted CTM. "CTM?" what's that I asked. "Oh, Kadum, that's almost England's national dish now - you know chicken tikka massala." He brought it out on a bed of salad.
What a difference two months makes! The last time I had visited Café Viva was after the anti-Mabira/anti-Asian riots. Jamal was caught up in it, first as he came off the matatu and then in his office while the mob tried to come to City Square. Revenues plummeted by one-third. Competitors started "vulturing" (his usage) for his place, figuring to pick it up on the salvage market.
"Then you know who your well-wishers are!" During the last fortnight, Jamal has sunk in a modest amount of money in new investments. Dr Jamal has Chogm in sight and before that the Aga Khan's visit. Wireless Internet is being installed. What a long way to come!
Rating
XXXX: UNRIVALLED
XXX: EXCELLENT
XX: VERY GOOD
X: WORTH A VISIT
NONE: POOR to SATISFACTORY
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The menu listing may change without notice.
Place: Café Viva
Location: Kampala Road, next to Crane Bank, opposite City Square
Rating: XX
Food: A variety of European and local fare
Price range: Very reasonable
Service: Good
Opening hours: Throughout the week
Parking: On the street

Original_Search_Title: 'We Live on Rich People's Garbage'
Headline: Burundi: Ancilla Riziki - "We Live On Rich People's Garbage"
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Ancilla Riziki, 38, lives with her nine children in Buterere, one of the poorest suburbs of the Burundian capital, Bujumbura. Struggling to find formal employment and with a large family to feed, she works on a rubbish dump, foraging through the garbage all day with no protection for her hands and feet.
"We were living at Gasenyi in the north of Bujumbura in 1993 but we had to leave because of the war. Kamenge was becoming a battlefield; every house was being destroyed. The whole population was leaving, so we moved to Buterere.
"I have no job here. With nine children to feed and clothe, it is not easy. I have hands to work, but there are no jobs. I would do any petty job I could find just to leave this place, but I cannot find one. Look at my eyes [Riziki's eyes were very red and sore from the fumes of the rubbish dump]. A charity worker bought me glasses, but they are broken.
"There is no choice here; we live on rich people's garbage. Every morning, I wait for the lorries. They normally come at eight and then we start digging and digging the mountains of rubbish the whole day.
"Many of us are widows or very poor. We live on scavenging. There are also men, young people and even children. Each group looks for specific objects; plastic bags, metallic items or food. Women normally look for charcoal, but sometimes, people throw away useful things. Then there are big battles here, especially among young men; the strongest get them.
"I normally get charcoal worth 200 francs (20 US cents) per day. We get very little because it is already used charcoal. With the money, I buy sweet potatoes for my children. But it is not enough for a big family like mine. Mostly, they go to sleep still hungry. Sometimes I can get clothes from here. With 200 francs, I cannot afford to buy them.
"I want to make sure my children do not end up here too. They all attend schools, except the three youngest, who are small. My oldest daughter is finishing secondary school thanks to a godfather who paid her expenses. The others are still in primary school. We are lucky that we do not have to pay school fees and each year charity organisations offer us school materials."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Deseases of Affluence in Africa
Headline: Uganda: Money Can Make You Ill
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE headline in a recent national Kenyan newspaper ran: "New epidemic threatens to wipe out more than Aids". So what is this new epidemic? The answer is that there is a real threat emerging, in the form of diseases which are common in developed countries, but are now affecting this generation of urban affluent Africans, at a much earlier age.
We have all heard about the diseases involved already, but the newspaper was highlighting the fact that Africans are now becoming uniquely susceptible to diseases which we thought only affected old, white people. In Western countries we refer to them as the diseases of affluence, or the degenerative diseases and perhaps it is a tangible sign of development that such ailments are now prevalent in Africa. So which diseases are we talking about? The epidemic consists of conditions such as diabetes, high blood pressure and high cholesterol (too much fat in the blood), all of which are causing unhealthy blood vessels, which in turn are leading to deaths from heart attacks and strokes.
My birthplace, Northern Ireland, has had one of the highest rates of heart disease. Hence, over the past 30 years, there has been a wide-ranging campaign to educate people about the type of lifestyle which puts them at risk. The problem for the Irish was simple - they enjoyed eating, drinking and smoking and they didn't like to exercise much.
On top of that, they liked the wrong types of food - fatty foods and sweet foods. The classic Northern Irish meal was called the Ulster fry - consisting of fried bacon and eggs, potato bread, soda bread, sausage, baked beans and tomatoes, all cooked in a pan swimming in fat. This type of diet fills up the blood vessels with fat and eventually blocks them. If the blockage is to an artery supplying the heart, the person gets a heart attack. If it is in a vessel supplying the brain, he gets a stroke. Either way the outcome is not desirable.
Too much food and too many sweet things cause diabetes and cigarette smoking also damages the blood vessels, not to speak of causing lung cancer and lung disease, while too much alcohol damages the liver.
The Northern Irish people enjoyed their vices and they died happy, but they died young, so over a period of years they had to learn to change their lifestyle - if they wanted to live longer.
People who are obese, eat the wrong diet and don't exercise are an accident waiting to happen. Either their vital blood vessels will get blocked, or their pancreas, which produces the insulin to keep their sugar level normal, will pack up. When this happens, the excess sugar in the blood causes damage to the blood vessels to the eyes, or the vessels to the legs, so that the circulation doesn't work and they develop gangrene. In this case the choice is of either going blind, or having your leg amputated.
In the West, people classically had a heart attack, or developed 'type two' diabetes in their 60s, but Africans are getting the same diseases in their 30s and 40s. But there is some good news as well - most of these diseases can be reversed if one adopts a healthy lifestyle. I have treated patients in their 30s who had developed diabetes and high blood pressure, but when they lost weight and exercised, their diabetes and high blood pressure disappeared.
The right diet consists of eating the correct foods in the right quantity. It may be normal in terms of survival of the species, for one to eat as much as possible when it is available, in case there is no food tomorrow. However in the affluent urban community, where there is no danger of running out of food, heaped plates of food are still the order of the day and we may need to rethink our eating policy.
As adults, our bodies are designed to use the precise amount of food we eat to provide us with energy and if we don't need all the energy provided, the body will cleverly store it away for future use, as fat reserves.
However, if we have no future use for this fat, in terms using it as energy, we simply grow fatter and fatter. African women put the fat on their bottom - akabina kanene - while the men put it on their gut - the patriotic front. And all the dietary books which have been written can be summed up under two headings - take in less calories, i.e. food or, use up more energy, i.e. take more exercise.
So this epidemic which is affecting affluent Africans can be curbed and all we need to do is eat less, eat the right diet, exercise, don't smoke, and drink alcohol in moderation. So, the next time you are about to stop at the pork joint for beer and muchomo, just drive on past and go to the gym.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Bread Goes Up As Its Weight Falls
Headline: Kenya: Price of Bread Goes Up As Its Weight Falls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The bread on your table has lost much weight and become more costly during the past five years.
A 500-gramme loaf retailed at Sh18 in 2002 when the then minister for Trade and Industry directed bakers in the country to cut the weight to 400 grammes.
The new directive was implemented by July 2002 but the retail price of bread remained largely unchanged in spite of the 100 grammes loss per loaf.
Many bakers at the time spent millions of shillings to import new mould trays in order to comply with Section 3(i) of the gazette notice.
Some of the bakers who talked to the Nation at the time protested against the order claiming that most of the major players had mould trays that had been designed to bake bread in weights of 500 grammes and a kilogramme.
Taxation on plastic
Investigations carried out by the department of weights and measures in Nakuru indicated that some bakers had been selling bread weighing 480 grammes instead of 500 grammes.
One bakery was fined Sh60,000 for selling underweight bread.
The lighter bread weighing 400 grammes will now retail at Sh28 which is Sh10 more than it cost in 2002.
The price will certainly be higher in the rural areas owing to the cost of transport, which means that consumers in those areas will part with Sh30 for a 400-gramme loaf.
The new prices have been dictated by the high taxation on plastic packaging material, which the manufacturers want to pass on to consumers.
A survey done by the Nation last year in Nakuru found that more low income families had opted for sweet potatoes as a breakfast alternative for break.
A supervisor at one of the supermarkets in Nakuru Town yesterday said that the new prices of bread would be implemented this week.

Original_Search_Title: From Cape to Cairo On Fork And Knife
Headline: Zimbabwe: From Cape to Cairo On Fork And Knife
Body_Text:
Harare — Thanks to the deliberations of the leaders of thirty two independent African States in Addis Ababa some forty four years ago, May 25 was declared an annual holiday in various countries.
The intention of those revolutionaries of yore was to mark a day to " symbolize the determination of the People of Africa to free themselves from foreign domination and exploitation".
In Zimbabwe this annual holiday provides an opportunity for cultural and leisure pursuits, such as exploring the ruins at Great Zimbabwe, enjoying a boerewors and sadza braai in your front garden, or indulging in a traditional feast at a top-end restaurant.
So rather than contemplating the state of the nation, or speculating on the fate of all those running dogs and lackeys of imperial times, I decided to embrace the ideals of the African Union and get down with the home boys and girls at Msasa's Doon Estate. Every year on Africa Day Chef Kerry Wallace of the Shop Café serves a spectacular banquet of African dishes. Last Friday three distinct cuisines were featured on three separately groaning trestle tables: North African /Mediterranean, Central African and South African/Cape Malay specialities.
Wearing his trademark baseball cap and Yasser Arafat-style tea towel, Chef Kerry did the rounds of his jam-packed restaurant, requesting patience while finishing touches to the feast were made. In no uncertain terms we were asked not to mix regional dishes on one plate, although we could sample separate courses from each cuisine. If we didn't like it, we could foxtrot oscar. But of course everyone agreed, and there was almost a stampede when the green light was given. Kerry and his staff had worked hard, and there was plenty and more to go round.
George and I veered towards the Central African table to sample a subtly delicious fish and ginger soup made with local tilapia, tomato, haricot beans and brinjal. Kerry relented and allowed us to eat this with vetkoek, a South African bread roll. Hearty soup with the consistency of garbure or bouillabaisse, this could provide a satisfying meal on its own.
Continuing with the Central African theme, George then tasted a rich beef stew served with green herby rice (Mozambican influence), pot roast chicken, muriwo, roasted butternut and sweet potatoes.
Lured by the colourful North African spread, I discovered an aubergine salad, which proved the perfect foil to labneh, which is cream cheese made from yogurt. A couscous and garbanzo salad was delicious, as were the stuffed cabbage rolls, roasted vegetables and chicken curry with brown lentil rice.
I'll have to wait for next year to taste the Cape Malay classics of pickled fish, vegetable bobotie, lamb and tomato bredie and sweet roast pumpkin.
Chef Kerry was weaned on pickled fish and watermelon konfyt in his mother's Sea Point kitchen, so it's hardly surprising that the delicious array of desserts included kumquat konfyt (aromatic preserve of the exotic kumquat fruit in sugary syrup) and perfectly made milk tart. A South African milk tart bears more than a passing resemblance to the classic English custard tart, which English chef Marcus Waring made for Queen Elizabeth's birthday luncheon last June. Who's to say the Queen might not have preferred a genuine melk tert?
A freshly sliced orange, date and mint dessert, steeped in rose water could make a North African yearn for the souk or casbah, while a coconut milk crème caramel recalled the palm-lined beaches of Mozambique. But what really took the biscuit were the Romany Creams, baked South African style with coconut, and covered with chocolate. Perfect with a cup of filter coffee, served by efficient waiters.
While politicians may speak of exploitation and domination, chefs and cooks are more into borrowing, sharing and creating. Kerry's Africa Day feast, a fusion of so many regional specialities, is something to look forward to every year.
The Shop Café
Doon Estate
1 Harrow Road
Msasa
Tel: 446684

Original_Search_Title: Public Varsities Have Yielded to Crude And Undisguised Greed
Headline: Kenya: Public Varsities Have Yielded to Crude And Undisguised Greed
Body_Text:
Nairobi — If all afflictions that a people could suffer from, nothing is perhaps as bad as the scourge of ignorance. It was not coincidental that the founders of our republic listed ignorance as one of the three cardinal enemies to be fought, alongside poverty and disease.
Ignorance is the driver of both poverty and disease and, indeed, of most undesirable things of life. Our people say that if only the hungry dog knew that the ten shillings note it was staring at could buy it a kilo of bones, it would not just stare at the money. But how would the dog make the link between the shilling and a kilo of bones?
Fortunately, unlike the ignorant dog, we have learnt to turn most things in our environment to our advantage. This is the very essence of education - that we shall discover how to manage our environment better, for purposes of wholesome living.
From the most primitive hunter-gatherer societies to the ultra-modern Information and Communication Technology age, the one thing human beings have religiously done has been to educate their youth.
Education of the youth is critical not just for purposes of self-preservation, but indeed for preservation of the society. The youth are taught all about the things that have worked well for the community and the need to carry them into the future. They are also taught about that which has not worked and the need to avoid it. Together with their teachers, they experiment and look out for fresh knowledge. All this seeks to make society a better place to live in.
It is for this reason that Africa has always placed high premium on universal education. But traditional education in Africa was not only universal, it was also free and compulsory, up to the very highest level of learning one could achieve. Of course there were societies that practiced slavery and other oppressive forms of keeping segments of society down. Still, by and large, the free enjoyed universal education, marked at various levels by different rites of passage. And so we developed universities in Africa centuries ahead of the first European university.
Europe would later refine the university education it borrowed from Africa and bring it back to us ages later in different containers. It came complete with new trappings from the western world. One such a trapping was the de-emphasised role of the benefits of the learned person to society. Emphasis has since been a lot more on what the learned person can do for himself and for his immediate family and - if he is that generous - what he can do for the extended family. Education ceased to be a public good and became instead an individual's best escape route from poverty.
Professional courses in our universities are today valued not because of the good the professional can give to society, but because of how rich the professional expects to become and how comfortably he expects to live.
The medical doctor is today concerned less with the plight of his patients and more about his fee note. Every school leaver wants to study medicine because it will make him or her rich.
Today, our universities are intrinsically caught up in this avaricious deathtrap. Public universities have since the advent of what we call parallel degree programmes succumbed to raw and undisguised greed. Our universities are steadily deteriorating into academic kiosks. Where the public university should exist to provide the highest level of education as affordably as possible, it would appear to exist primarily to make money.
The difference between the university vice-chancellor and the common duka wallah on River Road defines itself only in terms of the merchandise on offer. The one offers motor vehicle spare parts and sweet potatoes. The other one offers degree certificates.
The vice-chancellor has no reservations at all in publishing the names of his debtors in public newspapers; much the same way it means nothing to him to propose to raise university fees by 150 per cent. It means nothing to him that he is locking some of the best brains out of the university system.
And now it looks like university administrators have consciously and deliberately purposed that from next year, university education should be decidedly and unashamedly be the preserve of the super-rich. For how many Kenyans can afford Sh500,000 annually as the minimum fee payable for a good professional course?
From institutions of academic excellence, public universities are steadily degenerating into third-rate commercial entities concerned not about academic standards, but about business plans and balance sheets. This is a dangerous trend, which must be arrested before it completely goes overboard. For a start, all men and women of goodwill must reject the proposed new fees for public universities. It is scandalous to note that in some respects, these fees are even higher than what most private universities charge for the same courses! Or people say that greed is a leper, he creeps up on you very quietly.

Original_Search_Title: Mascom Changes Colours
Headline: Botswana: Mascom Changes Colours
Body_Text:
Francistown — Mascom has introduced a new theme and marketing campaign called "Closer to you".
Mascom Chief Marketing Officer, Dzene Makhwade-Seboni said the campaign is visible mainly on outdoor billboards that can be seen as one drives around the country and it represents a move away from the curved red swish template that has been used for the past 18 months.
She said on a regular basis, to remain fresh to the target market, Mascom changes the look and feel of its marketing communication. The new campaign maintains all the positive aspects of the brand, such as the logo; brand colours; In touch with your life. However to revamp the look, feel and appeal of communication certain aspects have changed.
The introduction of Closer to You is one such change. "Closer to You is not a replacement of In touch with your life. In touch with your life is integral to the Mascom logo and the essence of the brand. "Closer to you describes the connection shown by the images used, of scenes common to Batswana or people living in Botswana. These pictures are underscored by strong wording that reinforces the sense of close connection by stating that Mascom shares the same values, the same interests and heritage as Batswana. The main emotional takeaway is: Mascom understands me as part of a nation, a people and as an individual because they are closer to me," she said.
The strong statements and photographs that are shown in these new adverts are varied. Examples are a photograph of the popular cultural group Culture Spears, of Kulenyane fame, which states Local ke yone, re le eme nokeng, highlighting Mascom's sponsorship of the first Botswana Musicans' Union Music Awards in 2006 at which Culture Spears was a multi-award winner. Another image shows and elderly couple in a tender moment and is captioned Loyalty, Trust and Going the distance to underscore the values that contribute to a long, reliable and rewarding relationship that the couple in the picture has and that Mascom aims to have with its clients.
Makhwade-Seboni, said: "Closer to you is a campaign that emphasizes the fact that Mascom is closely engaged with Botswana people and culture. The Closer to you campaign keeps Mascom's devotion to its subscribers at the heart of the brand. The key to successful brand positioning, is in finding just the right balance between tradition and modernity and the new look encompasses both."
"Mascom subscribers can look forward to the continued tradition of Mascom introducing exciting and very beneficial products and services that will show that indeed Mascom is closer to them and their everyday lives", adds Makhwade-Seboni.
The Closer to you campaign will be carried throughout the entire country in Setswana and in English and will be most visible through billboards.
Dear Consumer's Voice
A few days ago I visited a Supermarket and was appalled by the quality of the fruit and vegetables available. The bananas were bruised and over-ripe, the naartjie's skin was too dry, the mushrooms had mould and the sweet potatoes were so dry they looked like they were picked last year.
I complained to the manager and asked why they were priced the same as other fresh produce. The manager basically told me that I could take it or leave it. What should I have done?
A. Do what the manager suggested! Leave it.
The most powerful weapons customers have in the war against dubious stores and suppliers are their feet. Just turn around and walk out. Take your money somewhere where the produce is what you are looking for, where you will be treated with a little respect and where your custom will be valued. Sooner or later the stores that disrespect us will either get the message or will go out of business. Good riddance to them!
Of course it would also be reasonable for a shop owner to reduce the price of produce that isn't perfect quality. Some stores do that already. Produce that has just reached its sell-by date is often discounted which is very reasonable. It's still perfectly safe and edible but isn't going to last very long so they knock an amount off the price. They're being practical. They would rather get some money for the produce that just throw it away.
So long as food being sold isn't so bad that it's dangerous to our health there's not a lot the law can do but there's a lot that we can do. Let's use our feet and walk away!

Original_Search_Title: Restaurant Review - Le Francais
Headline: Zimbabwe: Le Francais - Remarkably Reasonable Repasts (recently!)
Body_Text:
TWO consolations on arriving slightly late at Monomatapa and learning the urbane James Maposa was sick and unable to join me for lunch at Le Francais, was that his place was taken by giggly Nelsy Mugadza, whose name tag announces: Meetings Success Director ("banqueting manager", in plainspeak!) and guest relations manager, Altah Nengomasha, who could have a career change to modelling tailored business suits on international catwalks if she chose.
Le Francais was at its light, airy best, with pleasant breeze blowing through the open french windows across a verandah on which I can't understand why they don't lay tables. The few overseas travellers we get all want to dine alfresco.
I can never make up my mind whether La Francais is a better lunchtime or supper venue. It does well at both times, but I hated it when breakfast was served there.
Maitre d'hotel, Never Chirinda, from Nyazura, has worked at Le Francais for 22 years. He kept me supplied with delightfully chilled articles of a moderately intoxicating nature (Pilseners); the girls sipped at fruit juices and "non-alcoholic cocktail" (perish the thought!) called tsanga (Shona for reed) which looked too good to drink.
It was the Friday after the Independence Day mid-week holiday; the place was as busy as I've seen it for years. Only cloud on the horizon was I had to return to work to file a few stories to keep readers happy for the first bit of my seven week "sanity break", otherwise I might have ordered a bottle of the wine Never was disappointed I spurned... and had a liqueur or two with post-prandial coffee.
Harare's original French restaurant's version of French onion soup wasn't what was expected: opaque rather than almost clear, rather like a brown version of the classic English white onion soup. I quite enjoyed it at $20 000, with excellent warm continental bread and really good butter.
Other hot appetisers are peppered ostrich fillet salad with deep fried onions ($45 000); grilled prawn kebab on spinach, leek and potato mash with sweet chilli sauce or calamari tempura with mushrooms and beurre blanc, both $70 000.
Cold starters are raw (can it be cooked?) beef carpaccio with marinated mushrooms and gazpacho dressing ($45 000); smoked Norwegian salmon on potato cake with cucumber vinaigrette, $65 000; or the French salad with citrus and olives at $25 000 which I had, making the mistake of grinding fresh black pepper over what should have been a subtle dish. It was grossly over seasoned: but my fault.
Le Francais' food prices were -- I thought -- surprisingly reasonable when I went on April 20, please don't threaten to sue if they've risen stratospherically when you read this. (You'll lose!)
Both Nelsy and I had flambéed sole. We both admitted we were sorry to reach the last mouthful. Altah chose a superb tender fillet mignon, also flambéed and also $80 000, with boiled parsley potatoes and young spring vegetables. Flambéing is part of the theatre of dining out.
Nelsy was pleased I showed her how to professionally debone the succulent sole, so not a sliver of the tasty flesh was wasted.
Other fish dishes were whole poached Nyanga trout with black pepper hollandaise and samp-and-bean ragout ($70 000); pan-fried salmon and mushroom teriyaki on rice timbale with stir-fried vegetables ($120 000); piri-piri or garlic Mozambique prawns with pilaf rice, $85 000, and baked kingklip on lemon thyme risotto cake with deep-fried root vegetables ($120 000).
Grilled whole baby chicken was $80 000 and chicken breast pitou, stuffed with mushrooms, with chateau potatoes and fennel cream sauce $70 000.
All grills sounded very reasonable (these days), including free sauce, if you like glop and gloop: T-bone, fillet, rump, or sirloin at $80 000, or $85 000 for rack of lamb. (At a humorous private home dinner party recently, with a satirical political theme, main course was "Iraq of lamb with Condoleezza Rice!")
Nelsy and I tucked into magnificent crepes Suzettes -- again theatrically flambéed by the super attentive Never -- while Altah gapped for a 3 o'clock; (which could be why she could take up modelling!)
Apart from this trademark French pancake pudding, other sweets are Black Forest gateaux, Tuiles baskets with ice-cream or sorbet and fresh fruit; coconut brulée or chocolate pudding with a soft ganache (chopped chocolate and heavy cream) centre all $30 000 or cheese board: $45 000.
We both declined coffee at $10-$15 000 or Dom Pedro's at $20 000 and I strolled slowly and reluctantly back to work. Sod's law the second I sat down after climbing six steep wind-challenging flights of steps, puffing like a grampus, the phone rang. It was the bank, 300 metres the far side of the restaurant! My new ATM card was ready for collection. I needed it urgently. Oh well...Oh hell!
Le Francais, Crown Plaza Monomatapa. Open lunch and supper. Closed Sunday.
Reasonably handicapped friendly; not really a venue for small children, but there's a buffet/carvery next door. Smoking or non-smoking. Tel 704501 (but it's never answered!).

Original_Search_Title: Treat Yourself to Chilled, Bubbling Glass of Champagne
Headline: Zimbabwe: Treat Yourself to Chilled, Bubbling Glass of Champagne
Body_Text:
Harare — WHENEVER I hear the word champagne, I immediately think of celebration, bubbly, happy times -- all those things rolled into one.
This is one succulent liquid that many people associate with special occasions such as birthdays, the arrival of a new baby, graduation parties on any other grand occasion, which needs to be sealed with the popping of a champagne bottle.
I have often noted during parties that when wine lovers party, champagne is served as an accompaniment to food.
I had my first sip of champagne when I passed my A-levels some 12 years ago, and since then, I often treat myself to a glass or two when the need calls for it. And I am sure there are lot of people out there who sometimes go for this sparkling wine, not so much to celebrate anything, but just to get a feeling of exhilaration.
Whether it be blanc, de blancs, blanc de noirs or single-grape (there are various types of champagne, mind you) the truth is champagne is in a class of its own when it comes to taste.
But what is champagne?
Champagne is a sparkling wine produced by inducing the in-bottle secondary fermentation of wine to effect what is known as carbonation.
The wine is named after a region in France which is renowned for the production of this sparkling liquid.
While the term has often been used by makers of sparkling wine in other parts of the world, many claim it should ideally be used to refer only to the wines made in the Champagne region.
Most champagnes, either locally or internationally produced, are a blend of chardonnay and pinot noir grapes, from across several vintage and, like any wine, they range from sweet to dry.
Experts and amateurs who have an interest in wine will certainly agree that champagne can be taken with an array of popular foods, beyond the predictable pairings we grew up reading in the Sydney Shelton's collection and in the Mills and Boon of caviar and oysters!
A glass of champagne can be accompanied by any mushroom dish, cheese, nuts, especially almonds popcorn and potato chips, any type of pasta, meat, risotto, any type of seafoods, poultry and game.
Deep fried foods, such as fried chicken, french fries or calamari, for the same reason beer goes well with these foods are good accompaniments for champagne.
Legendary Prime Minister of England Winston Churchill once said: "A single glass of champagne imparts a feeling of exhilaration. The nerves are braced, the imagination is agreeably stirred; the wits become more nimble."
Go on, treat yourself to a chilled, bubbling glass of champagne this weekend!

Original_Search_Title: Solwezi - Where It is All Happening
Headline: Zambia: Solwezi - Where It is All Happening
Body_Text:
SOLWEZI is the buzzword in Zambia.
And as if to emphasise that the district is probably the place to be at the moment, a big billboard emphatically screams at you on arrival using the Chingola road: "Welcome to Solwezi, Where It Is All Happening."
Surely, everything is happening in this provincial headquarters of the once Cinderella North-Western Province. Activities in this town range from very extensive mining of copper and gold at Kansanshi Mine, and soon copper at Lumwana mining company, bee keeping to prostitution.
And with "all happening in Solwezi" corruption is also on the rise. Anti-Corruption Commission (ACC) director, Nixon Banda, said recently that his office was receiving more reports of corruption, especially from the public sector, which is the largest employer.
Once in a while the district also provides a strange kind of 'tourism' in the form of a practice called 'chikondo', where the deceased allegedly refuses to be buried until it identifies the killer, dragging, real or imaginary, the pall-bearers are into a hunt round the village for the wizard or witch.
The practice, which has seen some people either injured or killed, has been condemned by traditional and the Government leaders but unfortunately continues, what with some people strongly believing that deaths don't just happen.
The district, which according to mayor Emmanuel Chihili is one of the fastest growing towns in Central Africa, never goes to sleep. Not only is mining a 24- hours affair, but other activities too. You only have to visit some notorious nightclubs and guest houses at night to familiarise with the nocturnal side of Solwezi.
The thriving mining activities have attracted multi-nationals including South Africans, Zimbabweans, Australians, Ghanaians, and Philippinos. And following the boom commercial sex workers have thronged the district, which a few years ago was just another sleepy rural town known for its beans and sweet potatoes.
Coming from as far as Livingstone and Lusaka, these women have permanently booked into some guest houses, which they have literally turned into brothels. The Solwezi Municipal Council recently evicted such women from the council guest house.
Sex workers, including young girls, have taken advantage of the scores of miners who have left their families on the Copperbelt Province and other places due to lack of infrastructure in this fast-growing district. You only have to spend time at the local court to hear stories about what miners are up to after working hours.
Unlike Lusaka and towns on the Copperbelt, the district has no social infrastructure to write home about where the ever increasing workforce can probably relax after a hard day's work.
The community squash court, which was renovated by Kansanshi Mines and launched in January 2006 by the then Sports Minister George Chulumanda, remains unused. And just a stone throw away, the stadium is in ruins.
But, if indications, at least on paper, are anything to go by Solwezi will soon have a makeover, thanks in part to Kansanshi Foundation, which is funded by Kansanshi mine board to upgrade social and physical infrastructure in the district.
But it remains to be seen just what will change in this district that looks like an unplanned settlement. Structures have been permitted everywhere including in places where there are no roads. Expectant first time visitors to the district have been disappointed at what they get to find on the ground.
Kansanshi Mine is constructing the Solwezi golf and country club to house disciplines including golf, squash and tennis. But it remains to be seen how many ordinary members of the community will be finding their way to the country club.
To serve the international community and jet-set locals, the district will have an international airport of sorts following the Government's nodding Kansanshi Mine to upgrade the infrastructure at Solwezi Airport.
Head of Kansanshi Foundation, Rusty Cormack says the upgrading of the airport, expected to take off this year, would be done in three phases and would include construction of immigration offices. It would take about two years.
Mr Cormack, who has carried out projects worth more than K600million in the last six months ago, says the Foundation will soon start constructing a new multi-million-Kwacha market in Chawama.
The Foundation has started on various projects including installing a mortuary compressor at Solwezi General Hospital at the cost of K70 million.
"The Foundation has made a difference in the Solwezi area in the past four months and will continue to improve the standard of living conditions in and around the town.
The projects carried out include sinking six boreholes, upgrading road signs, working on drainage, buying science laboratory equipment at Solwezi Technical School, construction of an orphanage, repairs on two bridges on the main road.
"North-Western Province, Solwezi in particular, has a high rate of people coming to this district due to the opening of Kansanshi, and for this reason a lot more people are benefiting from the projects taking place, especially the schools," he says.
The proposed future projects include resurfacing of the main bus station, construction of public lavatories, street lighting on Independence avenue lighting, buying of refuse truck for Solwezi, construction of a dumpsite for refuse, second phase of drainage.
Others are construction of bus shelters on the route to the mine with a total 16 shops, buying of X-ray equipment for Solwezi General Hospital, improvement of town and township roads.
Apart from an airport to receive and dispatch international visitors, other projects lined up in the district include the North-West rail line, planned for by Northwest Railway Limited, which would connect Solwezi to the line of rail on the Copperbelt.
And then there is Royal Solwezi- Hotel and Villas: probably. A Zambian property investment company has invested U$4.5million in the construction of the hotel. The hotel project, which started in September 2005, is almost complete.
Hotel general manager, Gary Beardsley, says once fully operational in the next few weeks the three-star hotel will have 48 rooms, 30 single and 18 double rooms, all with 10 DSTV Channels.
The hotel is on old Chingola road, and is conveniently located 3km from the town centre, some 5km from the Kansanshi Mine, and 60km from the Lumwana Copper Project, which is expected to open in the second quarter of 2008..
Every room within the hotel will offer queen size beds, air-conditioning, 10 DSTV channels, private balconies and wireless Internet access providing you the client the ultimate convenience and comfort during their stay.
In addition to the luxury guest suites, are 10 stand-alone 2 bedroom self-catering homes. Each unit will be furnished with all the necessary items to cater for your needs. Amenities within the hotel will include: conference centre, fully licenced restaurant and bar, swimming pool with poolside lapa, laundry and room service.
The hotel has a restaurant that can seat 100 inside and 30 outside. It will also have a poolside mini bar. It will have a conference centre with a capacity of 200.
The hotel rooms are spaciously built in four buildings of 12 rooms each giving privacy, and with wooded siding doors open on to a balcony overlooking the picturesque Solwezi view.
And then there is the biggest project of them all. Lumwana mine. Solwezi is the home of Lumwana mining company, which after completion would become the single largest copper mine in Africa. Requiring an investment of more than $762m, it is the single largest investment in Zambia's history.
The amount is outside of $65m sourced for the construction of a township. About $2m has been spent just on planning the ultra-modern town to accommodate 6,000 housing units, 1,500 being for the miners, schools, clinics and shops.
And talking about shops, mayor Chihili says his Council was in talks with Platinum Gold, developers of Arcades mall in Lusaka, who have expressed interest in building a shopping centre in the district.
Spur have also shown interest in opening a supermarket in Solwezi, while Toyota Zambia are considering to open a showroom to service mine vehicles, while Engine were planning to open a service station. Barclays Bank is also considering a comeback.
With all these lofty projects, and more, there are also plans to relocate the Solwezi central business district from its current location, where it is crowded with cheap and ugly structures, to another area. But council land has run out, and the municipality has applied for traditional land from Kaonde chiefs Mumena and Kapijimpanga.
The council is also looking at ways of constructing a road to divert transit traffic to another route to decongest the central business district, which has only one overloaded main road: Independence avenue. The local authority is planning to divert traffic that comes through to othertowns.
And to support big business in the district, Bank of Zambia (BOZ) has also opened the sub-chest to be hosted by Zambia National Commercial Bank Solwezi branch.
BoZ director of banking, currency and payment systems, Morris Mulomba, said the opening of the cash-centre marked a major step for BoZ in its endeavour to facilitate cost effective provision of cash to the banking sector, business houses and the public.
He said the opening, which follows economic development to the region after the opening of the mines also contributed to the achievement of the bank's primary objective of ensuring price and financial systems stability for the enhancement of economic growth.
He said as a result of the mining activity, the province had seen the growth of other economic activities, thus ensuring that the people of North-Western Province begin to find sustainable livelihoods.
"As partners in the development there is a need for all major stakeholders to move in tandem for the economic good of our country," said Mr Mulomba.
And North-Western Province Deputy Permanent Secretary, Nkolola Hazemba said the opening of the cash-centre was highly recommended as it would stimulate development in the region, and would relieve the commercial banks of the stress of running out of cash.
And in a vote of thanks during the official opening of the BoZ sub-chest, Zambia State Insurance Corporation branch manager, Rodgers Kachungu, recalled how banks in the district ran out of cash on month ends.
Mr Kachungu said with some miners having left families on the Copperbelt Province, come month-end these miners make big withdrawals from local banks to go and spend with their families.
Accommodation is still short despite lots of construction taking place. And due to haphazard planning in the district, ugly structures have found space in the prime area of the business district. Add to that, structures have been built on telecommunication, water/sewer and under electricity lines.
With the district at the risk of being turned into one big slum, the ministry of Lands was forced to step in and revoke the land alienation urgency from the Solwezi Municipal Council. It was only recently restored.
And for those who still think Solwezi is a rural town in everything, with rentals cheaper than talk-time, they better think again. Rentals here are in some cases the same as Lusaka. You will be lucky to get an electrified shack at K500,000 per month.
"I've left my family in a comfortable three bed-roomed house on the Copperbelt Province, but here I am staying in a chicken run," confessed one miner.
All said and done, opportunities abound in Solwezi. Or just why else would all those expatriates on the mines leave their far-flung countries like Australia and Philippines to come and work in this district?

Original_Search_Title: Unsurpassed Eating Experience From Starters to Sweets
Headline: South Africa: Unsurpassed Eating Experience From Starters to Sweets
Body_Text:
FROM the creators of Andiamo in the Cape Quarter comes another fabulous eating experience, one which begins on the telephone.
Everybody always bangs on about food and service, but your first contact with a restaurant will often determine how your meal will turn out. For example, I am in no hurry to return to the place where the person on the other end of the line hung up on me while I was still mid-sentence with a question.
When I called Porcini, however, I got a friendly and helpful Graham who answered my questions with enthusiasm and assured me that although they don't take bookings for the courtyard, he would be there when I arrived to look after me.
True to his word, Graham had us seated at a table in the leafy courtyard, where we were attended to by Dennis, whose service was just as impeccable as his Italian.
A hot foccacia bread, along with bowls of fresh garlic, grated parmesan cheese and red chilli quickly materialised, along with olive oil and balsamic vinegar. A dangerous thing this, because the bread is completely irresistible but it can fill you up too much, preventing you from doing the rest of the meal justice.
Porcini employs the same delicious Mediterranean simplicity in its menu as Andiamo, with a careful selection of a few choice ingredients complementing each other in every dish. Antipasti include beef and tuna carpaccio; bruschetta with toppings of tomato and basil, roasted peppers or porcini mushrooms (or a combination of all three); Parma ham with seasonal fruit; and an antipasto misto platter with Parma ham, coppa, pancetta, mortadella, salami and marinated vegetables. There are also some delicious-sounding salads, some of which are available in individual or table-size portions.
We began our meal with calamaretti e zuccine, which is meltingly tender pan fried baby calamari tubes and tentacles on a bed of thinly sliced baby marrows with garlic and olive oil (R46.50) and porri gratinati - leeks with parmesan cheese and sage butter and gratinated in the pizza oven (R29.50).
The starters arrived at the table very quickly, so if you are intending to have a leisurely meal, it's best to advise your waiter of this. We asked for a little more time before our main courses were brought out.
There is a wide selection of wood-fired pizzas and pastas (penne, spaghetti, fettuccine). Sauces include anchovies, capers, olives and chilli tomato; the signature white wine, garlic, cream and porcini mushrooms; and of course, the famous clams, white wine and parsley. There is also a porcini risotto.
Standard pizzas mostly have two toppings, but extras are available. My partner ordered the carciofi, with fresh asparagus and artichokes (R52.50) with the addition of porcini mushrooms (R18.50) and managed to eat only half.
My main course came from the secondi section of the menu, which offers meat, poultry and seafood dishes such as agnello al forno (rack of lamb marinated in a light mustard, garlic and herb sauce, roasted in the pizza oven) and grigliata di mare (a selection of prawns, kingklip and linefish spiedini - a baby calamari skewer). Your waiter will advise you of the daily specials; on the night we were there one of those was tuna, "kissed" by the grill according to Dennis.
Clearly we both had to have velvety porcini mushrooms so I ordered filetto ai porcini - a butter-soft 300g fillet medallion with a creamy porcini sauce (R95.50). It was served with lightly seasoned al dente vegetables - courgettes, carrots, broccoli, cauliflower - and three huge roast potato wedges. The steak was grilled to perfection, just on the medium side of rare and had no need of a serrated knife.
Because of that wicked foccacia in the beginning, we had no space for dolce (sweets) although the tiramisu and panna cotta were tempting.
In addition to its wine list Porcini offers reds and whites by the carafe, either 250ml or 500ml, which is great if you want to mix and match with your food without ordering more expensive bottles. We had a 500ml sauvignon blanc for R35, and a bottle of Italian mineral water for R18.50.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandans Die of Meningitis in Juba
Headline: Ugandans Die of Meningitis in Juba
Body_Text:
Juba — MENINGITIS has killed three Ugandans in Juba.
Twalib Asibazuyo was found dead by his neighbours in the morning.
