Swaibu Aringa died on the way to Juba Teaching Hospital on February 25. Daawa Fatuma, too, died within 24 hours of getting to the hospital.
All the deceased came from West Nile. Asibazuyo was a dealer in general merchandise, Aringa a driver with a Chinese engineering company, while Daawa sold sweet potatoes from Uganda, according to relatives.
At the Juba Teaching Hospital, the cholera section is also handling meningitis patients. Patients have been registered daily beginning from late January, according to the hospital book. Health officials said this week that there are no vaccines yet. Aid workers fear that the worst is yet to come and the outbreak is just beginning.

Original_Search_Title: Many 'No Shows', On Valentine's Night
Headline: Zimbabwe: Many 'No Shows', On Valentine's Night
Body_Text:
AN amazing aspect of St Valentine's night at Meikles -- arguably the country's best hotel -- was an incredibly large number of "No Shows": people who had booked -- in many cases paid -- for a table, usually for two, then stayed away in droves.
I have mixed feelings about Bagatelle, re-opened especially for the event after years in mothballs (since tourists stayed away from Harare in droves.) It is filled with poignant, mainly very happy, memories and almost tangible ghosts of departed bons viveurs. It always was slightly claustrophobic, being a window-less internal room. The rich carved darkwood panelling and other decor is superb and air-conditioning helps make up for a lack of natural ventilation.
Sadly "they" never get the B/ThU setting right when the exclusive 5-Star+ fine dining restaurant temporarily re-opens. It was uncomfortably cool for my partner, hairdresser Barbara Georgiadis, with no shawl, stole or jacket to drape over deeply sun-tanned shoulders above a designer cocktail dress.
Maybe if some No Shows had shown, the ambient temperature would have been slightly more comfortable, but with thermostatic control, that shouldn't make much difference.
Lights were also too bright in what was designed as a personal, sophisticated romantic restaurant and on this quintessential night of highly commerialised kitsch "someone" might have turned the rheostat down to intensify the red candle-lit atmosphere.
Food was by and large memorably good, service superb, except one of the waiter's best friends should really whisper in his ear something about personal hygiene. Table setting and decorations belonged in a coffee table book of how definitely to do such things professionally.
Tony Vas coaxed smooth sweet serenades from his sexy saxophone for perhaps three hours and Barbs joined in, playing a Cuban instrument like the maracas, instead of her trademark tambourine, for the odd number.
I had sampled executive chef Chris Gonzo's special Valentine's Day creation, weeks earlier. Serving a few famously faddy fastidious foodies at a chef's table in a working kitchen is, however, much easier than cooking the same dishes for several hundred demanding clients on the actual event.
A tweely named Lover's Nest starter of a hearty salad of mango, orange segments, lichees, and garden greens, drizzled with olive oil and citrus juice was as zesty as I recalled it and Barbs praised it to Meikles' multi-storey rooftop, as she did the cappuccino cup of tomato, basil and leek soup, served with a chilled gin chaser: a combination I'd never order off a menu in a millennium, but one which worked remarkably well.
I anticipated glitches and hitches with the main course, but our only slight moan was that "new" potatoes (delicious at the rehearsal) proved one "old" potato boiled Portuguese-style. Unfortunately, as in many Lusitanian restaurants, it needed some more boiling!
The "marriage" of grilled beef fillet steak with a red wine and shallot reduction, topped with Hollandaise sauce, to pot roasted breast of chicken with coriander, cumin and coconut cream (their alliteration!) was blessed in heaven; Barbs especially liked a vegetable ravioli, rich in spinach.
Friends in La Fontaine, later, said some steaks weren't cooked as ordered, there was a lack of consistency in the beef's quality and some service left much to be desired. (These complaints can possibly be explained by the fact that many otherwise unemployed folks -- some arguably unemployable! -- are recruited for "busy" nights in the city's hotels. It always shows.)
Light chocolate mousse and snow-white egg pudding, with passion fruit coulis and lady-fingers was delightfully, decadently sweet. Barbara said "no" to excellent filter coffee and petite-fours.
When Vaz packed away his sax, we looked, briefly, in the Stewart Room, where many tables were apparently booked by No Shows. The Rusike Brothers played great, catchy music -- but far too loudly (even when near the door almost as far as you can get from the bandstand.)
Harare Drive trio were their usual swish middle-of-the road musical selves when we entered La Fontaine. Artist Mike Daines and vivacious wife, Tina, just leaving the dance floor, invited us to a large table of extroverts. Barbara was intrigued to hear Daines is possibly this country's best painter of nudes, (fairly raunchy ones, too); I had to
promise to take her to his next exhibition. We declined an invitation to a party at a Steppes Road home (at 12:30am!)
Al fresco garden lunch
Meikles also own Barbour's First Street department store. The day after Valentine's I had late lunch in the very pleasant al fresco garden of the 3rd Floor Terrace Restaurant. There was hardly a customer indoors or out at a place which used to often see folk queuing for a table a few years ago.
In a column, last week, I thought fish and chips at Macadams were too dear at $20 000 for a quick workday lunch. I still believe that but, at Barbours, paid $21 500 for fish and chips (albeit excellent, tasty, fresh, firm whole trout) with piquant tartar sauce, a good crisp chips, young green beans and edible carrots.
I did so mainly because I was very hungry and professional pressures indicated a missed supper; also because the $14 000 spaghetti Bolognaise was "off" (it nearly always is, so why not take it "off" the menu?) I didn't fancy a slab of grilled dead mombe or pig (T-bone, rump, or pork chops $18 500; pork spare ribs $19 500.)
Soup of the day "home made cream of mushroom" was straight from a Royco or Knorr packet, meaning $4 000 a throw was unjustified.
It was my fault I didn't don glasses when confidently ordering "plum pudding with ice-cream" assuming it would be a lovely old-fashioned steamed, rib-clinging suet pud. It actually said "plum jam" pudding and the chef was tardy with jam: hardly a smear in fact in a cold, stodgy Swiss roll-type confection.
Enjoyed the fish; starters and sweet get a big thumbs down: soup, trout, pudding and a four cup pot of tea $32 500.
* Barbour's Restaurants open store hours Monday to Saturday.

Original_Search_Title: Good Food, Good Music
Headline: Zimbabwe: Good Food, Good Music
Body_Text:
Harare — SOPRANOS in Avondale, Harare, is about food and music. Of course, there are no live bands or acapella groups that perform at the well-ventilated and spacious restaurant, but diners are treated to jazz music that fills both the inside seating and veranda where, it appears, the majority of diners prefer to sit.
Just like in music where a beat is a product of many different influences, food at Sopranos is a bit of the east, the west, the south and the north.
For one thing, Sopranos is very rich in coffees and teas. There is a long list of what can be chosen from. The list ranges from appuccino to choccaccino for coffees; and from rapuccino to masala if it's tea.
To cater for a wide range of customers, Sopranos has on the list slimming and therapeutic teas besides the specially-made house brands such as the Sopranos smoothie, chocolatte Brazilian and Swiss chococcino.
Of course, one has to part with anything between $2 100 and $4 990 to enjoy these.
To go along with coffee or tea are burgers, scones and spring rolls, which come in different shapes and sizes. There is the freshly baked scone that is served with jam and butter. There is also the samoosa trio, which is a cocktail of three samoosas served with a touch of mint chutney, beef, chicken or vegetables.
The spring rolls are either served with vegetables or chicken and there is a Sopranos special recipe served with a sweet or sour sauce.
Other options are sandwiches in the form of toasted cheese melt which are toasted cheddar, brie and gouda, chicken mayo or salami and chicken.
Burger-wise, Sopranos offers the Godfather and its special home-made burger, which is a beef patty served on a fresh toasted roll, as well as a variety of others such as cheese, chilli, chicken and spicy chicken burgers.
Of interest could be the bean burgers for a light-hearted meal. There is a special bean patty served on a toasted roll with fried onion and salsa.
To be tried too are the spicy bean burger, cheesy bean burger, vegan bean burger and vegan spicy burger. There is also the fiesta vegetable burger, which is a crunchy vegetable in a creamy sweet corn fix, served with a creamy cheese and cucumber sauce.
After the Hit segment is all about beef, chicken and fish. There is the Soprano fillet and rump, a succulent potion of grilled rump or fillet served with herb butter, onions, minted potatoes and chips.
Some of the most filling dishes are the chicken escalopes, crumbed chicken served on a bed of pasta with pomodoro and a side salad; and Pasta alla Brodo that comprises strips of spicy beef, peppers and tomato, served with a creamy pomodoro sauce with a mint of lemon and mustard.
From the wide variety of salads available, the luscious chicken noodle salad that consists of chicken nuggets tossed on a bed of lettuce with green peppers, cucumber, onion and crispy noodles splashed with an oriental dressing is worth trying.
While the ordinary man will wince when he checks the prices of up to $23 900 for a plate of salad, Sopranos fills up every lunch hour. This is proof that it's an upmarket restaurant.

Original_Search_Title: Cooking Up a Storm in the Fairest Cape
Headline: South Africa: Cooking Up a Storm in the Fairest Cape
Body_Text:
Harare — The Cape Peninsula must currently be the hottest tourist destination in the world -- believe me; I've just spent ten days there.
Whether you're into sport, diving with sharks, wine tasting, or simply gazing at the sheer beauty of mountains, sea and surf, Cape Town has it all. But as a dyed-in-the-wool foodie, I'm attracted by the proliferation of young, highly qualified and passionate chefs who are currently cooking up a storm at the fairest Cape of all.
Forget, for a moment, kitchen magicians such as Marco Pierre White, Raymond Blanc and Ferran Adria, names that roll so smoothly off the tongues of globally travelled food fanatics. Take note instead of Bruce Robertson, Steve Edwards and Craig Paterson. These young chefs with macho, thrusting, one-syllable first names are establishing enviable reputations for their cuisine and making waves in the Atlantic Ocean. Bruce Robertson, who honed his skills at the Cape Grace Hotel, is already a celebrity at his new Waterfront restaurant, The Showroom. Craig Paterson, a rising star, is executive chef at the Cape Grace Hotel, where he's assisted by sous-chef Steve Edwards. (I'll have more to say about Steve next week.)
Home boy Craig Paterson, who grew up on the Cape Flats and went to chef school at Granger Bay Hotel School in Mouille Point, is well known for his creative cookery and love of local and traditional flavours. So when George and I spent two nights recently at the Cape Grace, one of the highlights of our stay was dinner at onewaterfront.restaurant. I ate sparingly all day, and swam several laps in the salt-water swimming pool, to be sure to be good and hungry when evening came. I even cancelled a pre-prandial opportunity to taste six selected malt whiskies at Bascule Whisky bar overlooking the marina, lest my appetite be blunted.
Craig's exciting and innovative dinner menu offers a wide range of delicious options. Like a consummate artist, he combines a classical training with a flair for the spicy and exotic. Consider Tempura Fried Prawn Tails, a starter which offers "masala scented prawn tails on avocado and crème fraiche tian with squid ink vinaigrette", or Smoked Duck and Foie Gras Pizza with "caramelised berries and mesculun salad". After deliberating the choices, George was delighted with his choice of Honey and Soy Marinated Quail, with walnut and pear salad. The delicious green sauce was a reduction of watercress.
My starter, onewaterfront. Summer Salad, looked and tasted enchanting. Bright green al dente asparagus, fresh orange coloured melon, a conoisseur's smoked goats cheese, tender rocket salad and an exquisite balsamic dressing were skillfully combined and presented. As I munched thoughtfully, pictures of rustic peasants tending their flocks or preparing cheese from recipes handed down through generations, and visions of hillside herb gardens and orchards floated in and out of my mind.
A fresh tasting sorbet followed, after which our helpful waiter Taras ran through the main course options. Should I go for fish of the day, with sauteed pakchoi, vine tomatoes, buttered new potatoes and tomato vinaigrette? Or Confit of Duck Bobotie with rocket salad and roti? Here was an opportunity to experience Craig Paterson's way with Malay flavours, and skill in preparing traditional dishes, particularly as I probably wouldn't have time to visit a Malay restaurant in the Bo-Kaap district.
In the event I went for Rosemary Crusted Karroo Lamb Rack, with 'soet patat', artichoke salad and red wine jus. I find it difficult to resist lamb in any form, and the same goes for sweet potatoes and artichokes. The dish was superbly cooked and presented, with the richly roasted lamb tender and pink inside.
George and I were in over our heads with the Warm Chocolate Fondant Dessert, served with white chocolate ice cream and milk chocolate sauce. It was almost "death by chocolate", and an extra serving of vanilla ice cream provided the coup de grace. Other options were Martini of Fresh Fruit, topped with champagne sorbet and Vanilla Panacotta with basil syrup and fresh seasonal fruit.
When Craig Paterson takes a break from the sophisticated kitchen of fine dining, he says he enjoys "relaxing around a good South African braai. My favourite braai would have to be pork, and that's any pork. Mmmmh!"
For a man on the cutting edge of the restaurant business, it's hardly surprising that Craig's favourite kitchen gadget is a mandolin. But he stresses the importance of team effort and admits that he values his kitchen team above any other asset. "If I could fit my team in my knife box", he said, "I would take them with me for the rest of my career."
Only time will tell whether Craig Paterson's career will take him abroad, or whether he'll continue to be a trend-setter in Cape Town, one of the world's most popular gourmet destinations.

Original_Search_Title: Peace And Boom in South
Headline: Sudan: Peace And Boom in South
Body_Text:
Two years after a deal was signed to end the long conflict in southern Sudan, bulldozers and diggers are hard at work transforming Juba, a town of mud huts and dirt roads, into what could soon be a 21st Century international capital.
Long-time southern rebel leader John Garang told a signing ceremony in Nairobi on January 9, 2005 that true peace would only come with economic development.
He died in a helicopter crash later that year but in the roads around his steel framed mausoleum in Juba, Mr Garang's dream of lifting Southern Sudan out of poverty is still alive.
A plume of dust surrounds the heavy duty machinery on the track that leads from the airport to parliament.
Six months ago the whole of southern Sudan had just 10km of tarmac road.
Electricity pylons are being erected and new water and sewage systems are planned for later in the year.
Oil money
"We can be proud of what we've achieved so far," says South Sudan's Vice-President Riek Machar.
"Before we could do anything we had to set up a government, a civil service and to establish accounting and financial procedures."
Though it started work without basic infrastructure, the Southern Sudanese government does have money.
As part of the peace agreement with the north, the south gets 50% of Sudan's oil revenue.
At the moment that means about $800m a year.
But not much of that cash has reached Bulluk-A primary school.
Ragged white tents in the dusty playground now serve as classrooms to many of its over 2,000 pupils.
High prices
Encouraged by the peace deal, hundreds of thousands of southerners have returned to their homes but the education system simply can't cope with the influx of children.
"These children instead of sitting on the floor bring stones so they don't get their dresses dirty," says teacher Mary Sufu with an exasperated smile.
As if learning with no desks, tables or textbooks wasn't hard enough, Ms Sufu's class of 180 children are also grappling with a syllabus that is being changed from Arabic to English.
Just down the road from Bulluk-A is Customs market where Ms Sufu shops .
During the civil war, government-controlled Juba was surrounded on all sides by rebel forces and food had to be flown in from Khartoum.
The food at Customs market was expensive and most of it in tins.
The opening of roads to Uganda and Kenya has changed everything.
"There's plenty of food now in the market and it's cheap," Ms Sufu says as she shoves sweet potatoes and pineapples into her plastic bag.
"I feed my children now with food that they didn't even know about before."
It's not just fruit and vegetables that are coming across the border in trucks.
Independence
Ugandans and Congolese have established a presence in a market once dominated by northern traders.
Motorbikes, mobile phones, cheap electronics and beer are all selling well to eager Juba residents.
"The prices are high, we're getting a lot of profit," says one bare-chested trader as he plays a board game with his fellow Ugandans.
"A generator here costs 4.9 million shillings ($2,500), we buy it in Uganda for 2.9 million($1,600)."
At the moment, the south governs itself but remains part of Sudan.
Under the terms of the peace agreement, a referendum on independence will be held in 2011.
If Juba's boom continues, that could encourage a vote for independence and the city completing its remarkable transformation.

Original_Search_Title: Chatting At Chatters And the Blue Banana
Headline: Zimbabwe: Chatting At Chatters And the Blue Banana
Body_Text:
AMAZING to see how busy Newlands shopping centre was the day many revellers parted with huge amounts of cash as crippling school fees.
Don't think I've seen the twin Baobab Grill/ Blue Banana restaurants so bustling since the night of the former's official opening; next door, Mama Mia's was even more packed.
A few curry fans were in the Sitar; Billy Fudpucker's was thinly patronised and Trax not much busier. Should really have crossed the road to check Libby's, for the full picture but anxious to eat, rather than snoop.
Heath and Lee Stewart own Baobab Grill. I got the usual cheery greeting and sat outside on a pleasant balmy rain-less night. My back was to Nick and Annette Kalamatas who were having supper alfresco at Mama Mia's, which they own and two tables away in BG/BB was Andrew Mama, proprietor of Amanzi, due to re-open the next day, after a long Christmas/New Year break.
Incidentally Heath said he'd never again close for such a long period, revealing he was inundated with complaints from discontented would be diners.
He insisted on serving deep-fried Haloumi cheese, which I'm fond of but rarely order as I'm sure it plays havoc with the cholesterol, but mainly as it instantly suppresses appetite for main course. This delightful dish was $2 900, deep-fried mushrooms $3 000; Zambezi Ritz or beef kebabs $2 500; buffalo wings or chicken giblets $2 300 and stuffed potato cakes $1 900. Punch-drunk with price rises I didn't realise how reasonable was the tariff, until eating at a rival establishment three days later.
Soups are $2 300 to $2 850 and salads (I recommend the Waldorf) $3 000 to $4 500.
What I like about the Baobab Grill/Blue Banana concept is you can either get stuck into solid, sensible Zimbabwe food like steaks, chops, burgers and ribs or hot, spicy Thai food which, candidly, I'm not mad about. However some of my guests prefer South-East Asian cuisine and they can have lemon grass and glass noodles to their hearts' content while I eat something more substantial, less sinus-draining!
One of the sad things about hyper-inflation-hit Zimbabwe is simple chicken dishes once taken for granted are now hugely expensive: often the dearest menu item. Apparently the case at Baobab Grill where chicken Kiev (too rich for me) was $9 000; the very attractively presented and delicious chicken tarragon I did have $8 000; yet 250g fillet or rump steaks were $7 500. I doubt that happens anywhere else in the world.
Tarragon chicken comprises plump white breasts cooked in a subtle sauce featuring the eponymous herb and served with young broccoli, the dreaded butternut, chips, fragrant long-grained white rice with vegetables folded in and spring onions. You would have to be ravenous to finish the generous portion.
Tempted by fish and chips at $7 500; vegetarian specialities are $5 000 and there's a children's menu priced $850 to $3 500. The complex is extremely child friendly with a playroom supervised by a qualified nurse.
From a short list of puddings (dearest $1 950 : apple crumble), I had a fine ice-cream and chocolate sauce, presented like a still-life masterpiece.
Baobab Grill/Blue Banana, Newlands shopping centre. Licensed, but no corkage if you take your own. Smoking/non-smoking. Reasonably disabled friendly; extremely child friendly. Indoor or outdoor tables. Closed Sunday. Tel 252269/252276.
Cresta Lodge re-visited
Last week in this column I roasted the Bird and Bottle at the New Ambassador for charging $30 000 for a very pedestrian three course table d'hote meal without potatoes: one untasted as I walked out in disgust.
For that same figure, at the rather tweely-named Chatters at Cresta Lodge, Msasa, I enjoyed steamy, fragrant chicken and sweetcorn soup, thickened with eggs, with great rolls and butter, a huge range of attractive colourful salads with cold meats, fish and wonderful Scotch eggs. Straightforward buffet stuff, you might think!
But Cresta group have brought in talented Pakistani executive chefs and certainly Mohammed Mukeem Soomro, who has worked almost everywhere in the world, but lately at the palatial Dubai Grand Hotel, is already making his mark.
The Chinese-type soup was his idea; there was also murgh malai hara: boneless chicken cubes cooked in dry nut paste with hot spices; shami kebabs (beef and lentil cutlets); paneer karahi: cottage cheese cooked in tomatoes and specially blended Indian spices and Chinese crispy fried drumsticks. They were all wonderful
There was also rice, sadza, roast potatoes and pasta, a wide range of freshly cooked vegetables, sauces, gravies and pickles and the most superb looking pinkly rare, still spurting blood, buttock of baby beef, being carved expertly. A few years ago I would have died for it but, sadly, the occasional bout of gout might turn more frequent if I risked it.
The place was packed, mainly with folk attending talkfests, plus the odd Msasa business man; all the cheesecake was finished when we (sales executive Rufaro Mushonga and myself) went to the pudding counter.
I had fresh fruit salad with jelly, cream and a dinky chocolate éclair. Rufaro's sweets featured superb looking vanilla slice, which I hadn't spotted and meant to return for. But I'd no room and no time for coffee.
Chatters is oddly designed: the smoking area lower than the non-smoking section, seemingly defying the law of physics! Comfortable tables are indoors, on an attractive stoep by a water feature, or in the garden. Reasonably disabled friendly.
Open for breakfast, lunch and supper daily, Friday is Africa Day when you can eat mopani worms (fried caterpillars) should you so wish!

Original_Search_Title: Enjoy Your Meals in the Woods
Headline: Uganda: Enjoy Your Meals in the Woods
Body_Text:
In the last two years, the National Theatre's Snack Centre has transformed from just a snack corner into a joint with most kinds of traditional foods.
The nook boasts of a menu that consists of steamed matooke prepared from banana fibre and they serve it with other foods like steamed rice, sweet potatoes and chicken luwombo (on order).
On the lunch menu is also pilao and katogo for breakfast.
To locate the place, you would have to move through to the end of the National Theatre corridor. The Snacks and Foods corner is grass thatched and semi-detached from the main National theatre building.
This makes the wooden house cool all day owing to its construction of wild grass and woodwork. It is basically a wooden haven.
There's also an extension of a mini-bar to the centre. Meals will take a few minutes depending on which foodstuffs you may have ordered for. However, you may not want to peep into the kitchen, which is quite in a messy state. Thus kept private to the reveller's eye.
On any day, you would find a group of people engaged in a business meeting. The place is also popular with actors who prefer to chill out during the week when they do their rehearsals.
Their toilets are fairly clean and also made in woodwork style.

Original_Search_Title: Where Good Food Abounds, Love is Not Far Off
Headline: Zimbabwe: Where Good Food Abounds, Love is Not Far Off
Body_Text:
Harare — It's love that makes the world go round, so if you've been trying to shed the post-Christmas flab by marking time on the treadmill or torturing yourself with stomach crunches, admit that enough's enough and get ready to celebrate St Valentine's Day on February 14.
By mid-January, the hot water and lemon juice with a slice of brown toast regime has lost its Spartan appeal, and I'm ready to worship at the temple of High Priest of Gastronomic Delights, Chef Chris Gonzo. With his band of dedicated acolytes, he has come up with a Valentine's menu to delight the heart of any lover and his partner. Welcoming a small group of devotees to the Chef's Table at Meikles Hotel this week, Chef Gonzo remarked that "Wherever there is love, you'll always find good food".
Since traditionally aphrodisiac foods such as raw oysters are not readily available in Zimbabwe, the Meikles team has concentrated on a sophisticated menu created from fresh, local produce, which promises to have a similar effect. The Lovers' Nest is a tantalising starter salad, combining slices of exotic and heady mango with segments of citrus, on a bed of lettuce and rocket. The citrus and olive oil dressing draws all the flavours together and the combination is delicious.
The Essence of Romance is up next, in the form of a tomato, basil and leek soup, served with chilled gin. This is the most exciting and flavoursome soup I've tasted in a while. The gin chaser is wickedly successful in terms of taste, flavour and contrast of texture and a great curtain raiser for the main course Valentine Feast, described as a marriage of grilled fillet and pot roast chicken breast. It's difficult to imagine two more unlikely bedfellows than a cow and a chicken, although they are great friends in a cartoon of the same name.
The grilled fillet in a red wine jus with hollandaise sauce was nigh on perfect, as were the fondant potatoes. What was most exciting was seeing this oh so elegant dish being assembled right in front of our eyes, complete with asparagus spear and minute sprigs of rosemary and thyme.
Cupid's Sweet Success, a dessert presented upon a heart-shaped fruit coulis traced with chocolate, was a romantic combination of chocolate mousse, meringue, strawberries and passion fruit. Chocolate truffles, chocolate-coated grapes, chocolate chip cookies and brandy snaps were the final offerings in this extravaganza for sweethearts.
Two well-chosen wines, a Chenin Blanc Vansha 2003 from Paarl, and Bacchus Speaks, a Flagstone Pinot Noir 2001, were delightful accompaniments to our meal. While the aphrodisiac properties of certain foods provide topic for debate, Jonathan, a colleague from another newspaper, assures me that our national dish of mazondos is the undisputed leader in this field. It would not be easy, however, to eat this dish by candle light, and it requires a lot more concentration to do justice to a plate of mazondos, than to eat a bowl of salad.
So whether you're in love, or planning to show someone just how loving you can be, take them to La Fontaine or the Stewart Room at Meikles Hotel and experience the cuisine and artistry for which Chef Gonzo and his team have become so well known. You might spare a thought for St Valentine, martyred for his love of Christianity, or for Michael Furey, a James Joyce character, who died for love after standing long hours in the snow, waiting to glimpse his girlfriend at her window.
February 14 is just around the corner, and if you heed Chef Gonzo's words, where good food abounds, love will not be far off.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, We Have No Potatoes!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Yes, We Have No Potatoes!
Body_Text:
WE HAD been without electricity at the office, thanks to a wildcat Zesa strike, for two hours when, faced with the impossibility of doing any meaningful work in the 21st century without power ...and bored stiff... lunch came tantalisingly to mind.
In particular I fancied -- craved even -- lovely crisp, fresh, crunchy salad.
Hard luck, mate!
Remember the days when you were lucky to get a table at the Bird and Bottle Restaurant at the (now NEW) Ambassador Hotel, if you hadn't booked three days before? My chums, textile magnate Darkie Jones and IANA editor Ken Mobbs were two of many captains of industry who had lunch tables permanently booked each weekday and entertained with lavish largesse.
There wasn't a single customer when I walked in at 1:17 to find the always attractive colourful buffet enjoyed on and off for the past third of a century no more! The large space it used to occupy was just gapingly, glaringly bare.
Thinking this something to do with the almost city-wide power outage (and outrage!), I asked -- naively it transpired -- "Where are the salads?"
"No, we don't do that any more," was the snappy answer from a waitress. "We now have a full set meal. Please sit down, I'll bring a menu."
I'd just noticed that "restaurant" was misspelt "restuaurant" engraved on a brass plaque in the now gleaming corridors of the "New" Ambassador Hotel. It didn't augur well for efficiency. Sorry to owners Rainbow Tourism Group I, for one, much preferred the "old" Ambassador, which had lots of character (and characters).The Bird and Bottle had among the best tables and finest cellars in the country. Management, waiters, chefs and sommeliers were par excellence.
Not a single toper drank in the now soulless, tiled open-plan cocktail bar outside the restaurant; the former Long Bar (in the past permanently patronised by picaresque punters) is now a supermarket.
An A4 menu computer-printed but unpriced "menu" was brought. No argument with a no-choice starter of pickled crocodile, but I could imagine many people shuddering at the thought, roundly rejecting rapacious riverine reptile. After all, they might well ask, do I really want to eat a creature whose grandfather may have grazed my grandmother?
Soup was cream of mushroom. Difficult to botch, although there's a growing tendency to leave "potage du jour" to the culinary skills of Messrs Maggi or Knorr. Hardly fair when charging $4 000 or $5 000 a bowl for something costing $1 200 a dehydrated packet -- retail -- which feeds four!
Main course was crumbed chicken (good!), or beef olives (siss!) or gammon steak (yippee!!) but what's this? Served with vegetables and rice or sadza?
"Excuse me, miss. If I have gammon, can I have chips with it...or baked potato...or mash...or whole boiled might be nice."
"No. We don't do potatoes any more. They're too expensive!"
You could have knocked me down with a single soiled serviette.
"Too dear? How much is this set lunch, anyway?"
"Thirty-thousand!"
"Whaaaat?" I gasped disbelievingly. "Five months ago that was $30 million...and you can't throw in a few chips?"
"No sir, sorry!" (Bet you are, I thought.)
There were some ordinary sounding sweets and coffee bunged in for thirty-grand. (Preposterous sum for lunch, when the average person in this country doesn't clear five times that a month.) Correction, as we apparently now admit to more than 80% unemployment, the average Zimbo must earn slightly above zip!
I made my excuses and left the still client-less restaurant, walking through the cheerless empty "cocktail" bar and into bright daylight. The law of diminishing returns personified, I thought; mismanagement made manifest.
Walking into town, no traffic lights worked; the pedestrian's life was in his hands and at the mercy of lunatic motorists who drive as if Second Street is Kyalami. ATMs were all "down". I wasn't flush, having paid $14 400 ($14,4 million of the old loot) for a rollerball refill for a stainless steel Parker pen which originally cost $1 100 of the old money. And that wasn't cheap!
At Batanai Mall neither escalator was operating. Clambering robot-like up steel steps which should have been moving smoothly, I found Upper Crust in almost total stygian darkness, slightly relieved by odd tiny flickering candles at odd occupied tables in depressing gloom; atmosphere muggy: no air-conditioning worked.
"What have you got, under these trying circumstances?" I asked a waiter.
"Everything, sir."
"Really? Amazing! Well done, you chaps! Tea, please, while I scan the menu."
Two minutes later he returned: "Sorry. No tea, the kitchen can't boil water." (Hmmm...precisely what I'd thought.)
A cola, pre-poured into an ugly, clumsy, heavy, handle-less beaker cost $1 200; lasagna, a little stodgy and definitely on last legs as "hot food", with a mere fringe of salad craved desperately earlier, was $4 000 and a rather good, generous slab of piquant lemon meringue pie was $1 000. Bottom line: $6 200.
Had I tried Bird and Bottle's table d'hote, along with a refreshingly chilled article of a moderately intoxicating nature (or three) and tipped even tardily, I'd have been lucky to get away with $40 000 ($40 million of the old money) For a lunch, without even a spud? Someone's joking, but, if so, it's very sick humour.
The sad thing is, in a few months such a sum will probably sound bargain basement, so price punch-drunk have we become by almost daily hikes!

Original_Search_Title: TZ Returnees Receive Food Aid
Headline: Rwanda: TZ Returnees Receive Food Aid
Body_Text:
Nyagatare — A delegation from Mukama Sector on January 1, donated food worth over three million Francs to Tanzanian returnees in Matimba Sector, Nyagatare District.
Among the items donated were beans, bananas, maize flour, sweet potatoes, washing soap and scholastic materials.
"As local leaders, we feel sorry for the returnees since all their life was devastated to almost nothing. We prepared to visit them on January 1 for comfort by showing them love and the sector collected food donation from all cells which comprise Mukama sector worth Frw3, 142, 400," said Ernest Rusenyanteko, the sector chief accountant who led a delegation of over forty local leaders and residents
Rusenyanteko added that the Tanzanian returnees are citizens who need support to alleviate them from the current miserable conditions they are facing.
Meanwhile, reports reaching The New Times indicate that different sites of Tanzanian returnees face a possible outbreak of chorea due to the lack of toilets.
Sources also said the situation has been compounded by the shortage of clean water in the camps.

Original_Search_Title: Small Business Council Meets Palapye Entrepreneurs
Headline: Botswana: Small Business Council Meets Palapye Entrepreneurs
Body_Text:
The Botswana Small Business Council (SBC) board on Tuesday met members of the business community here in a bid to hear their concerns regarding the impediment that hinder their businesses.
The three-year old SBC brought along experts in tax, labour relations, and in company formation and company registration. The Chairman of the SBC Palalani Moitlhobogi said the board does not always have answers to questions asked by businesses; that is why they brought experts with them.
The SBC was set up in 2004 as a high level advisory body chaired by a member of the private sector within the Small, Medium and Micro Enterprises (SMME) community.
It draws its membership from government ministries, parastatal bodies and institutions inclined to small businesses. The board brought along members of Botswana Unified Revenues Services (BURS), the Department of Labour and representatives of Ministry of Trade and Industry, including Registrar of Companies.
The members of Palapye business community were advised to file their tax returns, whether they were making a profit or not.
They were advised that as employers, they had to register for Income Tax so that employees getting over P2,500 monthly rendered their Pay As You Earn (PAYE), which must be paid to BURS every 15th day of the following month.
"If you do not bring returns to BURS, you are breaking the law. Most of the companies have not registered; they only register when they go for a tender and that is when they break the law", said a representative of BURS. "As an employer, you need to have a tax table to see who is taxed and how much. If you do not send it (tax returns) to BURS, you will be charged interest."
The entrepreneurs were also advised that they could register for Value Added Tax (VAT) even if their annual turnover is less than the P250, 000 annual threshold.
They were also advised on some goods that are zero-rated and those that are exempted from tax. The BURS representative noted that education and health are exempted from tax, saying that if education was taxed, parents would be paying P330 instead of P300.
She also advised that zero-rated goods include maize and maize meal, sorghum, millet, petrol and pesticides. The BURS representative also said as taxpayers, business owners have the right to object and appeal an assessment; they could even approach adjudicators.

Original_Search_Title: Eagle Lager Lifts Delta's Fortunes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Eagle Lager Lifts Delta's Fortunes
Body_Text:
Harare — DELTA Corporation significantly pushed Eagle lager beer volumes to survive the controversial price blitz launched by the government in June and also pushed exports of malt and glass to earn foreign currency to sustain the brewer's operations.
Delta Corporation chief executive officer Joe Mutizwa told a media and analysts briefing last week that the country's leading brewer pulled through the harsh trading environment by launching a survival mode called "Project Thrive."
"There was a significant push on Eagle lager volumes which don't rely on barley malt but sorghum to earn foreign currency from malt exports," said Mutizwa. "I do hear in the market that you are giving us a lot of Jatropha (Eagle). This was a survival strategy and it has worked. We will push sorghum based beer locally as a survival strategy," added Mutizwa.
Eagle lager volumes, which declined 37.5 percent from May to June were up 40 percent from July to September while barley based lagers' volumes were up 16 percent in the second quarter of the reporting period from a decline of 4.9 percent. This raised the total lager volumes to September by 4.1 percent.
On the contrary sorghum beer volumes crashed 16.5 percent in the first quarter and 3.4 percent in the second quarter and dragging down total volumes to September by 9.8 percent.
The Delta boss said the interim trading period to September was characterised by two different quarters in which price controls hurt the company's earnings.
Mutizwa said the six months to September was characterised by two different quarters in which during the first quarter inflation was taking off dramatically with prices and costs rising sharply and frequently as replacement cost pricing models became the norm. During this first quarter aggregate demand collapsed as price escalations ran ahead of consumers' incomes. During this trading period Delta said volumes were coming off threatening revenues and cash flows.
The top brewer said the second quarter from July to September, in which prices were frozen saw excessive consumer demand and speculative hoarding which resulted in severe product shortages in the market.
However, the brewer said it survived through pushing volumes rather than margins. Delta Mutizwa said also significantly curtailed the distribution and supply chain costs through cutting off external freight hire by using rail transport at a fraction of the cost of external
Mutizwa now believes a deal reached between Delta and ZESA Holdings to pay for electricity imports in foreign currency will guarantee uninterrupted power supplies at Headend, its glass manufacturing business where severe power disruptions curtailed meaningful production for most of second quarter
"We have been paying ZESA bills in foreign currency for four weeks now," Mutizwa said.
Nonetheless Delta said electricity supply is still very unstable particularly at its rural sorghum breweries
Mutizwa also disclosed that a shortage of carbon dioxide supplies, which last year dipped by 20 percent to the entire Southern African market had stabilised.
But the Delta chief warned that existing supply gaps in the market opens space for competition.
"It (imports) is not good for us. That is a threat to Delta," Mutizwa said.
Zimbabwean retails shops and hotels have over the past few months been stocking various brands of imported beverages to cover the gap created by a government blitz on manufacturers and retailers, which however has emptied most supermarket shelves.
Delta said the cost containment measures implemented in the second quarter are beginning to bear fruit but warned that they will could be
diluted by the expected sharp rise in inflation in the period ahead.
Delta reported a 13 276 percent rise in attributable profit to $1.3 trillion and a cash on hand of $242 billion which will channeled towards capital expenditure and barley purchases.

Original_Search_Title: My Husband Lived a Double Life And Left Me With HIV
Headline: Uganda: My Husband Lived a Double Life And Left Me With HIV
Body_Text:
Kampala — HER footsteps sound like quick slaps as she walks down to her mud-and-wattle house. She is wearing sandals. Her house is only lit by the sun rays streaming in through the door. Inside the house, Sharon Nyirahabimana aka Kakikuru, picks a comb and brushes her hair. She appears keen to look cheerful, if only for a while.
Kakikuru's dream of a better life was shattered way back in her teen years when her mother, Kampaire, was beaten into a coma by a gang of thugs. On the fateful night, Kampaire had been drinking at a pub in Rwakaraaba, Kabale district.
On her way back home, thugs pounced on her, hit her with metals and left her unconscious. "A neighbour picked her up and took her for treatment. She was later transferred to Kisizi Hospital in Rukungiri district," Kakikuru, 39, narrates.
Being the only child, with no clue on who her father was, Kakikuru had no option but to drop out of school and nurse her mother until she died.
"I was about 15 years, in P.6. I started smoking to keep the trauma off. I needed someone to turn to. I needed food, clothes and a shoulder to lean on. Marriage seemed her only option."
Yet now, as Kakikuru ponders, marriage seems only a 'pleasant destruction', for things quickly turned sour. The first man he met, Kashaki, was a boda boda cyclist in Kabale town. There was not enough income to support them.
But Kakikuru was happy she had someone. She rejoiced even more at the birth of her daughter, Mahoro, and a few years later, her son Alex.
"But this meant two more mouths to feed! I borrowed money and started a shoe-vending business. My husband moved to Mbarara to get a better job."
For about three years, the couple struggled to make ends meet. "He would come home occasionally, bring some money and return to work." However, the joy vanished like passing wind. One morning, Kakikuru demanded to know where her husband stayed while in Mbarara.
"The pressure gradually pushed our marriage to the edge. He kept promising to show me the place, but he never did. We ended up shouting at each other until he finally threw the bombshell - he had another wife, another family.
Soon, he came home less frequently and finally disappeared. "Then one day, a friend called to tell me my husband was critically ill. Since the woman he had talked about had abandoned him, I went and I brought him back home. He died a few months later in 2005," she pauses as she tries to hold back tears.
The news that her husband had AIDS spread through the village like a bushfire. But Kakikuru was yet to believe it.
"I had always convinced myself that he was safe. So when he told me he had another woman, it was too late because we had lived as husband and wife for long and even had children. The thought of protecting myself had never crossed my mind."
A few months later, Kakikuru fell sick and eventually tested HIV-positive. "I was treated for malaria, typhoid, but the fever kept on recurring. I lost weight, vomited, and developed rash all over my body. HIV was the last thing I could think of until I took the test. After living in denial for all this time, here it was..." she recollects, a tear escaping her eye.
In September 2006, a friend advised her to get anti-retroviral treatment. "She taught me how to care about myself. I had lost hope. I knew death was on my doorstep, but she told me many people were living with HIV, and still looking healthy and caring for their families. I went to Rugarama Hospital where I was asked to pay sh15,000 for check up, and sh20,000 for a CD4 count. I could not raise it; so I went to Kabale Referral Hospital where I got free medication. I have gained weight," she says, her face brightening up.
Her only joy comes from knowing that her children are HIV-negative. Kakikuru advises young girls to be very careful. "You cannot tell one's HIV status by merely looking at them. My husband looked healthy and full of life. I loved and trusted him. I was faithful but he infected me with HIV," she says.
Her major worry now is dying before her one-and-a-half-year-old adopted son, Solomon, grows up. Solomon's mother, died shortly after he was born and Kakikuru "I found some women feeding him on water. I took him over. His father pledged to buy him milk everyday but he disappeared, probably thinking the boy would die soon.
I have raised Solomon on sorghum porridge and he is now 10kg!" All she does for a living is hair-braiding for as little as sh5,000. Her two biological children do not have school fees, but she volunteered to raise Solomon!

Original_Search_Title: Delta Revenue Grows 16 173pc
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Revenue Grows 16 173pc
Body_Text:
Harare — Beverage manufacturer Delta reported inflation beating 16 173 percent growth in revenue in the six months to September 30 although margins had come under pressure as a result of power outages and price distortions.
Turnover was $6,9 trillion from $42,2 billion and operating profit was $2,2 trillion, 15 564 percent up on last year's figure of $14,45 billion. The figure would have been $1,6 trillion if $598,7 billion from the biological asset revaluations from subsidiary Ariston were ignored.
Operating margins were 39,96 percent from 42,7 percent in the comparable period in 2006 as a result of the June 18 price freeze. Chief executive Mr Joe Mtizwa told an analysts briefing this week that Delta continued to "aggressively" lobby for price adjustments and State support for forex and fuel. Megapak received US$1,1 million from the RBZ enabling it to operate at full capacity, and the group had got some forex for concentrates. However, Megapak volumes had fallen 25 percent during the review period and Headend was down 52,6 percent, a 75,4 percent decline in the second quarter as "severe power outages curtailed meaningful production". The group was now paying Zesa in hard currency. In addition, they had procured 150 000 litres of diesel from Noczim in October out of a monthly requirement of 800 000 litres. Total beverage volumes in the period fell 6,6 percent with a 0,8 percent gain in the second quarter unable to offset the 14,1 percent loss in first quarter. Sorghum beer volumes were "managed lower" in the first quarter through pricing, as there were worries over whether there would be enough maize.
Volumes dropped 3,4 percent in the second quarter leading to a 9,8 percent drop in the half-year on last year. Sparkling beverages were up 1,9 percent, after gaining 5,4 percent in the second quarter. Mr Mtizwa said the volume recovery had been assisted by support from Coca-Cola. Malt exports grew 16,2 percent, as the group tried to augment its forex requirements. In the period barley malt beers were up 4,7 percent on the back of a 16 percent volume increase in Q2. Eagle volumes were level on last year after falling in Q1 only to recover in Q2, the group said adding that the beer made up 18 percent of total beer volumes against 8 percent last year.
Earnings per share were $1 302 from $9,96 last year, an increase of 12 978 percent but finance director Rob Maunsell told the analysts' briefing that excluding biological assets and container revaluations earnings per share were $1 141 up 15 232 percent on $7,44 last year Mr Mtizwa said that to match Delta's FCA balances the group had reduced output of products requiring forex inputs so as to "escape the tyranny of the parallel market".
Mr Mtizwa said this resulted in the reduction of sparkling beverages and sorghum beer but the group pushed malt and glass exports to support the rest of the business. The group said that they had used an "aggressive pricing" model to maintain margins and moderate forex requirements.
Delta declared a dividend of $250.

Original_Search_Title: Man Sentenced to Death for Murder
Headline: Botswana: Man Sentenced to Death for Murder
Body_Text:
Francistown — Justice Thomas Masuku has sentenced a man to death for a 2004 murder at Samatsina village in the North West District.
The judge said he found no extenuating circumstances in the case of 31-year-old Shakuhungira Loeto Baitsholedi who was mature at the time of the offence. The convict was charged with the murder of Keamogetse Thapiso with one Gaolelekwe Kawire who was however discharged before the trial concluded. Thapiso was found dead by his live-in lover Meleko Chapi on April 24, 2004 in the compound of his employer Josephine Burns.
It is strongly believed that he died from shock and haemorrhage after he was shot with a gun. Forensic pathologist, Dr Varapraisad Patnaik testified that the deceased was shot at close range. The bullet was from a gun owned by a prosecution witness Botsholelwang Sarega. Baitsholedi borrowed the gun saying that he needed protection because he was going to look for his lost donkeys in an area infested by dangerous wild animals. Instead of returning the gun the same day as agreed, he went to a funeral in Shakawe in the evening with the weapon. Later the gun was found by Serega's wife in a granary used to store sorghum with two bullets missing. Sarega testified that he met Baitsholedi and asked him about the bullets but he did not respond. Sarega was arrested a day after attending the funeral of Thapiso.
Justice Masuku pointed out that the facts suggested very strongly that the accused is the one who may have committed the dastardly act. "What in my view is consistent with that line of reasoning is that the accused could not account for the ammunition. His failure to account was, I find, not as a result of him not knowing what may have happened to it. He knew what happened to it... This was not contradicted in the cross examination... This conclusion heightens the reasonable inference that it is the accused who utilised the round of ammunition in question by shooting the deceased, although there is no motive suggested."
Masuku said the absence of motive cannot be considered alone to be the basis for excusing an accused where the facts pointed inexorably to him being the murderer. He said that the suggestion that somebody else could have taken and used the firearm is not an inference as there are no objective facts supporting it. He said the claim amounted to nothing but conjecture or speculation.
"In view of the foregoing, it is my opinion that the proved facts inexorably led to the one and the only inference in this matter, viz that the accused is the one who killed the deceased by shooting him with the Gauge Russian shotgun. He is accordingly found guilty of murder contrary to the provisions of Section 202 of the Penal Code," he pronounced.

Original_Search_Title: Legalise Shebeens - Study
Headline: Botswana: Legalise Shebeens - Study
Body_Text:
Gaborone — A study commissioned by the Department of Trade and Consumer Affairs (DTCA) has recommended the licensing of shebeens. The acting director of the department John Matsheng says they will hold a stakeholders meeting at the end of this month to discuss the report.
The study, which was conducted by Psychologists Botswana, a local consultancy company, also looked at the existence of shebeens in the country and alcohol consumption patterns in them. The findings of the study indicate a need to regulate the industry. Regulation should pay attention to issues of hygiene, provision of separate toilets for male and female patrons is recommended.
The study says there should be practice standards for all home-made brews, especially khadi, in terms of hygiene. The selling of food at shebeens (which is typically maize with serobe, mogodu, tlhogo and ditlhako) should conform certain standards. A wash-basin for patrons and for those serving them to wash their hands in keeping good standards. The consultants further recommends control of litter and noise. As for security and safety, it was noted that if the operators know that they are operating legally, they would be more inclined to call the police for assistance when necessary.
The study calls for the protection of family members staying in a shebeen. "Not all family members should be exposed to the shebeen environment, and those who are asked (to help) with serving should wear identifying aprons," it says.
Family members living on a shebeen plot are exposed to several types of undesirable behaviour, especially loud noise from patrons, public urination and violence. It further notes that family members share a number of items with patrons especially the toilet, which is unhygienic. The level of cleanliness as well as alcohol abuse at the shebeens are areas of concern for neighbours who are concerned about the negative behaviour their children seem to develop as a result of exposure to shebeens.
The study notes that the mushrooming of shebeens in residential areas is not desirable but that they cannot be removed successfully. The findings recommend that shebeens should not be allowed to operate under trees and that the actual "shebeen" should be in a separate and distinct part of the yard/house. The consultants say the issue of whether a shebeen can sell alcohol and simultaneously operate as a tuck shop or phone shop needs to be addressed. The study says there should be guidelines on how laws and policies addressing under-age drinking patterns could be enforced. The regulations should also address the enforcement of laws against the sale and distribution of dagga at shebeens.
The study also found that some of the shebeens are gang-related. There should be public education and adherence to conduct that does not condone any illegal activity on the premises. There is a need to set standards for addressing the variability of the alcoholic potency of home-made brews. Before regulation is in place, operators should be consulted on the correct measurements for their home-made brews, as serving alcohol in buckets is not desirable.
Adherence to responsible drinking practices and education/monitoring by the operators is important. The findings of the study note that any form of regulation will require some level of consultation with the shebeen industry and that this will be challenging as the industry has no formal organisational structure. The consultancy observed that most of the shebeens provide the only financial support for operators and their dependants. "In the event of this critical source of livelihood being removed from them as they cannot comply with regulations, they will be facing a poverty situation," it observes.
The study found that shebeen operators are predominantly unmarried middle-aged mothers. The operators use family members to help in the running of the business, and it is very rare that they hire any employees formally. Regarding income, it says shebeens provide the operators with an average monthly amount of P959.68, which they use for the living expenses of their families and themselves. Commercially brewed sorghum beer (chibuku) provides the lowest returns for the operators, while home-made brews provide better returns. The report says children in shebeens were exposed to the shebeen environment through living with their shebeen operating mothers as they were growing up. It went on to point out that usually the operators' parents drank alcohol. Most of the operators had a family member who owned a shebeen in the past.
The start-up capital for opening and expanding the business is obtained from family members. Lack of jobs, un-employability and the need to earn a living are the major factors which drive operators to run shebeens, but most of them do not drink alcohol.
According to the study, patrons of shebeens tend to come from backgrounds where there is a history of alcohol usage. The report notes that shebeens are so entrenched in Botswana society that it is be very difficult to imagine how their present functions could be replaced by other structures or interventions.

Original_Search_Title: Testimony of Fowsia Abdulkadir, Ogaden Human Rights Committee
Headline: Ethiopia: Testimony of Fowsia Abdulkadir, Ogaden Human Rights Committee
Body_Text:
Washington, D.C. — The following is the testimony of Fowsia Abdulkadir, chair of the Ogaden Human Rights Committee in Canada before the  United States Congress House Committee on Foreign Affairs Subcommittee on African and Global Health hearing on “Ethiopia and the State of Democracy: Effects on Human Rights and Humanitarian Conditions in the Ogaden and Somalia” on October 2, 2007.
Chairman Payne, ranking member Smith and the distinguished members of this committee – Thank you for holding this very important hearing that puts a spotlight on Ethiopia and the documented human rights abuses in the Ogaden and elsewhere in Ethiopia.   Mr. Chairman I am honored to be given the opportunity to come and speak today on a subject that I hold dear, the plight of the people of Ogaden, also known as the Somali region in Ethiopia.
Mr. Chairman, at this time I would like to ask that my full testimony be submitted into the record, consisting of the paper I am reading and the 2007 annual report of Ogaden Human Rights Committee, released on August 8th, 2007.
I represent a human rights advocacy organization. The Ogaden Human Rights Committee, (OHRC) is an independent, voluntary, non-profit community-based human rights advocacy organization. OHRC was founded on June 13th, 1995, in Godey, Ogaden, to monitor and promote the observance of internationally accepted human rights standards in the Ogaden. The OHRC researches, documents and reports human rights violations in the Ogaden.
The Ogaden Human Rights Committee has branches across the globe. As a volunteer and an independent researcher, I chair the Board of Directors of OHRC Canada.
Background
The Ogaden also known as the Somali Region of Ethiopia is located in the south-eastern part of Ethiopia; bordering the Afar region and the Republic of Djibouti in the North, the Oromia region in the south and west; and Somalia in the east, it is 250,000 SQK area.
The Ogaden is a place many of us hold dear. Partly because it is a place where Somalis have been historically marginalized by successive Ethiopian regimes; and it is laced with history of refugees and internally displaced persons; but most importantly it is a place dear to our hearts because it’s our homeland. My parents fled from the Ogaden in the early 50s, and became refugees in Somalia, where I was born. My mother died in 1988 in Mogadishu not fulfilling her life long dream of going back home to Werdher, Ogaden in her lifetime.
Like my mother, too many Ogadenis have died in exile as refugees, and in their memory I would like to share with you a quote from David Turton’s article titled “the Meaning of place in a World of Movement: lessons from long-term Field Research in Southern Ethiopia.
He states: “...to understand how a sense of place becomes bound up with a person’s social and individual identity, we must treat place, not as stage for social activity but as a ‘product’ of it. Such an understanding of the link between people and place helps us to appreciate that displacement is not just about the loss of place but also about the struggle to make a place in the world, where meaningful action and shared understanding is possible” (Turton 2005: 258)[1]
The dislocation and displacement of the people of Ogaden
Today, because of state sponsored violence, and a century long protracted ethnic-based conflict, the people of Ogaden are internally displaced and are forced to flee from their homes. It is important to note, unfortunately, this has happened too many times. Just like what is happening right now, massive displacement of civilian population, there were a number of crisis in this region, which resulted in massive displacement of people and mass exodus from the Ogaden. I have compiled in my research several such historical dates when the people of this region where internally and externally displaced over the years; here are two examples:
Through all these upheavals, women from this region were giving birth to children and raising them under such uncertainties, enduring the challenges that come with life as refugees. Although I was not born there, I grew up with the stories of these refugees who like my mother, their dreams of one day returning to their homeland overwhelmed the imagination.
The current state of Ethiopia under the current regime
Currently the present regime has engaged in what can be described as a war on the civilian population and as the case is always women and children are bearing the brunt of the pain. As you know Mr. Chairman, when the current regime came into power it promised a new beginning for all the people of Ethiopia including the people of Ogaden. A new constitution was written, chapter three of which enshrines the fundamental human rights principle. The new Ethiopian constitution is notably comprehensive and its human rights provisions are clearly stated. But so far, they remain only on paper[3].
Unfortunately, and to the disappointment of Ethiopian citizens and the international community the current regime failed to respect human rights it vowed to protect. Local and international human rights organization (such as Ogaden Human Rights Committee, Oromo Support Group and Sidama Concern, as well as Amnesty International and Human Right Watch) widely report on the violations of basic constitutional rights.
In the Ogaden recently, American journalists were harassed and jailed for some days. It is important to note that, according to the Washington Post (April 13, 1998), during the three-year period, from 1995 to1998, this current Ethiopian regime has arrested and detained more journalists than any other government on the continent of Africa[4]. The people of the Somali region and many other regions of Ethiopia have witnessed and suffered under this violent and aggressive state machinery.
Human rights abuses by Ethiopian armed forces in the Ogaden
Under successive regimes, the entire Ethiopian population suffered, my testimony today focuses on the Ogaden, The people of Ogaden have been subjected to harassment, unlawful detentions, rape and torture.
“Human rights violations reports are body counts, torture practices, an endless list of horrors; the violations seem beyond comprehension, mad men acting without reason. And the reports seem to be written by someone with stomach of a physician and the mind of a statistician” (David Matas, 1994:3)[5].
David Mata argues that human rights violations do not occur in an ideological vacuum, to the contrary, in many instances these acts are manifestations of an ideology[6].
Mr. Chairman, in researching human rights violations in the Ogaden, we have documented the rape of innocent young girls, the hijacking of privately owned vehicles, publicly shooting innocent people to instill fear in the communities, looting people’s properties and general dehumanizing acts by the Ethiopian military. Human rights violations are defined as “torture, disappearances, killings, detentions and unfair trails”’ these acts occur continuously in the Ogaden in a blatant manner.   This regime deals with the people of Ogaden with impunity in some instance going so far as to leave the murdered innocent civilians out in the public square. The local community is then instructed that no one can burry the dead, and so carcass is left to rot in full public view.
There are countless instances where it seems, that rape is being used as a weapon. In 2003, the Ethiopian troops’ commander, in Qabridaharre, told a gathering in the township, “Any women suspected of harboring or being a relative of an ONLF member would be raped and then killed” (OHRC Report, 2007:29.
Women from the Ogaden (the Somali region) have recounted horror stories of rape:
According to the OHRC 2007 report, on March 27th, 2007, Fathi Moalim Khalif, who is a former rape victim, was detained with other four civilians, in Dhagahabur. Prior to this arrest, members of the Ethiopian armed forces have gang raped her in January 2007. Fathi is pregnant as a result of that rape. Speaking to OHRC’s researchers her younger brother said: “They think they can hide their heinous crime by putting her behind bars. Everyone knows what happened to her. No jail or detention camp can cover their crime. We will never forget what they have done to her”. (OHRC, 2007:29)
Mr. Chairman, and distinguished committee members, you might be aware, almost the entire Ogaden population is Muslim; and as such they raise their daughters in a very traditional environments. It is with tremendous sadness that I report to you these women are not only living with the socio-cultural stigma that comes with rape in such traditional communities; but they are also contracting HIV/AIDS and other STDs at an alarming rate. This is one of the rare communities that HIV/AIDS has not been detected until recently.
In addition to these women getting STDs, they oftentimes get pregnant and bear children because of these rapes. It is important to imagine the magnitude of the suffering these women endure.
Since the current Ethiopian government came to power, a large number of women have been detained, tortured, raped, and some have disappeared or been killed. Women are the most vulnerable groups to suffer abuse and violence in the Ogaden, simply because they are the relatives of, or suspected sympathizers with the Ogaden National Liberation Front (ONLF).
It is important to note that the above mention actions are in direct violation of the international convention on the protection of women.
Article 1 of the Declaration on the Elimination of Violence against Women (DEVW) states that: “for the purposes of this Declaration, the term “violence against women” means any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in physical, sexual or psychological harm or suffering to women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private life”.
Similarities to the Darfur situation
Mr. Chairman, the Somali region in Ethiopia is heavily militarized zone, and it has not known a stable and peaceful period. It is a military outpost and the people of this region have suffered immensely. In the spirit of time, I would encourage the distinguished members of this committee to please consult the Ogaden Human Rights Committee report of 2007; more reports can be found in www.ogadenrights.org
Mr. Chairman, It is no secret that Somalis in Ethiopia have been historically marginalized by successive Ethiopian regimes. I would argue they have been particularly persecuted by this current regime.
Mr. Chairman, many have compared what is happening in Ogaden to Darfur, We all know what has happened in Darfur amounted to genocide. I would submit to you that, the actions of the Zenawi regime placed upon the Ogaden, could be defined as genocide. And to that end, it is morally imperative that action be taken to mend broken lives.
Furthermore, my presentation would be incomplete if I did not mention the one big difference between Ogaden and Darfur. And that is, the government is doing the very acts of terrorizing the communities.
The currently accepted definition of genocide is the one contained in the 1984 United Nations Convention on Genocide:
“In the present Convention, genocide means any of the following acts committed with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national ethnical, or religious groups, as such: 1) Killing members of the group; 2) Causing serious bodily harm to members of the group; 3) Deliberately inflicting on the group conditions of life calculated to bring about its physical destruction in whole or in part; 4) Imposing measures intended to prevent birth within the group; 5) Forcibly transferring children of the group to another group”.
Actions by the Ethiopian military such as these listed below are to genocide.
Mr. Chairman, I would submit to you that these acts fit very well within Article I and II of the current UN definition of genocide.
Mr. Chairman, it was clearly stated in the US State Department’s Human Rights report on Ethiopia that there is the existence of:
1.       Arbitrary or Unlawful Deprivation of Life
2.       Disappearance
3.       Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman, or Degrading Treatment or Punishment
4.             Arbitrary Arrest or Detention
5.             Police detained journalists during the year
6.             Arbitrary Interference with Privacy, Family, Home, or Correspondence
7.       Denial of Fair Public Trial
Mr. Chairman, the current regime has recently released some prisoner, however in the Ogaden the jails in major cities, such as Jigjiga, Dhagahbur, Qabridahare, Godey and Fiiq, are full of victims of unlawful arrests. And the resulting overcrowding in these un-kept below human standard prisons is further causing a health risk.
Mr. Chairman, the Ogden community as a whole is experiencing the brutality of the Addis regime. Collective punishment is the order of the day in the Ogaden. The communities of Dhagahbur; Qabridahare, Godey and Fiiq have been subjected to:
Ethiopia and the State of Democracy
Mr. Chairman, on August 3, 2007 - Senator Leahy made a passionate statement on the floor of the US Senate.    After sighting election fraud, illegal imprisoning of civilians, documented Human rights abuses, the government’s role of inciting violence, he finished in part
“It is no excuse that the Ethiopian military has impeded access to the Ogaden, as it has done. In fact, this should give rise to a sense of urgency. If we cannot properly investigate these reports, and if the Leahy Law, which prohibits U.S. assistance to units of foreign security forces that violate human rights is not being applied because the U.S. Embassy cannot determine the facts, then we should not be supporting these forces.”
Ladies and Gentlemen, there isn’t anything complex about what the people of Ogaden want.
The people of Ogaden want what is due to them under the current Ethiopian constitution. The Ethiopian constitution explicitly states that human rights, civil and political rights, economic and cultural rights and the essential tenants of living with basic human dignity must be honored.
Mr. Chairman, the current regime failed miserably to adhere to its own constitution.   And when questioned about its actions, this regime gets into defensive obfuscations to conceal the unpleasant realities on the ground. To that end, we are encouraged by the recent UN fact-finding mission into Ogaden. We look forward to the day when the Ogaden is open to international aid agencies and can begin to repair and rebuild where the current regime has destroyed and depleted.
Mr. Chairman the people of Ogaden are hopeful of the day they can participate in fair and democratic elections, enjoy freedom of the press, and access to development and investments from the outside world.
Mr. Chairman, being minority is not a reason to be killed and tortured, being Somali is not a reason to be raped and beaten; and certainly being a Muslim is not a crime punishable with indignity.
Mr. Chairman, Ethiopia is one of the poorest countries in the world, the Somali region, even by Ethiopian standards, is the poorest and least developed region of the country. As it is stated in the UN report that came out last week, the people of Ogaden urgently need the basics to live decently.
While we, the Ogaden community, in the Diaspora, applaud this very important hearing, we want you to know that this is only the beginning.   We are going to hold you accountable as well. You have now taken on the task of researching what is happening on the ground, therefore, you have no choice but to take action.
Ladies and Gentlemen, I applaud you for your leadership but I implore you to live up to the standards of Human Dignity that your constituents here at home live by.
The Ethiopian regime needs to be challenged by the United States to demand that the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC), Doctors without Borders and other aid groups be allowed to help the people and Ogaden.   And that their efforts and access to this region be unrestricted.
Concluding remarks
Mr. Chairman, having highlighted the difficulties the people of Ogaden are dealing with, I would like to point out that it is not only the Somali region that has suffered under the brutalities of the current regime of Ethiopia. The people of Oromia region, Gambella region, Amhara region and Sidama region have also been subjected to human rights abuses. We need to ask ourselves; where do we go from here?
Mr. Chairman, Ethiopia and the Ethiopian masses need the international community and particularly the United States to intervene, and put pressure on the current regime to allow substantive democratic processes to be put in place.
Setting the context for democracy and the process of democratization in Ethiopia is very important. Furthermore, Mr. Chairman, it is important to underline the facts about democracy as a universal principle of governance, it is a moral imperative, a social process, and particular kind of political system, which can apply to all societies including Ethiopia[7]. I would submit to you that internal weak leadership and narrow-minded dictatorship have derailed democratic process in Ethiopia.
Mr. Chairman, Ethiopia is a country with great ethnic, linguistic diversity as well as religious diversity. Ethiopians need to be very careful not fall into the trap of narrowly defined nation-building ideologies; which many African countries pursued at the dawn of independence to their determent. These ideologies are summarized in Womb’s formula, as cited by Shivji, “One people, one nation, one political party and one supreme leader” (Shivji, 2000:30)[8].
Mr. Chairman, the United States and the international community can facilitate a dialogue among the different Ethnic groups in Ethiopian; a dialogue I hope would lead to conflict resolution processes and substantive gender equity; and help the Ethiopian masses to heal after the abuses of successive regimes.
I thank you for giving me this opportunity to speak regarding this life and death issue and I thank you on behalf of the many whose lives you your action will touch. I look forward to your questions and I hope I can shed additional light on this issue.
[1] Journal of Refugee Studies Vol. 18, No. 3 David Turton (2005) “The Meaning of Place in a World of Movement: Lessons from long-term Field Research in Southern Ethiopia”. Published by Oxford University Press
[2] F. Abdulkadir unpublished research, 2007
[3] Journal of Oromo Studies Vol. 13, No. 1&2, Fowsia Abdulkadir (2006) “Human Rights Predicament: Analysis of the Somali Region (Ogaden) in Ethiopia.
[4] Review of African Political Economy, No. 91: 73-94; Khalif, Mohamud H. & Doornbos, Martin “The Somali in Ethiopia: Neglected Human Rights”.
[5] David Matas, 1994:3; “No More: The Battle Against Human Rights Violations”; Dundurn Press Ltd. Toronto, Canada
[6] ibid
[7] “The State and Democracy in Africa” edited by George Nzongloa-Ntalaja & Margaret C. Lee; published by Africa World Press Inc; 1998 – Chapter 1
[8] “Reflections on Leadership in Africa: Forty Years After Independence” edited by Haroub Othman; published by VUB University Press, 2000; chapter three; p. 30

Original_Search_Title: Modise Gets 10 Years
Headline: Botswana: Modise Gets 10 Years
Body_Text:
Gaborone — The Village Magistrates Court this week sentenced Gaborone businessman, Jeremiah Modise to 10 years imprisonment for armed robbery. The sentence is effective from September 20th 2007.
The court found that Modise and four others, who were not present used guns at Sir Seretse Khama International Airport last December to steal USD800,000 and 80,000 (P5million) Euros. Two of Modise's accomplices are at large, whilst the other two are in jail in South Africa and awaiting extradition to Botswana. Passing sentence, Principal Magistrate, Lot Moroka said that the main objective was to send a message to would-be robbers that engaging in such activities does not pay.
Addressing fully packed courtrooms III and IV, he said Modise played a very important role in the robbery in revealing information about airport security to his accomplices from South Africa. He planned the robbery, keeping guns for the robbers as well as transporting the loot to South Africa where eight people, including himself, shared the loot, each getting P100, 000 some US Dollars and 6,000 Euros.
Modise made the revelation in his confession statement to the court. He stated that they had planned the robbery for some time in November 2006 and it was aborted because of the heavy presence of police and soldiers as some dignitary was either leaving or arriving. The robbers went back to South Africa leaving their guns in the custody of Modise. The court heard that the South African police cracked the syndicate when one of them tried to buy a combi using US Dollars and they became suspicious. The young man was arrested and he nadisclosed a lot of information to the police about the origin of the money.
