Moroka added that armed robberies of this magnitude, in which millions of Pula involved, were rare in Botswana and could tarnish the country's image abroad because the money was shipped from Europe through South Africa en route to Botswana.
"This also has the potential to impact on Botswana as an unsafe place for investors at a time when the country is fighting to diversify its economy," he said. He added that this kind of robbery could inflict long-term instability on any economy. The stolen money belonged to Standard Chartered Bank and at the time of the robbery it was being transported from the airport to a bank in town. The magistrate said that before coming to his conclusion he considered the mitigating factors by the defence that Modise was an old man and he is in poor health. He pleaded guilty and his previous convictions were two decades old.
"The court has heard about the accused's condition of constriction of his oesophogus and need for a special diet. He had visited a string of doctors. However, I found that he faces no imminent life threatening condition," the magistrate said. He further acknowledged that by pleading guilty, Modise has shown remorse and contrition but insisted that the seriousness of the offence carried more weight than his mitigating factors. He stated that there was no exceptional extenuating circumstance on the part of the accused. Moroka told the court that he believes in equal treatment of offenders, irrespective of their wealth, hence the judicial system should be fair if it wants to maintain public confidence.
"There cannot be justice for the poor and another for the rich," he stated. The public wants to live in a peaceful environment and not in fear of being attacked or their property being stolen as envisaged in the national Vision 2016, hence the courts should play their role in ensuring that such goals are achieved by administering stiffer penalties for serious offences, pronounced Moroka.
However, on the issue of compensating the shipping company, Moroka instructed that that would require the complainants to lodge a case against Modise. He said the case was complex since it involved a number of people, some of whom were not in court. He assured them of proper civil proceedings in court. The main issue was that the prosecution and the defence could not agree on figures and the value of the money for compensation. He ordered that the recovered money should be returned to the lawful owners. Modise was driven from the court at high speed and under stiff security. Mmegi found Modise's wife, daughters and a family friend chatting at the gates of the Maximum Security State Prison though they did not see him enter.
The regular menu at the prison is: Breakfast and dinner for seven days, sorghum or maize meal soft porridge and 50g slice of bread with tea. Lunch: Maize meal, hard porridge, meat or chicken 3 times a week, spinach and other vegetables.

Original_Search_Title: Karamoja Malnutrition Stings
Headline: Uganda: Abraham Lokong - 'We Survive On Sorghum And Wild Green Leaves'
Body_Text:
Kaabong — Karamoja region, in northeastern Uganda, had the highest rate of malnutrition in the country in 2006, according to government statistics. Despite receiving the heaviest amount of rain in years in 2007, malnutrition continues to be a major concern in Karamoja, especially among children. Abraham Lokong, 31, an unemployed father of two, spoke to IRIN on 14 September during a health screening for children under an MSF-Spain programme aimed at reducing morbidity and mortality among children in Kaabong, one of the five districts in Karamoja:
"Perpetua Nakwang is my second child. I brought her to here to check if her weight is okay because she was down with a cold last week and has been weak since then. A health worker advised me to bring her to this clinic run by MSF-Spain.
"My wife could not bring Perpetua here because she is heavily pregnant and also very weak; we have a big problem finding food to eat because we are too poor. Besides, now that I am here someone has to take care of our first-born child.
"In fact it is a miracle that they have told me my child's weight is not bad, we hardly have anything to eat. We grind sorghum and cook wild green leaves to eat it with; sometimes we have only one meal and eat the leftovers the following day. I think my children are often ill because we never vary our food.
"As I am not in regular employment, I get by doing odd jobs here at Kaabong town; I cannot afford a lot of things that I know my family needs. I am the last-born in my family and did not benefit from the dowry that my elder sisters brought because they married when I was still young. My father lost the cows paid as my sisters' dowry to cattle-rustling, so we have nothing.
"I would like to have access to a small loan so I can start hawking sodas in town; this way perhaps I could help to buy food for my family. I am worried about the third child on the way, as you can see, even if this child is healthy now, who knows where her meal for tomorrow will come from?"
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Dakin Gari - One Flood Too Many
Headline: Nigeria: Dakin Gari - One Flood Too Many
Body_Text:
A monumental flood recently came upon Dakin Gari in Kebbi state, washing away 400 houses. Today, the townspeople are rebuilding lives recently shaken by the moving event
When the founders of the town, including the forebears of the present governor of the state, Alhaji Sa'idu Usman Nasamu Dakin Gari, for whatever reason, named it 'Dakin Gari,' they, perhaps, never envisaged an unfortunate natural occurrence in the distant future, which would strike a traumatising coincidence with the name, and form a significant stage in the history of the town.
They named the town, 'Dakin Gari,' or 'House of Flour,' oblivious, perhaps, of the fact that it is unheard-of that a house practically built with flour, if, at all, it is ever done anywhere, stands, especially after a light wind or sprinkle of water. Ages after the founding of the town, about four hundred of its houses behaved accordingly, under a 12-hour rainfall and flood between Friday, August 24 and Saturday, August 25. The situation was a climax of several earlier days of rainfall. The houses crumbled like structures actually built with flour.
Since then, after every rainfall any day, more houses have been crumbling in this large Fulani community, the headquarters of Suru Local Government Area of Kebbi state. They would continue to crumble, it is so feared, until the end of this rainy season.
There was nothing unusual in the rainfall, which began as a light shower by 8 pm of Friday, when most residents of this Muslim community were either saying their evening prayers or having their dinner. As they retired to bed, especially between 10 pm and midnight, it escalated to a heavy downpour. Consequently, the stream in the eastern outskirts of the town, which is a tributary of River Rima, heavily served by the rain water rushing with force down the nearby hills, overflowed its banks, and flowed into the low and rather fragile fairly lengthy dyke constructed years ago at the eastern edge of the town, to divert its water to the swamps in the southern and western outskirts.
The overflowing stream water, therefore, forced a new course for itself, submerging the entire town with a flood of unprecedented magnitude from knee-deep to navel-deep and, in some sections of the town, it is so said, even to chest-deep, levels for adults. Aided by the force of the strengthening torrents from above, the sweeping flood melted the very soluble foundations of hundreds of the age-old mud houses, downing them with ease and sacking their occupants to verandas of neighbouring concrete houses, but most to the central primary and government secondary schools, where they are fated to continue passing the night, perhaps to the end of this rainy season.
Every morning, the displaced population would flood back to their felled houses, trying to dig out and pick whatever valuables they could find, as many a household head makes efforts to erect even if a thatch hut to accommodate his family, as a rescue from the rainy season cold of the primary and secondary schools, and the discomfort of the neighbouring houses.
Most of the houses in the town are constructed in the traditional northern community fashion, each containing several households, with either an elder and his younger brothers, or a father and his sons, each having his own with his family of a wife or wives and children, and even grandchildren. The larger population of the town wallows in subsistence.
By 8 am of Saturday, when the flood abated substantially, hundreds of houses, comprising over 1500 households, had been reduced to debris. More houses crumbled after every rainfall. By last Sunday, therefore, no fewer than 1800 households had been demolished in the fashion of FCT's Development Control Department bulldozers pouncing on squatter settlements in Abuja.
No human life is reported lost, very interestingly, but the loss and damage in terms of livestock, food items, clothing and other property are still said to be unquantifiable.
So far, only the state and local governments and individuals have contributed relief items, comprising the usual bags of cement, bundles of zinc, wrappers and other forms of clothing, plastic buckets, mats, and timber planks of different sizes, bags of grains, drugs and cash. Depending on the quantity of damage and loss incurred, each affected household head is given a package of these relief items, and an amount of cash, which mainly ranged from N1500 to N5000 to rebuild his house. A Suru local government committee distributes the items.
Most affected people, however, complained that politics has already sneaked into the distribution of the relief items. While a household head, who may have lost just a room, could be given four or five bags of cement and four or five bundles of zinc, apart from a comparatively higher quantity of the other relief items, another, who may have lost about four or five rooms, could be given only a bag of cement and a few sheets of zinc.
Mallam Bello has crammed his about 30-member family into an only standing big room in his house, to avoid the discomfort of squatting in a neighbour's or a relation's house anywhere.
"There is a problem squatting in someone's house," he maintained, complaining that he had not received any relief item from anyone. "I have heard that some relief items have been brought here, but I have not received anything yet, not even a grain of corn or millet." He could not say when he would rebuild his house, resigning his fate to God, saying, "Until Allah provides. We will wait for the end of the rainy season before thinking anything in terms of resettlement. We have not received our share of relief items ,because we have been told that it is not our turn yet. When it gets to our turn, we will get our share. For now, we have resigned to fate and look up to Allah, Who will show us what to do. I will only plead to the authorities concerned to be just and fair enough to help us out of this difficult situation."
He said he has never witnessed a flood of this magnitude since he was born in the town. "Floods occur over the years, but they have never occurred in this magnitude. I have never witnessed such a frightening occurrence."
Mohammed Sani Dakin Gari has also lost his house to the flood. "What happened was that it rained heavily and there was a massive flood that swept away our houses. I had six rooms which we occupied with my two wives and 12 children. We are now squatting in my neighbour's house. His name is Muhammadu Dan Basiru. His house is not affected and he is not even resident here. That is why we are squatting in his house, together with his own family. I cannot think of rebuilding my house now, until the end of the rainy season. Even if I receive relief items of, say, a bag of cement, ten sheets of zinc and N1500, I cannot imagine how they can help me to resettle my family. If I must rebuild my house, I must rebuild at least four of the six rooms and make do with them, before I rebuild the other two in due course. Ten sheets of zinc can not roof even a room."
Aliyu Gardi is about 70. His house of six rooms is completely washed away. His two wives and 12 children are now squatting in one room in a neighbour's house, while he squats in the Mosque. He was given four bags of cement, and one and a half bundles of roofing sheets, complaining that they will not be enough for him to rebuild even one of his lost six rooms.
"I have now erected a thatch hut to resettle my family back home. In the dry season I will buy mud to reconstruct my house. The whole family will have to live in this hut before the dry season."
Elderly Maccido Galadima has also lost his house of two rooms. He has relocated his family of one wife, three children and eight grandchildren to his friend's house in another ward of the town. The friend has also lost a section of his house to the flood, but he could afford to cram both families in the standing section. Mallam Maccido said he lost about 50 bundles of millet to the flood. He was given three bags of grains, seven bags of cement and two bundles of roofing sheets. He said he will have to apply his strength to source for more building materials, because the relief items he has received will not be enough for him to rebuild his house during the dry season.
"I have never witnessed a flood of this magnitude," Usmanu Bahindi, 50, whose house was reduced to debris, said. "I have relocated my family of one wife, eight children and three grandchildren to the secondary school premises. I have now constructed two thatch huts and I am relocating the family there. We have lost everything. I will rebuild my house whenever Allah provides."
He said he has received three bags of grains, one each of sorghum, maize and millet, seven bags of cement and three bundles of zinc. "Don't ask me if they will be enough for me to rebuild my house, because you know they won't. I will only look up to Allah. I can only plead with government to give us more help. You know it will not be easy for someone whose house has been reduced to nothing."
"Even my parents have said that they have never witnessed a flood of this magnitude," Umar Faruq Daudu, 25, also displaced, said. He is a primary school teacher in nearby Aljannare village. He has a wife and two children, who are now trying to find some comfort in a thatch hut he has erected in the open space. The family relocated to the secondary school compound for a week, before he could fetch the thatch to erect the hut.
He has received a bag each of maize, sorghum and millet, four bags of cement and 28 sheets of zinc to rebuild his two rooms. He shared complaint with his fellows, saying that they will not be enough for him. "We only rely in God. I cannot tell you when I will rebuild my house, because I cannot afford to. I wait for God's time. I will wait for the end of the rainy season, and then I will build mud rooms and roof them with corn stalks and thatch. It is my view that before most people rebuild their house it will be after the festival of sacrifice (Sallar Layya or Eid-el-kabir). This is after four months. Some people may not even be able to rebuild their houses for ever. Someone lost over N300, 000 in cash to the flood. The flood carried away my goats and sheep. My bed is beyond repairs now. Only God knows when I can regain my belongings. On the day of the flood, all my wife and I had were the clothes we had on. Friends had to contribute cloths to both of us."
Muhammadu Maude lost eight rooms and two barns containing bundles of grains, all drained away by the flood. He has a wife, five children and four grandchildren. They are squatting in a neighbour's house.
He received four buckets, four blankets and a bundle each of wrapper and guinea brocade, six bags of cement, but no roofing sheet. Like his fellows, he cursed the quantity of the relief building materials in relation to what would be sufficient for him to rebuild his house. He could not even say when he could rebuild his house. He may only have to erect thatch huts to resettle his family.
He advised government to build houses for them instead of giving them relief materials.
Alhaji Abubakar Dakin Gari is the chairman of the 13-member Suru local government committee in charge of distribution of relief items to the victims of the Dakin Gari flood disaster.
"Immediately after the occurrence of the disaster, we received two truck loads of relief materials from Kebbi Emergency Relief Agency (KERA) worh N3 million, N500, 000 from a Minna-based engineer who hails from here, several contributions of N100,000 and N200,000 from individuals. The local government gave relief materials worth N5 million, comprising clothing, beddings and building materials. The state government later came in with its own relief materials which I cannot quantify now. We received a total of N1.6 million cash. The state governor individually contributed an additional N1 million," he disclosed.
The affected wards are Shiyyar Hakimi and Shiyyar Marafa. Instantly it affected 1500 households comprising about 20,000 members.
"We give each household head a package of the relief materials and cash, according to the gravity of loss and damage," the committee chairman announced, explaining, "For example, for any big room lost, we give a cash relief of N1000 and a small room, N400. So, if you lost four big rooms, you will be given N4000 and if you lost three small rooms, you will have N1200."
He said a committee of town planning officials from the state government also surveyed the entire town ,and took a census of all those affected, announcing that a proper network of township roads and drainages would be laid. He described this decision as an opportunity the town has now got, to avert future occurrence of the disaster, saying that the governor has issued a fiat to ensure that these and other necessary facilities are provided in the town.
"The governor has, for example, instructed that a bigger and higher dyke be constructed to divert the water from flowing into the town in future, and wider roads and drainages should also be constructed," he announced, adding, "During our discussion with the governor he also announced that the state government would source money to build 1000 housing units, but he has stressed that he will when he gets the money to do so. So, it will not be immediate."
He observed that the permanent measures to be taken against future occurrence, are a proper layout of the town to relocate people to a higher level of land, wider streets and drainages, as he lamented the failure of the neighbouring Bunza, Dandi and Bagudo local governments to contribute relief materials, saying, "We thought they would immediately rush in emergency contributions. They have not done anything up till now. This is what is still confusing me. Whatever happens to them, we commiserate with them. I don't know why they have not expressed any sympathy to our plight yet." The member representing the local government area in the House of Representatives, Alhaji Abubakar Bagudo, and the senator for the senatorial district, Alhaji Umaru Tafida, contributed N200, 000 and N100, 000 respectively.
Alhaji Abubakar said the relief materials are meager, but the beneficiaries are still happy. He said the affected people have already been warned against selling whatever items they are given.
Alhaji Aminu Abdullahi, Sarkin Kudun Dakin Gari, is the district head of Dakin Gari.
"We now have at least 1800 households affected by the disaster. This figure will rise because more houses are crumbling as the rains fall. More people are reporting to me about the crumbling of their houses. We cannot quantify the loss and damage because they are continuous as rains continue to fall," he observed.
The district head said the aged members of his community told him that they have not witnessed a flood of this magnitude in the town since the last 80 years. He said he has not received any report of those affected by the disaster to emigrate, saying that he would not even expect them to do so, because people are sufficiently assured that the government would fulfill its promise of constructing housing units to relocate those in the disaster-prone area. "Nobody has emigrated, and nobody is intending to emigrate," he stressed, pleading with the federal government, which has not sent in any relief as at reporting time, to send "an immense quantity" of its own relief contribution.
Alhaji Salihu Attahiru Aliero, the state's commissioner of local government and chieftaincy affairs, led the delegation which delivered the relief materials contributed by the state government to the local government committee in charge of the distribution.
According to him, the state government contributed 750 bundles of roofing sheets, 2200 bags of cement, 4000 timber planks of various sizes, 100 bundles of wrappers, 1000 bundles of guinea brocade, 1000 bundles of blanket, 1000 rubber mats, 2000 plastic buckets, 1000 plastic plates, 1000 plastic cups and over 2000 bags of assorted grains, in relief to the victims of the disaster.
"I must tell you that it is a monumental disaster, as over 30 percent of the houses in that town have been washed away. The local government there also did commendably well, contributing relief materials worth over N6 million," he said.
"What we are doing is a temporary or relief measure. I think we need to have a permanent solution to this problem. The disaster of Dakin Gari is only the most recent one. It happened in Aliero and a lot of other villages within the state. When you check the causes of the problem, you will find that they are common," he said.
He explained, "I think the manner in which our people settle in their environment needs to be checked. They don't give consideration to ensuring that there are proper drainages. Even where drainages are constructed the maintenance culture is not there. You will see government constructing a drainage and people dumping waste in it. It will be blocked and nobody cares to de-silt and when rains come, because the water passage is blocked, instead of draining smoothly, it flows into people's houses, thereby destroying the houses. Relevant government agencies should also ensure that rules and regulations governing the building of houses are also strictly abided by. Sometimes people just go about building houses without abiding by the rules and regulations."
The commissioner said towns like Dakin Gari need proper town planning. He said the state government would formulate a concrete policy aimed at averting such occurrences in all the towns and villages of the state. The most reliable measure against future occurrence, according to him, is the relevant government agencies ensuring strict abiding by the rules and regulations governing the construction of houses and other structures.

Original_Search_Title: Life Story of a Notorious Slaver
Headline: Tanzania: Life Story of a Notorious Slaver
Body_Text:
Nairobi — JUST OVER A CENTURY AGO, ON JUNE 13, 1905, a dark-skinned Muslim man named Tippu Tip, then aged nearly 70, died in Zanzibar. Of mixed Arabic and African descent, this man had over the decades secured notoriety for himself in history books by making daring forays into the interior from the East African Coast right up to Central Africa and Congo.
During those forays, the slave and ivory trader fought many battles against black African peoples who attempted to stand in his way, and also encountered famous "explorers" like David Livingstone, Henry Morton Stanley, Cameron and the German Wissman. Ironically, Wissman's death was to occur the day after that of Tippu Tip, while Stanley had died the year before.
That the paths of these great explorers should over the decades have crossed those of a man like Tippu Tip, remembered as a brigand who pursued fame and fortune with calm ruthlessness, though tempered with tact and diplomacy, remains one of the most fascinating aspects of the "exploration" and "opening up" of the East African hinterland and the Congo.
Fortunately, Tippu Tip, despite only having rudimentary formal education, became one of the earliest natives of East Africa to write the story of his own life, encouraged by a German scholar, one Dr Heinrich Brode. From Tippu Tip's autobiographical manuscript, written in Arabic, Brode was able to reconstruct his story. The resulting text was first published in 1903, two years before the death of the legendary trader and warrior, after first appearing in the early issues of Proceedings of the Institute of Oriental Languages.
Although primarily published in the journal because of what Dr Brode considered to be its linguistic importance, Tippu Tip's account of his life was later expanded into an ordered, fully-fledged biography and eventually painstakingly translated into English.
A modern and corrected version of the original biography, Tippu Tip: The story of his career in Zanzibar and Central Africa, has just been published by Gallery Publications in Zanzibar. Edited by Mark Wilson, the book retains the original preface by Sir Charles Elliot, who served as the agent of the British government and Consul-General in Zanzibar between 1901 and 1902. In that preface, Sir Charles recalls having met Tippu Tip occasionally, and even describes his physical appearance.
"His features were of the African type, and produced at first an impression that he was a low-caste hybrid," writes Sir Elliot, "but this impression was dispelled by his polite and dignified manners and his flow of speech."
THE ORIGIN OF THE NAME Tippu Tip has never been conclusively verified, but it has been claimed that it had to do with a constant twitching of the eyes that was a marked feature of the slave raider's mannerisms. Tippu Tip, whose name was actually Hamed bin Mohammed el Murjebi, himself once claimed that the nickname was given to him by the Africans whose territories he plundered during raiding expeditions that were essentially commercial undertakings. Described as intelligent and not wantonly brutal like his fellow Arab slave and ivory traders, Tippu Tip was also reported to have been particularly friendly to those European explorers he met during his ventures into the hinterland of East Africa.
Tippu Tip's ancestry went back to Muscat, and he was the product of a lengthy history of intermarriage between Arab immigrants from Oman and local African women on the East African coast. Tippu Tip's father, Mohammed was a descendant of one Juma bin Mohammed el Nebhani, who migrated to East Africa in the middle of the 18th century, leaving behind sizeable family holdings in his native Muscat. He settled at Mbwa Maji, south of Dar-es-Salaam, and married an African woman who bore him a son and two daughters.
The eldest daughter was named Mwana Arabu ("Daughter of the Arab"), and was later to be betrothed by her brother Mohammed to a companion and namesake of his named Rajab bin Mohammed bin Said el Murgebi, who was Tippu Tip's great-grandfather. That marriage produced Juma bin Rajab, whose son Mohammed married Karunde, the daughter of one Fundi Kira, then the powerful Sultan of Tabora. Mohammed afterwards also married, in Zanzibar, another woman, Bint Habib bin Bushir, who belonged to a respected and prosperous family in Muscat, and she was to become Tippu Tip's biological mother.
WITH SUCH A LONG History of inter-marriage with Africans, Tippu Tip's ancestors had over the years been able to lead successful trading caravans into the interior, and it was no surprise that the young Tippu Tip should undertake his first trip when hardly 18 years old. In later years, his knowledge of the hinterland and the diversity of peoples inhabiting it would prove of great assistance to the white explorers and missionaries he met, a fact that would be acknowledged by Sir Charles Elliot later.
"By the assistance which he rendered to them he indirectly contributed in no small measure to the civilisation of Africa, for which the Arabs themselves had done very little," Sir Charles Elliot was to say of Tippu Tip. "He was practically King of an enormous territory, but his power was never officially recognised even by his own sovereign."
For instance, when King Leopold of Belgium was persuading the Berlin Conference to give him the so-called Congo Free State as his private property, Tippu Tip's trading posts had a strong presence in the north and east of the country and the area to the east of it (modern Uganda), and had effectively established an independent state. To begin with, Leopold dealt with Tippu Tip by entering into an alliance. At the behest of the explorer Stanley, who doubled up as Leopold's agent, the Belgian king later appointed Tippu Tip governor of Stanley Falls district in Congo.
In the longer run, commercial rivalry led to a brief war, with both sides fighting by proxy, arming and leading the peoples of the upper Congo forests. Tip's muskets were no match for Leopold's artillery and machine guns. By early 1894, the war was over and Tippu Tip retired to Zanzibar.
Apparently Seyyid Bargash, who was on the Zanzibar throne during Tippu Tip's most active years, was apathetic about spreading his influence to the hinterland, and was only interested in the ivory that traders like Tippu Tip delivered to him after their regular forays there. Fearful that the Europeans eventually planned to wrest even Zanzibar from him, Bargash concentrated on hanging onto his coastal territories for as long as he could.
Bargash's father, the famous Seyyid Said, had ruled Zanzibar with rather more confidence, and had been the last of the Omani Arabs to unite his native sultanate with the East African dominions under one sceptre. Reputed to be a great lover of women, Seyyid Said presided over a splendid harem, and begat some 20 sons and numerous daughters.
It was one of these sons, Thueni, who had inherited the reins of power in Oman upon the powerful sultan's death in 1856. Another son, Majid, had taken over the throne in Zanzibar, to the chagrin of Bargash, an ambitious man who had also had his eye on it, so much so that he had made a determined but unsuccessful effort, three years later, to overthrow his brother. British naval forces that came to the latter's aid, and the crackdown resulted in the vengeful Bargash being exiled to Bombay and his adherents imprisoned, banished or having their property confiscated. Sultan Majid was to rule Zanzibar until his death in 1870, when Bargash finally ascended to the throne.
Tippu Tip, who had always liked Majid, used his formidable diplomatic skills to continue working with the less inspiring Bargash.
It was during this determined pursuit of personal riches that Tippu Tip met David Livingstone, on July 29, 1867, at a place called Ponda, not very far from Lake Mueru. Apparently, Livingstone was in dire straits at the time, having run out of essential supplies, and Tippu Tip writes that he gave the now ageing explorer a goat, a piece of white cotton, four large bushels of beads and a bag of sorghum, and later helped him to reach Lake Mueru.
Tippu Tip was to later meet another explorer, VL Cameron, on the shores of Lake Tanganyika. Cameron had been asked by the Royal Geographical Society in 1872 to go in search of the older explorer, but soon came across Livingstone's servants bearing the explorer's body to the coast; he had begun his mission too late.
He subsequently met Tippu Tip on August 19, 1873, and from the latter received much material assistance for his onward journey.
The encounters between Tippu Tip and the eccentric explorer Henry Morton Stanley are also recounted in detail, conveying the tensions that rose between the two mercurial and charismatic men before they could agree to show at least a modicum of respect for each other's exploratory skills.
Tippu Tip's intimate knowledge of the vast terrain he had traversed over the years would later make him indispensable to a German administrator named Edmund Schnitzer, who was referred to by historians as Emin Pasha. The German had worked under Governor-General Gordon in Egypt, and later travelled with him to Sudan and Uganda, being appointed the Governor of the then Equatorial Province. While there, he found himself benefiting immensely from Tippu Tip's ability to organise expeditions.
BUT PERHAPS THE MOST striking aspect of the Tippu Tip book is that it is one of those rare texts that earn their timelessness through the fact that they record history as it is being made.
The vagaries of disease, wanton bloodletting and unparalleled greed that we encounter between the covers of the book may be daunting, but they are living proof of the horrors of colonialism and the slave trade that not even the most rabid apologist for global capitalism will be able to defend.
That many individuals made personal fortunes out of the trade in human beings and ivory during the period of slavery in East Africa does not in any way distract the reader from visualizing the extent of the pain and human suffering that these opportunists, including Tippu Tip, left in their wake.

Original_Search_Title: Country Prepares to Host the Commonwealth
Headline: Uganda: Country Prepares to Host the Commonwealth
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The billboards read: 'UGANDA IS READY FOR CHOGM'.
CHOGM is the short form for the Commonwealth Heads of Government meeting to be held in Uganda in November.
The traditional leader of the Basoga, the head of a parastatal and radio personalities say so, too, as if to convince the average Kampala resident, who is neither so sure nor too interested.
A lawyer I know foresees the worst, and is planning to move to Nairobi for two weeks.
Beggars and street people are being removed and plastic bags will be illegal. Boda boda cyclists - 4,000 in Kampala - will have been substantially reduced to help traffic flow, and only the licensed ones will be allowed to operate.
Key roads are being overhauled, new luxury hotels rushing to finish and tall buildings downtown given a facelift. The main road from Entebbe to the city has been widened and upgraded. It is hoped even mosquitoes will disappear for that week, and spraying has already started.
But this is only part of the story. There are fears of unsafe water in hotels, and the power system being unable to cope.
There are charges that materials used on the road to the airport were sub-standard. The road where three Commonwealth high commissions are located - South Africa, Nigeria and Kenya - is in a terrible condition.
The mosquito-spray campaign has started in a restricted area: A three-kilometre radius from the Speke Resort, where several Heads of State will stay. The operation will be repeated in the same area in October.
But why not spray the whole city? Mosquitoes are not selective and do not only bite foreigners. In the north, where people pronounce CHOGM like Sorghum, people say all the sorghum is being distributed in and around Kampala, and not in the north, where it is needed most.
Will the delegates see much of the rest of Uganda? A few may stay on to visit game parks. Roads to some parks are in good condition. The worst part will be getting out of the city, where exit roads are rough and the traffic heavy and chaotic.
Yet CHOGM is an ideal opportunity to sell Uganda as a tourist destination. It has much going for it. Security is better than in Kenya. The parks are now full of animals. The mountain gorilla is a unique attraction.
But this generation of Ugandans are new to the tourist industry. Their challenge will be to offer a service that is so attractive that tourists will want to come back just because the people are nice, the water is clean, the service friendly and the rates reasonable. But besides the few adventurous ones who will visit a game park, no one is likely to see much else: The displacement camps in the north, for example, or the appalling road that joins Kampala to Nairobi.
Ten years ago, a bus trip between the two cities took 11 hours. If it took longer, you complained. Now, 16 hours is considered normal, and people no longer complain. The record, I have heard, is 36 hours, with no apology from the management!
In addition to the main conference, a Commonwealth Youth Forum will run one week before. Preparatory workshops are going on throughout the country, aimed at sensitising and educating the youth about the Commonwealth and how it works.
They have given their views on what they expect of the conference. It will also discuss issues that affect young people throughout the Commonwealth - conflict resolution, trade, youth participation in decision-making and HIV/Aids.
President Yoweri Museveni will open the youth conference whose theme will be: 'Breaking barriers: Unleashing young people's potential for development'. So far, young people have said their problems are age restriction when applying for jobs, demand for job experience, unemployment and lack of access to financial services, including loans.
Each of the 53 member countries will send two members, one male and one female, to the youth conference. As the host, Uganda will send 25. The youth will present their action plans and recommendations at the end of the meeting, and they will be forwarded to CHOGM.
The writer is a social worker

Original_Search_Title: Mma-Ten Days At Your Service If You Wanna Tipple
Headline: Botswana: Mma-Ten Days At Your Service If You Wanna Tipple
Body_Text:
Francistown — Ask anybody who lives in Francistown or has been to the city whether they know Mma-Ten Days and the response will be yes.
Otherwise take any taxi plying the Blue Town route at the busy taxi rank and tell the driver- as confidently as possible, or with a good dose of Dutch courage- to take you to Mma-Ten Days. He will deliver you at the place with not much ado, even if you are drunk or have owned a blackout (in beer drinking parlance).
After the taxi has managed to extricate itself from other honking taxis moving in all directions, you are right on the way. You will pass Thapama Lodge at the western corner of Blue Jacket Street, drive around the roundabout a little bit ahead of the hotel and then on to the fly-over moving westwards.
In case your sight becomes impaired as a result of over-indulgence, it does not matter all taxi drivers know where Mma-Ten Days is. At the four-way junction of Bogatsu Street and Botswana Street, simply cross the road from Peter's Bar and walk four houses to the left - and you are home and dry.
Before your eyes will be a spacious compound surrounded by an embroidered waist-level wall, which does not have a gate. A fully-grown Morojwa tree is on your left while an emaciated mulberry tree leans forlornly against the wall on your right.
There a dark skinned woman of medium build who looks young for her age, 69, and has three grown up children will meet you. She is Tendani Matlhapi, popularly known as Mma-Ten Days. She is ready to serve you the fiery waters to either quench your thirst or simply drown your sorrows.
When Mmegi team arrive, the tranquillity of the place belies its fame as the Super-Shebeen of Francistown, the watering hole of who-is-who in the city that is known the length and breadth of Botswana.
"People come from as far as Gaborone saying they have heard about this place. It is just that if they come at night, I do not serve them. But if they come in time, I offer them any beer they want," she says later in the interview after she had warmed up to us.
The first thing that impresses about Mma-Ten Days shebeen is cleanliness of the vast compound whose foreground is bare and two small houses cover about a quarter of the space in the background.
Mma-Ten Days is initially cautious when we sit down on white plastic chairs and introduce ourselves. Her chariness stems from the fact that hers is an illegal business as she does not have a licence to sell liquor.
Also, she has had to deal with the police in the past after a local newspaper ran a story about her business.
"There is a lot of crime these days. The police warn us that criminals may attack us if we operate at night. So, as soon as it is night time, I close up. I do not open to anyone who comes during the night," she says emphatically.
Matlhapi left home in Nshakazhogwe as a young woman of 25 in 1963 to come and stay with her uncle who that year had bought the Blue Town plot, which had a nondescript shack for a house for R58.
The following year, with her first son, who is now an accountant with a large corporation in Gaborone, she started brewing traditional sorghum beer which she sold to Francistown workers. It was an illicit business those days and they had to come up with ingenious plans to hide the liquor from colonial police.
"In the mornings, we would take our blankets and pile them on top of clay pots full of the beer. Though they searched the houses, they always left the piled blankets intact," she recalls.
She graduated to selling khadi, another potent brew fortified with yeast and made of water, wild berries and brown sugar in 1968, the year her other son, an engineer, was born. It was not until 1970 that she started selling bottled lager. She sold the beer, which she 'imported' from the then Rhodesia for a mere 25cents.
Matlhapi, who finished her Standard Five at the then Native School, which is now Nyangabgwe Primary School in 1957, has been selling lager beer since then.
"I used the proceeds to take my children to school as well as to build myself this shelter," she says pointing at the houses. Gap-toothed Mma-Ten Days is nostalgic about olden days when crime was the stuff of fiction and business was booming.
"There was no crime those days. Also, we managed to make profit because we could open anytime we want.
"These days the police will tell you that one of the reasons they dissuade us from staying open until night-time is because they are also protecting us from these criminals.
"A criminal may come and knock and, because you think he is someone who just need to be served, you will open only for him to rob you. This is really killing our business. Nowadays I sell beer only for sustenance as there is no profit," she says as she bid us farewell.

Original_Search_Title: Are We Ready for Chogm?
Headline: Uganda: Are We Ready for Chogm?
Body_Text:
Kampala — Billboards are springing up all over Kampala City proclaiming that some people are ready for the November Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (Chogm). Some of the people who are ready are a traditional leader, the head of a parastatal and some radio personalities.
There are some people who are not ready for Chogm though. According to the Weekly Observer of August 2, the Cabinet sub-committee for Chogm had many concerns about the preparations, namely: some Chogm money had been diverted to cater for pressing infrastructure challenges, substandard material has been used on the main Chogm road, lack of safe water in four Chogm hotels, load shedding and high rates for hotel suites.
Now for those not in the know, it is a very Ugandan approach not to complete the preparations for events until the last moment. If a traditional marriage ceremony is going to take place in a homestead for instance, the compound will be weeded and swept two days to the function and the tents to be used by guests will be erected either on the eve or on the morning of the event. That is what makes a really fresh event.
According to my cousin who lives in Gulu town, a caller to one radio talk show also claimed not to be ready for Chogm. Now the Acholi, of whom I am a proud member, have this uncanny ability of substituting "Ch" for "S" and vice versa. In that respect therefore Chogm becomes Sorghum. The caller's complaint on the talk show was that all the sorghum was being distributed in and around Kampala but none was being taken to Northern Uganda which is the neediest part of Uganda.
For a moment I wanted to laugh at my tribemate's assertion but the more I thought about it the more it actually made sense. The investments made by both government and the private sector to ensure the success of Chogm can neither be recouped during the few days of the meeting nor can this be done from Kampala only.
We need to look beyond Kampala as well as beyond the one week or so meeting, and here is why. The biggest benefit of Chogm is its marketing potential for Uganda. The people involved in organising this meeting, especially the cabinet sub-committee must already be aware of the bent in thinking by certain people that nothing good ever comes from Africa. We should have candidly assessed our weaknesses and come up with a plan to address them so that Uganda is surely ready.
One thing that Uganda ought to market is its tourism potential. The bulk of our tourism attractions are in the game parks which are mainly accessible by road, except somebody forgot to fix the main roads out of Kampala. So instead of Chogm marketing our tourism riches, the message that may come out will be something like "Uganda has a rich mix of tourism attractions but the visitor has to contend with dirty hotel water, very high hotel rates and kilometres of very poor roads." Imagine how many new or repeat visitors we shall attract with such a message.
The involvement of Ugandans in the success of Chogm cannot be underrated either. We should for instance have a campaign that says each village will have a dirt-free and crime-free Chogm. This campaign can bring on board schools, places of worship and perhaps get all radios and newspapers throwing in their lot as well. The point is that we need to get the population more involved in this Chogm thing and the sooner it is done the better.
Another thing that concerns me whenever I here the Chogm planners and managers speak is as though the sun will stop moving during the Chogm week. I have heard pronouncements like the city is being cleaned for Chogm or beggars are being carted off the street for the sake of Chogm and so forth. The unintended message that this sends out is that "even if we give visitors a good time during Chogm we shall revert back to our ways once the event is over."
Some of the changes that we have got the nerves to institute during Chogm ought to stay on long after the meeting. Who can argue with me that having a clean Uganda House (one of the most prominent buildings in Kampala) after Chogm is not a good thing?
One hopes that Chogm will not become yet another time when Uganda has not missed an opportunity to miss an opportunity.
Mr Abola is a business/finance consultant

Original_Search_Title: Fleeing Chadians Should Be Classed As Refugees, Says Joint UN-Sudanese Report
Headline: Sudan: Fleeing Chadians Should Be Classed As Refugees, Says Joint UN-Sudanese Report
Body_Text:
A joint report by the United Nations refugee agency and its Sudanese Government counterpart has recommended that the estimated 30,000 Chadians who have fled to neighbouring Darfur to escape a worsening security situation in their homeland be classified as refugees.
But the report also warned that anyone in that group who is an active or former combatant in the clashes in Chad should not be granted refugee status, UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) spokesperson Jennifer Pagonis said today.
Ms. Pagonis told reporters at UNHCR headquarters in Geneva that the recommendations follow an in-depth field assessment carried out in West Darfur state this year by staff from UNHCR and Sudan's Commissioner for Refugees (COR).
Most of the Chadians interviewed by the UNHCR-COR teams said they fled their country after armed men in military uniforms entered their homes, searching for weapons and accusing locals of participating or backing militia activities. Many of the searches turned violent, culminating in lootings, beatings, arrests and even murders.
"In addition to the brutal searches, many families said they left Chad because of the general insecurity and fighting between governmental forces and opposition groups," Ms. Pagonis said, noting that most people interviewed indicated they feared returning to Chad.
The new arrivals in Darfur, which has been beset by its own violence and humanitarian suffering since 2003, are mainly from Arab nomadic or semi-nomadic tribes, although some are from non-Arab tribes.
Ms. Pagonis said most of the group entered West Darfur near Foro Baranga, about 200 kilometres south of El Geneina, the state capital, and then settled along the Wadi Azoum river bed. The majority of arrivals came from the Tiero, Marena, Mayo and Awinrado areas in south-eastern Chad.
Most of the Chadians who have crossed the border do not seem to need urgent food or material assistance, having brought livestock and bags of sorghum with them, according to the report. But it recommended that the most vulnerable people among the new arrivals be provided with a food ration, and called on the Sudanese health ministry to launch a vaccination campaign for children as soon as possible.

Original_Search_Title: Homes Cut Off Solar By Costly Accessories
Headline: Uganda: Homes Cut Off Solar By Costly Accessories
Body_Text:
Kampala — Ugandans use solar energy every day: the sun dries sorghum for local brews, maize for food, fruit and cocoa for export and of course, the laundry. However, less than one per cent of Ugandans actually use solar technology for electricity. What prevents Ugandans from taking full advantage of their equatorial rays?
Fake or low quality panels hawked on the street do not live up to expectations and give solar a bad reputation, while high transportation costs and marketing challenges prevent solar energy from reaching a large rural market.
There are hundreds of little shops in Kampala that profess to sell solar equipment, but these products are not worth the cheaper price, according to solar industry leaders.
"They don't care about the consumer's needs. They will sell you the wrong size because it's the only thing they have in stock, they offer no warranty, and customers end up with broken or badly installed products. It gives solar a bad name," warns Mr Dhananjay Nandedkar, Energy Consultant at UltraTec, one of Uganda's leading solar companies.
Vendors hawk "Energy Savers" light bulbs on the street for Shs2,000, but these bulbs last only 1 or 2 months, says Mr Nandedkar, "customers are disappointed, and mistakenly equate 'solar' with 'low quality.'" UltraTec bulbs sell for Shs6,500 and last for 2 to 3 years, guaranteed.
Quality issues
In 2000, the Uganda National Bureau of Standards (UNBS) initiated a programme to regulate the importation of solar panels. According to Mr John Okumu, Senior Materials Engineer at UNBS: "Products with inadequate or illegible labels, and products that are not from accredited suppliers will be impounded for testing."
To date, they have educated import inspection officials on methods for quality control and have impounded 1,500 solar panels for testing.
Impounded products pose a significant cost for local businessmen. Even if the panels can be returned to the supplier, such complications damage vendor-supplier relationships and delays can result in customer dissatisfaction. Mr Okumu urges small businessmen importing solar panels to get information from UNBS on quality standards before selecting a supplier.
"Businesses should first import a sample to be tested by the UNBS," he says. Basic tests are free, while more thorough tests required for non-accredited sources range from Shs50,000 for a solar panel to Shs150,000 to test a whole system. Unfortunately, the UNBS programme does not deal with those most likely to sell poor quality panels: small-scale vendors who often display a single solar panel in a hardware shop.
Inspection requirements increase costs for well-established solar companies which are likely to import quality panels, while small vendors continue to hawk unlabeled, unaccredited panels at a lower price.
But what about the 97 per cent of rural Ugandans who don't even have access to electricity? Transportation costs for fragile solar equipment make it hard to offer the poor an affordable option.
"A 50 watt system can cost as much as a 1,000 watt system if we have to take it up-country for installation" says Mr David Sekalegga of Energy Systems Limited.
Some companies have already begun to solve the transportation issue by creating franchises and training agents in rural areas throughout Uganda. UltraTec has seven franchises in rural areas, with plans to open more.
"We train rural agents to market, install, and service our solar products," Mr Nandedkar explains, "that way the customer only has to pay a small transportation fee."
"The need is there, and the market could be," says Mr Martin Kayongo, leasing manager at Uganda Microfinance Ltd. (UML) who facilitates rural access to solar products through loans and leases.
"Rural people who struggle to pay a Shs20,000 school fee certainly cannot afford to pay Shs190,000 upfront for a solar lantern," he says. Microfinance loans or leases offer families an option to pay for the product over a period of up to two years.
"Many rural families already spend around Shs180,000 for one year's electricity needs," says Mr Nandedkar, "It's just spread out over twelve months." Say a family uses 6 litres of kerosene per month, at Shs1,700 per litre; that adds up to Shs122,400 annually. Add to that two phone charges per week at Shs600 each for an annual total of Shs57,600 and a family has spent Shs180,000 for one year's light and electricity.
Not to mention the cost of matches, lamp glasses, and transportation required to reach phone charging kiosks. For the same price, a family could have paid for a solar lantern through a loan from UML, saving Shs120,000 per year in electricity fees thereafter.
Marketing solar to rural communities is a challenge. "We need more rural education programmes" says Mr Kayongo, "many people don't know solar is even an option, much less how much they can save, or that loans are available to help."
Advertising
A little advertising can go a long way, as shown by the partnership between UltraTec, UML and GTZ. With just a Shs44m grant, they trained rural technicians and launched a marketing campaign in Kayunga District. Sales have doubled since the project began six months ago.
With less than one per cent of the energy market, solar energy is not in position to solve the current energy crisis. But with better education, marketing, and real quality regulations - not to mention hopes for cheaper technology, it could provide an excellent option for both Uganda's rich and poor.

Original_Search_Title: Lucy Mugure - "I Do Not Want My Children to Bring Up Their Children Here"
Headline: Kenya: Lucy Mugure - "I Do Not Want My Children to Bring Up Their Children Here"
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Lucy Mugure, 38, is a single mother of five and a resident of the sprawling Mathare slums. Mugure brews a type of illicit liquor, popularly known as chang'aa, to sustain her family.
Chang'aa is illegal in Kenya and has caused hundreds of deaths and blindness when adulterated by unscrupulous brewers who add methanol to 'increase' its potency. Mugure talked to IRIN about the challenges of being a mother in Mathare:
"We spend most of our time trying to avoid arrest by the police who pour our liquor away before taking us to court where we are fined or jailed.
"Sometimes you brew the chang'aa and there is no one to buy it. After spending 4,000 shillings (US$58) on sugar, molasses and sorghum or millet flour to produce a few gallons, which take two to three days to prepare, we sometimes go to sleep hungry.
"On the days when there are customers, I sell a glass at 10 shillings (15 US cents) - less than half the price of legal beer. Although most of my customers drink on credit, paying at the end of the week or the month, some refuse to pay.
"During good months, I make a profit of between 500 and 1,000 shillings ($7-$14) which is still not enough because I pay rent of 800 shillings ($12).
"Sometimes I only make 50 shillings (70 cents) in a day yet the children need to eat and we need to buy fuel. I usually end up sending the children to an eating place to spend 10 shillings (15 cents) each on a small plate of rice or a doughnut and beans to survive.
"The uniforms for my three school-going children are also expensive and I have to pay examination fees. Sometimes the children are sent home from school and I cannot afford to take them back to school; most of the time, at least one is out of school.
"Currently, my daughter is at home because she lost some text books belonging to her former school during a police raid on the Mungiki. [The Mungiki are a terror gang mainly operating from the Mathare slums. They also control public transport businesses.]
"I have been arrested several times but I will continue brewing chang'aa for the sake of my children. At least my customers trust me and know that I never add methanol to the chang'aa.
"Life is very difficult here in Mathare and I have decided not to have any more children. My last born, who is nine months old, was unexpected; I had gone to a private clinic for treatment but I still ended up pregnant.
"I have also decided not to get into another relationship; I want to concentrate on bringing up my children.
"I am hopeful that my children will get a good education so that they can start their own lives outside Mathare; only education can get them out of here; I do not know how I will pay for their secondary education but I will try. [Primary education is free in Kenya's public schools.]
"I was brought up here but I do not want my children to bring up their children here."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Mubika Loses Parliamentary Seat
Headline: Zambia: Mubika Loses Parliamentary Seat
Body_Text:
Ndola — THE Lusaka High Court has nullified last September's election of Sinjembela constituency member of Parliament, Mubika Mubika, saying he was not duly elected.
This is in a case in which losing United Liberal Party (ULP) candidate, Peniso Njeulu, had petitioned the election of the MMD candidate who is Communications and Transport deputy minister as first respondent and the Electoral Commission of Zambia (ECZ) as second respondent.
Mr Njeulu had petitioned the results on grounds that the elections were marred with electoral malpractices and urged the court to declare the poll null and void.
In delivering judgment, High Court Judge, Naboth Mwanza, said the allegations against the petitioner potraying him as a poor grade nine, who stole K500, 000.00 as announced at a number of public rallies contravened the electoral code of conduct.
"It is evident that vilification became a sing-song at rallies held by Mr Mubika hence its repetition at almost all the meetings and the whole story blew up in the area and affected the parliamentary election results. I am satisfied that he uttered these words.
"Consequently, I grant the relief which Mr Njeulu seeks by declaring that the illegal malpractices by Mr Mubika and his agents during the elections in Sinjembela's Shang'ombo district affected the election results which I nullify today," Judge Mwanza said.
Mr Justice Mwanza advised the ECZ to ensure that it improved organisational operations in future because it failed to provide satisfactory answers concerning the funds that were distributed late for operations in the area.
He said that he established the fact that Mr Mubika and his agents gave village headmen 2x50kg bags of sorghum to brew beer for distribution to people who would vote for him.
Mr Mubika was alleged to have threatened the voters in the area that he would ensure that on the actual voting day, a helicopter with gadgets would fly around all the polling stations detecting which people were voting against MMD.
He was accused of organising a victory party before the results were announced.
Mr Mubika was seen ferrying electoral officers and materials on nomination day and took some ballot boxes to his guesthouse, which were kept overnight unsealed.
He also intimidated the electorate by threatening them with deportation to Angola especially the Mbundas if they voted for Mr Njeulu.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Jubilant After Escaping the Hangman's Noose
Headline: Kenyans Jubilant After Escaping the Hangman's Noose
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Disembarking from an ALS flight from Rumbek, Southern Sudan, at Wilson Airport they looked worn-out.
Having been released from a Sudanese jail just a few hours earlier it was the first day in three months that they were enjoying freedom.
Last month the Kenyans - Mr James Munyao Mbithi and Mr Bernard Alumasa Mheri - and Mr George Forbes, a Kenyan born Australian, were charged with the murder of Ukranian Mykola Serebrenikov, an engineer.
Perhaps this is why they seemed overwhelmed and confused as jubilant relatives, friends and workmates hugged them.
But soon they were all smiles.
"We are straight from hell. They wanted to secretly hang us," said a pensive Munyao, 35.
"We are very tired. Why don't you allow us to rest then do an interview tomorrow when we are composed?" they request when we asked them for an interview.
The three men work for a Kenyan and Sudanese Trax Construction Company, a firm specialising in building military barracks, hospitals and facilities for non-governmental organisations in Southern Sudan.
A few days later we finally sit down with them for an interview where they recount the anguish and suffering they faced in jail.
They were twice sentenced to hang for a crime they insist they never committed, before being set free by the Sudan Court of Appeal.
The three were arrested after the Ukrainian was found hanged from a towel rack at the Trax's compound. The company is registered in Kenya and Sudan.
A magistrate's court found them guilty of murder and sentenced them to hang. The sentence was upheld by the High Court before the Court of Appeal repealed it.
It took the combined intervention of the Governments of Kenya, Australia, and British for the three to gain their freedom.
Indeed, President Kibaki had to ask for the intervention of Sudanese vice-president and Southern Sudan President, Salva Kiir, at State House in Nairobi.
"We were kept in a room without a roof," recalls Munyao. "Sometimes we would be rained on and the nights were too long and extremely cold," he says.
During the day they encountered extremely hot conditions with temperatures exceeding 45 degrees centigrade without a roof over their heads.
"At night it was worse. The concrete floor was too hot. Initially we did not have mattresses," says Forbes. For toilets, they made do with manholes in the open.
Every day they lived with the dread of facing the hangman.
"Sick prisoners were unattended to. Two people died while we were there," says Forbes.
Asked how the food was Forbes wrinkles and acts like he is nauseated. "Luckily, the prison authorities allowed our company to bring food to us daily," he says.
"The prison food was unacceptable. There is no way one could have survived on it."
He says it was uncooked ugali made of sorghum with soup. "It smelled like shit. I tried it once but I it made me sick," he says.
But occasionally they would get breaks from the dreadful conditions when their country representatives Kenya's consular in Southern Sudan, Mr Katana Angore, and the Australian Ambassador to Sudan visited them. "We thank the Kenyan UN peace keeping force led by Colonel Lucas Tumbo and Major Waria.
They came for us from prison and took us to their compound where they fed us and gave us medical checkups and treated us," says Munyao.
Forbes was born in Nakuru in 1961 before he moved he Austarlia in 1987. "I thank Kibaki from the bottom of my heart for what he did on our behalf. I would like to shake his hand to thank him."
The three together with a Sudanese colleague, Mr Joseph Nduti, were arrested in February on allegations of murder of the Ukrainian who worked for another company based in Rumbek.
After spending 17 days in remand in police cells in Rumbek they were charged with the murder.
After two days of court hearings they were convicted of murder.
Upon appealing to the High Court, the superior court upheld the conviction.
Unsatisfied by the judgements by the two courts, they appealed to the Court of Appeal where a three-judge Bench composed of the court's president Justice Kurkurlopita Marino Pitia, Justice Kuc John and Justice Alexander Muludyang freed them.
The appellate judges criticised the High Court judge for taking on the role of prosecutor.
They also faulted the two junior courts for not considering the post-mortem reports, which clearly indicated the deceased had committed suicide.
"The trial court, has therefore failed to establish and prove causation - the link between any acts attributed to accused and the death of the deceased," ruled the judges.
"This is based on the fact that, the conclusion as to the cause of death due to hanging is unequivocal and that the site and manner of the ligature application are consistent with self hanging and that the marks of bruising on the legs of the deceased were not consistent with a struggle, as expounded by the expert authors opinion," concluded Justice Pita.
"There is no arguable case of murder against the accused. It is not a case where varying interpretations of the facts may lead to a different result. Accordingly the verdict of guilty of murder made by the High Court of Lakes State against the four convicts is hereby annulled and the convicts acquitted," he ruled.
Munyao, who was a chef and supervisor at their campsite, recalls how a drunken white man came into their compound in Rumbek at midnight shouting, "Security! Security!"
After consultations with his seniors and the company's security officials, Munyao offered him an unoccupied room for the night.
"He spent the whole night making incoherent noise in a strange language," recalls Munyao. The following morning he came out of the room stinking of liquor and asked for a cigarette before returning to the room.
"I went to take tea and 30 minutes later I was told he had hanged himself in a towel rack," he says.
His body was discovered after a white man came to identify him.
When there was no answer from the room, the white man and the security officials went round to a window from where they peeped inside. "They saw his body hanging on a towel hanging line," said Munyao.
Police later concluded he had committed suicide and released his body to his employer, Acarica Aviation.
Before his body was moved officials from the United Nations took pictures of the body.
His employer transported his body to Nairobi's Wilson airport in a cargo plane.
The next day the three men together with some Sudanese were asked to report to a police station to record statements on the incident. They were ordered to obey the summons.
The prosecution preferred against them five charges some of which were contradictory. The charges included murder, failure to prevent a suicide, omission, smuggling a body to Kenya and negligence.
"They were malicious and cooked up charges. The magistrate and judges convicted us even before listening to our case," says Munyao.
Munyao says the police found that there was no case against them but a senior official of the Southern Sudanese Government insisted that they be prosecuted.
'The President of the Court of Appeal admitted the charges were malicious," he says.
Munyao and Forbes concur that their treatment in Southern Sudan is a classical example of how foreigners, especially Kenyan and Ugandan businesspeople. are mistreated in Southern Sudan.
To do business there, they say, one has to have a senior person in the SPLM to front him.

Original_Search_Title: Mugisu Man Caned
Headline: Uganda: Mugisu Man Caned
Body_Text:
Kampala — A Mugisu man was seriously caned in Mbuya recently, for terrorising malwa (local brew from sorghum) drinkers with the Mugisu herb which reportedly makes one start dancing, while asking for circumcision.
It all happened when one Wafula, developed an argument with his colleagues about circumcision. The basis of the argument was that anybody given the herb, regardless of their tribe, would ask to be circumcised.
During the argument Wafula assured them that he was the first person to sit around the pot and he had already put the herb in the malwa. He said he was just waiting for them to start dancing the kadodi, the local Mugisu dance.
When Wafula's colleagues heard this, they pounced on him and started beating him.

Original_Search_Title: Tepeth to Resume Trade With Kenyans
Headline: Uganda: Tepeth to Resume Trade With Kenyans
Body_Text:
Kampala — TRADE between the Matheniko from Kenya and the Tepeth from Uganda is to resume, a district official has said. "Trade in the Karamoja region with their counterparts in Kenya is set to resume following efforts by the Uganda Peoples Defence Forces (UPDF) to disarm the Karimojong," the district's commercial officer, Jefferson Joshua Riisa, said.
Riisa said the trade was interrupted by ambushes and cattle raids, but they had been reduced due to disarmament of the armed groups by the UPDF. "As soon as the guns go out of Karamoja, there will be good business opportunities. We used to trade in hides and skins, cattle, and beads in exchange for sorghum, grains and maize," he said.
"I have written a proposal aimed at resuming the trade, identifying new trading opportunities and sensitising both groups about the need for peace in the region," Riisa said.
"The sensitisation will include enabling these groups to form association and establish small and medium firms," he said. Riisa said the district had formed market access dry lands where traders and producers can exhibit and share experiences.
He hailed the private sector for setting up six village banks that would enable the locals to start saving. The Tepeth live in the Karamoja region. The Government tried to re-stock the cattle, but had failed.

Original_Search_Title: Nile Breweries Aids Jinja Main Hospital
Headline: Uganda: Nile Breweries Aids Jinja Main Hospital
Body_Text:
Jinja — NILE Breweries Ltd has donated Shs12 million to give Jinja Main Hospital a facelift and to re-paint Zebra crossings in the town.
Human Resources Manager Director Kenneth Wanyoto and the Corporate Affairs Manager Onapito Ekomoloit launched the project in Jinja town recently.
Mr Onapito said they found it fitting to give Jinja a facelift as the town celebrates 100 years of existence.
He urged the contractors, Ms Baliddawa Construction, to do a good job within the stipulated seven days.
Mr Wanyoto said this was their way of paying back to the community. He said the painting of the seven Zebra crossings would enhance road safety in the town.
Meanwhile, the Managing Director Mr Nick Jenkinson said the sales volumes for the company had increased by 12 per cent.
He was speaking at the workers party at Cool Breeze Hotel shortly after the launch of the social corporate responsibility project.
He said 10,000 tonnes of sorghum worth Shs3 billion of earnings to Ugandans were produced in the past one year.
"I am proud, we are locally sufficient," he said.
Mr Jenkinson said their major challenge was consolidating market leadership over the next 12 months.

Original_Search_Title: Puzzling Pokot
Headline: Uganda: Puzzling Pokot
Body_Text:
Kampala — When a pokot man gets a wife, she is expected to build him a hut. that was not the only odd thing Elvina Nawaguna discovered on a visit to Pokot land
A long ride on dusty roads, peopled with half-dressed folks walking quietly, leads to Alakas County, where the Pokot people live.I was travelling to the area to take shots of people suffering from a neglected tropical disease called Kalazar, a disease that only the Pokot in Uganda suffer from.
Except for the Kadam hills which can be seen in the distance, the area is flat and mostly dry and sandy with a lot of cracks, gullies and cone-shaped anthills. The vegetation is mainly short trees and shrubs, so one can see a long way off. Under the shade of a short tree, four old men sprawl lazily, their heads resting on small wooden stools. This is how the old Pokot men spend their day as the young men are away grazing cattle.
The women and girls, dressed in colourful clothes and even more colourful hair, neck and wrist beads spend the afternoon plaiting each other's hair, or simply gossiping. When any passing pick-up truck with room on the back stops, the men and some of the women, without any particular mission or permission from the vehicle's owner, hop onto the back. Where are they going? Nowhere in particular; wherever the car goes.
Along the way, they jump off at one stop or another and start walking back home. It is common to find lone men or a group of them walking miles, walking stick in one hand and stool locally called kaideke in the other.
Apparently the walking sticks make the distance shorter. The Pokot are a small tribe in an area about 16km from Amudat, in Nakapiripirit District in northeastern Uganda. According to the Pokot Constituency Member of Parliament, Francis Adamson Kiyonga, there are about 75,000 Pokot in Uganda.
The Pokot are related to the Pokot and Kalenjin of Kenya and the Sebei of eastern Uganda. Kiyonga says the Pokot are Nilo-Hamites who trace their roots in Ethiopia. As pastoralists, the thing the Pokot care about most is their cattle - tany.
Each Pokot believes that all the cows in the world belong to him. They frequently raid one another's villages for cattle, often using force and leaving some people dead. This leads to counter-raids. One would think that they raid cattle for its monetary value.
On the contrary, they amass the cattle for prestige. Middle-aged Toyin, who has over 15 camels and several head of cattle says they just keep collecting the cattle. "You never sell or kill one unless it is sick, or unless someone in the home is very ill and the only way to pay the hospital bills is to sell a cow," he says. The cattle are used to pay bride price as well.
The richer Pokot keep camels, which they only give away as bride price. Toyin is considered one of the rich men of the area. "I give 30 camels and many cows for my sons' wives," he says.
Camels survive in the dry Pokot area because they can go several days without drinking water. Because of the dryness of the land, the boys walk as far as 30km away with the cattle to find water and grass.
The Pokot also grow food that can survive in dry areas like maize and sorghum, which along with milk - locally called can - make up their staple food. The strangest thing though about these people is their nakedness, particularly among the men.
They seem to be more protective of their walking sticks than their other 'sticks'. The men dress up in a piece of colourful material - lesen - draped around their bodies and knotted at the shoulder. As they walk, the lesen moves from side to side making embarrassing revelations.
They seem to be unbothered. One elderly man, for example, puts his stool down and sits with his legs wide apart in front of me. As he talks to me, one half of me is tempted to keep looking while the other is in shock at the display.
One of the men under the tree is 60-year-old Chodomoi. He has several wives and an even bigger kraal of children. This does not stop him from pulling aside my guide and translator, Andrew Ocen, to ask for my hand in marriage.
When I figure out what he wants, Chodomoi starts to get shy. Pokot men are allowed to marry as many women as they want. For a Pokot man to marry a girl he has to wrestle her to the ground. "If she wins, you have lost. Some of the girls are very strong," explains Ocen, a community worker who has a Pokot mother and has lived in the area all his life.
From the time the girls are circumcised, between the ages of 12 and 14, they have 'signed an agreement' that they are ready for marriage. From this time on, the girls can start wearing neck rings called karn.
Ocen explains that if a boy is interested in a certain girl, he will put a karn around her neck as a way of 'befriending' her.
If the girl already has a 'boyfriend' she may refuse the beads. If she likes the boy, she will put a bangle on his right wrist.
Ocen, however, explains that if the girl refuses, the boy may grab her by force. He and his peers waylay her on her way from the well and hold her down so he can have sex with her or take her to his home. When the girl's parents hear about this, they send a procession to demand a certain number of cows and the girl is officially taken.
The boys too cannot get married until they are circumcised. This happens every two to three years when there is a sizeable group of boys aged 14 to 16. "After the circumcision ceremony, you go and live in the bush for one month, with special men to take care of you until you heal," Ocen reveals.
After the healing, the boys are let loose; this is when the process of seducing or grabbing a wife starts. "When you bring a woman into the home, she is taken to the boy's mother's house and later in the night, they take her to a room for the boy to have sex with her," Ocen translates Chodomoi's explanation. Unlike in most Ugandan tribes, the Pokot boys do not build houses.
After getting a wife and bringing her home, it is her job to build a hut for her and her husband. "After all you have paid a lot of cows for her," one middle-aged man among the group that has gathered around adds.
The huts, with conical roofs, are made from mud. "When it rains, the women climb up to the top of the huts to restructure the mud roofs," Ocen narrates. The huts have no doors.
This, they say, is for security reasons. Kiyonga says when there are raids it is easy to come out and run for your life. "You should be able to run away without struggling to open the door," he explains. Some of the houses are 'storeyed', standing on strong wood, with a ladder leading to the entrance.
Because the houses have no doors they are open to anyone all day. "When a visitor comes and finds a gourd of milk inside the house, he can drink it and tie a certain type of grass around it to signify that he was here and he was hungry," says Chepseker, whose home we visited. Inside the huts are two beds made from mud and attached to the floor.
These are meant for the man and his wife, who can only have sex at night. "During the day time, it's an a bad omen (to have sex)," Ocen explains. The Pokot men despise educated girls and believe that they are prostitutes. "Those are not our girls.
They are just useless!" Chemongin, an elderly man comments as he spits in disgust. The Pokot don't celebrate the arrival of a new life or mourn a death. Children are named according to their clans.
Unlike most Ugandan tribes where people even fight for the dead bodies of their loved ones, there are no graves in Pokot. When someone dies, they dump the body in the bushes for the hyenas to feed on. In some cases, if the deceased is an old man who had a home, he may be buried in the middle of his homestead.
If someone commits suicide, it is considered a bad omen, so the family leaves the dead one wherever he or she committed suicide and migrates. In case of murder, the accused is liable to a fine called lapai.
This involves payment of 100 cows to the victim's family. However this crime does not end with just that fine. "If you kill, they come and kill and plunder all your animals and property," Ocen explains.
