He says even after paying the 100 cattle, the victim's family may keep coming back to demand more cattle for four or five years. Chodomoi follows us everywhere as Ocen takes me around the village. Even though I am clearly educated and 'different', Chodomoi insists that he wants to marry me.
He invites me to his home to see his collection of animals from which he would pick some to send to my father. As we walk to his home, a pick-up truck belonging to a district office stops.
That is the last I see of my husband-to-be as he hops onto the back of the truck for a ride to nowhere.
Quote
You never sell or kill a cow unless it is sick or unless someone in the home is very ill and the only way to pay the hospital bills is to sell a cow

Original_Search_Title: Ineeleng Strives for Full Flowering of Her Career
Headline: Botswana: Ineeleng Strives for Full Flowering of Her Career
Body_Text:
Years ago Ineeleng Kavindama was a household name in the fashion industry, only to be followed by a lull. As many wondered what had become of the haute couture designer she was busy honing her other creative side - this time in flower arranging.
Mmegi caught up with the woman who once made headlines in South Africa before returning home in 2001.
Kavindama, who has recently opened De-flower shop, hopes to promote culture through silk flower arrangements fusing them with Setswana curios.
The flowers that are made of silk are placed in dikika, metal buckets, dinkgo, wood and decorates with sorghum grain and letlhodi. She uses bamboos and traditional baskets as decors.
She said with different players in the market, she wanted her flower arrangements to be different and carried out a market research and tapped in where there was a loophole. Her target market varies from grass root to tourists to corporate and people having different functions. Over the weekend they have fresh flowers arrangements. She said one could either hire, lay-bye or pay in cash.
She said OK Foods at Westgate Mall, had agreed to give her space to display her wares. Kavindama said she and her two employees want to bring romance into people's lives. "Batswana have a mentality that flowers are a 'white man's thing' but flowers have always been there at celebrations, weddings and funerals.
Sending flowers should be a form of appreciation any time - not just on St Valentine's Day - especially when one is sick in hospital or at funerals," she said.
"Life is too short, so why don't you say it with flowers?" Kavindama said no one should be embarrassed to buy flowers or show emotions to family members, colleagues or their partners.
"We do deliveries to the doorstep of your loved or wronged ones," she added.
Asked about her fashion designing, she said she felt the need to diversify.
"I am a qualified fashion designer and have tried my hand at being the fashion police (freelance writing). You never know your forte until you find out and hope that it works out....do not quit," she emphasised.
She added that diversifying would help the economy in terms of job creation. She said that is why the United States of America (US) is a world power because people do two to three jobs at a time.
Not only is her interest directed to flower arranging, she also wants to start a business in tourism. She says her company, Okavango Island Safari, will operate as a tour operator.
"I am from the delta (Okavango), have lived in a bamboo built house, have eaten the food and have enough knowledge to run this business".
Her interest is to position herself for the 2010 World Cup in South Africa.
Kavindama, who is determined to make it, says it is her six-year-old daughter Ditiro who inspires her to do all these. "It is the thought of her not having anything to eat or not being able to go to school that makes me stay up late at night," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Chicken Thieves Convicted
Headline: Botswana: Chicken Thieves Convicted
Body_Text:
Two sisters of Makakatlela ward in Mochudi were last week Friday brought before Kgosi John Pone of Mochudi Customary court charged with stealing eight chickens.
A statement read in court said that on December 12, last year, Malebogo, 30 and her younger sister, Ziphora Kgwefane, 25, stole eight of their landlord's chickens worth P 200.
Testifying on behalf of the landlord, a 15- year-old girl who lives with the accused sisters described how for several days she came home from school to a meal that included chicken.
She told the court that on three occasions either Malebogo or Ziphora instructed her to catch the landlord's chickens and slaughter them for supper.
She further said that she was warned not to tell a soul about the chickens. Giving a description of how the chickens were caught, the girl said she would lure them by feeding them sorghum under a big metal bowl with a string attached to it.
Once the chickens were under the bowl, she would pull the string for the bowl to fall and trap the chickens. The customary court president said that the statement was believable and found the accused guilty of theft.
The Kgwefanes pleaded with the court not to send them to prison but to fine them instead.
Malebogo, who committed a similar crime in 2005, was fined P100 while Ziphora; a first offender was fined P60 payable within 14 days.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Rebels Removed from Kitgum
Headline: Uganda: LRA Rebels Repulsed From Kitgum
Body_Text:
Kampala — A group of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels that was found stealing food in Ngomoromo internally displaced people's (IDP) camp in Kitgum, has been forced back into Sudan by the UPDF.
A local official said about 30 rebels were involved.
"They tried to steal simsim, sorghum, groundnuts, cowpeas and other foods from our gardens. They entered Uganda through Lelabul which is about two miles from Ngomoromo."
According to the source, the camp has more than 2,500 residents. many of them had returned from Pajok, Poge and other areas in South Sudan, in response to the Government's appeal for people to return home.
The 4th division commander, Brig. George Etyang, urged the LRA to respect the Cessation of Hostilities Agreement. "they have violated the arrangement. We shall not treat this lightly.
"They have left food rotting in Owiny Ki-Bul because Kony told them that it is poisoned."
Meanwhile, the Sudan Tribune reported on Friday that the LRA looted the Western Equatorial State and abducted some people.
However, reports indicate that the abductees were freed before the rebels crossed into DR Congo. The area governors have threatened to forcefully evict the rebels.
Meanwhile, the UN has evacuated its staff from Tambura town in Western Equatorial province to Juba, after learning that the rebels were heading there.

Original_Search_Title: Beware of Cholera, Returnees Told
Headline: Rwanda: Beware of Cholera, Returnees Told
Body_Text:
Tabagwe — A sector official in Tabagwe, Nyagatare District has called on families of returnees to improve their hygiene to avoid catching cholera, clean by, among other ways, constructing temporary pit latrines. which has recently claimed several lives in the sector.
John Mujarugamba who is in charge of social affairs at Tabagwe Sector, told Rwandans who returned from Tanzania on Saturday that they should keep the environment around them
He was speaking after the monthly community clean-up exercise (Umuganda), which was carried out at the returnees' camp.
The sector official also told the returnees to feel at home, saying they should equally enjoy their country the same way other Rwandans do.
"Take heart and feel at home because Rwanda is your motherland," he said, adding that the government plans to help them construct permanent houses in the near future.
A returnee, who only identified himself as Rukundo, told The New Times that the area lacks clean water, saying that they (returnees) only obtained water from a valley dam. This, he said, puts them and other residents at the risk of acquiring cholera.
He however said that the returnees had been treated with kindness by the area local leaders and residents.
The sector residents gave donations to the returnees worth three tonnes of foodstuffs comprising beans, maize and sorghum. They also gave them over 100 litres of milk and constructed over 30 huts for them during the Umuganda exercise.
Speaking to The New Times, a local leader praised the residents saying they were generous to the returnees.
"The residents of Nyabitekeri Cell have been supportive to the returnees from the first day of their arrival. They brought them foodstuffs and helped them to put up over 30 huts, Nyabitekeri Cell Executive Secretary, Sam Baguma, said.
He also said that at least 20 children of the returnees had already joined various primary schools in the area, 12 in nursery schools and five joined secondary schools in different areas.
Recently, Nyabiteri Cell received over 100 families of returnees comprising 264 people from Matimba Transit Camp
The returnees are among hundreds of Rwandans expelled from Tanzania since last year.

Original_Search_Title: France-Africa Summit - So Much Ado About Little?
Headline: Africa: France-Africa Summit - So Much Ado About Little?
Body_Text:
Harare — The difference between Berlin in 1884 and Cannes in 2007 is that at Cannes, Africans were there! No longer a terra incognita that it was to imperialist "statesman" of 1884, this time around Africa sat around the French hearth, happy to hear a refrain from French President Jacques Chirac: "I love Africa, its lands, peoples and cultures."
It was an eminently fatherly message which no doubt sank scented and sweet into the habitually heavy hearts of Africa's 30 or so leaders who took the trouble to gather in the Riviera resort. I can almost visualise the wince and flinch on President Mugabe, should he have been present to hear a western leader speak of deep love for Africa, deep love for "its land."
More than anyone else, he knows how such profound western love has been lethal, coiling around and squeezing Africa's windpipe, in the process throttling it to its last. He, more than any of the leaders in Riviera, knows what wars and woes are needed to break that stranglehold. Fortunately he was not there to be part of that leadership, privileged enough to throng and mob the French President.
Chirac's farewell, France's farewell.
This was Chirac's farewell to leaders of Africa, arguably France's farewell to Africa in more ways than can be enumerated. After 12 years at the helm, after losing so many gallons of the French voter affection, Chirac is definitively giving the Palace his back, risking turning into a lump of politically tasteless salt should he deign look back.
And as he sprints out of political view, the man sprinting in, one Nicolas Sarkozy -- himself a birth compatriot of George Soros -- is belligerently anti-immigrants that he once was long back when his family left Hungary. It sounds like an attack on one's origins. But then who cares, now that we all are global citizens?
Or so we are told. In France, immigrants are on a sliding scale of acceptability, with those from Africa regarded as the least attractive, the most insufferable. Sarkozy rides to the Palace on the crest of French fear of the black and brown peril.
Once inaugurated, he will waste no time in slamming the big French door to all ye Africans that seek to enter. I wonder whether African leaders in Riviera heard the creaking hinges of the French door on a neat closing swing.
No French umpire
But much more. Chirac's last days saw France trying a rough copy of Blairite re-colonial activism on the continent. In Ivory Coast, where such an experiment drew a bit of French blood, and lots of love loss with Mbeki's South Africa.
In Rwanda, where the French judiciary committed monumental indiscretion by seeking a vicarious indictment of Paul Kagame, the unforgiving President of Rwanda. All this has not been lost upon the dark continent.
Beyond this, the effeteness of French diplomacy and military has also been much in evidence, spectacularly in Chad and Central African Republic where insurgency and conflict with Sudan have gone on without a metropolitan umpire. This does not do good to France's urge to grandstand internationally.
Just as Iraq has dented USA and UK claims to world dominance, France has been suffering the same in a typical quiet, demure and urbane fashion consistent with the mores of the beau monde. To complete it all, Alliance Francaise, France's cultural arm, has been struggling to fend off forays from the British Council, its British rival. In a very major way, the valediction in Riviera was as much to African leaders as it was to Africa.
When 30 equals 1
I said this time Africa was present. Maybe some will view this as a huge departure from Berlin in 1884. I don't, I won't. The leadership of Africa of the 19th century had at least had a compelling honourable alibi. They were victims of a European imperialist conspiracy, one hatched when they were away, possibly fighting tribal wars, hunting elephants, or hey, visiting freshly acquired fleshy concubines.
Whatever reason, however ignoble, at least they were not there to witness or participate in the raping and cutting up of the continent which was soon to follow. The generation that went to Cannes does not have the pleasure of such an alibi, and comes back to face very hard questions. Is it really proper for 30 leaders from as many African countries to attend to one European Head of State who barely visits the continent?
Does this suggest a healthy scale of things? And over a theme so clearly revealing of France and Europe's anxiety of possibly losing Africa's resources? And all this against successive France-Africa Summits which have not yielded a dime for the continent? And all this, all this with the present political prospect in France?
China versus France
I hear you gentle reader. You ask: why did you not make the same points in respect of the China-Africa Summit of last year. I did not. I will not. More than a force of commercial relations, China is a vector of development on the continent. Even at a commercial level, China's emphasis is on joint ventures yielding a win-win outcome.
But what is more, Chinese leadership is humble enough to requite Africa's visit, in the process consolidating the image of equal peers. Against the background of Biaritz, of Paris itself, of Mali, someone still has to quantify for me the gains that flow from such French Summits, gains to Africa that is.
Honey from a fly
Bitter grapes, you will probably say. Why did President Mugabe attend previous such Summits when it is clear nothing comes from them, outside humiliation of the continent? Let one thing be very clear: whether in the past or now, Zimbabwe never viewed France-Africa Summit platform for what it was intended by mandarins of the French government.
Zimbabwe sought to exploit it for its own national gain. Paris was useful to Zimbabwe to poke holes into Britain's European diplomacy. It did and did it so well that Blair writhed in agony.
The conditional invite, which Zimbabwe got from France, is the comeback of British diplomacy. Everyone in the Zimbabwean Foreign Ministry knew the French would not hold out indefinitely. Indeed France crumbled in 2003, when Blair and Chirac harmonised positions on Zimbabwe, less because France's attitude to Zimbabwe has changed, but more because France's European diplomatic needs have risen. No one ever expected France to stick her neck out for Zimbabwe.
And as Zimbabwe interacted with the French leadership to persuade it to take moral leadership on the Zimbabwe debate in the European Union, it became clear France's power and influence in the EU was far less than that of Britain, the same way it is so globally.
Zimbabwe also learnt that there was -- there is no preparedness in the entire Europe -- to humiliate Britain, or have Britain humiliated by a non-white, non-European player that Zimbabwe is.
All European nations -- Britain included -- know fully well that the tiff between Zimbabwe and Britain is bilateral; know that in terms of all benchmarks of material democracy, Zimbabwe is a nonesuch on the continent; indeed know that even in the present deep recession, Zimbabwe is well above her peers across all measurements of human welfare.
But Zimbabwe -- black Zimbabwe under Robert Mugabe -- must not humiliate Britain. It could only be UDI; it could only be white Ian Smith; it could only have been in 1965. This is the pact which binds imperial Europe and over which EU acts in unison.
Mali Trojan Horse
Mali was crucial to Zimbabwe in so far as Zimbabwe was able to delay Annan's ill-fated and ill-conceived proposed visit to Zimbabwe. It was in the Malian capital, well away from the France-Africa Summit, that the Zimbabwean delegation met with Gambari, and prepared for the burial in Banjul of Annan's intended visit which would have hurt the country quite badly. The fan-fire and bonfire around France and Africa was a very good Trojan Horse.
Decline and delegate
Well before December, well before former President Chissano's visit as an emissary of the pleading French Government, the Zimbabwean Government had already taken a position regarding that Summit. It had long got whiff of French buckling in the furnace of British diplomacy.
The contents of the French emissary were long known before he came, and interestingly, Chissano did not expect to succeed in getting Harare to vindicate French's quiescent diplomacy vis-à-vis the British, which vindication translated into a plea to the Zimbabwean leader to decline and delegate the invite.
What Harare feared was for Britain to use Cannes for kite-flying for the EU-Africa Summit. And Zimbabwe's diplomacy was not directed at getting the French to change heart and snub Albion; rather, it was directed at ensuring Cannes would not pass for a precedent for anything to follow.
If it did, especially in respect of the long-stalled Europe-Africa Summit, Africa would have helped unite EU in support of Britain in her fight against Zimbabwe. Which would mean Africa dividing itself for the sake of British EU policy against Zimbabwe.
It was also important to make sure Portugal was clear the EU-Africa Summit was not a bilateral matter the same way France wrongly made her Cannes Summit an EU matter. Once both objectives had been met, Cannes became inconsequential. Indeed, the Summit itself turned out to be little else beyond Chirac's bye bye, and Sarkozy's undelivered hello to Africa.
Harnessing resources for diplomacy
Countries are resource-rich or resource-poor. That is the bottom-line around which all else is built or follows. That includes a country's degree of integration and engagement, the obverse of which is a country's level of isolation. Beyond overdrawn humanitarianism, poor countries tend to be neglected, isolated and forgotten, tend to have fewer and fewer friends. After all, befriending them is a cost for the developed, knowing or getting engaged with them amount to an immolating wish for a burden, a wish for a millstone.
From such a standpoint, it is not difficult for one to understand why I have never had to worry about the efficacy of mouth diplomacy in engaging the rest of the world. With its fabulous resource base -- and one gets the sense that it is only in Zimbabwe that new, precious minerals grow -- Zimbabwe is needed, is courted like some plum damsel.
I remember that in 2004-5 when Zimbabwe had her latest mineral find -- uranium -- Howard, the Prime Minister of Australia simply forgot his tiff with Mugabe, the President of Zimbabwe, to make desperate proposals to this nubile girl called Zimbabwe.
Hard on the heels of the Aussies were the British, the Americans, the Canadians, the Germans, the... ah I just cannot exhaust the list. Zimbabwe, like a woman quite aware of her much sought-after endowments, simply played the flirt, played hard to get, to this day!
Ngoda, uranium and others
Then came ngoda -- diamonds in the language of the uneducated. Again, sanctions were forgotten, the country meant to be reviled and shunned simply becoming the cynosure, a real focus of intense, frantic courtship.
As with uranium, Zimbabwe will not yield too quickly, although not always appreciating the need to keep her good legs tight and together, to keep suitors interested. I will not retell the story of gold, of platinum, of chrome and many other such vital metals.
I shall not speak of the fabulous land, the flora, the fauna which both the British and the Germans so angry with, and yet so close to and entangled with this country. Resource-hungry China has simply docked here, served in good measure by her historical role as a benefactor in our hard fight for freedom. The Russians, it is the same story underlining how resources simply mould and melt attitudes, however hardened.
Searching for night at sunset
What Zimbabwe has not always done is to count her power -- take stock of her vast leverage against the rest of the world -- to beat sanctions, to build friendships. Often, the people kicking us in the mouth are the same people fastened on her fecund breasts, suckling fat, buckling her frame. She cannot bring discomfort to enemies, bring comfort to friends.
And this failure starts with her inability to shape behaviours of her entrepreneurs, particularly those in leadership, many of whom end up cutting business deals with her enemies. And this failure starts with her inability to shape decisions of Government agencies, many of which simply fail to consolidate budding friendships. It is something the Cubans do very well, and it pays to take a leaf from Castro, my hero.
Tribute to Billy Saidi
Whether I like it or not, Billy Saidi's piece on this column, or more accurately, on the hand he thinks is behind this column, was just well done. I do not care what hard points he made against the column, the old goat wrote well and knows how to masterly kill a man's soul.
It was like Okonkwo roaring: "This meeting is for man!" With my tail wedged between my hinds, I limped away, whelp in mouth. The message was clear: I am too young, too junior to peck the horns of an old bull.
I hear you Bill and promise good behaviour, here and after. But tarry thee old bull. The profound lesson you gave on deferring and doffing to age, surely relates to others in or before your advanced category? Does it not, Sir? In 1957 you were 20, which mathematically suggests you would have made your entry cry about 1937.
Correct? In this demesne already existed someone who was already 13 at your birth. The trouble with age is that no matter how good you are, how clever you are, you can never overtake those that came before you. It ordains your place immutably. That someone is Robert Mugabe, who happens to be the leader of Zanu-PF; who happens to be your President.
Is it too much to ask you to accord him just an ounce of the pound of respect you demand of me? Zvobva zvaitaka muchechetere nhai sekuru? But beyond that, I also wonder why you allow the soundness of your age to give way to the dash of editorial impulse. Why again you fail - both by deed and by word - to guide the little ones who continue to mistake the stem of sorghum for sweet soft cane.
Surely you have learnt much from the proverbial crab that will not understand why its grandchildren move sideways. Just moving straight editorially brings about profound change into the industry you joined way back in 1957. Will you, Bill? Icho! - nathaniel.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Drop Tax Cut Proposal
Headline: Uganda: MPs Drop Tax Cut Proposal
Body_Text:
Kampala — PARLIAMENT has dropped a proposal to lower taxes on Ugandan beer, cement, telephone landlines and payphones.
The proposal was contained in the revised Excise Tariff (Amendment) Bill 2006 which the President refused to approve.
President Yoweri Museveni returned the Bill to Parliament in November 2006 for reconsideration.
The president's argument was that it would lead to a revenue loss of sh13.1b, money badly needed for Universal Secondary Education and CHOGM.
State minister for finance Fred Omach communicated that message to Parliament on Thursday during the reconsideration of the Bill.
He explained that reducing the taxes on these items would lead to budget deficits. He pointed out that lowering the duties on telephone landlines and payphones would create a revenue loss of sh1.2b, for local beer sh4.3b and cement sh7.6b.
The majority of the members of Parliamentary Committee on finance accepted the argument.
However, a minority of MPs on the committee disagreed.
They argued that the proposed taxes, particularly on cement, would disadvantage the poor communities.
They observed that it would undermine local production efforts, especially sorghum which is used to Ugandan beer.
They also argued that it would jeopardise efforts to move towards the information age.
MPs; Albert Oduman Okello (Bukedea), Nandala Mafabi (Budadiri West) and Matia Nsubuga (Bukoto South), said communication is an engine in the struggle for poverty eradication, especially among the rural population.

Original_Search_Title: Curtains Come Down for Wazees
Headline: Kenya: Curtains Come Down for Wazees
Body_Text:
Nairobi — IN an apparent generational shift, voters forced ageing politicians to retirement - some who were high-ranking Government officials in the Ninth Parliament.
Vice President and outgoing Funyula MP, Mr Moody Awori leads the pack of leaders who made up President Kibaki's private club of elders. The others are Cabinet ministers, Mr Simeon Nyachae (Roads and Public Works), Mr Njenga Karume (Defence) and Mr Joseph Munyao Konzolo (Livestock).

Original_Search_Title: Poll Results Set Stage for Major Changes
Headline: Kenya: Poll Results Set Stage for Major Changes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A battle of supremacy looms in Nyanza and Western provinces after most of the political heavyweights were swept aside in Thursday's General Election.
Top on the casualty list were Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo, Vice President Moody Awori and Trade and Industry minister Mukhisa Kituyi.

Original_Search_Title: ODM's Bumpy Ride to National Limelight
Headline: Kenya: ODM's Bumpy Ride to National Limelight
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When the Electoral Commission of Kenya selected the orange as a symbol for those opposed to the draft constitution during the 2005 referendum, little did Kenyans know that the juicy fruit would one day be associated with the most popular party in the country.
ECK chairman Mr Samuel Kivuitu also chose a banana as a symbol for the proponents of new document.

Original_Search_Title: New Shoes, Now Let's Step On Some Toes
Headline: Uganda: New Shoes, Now Let's Step On Some Toes
Body_Text:
My buddy admires journalists and columnists greatly. It has nothing to do with breaking stories, rather, their rich imagination.
"Half of what scribes write is made up," he says, hopefully not alluding to Wang. "A scribe can find himself with nothing to write, and he decides to write about his shoes!"

Original_Search_Title: 4 Die As Fires Hit City
Headline: Uganda: 4 Die As Fires Hit City
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOUR people died in the city on Christmas Day that also registered several fire outbreaks.
Two of the deaths are being considered by the Police as murders, one from a road accident and the other an assault.

Original_Search_Title: South-East Senators in New Song
Headline: Nigeria: South-East Senators in New Song
Body_Text:
Lagos — For the south east senators, the inauguration of the sixth National Assembly was indeed a new beginning in the over eight years history of the caucus.
And for most of the lawmakers, it was time to emphasize only those things that would further unite the caucus rather than that which would divide them as a people.

Original_Search_Title: Country Will Lead the Emerging Markets in IP Explosion - Intel Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Country Will Lead the Emerging Markets in IP Explosion - Intel Boss
Body_Text:
Lagos — The importance of Nigeria to the major IT players came to the fore recently when Intel Chairman, Mr. Craig Barrett visited the country. With earnings of over $35bn, Intel a global semi conductor leader is digging into the Nigerian market by way of being a major beneficiary when the IT market explodes.
And that is already happening, according to Barrett. He spared some time to speak to Vanguard's Asst Editor, Communications, Okoh Aihe. The stuff will make a fascinating read.

Original_Search_Title: Iwu's Indefensible Grandstanding
Headline: Nigeria: Iwu's Indefensible Grandstanding
Body_Text:
Abuja — Court jesters carry out their duty with every sense of seriousness when they know that nobody is taking them serious. Jesters are naturally low bred, incapable of appreciating how unserious they are taken. Clowns are more pitiable category.
They go about with an impudence to impress their principal. They are more despicable than jesters because they usually mistake foolery with wisdom. They elevate grandstanding to an art. Naturally they stand for nothing but pretend to stand for everything. Their capacity to provoke and annoy is limitless. Serious nations relegate them in public life to the margins of theatrics, where they can relieve serious moment with comical amusement.

Original_Search_Title: Family Suicide Bid Nipped in the Bud
Headline: Kenya: Family Suicide Bid Nipped in the Bud
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The family of Mzee Patrick Ngugi Karuri was well aware of Kikuyu traditional norms when they began digging graves in their compound on Wednesday last week in readiness for "mass suicide".
But they were not scared of what would otherwise befall them since customary tenets dictate against such an action as it is believed to be a sure way of inviting calamity to the home.

Original_Search_Title: A Government of Many Parties
Headline: Kenya: A Government of Many Parties
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Had these been normal times, Kenyans would be going to the polls on Thursday to decide whether to renew the mandate of the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc), the party they elected in 2002, and that of the presidential candidate Mwai Kibaki.
But the last five years have been abnormal times that have left a record of sorts.

Original_Search_Title: To Mt Kamwala, Where Beer Tastes So Different
Headline: Tanzania: To Mt Kamwala, Where Beer Tastes So Different
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — The end of the year is here. It is time to think of what you will do with all the holidays ahead. As the reader is going through this, Wa Muyanza is on a bus the ticket for which he paid the earth heading for the slopes of Mount Kamwala.
It happens every year, like the clockwork. Come December and the kande eaters, like their watani', i.e. the banana eaters from the slopes of Mount Kilimanjaro, troop to their ancestral homes like their very lives depended on it.

Original_Search_Title: Country Imports Frw48 Billion Ugandan Goods
Headline: Rwanda: Country Imports Frw48 Billion Ugandan Goods
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda has imported goods from Uganda worth Frw48 billion this year compared to the Frw30.6 billion last year, statistics from Rwanda Revenue Authority indicates.
This is an increase of about Frw18 billion. The goods imported from Uganda include meat, fish, milk, fruits, vegetables, bananas, cereals, tea, sugar, water (mineral and aerated) and spices.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni's Address to NRM Caucus
Headline: Uganda: Museveni's Address to NRM Caucus
Body_Text:
Statement by His Excellency Yoweri Kaguta Museveni
President of the Republic of Uganda / Chairman of NRM

Original_Search_Title: Musicians Prove Crucial in Current Election
Headline: Kenya: Political Songs Windfall
Body_Text:
Nairobi — DNA, Jua Cali, Prezzo, Wyre, Clemo and Homeboyz are among musicians and producers earning millions of shillings from political songs that are being remixed for campaign rallies.
Five years after the 2002 General Elections, the then popular song Unbwogable is long forgotten, but the trend of politicians and political parties seeking the services of our musicians is still here to stay.

Original_Search_Title: Forget the Mudslinging And the Cash, Vote Responsibly
Headline: Kenya: Forget the Mudslinging And the Cash, Vote Responsibly
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I've always thought it a sad day for the trade when advertisements eclipse the editorial in a newspaper. And so there I was yesterday, barely registering the last gasp propaganda-pretending-to-be-stories that politicians have been throwing at us in the hope that we would have a change of heart when it is just the ballot box and us.
I've made up my mind. I know what I want and I am going for it. Spare me the theatrics, thank you!

Original_Search_Title: Country Imports Frw 48bn Ugandan Goods
Headline: Rwanda: Country Imports Frw 48bn Ugandan Goods
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda has imported goods from Uganda worth Frw48 billion this year compared to the Frw30.6 billion last year, statistics from Rwanda Revenue Authority indicates.
This is an increase of about Frw18 billion. The goods imported from Uganda include meat, fish, milk, fruits, vegetables, bananas, cereals, tea, sugar, water (mineral and aerated) and spices.

Original_Search_Title: Tuju Treads Cautiously to Weather ODM Wave
Headline: Kenya: Tuju Treads Cautiously to Weather ODM Wave
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Faced with a tricky assignment of marketing President Kibaki in an ODM stronghold, Cabinet minister, Mr Raphael Tuju, is treading cautiously.
In a carefully crafted strategy to survive the ODM wave in Rarieda constituency, Tuju is avoiding public rallies that could put him at loggerheads with ODM supporters.

Original_Search_Title: Lodge is the Only Source of Hope
Headline: South Africa: Lodge is the Only Source of Hope
Body_Text:
Bulungula Lodge offers a sustainable way of life within a tight community.
David Martin doesn't do anything in half measures -- whether it's helping his Eastern Cape community to get the local school running or putting pressure on local government to commit itself to service delivery.

Original_Search_Title: We Love Our City in the Garbage Because It's the Way of the Future
Headline: Uganda: We Love Our City in the Garbage Because It's the Way of the Future
Body_Text:
Barely two weeks after the Commonwealth summit in Kampala, the expected has happened - uncollected garbage has began to pile up in parts of the city.
Typically, as happens in most cities that are cleaned up for big international events, very soon, the street children, pickpockets, potholes and broken traffic lights will be the order of the day again.

Original_Search_Title: 17-Yr-Old Girl Gang-Raped, Murdered
Headline: Uganda: 17-Yr-Old Girl Gang-Raped, Murdered
Body_Text:
Kampala — SHOCK gripped residents of Nangabo sub-county, Wakiso district, following the gruesome murder of a 17-year-old girl.
Bernardette Nantongo Ssenyama, a Senior Six student of Nnalinya Lwantale Secondary School in Ndejje, Luweero district, had gone to the area to attend a friend's introduction ceremony on Sunday.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Partnership Agreements And Preferential Treatment
Headline: Mauritius: Economic Partnership Agreements And Preferential Treatment
Body_Text:
Port Louis — In order to allow readers to have a better understanding of the whole issue, it is proposed in this article to put the EPA into context and see how it fits in the overall framework put into place by the WTO.
The new arrangements would be introduced gradually with a preparatory period during which the non-reciprocal preferences under the Lomé IV Convention would be maintained.

Original_Search_Title: Why Mugabe is a Precious Asset for African Leaders
Headline: Zimbabwe: Why Mugabe is a Precious Asset for African Leaders
Body_Text:
It showed courage and solidarity. African leaders drew the line for their European counterparts prior to the European Union-African summit in Lisbon, Portugal recently.
The Africans insisted that despite his 'blemishes;' including a poor governance record, human rights abuses, well documented election thefts and a run-down economy etc, Zimbabwe's autocrat Robert Mugabe (83) would be part of their contingent or else they would stay away. It came to pass.

Original_Search_Title: It's Two New Districts for Gusiiland
Headline: Kenya: It's Two New Districts for Gusiiland
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Mwai Kibaki has created two districts in Gusiiland as he campaigns for his re-election.
The creation of Manga and South Gucha districts brings to eight the number of districts in the area in addition to Kisii, South Kisii, Nyamira, Borabu, Masaba and Gucha.

Original_Search_Title: Country's 'Stepping Stone' Trade Deal With EU May Well Be a Sellout
Headline: Ghana: Country's 'Stepping Stone' Trade Deal With EU May Well Be a Sellout
Body_Text:
Despite all expectations Ghana has signed an interim trade deal to liberalise 80 per cent of its trade with the European Union (EU), which in turn has offered 100 per cent quota-free and duty-free access to Ghanaian goods, except sugar and rice.
The signing followed weeks of negotiations between the two sides in a move to safeguard the country's exports after preferential trade terms expire at the end of this year.

Original_Search_Title: Theologian Mkwakwami Dies At 74
Headline: Zimbabwe: Theologian Mkwakwami Dies At 74
Body_Text:
Harare — Former president of the United Church of Christ in Zimbabwe, Dr Watson Mkwakwami, has died. He was 74.
According to Reverend Edward Matuvhunye, the incumbent UCCZ president, Dr Mkwakwami was actively involved in politics during the pre-independence era and influenced a number of people to cross the border into Mozambique for military training.

Original_Search_Title: 13 Arrested With Bhang Haul
Headline: Kenya: 13 Arrested With Bhang Haul
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police netted bhang worth Sh5 million and arrested 13 suspected traffickers in Nairobi Sunday.
The consignment, packed in 73 gunny bags and concealed as bananas and sugarcane, was packed in a lorry from western Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: Living On Monkey Tricks
Headline: Nigeria: Living On Monkey Tricks
Body_Text:
Sometimes life does not seem to be as hard as it is assumed to be, especially for someone who just performs the task of making a monkey play tricks for a fee. It would then mean that it is the monkey that does the job while its owner smiles to the bank.
That is the synopsis of the story of Musa Isiyaku, from Kofar Ruwa, Kano and his monkey, which he calls 'Chinedu'

Original_Search_Title: Athletics - Stars Head to Kisii for Cross-Country With Sights On Jackpot
Headline: Kenya: Athletics - Stars Head to Kisii for Cross-Country With Sights On Jackpot
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A star-studded field is expected to battle it out for honours in the fifth leg of the KCB Cross Country Championship at the Kisii Golf Course on Saturday.
It will be the last event of the year in the seven-leg series and athletes will be seeking to enhance their hopes of qualifying for the final round scheduled for January 12 in Eldoret.

Original_Search_Title: EU is Using 'Bully Tactics' to Push Through EPAs
Headline: Africa: EU is Using 'Bully Tactics' to Push Through EPAs
Body_Text:
Cape Town — "We succumbed," lamented a diplomat from Namibia. "We signed on the 12th of December. The pressure was too much. The private sector felt that they would be disproportionately affected. In terms of markets, they would be losing access for beef, grapes, fish and fish products."
"The political and economic strength of the European Commission (EC) is in itself a threat and a pressure in the negotiations," he explained on condition of anonymity to IPS.

Original_Search_Title: Why Our Theatre is Struggling
Headline: Uganda: Why Our Theatre is Struggling
Body_Text:
Most people in theatre these days attach little importance to what they do, what they mind about most is popularity. Because of this people have lost trust in theatre
If you have been to the theatre in the recent past, you must have realised the mess in Uganda's oldest form of entertainment. As soon as you enter some of our theatres today you are met by the empty staring seats, old curtains and flood lights (instead of stage lights).

Original_Search_Title: Desert May Soon Swallow the Country
Headline: Uganda: Desert May Soon Swallow the Country
Body_Text:
The saying that Sahara Desert is expanding southwards; deforestation is causing desertification; dry lands are becoming deserts and so on are familiar phrases in the field of natural resource and environment conservation.
When people use them, many of us do not imagine what it looks like for productive land to turn into a desert. Some imagine that the process of desertification takes centuries to be visible.

Original_Search_Title: Murungaru Faces Uphill Task After Loss in Primaries
Headline: Kenya: Murungaru Faces Uphill Task After Loss in Primaries
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In his heyday, Dr Chris Murungaru was a powerful politician in Nyeri, but now it looks like voters could usher him to the periphery.
The former Cabinet minister is fighting to retain the Kieni parliamentary seat. He starts from a disadvantaged position after being humiliated at the PNU nominations, a move that compelled him to seek re-election on a Narc Kenya ticket.

Original_Search_Title: Makaya - He's Seen It All in Music
Headline: Zimbabwe: Makaya - He's Seen It All in Music
Body_Text:
Harare — GUITARIST Timmy Makaya, who was instrumental in the formation of several musical groups and reported to have suffered a mild stroke, has recovered tremendously.
The Highfield-born Makaya can now walk, talk and strum his guitar.

Original_Search_Title: World's Poorest Country
Headline: Sierra Leone: World's Poorest Country
Body_Text:
Freetown — Another of Sunkari Conteh's children is sick. The last/ time one of her children was ill, it died.
Almost three years old, her daughter succumbed to diarrhoea because Conteh couldn't afford to pay a Le 90,000 medical consultation fee.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala's Littered Dumping Culture
Headline: Uganda: Kampala's Littered Dumping Culture
Body_Text:
Kampala — Scenes of mounds of rubbish by the roadside, street children and dogs rummaging through them (provoking a foul stench and the feasting winged predators), and scavenger birds hovering overhead are an everyday sight in this city.
City authorities are wrestling with the nightmare of establishing an effective garbage disposal system. Tonnes of cash have been injected in; employing city rubbish collection services; painting 'Keep Kampala clean' signs; 'delivering as promised' just a handful of dustbins, and, more recently, installing surveillance cameras to monitor those who litter.

Original_Search_Title: EPAs are Not as Dirty as They Have Been Said to Be
Headline: East Africa: EPAs are Not as Dirty as They Have Been Said to Be
Body_Text:
Kampala — Some readers will recall a statement made by the civil society as published in Daily Monitor on November 26. Whereas civil society plays a role in disseminating information about the recently signed Economic Partnership Agreements (EPAs), their statement included seven misrepresentations.
The first untruth is that the "East African Community has not made adequate preparations because the agreement was signed in an "indecent haste of nine days".

Original_Search_Title: Let the Children Play And Exercise
Headline: Uganda: Let the Children Play And Exercise
Body_Text:
Kampala — Children  are a blessing from God. They bring joy to their parents and when they play, they make the home lively. Parents should provide a good environment for their children to play in, to enable them to develop physically, emotionally and spiritually.
Emmanuel Mugerwa, a director at the Coalition Against Women Abuse, says a child who enjoys games will have self-confidence and a sense of self-worth.

Original_Search_Title: God Gave You the Authority After Your City, Use It!
Headline: Uganda: God Gave You the Authority After Your City, Use It!
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA paryers were held at Kololo Airstrip. President Yoweri Kaguta Museveni attended the prayers and gave the following speech.
The Ministers who are here; The Mayor of the city; The Pastors; and All of you ladies and gentlemen;

Original_Search_Title: When Schools Become Mere Robot Factories
Headline: Uganda: When Schools Become Mere Robot Factories
Body_Text:
Kampala — Obviously you never heard of a little school in Ntinda, Kampala, called Marigolds Nursery; near the Ndere Centre, along the Ntinda-Kisaasi Road.
Never mind.

Original_Search_Title: Down Memory Lane - Soccer - Superstition At Play in Big Derby Match
Headline: Kenya: Down Memory Lane - Soccer - Superstition At Play in Big Derby Match
Body_Text:
Gor Mahia's chairman Zack Mbori and his AFC Leopards counterpart Alfred Sambu mutually agreed in the mid-1980s to jointly carry the ball to the centre of the field before the kick-off of the Kenya National Football League match between the two arch-rivals at Nairobi's City Stadium.
This was not because the ball was too heavy for one person to carry, but because of the prevailing superstition and witchcraft which was so rampant in those days.

Original_Search_Title: 'Hiv/Aids is Nothing Compared to Plague'
Headline: Uganda: 'Hiv/Aids is Nothing Compared to Plague'
Body_Text:
We returned from burying a friend in Luweero District crestfallen. He had died of Aids.
But our father, Jeremiah Musisi (RIP), was not taken up with the aura of sorrow around us. That was in the early 1990s when Aids killed many Ugandans, especially those in the productive stages of life.

Original_Search_Title: 4 Feared Dead in Siavonga
Headline: Zambia: 4 Feared Dead in Siavonga
Body_Text:
FOUR people are feared dead after a banana boat they were paddling capsised on Lake Kariba in Siavonga on Tuesday night.
However, two men who were also on the boat survived the accident that occurred around 19:00 hours few metres from the Siavonga Main Harbour.

Original_Search_Title: Clashes to Disrupt Polls, Says Church
Headline: Kenya: Clashes to Disrupt Polls, Says Church
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Voting is unlikely not take place in 14 polling stations in Kuresoi because residents had fled the area, a church agency has said.
A report by the Catholic Justice and Peace Commission, Nakuru branch, says the families that fled were unlikely to return soon because they were traumatised by the fighting in which scores have been killed and more than 200 houses burnt.

Original_Search_Title: Hanky-Panky is Out As Fair Trade Kicks in
Headline: South Africa: Hanky-Panky is Out As Fair Trade Kicks in
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — ASK any sales assistant in the coffee aisle at your local supermarket where you might find Fairtrade products, and you're likely to be greeted with a blank stare.
Try the same in the UK, and you'll be spoilt for choice -- market research has shown that eight out of 10 Britons recognise the Fairtrade label and believe it is an effective means of helping the world's poorest people.

Original_Search_Title: No Hotels in Cold Ishaka
Headline: Uganda: No Hotels in Cold Ishaka
Body_Text:
I can't feel my hands, and they are shaking, terribly. Now this is where I start to look around me; the little boys and girls next to me seem fine and comfortable in their shorts and oversize t-shirts. There are a number of young women (from KIU Western Branch) dressed in three-quarter jeans and size two blouses.
This, after all is a concert night and I must be sick. No one else is feeling as cold as I am and it is not even threatening to rain - the skies are very clear actually, it is a full moon.

Original_Search_Title: Congratulations On a Successful CHOGM
Headline: Uganda: Congratulations On a Successful CHOGM
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE CHOGM that ended on Sunday November 25, 2007, was preceded by Her Majesty the Queen of the United Kingdom and Head of the Commonwealth's State visit to Uganda, which started on Wednesday, November 21, 2007. Both events were successfully held. According to the comments that I kept hearing in our meetings and the expressions of the public in the media - this was the most successful and well-organised CHOGM ever held. Since I have attended many CHOGMs, I can also confirm this.
It was, of course, not difficult for the NRM Government to host such an event given our proven capacity to organise events. There are, however, groups linked to negative forces in Uganda that try to eclipse the time-tested capacity of the NRM to handle big issues, through the newspapers and radios here in Uganda and deceiving foreigners abroad. I, however, had no doubt that we would hold a very successful CHOGM, given our long experience in organisation and the good performance of our economy, the sabotage by the negative elements in the 6th Parliament that delayed Bujagali hydro-power dam notwithstanding.

Original_Search_Title: Country Has to Wait for 2009 Qualifiers
Headline: Uganda: Country Has to Wait for 2009 Qualifiers
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA will have to return to compete in a Division III tournament in 2009 if they are to make it to the ICC World Cup qualifiers. Only the four top teams in the Nambia Division II league earned automatic places to the 2009 ICC World Cup qualifiers in UAE. Uganda finished 5th out of six countries and return home today with their heads bowed down.
Uganda has over a year to try and recover. In January 2009, they will compete with potential banana skins in Papua New Guinea and Cayman Islands, and likely Fiji and USA from Division 4.

Original_Search_Title: Wahapahapa Surfs Airwaves With Unique Messages
Headline: Tanzania: Wahapahapa Surfs Airwaves With Unique Messages
Body_Text:
Arusha — A new exciting and entertaining radio serial drama hits the airwaves on Radio Free Africa on Saturday December 1 2007 at 6:30 p.m.. This new radio series brings the power and passion of the current Tanzanian music scene together with key social issues- unemployment, relationships, women's and youth's hopes, dreams and roles in today's Tanzanian society where HIV touches all of us.
The radio series is made possible through the support from the Tanzanian Ministry of Health and Social Welfare and the United States Agency for International Development through the US President's Emergency Fund for AIDS Relief.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye Got It Wrong By Blaming Colonialism
Headline: Uganda: Besigye Got It Wrong By Blaming Colonialism
Body_Text:
Kampala — APPEARING on a WBS Talk show, and, later, addressing a demonstration rally at Kololo airstrip recently, FDC president Dr. Kizza Besigye accused the British of having looted our continent during the colonial era. In the same spirit, the king of Bunyoro is pursuing compensation for the havoc that, he alleges, the British wrecked on his kingdom.
While their arguments and claims may carry some weight, I think they ignore the other side of the coin. Dr. Besigye should know that whatever sand and bananas the British may have looted, they left us better than they found us. The reason why his allegations can be heard by people with different native dialects is that colonialism forced on us a common language. Because they forced the same language on many other colonies, we can now communicate with a third of the globe without needing an interpreter. And we can directly benefit from the ingenuity of Isaac Newton, and the imagination of George Orwell. If we are sure that colonialism brought no good, shouldn't we try boycotting the language, and speaking exclusively in our native dialects?

Original_Search_Title: Glass House Vows to Foil Ghana's S'Africa Romance
Headline: Africa: Glass House Vows to Foil Ghana's S'Africa Romance
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigeria Football Association (NFA) has assured Nigerians that the Dream Team will not step on banana peel like England's three Lions did at Wembley Stadium against Croatia last Wednesday in their quest for a Beijing 2008 ticket.
The Three Lions only needed a draw in their final game against Croatia to make next year's finals but surprisingly lost 3-2 to exit next year's Euro 2008, to be co-hosted by Austria and Switzerland.

Original_Search_Title: What We Need is Another Half a Planet
Headline: Ethiopia: What We Need is Another Half a Planet
Body_Text:
Why are so many people, especially the business bunch everywhere, so excited about present day China? Mainly because it is home to one billion-plus people who are supposedly waiting to snap up every conceivable goody with a briskly growing economy to give them the buying power to do so.
There are, of course, millions of Chinese who will be left behind, remain poor far into the future, and would be quite delighted if they can satisfy their daily basic needs, leave alone conspicuous consumption. The marketing advertisements, however, continue to sweet-talk everyone to buy and spend more and more, even if it means many of the things advertised are beyond their means. In China especially, foreign business firms keep jostling to have a toe on the beachhead.

Original_Search_Title: Court Acquits Witch Doctor of Murder
Headline: Uganda: Court Acquits Witch Doctor of Murder
Body_Text:
Kasese — HIGH court in Kasese has acquitted a witch doctor of murder charges.
High court Judge Justice Atwooki Rudagya acquited Mr Ernest Bazarwa, 43, a resident of Ndugutu village Kibito sub-county in Kabarole District after prosecution, led by Resident State Attorney A. Sserwada failed to prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused the offence.

Original_Search_Title: The Oversights in Cheeye Story
Headline: Uganda: The Oversights in Cheeye Story
Body_Text:
Reference is made to the article "There is no need for Africa to sign EPA", (Daily Monitor, November 26). Let me thank Mr Cheeye for contributing to the EPA debate.
Increased participation in trade policy debates will; "reduce business community strikes due to non-participation in important trade negotiations such as the ongoing Common Market talks in the African Community and; ensure the negotiators at the Ministry of Tourism, Trade and Industry present pro-private sector positions.

Original_Search_Title: As Money Bag Repeats Claim
Headline: Ghana: As Money Bag Repeats Claim
Body_Text:
With less than 27 days to go to Congress, Mr Alan Kyerematen, 52, former Trade Minister literally buried the competition in an obscene display of cash, pomp and colour at Victoria Park, Saturday, when seven out of the 18 registered contestants jammed the colonial capital for a rally and fund raising dinner dance.
The event organised by Mr Bootie Dankwa Smith, Central Regional Chairman of the New Patriotic Party (NPP) with initial funding from Messrs Nana Awuku and Hon Kennedy Agyepong, began around 10.00 am with supporters of the various aspirants gyrating through the principal streets of this former colonial capital, hoisting placards proclaiming the superiority of their candidates and a beautiful event, free.

Original_Search_Title: Ssali Made the Queen's Bouquet
Headline: Uganda: Ssali Made the Queen's Bouquet
Body_Text:
Kampala — HARRIET Ssali dreamt of making the Queen's bouquet, but it never crossed her mind that it would come to pass.
Early October, Ssali was given the best news ever. She was to make the Queen's posy after she had out-competed the five people in a bouquet arrangement.

Original_Search_Title: Commonwealth Urged to Break Trade Barriers
Headline: Uganda: Commonwealth Urged to Break Trade Barriers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Uganda's President Yoweri Museveni has asked the Commonwealth Business Council (CBC) to help break down trade barriers preventing poor commonwealth member states from exporting value-added goods instead of raw materials.
"We have confined ourselves to the export of raw materials such as coffee, where we have been getting $1 per kilogramme while Nescafe in London is getting $20 per kilogramme," Museveni told delegates. "It is possible for us to break into these markets if we can add value, we need the CBC to help out."

Original_Search_Title: Rising Food Prices Add to Misery in the East
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Rising Food Prices Add to Misery in the East
Body_Text:
Goma — Janine Maombi came to the bustling central market in Goma, the capital of the restive North Kivu province in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, to buy potatoes.
But after learning that the price had again risen, the widowed mother of six slumped on a stool next to the seller and quietly requested a discount.

Original_Search_Title: Country's House Of Evolution
Headline: Nigeria: Country's House Of Evolution
Body_Text:
Few weeks back, this writer was compelled to write a scathing, satirical piece, an apparent reaction to the needless bickering, rancour, acrimony and appalling furore that had engulfed the Nigerian House of Representatives, for several weeks on end and which had stalled the constitutional responsibilities of the lower chamber.
The piece, which was titled, "Nigeria's House of Commotion" was a vivid pointer to the degeneration and rot which had, more or less, turned the noble profession of lawmaking into a theatre of thuggery, buffoonery and absurdity, with a great slight on our national prestige and international reputation. Interestingly, the quagmire of gross indiscipline and recalcitrant leadership, which had put on hold, legislative activities, finally dissipated with the ouster of the battle-scarred former speaker, a few days after the piece was published.

Original_Search_Title: Give Her the Drumstick But Beware of the Smile
Headline: Tanzania: Give Her the Drumstick But Beware of the Smile
Body_Text:
You've just entered this pub located on the outskirts of Bongoville, off the Dar-Bagamoyo road. You carry out a quick survey of the place and you note that none of the patrons is familiar. So you head for the counter.
You 'beg' the 'akaunta' to give you a drink, which you imbibe slowly as you read a magazine you've carried along. You consider it a good idea to carry something to read in the event that you find there's nobody to chat with at the pub. It makes more sense than just sitting idle, listening to loud music.

Original_Search_Title: Can We 'Trust' the Commonwealth to Deliver Us?
Headline: Uganda: Can We 'Trust' the Commonwealth to Deliver Us?
Body_Text:
Kampala — "We work as a "TRUSTED" partner for all Commonwealth people [including Ugandans] as a force for peace, democracy, equality and good governance; a catalyst for global consensus-building; sustainable development and poverty eradication", declares the Commonwealth's mission statement.
The British Queen confirmed this statement in her 2004 Commonwealth Day message when she stated, unequivocally, "Democracy, national self-determination, individual liberty and human rights -- all these are fundamental to that which binds the Commonwealth together".

Original_Search_Title: Rules of Survival in This Country
Headline: Uganda: Rules of Survival in This Country
Body_Text:
Kampala — Consider this piece of writing my contribution to the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM). Uganda is the Pearl of Africa through which the largest lake, Victoria in Africa runs and the longest river, The Nile, originates.
There is probably more you as a foreigner know about Uganda, like Amin? There is more to this country that you will to be acquainted with to survive among Ugandans. Think of this tirade of advice as my Chogm sermon for all the foreign delegates that are in the country for the momentous event, especially those that have not been in this country before.

Original_Search_Title: President Can Be Known Before Voting Ends On Dec 27
Headline: Kenya: President Can Be Known Before Voting Ends On Dec 27
Body_Text:
Nairobi — We may know on December 27 who will be President even before the polling stations close at 5pm. Exit polling has become such an exact science that professional pollsters can tell us the winner before the votes are counted.
This category of opinion polling is coming to Kenya. Several organisations, including the University of Nairobi, are reportedly planning to carry out exit polls so that Kenyans do not have to sit up late on Thursday night waiting for votes to be counted to know will form the government.

Original_Search_Title: Queen Leaves
Headline: Uganda: Queen Leaves
Body_Text:
Kampala — Queen Elizabeth II and her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, watch a performance by Kitante Primary School pupils on Saturday
Britain's Queen Elizabeth II left for London on Saturday after a four-day visit to Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Book Review
Headline: South Africa: Book Review
Body_Text:
I changed countries more often than I changed shoes
Paulo Coelho (2007) translated from Portuguese by Margaret Jull Costa. Like the Flowing River - Thoughts and Reflections. Johannesburg, Jonathan Ball, 245 pages, Paperback. P112, ISBN 000 7235 80 1. Available at Exclusive Books, Riverwalk.

Original_Search_Title: EU, Cemac Disagree Over Trade Accord
Headline: Cameroon: EU, Cemac Disagree Over Trade Accord
Body_Text:
A split has erupted among big economic operators in Cameroon as to whether or not government should respect the dateline of December 31, 2007, to sign the free trade Economic Partnership Accord between the European Union, EU, and some African, Caribbean and Pacific, ACP, countries.
It would be recalled that on June 23, 2000, the EU and some ACP countries signed the Cotonou Accord to prepare the way for the eventual signing of the Economic Partnership Accord, latest December 31, 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Army Truck Knocks Down Entebbe Pupil
Headline: Uganda: Army Truck Knocks Down Entebbe Pupil
Body_Text:
Kampala — PROSSY Najjuma, a P.3 pupil of Namate Primary School in Entebbe, was on Wednesday knocked down by an army vehicle. Najjuma, who sustained injuries on her eye and left thigh, was hit near the State House and was admitted to Entebbe Hospital.
Entebbe traffic Police chief Chris Onen said they were still trying to identify the vehicle. witnesses said it hit the girl while trying to avoid a head-on collision with another vehicle.

Original_Search_Title: Does the Commonwealth Matter?
Headline: Uganda: Does the Commonwealth Matter?
Body_Text:
It's the day today of the year 2007 B.C (Before Chogm); and this banana republic gears itself up to receive an avalanche of visitors who have sojourned thousands of miles to see the Ugandan miracle for themselves, it is not all impertinent to ask whether the over Shs200b spent on preparing for the event have been worth the eartache.
Established under the statute of Westminster of 1931, the Commonwealth was intended to be an association of equal and independent nations and subordinate areas formerly part of the old British Empire, united by that political heritage, common language and (for some), their symbolic allegiance to the Crown.

Original_Search_Title: Cultural Dance - Celebrating Unity in Diversity
Headline: Uganda: Cultural Dance - Celebrating Unity in Diversity
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDAN music is intertwined in poetry, ritual or dance. It is through music that people express their history and culture. All rituals are performed with dances. Dances are age-old traditions, handed down from generation to generation. Conan Businge brings you the different dances performed by different tribes at the Unity Diversity festival at Shimon grounds
Baganda

Original_Search_Title: Banana Hawker to Hang for Violent Robbery
Headline: Kenya: Banana Hawker to Hang for Violent Robbery
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A banana vendor was on Wednesday sentenced to death for violently robbing a nurse in Kirinyaga District two years ago.
James Munene Kanyi alias Baite, 27, was convicted by Nyeri senior resident magistrate Elizabeth Osoro who said the prosecution had proved the case against him.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo, Kind of Leader We Needed - Mark
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo, Kind of Leader We Needed - Mark
Body_Text:
Lagos — Senate President David Mark was in Lagos last weekend for some engagements. He seized the opportunity to hold an interaction with journalists. Nosike Ogbuenyi was present at the session
What is your opinion on the growing call for the removal of former President Olusegun Obasanjo as the chairman of the Board of Trustees (BOT) of PDP?

Original_Search_Title: I Didn't Spend N400 Million to Renovate My Official Residence - David Mark
Headline: Nigeria: I Didn't Spend N400 Million to Renovate My Official Residence - David Mark
Body_Text:
Lagos — IT was not until recently that the Senate President, Senator David Mark was alleged to have been involved in a N400m official residence renovation scandal, smuggling of N2.5bn into the budget of the National Assembly and spending N500m on the live telecast of the screening of ministerial nominees.
In this interview with some select journalists, the Senate President has asked those who levelled the allegations against him to prove their allegations. He, however insists that he will rise to his defense whe called upon to do so. Aside this, the number three man says he is not planning to control the PDP in his state. He also speaks on other sundry issues. Excerpts:

Original_Search_Title: What the Kampala Club Talks Should Aim to Achieve
Headline: Uganda: What the Kampala Club Talks Should Aim to Achieve
Body_Text:
Nairobi — THE COMMONWEALTH biennial summit is here with us again. The 53 nations of the association meet in Kampala, Uganda, this Friday. In the wake of meetings of Foreign ministers, youth, businesses and civil society organisations, our Heads of Government will spend the weekend in retreat on the shores of Lake Victoria.
No officials may accompany them into their talks at Munyonyo; there will be no television cameras either. They are free to talk as they please, and to address what concerns them. The decisions they take will touch the lives of many.

Original_Search_Title: Guns, Blood And Tears At Party Primaries
Headline: Kenya: Guns, Blood And Tears At Party Primaries
Body_Text:
Nairobi — TV footage showed an Assistant minister doubling up in pain, his face drenched in tears, after he was kicked and punched senseless.
A policeman was killed by his own gun. A shattered Rainbow House, Nairobi, remained forlorn and ghostly. Somewhere, bonfires blocked an entire highway.

Original_Search_Title: U.S. Embassy Provides US$140,000 To 17 NGOs
Headline: Africa: U.S. Embassy Provides US$140,000 To 17 NGOs
Body_Text:
17 Liberian non-governmental organizations (NGOs) were over the weekend proud recipients of several thousands United States dollars in grants to undertake manifold projects in parts of the country.
The money was given by the U.S. through its Self Democracy and Human Rights Fund Program, a gesture intended to assist community-driven projects in developing third world countries such as Liberia.

Original_Search_Title: Artists of the Month
Headline: Uganda: Artists of the Month
Body_Text:
Kampala — IN a bid to promote art during the commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting, the Uganda German Cultural Society and Alliance Française de Kampala are holding an exhibition featuring artists of the month, Washington Ssebina-Kaggwa and Eleki George Bokota at the French German Cultural Centre in Kamwokya, a Kampala suburb.
The exhibition that started on November 14 will run until November 28. Kaggwa's, a self-taught artist, passion for art dates back to his childhood. Teachers would comment in his school reports: "He draws very interesting pictures."

Original_Search_Title: Shillings Galore On NTV's 'Show Me the Money'
Headline: Uganda: Shillings Galore On NTV's 'Show Me the Money'
Body_Text:
Hollywood actor Cuba Gooding Jr turned "Show Me The Money" into a catchphrase in the 1996 movie Jerry Maguire for which he bagged a best Supporting Actor Oscar. NTV has adopted the phrase as the title of its new reality show that promises to hand out Shs50m to a local with the most unique, viable and sustainable business idea as start-up capital.
The show's premise is that much as Uganda is the most entrepreneurial country over Venezuela, Thailand, India and Argentina according to the 2003 Global Entrepreneurship Monitor, most businesses fail in the first year due to inadequate start-up capital.

Original_Search_Title: The Commonwealth's True, Unique Nature
Headline: Kenya: The Commonwealth's True, Unique Nature
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is time for the Commonwealth's biennial summit. The 53 nations of the association meet in Kampala, Uganda on Friday. In the wake of meetings of Foreign Ministers, young people, businesses and civil society organisations, our Heads of Government will spend the weekend in retreat on the shores of Lake Victoria.
No officials may accompany them into their talks at Munyonyo, and far less any television cameras. They are free to talk as they please, and to address what concerns them. The decisions they take will touch the lives of many.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala Shops Won't Close - Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Kampala Shops Won't Close - Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has told the Kampala business community to keep their shops open during the Commonwealth Heads of Government meeting this week.
Addressing a press conference at State House Nakasero yesterday, Museveni dispelled rumours that shops would be required to close during the summit on Thursday and Friday, which have been declared public holidays, and that the city would be sealed off.

Original_Search_Title: Autobiographical Dance Drama
Headline: Namibia: Autobiographical Dance Drama
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Banana Shekupe's The Journey, will be performed at the National Theatre of Namibia next week.
Shekupe says the musical is based on the true story of his childhood experiences and observations during the days of struggle in exile before, during and after independence.

Original_Search_Title: How Bankole Emerged Speaker, By Salako
Headline: Nigeria: How Bankole Emerged Speaker, By Salako
Body_Text:
Lagos — Honourable Dare Salako is the leader of the South-west caucus of the House of Representatives. He represents Remo Federal Constituency of Ogun State. In this interview with Jaiyeola Andrews, he speaks on the lessons of the recent crisis in the House, emergence of the new Speaker and the revolt of the House members against the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) among others
What lessons do you think the House has learnt from the protracted crisis which led to the ouster of the former speaker, Mrs. Patricia Etteh?

Original_Search_Title: Europe Will Be Watching the Soweto Derby
Headline: South Africa: Europe Will Be Watching the Soweto Derby
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE Soweto derby between Kaizer Chiefs and Orlando Pirates is set to soar to unprecedented heights after sports satellite and cable network Eurosport secured the rights to televise South African football's biggest game live to more than 43 European countries on November 24.
Pirates chairman Irvin Khoza said yesterday Eurosport -- the largest European sports satellite and cable network -- would televise live images from Durban's Kings Park stadium to more than 22,3-million households in nine languages.

Original_Search_Title: Business Strategy Lessons From Electioneering
Headline: Kenya: Business Strategy Lessons From Electioneering
Body_Text:
It has been said before the best business strategies do not come from business or commercial settings. Even Sun Tsu - The Art Of War, a military warfare rule book turned out to be the most read, most dependable business strategy book.
The book of five rings, was for long Japan's answer to a Harvard MBA; and a Western classic movie Red River featuring cattle crossing a sand river, can teach one more business strategies in its 156 minutes running time than most two year MBA programmes proliferating across the world of business.

Original_Search_Title: No Tribe is Too Big to Need Others in Uganda
Headline: Uganda: No Tribe is Too Big to Need Others in Uganda
Body_Text:
Kampala — Soundbytes from various quarters regarding the issue of land in Buganda are cause for concern.
Listening to the debates on the matter over the FM radio stations, in many cases both presenters on one hand and some members of the public on the other, have gone far overboard, uttering alarmingly provocative words with the ease of one saying the rosary.

Original_Search_Title: Security Tightened As Examinations Begins
Headline: Kenya: Security Tightened As KCPE Begins
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Thirty-eight thousand more pupils will sit this year's Kenya Certificate of Primary Education (KCPE) examinations starting Tuesday.
Rehearsals start this morning in over 20,000 centres, which is 1,000 more than last year's 19,000.

Original_Search_Title: Now That Amaechi's 'K-Leg' Has Been Straightened...
Headline: Nigeria: Now That Amaechi's 'K-Leg' Has Been Straightened...
Body_Text:
You miss him somehow, uh? I mean the most hilarious president in the history of Nigeria, Matthew Aremu Okikiola Obasanjo. He would go down in history as Nigeria's funniest president.
The event was the presentation of flags to South-East PDP state flag-bearers in the run-up to the last general elections. It was also the day of the South-South zonal presidential campaign of the party.

Original_Search_Title: Through Region By Bike
Headline: North Africa: Through Region By Bike
Body_Text:
I'm still a bit sleepy as I leave the ferry in Nuweiba to cycle my first kilometres on Egyptian soil. The sun just peeps over the bald but impressive mountains. It's silent, a welcome change to the noisy atmosphere at the overcrowded ferry. I still enjoy the companionship of Mika and Hugo, my two French "colleagues". We arrived in Africa! Or? In a small café we proudly declare "We reached Africa by bicycle!" - only to be mildly but firmly corrected by the locals: This is the Sinai.
That's not Africa! But where does the Sinai belong, we ask. The café owner smiles silently: "It's something special!" He is right. Not only that every resident repeats it or because of the portentous history of "Mount Sinai" where Moses got the 10 commandments - it is not long that we realise it on our own. The landscape is spectacular. The road winds its way through mountains covered by sand snow-white clouds. I label it the "Wedding of Sand, Rock and the Sky". Our ride through this fairy-tale-like scenery is only disturbed by the regular police checkpoints! Though the guards seem keen to find some explosives in our bags and walk with trained dogs round our heavy bicycles - we are sorry: Just today we don't have explosives! The goodbye is always a hearty one.

Original_Search_Title: We Need Holistic, Proactive Models in Shaping the Youth
Headline: Uganda: We Need Holistic, Proactive Models in Shaping the Youth
Body_Text:
Kampala — The question for me is not whether or not we are sparing the rod in order to spoil our children as Pastor Martin Ssempa alluded to in his article: Spare the rod, Spoil the Child in The Sunday Vision of October 7, but rather, what you and I are doing to understand the root cause of school strikes and vandalism.
How willing are you and I ready to accept that we too have a thing or two to contribute to the menace in our educational institutions?

Original_Search_Title: Mt Elgon, Molo Raids - KCPE Candidates Missing
Headline: Kenya: Mt Elgon, Molo Raids - KCPE Candidates Missing
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hundreds of school children who fled recent ethnic clashes in Molo are yet to return. Eight primary schools were closed following the attacks.
A survey in Kuresoi showed that only a few pupils were attending school. Parents yesterday expressed fear that KCPE candidates would not sit the examination that begins on Monday.

Original_Search_Title: Avoiding the Banana Peel
Headline: Nigeria: Avoiding the Banana Peel
Body_Text:
With quality education and a good upbringing, House Speaker Dimeji Sabur Bankole seems to be a square peg in a square hole but surviving the cloak-and-dagger politics of the House of Representatives is a totally new field of study which he needs to learn.
For Dimeji Bankole, Ogun State representative, the month of November is a special one. On November 1, after a round of voting, he became the new House Speaker and Bankole, five days later, tied the nuptial knot with Olaita. Last Wednesday, a day after the marriage, the new husband turned 38 years old on November 7. Friends and relatives, including political associates, throughout this month, will roll out the drums to celebrate these milestones. Similarly, after debating and dueling, House members are now battle weary and deserve some respite after the Etteh saga. Bankole, to this extent, can lower his guard, enjoy his new status and plan his survival strategy, especially how to avoid the ubiquitous banana peel. Very soon, the honeymoon will be over as the contest for power will begin.

Original_Search_Title: Speaker Bankole And The Challenges Of Legislative Responsibility
Headline: Nigeria: Speaker Bankole And The Challenges Of Legislative Responsibility
Body_Text:
Abuja — I have a vision, a vision of a House of Representatives which shall once again be fondly referred to as the house of the Nigerian people. I believe that from this hallowed chamber, shall flow robust debates, exciting motions, timely bills, and qualitative legislation that will significantly impact on the standard and quality of lives of our expectant people."
With such statement from the new Speaker of the federal House of Representatives, the stage seems truly set for the commencement of serious legislative business which had been stalled for some couple of months apparently due to the crisis of credibility and integrity that was generated by the house renovation scandals involving former Speaker Patricia Olubunmi Etteh and her deputy, Babangida Ngoroje.

Original_Search_Title: My Son Won't Be a Stooge - Bankole's Father
Headline: Nigeria: My Son Won't Be a Stooge - Bankole's Father
Body_Text:
Ibadan — Chief Saubana Alani Bankole, father of the newly elected Speaker of the House of Representatives, Mr Dimeji Bankole, yesterday assured Nigerians that the Speaker will not be a stooge to anybody.
He told newsmen in Ibad-an at a symposium organised as part of the funeral rites for the late Chief Augustus Meredith Adisa Akinloye, Chairman of the defunct National Party of Nigeria (NPN), that despite his youthful age, the Speaker has an independent mind.

Original_Search_Title: New Speaker Accused of Dodging Youth Service
Headline: Nigeria: New Speaker Accused of Dodging NYSC
Body_Text:
Abuja — Barely one week in office as Speaker of the House of Representatives, Hon. Dimeji Bankole may have been presented with his own political baptism.
Some unnamed members are alleging that the new Speaker evaded the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) programme.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Subverted Democracy to Humiliate Me - Atiku
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Subverted Democracy to Humiliate Me - Atiku
Body_Text:
Yola — Former Vice-President Atiku Abubakar yesterday alleged that his former boss, former President Olusegun Obasanjo, deliberately subverted democracy by manipulating the election results in Adamawa State to humiliate him.
Atiku stated that Obasanjo resorted to many anti-democratic tendencies which could have culminated in the country becoming a Banana republic, adding that every Nigerian knew that the Action Congress (AC) won the election in Adamawa State.

Original_Search_Title: Kuresoi Chaos Halts Learning
Headline: Kenya: Kuresoi Chaos Halts Learning
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Learning in various schools in clash-torn Kuresoi Division of Molo District is paralysed a week before the Kenya Certificate of Primary Education examination.
