Last year the government banned the use of polythene bags and, speaking to The New Times recently, the REMA Director-General, Dr Rose Mukankomeje, said that the Authority had asked relevant agencies to enforce the ban in accordance with the environmental law.
"We have asked all concerned authorities to strictly enforce what the law prescribes," Mukankomeje said and added: "Having given enough time to defaulters to adjust, this is the right time to act."

Original_Search_Title: Mauritius, Not Morally Clean?
Headline: Mauritius, Not Morally Clean?
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Mr Meetarbhan is surprised (Editorial - 15th March) that the governments of South Africa and Mauritius, "deux phares de la region", are silent over recent happenings in Zimbabwe, where opposition has been very violently quashed, one opposition member murdered and the opposition leader, Mr Morgan Tsvangarai, badly beaten.
What is happening in Zimbabwe is very dangerous, both for the people and the future of this country and the region. But my point is whether Mauritius is morally clean enough to speak.

Original_Search_Title: PTDF: Still Striving to Unveil the Truth...
Headline: Nigeria: PTDF: Still Striving to Unveil the Truth...
Body_Text:
Lagos — Senate President, Kenechukwu Nnamani has tasked the Senate Review Committee looking into the Ad-Hoc Committee report on the Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF) to come up with a coherent and truthful report. Sufuyan Ojeifo in this report provides some insights into why Nnamani is goading the committee to unveil the truth.
Kenechukwu Nnamani, President of the Nigerian Senate, comes from the part of the country-Igbo land in the Southeast zone- where dancing is a part of the people's very rich culture. He is a red cap chief by virtue of his being the Onwa (the moon) of Enugu; and he is therefore very conversant with the art of dancing. He may not be able to do the king of dancing by the Atilogu dancers. But he seems to be practically versed in some other kinds of dance on some other terrain: politics. This is why he is dancing to the melodious rhythm of his soul as the entire country is regaled by the odious tales of sleaze, aiding and abetting of the criminal offences that wafted from the windmills of the Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF). Far away from the ordinary eyes, the dance may become more agitated Tuesday, this week, if the Review Committee on PTD is able to submit its report to the Senate.

Original_Search_Title: NEMA Speaks Out On Controversial Central Forest Reserves.
Headline: Uganda: NEMA Speaks Out On Controversial Central Forest Reserves.
Body_Text:
Kampala — The National Environment Management Authority (NEMA) says that controversial central forest reserves have not been given away and they will not be degazetted without fulfilling the necessary legal requirements.
Nema Executive Director Henry Aryamanya Mugisha said even before proposals to degazette the reserves in Mabira and Bugala Island came in, "President Museveni asked us to provide him with a technical report on whether the central forest reserves can be degazetted. There is therefore no way they can degazette the forest reserves without even an environmental impact assessment."

Original_Search_Title: Behind Stanley Livondo's Moneybags
Headline: Kenya: Behind Stanley Livondo's Moneybags
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When five-year old Stanley Livondo appeared at his grandmother's door in Bushiangala, Kakamega District, one freezing morning in 1972, he had nothing, but a tattered blanket, a worn out exercise book and a pencil in hand.
His grandmother, Mrs Niffah Anjia, had to walk long distances to beg for food from relatives to feed his grandson, who had ran away from his parents' home owing to negligence and biting poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Raila's Son Weds Traditionally
Headline: Kenya: Raila's Son Weds Traditionally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The first-born son to Lang'ata MP, Mr Raila Odinga, formalised a traditional wedding that brought two of Kenya's most prominent political dynasties together.
Fidel Odhiambo Castro wedded Veronicah Wanjiru Ng'ang'a, a relative of the first President, Mzee Jomo Kenyatta.

Original_Search_Title: Garbage Management - Akright Introduces Four-Bin System
Headline: Uganda: Garbage Management - Akright Introduces Four-Bin System
Body_Text:
Kampala — AKRIGHT Projects Limited has launched a campaign on safe disposal of solid waste and sewage. While launching the programme recently at Kirinya Housing Estates in Bweyogerere, the consulate of Pakistan in Uganda, Boney Katatumba, said the system would make disposal of wastes easier.
The managing director of Akright Projects, Anatoli Kamugisha, said the four-bin system requires every home to have different bins for garbage.

Original_Search_Title: Akright Launches Garbage Collection System
Headline: Uganda: Akright Launches Garbage Collection System
Body_Text:
Kampala — Akright Projects has launched a four-bin system where every home will have a different bin for different garbage.
The firm's managing director, Anatoli Kamugisha, said the system aims at separation of different types of solid wastes generated by homes.

Original_Search_Title: Read About a Greedy Man Called Kibediko
Headline: Uganda: Read About a Greedy Man Called Kibediko
Body_Text:
Kampala — Title: Kibediko the Glutton
Author: Bob Kisiki

Original_Search_Title: Resist Civil Unrest
Headline: Uganda: Resist Civil Unrest
Body_Text:
Okurut — A FEW months ago, after the parliamentary and presidential elections, you'd meet some people and they would say: "There is a general disillusionment and discontent among the populace."
"Discontent over what?" you'd ask. "Elections perhaps?"

Original_Search_Title: Gen. Tumwine's World of Music, Art and Fashion
Headline: Uganda: Gen. Tumwine's World of Music, Art and Fashion
Body_Text:
Kampala — His name comes to mind when you mention the head of the General Court Martial who moonlights as a fine artist, fashion designer and more recently, a musician. And when you visit Gen. Elly Tumwine's home near the Nigerian High Commission in Nakasero, it speaks of the character of the man of the house.
The gate and wall fencing are painted in patterns of black and white, but as we are still marvelling at the designs, we discover that we won't get to the general that easily. We have to wait for a while as one of his bodyguards crosschecks with him about our visit.

Original_Search_Title: Our Teams Should Be Prepared for Bad Hosts
Headline: Botswana: Our Teams Should Be Prepared for Bad Hosts
Body_Text:
It is not always a bad idea to compare apples to bananas. Sometimes you need to compare bananas to apples just to see how thin and long bananas are.
Lately, there has been animated talk that our football teams are subjected to bad treatment when they visit other countries. That issue has nothing to do with illegal immigrants. But for better appreciation, we want to draw parallels with an illegal immigrant. We all know how they are treated. As an illegal immigrant, you are unwanted. People might understand why you are in their country, some might even value your skills but you will always be treated like an inconvenience. This is how the Botswana Under-23 team that played in Angola over the weekend say they were made to feel. It is alleged that our team was made to wait for hours on end at the airport with no officials from the Angolan football association in sight. There are other allegations of bad hospitality up to the hotel. What is even sadder is that the players seem to have been subjected to unsportly behaviour while training and during the game. This defeats FIFA's credo on fair play.

Original_Search_Title: Muea-Kumba road - Dream Come True
Headline: Cameroon: Muea-Kumba road - Dream Come True
Body_Text:
The Prime Minister yesterday launched rehabilitation work on the 62 kilometre stretch of road which has been a nightmare to travellers.
What many saw as a distant dream, is now a reality thanks to sustained cooperation ties between the European Union and the Government of Cameroon. Yesterda, at the sleepy village of Liongo at the outskirts of Muea, the Prime Minister and Head of Government, Chief Ephraim Inoni launched the debut of rehabilitation work on the bumpy 62 kilometre Muea to Kumba stretch of road. For hundreds who attended the historic occasion, it was a dream come true for it usually took travellers more than four hours for a distance that could be done in less than one hour.

Original_Search_Title: Need to Communicate And Democratise Our Foreign Policy
Headline: Namibia: Need to Communicate And Democratise Our Foreign Policy
Body_Text:
OUR foreign policy has never been a reflection of the values and norms we cherish as a young democracy and our recent guest (or the company we keep) closely attests to that.
One would note our support for the late Nigerian dictator Sani Abacha, our friendship with the nuclear-weapons-obsessed Kim Jong-Il of North Korea (whose manual labourers are constructing our new State House) and Robert Mugabe of Zimbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Mugabe is Unrivalled Master of Buffoonery
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mugabe is Unrivalled Master of Buffoonery
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Robert Mugabe's gleaming motorcade snaked its way into the clinic where Zimbabwean Opposition leader Mr Morgan Tsvangirai was grappling with pain.
Hovering unseen above him were the angels of death. His face was swollen and one side of his head clean-shaven to make it easy for doctors to stitch the wound opened up by the rungus (club) of Mugabe's brutes.

Original_Search_Title: Gallery Cafe a Perfect Work of Art
Headline: Zimbabwe: Gallery Cafe a Perfect Work of Art
Body_Text:
I ALWAYS enjoy eating at Botanic Gardens Restaurant in Alexandra Park/Belgravia: vaguely proprietorially, as I was instrumental in delightful Nomsa Gwataringa acquiring the lease of the attractive thatched premises.
Now Nomsa's wings have spread, taking over Gallery Café at the National Gallery, which is much nearer for me, there's plenty of (free) parking close and it's 10 minutes' walk from the office on a nice day. A 10 minute stroll back is a splendid idea, to aid digestion, given large portions served.

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Ambitions
Headline: Uganda: Presidential Ambitions
Body_Text:
The voice of the president is only second to that of God, it is often said. As Commander-in-Chief of the national army, the president has the powers to declare war and make peace. Some use their superior powers to torment their counterparts. We've had George Bush Junior driving Saddam Hussein into a rat hole and eventually leading to his demise.
The presidential seat is so cozy that Yoweri Museveni was forced to cling on at the time he was meant to leave. In the end, he validated the Machiavellian principle of power corrupting and absolute power corrupting absolutely!

Original_Search_Title: INEC has no power to verify candidates - Akintola (SAN)
Headline: Nigeria: INEC has no power to verify candidates - Akintola (SAN)
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Federal High Court sitting in Abuja, last week ruled that the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) has no powers, under the law, to disqualify candidates already okayed by their political parties for the April poll.
The trial judge who handed down the verdict, Justice Babs Kuewumi held that he had combed through the entire Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria (1999) and other relevant laws including the Electoral Act 2006 and that no single provision therein empowered INEC to disqualify candidates for election.

Original_Search_Title: Will the 'Kibaki Factor' Work Against Nobel Hero Maathai?
Headline: Kenya: Will the 'Kibaki Factor' Work Against Nobel Hero Maathai?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai has a hard task ahead to retain her Tetu seat. Many voters will be looking for a candidate who resonates with the local mood: the Kibaki factor.
This is the reason at least seven aspirants are seeking the Narc Kenya ticket, as the party is associated with President Kibaki and his supporters.

Original_Search_Title: Community and Hospitality Group Strike Landmark Deal
Headline: South Africa: Community and Hospitality Group Strike Landmark Deal
Body_Text:
Tshwane — The multi-million rand deal between the Sandford Trust, representing the Mapulana community, and Hollow Group, private investors who own an exclusive country estate, is expected to transform the community's dilapidated Tembi Guest Lodge near Hazyview.
The Lodge will be revamped into a hotel with its own convention centre, tourism and hospitality training school.

Original_Search_Title: Awori Faces Rough Ride in Funyula
Headline: Kenya: Awori Faces Rough Ride in Funyula
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The big battle in Busia district will be in Funyula constituency where the Vice-President Moody Awori must overcome mounting opposition to sustain his political survival.
It is a constituency where political positions alone could override all other determinants in play such as poverty, health, education and poor infrastructure.

Original_Search_Title: The Making of a Local Circumcision Surgeon
Headline: Uganda: The Making of a Local Circumcision Surgeon
Body_Text:
Kampala — The ringing of bells strapped to the legs, the beat of the traditional drums, the sound of fiddles, flutes and songs blend into an event memorable to the curious onlookers.
A group of people dancing and screaming is advancing towards a clearing where another group is holding up twigs and tree branches.

Original_Search_Title: After the Cyclone, a Visit from Mick Jagger
Headline: Mauritius: After the Cyclone, a Visit from Mick Jagger
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Water and leaves after Gamede make a perfect breeding place for mosquitoes.
The air is calm again and the end of the summer has gone back to its familiar stillness, warmth and stickiness. Gamede has moved on, after its very bad- mannered and bad-tempered visit, leaving us all with extra chores and breakages. (That reminds me Why is a cyclone like an ex-wife? - Because she blows for a while, with hot, wet passion and when she leaves - she takes the house with her!)

Original_Search_Title: Nothing Flat About the 'Dog'
Headline: Zimbabwe: Nothing Flat About the 'Dog'
Body_Text:
STILL reeling from all round price increases (fuel up $1 000 a litre, beer up $2 000 a bomber, "reader" glasses soaring from $41 000 to $106 000 a pair while being tried on, the London air fare on "ScareZim" rocketing upwards by more than $1 million of the new money (a billion plus of the old) overnight, and bread disappearing from supermarket shelves, it was nice to get some sense and sanity at a lunch of the Greendale Good Food & Wine Appreciation Society at Flat Dog Diner, Msasa.
Co-owner Paul Beard put together an attractive package, but sadly, as it clashed with a major fishing event at Chirundu attracting members and Cambrian Society's St David's Day lunch, we were not over strong on the ground. I suspect an 11th hour change of venue and cost and scarcity of fuel didn't help either.

Original_Search_Title: Dutch Firm Cons Local Exporter
Headline: Uganda: Dutch Firm Cons Local Exporter
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOR months last year, gossip about his business acumen was the talk of the village. John Osapil of Amagoro Tororo district had everything going his way as he exported bananas, mangoes, pineapples and other tropical fruits to the Netherlands. Three months ago, he was at his peak.
But his fortunes turned as quickly as they arrived. Osapil's company has closed down after losing sh30m, the result, he says, of fraud by a foreign company. He has since retreated to his village as he tries to come to terms with the reality that hit at the heart of his livelihood.

Original_Search_Title: The African President Who Claims to Cure Aids
Headline: Gambia: The African President Who Claims to Cure Aids
Body_Text:
Kampala — What is happening in The Gambia deserves mentioning here because it can very much easily occur in any African country where the leader has absolute power and all other departments fear to cross his path.
In January, President Yaya Jammeh, 41, announced that he had been sent by God - this God who loves African State Houses - to rid his country of HIV/Aids. Baffling public health workers struggling against faith healers, he declared that he was given mandate to cure HIV. He produced a herbal concoction which he says cures HIV/Aids as long as the patient on treatment refrains from drinking alcohol, tea and coffee; eating kola nuts; and having sex.

Original_Search_Title: President Obasanjo - The Elections Must Hold
Headline: Nigeria: President Obasanjo - The Elections Must Hold
Body_Text:
It is clearly an indication of the sorry pass that Obasanjo's desperacy to remain in power has taken Nigeria, that a few weeks to the elections, the Nigerian people have to commence a campaign that the elections must hold. If it was in the realms of speculation before now, the proverbial wind has exposed the hind place of the Obasanjo fowl. The bid to truncate the electoral process has now come out in the open, even if the Obasanjo entourage is still using subterfuge.
The Tuesday, March 6th, 2007 edition of most Nigerian newspapers carried the report that the Attorney General of the Federation will be meeting with the Chief Justice of the Federation and the Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) over the April elections. The report went further that the proposed meeting was at the instance of the National Council of States, and that push had been initiated by President Olusegun Obasanjo.

Original_Search_Title: Not Another Lean Year!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Not Another Lean Year!
Body_Text:
Harare — LIES in all shapes and sizes have gone forth unchallenged in the state media where highly charged rhetoric about the supposed success of the land reform exercise has been substituted for informed and reasoned analysis.
Zimbabwe and a highly sceptical international community have been served a daily diet of fawning coverage of government and ruling ZANU PF officials invariably engaged in an orgy of self-congratulation on the "resounding success" of the land reform exercise. A visitor from Mars would think Zimbabwe boasts food self-sufficiency.

Original_Search_Title: Koome Bans Drinking 'Waragi'
Headline: Uganda: Koome Bans Drinking 'Waragi'
Body_Text:
Kampala — AUTHORITIES in Koome Island have banned the consumption of waragi, a local potent gin, following the death of 20 people in Kalyambuzi LC1 village, Mukono, recently.
Patrick Mukasa, the LC3 chairman, said any one found selling or buying the gin would be arrested.

Original_Search_Title: High Court Premises- Just Perfect for a Chogm Hotel
Headline: Uganda: High Court Premises- Just Perfect for a Chogm Hotel
Body_Text:
If you've never read The Trial by the Jewish- Czech novelist Franz Kafka, this might be an opportune moment to do so. The story is about Joseph K., a junior manager of a large bank, who is arrested one fine morning on charges, which are never spelt out.
As he is dragged out of his bed he ponders to himself: "who could these men be? What were they talking about? What authority could they represent? K lived in a country with a legal constitution, there was universal peace, all the laws were in force; who dared seize him in his own dwelling?"

Original_Search_Title: PTDF - Senate's Intervention As Political Carthasis
Headline: Nigeria: PTDF - Senate's Intervention As Political Carthasis
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE renowned English writer, Mark Twain in his study of human mind several years ago said: "It is not the best that we should think alike. It is the difference of opinion that makes the horse". His postulation many years ago when Britain was passing through some political, social and economic upheavals is certainly true of all nations, whether in prosperity or adversity. Sameness is synonymous with monotony, and therefore, prone to dullness and resection by analytical minds.
The Senate of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, nay, the National Assembly of the Third Republic, has suffered the stigma of being referred to as an appendage of the executive arm of government rather than being an independent legislature, arm working in harmony with the other two arms to promote democratic ideals, rule of law, fundmanetal human rights and social justice.

Original_Search_Title: Thisday Award Winners... Profiles in Excellence
Headline: Nigeria: Thisday Award Winners... Profiles in Excellence
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prologue
The award recipients essentially turned mettle into gold, achieving what ancient alchemists had sweated but failed to do with metal. But beyond the traditional panache, a bit of swagger, glitz and glamour, the 12th THISDAY Annual Awards for Excellence and Good Governance, at a more fundamental level, represents a sober effort to recognise and fete merit and excellence. In an environment where these qualities are often not sufficiently highlighted, a danger of moral atrophy and creeping indifference to quality governance, optimal industrial and service delivery comes to the fore. No country or society can be leveraged meaningfully or journey safely on such a Philistinic scenario. In effect, no society can live without its dreamers and achievers.

Original_Search_Title: Where Simplicity Meets Variety
Headline: South Africa: Where Simplicity Meets Variety
Body_Text:
Cape Town — With just about everything a person could possibly want right in the city bowl, combined with roads which seem to be increasingly clogged with way too many vehicles propelled by morons, it takes a lot to get me out of my comfort zone.
There are restful little oases to be found in town if you know where to look, but one thing we don't have in abundance are tranquil forests of tall trees, especially ones with restaurants and coffee shops nestling in their shade.

Original_Search_Title: Veep Lauds Business Community
Headline: Zambia: Veep Lauds Business Community
Body_Text:
Lusaka — VICE-President Rupiah Banda, has commended local business houses for donating various items worth millions of Kwacha to flood victims in the country and maintained that his office will ensure the items benefit the affected families.
Mr Banda said the donations were a true reflection of the importance local business houses attached to supplementing the Government efforts in responding to communities that were afflicted by hazards.

Original_Search_Title: Memoirs of a Magazine
Headline: Kenya: Memoirs of a Magazine
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Exactly a year ago to date, our offices (KTN-Standard) were raided by hooded goons allegedly led by two 'brothers' the world would come to know as the Arturs from Armenia. In the following three months, Pulse would closely follow in only the sensational manner we can the antics of the Armenians - especially the bearded goon called Artur Margaryan - as these arrogant scum-bags wreaked havoc on Kenya's normally predictable celebrity scene.
KTN's Straight-Up captured the VIP swagger and march of the Armenians at JKIA on TV last march. Pulse was there at the CHAT-awards in April as celebrities like Mongolo made monkeys of themselves by asking the allegedly Sh150,000-a-day spenders for loose change.

Original_Search_Title: WHO Dampens  President's HIV/Aids Claim
Headline: Gambia: WHO Dampens  President's HIV/Aids Claim
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Jammeh claims his herbal remedy can cure HIV/AIDS
The World Health Organization (WHO) has implicitly disputed claims by the Gambian president Yahya Jammeh that he has found a cure for HIV/AIDS.
The president's unsubstantiated but well-publicised claims to have "perfected a treatment for the AIDS virus" using herbs and bananas were reported by the Gambian and international news outlets in February.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
The Gambian department of health has released statements saying that in some patients the HIV virus was no longer detectable.
The treatment's secret ingredients, according to minister of health Tamsir Mbowe, are Jammeh's "family knowledge of traditional medicine" and "the teachings of the holy Koran".
But a statement released in Congo Brazzaville this week (27 February) by the WHO does not endorse Jammeh's claims, maintaining that "so far, there is no cure for HIV infection or its most severe form of disease -- AIDS".
"WHO and UNAIDS are not associated with the treatment for AIDS announced by the president of the Republic of Gambia," it states.
Antiretroviral treatment offers the best hope for AIDS patients, say the WHO, as it interrupts the HIV virus's replication process, improving a patient's quality of life and survival.
They urged patients not to discontinue medical treatment, saying "Herbal remedies cannot take the place of comprehensive treatment and care".
The WHO offered to help The Gambia assess the safety, efficacy and quality of the proposed treatment. So far the president has refused the support.
Ed Rybicki, a HIV researcher at the University of Cape Town, South Africa, told SciDev.Net: "If it [the claim] is true -- which I most sincerely doubt -- then he is doing the world a disservice by not spreading it as widely as possible."
"If it is false -- which I most fervently believe to be the case -- then he is quite possibly mentally ill, or severely deluded."
Jerry Coovadia, an HIV specialist at the University of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, told News Medical that the statements made by the president and the minister of health violate "every foundation of science and public health". He blames the country's political environment for allowing the situation to arise.
AIDS activists have also criticised Jammeh's claims, and say it could set back campaigns to raise awareness about HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis.
Last month the UN Resident Coordinator in the Gambian capital Banjul, Fadzai Gwaradzimba, was expelled from the country after she criticised the president's claims.
The World Health Organization
The University of Cape Town
UNAIDS
The University of KwaZulu-Natal

Original_Search_Title: Taiwan Donates to Cassamance Refugees
Headline: Gambia: Taiwan Donates to Cassamance Refugees
Body_Text:
Dr Patrick Chang, Taiwanese Ambassador to The Gambia on Wednesday gave a donation of 100 bags of rice, vegetables and bananas to the Cassamance refugees in The Gambia through the Jammeh Foundation for Peace at a ceremony held in Ndeman in Foni.
Speaking at the presentation ceremony, Ambassador Chang disclosed that the donation is the 8th to the Cassamance refugees by his Embassy. The refugees, according to Ambassador Chang in total are seven thousand five hundred. "This are people who fled from Cassamance to The Gambia and we want them to enjoy their stay in The Gambia".

Original_Search_Title: South West - Student Stabbed in Fiango
Headline: Cameroon: South West - Student Stabbed in Fiango
Body_Text:
Eighteen-year-old Cyril Atabong Momo was stabbed to death Friday, 8 December, 2006 at the Mbo Street in Fiango-Kumba.
Preliminary Police investigations hold Numisi Michel as suspect. He is currently helping the police to unearth the truth. Accounts by Mbo Street residents and a Local Radio hold that Numisi stabbed Atabong at about 2:30 p.m. on the mentioned date. The dispute, Mbo Street residents explained, arose from Atabong's refusal to share his CFA 800 (eight hundred francs), which he got from selling his cloth-stand and hangers. Numisi claimed that he brought the customer who bought the articles and so deserved compensation. But the victim continued to resist paying the "demarcheur."

Original_Search_Title: How Raid Created Camps in the Police
Headline: Kenya: How Raid Created Camps in the Police
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The raid on The Standard Group premises showed just how easily Government agents could be used to do dirty jobs for the benefit of a section of the ruling clique.
Police were used to attack the country's oldest newspaper and dismantle equipment before escaping in the darkness after robbing some of the employees.

Original_Search_Title: Standard Raid - State Egg-Faced a Year Later
Headline: Kenya: Standard Raid - State Egg-Faced a Year Later
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The conversation was barely over. I was already at the window looking out for the usual suspect cars - beige Peugeot 504s, cream Land Cruisers and red or white Hyundais.
On the phone was my then Managing Editor Pamela Makotsi and her message was chilling: "Tanui, are you aware the Government is shutting us down?" As the fogged mind cleared, I found myself asking if she was serious. What followed that 1am incident a year ago was gritting to the ear and chilling to the heart.

Original_Search_Title: Garakara Releases Smashing Video
Headline: Zimbabwe: Garakara Releases Smashing Video
Body_Text:
Harare — EMERGING sungura giant, Joseph Garakara, seems unstoppable and ready to conquer the genre.
With two of his albums, Tapinda Tapinda and the latest Kechi-One neZai, in the top three of the weekly charts, the pint-sized musician has unleashed a new video off his new album called Simbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Grenade Injures 3 in Mityana
Headline: Uganda: Grenade Injures 3 in Mityana
Body_Text:
Kampala — THREE children from the same family were on Saturday rushed to Mityana Hospital in critical condition after a grenade exploded at their home in Busere village, writes Luke Kagiri.
The victims were identified as Ssande Kibirige, 9, Joseph Ssekyole, 9, and Agnes Nantaba, 15.

Original_Search_Title: That Orphans May Have Quality Life . . .
Headline: Nigeria: That Orphans May Have Quality Life . . .
Body_Text:
Lagos — The woman many people knew as Mama Emeka woke up one morning and walked to the bush very close to her house to gather banana leaves she would use to wrap moi-moi (a local dish made from beans). As she leaped and aimed to cut the large, long leave, stretching her right hand with a short knife, she heard the cry of a baby in a groove close to her, on the way to the main stream that supply water to the village. She ignored the sound, thinking it might be a squawking of a peculiar bird. The intermittent, but piercing cry of the baby continued and gnawed at the crevices of her womanhood.
She left what she was doing, held the knife more firmly and went in search of the baby. She traced the baby, guided by her soft, insistent cry that was now becoming feeble, as the energy to cry was becoming dissipated. She walked to the groove as the voice continued to beckon at her.

Original_Search_Title: Yahya Jammeh - a Clown With a Mandate
Headline: Gambia: Yahya Jammeh - a Clown With a Mandate
Body_Text:
Perhaps it is naiveté, but U.S President Lincoln's claim that you can fool all the people some of the time, some of the people all of the time, but you cannot fool all of the people all of the time sounds quite convincing to me. Could it be then that The Gambia's President His Excellency Dr. Alhaji Yahya Abdul Aziz Jemus Junking Jammeh has proven Lincoln wrong? Oh, and yes, Bob Marley comes to mind too.
While many politicians deceive, only a few have made deception the hallmark of their operational criterion - a modus operandi. Many well meaning Gambians have publicly or otherwise concluded that Jammeh cannot be trusted one bit; not the least what he spews with impudence very so often. Beyond the damage he has done to our peace, tranquility and to Gambia's economy, Jammeh has introduced and nurtured a culture of mendacity into Gambian civil and political affairs. One need only examine recent history to realize that the most dangerous regimes evolve in countries where leaders lie without shame. A shameless leader is a leader who can commit atrocities like killing twenty-thousand Gambians and going to sleep. Yahya said he can do that, it is on record. Matter of fact, that is one lie we wish remains a lie for ever.

Original_Search_Title: Water Flows Into Katima Houses
Headline: Namibia: Water Flows Into Katima Houses
Body_Text:
The constantly rising water level in the Zambezi River started flooding houses occupied by the manager and the senior accountant at Bank Windhoek who are stationed in Katima Mulilo, prompting them to start evacuating their houses.
By 09h10 yesterday morning the water level was exactly on the 7,00-meter mark when a New Era journalist visited the stout concrete pillar to which the gauge plate for measuring floods and the level of the water is attached.

Original_Search_Title: Struggling Through His Blindness
Headline: Uganda: Struggling Through His Blindness
Body_Text:
Kampala — BECOMING blind does not mean you have hit a dead end. The director of Excel Training Centre in Nabweru, Shafiq Mwanje, 35, lost his sight before celebrating his sixth birthday. But he braved all the odds and today is a teacher, giving vital skills to disadvantaged students.
"We have enrolled 70 students since the school started in 2005," Mwanje talks business. He has a braille machine on his knees. He spreads his fingers to feel the texture of a sheet of paper before inserting it into the machine. This is his office. "We offer Catering, Business Administration, Accountancy, Secretarial Studies and we teach people how to repair radios, television sets, fridges and motor cars," he says.

Original_Search_Title: Why Every Businessperson Needs a Mentor
Headline: Uganda: Why Every Businessperson Needs a Mentor
Body_Text:
Kampala — FORTY-YEAR-OLD Faridah decided to set up an internet café. "I wired all the computers at once but they failed to work. This went on for a whole week and I lost all my new clients.
So I got someone to teach me and everything has been perfect ever since," Faridah says.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Wilt Hits Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: Banana Wilt Hits Mbarara
Body_Text:
Mbarara — Banana wilt disease has hit parts of Mbarara district, the chairperson of the production committee has said.
"If we do not take this matter seriously, our people will suffer from food shortage and loss of income," Stella Bamurekye Rugunda said. She was speaking during a district council session recently.

Original_Search_Title: Now That Obi is Back
Headline: Nigeria: Now That Obi is Back
Body_Text:
Lagos — HIS story is a familiar one; he is a victim of the unjust Nigeria condition. Here we are talking of Mr. Peter Obi, who won election and was denied victory. Like a wounded lion, he fought fiercely and was able to regain his mandate. Seven months after regaining the mandate, some lawless miscreants in the Anambra State House of Assembly led by Balonwu unconstitutionally impeached him. When other common run of men should go home rolling and wailing on the ground, Obi refused to succumb to what was clearly unjust. Unlike other people, he knows only one weapon: the rule of law. Again and again, he has gone to court and again and again came out triumphantly that today, he is back as the governor of Anambra State.
Now that Obi is back, the question on so many lips is: what next? In the period of Obi's absence, so many things happened that took the State back to dark ages.

Original_Search_Title: ECK Seeks Companies to Print Ballot Papers
Headline: Kenya: ECK Seeks Companies to Print Ballot Papers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Preparation for this year's General Election are moving into top gear with the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) calling on firms to tender for printing and supply of ballot papers.
The ECK has also advertised for tenders for other election materials like rubber stamps, security seals, embossers and carton collapsible polling booths and hacksaw blades.

Original_Search_Title: VP's Seat - How Atiku, Parties Reacted to Judgment
Headline: Nigeria: VP's Seat - How Atiku, Parties Reacted to Judgment
Body_Text:
Lagos — "This judgment has brought light into our crawling democracy' that statement from Wole Olanipekun (SAN) lead counsel to the Vice President in the landmark case between the Vice President and the Federal Government last Tuesday captured aptly the feeling of not a few politician and political watchers in the nation's polity since the third term project collapsed and aggravated the feud between President Olusegun Obasanjo and his deputy, Atiku Abubakar.
But what the five Justices did on Tuesday was merely to do what John Marshal identified as the duties of the judiciary as far back as 1803 in the case of Marbury vs Madison in the United States when he declared 'It is emphatically the province and duty of the judicial department to say what the law is. If two laws conflict with each other, the courts must decide on the operation of each. This is the very essence of the judicial duty'

Original_Search_Title: Thriving On Teamwork
Headline: Uganda: Thriving On Teamwork
Body_Text:
The Buhakaniras raised their capital from personal savings and credit from banks and have since established several businesses; Avemar Shopping Centre, Cave Bar and Restaurant, Snow White Security Guards and multi-million dollar hotel in Bwebajja on Entebbe Road
Since they got married 22 years ago, Joseph and Goodra Behakanira of the Avemar Group of Companies have worked as a team. Both are directors and share equal power in their chain of businesses.

Original_Search_Title: The Work of Play
Headline: Kenya: The Work of Play
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A girl screams in excitement as she peddles a ramshackle tricycle down an unkempt path. Five boys play football with a dummy ball and little boys and girls take turns in a serious role-play at another corner.
"Make the house faster," advises one of the girls in the group. Apparently, she has already decided where to place her teddy bear when the house is complete. In the meantime though, she will lull it to sleep with a Gikuyu lullaby, never mind that she only knows one line which she forgets often.

Original_Search_Title: 'Fatal Blow to Lawlessness'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Fatal Blow to Lawlessness'
Body_Text:
Lagos — VICE President Atiku Abubakar yesterday described the Court of Appeal's judgment that favoured him as "a fatal blow to executive lawlessness and the creeping culture of impunity and brigandage."
Atiku, in a statement from London, further averred that "the era of reckless use of power by a tyrannical ruler is gone forever. In our democracy, the law rules and due process prevails. I have no doubt at all that this ruling will further strengthen our young democracy and the institutions which serve as its pillars."

Original_Search_Title: Mtukudzi to Unleash New Album
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mtukudzi to Unleash New Album
Body_Text:
Harare — OLIVER Mtukudzi, who is set to unleash his new album in April this year, will this weekend surprise his fans when he performs alongside the dance group Mambokadzi for the first time.
In an interview yesterday, Mtukudzi who was busy with rehearsals, said he was preparing to release his 12-track album, Tsimba Itsoka, and other two re-recordings in April this year.

Original_Search_Title: Muhanga Residents Urged On Hygiene
Headline: Rwanda: Muhanga Residents Urged On Hygiene
Body_Text:
Muhanga — The Mayor of Muhanga District has urged residents to take personal hygiene and general cleanliness activities seriously to improve the outlook of the district's urban centre.
Mayor Augustine Hategeka made the call during his visit to Nyamabuye Sector (former Gitarama Town) on February 16, where he warned that tough measures will be taken against residents who cannot abide by the district's development strategy and policies.

Original_Search_Title: Can Another Idi Amin Grab Power Today? Yes
Headline: Uganda: Can Another Idi Amin Grab Power Today? Yes
Body_Text:
Charles Onyango-Obbo — The Last King of Scotland, a half-fictional film about dictator Idi Amin, premiered in Kampala on the weekend. The mighty of the land, and the star of the film Forest Whitaker (what a powerful performance) were in the house.
And yes, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Regional Co-operation, Mr Eriya Kategaya, was also at hand. We mention Kategaya, because twice in the past in discussions with him, he explained to me that Uganda had changed "irreversibly" in several respects.

Original_Search_Title: Bringing Back DDT Will Have Disastrous Results
Headline: East Africa: Bringing Back DDT Will Have Disastrous Results
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Tanzanian government recently announced plans to use the highly toxic chemical DDT (dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) as an indoor fumigant against malarial mosquitoes in 25 districts in the country.
This follows last year's decision by the WHO to lift a 30-year ban on the chemical, despite the fact that the chlorine compound has been found to persist in the environment and in human and animal tissue.

Original_Search_Title: It Needs Commando Warfare to Feed the Urban Working Class
Headline: East Africa: It Needs Commando Warfare to Feed the Urban Working Class
Body_Text:
Nairobi — For over a century, we have grappled with the problem of feeding the urban worker; finally, we seem to be finding a solution. And this solution arises, strangely enough, from our obsession with matters military.
All urban workers need some form of fast food to power them through the day, and it has taken Kampala nearly 117 years (since it was founded in September 1890) to find a quick way of feeding its ever-growing workforce.

Original_Search_Title: Solar Drier Making Fruit Processing Easier
Headline: Uganda: Solar Drier Making Fruit Processing Easier
Body_Text:
Kampala — ENERGY is crucial for any social and economic activity. And a consistent and reliable source of energy is a prerequisite to production, improvement of livelihood and economic competitiveness on global and domestic market.
The sun is a source of unlimited energy. Just the tiny fraction of the sun's energy that hits the earth is enough to meet all our power needs many times over.

Original_Search_Title: KCC to Tax Cars Ferrying Charcoal
Headline: Uganda: KCC to Tax Cars Ferrying Charcoal
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kampala City Council (KCC) will impose a fee on vehicles that litter the city.
The acting district director of finance, Bakka Musujja, disclosed this during the annual budget conference at Hotel Africana on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: Renewed Demands for Partial Constitutional Review
Headline: Kenya: Renewed Demands for Partial Constitutional Review
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The clamour for a new constitution in Kenya has taken a further twist: civil society, political parties and the Parliamentary Committee on Administration of Justice and Legal Affairs are teaming up under Muungano wa Katiba Mpya (the Union for a New Constitution, in Swahili) to demand partial constitutional reforms before general elections later this year.
This comes despite the announcement by President Mwai Kibaki in August 2006 that there would be no partial reforms before the poll. His declaration was prompted by calls from certain parliamentarians for "minimum reforms", which included stripping the president of his right to appoint members of the 21-person Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK). It was feared that Kibaki could stack the ECK with supporters to favour government in the upcoming ballot.

Original_Search_Title: Jazzy Treat for Lovers On Valentine's Day
Headline: Zimbabwe: Jazzy Treat for Lovers On Valentine's Day
Body_Text:
LOVERS of jazz and soul music were treated to an unforgettable Valentine's Night last Wednesday when current and former students of the Zimbabwe College of Music held a fund-raising show for their institution at the National Art Gallery.
The show, dubbed The Gallery of Heart, turned out to be a unique exhibition of some of the country's untapped musical talent.

Original_Search_Title: Bringing You Local Crafts On Fridays
Headline: Uganda: Bringing You Local Crafts On Fridays
Body_Text:
Almost anything you would want to buy, from kitchenware, house decorations to clothes and ornaments can be got at this market
Every Friday, the Uganda Railways yard on Kampala Road is abuzz with human activity. Makers of arts and crafts come together to sell their wares to the general public.

Original_Search_Title: 'A' still not for 'apple'
Headline: Nigeria: 'A' still not for 'apple'
Body_Text:
Kaduna — There has always been a gulf of difference between work satisfaction and work compensation. A decade ago, I was a university lecturer: a job I so loved (and still long and yearn for) and from which I derived the ultimate job satisfaction. However, 'my take-home pay', as our union ASUU aptly put it in the late 1980s and early 1990s, 'could not take me home,' when we were confronting perhaps the most brutal and most corrupt regime in the nation's history.
In those days, lecturers (and other teachers) were the butt of jokes of many a marriageable girl: "Are you a worker or a teacher?" the girl would ask you, the potential suitor, as no girl wanted to be eternally hooked up with someone whose reward was providentially expected only in heaven. Women would rather want a little of the reward here on earth and so many male lecturers ended up marrying idealists like them or, when push came to shove, the rejects.

Original_Search_Title: Neo-Liberalism is Extremely Dangerous
Headline: Uganda: Neo-Liberalism is Extremely Dangerous
Body_Text:
Last Sunday we responded to an article by Rev. David Kashangaki, in which he took issue with our end of year article on political commentary. Our central argument in all this has been that all the opposition and "oppositionists" talk about is "regime change", and that instead of necessary focus on strategic basic development policy issues, they concentrate on probably important but definitely secondary matters.
This week we stay with the same issues. An important aspect of this is the multi-disciplinary approach that we need to bring to the discussion. Politics, societal norms and values, etc, must be an integral part of any discussion of basic development policy. Questions of philosophy, world outlook, etc, must arise.

Original_Search_Title: Being the Best At the Little Things Sets Us Free
Headline: South Africa: Being the Best At the Little Things Sets Us Free
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — CARL Latich from the US can blow bubbles with his eyes by holding his nose, inverting his head and lowering it slowly into a fish tank filled with water where he proceeds to gee-up his eye-balls until they jump about in their sockets like pinballs.
The by-product of this exertion is a thin, steady stream of air bubbles emitted from the corner of his eyes. I don't know how Latich does this, but he does it. More intriguingly, I don't know how he discovered that he could do this.

Original_Search_Title: So Full of Life - Angelina Wapakhabulo
Headline: Uganda: So Full of Life - Angelina Wapakhabulo
Body_Text:
Kampala — Q You grew up in a religious environment. Did you ever consider becoming a nun?
A I did. My parents were both teachers in Tosamaganga, a big Catholic mission, where I grew up. One of the big disappointments for my father was that none of his nine children became a missionary. I used to admire nuns but when I joined a secondary school away from my home, I began to see that apart from being a teacher and a missionary, one could be other things and still be God fearing.

Original_Search_Title: Son Tortured for Wetting Bed
Headline: Uganda: Son Tortured for Wetting Bed
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police in Kyotera town have arrested a man who tied a brick on the manhood of to his nine-year-old son for wetting his bed.
John Mugerwa of Kasaali sub-county in Rakai district tied the boy's hands and laid him on the ground.

Original_Search_Title: The Exemplary People in Kamwenge
Headline: Uganda: The Exemplary People in Kamwenge
Body_Text:
Kibale County MP Frank Tumwebaze is the owner of Rwenzori FM in Kamwenge town. He is also one of the directors of Kamwenge Development Agency, an organisation that sensitises the public on poverty eradication. He has started a solar project in schools and health centres in his constituency to improve the education standards and health service delivery.
Florence Hashaka is the Kamwenge Woman MP. She is a former cashier at Kamwenge town council and a daughter to Jackson Hashaka (rip) former Kibale county MP. Her goal is to help women of Kamwenge get out of poverty and to also participate in politics.

Original_Search_Title: Is the Race Now On for Second?
Headline: South Africa: Is the Race Now On for Second?
Body_Text:
The Premier League title is Mamelodi Sundowns' to lose.
While the championship race is really tight - with even Santos in 10th position just six points off the top - it's the form, class and strength in depth of Sundowns that makes them the obvious favourites to retain their grip on the PSL trophy. (And they also have three games in hand over the other title candidates).

Original_Search_Title: FDC to Hold Own Women's Day Celebrations
Headline: Uganda: FDC to Hold Own Women's Day Celebrations
Body_Text:
Najjanankumbi — MEMBERS of the Forum for Democratic Change Women League will hold separate Women's Day celebration from the national event on March 8.
The party's secretary for women Ingrid Turinawe yesterday told journalists at the party's headquarters in Najjanankumbi that the resolution to hold the celebrations separately stems from "the continued humiliation FDC women have endured in the past at the hands of the ruling NRM party."

Original_Search_Title: FDC Launches Women's Month
Headline: Uganda: FDC Launches Women's Month
Body_Text:
Kampala — WOMEN of the opposition Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) are planning to organise their own International Women's Day celebrations, claiming that the government only honours the great women of the government NRM.
"Women's day in Uganda is for women who are regarded greater than others, those donning yellow NRM T-shirts and dry banana leaves," the party's National Secretary for Women, Ingrid Turinawe, said while announcing the FDC Special Women's Month, ending on March 8.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Kill Two in Rusizi, Over 100 Houses Razed
Headline: Rwanda: Heavy Rains Kill Two in Rusizi, Over 100 Houses Razed
Body_Text:
Rusizi — Two children, Elida Kayitesi, 6, and Theogene Musoni, 4, were last week killed by heavy running water which entered into their house in Gashinjano Cell, Muganza Sector of Rusizi District.
When contacted, the district Executive Secretary, Boniface Bajyanama, said that the failure of residents to construct soil erosion terraces on Rwimbogo, Gitambi and Nzahaha mountain sides had led to the heavy slides during rainy season.

Original_Search_Title: Moupo is On the Right Track
Headline: Botswana: Moupo is On the Right Track
Body_Text:
News that the president of the Botswana National Front (BNF) has taken up his cudgel to fight for what he has been hired to do as the Leader of Opposition in Parliament is highly welcome. We were beginning to write Moupo's political obituary given his lukewarm performance until Wednesday's sterling delivery when debating the budget speech.
Contrary to some insensitive supporters of the BNF who equate any criticism levelled at their leader as political heresy, it is not our favourite pastime to be bashing political leaders without justification. The fact is that a democracy becomes a democracy when all its institutions and agents are vibrant. A democracy of docile and moribund institutions is worse than a dictatorship. While a dictatorship does not carry any pretensions, a docile democracy is one huge façade. As the Leader of Opposition in Parliament, Moupo's position is too critical and he cannot escape censure when he failing to live up to expectations. He needs to be a busy body to put our democracy on a geyser and this is what the nation expects from him. The critical questions that Moupo raised in his response to the budget speech are the very concerns that we expect a leader in his position to always put forward. The relationship between the Botswana government and De Beers has always been shrouded in mystery. It takes the persistent scrutiny of institutions such as the media, the opposition, which Moupo leads in Parliament and other civic organisations to demand answers. It has been said that Botswana is shortchanged in its dealings with De Beers and Moupo's contribution has greatly assisted in forcing the issue into public debate. There are of course a number of national challenges, which require some soul searching and alternatives. Our hope is that politicians like Moupo would always come forward to help deconstruct these complexities. It would not help the voters, his party and Batswana and indeed our democracy if Moupo's show in Parliament was just a flash in the pan. We expect him to maintain the momentum. Batswana need a vibrant democracy and they can ill-afford an opposition leader whose meteoric rise is based on banana peels. Bravo to the Under-23 side We congratulate the national Under-23 football team after defying the odds to hold fancied Tunisia to 0-0 in the Olympic Games qualifiers on Wednesday. What makes the draw very sweet is that our boys were playing away from home. It is not just an average side that we were playing but Africa's football power house -Tunisia. We have every reason to expect great things from this team. When the second leg comes to Gaborone, the nation should rally behind the team to ensure that it proceeds to last qualifying group stage of the Olympics.

Original_Search_Title: Handicraft Improving Women's Lives
Headline: Rwanda: Handicraft Improving Women's Lives
Body_Text:
Kigali — It is Monday morning. Several women, about 15 in number, of different ages wrapped in different bitengi are crammed in a tiny room. Several pieces of banana fibres are scattered on the cement floor. The filth on the floor has accumulated but no one pays attention.
Parched on wooden chairs the women concentrate on their work as they keep glued on the items they are making, like primary pupils attending a hand work lesson.

Original_Search_Title: Reaping From Hardwork
Headline: Uganda: Reaping From Hardwork
Body_Text:
Bonney Katatumba started small and at young age, juggling school and business. It wasn't long before his efforts paid off, and today, he's one of the most successful businessmen to reckon with
His grandmother christened him Bagwehangira, a Runyankore word for innovative, and before he was 10 years old, the Pakistan Honorary Consul to Uganda, His Excellence Bonney Katatumba, was already a businessman.

Original_Search_Title: Bushenyi Split - Who Will Win?
Headline: Uganda: Bushenyi Split - Who Will Win?
Body_Text:
Kampala — On January 10, 2007, Bushenyi district council held a crisis meeting and passed a resolution to split the district into five. All the 50 councillors backed the resolution that the five counties of Buhweju, Bunyaruguru, Sheema, Ruhinda and Igara, be turned into separate districts. A lot of dust has been raised, causing conflicts among politicians, civil and religious leaders and the population. A cross-section say the district should be split, while others disagree. Some say it should be split into two districts, instead of five. Bushenyi has a population of 800,000 people. Councillors say each county would be turned into a district. Kyomuhendo Muhanga sought district leaders' and locals' opinions about the split.
Prof. Tarsis Kabwegyere, MP Igara South and minister for disaster preparedness said: "As long as I'm around and still serving in this government, forget the so-called districts or splitting Bushenyi and every bogus resolution."

Original_Search_Title: Hope for Deaf Children
Headline: Uganda: Hope for Deaf Children
Body_Text:
Kampala — MAAMA wa bakasiru' (meaning mother of dumb children) is what everyone calls her. Scovia Nsamba, a resident of Kitengesa village in Masaka district, is no ordinary mother.
Hers is a family of 98 children, all deaf, dumb and most of them unwanted by people who should love them - their parents.

Original_Search_Title: Tazara - an Epitome of China's Technology
Headline: Zambia: Tazara - an Epitome of China's Technology
Body_Text:
AT about 15:50hours the locomotive is set in motion from Kapiri Mposhi much to the delight of the awaiting passengers some of whom have been at the station as early as six hours ago.
This express train coded K-4 arrived the previous day from Dar es Salaam in Tanzania and already it is on its way to the "Land of Plenty". It is now on a 38- hour-or-so journey which will be stretched over a three-day period.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Considers Party Options
Headline: Kenya: Poll - Kibaki's Party Options
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Even as he keeps his re-election game plan a guarded secret, the head of state may have to resort to deals similar to those of 2002 to survive.
President Kibaki has chosen to keep his re-election game plan a guarded secret, declaring he would be going for a second term but stopping short of naming the party on whose ticket he would be running.

Original_Search_Title: Eight Year-Old Child Murdered
Headline: Rwanda: Eight Year-Old Child Murdered
Body_Text:
Muhanga — An eight year old child identified as Issa Nsenginyunva of Gasenyi Village in Nyamabuye Sector, Muhanga District was on January 27, allegedly murdered by unidentified people. According to deceased's father, Iddi Musabyimana, Nsengiyunva was allegedly attacked and strangled by unknown people on the morning of the National communal work day (Umuganda) while at his father's home. He said the child was later damped in the home toilet and the killers placed a banana fiber around his neck in a leaning position of the toilet wall. The child, Nsengiyunva added appeared to be in good health at the time of his death.
The deceased teenager was a fifth born of eight children and primary one pupil at Meru primary school. Nsengiyunva further said that the child was buried on January 28, according to the Islamic rituals at the cemetery in Murambi village.

Original_Search_Title: Teenager Accused of Raping Niece's Daughters
Headline: Zimbabwe: Teenager Accused of Raping Niece's Daughters
Body_Text:
Chinhoyi — A RAFFINGORA teenager took advantage of his niece's absence to rape two of her three daughters, aged six and 10, a Chinhoyi magistrates' court heard last week.
The incident happened in November 2003 when the teenager, who was 16 at the time, visited his niece and found the girls alone.

Original_Search_Title: Unforgettable Valentine's Dance Lined Up for Lovers
Headline: Zimbabwe: Unforgettable Valentine's Dance Lined Up for Lovers
Body_Text:
Harare — IN less than three weeks from today lovers -- young and old -- will exchange gifts and declare the undying love for each other. I am, of course, taking about Valentine's Day, which falls on February 14 of each year.
This year the National Gallery of Zimbabwe and the Zimbabwe College of Music, with support from the United States embassy, have joined hands to provide an unforgettable and unique Valentine's Day dance dubbed "The Gallery of the Heart".

Original_Search_Title: Having Fun With Chimps On the Ngamba Island
Headline: Uganda: Having Fun With Chimps On the Ngamba Island
Body_Text:
Ngamba Isand Chimp sanctuary has become one of the destinations for international tourists, recording an average of 5,000 visitors a year.
It was drizzling in the morning when together with a group of friends, I jumped onto a boat at the landing site opposite Uganda Wildlife Education Centre (UWEC) in Entebbe and sailed to Ngamba Island Chimpanzee Sanctuary.

Original_Search_Title: Branding Beer
Headline: Uganda: Branding Beer
Body_Text:
A former Teaching Assistant at Makerere University, Ms Estella Muzito, 26, is the Brand Manager, Pilsner Lager, a beer brand of Uganda Breweries Limited (UBL). She told DENNIS D. MUHUMUZA about her career and Pilsner's love affair with drama.
You have a bottle of Pilsner Lager on your desk; do you start your day with a cold one?

Original_Search_Title: How Dr. Edwadi Kayondo Did It
Headline: Uganda: How Dr. Edwadi Kayondo Did It
Body_Text:
Kampala — The fear of misfortune befalling his family drove Ssalongo Dr. Edwadi Kayondo to go against his strong Christian beliefs and initiate his twins on January 12 at his home in Bunga, a Kampala suburb.
"I have been told the common punishment that twins give to those who refuse to perform the traditional erotic night dance is losing all their wealth and falling sick," Kayondo said.

Original_Search_Title: Our Politicians - Betty Bakireke Nambooze
Headline: Uganda: Our Politicians - Betty Bakireke Nambooze
Body_Text:
Kampala — HER academic background is rather shaky; the effects of a deprived childhood. But she speaks with such authority that supporters call her "Madam Ttica (teacher)".
Betty Nambooze Bakireke, the Democratic Party's (DP) outspoken spokesperson, is currently minting political capital out of her checkered upbringing.

Original_Search_Title: When Twins Are Born...
Headline: Uganda: When Twins Are Born...
Body_Text:
Kampala — The woman danced around the compound, a live cock tied around her waist. It was left hanging around her buttocks, and as she danced from one corner to the other, the cock's body bumped against her bottom. Her son-in-law matched her frenzied moves, a cord with enkejje attached to it tied around his waist.
They danced backwards towards each other until their backsides touched. The onlookers cheered. Seduced by the wild cheers around them, mother and son-in law engaged in a mock fight as they pulled at each other. The cock, previously dazed into a trance by the wild movements, kicked as the man yanked it from the woman's waist. The crowd screamed excitedly, drowning its agitation with their cheers.

Original_Search_Title: Rewarding 110 Days in Marangu
Headline: Tanzania: Rewarding 110 Days in Marangu
Body_Text:
Arusha — The sun has not yet risen over the sprawling villages of Marangu as I stumbled sleepily outside to catch the early morning bus to Moshi. Minibuses were already ferrying passengers in the purple predawn light.
I boarded the bus and safely stored my luggage. It was good to be in a public transport again, I relaxed as I sat properly contented to watch the Marangu countryside fly past in a blur of green fields, waterfalls, rivers and scenic hills.

Original_Search_Title: Legacy of Two Worlds for Residents of Border Towns
Headline: Kenya: Legacy of Two Worlds for Residents of Border Towns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When sickness strikes and death stares in the face of Kenyans living in Taveta, they have an easy solution: They take their sick to Mawenzi Hospital in Moshi, Tanzania, or Dr Minja's clinic in the same town.
And on bright days, freelance photographer James Kyalo Mutua waits for customers at the border town and secures enough business, before hopping into a bus to Arusha where the cost of processing pictures is cheap and services fast. Then he returns to Kenya to deliver his orders.

Original_Search_Title: Red Faces At Blue Banana!
Headline: Zimbabwe: Red Faces At Blue Banana!
Body_Text:
HAVING said, in this column last week, how comparatively reasonable Blue Banana/Baobab Grill twin restaurants at Newlands were the previous week (certainly when compared to a local Greek outlet visited three days later) I had a very flustered owner, Heath Stewart on the cell saying...oops....they'd e-mailed me a wrong menu and price list!
He didn't say how out-of-date was the one sent, but they had customers clamouring for bargain meals and members of the trade wondered if Blue Banana management had gone ...well...bananas?

Original_Search_Title: Tororo District Moves to Tackle Garbage Problem
Headline: Uganda: Tororo District Moves to Tackle Garbage Problem
Body_Text:
Kampala — TORORO town will soon be garbage-free. The town authorities say they have devised a community-based strategy to solve the problem.
The acting town clerk, Kenneth Ofwono, said starting this month, every household will be given two sacks for garbage disposal.

Original_Search_Title: Vendors - We Could Buy Nakasero Market
Headline: Uganda: Vendors - We Could Buy Nakasero Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kampala businessman Hassan Basajjabalaba has bought Nakasero Market in a controversial deal that has temporarily been halted by Kampala City Council. The New Vision sought views from the various stakeholders
Farouk Kigowa, trader No one from Kampala City Council explained to us what was going on, until last week when Mayor Nasser Ntege Sebaggala and Kawempe North MP Latif Ssebagala toured the market. They said it was sold during John Ssebana's regime and was divided into four plots. Only one plot, where they plan to cram the market has remained. This place is like home to us because it is where our parents worked. When school failed, we came back. Development is good, but the people should be involved. If they want a modern shopping mall, they can get land out of town like Game Stores did. Nakasero Market is for the local people.

Original_Search_Title: Billions to Be Spent On Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Billions to Be Spent On Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two leading political parties are preparing budgets running into billions of shillings to finance their candidates in this year's General Election.
While ODM Kenya has a tentative budget of Sh2 billion, Narc Kenya's leadership is working on its own estimates for the election which sources said could go up to Sh5 billion.

Original_Search_Title: Hope for Diabetic Patients
Headline: Nigeria: Hope for Diabetic Patients
Body_Text:
Lagos — Suppose you have just been diagnosed with diabetes. Chances are you have a million questions running through your head. Which type of diabetes do I have? What can I eat if I have diabetes? What affects my blood sugar? How do I check my blood sugar? Will diabetes lead to my early death? Your life before your diagnosis of diabetes would have been dogged by signs of being very thirsty, or urinating rather more often, or feeling hungry or tired, or having sores that heal slowly, or having dry itchy skin, or losing feeling in your feet or having tingling in your feet, or having blurry eye sight. Of course you may have had one or more of these signs before you found out you had diabetes. Or you may have had no signs at all. A blood test to check your glucose levels will definitively show if what you have is an early form of diabetes called pre-diabetes, or diabetes proper.
To be diagnosed with diabetes is quite a serious matter because diabetes can be a life-long disease marked by high levels of sugar in the blood. Diabetes means your blood glucose (often called blood sugar because glucose is the simplest form of sugar) is too high. Your blood always has some glucose in it because your body needs glucose for energy to keep you going. Indeed athletes often take raw glucose powder, glucose rich fruits like banana, or glucose rich drinks to enable them source extra energy before their performance. But too much glucose in the blood isnÃ­t good for your health.

Original_Search_Title: Zanu-PF Remains Mother of All Political Parties in Zim
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zanu-PF Remains Mother of All Political Parties in Zim
Body_Text:
Harare — IT is interesting to note that many people who wrote off Zanu-PF as a dead institution over the past five or so years, are back in the party.
They masquerade as opponents but are dying to influence events within the vanguard party, the only institution with the impetus to rule in Zimbabwe at the moment.

Original_Search_Title: The Last Word for a Dear Colleague
Headline: Sierra Leone: The Last Word for a Dear Colleague
Body_Text:
I was walking along Garrison Street in the centre of the capital Freetown on January 20 when I bumped into a colleague, Christian Williams, who asked me if I knew Donald John was dead. I was shocked. He said it was on the newspapers, but rather unfortunately, my engagements now days have even deprived me of having ample time to even read newspapers. Those who know me know that I am a voracious reader because I believe that the ideal life is a life of intellectual contemplation. But I am one man who has felt disillusioned with the Sierra Leonean media because of its retrogressive nature and malevolent characterisation. Nevertheless, that does not mean that I do not feel proud to be associated with it, even with the Octopus guys around still clutching the destiny of the profession. God damn me if I am optimistic that the media would pay its last respect to Donald. To journalists today, it is not something that has to do with them. Their private interest is much more important than the professional interest and the nurturing of solidarity.
Back to my friend Donald, we have known each other for so many years. He was always cheerful and very gregarious. Being gregarious is part of human nature, but Donald's gregariousness became notable through his too much fond for public places, so much so that his active participation in the media became less noticed.

Original_Search_Title: Mind What You Pack for Your Child
Headline: Uganda: Mind What You Pack for Your Child
Body_Text:
Kampala — Did you know that mid-morning hunger can make it difficult for your child to concentrate? And that the wrong snacks can be unhealthy for your child in the long run? It is, therefore, extremely important to choose healthy snacks for your child.
By 10:00am, the child is likely to start feeling hungry and hunger pangs can cause him or her to become irritable and lose concentration. As such, it is important for your child to have a healthy snack between breakfast and lunch.

Original_Search_Title: Let Everyone Fight Corruption
Headline: Rwanda: Let Everyone Fight Corruption
Body_Text:
Kigali — In The New Times edition of January 8, 2007 appeared an article titled 'People need to back the country from corruption'. The author whose names were withheld shed light on the fact that corrupt officials in government are still at large with the law not catching up with them.
What the author needs to know is that currently many African heads of states are up in arms to fight corruption at all levels in society. Rwanda in particular has strict laws against corruption. It does not matter what post one is holding at government level or what influence one wields in society the law will simply deal with such fellows as long as they are convicted of corruption.

Original_Search_Title: How We Reported Kayiira's Murder
Headline: Uganda: How We Reported Kayiira's Murder
Body_Text:
Kampala — Nearly 20 years ago the New Vision extensively covered the murder of Dr. Andrew Kayiira. In what is on record as the first interview following the killing, Henry Gombya said he didn't know who the killers were. Today, we reproduce the story as published on March 9, 1987...
Police are hunting for the killers of Dr Andrew Kayiira , the former Minister of Energy, who was shot at the home of Journalist Henry Gombya on Friday in the Konge-Lukuli suburb of Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Gombya to Sue Scotland Yard
Headline: Uganda: Gombya to Sue Scotland Yard
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Kayiira saga has spread to Britain as Henry Gombya has told the British press he will take legal action against Scotland Yard, and the UK Home Secretary has been asked to release the investigation report.
On Monday, John Reid, the home secretary, is scheduled to respond to a request by Mike Hancock, MP for Portsmouth South, to publish the investigation, according to The Guardian newspaper.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Gives Kenya 37 New Districts
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Gives Kenya 37 New Districts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has formally launched 37 new districts and is waiting for the re-opening of Parliament to table a Bill for their ratification.
If approved by the House, the number of districts countrywide would rise to 108 up from the current 71.This comes ahead of the General Election set to be held later in the year. In 2005, barely a few weeks to the November 21 referendum vote on a new constitution, President Kibaki announced 33 proposed new districts in what was seen as move to woo voters to the Banana camp.

Original_Search_Title: Bobi Wine to Extend an Olive Branch This Year
Headline: Uganda: Bobi Wine to Extend an Olive Branch This Year
Body_Text:
Popular musician and self-proclaimed ghetto President Bobi Wine talks about rumours that he has been banned from releasing any more controversial songs after his popular hit Ghetto that castigates elected leaders that turn their backs on their electorate.
Have you been stopped from releasing any more songs?

Original_Search_Title: 2007 NAMA Nominees Announced
Headline: Zimbabwe: 2007 NAMA Nominees Announced
Body_Text:
Harare — THE 2007 National Arts Merit Awards nominees were announced in Harare on Wednesday.
This year's edition of the awards, to be held on February 4 at the 7 Arts Theatre, has 25 categories with the media awards being the latest addition.

Original_Search_Title: It's Party Time Once Again
Headline: Zimbabwe: It's Party Time Once Again
Body_Text:
Harare — PUT on your dancing shoes, the party is about to start!
After taking a sabbatical, most artistes return this week and have several high-octane gigs in store for their fans.

Original_Search_Title: Mudavadi Seeks Kombo's Support
Headline: Kenya: Mudavadi Seeks Kombo's Support
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ODM-Kenya presidential hopeful, Mr Musalia Mudavadi, has appealed to Ford-Kenya chairman, Mr Musikari Kombo, to support his bid for the top seat.
Mudavadi said he was better placed in Luhyaland to successfully contest the presidency and that Kombo should lead Bungoma residents and his Ford-Kenya supporters in backing him. "I do not want Bungoma District to be isolated politically by remaining out of ODM-Kenya. People of Bungoma should learn from the 2005 referendum poll when of the 24 constituencies in Western Province, only five in Bungoma voted for Banana which lost," said Mudavadi.

Original_Search_Title: DP Takes Kayiira Case to British Parliament
Headline: Uganda: DP Takes Kayiira Case to British Parliament
Body_Text:
The Democratic Party has now decided to advance its crusade for justice in the murder of Dr Andrew Lutaakome Kayiira to the British parliament, the House of Commons.
Opposition (shadow) Attorney General, Kampala Central MP Erias Lukwago, said yesterday that the party's new move is inspired by fears that the Scotland Yard report released by the Uganda Government could have been doctored, an accusation that would considerably damage the credibility of the British Metropolitan Police, which investigated the crime in 1987.

Original_Search_Title: Mudavadi Receives Rousing Reception
Headline: Kenya: Mudavadi Receives Rousing Reception
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hundreds of ODM-Kenya supporters converged at trading centres in Western Province in the past two days to receive presidential aspirant Mr Musalia Mudavadi.
They danced with the former Sabatia MP, sang in his praise and displayed their support for the party by dressing in orange colours and through written messages.

Original_Search_Title: Why Opposing the 'Bush Doctrine' is a Good Thing
Headline: Nigeria: Why Opposing the 'Bush Doctrine' is a Good Thing
Body_Text:
As part of the growing disconnect between the American military and its commander-in-chief, a letter signed by over a thousand active servicemen is due to be submitted to the United States Congress tomorrow in which the soldiers would express an open opposition to President Bush's misadventures in Iraq.
They would argue that the war in Iraq was needless; that it has caused over three thousand American deaths (and still counting) and the death of nearly a million Iraqis; that its casus belli, if there was one in the first place, had expired; and that U.S involvement in the country, which has caused so much pain and loss not just in Iraq but in other parts of the Middle East, does not really serve the interest of the American people.

Original_Search_Title: Pilsner's Theatre Now Upcountry
Headline: Uganda: Pilsner's Theatre Now Upcountry
Body_Text:
Kampala — UPCOUNTRY drama shows organised by the Golden Drama Foundation and sponsored by Pilsner Lager are scheduled to start this weekend.
There will be performances at Entebbe Cinema on January 19, Lydrines in Luweero on January 21 and Mukiibi's Theatre in Namasuba on January 26.

Original_Search_Title: USA/Africa - Constructing a Terror Front
Headline: Africa: USA/Africa - Constructing a Terror Front
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — AfricaFocus Bulletin
"Notwithstanding the lack of evidence, Washington saw a Saharan Front as the linchpin for the militarization of Africa, greater access to its oil resources (Africa will supply 25% of U.S. hydrocarbons by 2015), and the sustained involvement of Europe in America's counterterrorism program." - Jeremy Keenan

Original_Search_Title: Court Slams Lover's Killer 40 Years Imprisonment
Headline: Cameroon: Court Slams Lover's Killer 40 Years Imprisonment
Body_Text:
38-year-old David Ngenge of Mbarse village in Ndu Subdivision has been sentenced to 40 years imprisonment.
The Nkambe Court of First Instance holden on December 8, 2007, before his Lordship James Mukube Eni found Ngenge guilty of stabbing one Nyeh Emelda, his one time lover, to death.

Original_Search_Title: CDC's New Multi-Million Investment
Headline: Cameroon: CDC's New Multi-Million Investment
Body_Text:
The management of the Cameroon Development Corporation, CDC, has announced a multi-million FCFA package meant for the acquisition of heavy duty equipment, building a new oil mill, construction of toilets and rehabilitation of camp houses, supply of water and electricity and several other things.
The CDC General Manager, Henry Njalla Quan made this known while he was addressing top management staff of the Corporation during the first session of the Corporation's bi-annual management meetings.

Original_Search_Title: Veil of Ta-Ladan
Headline: Nigeria: Veil of Ta-Ladan
Body_Text:
I am not the official or even unofficial spokesman of the National Governors Forum, but I am very surprised that this country's 35-or-so state governors, each one with an elaborate media office of his own, would allow last week's very damning condemnation of themselves by President Obasanjo to go unchallenged.
Speaking at the PDP retreat for its candidates, PDP's "national leader", whose party has 25 "loyal" governors, two estranged governors, one floating governor and one martial law administrator, condemned all the country's governors for non-performance and said he could not vouch for "more than 15-or-so" of them.

Original_Search_Title: AEPB Raids Fruit Vendors
Headline: Nigeria: AEPB Raids Fruit Vendors
Body_Text:
The Abuja Environmental Protection Board (AEPB) on Friday raided fruit vendors who hawk their wares in front of motor parks, City News reports.
The traders who displayed their wares at the entrance of motor parks in the city were taken by surprise as officials of AEPB swooped on them without prior notice.

Original_Search_Title: Peter Stands Out
Headline: Zimbabwe: Peter Stands Out
Body_Text:
Harare — I have been following the debate on the Golden Footballer with keen interest and have realised that, firstly the Press has led the fans to believe that Peter, Chunga, Shaya and Bruce are the major contestants.
My understanding is that the Caf Award looks at footballers who did their country proud for the past 50 years, to put it straight since 1956. Secondly, people are basing their arguments on mere things like, "his skills were good, he was the best passer of the ball" without looking at records or facts. I am challenging others to come up with facts. I will do so for Peter Ndlovu because he is the player I saw playing.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandan Juba Traders in Accident
Headline: Ugandan Juba Traders in Accident
Body_Text:
